source.title: Three Rival Versions of Moral Enquiry
source.author: Alasdair MacIntyre

pages.summary: This is the closing page of MacIntyre's Preface to 'Three Rival Versions of Moral Enquiry,' in which he acknowledges his intellectual debts to colleagues at Yale's Whitney Humanities Center and thanks the National Endowment for the Humanities for funding his historical research into the place of philosophy in the curriculum during 1987–1988. This research, he notes, directly underwrites the argument of lectures III–VII, situating the book's project in a concrete institutional and historical context. The page thus frames the entire inquiry as emerging from a specific tradition of scholarly community and institutional support — itself an enactment of the book's themes.

annotations.content: Joseph Raz
annotations.anchor_text: _debt of Jonathan Lear and of Joseph Raz_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has underlined Joseph Raz's name, likely flagging the significance of MacIntyre's intellectual engagement with a major liberal political philosopher known for his work on authority, practical reason, and value pluralism. This is notable because Raz represents a tradition broadly at odds with MacIntyre's Thomistic communitarianism — their dialogue on practical rationality would be a productive tension worth tracking. Tom may be noting this as evidence that MacIntyre was in serious conversation with liberal rivals, not merely dismissing them.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects directly to the RC theme of tradition vs. Enlightenment rationalism: Raz is a sophisticated defender of a broadly liberal, autonomy-centered ethics, and MacIntyre's acknowledgment of genuine intellectual debt to him complicates any simple opposition between the 'encyclopaedist' and 'tradition' versions of moral enquiry that the RC project must navigate.

annotations.content: place of philosophy in the curriculum
annotations.anchor_text: _on the history of the place of philosophy in the curriculum_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has underlined this phrase, which is the actual historical research topic MacIntyre was funded to pursue — not the abstract philosophical argument itself, but its institutional and curricular location. This suggests Tom is alert to MacIntyre's insistence that philosophy's authority is always embedded in institutional structures, and that the 'crisis' of moral enquiry is also a crisis of how and where philosophy is taught. The underline signals that this phrase carries more argumentative weight than a casual reader might notice in a preface.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly central to RC's concern with the role of institutions in forming character: MacIntyre's research into the curriculum historicizes the question of who gets to shape moral formation and through what institutional channels, which maps onto RC's subsidiarity theme and the question of whether universities can still function as tradition-bearing communities.

pages.summary: MacIntyre opens by distinguishing two historical conditions for philosophical lecturing: periods of stable consensus, in which the relationship between lecturer and audience rested on unspoken but deep agreements about subject matter and purpose, and periods of crisis, in which those agreements have been challenged or rejected and audiences have become fragmented. He uses the Gifford Lectures themselves as a concrete historical illustration, contrasting Lord Gifford's era (stable consensus) with MacIntyre's own moment of delivery (contested fragmentation). This sets up the book's central diagnostic claim: we are now in a period where rival versions of moral enquiry compete without a shared framework to adjudicate between them.

annotations.content: L.R. Ad
annotations.anchor_text: _There have of course been long pe-_
annotations.interpretation: This is almost certainly an abbreviated bibliographic or cross-reference note — 'L.R.' likely refers to a specific text, thinker, or concept Tom is tracking, and 'Ad' may stand for 'Address,' 'Alasdair,' or a source abbreviation. Its placement at the top right, near the discussion of the lecture-audience relationship, suggests Tom is flagging a connection to another work dealing with the social conditions of intellectual discourse — possibly linking MacIntyre's account of stable vs. contested periods to a specific theorist of rhetoric, civic address, or tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: This note likely cross-references a source relevant to RC's interest in the institutional and communal conditions required for genuine moral discourse — the idea that civility and shared enquiry presuppose a kind of pre-theoretical consensus that modernity has eroded.

annotations.content: no one needed to advert explicitly to this latter type of issue
annotations.anchor_text: _riods in the history of the lecture as_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this to flag MacIntyre's key claim that healthy intellectual traditions operate on implicit, unarticulated shared presuppositions — the moment you have to make them explicit, the tradition is already under stress. This is a structurally important move: the need for explicit articulation is itself a symptom of crisis. Tom is likely tracking this as a diagnostic marker that recurs across MacIntyre's work.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's concern with the preconditions of civility: genuine civic friendship and moral community operate through embodied, habitual consensus rather than contractual explicitness — a point MacIntyre shares with Aristotle and Aquinas and which RC seeks to recover.

annotations.content: There have also however been periods in which such agreements have to some significant degree been challenged or rejected
annotations.anchor_text: _cerning the point and purpose of lecturing_
annotations.interpretation: The circle draws a box around this sentence, marking it as a structural pivot in the argument — the turn from describing stable periods to describing crisis periods. Tom is likely identifying this as the hinge sentence that introduces the 'rival versions' problematic proper. Circling rather than underlining suggests Tom sees this as a unit to be isolated and returned to, possibly as a thesis statement or as a node connecting to other passages in the book.
annotations.rc_relevance: This circled passage is the diagnostic core of RC's historical narrative: the transition from a culture of shared moral presupposition to one of fragmentation and contestation is precisely the crisis RC is trying to address through the recovery of Thomistic and classical traditions of civility.

annotations.content: When I first wrote these Gifford Lectures
annotations.anchor_text: _the same way. When I first wrote these_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles the phrase marking MacIntyre's autobiographical-reflexive turn, where the author situates himself historically within the very crisis he is diagnosing. This is a moment of performative self-awareness: MacIntyre is not merely describing the problem from outside but acknowledging he is inside it. Tom likely marks this because it models a kind of intellectual honesty about one's own situated perspective that RC values — the scholar as participant in, not just analyst of, the tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's project is itself a situated intervention, not a view from nowhere; Tom may be noting MacIntyre's self-positioning as a methodological precedent for how RC should frame its own relationship to the traditions it seeks to recover.

annotations.content: while that in which I had the task of carrying out these duties was of the latter
annotations.anchor_text: _duties to his lecturers was of the former kind_
annotations.interpretation: This circle brackets the explicit historical contrast MacIntyre draws between Gifford's era and his own, marking the sentence that crystallizes the two-period typology. Tom is likely isolating this as the clearest formulation of the book's opening diagnostic — useful for citation or for anchoring later discussion of what kind of moment we currently inhabit.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'former/latter' contrast maps directly onto RC's historical narrative: a recoverable past of moral consensus versus a fragmented present that demands active reconstruction — the RC project positions itself precisely in MacIntyre's 'latter' period.

pages.summary: MacIntyre reflects on the reception of his Edinburgh and Yale lectures, noting that audiences did not hear them as neutral interventions but as contributions to already-ongoing debates within which they were already partisan. More significantly, he observes that the audiences were not merely divided but inhabited three distinct, mutually antagonistic frameworks of moral enquiry — so that the lectures were not episodes in parallel conversations but interventions in a continuing quarrel among incommensurable traditions. This sets up the book's core tripartite structure: encyclopaedist, genealogist, and Thomistic-traditionalist modes of moral enquiry.

annotations.content: But even when so writing I had not yet reckoned sufficiently with the way in which, or the degree to which, the problems and questions arising from it would be raised for me by the characteristically generous and often searching responses of my audiences at Edinburgh and at Yale.
annotations.anchor_text: _had not yet reckoned sufficiently with_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has bracketed the opening confession of intellectual inadequacy — MacIntyre admitting that audience challenge forced him to deepen his thinking. This is not a passive mark; Tom is flagging the performative dimension of MacIntyre's rhetoric: the author models the very tradition-constituted self-correction he will later argue is the hallmark of living traditions. The bracket likely signals Tom's interest in how scholarly community functions as a corrective mechanism — audiences as interlocutors, not consumers.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly touches RC's theme of civic friendship and the role of community in forming and correcting moral enquiry — the idea that intellectual virtue is not solitary but dialogically constituted, which is central to MacIntyre's Thomistic account of tradition.

annotations.content: diences the differences and divisions were such that different groups in both audiences understood the lectures as episodes within very different enacted narratives of enquiry and debate, thus interpreting and evaluating them from a number of very different standpoints.
annotations.anchor_text: _different enacted narratives of enquiry and debate_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has bracketed the phrase 'enacted narratives of enquiry and debate' — a philosophically loaded term. MacIntyre is asserting that moral frameworks are not just propositional but narrative and practical, and that interpretation is always already tradition-indexed. Tom is likely flagging this as a key epistemological claim: standpoints are not chosen but inhabited. The bracket may also track this as evidence for MacIntyre's narrative theory of rationality.
annotations.rc_relevance: The concept of 'enacted narratives' is central to RC's interest in how institutions and traditions form character — moral enquiry is not abstract but embedded in lived communal stories, resonating with MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment decontextualized rationalism.

annotations.content: point of intersection of three very different groups
annotations.anchor_text: _remarks made by someone standing at the point_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has highlighted the geometric metaphor of 'intersection' — this is the precise moment MacIntyre introduces the tripartite structure that organizes the entire book. Tom is marking the conceptual hinge of the introduction. The highlight suggests he sees this as a passage to return to, possibly for citation or as an organizing motif in RC work. The word 'intersection' is important: it implies the three traditions are not merely different but spatially incompatible, occupying the same contested terrain.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the foundational moment of MacIntyre's encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition tripartite structure, which RC uses as a diagnostic framework for understanding contemporary social and moral fragmentation — each competing tradition generating its own account of civility, virtue, and community.

annotations.content: mode of interpretation and evaluation
annotations.anchor_text: _degree to which each such mode of interpretation_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has underlined this technical phrase, likely to track MacIntyre's use of 'mode' as a quasi-Kuhnian term — not just disagreement within a shared framework but incommensurable modes of reading and judging. The underline isolates the epistemological claim: the problem is not factual disagreement but framework-level divergence in how evidence and argument are processed. Tom may be cross-referencing this with Kuhn, Gadamer, or MacIntyre's own 'Whose Justice? Which Rationality?'
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with tradition vs. reform hinges precisely on this: if modes of evaluation are tradition-constituted, then appeals to neutral 'civil' discourse are themselves partisan moves — a key RC thesis about the limits of procedural civility.

annotations.content: quarrel
annotations.anchor_text: _merely in a continuing set of conversations, but in a continuing quarrel_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has highlighted the word 'quarrel' at the end of the paragraph — MacIntyre's deliberate escalation from 'conversations' to 'quarrel.' This is a rhetorically charged move: MacIntyre is denying the liberal pluralist fiction of civil dialogue among different perspectives. Tom's highlighting isolates this word as a conceptual intervention, likely because it directly challenges the RC project's aspirations toward 'resurrecting civility' — if these are quarrels, not conversations, what does civility even mean?
annotations.rc_relevance: This is perhaps the most directly provocative annotation for the RC project: MacIntyre's insistence on 'quarrel' rather than 'conversation' is a challenge to any naive recovery of civility, forcing RC to grapple with whether civility is a virtue within a tradition or a meta-framework that transcends traditions.

annotations.content: What were these differences? They had two dimensions.
annotations.anchor_text: _What were these differences They had two dimensions_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has underlined MacIntyre's explicit structural signpost — the announcement of a two-dimensional taxonomy of the differences among the three groups. This is a tracking mark: Tom is following the argumentative architecture and flagging the transition from descriptive scene-setting to analytical breakdown. The underline likely also signals that Tom intends to map these two dimensions explicitly in his RC notes.
annotations.rc_relevance: Understanding the two dimensions of difference among encyclopaedist, genealogist, and Thomistic traditions is essential for RC's deployment of MacIntyre's framework — the dimensions will likely correspond to substantive moral disagreements and methodological/epistemological disagreements, both of which RC must address.

annotations.content: the lectures I discuss three very different and mutually antagonistic conceptions of moral enquiry, each stemming from a seminal late nineteenth-century text: the Ninth Edition of the Encyclopaedia Britannica, Nietzsche's Zur Genealogie der Moral and the encyclical letter of Pope Leo XIII Aeterni Patris.
annotations.anchor_text: _three very different and mutually antagonistic conceptions_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has bracketed the identification of the three founding texts — the Encyclopaedia Britannica, Nietzsche, and Aeterni Patris. This is a key structural annotation, marking the precise moment MacIntyre names his three rivals. The bracket suggests Tom is archiving this as a reference point. The choice of 'stemming from a seminal late nineteenth-century text' is also notable: MacIntyre is historicizing the supposedly timeless positions, which Tom may be tracking as itself a Thomistic methodological move.
annotations.rc_relevance: The three texts map directly onto RC's tripartite framework: the encyclopaedist tradition (liberal progressivism), the genealogist tradition (Nietzschean critique of morality), and the Thomistic tradition (Catholic natural law). RC's project of 'resurrecting civility' must situate itself within or among these — most likely as a recovery and defense of the Thomistic tradition against the other two.

annotations.content: Vertical bracket with downward arrow on right margin beside the final paragraph
annotations.anchor_text: _What were these differences They had two dimensions_
annotations.interpretation: The downward arrow attached to the bracket on the right margin suggests Tom is flagging this passage as pointing forward — the argument continues and this is a node to return to. It may also indicate that Tom sees the 'two dimensions' claim as carrying weight for subsequent pages and wants to track how MacIntyre develops it. The arrow is directional, not merely emphatic, suggesting active reading rather than passive marking.
annotations.rc_relevance: This forward-pointing symbol suggests Tom is building a structural map of MacIntyre's argument as it unfolds — consistent with RC's need to systematically deploy MacIntyre's framework rather than cite it selectively.

pages.summary: MacIntyre observes that his lecture audiences at Edinburgh and Yale were already embedded in rival evaluative traditions — Nietzschean genealogy, Thomism, analytic philosophy, Scottish Enlightenment culture — and heard his arguments through those pre-formed allegiances, producing predictable disagreements. This multiplicity of interpretive frameworks is not merely academic noise but illustrates the core problem of the book: that the three rival versions of moral enquiry are not simply competing theories but incommensurable traditions that shape what counts as a valid argument in the first place. The page closes by raising the diagnostic question of whether academic communities are now so fragmented that any common address is impossible.

annotations.content: among the matters on which the three types of moral enquiry with which I am concerned differ is the nature and scope of moral enquiry.
annotations.anchor_text: _among the matters on which the three types_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is marking the precise formulation of MacIntyre's tripartite thesis: the three rivals don't merely disagree about moral conclusions but about what moral enquiry itself is. This is a meta-level disagreement, not a first-order one. Tom is likely flagging this as the structural hinge of the whole Introduction — the point where MacIntyre signals that the book is about epistemological and methodological incommensurability, not just ethical content.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly tracks the RC theme of MacIntyre's encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition tripartite structure and the problem of tradition-constituted rationality — you cannot adjudicate between traditions using neutral criteria because the criteria themselves are tradition-dependent.

annotations.content: those who listened to the lectures from an evaluative standpoint already formed by allegiance to one of those types of enquiry differed in predictable ways from each other
annotations.anchor_text: _those who listened to the lectures from an evaluative standpoint_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the phrase 'already formed by allegiance' — this is the key phenomenological claim that listeners are not neutral receivers of argument but traditioned subjects. The word 'predictable' is doing heavy philosophical lifting: it implies that tradition-membership is not arbitrary but shapes cognition in structured, foreseeable ways. Tom may be noting this as evidence for MacIntyre's anti-liberal epistemology: there is no 'view from nowhere.'
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's concern with how traditions form character and judgment — the audience's pre-formed allegiances illustrate how moral communities shape what can even be heard as a valid argument, connecting to subsidiarity and the formative role of institutions.

annotations.content: Small circle or 'D' symbol with a dot, drawn in the right margin beside the 'nature and scope of moral enquiry' underline
annotations.anchor_text: _scope of moral enquiry. So that those who listened_
annotations.interpretation: This symbol — possibly a circled initial or a shorthand marker Tom uses for definitional moments — appears precisely where MacIntyre names the central differentiating issue between the three rivals. Tom may be using this as a navigational flag, distinguishing this as a 'definition' or 'key claim' moment in his annotation system. The placement alongside the meta-level disagreement about moral enquiry itself suggests he treats this as architecturally important to the book's argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project is explicitly built around MacIntyre's tripartite structure; this symbol likely marks the passage Tom will return to when explaining why the three traditions cannot simply debate on shared terms — foundational to the RC diagnosis of contemporary moral fragmentation.

annotations.content: the further range of traditions, modes of enquiry, and debates in terms of which others in the audience understood and evaluated the arguments of these lectures.
annotations.anchor_text: _Only a little less predictable was the further range_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the expansion of the problem beyond the three named rivals to a broader plurality of interpretive frameworks. MacIntyre is acknowledging that the real-world intellectual landscape is even messier than his three-rival schema — Hegelianism, phenomenology, analytic philosophy, Scottish Enlightenment culture all enter. Tom may be noting this as a tension: does the tripartite schema oversimplify? Or is Tom tracking MacIntyre's empirical honesty about the complexity his schema must accommodate?
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to the RC theme of tradition vs. reform and the challenge of speaking across incommensurable frameworks — if even the Yale and Edinburgh audiences fragment into sub-traditions, the RC project's hope of 'resurrecting civility' must reckon with this deeper pluralism.

annotations.content: being heard as contributing to ongoing discussions about how enquiry and teaching in the humanities ought to be pursued, discussions in which successive chairmen of the National Endowment for the Humanities have taken positions critical of both the theory and practice
annotations.anchor_text: _In a similar way at Yale I could not avoid_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the institutional-political dimension of MacIntyre's reception at Yale — his lectures were heard against the backdrop of the culture wars over the humanities, with the NEH chairmen representing a conservative institutional pressure on university culture. Tom is likely flagging that MacIntyre's philosophical project was received as politically charged whether he intended it or not. This may also interest Tom as a case study in how philosophical arguments get co-opted by institutional agendas.
annotations.rc_relevance: Highly relevant to RC's concern with the role of institutions in forming character and the tension between civic/political authority and intellectual tradition — the NEH controversy illustrates how questions of moral enquiry are never purely academic but are embedded in struggles over institutional power and cultural formation.

annotations.content: being heard as contributing to ongoing discussions about how enquiry and teaching in the humanities ought to be pursued, discussions in which successive chairmen of the National Endowment for the Humanities have
annotations.anchor_text: _being heard as contributing to ongoing discussions_
annotations.interpretation: The vertical bracket in the right margin reinforces the underline here and signals this passage deserves extended attention — Tom uses brackets when a passage is not just a key phrase but a developed unit of argument he wants to return to. The bracketed section captures the sociological embedding of philosophical discourse in institutional politics, which is a distinct point from the epistemological one made elsewhere on the page.
annotations.rc_relevance: This bracketed passage connects to the RC theme of subsidiarity and the question of which institutions legitimately shape moral and intellectual culture — the NEH controversy is a concrete historical instance of the state attempting to adjudicate between rival traditions of enquiry.

annotations.content: Are the differences and divisions which exist within academic communities by now so great that any notion of addressing the aca-
annotations.anchor_text: _Are the differences and divisions which exist within academic_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the closing diagnostic question of the page, which MacIntyre leaves deliberately open (the sentence continues on the next page). This is the existential question underlying the entire RC project: has fragmentation reached the point where common address is impossible? Tom's underlining here suggests he sees this as the motivating anxiety of MacIntyre's book — and presumably of the RC project itself. The sentence being cut off mid-page may itself be symbolically noted.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the RC project's central question in compressed form — 'resurrecting civility' presupposes that fragmentation is real and severe, and MacIntyre's framing here gives Tom the theoretical grounding for why the RC project is urgent rather than merely desirable.

annotations.content: Circled checkmark or 'D'-like symbol in the bottom-right margin beside the final underlined question
annotations.anchor_text: _Are the differences and divisions which exist within academic communities_
annotations.interpretation: This symbol mirrors the one placed earlier beside the 'nature and scope of moral enquiry' passage, suggesting Tom uses a consistent notation system. Placed at the bottom beside the fragmentation question, it may signal 'this is the payoff' or 'connect this to earlier marked passage' — functionally linking the meta-level epistemological claim at the top of the page to the sociological-diagnostic question at the bottom. The repetition of the symbol across two marked passages may indicate Tom is building a chain of cross-references.
annotations.rc_relevance: If both symbols mark structurally related moments — the nature of the disagreement (top) and its social consequence (bottom) — Tom is tracking the arc from MacIntyre's epistemological thesis to its civic implications, which is precisely the bridge the RC project needs to make.

pages.summary: MacIntyre opens by asking whether the failure of philosophical traditions to communicate with one another is merely a sociological accident of university specialization, or something more deeply rooted. He identifies two key concepts — incommensurability and untranslatability — as necessary to characterize this second, more fundamental dimension of disagreement. He then begins sketching two opposed positions on these concepts, introducing the relativist view (associated with Kuhn) that rival large-scale systems of thought have no independent standard by which their claims can be adjudicated.

annotations.content: Q2
annotations.anchor_text: _failure to communicate with each other which_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is labeling this passage as the second of at least two questions MacIntyre is posing in the Introduction. The first question (Q1, presumably on an earlier page) likely concerned the fact of philosophical disagreement; Q2 sharpens it by asking whether the failure of communication is merely structural/sociological or something more fundamental. Tom is tracking MacIntyre's rhetorical architecture — the book is built on a sequence of diagnostic questions that progressively deepen the problem.
annotations.rc_relevance: For the RC project, this distinction matters enormously: if the failure of civic and moral communication is merely institutional (fixable by reform), it is a different problem than if it is grounded in incommensurable traditions. MacIntyre's answer will bear directly on whether RC's recovery project is a matter of institutional redesign or something more radical.

annotations.content: has become vain and empty?
annotations.anchor_text: _public lectures has become vain and empty_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this striking evaluative phrase, which charges that the ideal of a shared educated public discourse has become hollow. The underlining suggests Tom finds this claim either provocative or important as a diagnostic starting point — MacIntyre is not merely describing fragmentation but declaring a kind of cultural and intellectual bankruptcy.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly echoes the RC project's animating concern: the collapse of genuine civic discourse and the question of whether inherited institutions of public reason can be revived or whether they have been hollowed out beyond repair.

annotations.content: de facto failure to communicate with each other, which is now sometimes evident
annotations.anchor_text: _de facto failure to communicate with each other_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the specific charge of communicative failure as a de facto — actually existing — condition, not merely a theoretical possibility. This distinguishes empirical breakdown from normative concern. Tom may be noting this as MacIntyre's empirical premise before he moves to the philosophical explanation.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is fundamentally concerned with the breakdown of civic friendship and moral community; MacIntyre's de facto framing grounds the RC project's urgency in observable social reality rather than mere philosophical abstraction.

annotations.content: is not simply an unfortunate accidental side effect of the specializations of the social structure of the contemporary university, but is grounded in something more fundamental
annotations.anchor_text: _not simply an unfortunate accidental side effect_
annotations.interpretation: This is arguably the most important underline on the page. Tom is marking MacIntyre's core diagnostic claim: the problem is not institutional but philosophical — it reaches down into the nature of rival traditions themselves. Tom likely underlines this because it is the hinge on which the entire book's argument turns, and because it has direct implications for what kind of remedy (if any) is possible.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is central to RC's theoretical stakes: if fragmentation is merely institutional, subsidiarity and institutional reform might suffice; if it is grounded in something more fundamental — rival and incommensurable rationalities — then RC must engage at the level of tradition, not just structure.

annotations.content: Vertical bracket on left margin with number '1' above and circled '2' below, connected by a downward arrow
annotations.anchor_text: _second dimension to their disagreements key concepts needed_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is visually distinguishing two dimensions or layers of disagreement that MacIntyre identifies on this page. The '1' likely refers to the first-order substantive disagreements (about what is right, just, etc.), while the circled '2' marks the second-order, more fundamental dimension: disagreement about rationality itself, characterized by incommensurability and untranslatability. The downward arrow indicates Tom is tracking a deepening or descent in the argument's structure.
annotations.rc_relevance: This structural annotation shows Tom mapping MacIntyre's layered analysis of disagreement — a distinction RC must take seriously when deciding whether its project of recovering civility operates at the level of substantive moral content or at the deeper level of competing rationalities.

annotations.content: incommensurability
annotations.anchor_text: _key concepts needed to characterize this second dimension_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this as a key technical term being formally introduced by MacIntyre. The highlighting (rather than underlining) suggests Tom is flagging it as a vocabulary item to track throughout the book, not just a claim to evaluate. Incommensurability is the thesis that rival systems share no common measure of rational adjudication.
annotations.rc_relevance: Incommensurability is one of the central conceptual challenges the RC project must either accept, contest, or navigate around — if rival moral traditions are truly incommensurable, the project of recovering shared civic discourse faces a deep philosophical obstacle.

annotations.content: untranslatability
annotations.anchor_text: _incommensurability and untranslatability I have_
annotations.interpretation: Highlighted alongside incommensurability as the paired key concept. Tom likely highlights both together because MacIntyre treats them as related but distinct — untranslatability concerns language and conceptual scheme, while incommensurability concerns standards of rational evaluation. Tom will want to track how MacIntyre distinguishes and relates them in what follows.
annotations.rc_relevance: Untranslatability poses a specific challenge for RC's ecumenical ambitions: if the language of classical virtue ethics and Thomistic enquiry cannot be translated into contemporary liberal moral discourse, the recovery project must decide whether to translate or to demand conversion to the tradition's own terms.

annotations.content: A.
annotations.anchor_text: _On the one hand are those who hold_
annotations.interpretation: Tom labels this paragraph 'A.' to mark it as the first of two opposed positions MacIntyre is about to sketch on incommensurability and untranslatability. This is Tom organizing the argument's bipolar structure — he anticipates (correctly) that a 'B.' position will follow. The labeling shows Tom reading analytically, extracting the logical skeleton of the argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: Identifying position A as the relativist-Kuhnian view is crucial for RC: MacIntyre will ultimately reject both A and its opponent in favor of a third, tradition-constituted view, and Tom is setting up that tripartite structure which maps onto RC's core 'encyclopaedist / genealogist / tradition' framework.

annotations.content: Relativism
annotations.anchor_text: _no independent standard or measure by appeal_
annotations.interpretation: Tom explicitly names the philosophical position MacIntyre is describing as 'Relativism' and attributes it to Kuhn with a downward arrow indicating derivation or exemplification. This shows Tom connecting MacIntyre's abstract characterization to its most famous scientific-philosophical source — Kuhn's thesis of paradigm incommensurability from 'The Structure of Scientific Revolutions.' The arrow may also suggest Tom sees Kuhn as downstream of a broader relativist tendency.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project's engagement with MacIntyre is precisely an attempt to move beyond both Enlightenment universalism and relativism; Tom's Kuhn annotation shows he is tracking MacIntyre's genealogy of the relativist position, which RC must name and respond to before it can argue for tradition-constituted rationality as a third way.

annotations.content: there is and can be no independent standard or measure by appeal to which their rival claims can be adjudicated, since each has internal to itself its own fundamental standard of judgment. Such systems are incommensurable
annotations.anchor_text: _no independent standard or measure by appeal_
annotations.interpretation: This is the core thesis of the relativist position (Position A), and Tom underlines it fully. The claim is that rival systems are self-sealing: each generates its own criteria of judgment internally, so there is no neutral external standpoint. Tom underlines this as the definitive formulation of the relativist horn of the dilemma MacIntyre is setting up — the position he will ultimately argue is incoherent.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the philosophical claim RC must directly contest or navigate: if moral traditions are self-sealing and incommensurable, there can be no rational basis for the recovery of shared civic or moral language. RC's Thomistic approach implicitly claims there is such a standard — natural law and practical reason — which makes this underlined passage a direct target of the project's constructive argument.

pages.summary: MacIntyre introduces two rival positions on incommensurability: those who hold that radically different systems of thought are untranslatable into one another's terms without distortion, and those (chiefly philosophers) who argue that incommensurability and untranslatability are always illusions — because recognizing an alien system as a rival already requires the capacity to translate it. MacIntyre then states that the lectures' aim is to show that admitting significant incommensurability need not make rational debate impossible; rather, such debate can reveal one standpoint as rationally superior on its own terms. The closing paragraph signals that in existing debates between opposing positions, resolution of first-order substantive differences has been indefinitely deferred — the problem the book sets out to address.

annotations.anchor_text: _untranslatability are always an illusion_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has numbered this position '3' and attributed it to Donald Davidson, the analytic philosopher whose arguments against the very intelligibility of conceptual schemes (notably in 'On the Very Idea of a Conceptual Scheme,' 1974) are the canonical philosophical case against incommensurability. The numeral '3' suggests Tom is tracking a numbered sequence of positions or thinkers across the introduction — likely cataloguing the 'encyclopaedist,' 'genealogist,' and 'tradition' camps, or perhaps a sub-list of philosophers within the anti-incommensurability camp. Davidson's inclusion here is significant: he is not a Thomist or virtue ethicist, so Tom may be noting where MacIntyre's target overlaps with mainstream analytic philosophy.
annotations.rc_relevance: Davidson represents the Enlightenment-rationalist assumption that a single, universal framework of rationality is always already available — precisely the 'encyclopaedist' confidence MacIntyre critiques. Flagging Davidson here connects directly to RC's engagement with MacIntyre's tripartite structure and the question of whether liberal modernity can even perceive rival traditions as genuinely rival.

annotations.content: historians of science
annotations.anchor_text: _held by some philosophers and historians of science_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this phrase to flag that the incommensurability thesis is not only a philosophical position but one with empirical/historical backing — Kuhn and Feyerabend are the obvious referents. By marking this, Tom is likely noting that MacIntyre is careful to give the incommensurability camp disciplinary breadth, making it harder to dismiss as merely speculative.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's interest in how traditions develop over time makes the philosophy-of-science dimension relevant: if paradigm shifts in science are incommensurable, analogous discontinuities in moral traditions (e.g., pre-modern to modern) carry serious weight for MacIntyre's historical account of moral fragmentation.

annotations.content: On the other hand are those, chiefly if not only philosophers
annotations.anchor_text: _On the other hand are those chiefly_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the transition marker 'On the other hand' and the qualifier 'chiefly if not only philosophers,' tracking the two-sided structure of the debate MacIntyre is setting up. The qualifier is philosophically loaded: MacIntyre is suggesting that only philosophers (not historians or anthropologists) tend to deny incommensurability — implying a certain abstractness or overconfidence in the philosophical tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: This maps onto RC's concern with the limits of purely rationalist, ahistorical approaches to moral enquiry — the 'encyclopaedist' mode that assumes universal translatability and misses the formative role of particular traditions and communities.

annotations.content: untranslatability are always an illusion
annotations.anchor_text: _incommensurability and untranslatability are always an illusion_
annotations.interpretation: This is the thesis statement of the anti-incommensurability camp, and Tom marks it as a precise philosophical claim to hold onto. The word 'always' is doing heavy lifting — it is a universal claim that MacIntyre will spend the book complicating. Tom likely marks this to track the exact scope of the position being contested.
annotations.rc_relevance: If untranslatability is always an illusion, then every tradition is always already accessible to a neutral rational observer — a view that undermines MacIntyre's (and RC's) argument that traditions have internal standards of rationality that cannot be judged from outside without distortion.

annotations.content: capacity to translate its terms and idioms into one's own
annotations.anchor_text: _always requires a capacity to translate its terms_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the anti-incommensurabilist's key argumentative move: that recognizing a rival system presupposes the ability to translate it, which presupposes commensurability. This is the Davidson-style transcendental argument — you cannot even identify something as a rival without already understanding it in your own terms. Tom is likely flagging this as the crux he will want to test against MacIntyre's response.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the precise philosophical hinge on which RC's account of tradition-to-tradition dialogue turns: can two traditions genuinely engage without one absorbing the other? MacIntyre's answer (that a tradition can learn a 'second first language') is the alternative Tom is building toward in the RC framework.

annotations.content: so bare an outline does a gross injustice
annotations.anchor_text: _To have sketched these two positions in so bare_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks MacIntyre's self-deprecating acknowledgment that his summary is reductive. This is a scholarly honesty marker — MacIntyre is signaling that the real argument lies in the complexity he has not yet shown. Tom may be noting this as a methodological move: MacIntyre is managing reader expectations and pre-empting the objection that his two-camp framing is a straw man.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this matters because the project must avoid the same trap — reducing complex traditions (Thomism, liberalism, genealogy) to caricatures. MacIntyre's self-awareness here models the kind of intellectual honesty RC aspires to in engaging rival positions.

annotations.content: the brevity of this exposition might lead some readers to conclude that
annotations.anchor_text: _More particularly the brevity of this exposition_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's anticipation of a specific misreading — that incommensurability entails the impossibility of rational debate. This is a meta-argumentative move: MacIntyre is flagging what his thesis is NOT before stating what it IS. Tom is tracking the argumentative architecture carefully, noting where MacIntyre pre-empts objections.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC must make a similar move: acknowledging that recovering pre-modern traditions of civility and virtue might appear to require rejecting modern rationality wholesale, when in fact the project argues for a more nuanced engagement across difference.

annotations.content: rational debate between the adherents of those two systems would be to that same degree rendered impossible
annotations.anchor_text: _rational debate between the adherents of those two systems_
annotations.interpretation: This is the mistaken conclusion MacIntyre is about to refute, and Tom marks it precisely because it is the negative thesis — the view MacIntyre's lectures are designed to contest. By marking the exact wording, Tom is setting up a contrast with the positive thesis that follows. The phrase 'to that same degree' is important: it assumes a direct proportionality between incommensurability and rational impossibility that MacIntyre denies.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's civic friendship theme: if incommensurability made rational debate impossible, cross-tradition civic friendship would be impossible. MacIntyre's refutation of this is thus foundational to RC's argument that genuine moral community can exist across difference.

annotations.content: But these lectures have as one of their aims to show that this is not the case, that an admission of significant incommensurability and untranslatability in the relations between two opposed systems of thought and practice can be a prologue not only to rational debate, but to that kind of debate from which one party can emerge as undoubtedly rationally superior
annotations.anchor_text: _But these lectures have as one of their aims_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets the positive thesis statement of the entire book — the claim that incommensurability is not a barrier but a prologue to rational debate, and that such debate can yield a verdict of rational superiority. The bracket signals this is a load-bearing passage Tom will return to repeatedly. The phrase 'prologue not only to rational debate' is MacIntyre at his most programmatic, and the claim that one party can emerge 'undoubtedly rationally superior' is audacious and contestable — Tom is almost certainly flagging it for critical scrutiny.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the architectonic claim of the RC project's MacIntyrean foundation: traditions are not hermetically sealed, and engagement between them — including the Thomistic tradition and its modern rivals — can produce genuine rational progress. RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry depends on this claim being defensible.

annotations.content: see Whose Justice? Which Rationality? chapters XVII, XVIII, and XIX
annotations.anchor_text: _rationally superior see Whose Justice Which Rationality_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the cross-reference to MacIntyre's previous book, signaling awareness that this argument is continued from prior work. Tom is likely tracking the intertextual architecture of MacIntyre's project — Three Rival Versions is the third major work after After Virtue and Whose Justice? Which Rationality?, and the argument about rational superiority across traditions is developed there.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this cross-reference confirms that MacIntyre's account of tradition-constituted rationality is a sustained, book-length argument, not an incidental claim. RC must engage with all three works to build a credible Thomistic framework for civic renewal.

annotations.content: asterisk (*)
annotations.anchor_text: _to remark how in the debate between the two principal opposing positions_
annotations.interpretation: A lone asterisk in the right margin beside the closing paragraph flags it as important for cross-referencing — likely marking a passage Tom wants to connect to a note, another text, or a specific RC argument. The asterisk without annotation suggests Tom intended to return to this passage or link it to an external source not yet identified at the time of reading.
annotations.rc_relevance: The flagged passage concerns the deferral of first-order substantive resolution in moral debate — directly relevant to RC's diagnosis of contemporary moral fragmentation as a failure to engage with substantive ethical differences rather than merely procedural disagreements.

annotations.content: in the debate between the two principal opposing positions
annotations.anchor_text: _remark how in the debate between the two principal_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks this phrase and appears to have bracketed 'in the debate' with vertical lines, suggesting he is isolating the locus of the problem MacIntyre is diagnosing — it is within the debate itself, not outside it, that resolution fails. Tom may be noting that MacIntyre is making a sociological as much as a philosophical observation about how actual intellectual debates proceed.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with civic friendship and institutional formation of character is relevant here: if debate between positions chronically defers substantive resolution, this points to a failure of the intellectual institutions (universities, journals, public discourse) that are supposed to adjudicate — a MacIntyrean critique RC must address.

annotations.content: the resolution of first-order differences on the substantive questions has been, so it seems, indefinitely deferred
annotations.anchor_text: _resolution of first-order differences on the substantive questions_
annotations.interpretation: This is Tom marking MacIntyre's diagnosis of the central pathology of modern moral philosophy: not that people disagree, but that the disagreements are never resolved because the debate never reaches the substantive (first-order) level. The phrase 'so it seems' is MacIntyre hedging — Tom may be noting this hedge as philosophically significant, leaving open whether the deferral is contingent or structural.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the precise problem RC aims to address: contemporary social fragmentation is sustained by the indefinite deferral of substantive moral questions in favor of procedural consensus. RC's recovery of Thomistic enquiry is proposed as a way to re-engage first-order questions about justice, virtue, and the good — making this sentence a foundational diagnostic claim for the entire project.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that debates between rival traditions of moral enquiry multiply into second-order disagreements about how to interpret disagreements, rendering direct engagement sterile. He identifies that the apparent theoretical impasse is not purely intellectual but is also sustained by institutional arrangements and social habits—particularly within the contemporary university—which reinforce the very philosophical commitments that prevent resolution. This institutional entrenchment produces ineliminable, continuing divisions across all humanistic disciplines.

annotations.content: Asterisk (*) in upper left margin area
annotations.anchor_text: _rival proposals concerning how debate should proceed_
annotations.interpretation: The asterisk signals that Tom has identified this moment as a key structural node in MacIntyre's argument — the point where disagreement becomes self-referential and recursive. Tom is likely flagging the epistemological trap MacIntyre is describing: you cannot escape the meta-level problem by appealing to first-order debate, because the meta-level is itself contested. This may also mark a passage he intends to cite or return to.
annotations.rc_relevance: This recursive disagreement structure is directly relevant to RC's engagement with MacIntyre's genealogist/encyclopaedist/tradition tripartite: each tradition not only disagrees about conclusions but about the very standards of rational adjudication, making institutional or civic recovery all the more urgent.

annotations.content: But also
annotations.anchor_text: _not only from the variety of stances on issues of incommensurability_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is pushing back on or extending MacIntyre's causal account. MacIntyre says the inability to reopen fundamental issues derives 'not only from' stances on incommensurability and untranslatability — and Tom's 'But also' suggests he wants to add another causal factor, possibly institutional habits (anticipated in the next paragraph) or something from outside the text such as failures of civic friendship or the absence of shared moral formation. The note sits precisely at the pivot point of MacIntyre's incomplete sentence, indicating Tom caught the rhetorical 'not only… but also' structure.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with institutional character-formation is directly implicated here: Tom seems to be pre-empting MacIntyre's own next move (institutional arrangements) and possibly extending it toward subsidiarity or the loss of local moral communities as additional causes of the impasse.

annotations.content: premature attempts at debate between fundamentally opposed positions on fundamental issues are rendered sterile
annotations.anchor_text: _premature attempts at debate between fundamentally opposed_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's core diagnostic claim about the Edinburgh and Yale encounters. The underlining suggests he sees this as a thesis-level statement, not merely illustrative. The word 'premature' is doing heavy work — MacIntyre is not saying debate is impossible, but that it has been attempted without the prior groundwork of tradition-internal rational development. Tom may be asking: what would non-premature engagement look like?
annotations.rc_relevance: This is central to RC's argument that civility cannot be recovered through direct confrontation of opposing positions without shared moral vocabulary — the project must first recover the tradition-internal resources (Thomistic enquiry, virtue ethics) that make genuine dialogue possible.

annotations.content: a large inability to use what was learned in such discussions in a way that could immediately reopen fundamental issues
annotations.anchor_text: _large inability to use what was learned_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks this as a precise description of intellectual failure — not ignorance, but an inability to translate local debate gains into traction on deeper questions. This suggests Tom is tracking MacIntyre's distinction between productive detail-work and fundamental-issue resolution. The underlining may also signal frustration or recognition: this inability is what RC is trying to address by going back to foundational traditions.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery project is implicitly an answer to this inability — by returning to Thomistic and classical sources, the project attempts to develop the kind of tradition-formed rationality that can actually reopen rather than merely rehearse fundamental debates.

annotations.content: It is always important not to confuse the consequences of intellectual positions with those of institutional arrangements. What appears to be an impasse resulting from the theoretical commitments of those involved in debate may sometimes, in part at least, be one brought about by institutional arrangements and social habits.
annotations.anchor_text: _consequences of intellectual positions with those of institutional arrangements_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets this entire passage, marking it as a structurally pivotal argument. MacIntyre's move here — distinguishing intellectual from institutional causes of impasse — is the key analytical lever of the page. The bracket suggests Tom sees this as quotable and foundational. He may be planning to use this distinction to critique universities or professional academic culture in the RC project.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is one of the most RC-relevant passages in the image: it directly supports RC's argument that social fragmentation is not merely a philosophical problem but an institutional one, and that recovering civility requires attending to the formative power of institutions — a core subsidiarity and character-formation theme.

annotations.content: institutional arrangements and social habits
annotations.anchor_text: _brought about by institutional arrangements and social habits_
annotations.interpretation: A secondary underline within the bracketed section, isolating the key causal pair. Tom is flagging the dyad — 'arrangements' (structural) and 'habits' (dispositional/virtue-language) — as especially important. The use of 'habits' here resonates with Aristotelian/Thomistic vocabulary of habituation, and Tom likely recognizes MacIntyre is sneaking virtue-ethics language into an ostensibly institutional analysis.
annotations.rc_relevance: The term 'habits' here connects directly to RC's interest in how institutions form character — a bridge between MacIntyre's institutional critique and Aristotelian ethics of habituation that underpins the RC framework.

annotations.content: this is particularly important to remember when the effects of these latter reinforce attitudes stemming from and defined by theoretical and philosophical commitments—as is the case in the university today.
annotations.anchor_text: _reinforce attitudes stemming from and defined by theoretical and philosophical commitments_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's claim that institutional and philosophical causes are not merely parallel but mutually reinforcing — a feedback loop. 'As is the case in the university today' is the polemical punch, and Tom's underlining suggests he agrees with or wants to foreground this critique of the contemporary university. This is MacIntyre at his most diagnostic about institutional failure.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with the university as a site of moral formation (or deformation) is directly addressed here. MacIntyre's claim that the university today reinforces philosophical fragmentation rather than resolving it supports RC's argument for alternative or reformed institutional contexts for moral enquiry.

annotations.content: On Contemp. Universities: endless debate in all humanities
annotations.anchor_text: _more striking in the contemporary university than the extent of the apparently ineliminable continuing divisions_
annotations.interpretation: Tom summarizes MacIntyre's empirical claim in his own compressed phrase. 'Endless debate in all humanities' is Tom's gloss on 'ineliminable continuing divisions and conflicts within all humanistic enquiry.' The note shows Tom is reading this not as abstract philosophy but as a concrete institutional diagnosis. The word 'endless' carries a note of exasperation or irony — Tom may be drawing on his own experience of academic culture.
annotations.rc_relevance: This annotation directly maps onto RC's critique of contemporary academic culture as incapable of sustaining genuine moral enquiry — the 'endless debate' without resolution is exactly the condition RC seeks to diagnose and remedy through recovery of tradition-rooted rationality.

annotations.content: what is striking about all these continuing divisions is the extent to which,
annotations.anchor_text: _what is striking about all these continuing divisions is the extent to which_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the opening of MacIntyre's concluding observation on the page, which trails into the next page. The highlight signals that Tom wants to track MacIntyre's answer to his own rhetorical question — what exactly is striking about the divisions? The highlight is incomplete (the sentence continues), suggesting Tom is marking it as a thread to follow into the next page.
annotations.rc_relevance: This cliffhanger observation likely leads into MacIntyre's core thesis about tradition-constituted rationality, which is the philosophical backbone of RC's argument that moral enquiry is only intelligible within a living tradition.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that academic debate between fundamentally opposed moral standpoints is inevitably inconclusive: each position appears irrefutable to its own adherents and refutable to its opponents, with no shared rational standard capable of adjudicating between them. He finds it ironic that secular humanistic disciplines reproduce the same condition of incommensurability that their nineteenth-century predecessors used to dismiss theology as unacademic. He then begins enumerating four characteristics of contemporary humanities universities: (1) high technical skill on narrow questions, (2) promulgation of mutually incompatible large doctrines, (3) inconclusive public debate between those doctrines, and (4) a fourth characteristic cut off at the page break.

annotations.content: elect/drinkin paradigms...
annotations.anchor_text: _adherents of each point of view tend_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is almost certainly writing 'electi[ng] paradigms' or possibly abbreviating 'eclectic paradigms' — flagging MacIntyre's implicit point that scholars self-select into paradigm communities, discussing only with those who already share their framework. The ellipsis suggests Tom is connecting this to a broader idea he hasn't fully articulated yet, possibly Kuhn's notion of paradigm-community insularity, which MacIntyre draws on throughout the book.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's concern with civic friendship and the conditions of genuine dialogue: if partisans only converse with co-believers, the prerequisites for a shared civic discourse — and for MacIntyre's tradition-constituted rationality — are absent.

annotations.content: adherents of each point of view tend to discuss in any depth only with those with whom they are already in fundamental agreement
annotations.anchor_text: _adherents of each point of view tend to discuss_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as the diagnostic core of the epistemic problem MacIntyre is naming: intellectual insularity as a structural feature of modern academic culture, not a personal failing. This is a claim about the sociology of knowledge that underpins everything else on the page.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly illuminates the RC theme of social fragmentation: the incapacity for genuine cross-tradition dialogue is the condition RC seeks to remedy through recovering shared standards of civic and moral enquiry.

annotations.content: One consequence is
annotations.anchor_text: _One consequence is that within each partisan_
annotations.interpretation: Circling 'One consequence' flags this as a structural hinge in MacIntyre's argument — he is moving from the sociological observation about who talks to whom, to its epistemological consequence (partisan camps develop internal pseudo-consensus). Tom is tracking the logical chain carefully, likely because he wants to use this inferential structure in his own writing.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this 'consequence' is the mechanism by which traditions calcify into ideological camps rather than remaining living, self-correcting enquiries — precisely the degeneration MacIntyre's Thomistic alternative is meant to resist.

annotations.content: rough and ready agreement as to what weight is to be assigned to different types of reason in different types of context
annotations.anchor_text: _rough and ready agreement as to what weight_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this phrase because 'rough and ready agreement' is doing significant work: it names a kind of practical, intra-camp consensus that is not rationally grounded but functionally operative. This is MacIntyre's point that camps have internal standards but no external ones — a form of coherentism without foundations.
annotations.rc_relevance: Connects to RC's MacIntyre thread on tradition-constituted rationality: the contrast between this 'rough and ready' internal coherence and the genuine rational justification that a living tradition can in principle provide is central to MacIntyre's whole project.

annotations.content: there is no general academic consensus on this within whole disciplines, let alone between disciplines
annotations.anchor_text: _no general academic consensus on this within whole disciplines_
annotations.interpretation: This underline marks the negative claim that complements the previous one: if internal camp consensus exists but cross-disciplinary consensus does not, the university is a collection of warring enclaves rather than a unified intellectual community. Tom seems to be building a picture of institutional fragmentation.
annotations.rc_relevance: Speaks directly to RC's concern with the role of institutions in forming character: a university that cannot achieve internal consensus about standards of reason cannot perform its formative function — it models fragmentation rather than civic virtue.

annotations.content: Yet at the same time there is an equally rough and ready general academic consensus, both within and between disciplines, as to what is to be accounted as at least some sort of relevant reason for upholding or advancing any particular conclusion
annotations.anchor_text: _Yet at the same time there is an equally rough_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this apparent concession by MacIntyre — that there IS a minimal shared standard — possibly because it creates a tension with the previous claim. MacIntyre is carefully distinguishing between a thin procedural consensus (what counts as 'some reason') and a thick substantive consensus (what counts as decisive reason). Tom may be flagging this as a possible opening for the RC project: this thin consensus is what civic discourse can build on.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the seed of hope within MacIntyre's bleak diagnosis — the thin consensus may be the residue of a once-thicker tradition. RC could argue this residue points toward the possibility of recovering shared standards of civic argument.

annotations.content: Arrow/greater-than symbol ('>') pointing toward the text
annotations.anchor_text: _Yet at the same time there is an equally rough and ready_
annotations.interpretation: The arrow in the right margin pointing at the 'Yet' concession signals Tom marking this as a particularly important turn in the argument. It may indicate he wants to return to this passage, or that it marks a crux he intends to cite. The arrow distinguishes this from the surrounding underlines as especially significant.
annotations.rc_relevance: The concession MacIntyre makes here — that some shared standard persists — is the narrow ground on which RC's recovery project must stand. Tom's arrow likely marks this as an anchor point for his own argument.

annotations.content: Each warring position characteristically appears irrefutable to its own adherents; indeed in its own terms and by its own standards of argument it is in practice irrefutable. But each warring position equally seems to its opponents to be insufficiently warranted by rational argument.
annotations.anchor_text: _Each warring position characteristically appears irrefutable_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this entire passage in pink, marking it as the sharpest formulation of MacIntyre's incommensurability thesis. The symmetry MacIntyre builds — irrefutable from inside, unwarranted from outside — is the philosophical heart of the book's problem. Tom likely intends to quote or paraphrase this directly in RC writing.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the precise epistemic condition that RC must diagnose before it can prescribe: the mutual irrefutability of rival moral positions is what makes the recovery of a common tradition — rather than mere procedural tolerance — necessary and urgent.

annotations.content: It is ironic that the wholly secular humanistic disciplines of the late twentieth century should thus reproduce that very same condition which led their nineteenth-century secularizing predecessors to dismiss the claim of theology to be worthy of the status of an academic discipline.
annotations.anchor_text: _It is ironic that the wholly secular humanistic disciplines_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets this irony passage, which is one of MacIntyre's most pointed rhetorical moves: secular modernity accused theology of irrationality, but has now generated the same irrationality within itself. The bracket suggests Tom sees this as a structurally important argument — possibly one he will use polemically in RC to challenge secular assumptions about rationality.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's engagement with tradition vs. reform: MacIntyre rehabilitates theology as an intellectual tradition not by special pleading but by showing that the secular disciplines are no better off. This levels the playing field for RC's Thomistic recovery project.

annotations.content: theology to be worthy of the status of an academic discipline
annotations.anchor_text: _theology to be worthy of the status of an academic_
annotations.interpretation: The pink highlight specifically on 'theology' within the already-bracketed passage suggests Tom is isolating this term as especially significant — perhaps because RC's project involves taking seriously pre-modern theological frameworks that secular academia would dismiss. This is a moment of explicit disciplinary rehabilitation.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, the rehabilitation of theology as a legitimate mode of enquiry is not incidental but structural: MacIntyre's Thomistic framework, which RC draws on, requires that theological reasoning be recognized as rationally serious rather than pre-rational.

annotations.content: ①
annotations.anchor_text: _remarkably high level of skill in handling narrow questions_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles the numeral '1' (which appears to be his own addition or a printed one he is activating) to mark the first of MacIntyre's four characteristics of the modern humanities university. Tom is tracking the enumerated structure of MacIntyre's diagnosis, likely because this four-part taxonomy will be important for RC's institutional critique.
annotations.rc_relevance: The four characteristics together constitute MacIntyre's account of institutional deformation — how universities that should form rational enquirers instead produce technically skilled but morally directionless graduates. This speaks directly to RC's theme of institutions and character formation.

annotations.content: a remarkably high level of skill in handling narrow questions of limited detail
annotations.anchor_text: _There is first a remarkably high level of skill_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines characteristic (1) — technical skill on narrow questions — likely because it captures a recognizable and damning feature of contemporary academic life. The implicit critique is that universities have elevated technical competence while evacuating substantive moral or metaphysical judgment.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's concern with virtue and character: technical skill without moral formation is precisely the condition that classical traditions of civility were designed to overcome. The university as skills factory is antithetical to the university as community of enquiry.

annotations.content: Secondly
annotations.anchor_text: _Secondly in a way which sometimes provides a direction_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'Secondly' to mark characteristic (2) in MacIntyre's taxonomy, consistent with his circling of ① above. He is building a visual map of the argument's enumerated structure. The circle functions as an organizational tool — Tom is preparing to use this four-part structure analytically.
annotations.rc_relevance: Characteristic (2) — the promulgation of large, mutually incompatible doctrines — is the RC-relevant diagnosis of ideological pluralism without rational adjudication, which is exactly the condition MacIntyre's tradition-constituted rationality is meant to address.

annotations.content: promulgation of a number of large and mutually incompatible doctrines often conveyed by indirection and implication, the doctrines which define the major contending standpoints in each discipline
annotations.anchor_text: _promulgation of a number of large and mutually incompatible_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as the key content of characteristic (2). 'Conveyed by indirection and implication' is particularly significant — MacIntyre is pointing out that these doctrines are not openly argued for but smuggled in through academic practice. Tom likely sees this as a critique of academic bad faith relevant to RC's concern with transparent civic argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: The concealment of foundational commitments 'by indirection' is the opposite of the transparent, tradition-acknowledging enquiry MacIntyre advocates and RC seeks to model. RC's project requires making these hidden doctrinal commitments explicit and debatable.

annotations.content: Thirdly
annotations.anchor_text: _Thirdly insofar as the warfare between these doctrines_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'Thirdly' to mark characteristic (3), continuing his structural annotation of MacIntyre's four-part taxonomy. The consistent circling of the ordinal markers shows Tom is treating this taxonomy as a framework he intends to reproduce or respond to systematically.
annotations.rc_relevance: Characteristic (3) — that public debate between contending doctrines is governed by shared standards that make all debate inconclusive — is the most directly relevant to RC's diagnosis of contemporary civic discourse: civility breaks down not from incivility but from incommensurability.

annotations.content: the shared standards of argument are such that all debate is inconclusive
annotations.anchor_text: _the shared standards of argument are such that all debate_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this as the culminating statement of characteristic (3) and possibly the most important single sentence on the page for RC purposes. 'Shared standards that produce inconclusive debate' is MacIntyre's paradox: the standards are shared enough to enable debate but not robust enough to resolve it. This is the formal description of contemporary liberal discourse.
annotations.rc_relevance: This sentence is the RC project's starting problem in concentrated form: if shared standards of civic argument guarantee inconclusiveness, then recovering civility requires not just better manners but a deeper recovery of the substantive tradition that once gave shared standards their resolving power.

pages.summary: MacIntyre reflects on his experience delivering the Gifford Lectures at both Edinburgh and Yale, noting a persistent institutional reluctance to acknowledge that divisions between intellectual traditions run so deep as to constitute genuinely rival conceptions of rationality. He argues that what the lectures revealed was that the central question — what rationality itself is — cannot be resolved by appeal to any neutral standpoint, and that even the goal of presenting an 'overall view' of the contending positions cannot secure general assent. The conclusion he draws is that such lectures must aim not at agreement but at rendering disagreements more constructive, which is the explicit purpose of the published book.
