annotations.content: we still behave for the most part as if the university did still constitute a single, tolerably unified intellectual community, a form of behavior which testifies to the enduring effects of the encyclopaedists' conception of the unity of enquiry.
annotations.anchor_text: _tolerably unified intellectual community enduring effects encyclopaedists_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets this opening passage, likely flagging MacIntyre's diagnosis of a persistent Enlightenment hangover in modern academic institutions — the fiction of unified rational enquiry. This is not merely descriptive for Tom; the bracket suggests he sees this as a foundational claim about how institutions misrepresent their own epistemological situation. He may be tracking the 'encyclopaedist' pole of the tripartite structure as it manifests in institutional behavior rather than just philosophical doctrine.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly maps onto RC's concern with how institutions shape (or distort) moral formation — universities here are cast as still operating under encyclopaedist assumptions about neutral rationality, which RC identifies as a root cause of contemporary civility's collapse.

annotations.content: to allow that our divisions run so deep that we confront the claims of genuinely rival conceptions of rationality
annotations.anchor_text: _divisions run so deep genuinely rival conceptions rationality_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this phrase as the core diagnostic claim of the passage — that the reluctance MacIntyre identifies is specifically a reluctance to admit incommensurability at the level of rationality itself, not merely disagreement within a shared framework. This is the hinge on which MacIntyre's whole project turns, and Tom's underlining suggests he recognizes it as such. The phrase 'genuinely rival' is likely being tracked as a technical term across the book.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's engagement with MacIntyre centers precisely on this point: if rival traditions have genuinely incommensurable rationalities, then civility cannot be grounded in Enlightenment-style procedural neutrality — it must be recovered from within a tradition, which is the Thomistic move RC wants to make.

annotations.content: the question of what rationality is
annotations.anchor_text: _discussions made plain was that nothing less than_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles or underlines this phrase as a key term — MacIntyre is elevating the question of rationality's nature to the status of the primary subject matter of the humanities. Tom likely marks this because it reframes what academic enquiry is actually about, and because this question is precisely what the three rival versions (encyclopaedia, genealogy, tradition) answer differently.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this is the meta-level question beneath debates about civility: different answers to 'what is rationality?' yield different accounts of what civil discourse can be and whether moral community is possible.

annotations.content: A)
annotations.anchor_text: _not only to present and to argue for one particular point of view_
annotations.interpretation: Tom labels this 'A)' to mark the first of two stated aims MacIntyre had for the lectures — presenting and arguing for one particular point of view. The use of a lettered list suggests Tom is structuring MacIntyre's argument analytically, preparing to evaluate each aim separately. This is a scholarly habit of tracking argumentative structure for later critique or cross-reference.
annotations.rc_relevance: The distinction between arguing for a position (A) and providing an overview (B) is relevant to RC's methodology: RC similarly must decide whether it is advocating for Thomistic tradition or charting the terrain for a broader audience.

annotations.content: B)
annotations.anchor_text: _but also to give something at least of an overall view_
annotations.interpretation: Tom labels 'B)' as the second aim — providing an overall view of the contending parties and the terrain of conflict. Paired with 'A)', Tom is mapping the dual ambition of the lectures, which MacIntyre then admits partially failed. The arrow pointing to the passage about 'cannot be achieved in any way likely to secure general assent' suggests Tom connects B) directly to MacIntyre's confession of limitation — the overall view was the aspiration that proved most resistant to success.
annotations.rc_relevance: This tension between advocacy and overview is central to RC's own project design: can one simultaneously champion a tradition-based account of civility and offer a fair map of rival positions? MacIntyre's honest admission of difficulty here is a methodological warning RC must heed.

annotations.content: Arrow (left margin, pointing right toward text) connecting the 'B)' label to the underlined phrase about 'cannot be achieved in any way likely to secure general assent'
annotations.anchor_text: _the degree to which and the reasons why this too cannot be achieved_
annotations.interpretation: The arrow visually links Tom's 'B)' structural label to MacIntyre's admission that the goal of providing an overall view cannot secure general assent. Tom is not just noting the claim but explicitly connecting MacIntyre's stated aim (B) to its acknowledged failure. This is a critical annotation — Tom may be questioning whether the tripartite framework itself is the right vehicle for 'overall view,' or whether MacIntyre is being appropriately humble or unnecessarily defeatist.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this is a crucial methodological moment: if even MacIntyre cannot achieve a view of the terrain that gains general assent, RC must be clear about whether it is attempting persuasion within a tradition or genuine cross-traditional dialogue — and whether those are even distinguishable.

annotations.content: the degree to which and the reasons why this too cannot be achieved in any way likely to secure general assent
annotations.anchor_text: _what I learned to understand better from their reception_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as MacIntyre's most candid epistemological concession in the introduction — that the meta-level project of mapping rival traditions is itself tradition-dependent and cannot escape the very problem it describes. This is philosophically significant: the observer is not outside the conflict. Tom likely marks this because it is the point where MacIntyre's humility becomes methodologically decisive.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly challenges any RC aspiration to speak from a neutral vantage point about civility's recovery. It reinforces that RC must be explicit about speaking from within the Thomistic tradition rather than pretending to adjudicate from above.

pages.summary: MacIntyre opens by framing his Gifford Lectures around the question of what fidelity to Adam Gifford's actual intentions would require, arguing that Gifford's will — a document from a culturally alien milieu — demands that theology be treated as a strictly natural science, comparable to astronomy or chemistry. He notes that early lecturers like Muller and Caird shared enough of Gifford's cultural presuppositions to engage this seriously, but that later lecturers largely ignored the question because genuine fidelity would have been intellectually embarrassing. The key criterion Muller and Caird identified for natural science status is that its history exhibits rational progress in enquiry — a standard MacIntyre will use to evaluate rival traditions of moral enquiry throughout the book.

annotations.content: fidelity
annotations.anchor_text: _the question of what fidelity to Adam Gifford's_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has isolated 'fidelity' as a load-bearing term. MacIntyre is not asking what Gifford's will says on its surface, but what genuine fidelity to its intentions would demand — a hermeneutical question about how we stand in relation to a tradition not our own. Tom is likely flagging this because 'fidelity' implies an obligation that goes beyond nominal compliance, pointing toward the problem of how one inherits and enacts a tradition across cultural distance.
annotations.rc_relevance: Fidelity to tradition is a central RC tension: how do contemporary actors authentically recover and enact classical or early modern norms of civility without merely performing them? This annotation marks the exact point where MacIntyre's method — taking a tradition's own standards seriously — mirrors what RC must do with Thomistic and civic humanist sources.

annotations.content: cultural milieu sufficiently alien to our own
annotations.anchor_text: _will is a document from a cultural milieu_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the phrase marking cultural distance as the core epistemological problem. MacIntyre is signaling that Gifford's presuppositions cannot simply be read off the surface of his text — they require recovery across a cultural gap. Tom is almost certainly connecting this to MacIntyre's broader argument that traditions are not self-interpreting and that understanding requires entry into a form of life.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly speaks to RC's challenge of recovering classical civility: the tradition of civic friendship and virtue ethics is also from a 'sufficiently alien' milieu, and the question of how to close that gap without distortion is fundamental to the project's methodology.

annotations.content: they were participants with him in a common culture
annotations.anchor_text: _shared to some large degree Adam Gifford's presuppositions just because_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks this as explaining why early lecturers could engage Gifford's intentions authentically — shared cultural participation made fidelity relatively unproblematic. The underline signals Tom's attention to the condition of possibility for genuine tradition-transmission: you must share enough of the background to even understand what fidelity requires. This sets up the contrast with later lecturers who could not or would not do the same.
annotations.rc_relevance: This speaks directly to MacIntyre's concept of tradition-constituted rationality and the RC theme of civic friendship — genuine moral community requires a sufficiently shared form of life. The fragmentation RC addresses is precisely the loss of this common participation.

annotations.content: to do otherwise than to ignore them would have been embarrassing
annotations.anchor_text: _attention paid to Adam Gifford's intentions has commonly been at best cursory perhaps because_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks MacIntyre's sharp diagnostic claim: the reason Gifford's intentions were ignored is not ignorance but intellectual embarrassment — fidelity would have exposed the gap between what the lecturers were doing and what Gifford required. Tom is likely flagging this as an instance of MacIntyre's rhetorical strategy of indicting the modern academy for bad faith rather than mere error, which is characteristic of his genealogical-critical mode.
annotations.rc_relevance: This maps onto RC's concern with institutional bad faith — institutions that nominally carry forward traditions of civility and virtue while actually hollowing them out. MacIntyre's critique of the Gifford Lectures as a case study in this hollowing-out resonates with RC's analysis of how contemporary institutions fail to form character.

annotations.content: wrote Adam Gifford in his will
annotations.anchor_text: _I wish wrote Adam Gifford in his will the lecturers_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the evidentiary moment — MacIntyre goes to the primary source, the will itself. This is methodologically significant: MacIntyre is modeling the practice of returning ad fontes rather than relying on received interpretation. Tom may be marking this as an example of the kind of careful tradition-recovery he wants RC to practice.
annotations.rc_relevance: The move to primary sources over received tradition tracks RC's commitment to recovering original texts of civic virtue rather than accepting modern paraphrases, mirroring MacIntyre's own philological-historical method.

annotations.content: strictly natural science
annotations.anchor_text: _the lecturers to treat their subject as a strictly natural science_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines Gifford's own programmatic phrase, which is the crux of the embarrassment MacIntyre has just described. Treating natural theology as a 'strictly natural science' is a claim that would have required later lecturers to submit their work to criteria of rational progress — criteria most were not prepared to meet. Tom is tracking the exact phrase that defines the standard of enquiry MacIntyre will use throughout the book.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to MacIntyre's encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition tripartite structure: the encyclopaedist tradition claims natural science as its model. MacIntyre is asking whether moral and theological enquiry can genuinely meet that standard — a question RC must also face when claiming to recover a tradition of virtue with normative force.

annotations.content: just as astronomy or chemistry is
annotations.anchor_text: _I wish it to be considered just as astronomy or chemistry is_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines Gifford's comparator sciences — astronomy and chemistry — which MacIntyre will use as benchmarks for what rational progress in enquiry looks like. By marking this, Tom is noting the specific empirical sciences Gifford chose, which are notably progressive and cumulative rather than interpretive, setting a very high bar for moral enquiry.
annotations.rc_relevance: The comparison to astronomy and chemistry raises the question of whether traditions of moral enquiry — including the Thomistic tradition RC draws on — can exhibit analogous rational progress. This is central to MacIntyre's defense of tradition-constituted rationality as a genuine form of rational enquiry, not mere dogma.

annotations.content: Both clearly took it to be one mark of a natural science that its history is one of rational progress in enquiry
annotations.anchor_text: _Both clearly took it to be one mark of a natural science_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets the passage identifying 'rational progress in enquiry' as the key criterion for natural science status. This is the theoretical pivot of the chapter: MacIntyre is extracting from Muller and Caird's responses the criterion by which moral enquiry will be evaluated throughout the book. The bracket suggests Tom sees this as a structurally crucial moment — the definition that everything else hangs on.
annotations.rc_relevance: The criterion of 'rational progress in enquiry' is directly tied to MacIntyre's defense of the Thomistic tradition as a genuine rational tradition, not mere fideism. RC's claim that classical traditions of civility offer resources for contemporary social repair depends on a similar argument that these traditions embody accumulated rational wisdom, not just cultural preference.

annotations.content: Arrow (>) pointing right in the right margin beside the bracketed passage about rational progress
annotations.anchor_text: _one mark of a natural science that its history is one of rational progress_
annotations.interpretation: The right-pointing arrow beside the bracketed passage signals that Tom wants to carry this idea forward — either to a later passage in the book or to his own notes. It is a cross-referencing device indicating this criterion will recur. Tom may be anticipating MacIntyre's later application of this standard to the three rival traditions, or flagging it for use in RC argumentation.
annotations.rc_relevance: The arrow suggests Tom is tracking 'rational progress in enquiry' as a key RC touchstone — the idea that a living tradition is one that makes rational progress over time rather than merely repeating inherited formulas, which is precisely what RC must argue about the tradition of civility it seeks to recover.

annotations.content: astronomy and chemistry have both exhibited continuous progress
annotations.anchor_text: _in the hundred years which have elapsed since Adam Gifford's death astronomy and chemistry_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's empirical claim that the benchmark sciences have in fact met the standard of continuous progress over the century since Gifford's death. This sets up the implicit contrast: has moral or theological enquiry done the same? Tom is noting the rhetorical structure — MacIntyre concedes the empirical sciences their progress precisely to sharpen the question about whether tradition-constituted moral enquiry can claim analogous progress.
annotations.rc_relevance: This bears on RC's claim that classical traditions of civility are not merely antiquarian but represent genuine accumulated wisdom. If the tradition cannot show something analogous to rational progress, it is vulnerable to the charge that it is simply nostalgia — a charge RC must answer.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that despite Adam Gifford's mandate for natural theology to encompass enquiry into the foundations of ethics, the Gifford Lectures have produced not progress but a 'museum of intellectual conflict' — no agreed results, no consensus even on what rational progress would look like. He identifies at least three sources of this multiplied, apparently unresolvable disagreement: first, the absence of any generally accepted starting point or first premises among lecturers; second, the way arguments in the Lectures are characteristically organized to point toward rather than entail conclusions. The page thus sets up MacIntyre's diagnosis that the Enlightenment encyclopaedist hope for tradition-independent moral enquiry has structurally failed.

annotations.content: natural theology understood as comprehending within itself enquiry into the foundations of ethics
annotations.anchor_text: _ter prescribed for Gifford lecturers—that is_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the scope of Gifford's original mandate — natural theology was meant to absorb ethics, not stand apart from it. This is the normative benchmark against which MacIntyre measures the Lectures' failure. Tom is likely flagging that this interdependence of theology and ethics is precisely what the encyclopaedist project tried to sever, and what MacIntyre wants to restore.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly bears on RC's interest in the Thomistic integration of natural theology and moral enquiry — the claim that ethics cannot be grounded without metaphysical and theological commitments is a cornerstone of MacIntyre's (and RC's) critique of Enlightenment moral philosophy.

annotations.content: no progress in respect of generally agreed results of such enquiries, but there is not even agreement as to what the standard of rational progress ought to be.
annotations.anchor_text: _it has of course been quite otherwise_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this as the sharpest formulation of the Gifford failure: not merely no results, but no shared criterion for what a result would even look like. This is a second-order failure — a failure of meta-rationality, not just first-order enquiry. Tom is almost certainly tracking this as the deepest symptom of the encyclopaedist crisis MacIntyre diagnoses.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's engagement with MacIntyre's critique of the Enlightenment 'encyclopaedia' project is precisely here: without a shared tradition-embedded account of rationality, moral discourse collapses into what MacIntyre elsewhere calls 'interminable disagreement.' This is the fragmentation RC seeks to address.

annotations.content: last hundred years
annotations.anchor_text: _Gifford Lectures of the last hundred years_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the temporal scope — a century of lectures — to mark that MacIntyre is making an empirical, historical claim, not merely a philosophical one. This may signal Tom's interest in the evidential weight MacIntyre is placing on intellectual history as a form of argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is similarly invested in historical recovery — the claim that tradition-based enquiry has been marginalized over a historical period (not just refuted philosophically) undergirds the project of 'resurrecting' earlier modes of civic and moral reasoning.

annotations.content: a kind of museum of intellectual conflict
annotations.anchor_text: _ments, a kind of museum of intellectual conflict_
annotations.interpretation: Tom singles out this phrase — MacIntyre's sharpest rhetorical image. A 'museum' preserves without resolving; it displays disagreement as artifact rather than live enquiry. Tom is likely struck by the irony: an institution meant to advance knowledge has become a monument to its impossibility under encyclopaedist assumptions.
annotations.rc_relevance: This image captures the RC problem of institutional decay — institutions meant to form character and sustain moral enquiry becoming instead showcases of fragmentation. It parallels RC's concern with how contemporary institutions have lost their formative, tradition-sustaining function.

annotations.content: What are the sources of this multiplication of unresolved and apparently unresolvable disagreements? They are at least threefold.
annotations.anchor_text: _What are the sources of this multi-plication_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the structural pivot of the page — MacIntyre's shift from diagnosis to etiology. The box drawn around this text (visible in the image) reinforces that Tom sees this as a key architectonic moment. The rhetorical question and numbered answer structure is MacIntyre organizing his argument, and Tom is tracking that organization explicitly.
annotations.rc_relevance: Understanding the sources of moral disagreement — not just its existence — is essential to RC's constructive project. If fragmentation has diagnosable causes, it may have recoverable remedies, which is exactly what RC proposes through retrieval of classical traditions.

annotations.anchor_text: _First there has been and is among Gifford_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles the numeral '1' in the margin, keying into MacIntyre's enumeration of the three sources of disagreement. This is structural annotation — Tom is building a reading scaffold. The accompanying pink handwriting ('which Ps?' or similar) suggests he is immediately questioning who the relevant parties are or which 'principles' are in view.
annotations.rc_relevance: MacIntyre's first source — no shared starting points — is directly related to RC's engagement with the tradition/encyclopaedia divide: traditions provide starting points; the encyclopaedist pretense of tradition-independence leaves enquirers without common ground.

annotations.content: which Ps?
annotations.anchor_text: _no set of first premises or principles upon which_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes 'which Ps?' — almost certainly shorthand for 'which Premises?' or 'which Principles?' — in response to MacIntyre's claim that there are no shared first premises or principles. Tom is pushing back or probing: whose premises? which tradition's principles? This is not passive reception but active interrogation, possibly because Tom wants to specify whether MacIntyre means logical premises, moral axioms, or tradition-embedded starting points.
annotations.rc_relevance: This question is at the heart of MacIntyre's tripartite scheme: the encyclopaedist, genealogist, and tradition-bearer each have different first principles. RC's project of recovering Thomistic enquiry is precisely a bet on one set of 'Ps' over others — so Tom is asking exactly the right question here.

annotations.content: accepted starting point for the enterprise, no set of first premises or principles upon which there is any consensus.
annotations.anchor_text: _First there has been and is among Gifford lecturers no generally_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the full formulation of the first source of disagreement: no shared starting point, no shared premises, no consensus. He is extracting MacIntyre's precise claim for later reference or quotation. The underlining suggests this is a key thesis statement, not merely descriptive context.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the epistemological core of MacIntyre's argument and of RC's concern: civic and moral discourse requires tradition-embedded starting points to be coherent. The absence of such shared premises is what RC diagnoses as the root of contemporary social fragmentation.

annotations.anchor_text: _A second source of disagreement lies in the way_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles '2' to mark the second enumerated source of disagreement, continuing the structural scaffolding begun at annotation 1. The parallel circling of numbered items confirms Tom is mapping MacIntyre's argument structure, likely for use in a summary, lecture notes, or comparative analysis.
annotations.rc_relevance: The second source — arguments that point toward rather than entail conclusions — is relevant to RC's interest in practical reasoning and phronesis. If moral arguments are structurally persuasive rather than demonstrative, the tradition within which one reasons shapes what counts as a compelling argument.

annotations.content: First there has been and is among Gifford lecturers no generally accepted starting point for the enterprise, no set of first premises or principles upon which there is any consensus. Hence even when the arguments proceed, each from their own particular starting point, with some rigor, their conclusions are such as to compel rational assent only from those who were already in agreement upon where to begin.
annotations.anchor_text: _First there has been and is among Gifford lecturers_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets the entire first source of disagreement, from 'First' through 'begin.' This is a unit-marking annotation — he wants to hold this passage as a discrete argumentative block, distinct from the elaboration that follows about multiplicity of traditions. The bracket likely signals intention to quote or closely analyze this passage.
annotations.rc_relevance: This bracketed passage is a microcosm of MacIntyre's broader argument in Three Rival Versions: rational compulsion is tradition-relative. RC's project of recovering a common moral tradition is a direct response to the circularity MacIntyre identifies here — only shared starting points make shared conclusions possible.

annotations.content: A second source of disagreement lies in the way in which characteristically the arguments in Gifford Lectures are organized. A set of relevant considerations are adduced which point towards rather than entail some conclusion which that particular lecturer wishes to establish.
annotations.anchor_text: _A second source of disagreement lies in the way_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets MacIntyre's articulation of the second source — arguments that are rhetorical-persuasive rather than logically entailing. This is a claim about the epistemic structure of moral argument, and Tom brackets it as a self-contained unit. He may be comparing this to what MacIntyre says about demonstrative vs. dialectical argument in the Thomistic tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's interest in the formation of practical wisdom (phronesis) and Thomistic moral reasoning is directly relevant: if Gifford lectures fail because they can only 'point toward' conclusions without tradition-embedded warrants, then the recovery of a tradition is precisely what would restore the entailment relation MacIntyre is lamenting.

annotations.content: A second source of disagreement lies in the way in which characteristically the arguments in Gifford Lectures are organized.
annotations.anchor_text: _A second source of disagreement lies in the way_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the topic sentence of the second source, extracting it as a quotable claim independent of its elaboration. The underlining within the already-bracketed passage suggests a hierarchy of emphasis — the bracket holds the unit, the underline marks the thesis within it.
annotations.rc_relevance: The organizational structure of moral argument is a recurring concern in RC — how institutions, texts, and practices organize moral reasoning shapes what conclusions are even thinkable. This annotation flags that MacIntyre is making a claim about argumentative form, not just content.

annotations.content: point towards rather than
annotations.anchor_text: _of relevant considerations are adduced which point towards_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines specifically the 'point towards rather than' phrase — the hinge of MacIntyre's distinction between persuasive and entailing argument. This is precision annotation: Tom is extracting the logical crux, the exact words that carry the argumentative weight, not just the surrounding context.
annotations.rc_relevance: This distinction between pointing-toward and entailing maps onto RC's concern with the difference between rhetoric and genuine moral reasoning grounded in tradition. MacIntyre (and RC) would argue that only tradition-embedded enquiry can move from the former to the latter.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that philosophy's failure to achieve rational consensus stems from three structural factors: no agreed starting point for justifying beliefs, irresolvable conflicts over how to rank competing considerations, and the inadequacy of even the most rigorous logical resources to settle these disputes. He then catalogues the breadth of philosophical disagreement — spanning method, concept-assignment, meaning, mind-body, and the standards of rational superiority themselves — to show that rival schools supplement shared minimal rationality with incompatible schemes that cannot be adjudicated by any neutral procedure. This sets up his broader claim that the 'de facto unresolvable' nature of these conflicts parallels conclusions reached by historians and philosophers of science (Kuhn) and anthropologists (Benedict), pointing toward his tripartite rival-versions thesis.

annotations.content: This inability to secure more than minimal agreement within philosophy upon how it is rational to proceed in respect of the formation and criticism of beliefs is rooted in the same three factors which underlie the lack of consensus among Gifford lecturers
annotations.anchor_text: _inability to secure more than minimal agreement_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has highlighted the thesis sentence that names the three-factor structural diagnosis of philosophical disagreement. The highlight signals this as a load-bearing claim — the hinge between the Gifford context and MacIntyre's broader argument. Tom is likely marking it as the argumentative crux he will need to cite or contest: is the 'inability' a permanent feature of rationality as such, or is it specific to Enlightenment-derived frameworks? The pink highlight distinguishes this from the blue underlines used for subsidiary claims.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly engages MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism — the RC project's central diagnostic claim that modernity lacks the shared teleological framework needed to adjudicate moral disputes, leaving only rival and incommensurable starting points.

annotations.anchor_text: _of any agreement upon where the justification_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has circled the numeral '1' to mark the first of the three structural factors: the absence of any agreed starting point for where justification of belief should begin. By circling rather than simply underlining, Tom is flagging the organizational structure of MacIntyre's argument — he wants to be able to retrieve the three-factor schema quickly. This suggests he sees the schema as architecturally important for the book's argument, not merely illustrative.
annotations.rc_relevance: The first factor — no agreed origin for justification — maps onto the RC theme of tradition vs. reform: traditions provide a starting point for justification (Aristotelian telos, Thomistic natural law), while Enlightenment rationalism claims to begin from nowhere, which MacIntyre argues is itself a tradition-laden fiction.

annotations.anchor_text: _de facto ineliminable conflicts as to how various relevant types of considerations ought to be ranked_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles the second factor: irresolvable conflicts about how to weight and rank competing considerations. The word 'ineliminable' is key — MacIntyre is not saying these conflicts are merely unresolved but structurally unresolvable within the current philosophical dispensation. Tom's circling suggests he is tracking this as the factor most relevant to moral philosophy specifically, since ranking competing goods is the classical problem of practical reason.
annotations.rc_relevance: This factor is directly relevant to RC's engagement with subsidiarity and the ordering of goods: Thomistic ethics claims a hierarchical ordering of ends grounded in natural teleology, whereas modern liberal ethics treats such rankings as merely subjective — MacIntyre's point is that without a tradition-constituted framework, ranking is genuinely impossible.

annotations.anchor_text: _limited resources provided for reasoning about the justification of beliefs_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles the third factor: the inadequacy of even the most sophisticated logical resources (entailment analysis) to settle justificatory disputes. This is MacIntyre's most technically philosophical point — formal logic cannot supply the substantive content that traditions provide. Tom's consistent circling of all three factors shows he is mapping the argument's skeleton, possibly for use in RC's own diagnostic framework about why liberal institutions fail to form character.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's concern with the role of institutions in forming character: if even rigorous philosophical argument cannot adjudicate between rival moral frameworks, then institutions cannot rely on 'rational persuasion' alone — they require the embodied practices and narrative traditions that MacIntyre associates with the Thomistic tradition.

annotations.content: The subjects of such disagreements include: questions about the methods and style appropriate to philosophical inquiry, issues concerning which concepts are to be assigned a fundamental and central place in framing philosophical theories, accounts of meaning, reference, and the place of language in the natural and social world, the way in which the relationship of mind to body is to be understood, and—inseparable from all of these and from the bearing that theses and arguments on each of these topics has on at least some of the others—the standards by which one particular mode of procedure and inquiry or one particular theory or account is to be judged rationally superior to others.
annotations.anchor_text: _subjects of such disagreements include questions about methods_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets this long catalogue of philosophical disagreement subjects with a single bracket and the numeral '1' in the margin, suggesting he treats this entire passage as a single unit of argument — the evidentiary base for MacIntyre's claim about the breadth of philosophical incommensurability. The bracket with '1' may also be a cross-reference marker, tying this passage to a larger organizational scheme Tom is building across the book. The final item in the list — 'the standards by which one particular mode of procedure... is to be judged rationally superior' — is underlined separately, indicating Tom sees it as the most philosophically significant item: the problem of meta-criteria.
annotations.rc_relevance: This catalogue is directly relevant to RC's MacIntyre theme: the impossibility of neutral meta-criteria for adjudicating rival traditions is the core of MacIntyre's 'encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition' tripartite structure, and Tom's bracketing suggests he is using this passage as evidence for why the RC project must engage tradition-constituted rationality rather than appealing to universal standards.

annotations.content: the standards by which one particular mode of procedure and inquiry or one particular theory or account is to be judged rationally superior to others
annotations.anchor_text: _standards by which one particular mode of procedure_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this specific item within the bracketed catalogue, distinguishing it from the other subjects of disagreement. This is the meta-level problem: not just disagreement about first-order philosophical questions, but disagreement about what would count as resolving such disagreements. Tom's separate underlining signals he sees this as the crux — the point where MacIntyre's argument becomes most threatening to liberal proceduralism, and most relevant to the question of how traditions can claim rational superiority.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the pivot point for RC's engagement with the 'encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition' tripartite: each version of moral enquiry has its own standard of rational superiority, and MacIntyre's argument is that only tradition-constituted rationality can give a coherent account of why its standards are defensible — which is why RC must engage Thomistic epistemology, not just Thomistic ethics.

annotations.content: facts support Kuhn
annotations.anchor_text: _de facto unresolvable character of these conflicts_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes 'facts support Kuhn' at the bottom left, circled, in response to MacIntyre's invocation of historians and philosophers of science. This is a cross-reference to Thomas Kuhn's paradigm-incommensurability thesis — Tom is noting that MacIntyre's empirical claim about philosophical disagreement has structural support from Kuhn's account of scientific revolutions. The note suggests Tom may be thinking about whether the Kuhn parallel strengthens or complicates MacIntyre's case: Kuhn's relativism is something MacIntyre explicitly distances himself from, so Tom may be flagging a tension.
annotations.rc_relevance: The Kuhn reference is significant for RC because MacIntyre uses Kuhn to support tradition-constituted rationality while resisting Kuhn's relativist implications — RC must navigate the same tension: acknowledging that traditions shape rationality without collapsing into the view that all traditions are equally valid, which would undermine the project's normative commitments.

annotations.content: Benedict
annotations.anchor_text: _by some historians and philosophers of science and by some anthropologists_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'Benedict' at the bottom of the page (likely Ruth Benedict, the cultural relativist anthropologist), which MacIntyre mentions alongside historians of science as reaching parallel conclusions about incommensurability. The circle — rather than a simple underline — suggests Tom is flagging this as a specific interlocutor he wants to research or return to. Benedict's cultural relativism is a more radical version of the incommensurability thesis than Kuhn's, and Tom may be noting the risk that MacIntyre's argument slides toward anthropological relativism.
annotations.rc_relevance: Benedict's relativism poses a direct challenge to RC's normative project: if moral frameworks are as culturally relative as Benedict argues, the recovery of Thomistic civility and civic friendship cannot claim universal or even cross-cultural validity. Tom's circling suggests he is tracking this challenge as something the RC project must address.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that within modern philosophy, just as in the history of physics and moral anthropology, rival standards of rationality are incommensurable — so deeply so that it is not hyperbolic to speak of 'rival conceptions of rationality' both theoretical and practical. He then diagnoses a professional pathology among English-speaking philosophers: trained to treat all disagreement as in-principle resolvable, they deflect intractable disagreements through dismissiveness or assimilation rather than confronting them. This condition of systematic, unacknowledged philosophical fragmentation, MacIntyre notes, is the same condition already visible in the Gifford Lectures' fields of natural theology and ethics.

annotations.content: philosophers of science have identified in different periods of the history of physics different and incompatible standards
annotations.anchor_text: _rians and philosophers of science have identified_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the opening empirical analogy MacIntyre deploys — that incommensurability is not a speculative worry but a documented historical fact in philosophy of science. This is likely flagged because it grounds what might seem like a relativist claim in concrete historiography, lending MacIntyre's argument credibility before the more provocative moves. Tom may be tracking the rhetorical structure: analogy from science → moral anthropology → philosophy itself.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism in RC: the Enlightenment project assumed a single, neutral standard of reason, but the history of science itself refutes this. Tom is likely marking this as evidence for the RC claim that rationality is tradition-constituted, not universal.

annotations.content: so it appears that within modern philosophy there occurs the kind of irreconcilable division and interminable disagreement which is to be explained only by incommensurability
annotations.anchor_text: _so it appears that within mod- ern philosophy_
annotations.interpretation: This is the thesis sentence of the paragraph — MacIntyre's application of incommensurability to philosophy itself. Tom's highlighting signals this as a key claim to hold onto, the argumentative payoff of the preceding analogies. The phrase 'so it appears' is epistemically cautious, and Tom may be noting that MacIntyre is not yet asserting this dogmatically but building toward it.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the diagnostic core of MacIntyre's genealogist/encyclopaedist/tradition tripartite structure in RC: the encyclopaedist denies incommensurability, the genealogist weaponizes it, and the tradition-bearer takes it seriously as a problem to be worked through. Tom is marking the problem that RC must address.

annotations.content: A. relativism
annotations.anchor_text: _so it appears that within modern philosophy_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes 'A. relativism' with a downward arrow, suggesting this is the first of a lettered series of positions or objections MacIntyre is cataloguing or anticipating. The label 'relativism' indicates Tom reads this passage as either MacIntyre raising the specter of relativism as a charge against his own incommensurability thesis, or as Tom flagging that a naive reader might mistake incommensurability for relativism — a distinction MacIntyre carefully maintains throughout the book. The letter 'A' implies Tom expects subsequent labeled positions (B, C) nearby.
annotations.rc_relevance: Relativism is the primary objection the RC project must navigate: if traditions are incommensurable, does civility across traditions become impossible? Tom's labeling suggests he is tracking how MacIntyre handles the relativism charge, which is central to RC's claim that cross-tradition civic friendship is possible without collapsing into relativism.

annotations.content: So general is the scope and so systematic the character of some at least of these disagreements that it is not too much to speak of rival conceptions of rationality, both theoretical and practical
annotations.anchor_text: _So general is the scope and so systematic_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the culminating claim of the first paragraph — the formulation 'rival conceptions of rationality, both theoretical and practical' is MacIntyre's sharpest statement of the problem. The double underline visible in the image (theoretical and practical) suggests Tom finds this bifurcation especially significant: it is not merely that people disagree about conclusions, but that they disagree about what counts as good reasoning both in abstract theory and in lived moral practice.
annotations.rc_relevance: The theoretical/practical distinction maps directly onto RC's concern with institutions forming character: a fractured theoretical rationality produces fractured practical rationality, meaning civic friendship cannot be rebuilt by argument alone — it requires the recovery of shared practices and institutions. This is the Aristotelian/Thomistic point MacIntyre is smuggling in here.

annotations.content: in recent French writing about the history of ideas
annotations.anchor_text: _This is a thought not at all unfamiliar in recent French writing_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this phrase, likely flagging that MacIntyre is gesturing at the genealogist tradition — Foucault, Derrida, Lyotard — without naming them. This is a significant structural moment: MacIntyre is acknowledging that the genealogists have correctly identified the problem (incommensurability, fragmentation) even if their response to it is wrong. Tom may be noting the irony or complexity here.
annotations.rc_relevance: In RC's tripartite structure, the genealogist tradition (represented by Nietzsche and his French successors) is the most dangerous rival precisely because it is partly right. Tom's underlining tracks where MacIntyre grants this partial correctness, which matters for RC's strategy of engaging rather than dismissing postmodern critique.

annotations.content: English-speaking philosophers about their own discipline
annotations.anchor_text: _too rarely been entertained by English-speaking philosophers_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this to mark MacIntyre's implicit critique of analytic philosophy's professional culture. The contrast between French philosophers (who acknowledge fragmentation) and English-speaking philosophers (who deny it) is a disciplinary-sociological claim, not merely a philosophical one. Tom may be noting this as a moment where MacIntyre's argument becomes institutional and cultural, not just logical.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's theme of the role of institutions in forming character: professional philosophy as an institution has trained practitioners to misperceive the nature of their own disagreements. The 'university' as a site of formation is a key RC concern, and MacIntyre here indicts it.

annotations.content: philosophers generally treat every philosophical disagreement as somehow or other and in the end rationally resolvable
annotations.anchor_text: _One outcome of education into professionalization_
annotations.interpretation: This is underlined as the specific diagnostic claim about professional philosophers — their trained assumption of in-principle resolvability. Tom flags this because it is MacIntyre's explanation for why the pathology persists: it is not intellectual dishonesty but a deeply ingrained professional formation. The phrase 'education into professionalization' is key — it makes this a claim about institutional character formation gone wrong.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's concern with subsidiarity and institutional formation: the very institutions meant to pursue truth (universities, professional philosophy) have, through their formative practices, produced practitioners incapable of recognizing the depth of their disagreements. RC must reckon with this as it considers how to rebuild moral community through institutions.

annotations.content: local framework of agreement about particular well-defined problems or issues
annotations.anchor_text: _the kind of immediate progress that can be made within some local_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines 'local framework of agreement' — this is a technical term in MacIntyre's vocabulary that Tom is tracking. The word 'local' here does real philosophical work: progress is possible within a tradition's own framework, but this does not constitute universal rational resolution. Tom is likely cross-referencing this against MacIntyre's concept of tradition-constituted rationality developed elsewhere in the book.
annotations.rc_relevance: The concept of local frameworks of agreement is foundational for RC's subsidiarity theme: genuine moral reasoning happens at the level of particular communities with shared practices, not at the level of abstract universal reason. This annotation shows Tom tracking the positive implication of MacIntyre's critique, not just the negative diagnosis.

annotations.content: larger disagreements
annotations.anchor_text: _About those with whom their larger disagreements are inescapably obvious_
annotations.interpretation: The underlining of 'larger disagreements' in isolation suggests Tom is marking a key conceptual distinction MacIntyre is making between tractable local disagreements and intractable larger ones. This is likely flagged for cross-reference — MacIntyre will develop what makes some disagreements 'larger' (i.e., involving rival first principles) throughout the book.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's project is precisely concerned with these 'larger disagreements' about the foundations of ethics and community — the kind that cannot be resolved within a single local framework. Tom is tracking the category that RC must address head-on rather than domesticate.

annotations.content: dismissive
annotations.anchor_text: _they are all too often either dismissive_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines 'dismissive' as one of the two pathological responses to intractable disagreement. The example given — 'That is not really philosophy' — is a gatekeeping move, and Tom likely recognizes this as a live practice in academic philosophy. This may also resonate personally or professionally for Tom as someone working across traditions.
annotations.rc_relevance: Dismissiveness is the opposite of civic friendship — it forecloses dialogue by denying the interlocutor's legitimacy. RC's recovery of civility must contend with this professional habit, making MacIntyre's diagnosis directly actionable for the RC project.

annotations.content: assimilative
annotations.anchor_text: _or assimilative ("A careful reading of Heidegger_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines 'assimilative' as the second pathological response — collapsing difference by claiming apparent rivals are secretly saying the same thing. The Heidegger/Wittgenstein example is pointed. Tom likely sees this as the more intellectually sophisticated but ultimately more dishonest evasion, and may be thinking of analogous moves in theological or ethical discourse.
annotations.rc_relevance: Assimilationism is a recurring temptation in ecumenical and inter-tradition dialogue that RC must resist: genuine civic friendship requires acknowledging real difference, not papering over it. MacIntyre's critique here supports RC's insistence that the tradition-bearer must engage rivals on their own terms rather than absorbing them.

annotations.content: So the facts of intractable and systematic disagreement are simultaneously all too familiar and yet all too readily passed over in practice.
annotations.anchor_text: _So the facts of intractable and systematic disagreement_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets this sentence as a particularly crystalline formulation of the paradox MacIntyre is diagnosing: everyone knows about the disagreements, but professional norms suppress acknowledgment of their depth. The bracketing (rather than mere underlining) suggests Tom sees this as a standalone quotable claim — the kind of sentence he might cite directly in RC writing.
annotations.rc_relevance: This paradox of familiar-but-suppressed fragmentation is the social-epistemic condition RC is responding to. The RC project's urgency depends on this diagnosis being correct: civility has broken down not because people are unaware of disagreement but because institutions have normalized its suppression.

annotations.content: this condition of contemporary philosophy in which systematic disagreement upon fundamental issues extends to disagreement over how such disagreements are to be formulated and characterized, let alone resolved, is clearly the same condition which was already beginning to manifest itself in the fields of natural theology and the foundations of ethics
annotations.anchor_text: _And this condition of contemporary philosophy_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets the entire closing sentence of the page, which is MacIntyre's structural link connecting the current philosophical situation back to the Gifford Lectures context. This is a crucial hinge in the book's argument: the crisis is not new, and it is not merely academic — it is the same crisis of moral and theological foundations that the Gifford Lectures were meant to address. Tom brackets it to mark its structural importance to the book's overall argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: This sentence directly connects MacIntyre's philosophical diagnosis to the substantive concerns of RC: natural theology and the foundations of ethics are precisely the domains where RC seeks recovery and renewal. Tom is marking the point where MacIntyre's meta-philosophical argument becomes a claim about the specific tradition — Thomistic moral enquiry — that RC is trying to resurrect.

annotations.content: is clearly the same condition which was already beginning to manifest itself in the fields of natural theology and the foundations of ethics
annotations.anchor_text: _is clearly the same condition which was already beginning_

The double-marking (bracket plus highlight) is a signal of exceptional importance.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the direct warrant for RC as a project: if the condition of contemporary philosophy — fragmentation, incommensurability, suppressed disagreement — is the same condition afflicting natural theology and ethics, then MacIntyre's proposed remedy (recovery of tradition-constituted Thomistic enquiry) is precisely what RC must operationalize in the domain of civic and moral community.

Given that this is page 13 of the book itself, it may be a self-referential anchor Tom is placing to help reconstruct the argument's flow, or it marks the 13th significant passage in his reading notes for this book.
annotations.rc_relevance: If this is part of a numbered annotation system Tom is building across the book, it suggests he is constructing a map of MacIntyre's argument for RC use — systematically cataloguing the key moves. This kind of structural annotation is consistent with the RC project's need to synthesize MacIntyre into a usable framework.

pages.summary: MacIntyre is establishing the intellectual context of Adam Gifford and his contemporaries, arguing that Gifford held a presupposition of unitary substantive rationality — the belief that a single, coherent set of rational standards governs all inquiry, including natural theology and ethics, treating them as sciences on par with astronomy and chemistry. To reconstruct Gifford's intellectual portrait, MacIntyre draws on a memoir by Gifford's brother John, a posthumous lecture collection, and Gifford's will itself. The broader point is that Gifford was a representative figure of a Victorian Scottish intellectual culture marked by democratized civic discussion of general ideas.

annotations.content: science defined
annotations.anchor_text: _application of the methods and goals of this single_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging the moment MacIntyre offers his working definition of 'science' in the Giffordian sense — not empirical science in the modern reductive sense, but the application of unitary rational methods to any subject matter, including theology and ethics. This is a pivotal definitional move in the book's argument, and Tom wants to hold it as a reference point. He may be preparing to contest or deploy this definition against later rival versions.
annotations.rc_relevance: This definition of science is central to the 'encyclopaedist' version of moral enquiry that MacIntyre critiques — the RC project's engagement with MacIntyre's tripartite structure requires precisely this definition as the target of the encyclopaedist's rationalist hubris.

annotations.content: natural theology and the study of the foundations of ethics jointly constitute in this sense a science, just as astronomy does and just as chemistry does, Adam Gifford, once again like the vast majority of his educated Edinburgh contemporaries, had no doubt.
annotations.anchor_text: _natural theology and the study of the foundations_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this as the core claim of Gifford's intellectual stance: that natural theology and ethics are sciences in the same sense as the natural sciences. The highlighting suggests Tom sees this as a foundational claim worth sustained attention — either because he finds it philosophically naive (from a MacIntyrean standpoint) or because it represents exactly the kind of Enlightenment confidence in rational universalism that MacIntyre's whole project is written to dismantle.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the encyclopaedist position in its purest form — the belief that moral and theological inquiry can be conducted under the same neutral rational standards as natural science. RC's engagement with the collapse of this confidence underpins its diagnosis of contemporary moral fragmentation.

annotations.content: spell out in more detail this conception of a science
annotations.anchor_text: _It is therefore important to spell out_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as a structural signal — MacIntyre is announcing that what follows is a deeper unpacking of the concept of science that Gifford assumed. Tom recognizes this as a methodological pivot in the text, where MacIntyre shifts from historical description to conceptual analysis. It suggests Tom is tracking the book's argumentative architecture carefully.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this moment matters because MacIntyre's unpacking of Giffordian 'science' is the diagnostic step before proposing Thomistic tradition-constituted enquiry as an alternative — Tom is likely marking the beginning of that critical arc.

annotations.content: social and intellectual context
annotations.anchor_text: _immediate society; to do so we need to place_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines 'social and intellectual context' as a methodological keyword — MacIntyre is insisting that ideas cannot be understood apart from the communities and institutions that form them. This reflects MacIntyre's broader anti-foundationalist and tradition-constituted view of rationality, and Tom recognizes it as load-bearing.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's theme of the role of institutions in forming character — the point that even a thinker's conception of rationality is socially embedded anticipates the subsidiarity and civic friendship themes Tom is developing.

annotations.content: Vertical bracket with downward arrow on the left margin, pointing down toward a reference notation at the bottom of the bracket
annotations.anchor_text: _in Gifford's social and intellectual context_
annotations.interpretation: This bracket with arrow appears to be Tom marking a section of the text as a unit — likely the passage about Gifford's social context and the two biographical sources — and routing it toward a cross-reference note at the bottom. The arrow suggests Tom is connecting this passage to something else, possibly a page reference (the notation at the bottom appears to read something like '→ p.23' or similar), indicating a forward or backward link in his reading notes.
annotations.rc_relevance: Tom's cross-referencing habit here suggests he is building an argument map across the book — tracking how MacIntyre's contextualism about Gifford's rationalism connects to later chapters where tradition-constituted enquiry is defended. This is central to RC's use of MacIntyre as a diagnostic and constructive resource.

annotations.content: in [Gifford?] context
annotations.anchor_text: _we need to place that conception in its immediate society_
annotations.interpretation: This appears to be Tom's abbreviated gloss summarizing the methodological point MacIntyre is making — that to understand Gifford's conception of science, we must situate it in its social context. The abbreviation style ('in [X] context') suggests Tom is developing a shorthand for MacIntyre's contextualist methodology that he can use across his notes.
annotations.rc_relevance: The insistence on social-intellectual context resonates with RC's anti-liberal individualism — character and belief are formed within communities, not by free-floating rational agents, which is a core tension RC seeks to address.

annotations.content: For such societies and their meetings were important to a nineteenth-century culture informed by a widespread discussion of and respect for general ideas.
annotations.anchor_text: _For such societies and their meetings were important_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as evidence of a pre-fragmentation civic culture — one where local societies (legal, academic, clerical) sustained shared intellectual life around general ideas. This is MacIntyre offering a historical contrast to modernity's fragmented discourse, and Tom recognizes it as precisely the kind of thick civic community RC wants to recover.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is a direct instantiation of RC's themes of civic friendship and subsidiarity — local voluntary associations forming the intellectual and moral character of their members through shared discussion of general ideas, exactly what RC argues has been lost.

annotations.content: democratization
annotations.anchor_text: _represented the wider democratization of a distinctive habit of_
annotations.interpretation: Tom singles out 'democratization' as a conceptually loaded term — MacIntyre is describing the spread of a particular intellectual habit (rational civic discourse about general ideas) across classes and localities. Tom may be interrogating whether this 'democratization' was genuinely inclusive or whether it masked elite assumptions about what counts as rational discourse, a question MacIntyre's own genealogical critics would raise.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with tradition vs. reform is activated here — was Victorian civic democratization a genuine broadening of moral community, or the imposition of a particular (encyclopaedist) conception of rationality under a universalist guise? Tom's underlining suggests he is holding the tension open.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that while the Edinburgh establishment rightly objected to the arbitrary exclusion of certain data from enquiry in the Robertson Smith case, they made a further, unjustified assumption: that in all enquiry, the adequate identification and classification of data requires no prior theoretical or doctrinal commitment — that data 'speak for themselves.' This 'unargued belief' in presuppositionless enquiry, inherited from the philosophical influence of Reid, Stewart, and Kantian successors via Hamilton, forms what MacIntyre calls a 'unitary conception of rationality,' which Gifford and his circle took for granted as a universal feature of rational minds.

annotations.content: were in fact bad actions, fet-tering and thus damaging enquiry to no good purpose, is something with which it is not difficult to agree
annotations.anchor_text: _were in fact bad actions fettering and thus_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's concession that the specific ecclesiastical interventions against Robertson Smith were genuinely bad — this is the 'granted' premise from which the Edinburgh liberals reasoned. By marking this, Tom is tracking the logical structure of MacIntyre's argument: he concedes the particular before attacking the universal principle derived from it. This is a classic MacIntyrean move — acknowledging the partial truth of an opponent's position before exposing its overreach.
annotations.rc_relevance: This marks the moment where legitimate reform (protecting enquiry from arbitrary authority) gets overextended into an Enlightenment principle that denies tradition any epistemic role — a core RC tension between justified reform and the corrosion of tradition-constituted rationality.

annotations.content: the imposition of religious tests as such, the requirement of a certain type of commitment and of the acceptance of a certain type of authority as a precondition for entering upon enquiry
annotations.anchor_text: _assumption that the imposition of religious tests as such_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the precise formulation of the principle MacIntyre is about to contest — that any commitment or authority as a precondition for enquiry is illegitimate. This is the hinge of the chapter's argument, and Tom appears to be isolating it as a claim to examine against MacIntyre's own tradition-constituted alternative. The underlining suggests Tom sees this as a statement he will need to return to when MacIntyre develops his positive account.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's engagement with subsidiarity and institutional formation of character: if no prior commitment is required for enquiry, then traditions and institutions have no legitimate epistemic authority — a position RC explicitly contests.

annotations.content: which needs to be put in question if we are to understand the culture of Adam Gifford and his circle
annotations.anchor_text: _which needs to be put in question if we are to understand_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks MacIntyre's methodological declaration — the book's project is itself an act of questioning what Gifford's circle took as settled. This is a meta-level annotation: Tom is noting where MacIntyre announces his critical agenda. The phrase 'needs to be put in question' signals that the enquiry into Gifford is simultaneously a genealogical critique of Enlightenment presuppositions about rationality.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's recovery project — understanding civility and moral community requires questioning the liberal assumptions baked into the very institutions (like universities, lecture series) that claim to be neutral forums for free enquiry.

annotations.content: What was rightly objected to in the condemnation of Robertson Smith was the invocation of orthodoxy in order to effect an arbitrary exclusion from enquiry of the consideration of certain kinds of data, data relevant, in his case, to determining the chronology and authorship of the Pentateuch. But accompanying this justified objection was an unargued belief that in all enquiry, religious, moral, or otherwise, the adequate identification, characterization, and classification of the relevant data does not require, and indeed may preclude any prior commitment to some particular theoretical or doctrinal standpoint. The data, so to speak, present themselves and speak for themselves.
annotations.anchor_text: _What was rightly objected to in the condemnation_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets the entire passage containing MacIntyre's crucial two-move argument: (1) the justified objection to orthodoxy's arbitrary exclusions, and (2) the unjustified slide to the 'unargued belief' that data are theory-neutral. The bracket signals that Tom sees this as a unit — the complete logical structure of the error MacIntyre is diagnosing. The phrase 'unargued belief' is likely the crux Tom wants to hold in view, as it frames the Enlightenment encyclopaedist position as itself a form of dogmatism.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the epistemic heart of RC's engagement with MacIntyre's tripartite structure: the encyclopaedist version of moral enquiry rests on an unargued premise about data-neutrality, which MacIntyre (and RC) contest by recovering tradition-constituted rationality.

annotations.content: unargued belief that in all enquiry, religious, moral, or otherwise, the adequate identification, characterization, and classification of the relevant data does not require, and indeed may preclude any prior commitment to some particular theoretical or doctrinal standpoint
annotations.anchor_text: _unargued belief that in all enquiry religious moral_
annotations.interpretation: Tom specifically underlines the term 'unargued belief' and the full formulation of the data-neutrality thesis — indicating this is a key philosophical target. The underlining within the already-bracketed passage suggests a double emphasis: the bracket captures the logical unit, the underline isolates the precise claim. Tom is likely noting that MacIntyre is turning the liberal epistemology's own standards against it — it is itself held as an 'unargued' dogma.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the conceptual lynchpin for RC's argument against purely procedural or liberal conceptions of civic discourse: the pretense of presuppositionless public reason is itself a tradition-specific, unargued commitment, undermining claims to universal neutrality.

annotations.content: Left-margin vertical bracket with a downward arrow at the bottom left of the second major paragraph beginning 'What was at issue here'
annotations.anchor_text: _What was at issue here is perhaps easier for us_
annotations.interpretation: The downward arrow attached to the bracket likely signals continuity — Tom is marking that the argument continues onto the next page and should be read as a unit. It may also function as a 'follow this thread' marker, indicating that the argument about Gifford's contemporaries learning from Reid/Stewart/Hamilton is the payoff of the argument developed above. The symbol suggests Tom is tracking the book's architectural logic, not just individual claims.
annotations.rc_relevance: The arrow points toward MacIntyre's explanation of the philosophical sources of Gifford's 'unitary conception of rationality' — directly relevant to RC's genealogical work tracing how Enlightenment assumptions about reason became embedded in institutional culture.

annotations.content: easier for us to recognize now than it was for Gifford and his contemporaries
annotations.anchor_text: _easier for us to recognize now than it was_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks MacIntyre's historical-critical self-positioning: 'we' (post-Kuhn, post-Sellars) can see what Gifford could not. This is a key moment where MacIntyre's own enquiry claims a kind of retrospective advantage — which is itself a tradition-constituted claim. Tom may be flagging this to examine whether MacIntyre's 'we' is itself positioned within a tradition, raising a reflexivity question about the genealogical method.
annotations.rc_relevance: Connects to RC's concern with how traditions develop and achieve self-understanding over time — the capacity to recognize past assumptions as assumptions is itself a fruit of tradition-constituted rational development, not a view from nowhere.

annotations.content: unitary conception of rationality and of the rational mind that they took it for granted not only that all rational persons conceptualize data in one and the same
annotations.anchor_text: _unitary conception of rationality and of the rational mind_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's coinage 'unitary conception of rationality' — a technical term that will recur in the book's argument. By marking its first introduction, Tom is tracking the book's key vocabulary. The phrase 'took it for granted' reinforces the 'unargued belief' motif above, and Tom appears to be building a chain of underlines that shows MacIntyre repeatedly characterizing Enlightenment rationalism as dogmatic rather than self-critical.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'unitary conception of rationality' is precisely what RC contests in favor of tradition-constituted, community-embedded forms of practical reason — this term anchors the encyclopaedist position in MacIntyre's tripartite schema and is foundational for RC's alternative vision of civic reasoning.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues, drawing on Kuhn, that any mode of enquiry operates with both pre-theoretical and post-theoretical conceptual frameworks: the criteria by which data are identified and characterized are always shaped by prior theoretical or doctrinal commitments, not by neutral observation. He applies this epistemological point to theological and moral enquiry, arguing that what counts as relevant data depends on the enquirer's standpoint. This sets up the claim that Adam Gifford's hostility to religious tests was not a commitment to neutrality but was itself embedded in a particular theoretical conception of natural theology — meaning the 'no religious tests' clause and his conception of enquiry are internally connected.
