annotations.content: have learned from Gaston Bachelard, Thomas Kuhn, and others that, relative to any particular type of enquiry, there are always at least two modes of conceptualizing and characterizing the data which constitute its subject matter, a pretheoretical (although not of course preconceptual) prior-to-enquiry mode and a mode internal to that particular type of enquiry which already presupposes one particular theoretical or doctrinal stance
annotations.anchor_text: _have learned from Gaston Bachelard, Thomas Kuhn_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has highlighted the core epistemological claim of the page: that all enquiry involves both a pre-theoretical and a post-theoretical (internal) mode of conceptualizing data. This is the Kuhnian point MacIntyre is importing into moral and theological epistemology. Tom is tracking this as a foundational structural claim — the distinction between pre- and post-theoretical concepts is the hinge on which MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment 'neutral observation' turns. The highlight suggests Tom sees this as a passage to cite or return to, not merely to note.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly illuminates RC's engagement with MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism: the encyclopaedist pretension to neutral, tradition-independent enquiry is precisely what this passage dismantles. Tom is likely flagging this as the epistemological foundation for why the RC project must take seriously the tradition-constituted character of all moral reasoning.

annotations.content: pre + post Abeoretia concepts
annotations.anchor_text: _always at least two modes of conceptualizing_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is glossing MacIntyre's two-mode distinction in compressed shorthand: 'pre + post Atheoretical [or Abeoretical] concepts.' The spelling 'Abeoretia' is almost certainly a rushed rendering of 'atheoretical' or 'theoretical' — likely 'pre + post-theoretical concepts.' Tom is creating a retrieval label for this structural distinction so he can cross-reference it elsewhere. The plus sign suggests he is treating these as a paired structure, not a hierarchy, which matters for how one uses them analytically.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this note signals Tom's interest in the epistemological architecture underlying tradition-based enquiry. The pre/post-theoretical distinction is crucial to explaining why MacIntyre's 'tradition' version of moral enquiry is not mere conservatism but a sophisticated epistemic claim about how moral data are always already interpreted.

annotations.content: criteria for the identity of everyday objects and persons are indeed pretheoretical, so that we are able to assert that it is one and the same swinging stone which is observed by both Aristotelian and Galilean physicists. But there is no way of identifying, characterizing, or classifying that particular datum in a way relevant to the purposes of theoretical enquiry except in terms of some prior theoretical or doctrinal commitment
annotations.anchor_text: _criteria for the identity of everyday objects and persons_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the Kuhnian pendulum/swinging stone example because it makes the abstract epistemological point concrete. The key tension MacIntyre is exploiting is that pre-theoretical identity (same stone) does not give you theory-neutral data characterization. Tom is likely noting this because it is MacIntyre's sharpest illustration of why the encyclopaedist's dream of raw, uncommitted data is incoherent. The word 'But' at the pivot is crucial — Tom may be tracking the argumentative turn.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's concern with civic and moral formation: just as the Aristotelian and Galilean cannot share a common characterization of the stone without a shared theoretical framework, citizens shaped by rival moral traditions cannot share a common characterization of civic goods without some shared standpoint. This is the fragmentation problem RC seeks to address.

annotations.content: (like Others almost (com- theory)
annotations.anchor_text: _no way of identifying, characterizing, or classifying_
annotations.interpretation: This note is partially legible but appears to read '(like Others almost (com- theory)' — likely 'like [commensurability] theory' or a reference to another thinker whose position is 'almost' equivalent to MacIntyre's here. The word 'Others' with a capital O may refer to a specific philosopher (possibly Wittgenstein, Davidson, or even Gadamer) whose views on theory-ladenness of observation Tom is cross-referencing. The 'almost' suggests Tom is noting a partial but imperfect parallel — he is distinguishing MacIntyre's position from a near-neighbor view.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is built partly on identifying what distinguishes MacIntyre's tradition-constituted enquiry from other communitarian or hermeneutical positions. Tom's 'almost' is doing important work: it signals he is tracking the precise boundaries of MacIntyre's claim, which matters for RC's argument that MacIntyre offers something distinct from, say, Gadamerian fusion-of-horizons approaches.

annotations.content: asterisk (*)
annotations.anchor_text: _see Thomas S. Kuhn The Structure of Scientific Revolutions_
annotations.interpretation: An asterisk placed near the Kuhn citation marks it as a key bibliographic reference Tom intends to follow up or has already engaged with. Given that Tom is annotating a book about rival traditions of enquiry, flagging the Kuhn citation suggests he wants to verify or deepen his understanding of exactly what MacIntyre is borrowing from Kuhn versus what he is transforming. It may also signal a point of potential critique — MacIntyre's use of Kuhn is contested.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's engagement with MacIntyre requires understanding his debts to and departures from Kuhn on incommensurability. If traditions are like Kuhnian paradigms, does tradition-switching require something like conversion? This asterisk may mark Tom's awareness that this analogy has high stakes for RC's account of how civility can be recovered across fragmented traditions.

annotations.content: asterisk (*)
annotations.anchor_text: _intimate connection in Adam Gifford's will_
annotations.interpretation: A second asterisk near the bottom of the page, placed at the conclusion about the 'intimate connection' between Gifford's no-religious-tests clause and his conception of natural theology. This mirrors the asterisk near the Kuhn citation, suggesting Tom is bracketing the two argumentative endpoints of the page — the epistemological premise (Kuhn) and the historical-theological conclusion (Gifford). Tom may be noting that MacIntyre has now closed the loop of the argument he opened.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this is a pivotal moment: MacIntyre is showing that what looks like institutional neutrality (no religious tests) is in fact theory-laden. This has direct implications for RC's analysis of modern liberal institutions that claim to be tradition-neutral arbiters of civic life — they too carry concealed doctrinal commitments.

annotations.content: commitment to some particular theoretical or doctrinal standpoint may be a prerequisite for—rather than a barrier to—an ability to characterize data in a way which will enable enquiry to proceed
annotations.anchor_text: _commitment to some particular theoretical or doctrinal standpoint_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets what is arguably the most consequential claim on the page: that doctrinal commitment is a prerequisite for, not an obstacle to, genuine enquiry. This directly inverts the Enlightenment assumption that neutrality enables enquiry. Tom's bracket (rather than mere underline) suggests he is treating this as a standalone propositional unit — something to be extracted, quoted, or argued with. The bracket may also indicate he finds this claim either surprising enough to isolate or programmatic enough to use as a thesis statement.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the RC project's epistemological charter in miniature. RC argues that recovering civility requires commitment to a substantive moral tradition, not retreat to procedural neutrality. MacIntyre's claim here — that commitment enables rather than distorts enquiry — is the philosophical warrant for RC's methodological stance.

annotations.content: the [unclear] [unclear] cfarnesto]
annotations.anchor_text: _intimate connection in Adam Gifford's will between the clauses prohibiting religious tests_
annotations.interpretation: This partially legible note at the bottom right appears to be Tom writing a summary phrase or a cross-reference — possibly 'the [Gifford] cfarnesto' or a name/term that connects to another text. The handwriting is very compressed. It may be a reminder to check a related argument in another work, or a condensed paraphrase of MacIntyre's conclusion about Gifford. The illegibility suggests it was written quickly, as a memory aid rather than a developed thought.
annotations.rc_relevance: Given its placement at the conclusion of MacIntyre's argument about Gifford's concealed doctrinal commitments, this note likely marks the moment Tom connects MacIntyre's historical analysis to a broader RC concern: that modern institutions (like universities, civic bodies) encode particular moral visions while presenting themselves as neutral, a dynamic RC must expose and address.

pages.summary: MacIntyre establishes that Adam Gifford's intellectual world was shaped by the milieu of the New Speculative Society and the Encyclopaedia Britannica's Ninth Edition, edited by Thomas Spencer Baynes and later William Robertson Smith. Baynes is presented as emblematic of a Victorian intellectual synthesis — broadly educated, institutionally embedded, and committed to organizing knowledge within a 'distinctive architectonic of the sciences.' This architectonic, emerging in the late nineteenth century, subordinated all disciplines (biology, physics, etc.) to a unified framework of scientific transformation, making the Ninth Edition the canonical expression of the encyclopaedist tradition MacIntyre is about to critique.

annotations.content: GE9th
annotations.anchor_text: _canonical expression of the Edinburgh culture_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is using shorthand to track the key actors and editions: 'GE9th' likely refers to the General Editors of the Ninth Edition, 'SE9th' to Spencer (Baynes) as editor of the Ninth, and 'Cs' possibly to 'contributors' or 'Clerk (Maxwell)' — or a shorthand for the society connections MacIntyre is mapping. The '11' may refer to a page or footnote number elsewhere, suggesting Tom is cross-referencing. This is structural bookkeeping: Tom is building a cast-of-characters map for the encyclopaedist tradition as MacIntyre constructs it.
annotations.rc_relevance: This tracks the institutional network behind the encyclopaedist project — directly relevant to RC's concern with how institutions shape intellectual traditions and the role of specific communities in forming (or deforming) moral enquiry.

annotations.content: ethics as a natural science
annotations.anchor_text: _ethics as a natural science a conception which_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as a foundational claim — the reduction of ethics to natural science is the defining move of the encyclopaedist tradition MacIntyre is targeting. This is not just a historical observation but the core methodological error MacIntyre attributes to the Gifford milieu. Tom likely flags it as the precise intellectual target the whole book is aimed at dismantling.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly central to RC: the naturalization of ethics is what MacIntyre (and Tom, through RC) opposes with a Thomistic account of moral enquiry rooted in tradition, telos, and practical reason rather than scientific method.

annotations.content: what became the canonical expression of the Edinburgh culture of Adam Gifford's day, the Ninth Edition of the Encyclopaedia Britannica
annotations.anchor_text: _canonical expression of the Edinburgh culture_
annotations.interpretation: The word 'canonical' is crucial — MacIntyre is not just describing the Ninth Edition historically but positioning it as the authoritative text of a rival tradition. Tom underlines this to mark where MacIntyre formally names the encyclopaedist 'version' of moral enquiry he will critique throughout the book. The phrase 'canonical expression' also carries ironic weight: it applies to the encyclopaedists the very language of tradition and authority they would resist.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the moment MacIntyre identifies the encyclopaedist tradition's own 'canon' — relevant to RC's interest in how rival traditions constitute themselves through authoritative texts and the institutions that produce them.

annotations.content: Thomas Spencer Baynes
annotations.anchor_text: _New Speculative Society Thomas Spencer Baynes who edited_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles Baynes as the key figure on this page — the person who personifies the encyclopaedist tradition. Circling a proper name (rather than underlining) typically signals 'remember this person; they are structurally important.' Baynes is important not just as an editor but as the embodiment of the Victorian intellectual synthesis MacIntyre is anatomizing.
annotations.rc_relevance: Baynes as an institution-builder and synthesizer of knowledge connects to RC's concern with how intellectual traditions are transmitted through persons embedded in institutions — and how those institutions can narrow or distort moral enquiry.

annotations.content: William Robertson Smith
annotations.anchor_text: _none other than William Robertson Smith whose indictment_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles Robertson Smith, likely because his 'heresy' trial is a signal moment: the encyclopaedist tradition, despite its liberal self-image, had internal orthodoxies it enforced. MacIntyre uses this to show that the encyclopaedist project was not neutral or purely rational but tradition-laden in its own way — a point Tom is clearly tracking as it undermines the encyclopaedists' own self-understanding.
annotations.rc_relevance: Robertson Smith's heresy case is a compressed illustration of MacIntyre's argument that no tradition is truly tradition-free — directly relevant to RC's challenge to Enlightenment claims of neutral rationality and to MacIntyre's genealogist/encyclopaedist/tradition tripartite structure.

annotations.content: Baynes died in 1887, the year also of Adam Gifford's death, Robertson Smith succeeded him as editor
annotations.anchor_text: _Baynes died in 1887 the year also_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the coincidence of deaths and the succession of editorship — likely tracking the historical continuity of the encyclopaedist project across persons. The succession narrative reinforces MacIntyre's point that traditions persist through institutional roles, not just ideas. The year 1887 may also be relevant for dating Gifford's bequest and the founding of the Gifford Lectures.
annotations.rc_relevance: The succession of editors models how intellectual traditions are transmitted through institutional roles — a theme central to RC's interest in subsidiarity and the formation of moral communities through concrete institutions.

annotations.content: an admirably comprehensive view of the British intellectual scene, probably understanding more of the strengths and weaknesses of the universities in both Scotland and England
annotations.anchor_text: _admirably comprehensive view of the British intellectual scene_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines Baynes's praised comprehensiveness — but in context, this is MacIntyre building up Baynes only to show that his 'comprehensive view' was still bounded by the architectonic of the sciences. The underline may flag the irony: comprehensiveness within a flawed framework is still limited. Tom may be noting this as a model of how intelligence can be co-opted by a deficient tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: Relevant to RC's concern with the limits of 'encyclopaedic' knowledge — broad synthesis without a proper teleological foundation produces the illusion of comprehensiveness while missing the moral core.

annotations.content: his own versatility was exhibited by his chair at St. Andrews in logic, metaphysics, and English literature
annotations.anchor_text: _his own versatility was exhibited by his chair_
annotations.interpretation: The combination of logic, metaphysics, and English literature in a single chair is notable — it reflects a pre-specialization academic world where these were unified. Tom may be underlining this as evidence of the kind of integrated intellectual culture that has since been lost, making it a positive datum even within the encyclopaedist critique.
annotations.rc_relevance: Touches RC's concern with the fragmentation of disciplines and the loss of integrated moral-intellectual formation in modern universities — a theme MacIntyre develops in 'God, Philosophy, Universities' and elsewhere.

annotations.content: framework of a distinctive architectonic of the sciences as that had emerged in the late nineteenth century
annotations.anchor_text: _distinctive architectonic of the sciences as that had emerged_
annotations.interpretation: This is one of the most important phrases on the page — 'architectonic of the sciences' is MacIntyre's technical term for the encyclopaedist's organizing framework. Tom underlines it to mark a key theoretical term. The phrase signals that the encyclopaedist project was not simply the accumulation of knowledge but a structured, normative ordering of disciplines — which is precisely what MacIntyre will contest by offering a Thomistic architectonic as rival.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'architectonic of the sciences' is the encyclopaedist tradition's answer to the question RC asks: how should knowledge be ordered in service of human flourishing? MacIntyre's argument that this architectonic is deficient is the intellectual spine of the RC project's critique of modernity.

annotations.content: everything
annotations.anchor_text: _framework of a distinctive architectonic of the sciences_
annotations.interpretation: This is a striking philosophical note — 'everything that is true are evidence' appears to be Tom's shorthand for an epistemological claim, possibly paraphrasing or reacting to MacIntyre's implicit point that the encyclopaedists treated all true propositions as data points within a single neutral framework. Tom may be flagging the epistemological naivety of this view: the encyclopaedist assumption that truth-claims are commensurable 'evidence' within a single architectonic ignores the tradition-dependency of what counts as evidence. The grammatical roughness ('are' instead of 'is') suggests rapid note-taking mid-thought.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's engagement with MacIntyre's epistemological critique: the encyclopaedist tradition assumes a tradition-neutral evidential framework, which MacIntyre (and RC) contest by arguing that what counts as evidence is always already shaped by a tradition's first principles.

pages.summary: MacIntyre, via his account of Thomas Spencer Baynes's editorial vision for the Ninth Edition of the Encyclopaedia Britannica, argues that the encyclopaedist project was not merely a neutral compilation of knowledge but a programmatic ideological vision: that comprehensive scientific synthesis would displace the Bible as the canonical cultural authority, subordinating theology and ethics to a unified architectonic of the sciences. Baynes's contributors were not monolithic, however — some, including Hamilton's daughter, were already moving in divergent intellectual directions. This page thus establishes the internal tensions within the encyclopaedist tradition even at its apex, and introduces the Ninth Edition's conception of science as individuated by subject matter rather than method.

annotations.content: will not merely report, but will themselves advance that progress
annotations.anchor_text: _will not merely report but will themselves_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is marking MacIntyre's claim that the Encyclopaedia was not a passive record but an active agent of ideological progress. This is a crucial distinction: the encyclopaedist project is performative, not descriptive. Tom likely sees this as MacIntyre's core indictment — the encyclopedia presents itself as neutral while covertly advancing a contested normative vision of human progress.
annotations.rc_relevance: This bears directly on the RC theme of institutions forming character and ideology: the encyclopedia as an institution does not merely transmit knowledge but shapes the terms of rational enquiry itself, which is precisely MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism.

annotations.content: The available facts of human history, collected over the widest areas, are carefully coordinated and grouped together, in the hope of ultimately evolving the laws of progress, moral and material, which underlie them, and which will help to connect and interpret the whole movement of the race
annotations.anchor_text: _available facts of human history carefully coordinated_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this extended quotation from Baynes to capture the full scope of the encyclopaedist ambition: not just scientific synthesis but the extraction of moral laws from historical data. The phrase 'laws of progress, moral and material' is particularly significant — it collapses the is/ought distinction by treating moral progress as empirically derivable, which is the Enlightenment move MacIntyre most contests.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the encyclopaedist tradition's version of moral enquiry — historicist, progressivist, and immanentist — which MacIntyre contrasts with Thomistic moral enquiry grounded in teleology and tradition. RC's concern with how moral frameworks are embedded in institutional structures is directly implicated.

annotations.content: has to do with knowledge rather than opinion
annotations.anchor_text: _What the Encyclopaedia presents has to do_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks this epistemic claim as a load-bearing assertion. The distinction between knowledge and opinion is central to MacIntyre's argument: the encyclopaedists claim the authority of science (episteme) while dismissing rival traditions as mere opinion (doxa). Tom is likely flagging this as a rhetorical move that forecloses genuine rational debate by pre-emptively delegitimizing tradition-based claims.
annotations.rc_relevance: MacIntyre's genealogist/tradition critique targets exactly this move: the encyclopaedist tradition masks its own tradition-bound character by claiming the universal standpoint of knowledge. RC's recovery of tradition-constituted rationality depends on exposing this sleight of hand.

annotations.content: the Ninth Edition pointed its reader towards some future edition in which the Britannica would in an important way have displaced the Bible
annotations.anchor_text: _Ninth Edition pointed its reader towards some future_
annotations.interpretation: This is perhaps the most dramatic claim on the page, and Tom's underlining signals he recognizes it as such. MacIntyre is arguing that the encyclopaedist project had an explicitly eschatological dimension — a secular replacement of scripture with encyclopaedia. Tom is likely struck by the boldness of MacIntyre's reading, which frames the Enlightenment project not as merely secular but as a rival religious form.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the heart of the RC concern with tradition vs. reform: the encyclopaedist project is not neutral modernization but the installation of a competing moral and cognitive authority structure. The displacement of the Bible as canonical book maps directly onto MacIntyre's tripartite structure — encyclopaedia vs. tradition.

annotations.content: the Encyclopaedia would have displaced the Bible as the canonical book, or set of books, of the culture.
annotations.anchor_text: _displaced the Bible as the canonical book_
annotations.interpretation: The bracket around this concluding sentence of the paragraph signals Tom treating it as a thesis statement or summary claim worth isolating. It encapsulates the entire argument of the preceding passage in one sentence. Tom may intend to quote or cite this directly in RC writing, or is marking it as MacIntyre's most quotable formulation of the encyclopaedist ambition.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this sentence crystallizes the stakes of the Enlightenment project: not mere secularization but a wholesale substitution of one canonical authority for another. This is directly relevant to RC's concern with civic and moral formation — what book, what tradition, forms the citizen?

annotations.content: women contributors were few but notable, among them Hamilton's daughter, who wrote the article on her father
annotations.anchor_text: _women contributors were few but notable_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks this detail, which MacIntyre uses to note internal diversity within the encyclopaedist project. The reference to Hamilton's daughter whose 'own thought was in movement' is MacIntyre's way of showing the tradition was not monolithic. Tom may be noting this as evidence that even within a tradition, individual agents maintain critical distance — a nuance relevant to MacIntyre's broader account of tradition-constituted rationality.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with civic friendship and the formation of intellectual community within institutions is touched here: the encyclopaedist community contained genuine intellectual diversity even while sharing a programmatic vision, which complicates any simple account of ideological capture.

annotations.content: whose own thought was in movement and who in the future would move in very different directions
annotations.anchor_text: _whose own thought was in movement_
annotations.interpretation: Tom separately underlines this phrase, distinct from the preceding underline about women contributors. The phrase 'thought was in movement' is a key MacIntyrean locution — it signals that rational enquiry is genuinely dynamic and tradition-transcending. Tom may be tracking MacIntyre's vocabulary of intellectual movement as a counterpoint to the encyclopaedists' claim to have achieved a stable synthesis.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's engagement with MacIntyre's account of tradition as living and self-correcting rather than static. The possibility of intellectual movement within and beyond a tradition is central to how RC thinks about moral reform without abandoning tradition.

annotations.content: Baynes represents a distinctive moment in the history of nineteenth-century thought in which it did appear to a great many otherwise very different thinkers that a comprehensive scientific synthesis was at hand
annotations.anchor_text: _Baynes represents a distinctive moment in the history_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks this as MacIntyre's historical contextualization of the encyclopaedist project — it was not a fringe view but a widely shared intellectual horizon. The phrase 'it did appear' is carefully hedged: MacIntyre is not endorsing the synthesis but explaining its plausibility at the time. Tom is likely noting this as MacIntyre's charitable historicism before the critique.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC must engage seriously with why the encyclopaedist vision was compelling — not merely dismiss it. MacIntyre's charitable contextualization here models the kind of intellectual engagement RC requires when confronting Enlightenment rationalism rather than caricaturing it.

annotations.content: Right-pointing angle bracket / chevron symbol ('>') in the right margin, adjacent to the paragraph about Baynes representing a distinctive moment
annotations.anchor_text: _a comprehensive scientific synthesis was at hand_
annotations.interpretation: The chevron in the right margin is a mark Tom uses to flag passages of high importance or to indicate 'look here' for later retrieval. Its placement next to the 'comprehensive scientific synthesis was at hand' sentence suggests Tom considers this MacIntyre's key historical thesis about the Ninth Edition's cultural significance. It may also indicate he intends to cross-reference this passage with another location in the text or in his RC notes.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'comprehensive scientific synthesis' claim is the positive face of what MacIntyre critiques as the encyclopaedist tradition — RC needs to understand both its appeal and its failure in order to make the case for tradition-constituted rationality as the more adequate alternative.

annotations.content: a powerful ideological vision of the nature and outcome of enquiry
annotations.anchor_text: _Baynes articulated both for his contributors and for his readers_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines 'powerful ideological vision' — the word 'ideological' is doing critical work here. MacIntyre is unmasking the encyclopaedist project as ideology in the pejorative sense: a vision that presents itself as neutral rational enquiry while serving particular interests and assumptions. Tom flags this as MacIntyre's diagnostic move, transitioning from description to critique.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's core project requires naming the ideological character of Enlightenment rationalism — that its claim to universal reason is itself a tradition-bound claim. This phrase is a concise handle for that argument and likely useful in RC writing.

annotations.content: Second right-pointing angle bracket / chevron symbol ('>') in the right margin, lower on the page, adjacent to the passage about Adam Gifford's will and the Ninth Edition
annotations.anchor_text: _Adam Gifford's will as well as other expressions of his views_
annotations.interpretation: A second chevron near the bottom of the page, marking the passage about reading Gifford's will alongside the Ninth Edition as showing 'a large coincidence of vision.' This may be Tom marking the structural argument that Gifford's project and the encyclopaedist project share the same ideological DNA — which is MacIntyre's way of contextualizing the Gifford Lectures themselves within the encyclopaedist tradition he is critiquing.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is significant for RC because MacIntyre is effectively situating his own Gifford Lectures as a response to the tradition that founded them — a self-aware act of tradition-contestation. RC's concern with how institutions shape the terms of their own critique is directly illuminated.

annotations.content: to read Adam Gifford's will, as well as other expressions of his views, alongside the Ninth Edition is to recognize a large coincidence of vision
annotations.anchor_text: _Adam Gifford's will alongside the Ninth Edition_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this claim about the coincidence between Gifford's vision and the Ninth Edition. This is MacIntyre establishing the intellectual genealogy of the lectures — Gifford himself was operating within the encyclopaedist horizon. Tom recognizes this as a key move in MacIntyre's argument: he is using the Gifford Lectures to critique the very tradition that authorized them.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this is a model of immanent critique — working within a tradition's own institutions to challenge its foundational assumptions. This mirrors RC's project of using classical and early modern resources to critique contemporary frameworks from within civic life.

annotations.content: Sciences were generally taken to be individuated by their subject matter, not by their methods
annotations.anchor_text: _Sciences were generally taken to be individuated by their subject matter_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks this as MacIntyre's account of the Ninth Edition's philosophy of science — science is defined by what it studies, not how it studies it. This has significant implications: it means theology could in principle count as a science (it has a subject matter), which is MacIntyre's opening for recovering Thomistic enquiry as scientifically legitimate. Tom is tracking the argumentative setup.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry: if science is individuated by subject matter, then the science of ethics and the science of God are not methodologically excluded from the encyclopaedist framework — they are simply given inferior or subordinate status, which is the move MacIntyre contests.

annotations.content: 1t'ing 4th / btlie 3 / on / about the / human / progress
annotations.anchor_text: _will not merely report but will themselves advance_
annotations.interpretation: This marginal note is partially legible and appears to read something like 'It's [about] the [Ninth Edition] being / about the / human progress' — Tom is glossing the opening paragraph's argument in compressed shorthand. The '4th' likely refers to the Ninth Edition (possibly a note about its place in a sequence of editions Tom is tracking), and 'btlie 3' may be an abbreviation for 'Britannica' or a section number. Tom is condensing MacIntyre's claim that the encyclopaedia is fundamentally about advancing a doctrine of human progress.
annotations.rc_relevance: The concept of 'human progress' as an ideological commitment rather than a neutral empirical finding is central to MacIntyre's critique and to RC's concern with how Enlightenment assumptions about history and moral development have displaced teleological and tradition-based accounts of human flourishing.

annotations.content: Q: 9th's / view of / science
annotations.anchor_text: _Sciences were generally taken to be individuated by their subject matter_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes 'Q: 9th's view of science' — a self-directed research question about what the Ninth Edition's philosophy of science actually was. This is Tom flagging that MacIntyre's claim about subject-matter individuation needs further verification or elaboration. He is not simply accepting MacIntyre's account but noting it as a claim that requires investigation, possibly by consulting the Ninth Edition directly.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's intellectual rigor requires not simply receiving MacIntyre's characterization of rival traditions but interrogating his sources. This annotation models the scholarly discipline of distinguishing between MacIntyre's argument and his historical evidence — important for RC's engagement with the encyclopaedist tradition on its own terms.

pages.summary: MacIntyre, via the nineteenth-century encyclopaedist tradition (represented by Baynes and the ninth edition of the Encyclopaedia Britannica), identifies four constitutive elements of genuine science: data/facts, unifying synthetic conceptions, methods of moving between facts and conceptions, and the continuous progress that results from their successful application. He then argues that this tradition held an understanding of science — both its internal details and its architectonic ordering — to be an essential part of general culture for educated persons, meaning there was no gap between professional and public scientific understanding, with figures like Agnes Mary Clerke exemplifying this ideal.

annotations.content: 9th. 4 elts of all sciences (1)
annotations.anchor_text: _four constitutive elements: first there are the data_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging that the ninth edition of the Encyclopaedia Britannica's framework identifies four constitutive elements of all sciences, and has numbered this as the first element (data/facts). The label '9th.' confirms he is tracking this as a specific historical-institutional claim about the encyclopaedist tradition's epistemology. By writing 'all sciences,' he is noting the universalizing ambition of this framework — it applies architectonically across disciplines, not merely to any one field.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects directly to MacIntyre's 'encyclopaedist' version of moral enquiry — the RC project needs to understand what the encyclopaedists claimed as the structure of rational enquiry in order to see why MacIntyre finds it inadequate as a basis for moral reasoning and civic formation.

annotations.content: not so
annotations.anchor_text: _ject matter not so individuated that treated_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is isolating the qualification 'not so' regarding the individuation of subject matter in Political Economy, suggesting he finds this epistemic diagnosis interesting or contestable. He may be noting that the encyclopaedists' criterion of individuation (clear subject matter) is itself a normative standard being applied to judge which disciplines are in 'good order,' which raises questions about who sets that standard.
annotations.rc_relevance: The question of whether a discipline's subject matter is properly individuated relates to RC's concern with MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism — fragmented or poorly-bounded disciplines mirror the fragmented moral self MacIntyre diagnoses in modernity.

annotations.anchor_text: _Secondly there are the unifying synthetic conceptions_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is numbering the second constitutive element — unifying synthetic conceptions supplied by methodical reflection upon facts. The circled number continues his structural mapping of the four elements begun in the margin note above, confirming he is treating this as an enumerated argument he wants to reconstruct or later critique.
annotations.rc_relevance: The idea of 'unifying synthetic conceptions' in science parallels what RC seeks in moral philosophy — a tradition-constituted framework that can unify disparate moral data. Tom may be considering whether MacIntyre's tradition-based enquiry offers a rival form of such synthesis.

annotations.anchor_text: _And thirdly there are the methods so employed_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is numbering the third constitutive element — the methods of moving from facts to unifying conceptions and back again in theoretical discovery and confirmation. The consistent numbering practice indicates Tom is building a schematic of the encyclopaedist epistemology that he can use as an analytical tool, likely to contrast it with Thomistic or genealogical alternatives later in the book.
annotations.rc_relevance: Methods of moving between particular facts and general conceptions is epistemologically central to MacIntyre's tripartite structure; understanding how the encyclopaedist method differs from Thomistic enquiry is essential for RC's engagement with the 'tradition vs. reform' tension.

annotations.anchor_text: _Fourthly as we noticed earlier it was taken_
annotations.interpretation: Tom completes his four-element schema by numbering the fourth element — continuous progress as the outcome of successful application of methods to facts. The consistent circling of all four numbers shows this is a deliberate structural annotation, not casual marking. Tom appears to want a portable summary of the encyclopaedist's positive scientific program before MacIntyre dismantles it.
annotations.rc_relevance: The notion of 'continuous progress' as the hallmark of genuine science is precisely what MacIntyre's genealogist version will subvert; Tom flagging it here suggests he is tracking the encyclopaedist's self-understanding as a foil for the RC project's recovery of non-progressivist, tradition-constituted rationality.

annotations.content: unifying synthetic conceptions
annotations.anchor_text: _Secondly there are the unifying synthetic conceptions supplied_
annotations.interpretation: Tom singles out this specific phrase, not just the element as a whole. 'Unifying synthetic conceptions' is a technical term in this framework and Tom appears to want to track it as a concept-label. He may be asking whether these conceptions are discovered, constructed, or tradition-dependent — a question MacIntyre will later press.
annotations.rc_relevance: The language of 'synthetic' conceptions echoes Kantian epistemology, which is part of the Enlightenment rationalism MacIntyre critiques; Tom may be flagging this as a site where the encyclopaedist project reveals its philosophical commitments.

annotations.content: from facts to unifying conceptions in theoretical discovery and from such conceptions back to the facts in the work both of explanation and of confirmation of our theories
annotations.anchor_text: _methods by which we move from facts to unifying_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the full hermeneutical circuit described here — facts to conceptions and back. This is the encyclopaedist's account of how science works as a self-correcting loop. Tom may be noting this as a proto-hermeneutical circle that MacIntyre will later argue requires a tradition-constituted framework to be intelligible, rather than the neutral rationalism the encyclopaedists assume.
annotations.rc_relevance: This hermeneutical movement between particulars and universals is structurally analogous to the Thomistic method MacIntyre champions; RC needs to articulate what distinguishes the tradition-constituted version from this encyclopaedist one.

annotations.content: continuous progress
annotations.anchor_text: _ous progress in supplying ever more adequate unifying_
annotations.interpretation: Tom isolates 'continuous progress' as a key term of the encyclopaedist tradition. This is the fourth element's defining claim and it carries significant ideological freight — it is what makes the encyclopaedist project optimistic and progressivist. Tom likely marks it because MacIntyre will challenge whether 'progress' is a coherent concept without a tradition-internal standard of evaluation.
annotations.rc_relevance: The progressivist assumption embedded in the encyclopaedist ideal is precisely what RC's recovery of classical virtue ethics resists; Aristotelian and Thomistic traditions measure enquiry by standards other than cumulative progress.

annotations.content: it is characteristic of genuine science, as contrasted with the thought of the prescientific and the nonscientific, that it has a particular kind of history, one of relatively continuous progress
annotations.anchor_text: _So it is characteristic of genuine science as contrasted_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's report of the encyclopaedist's defining criterion: genuine science is distinguished by its history of continuous progress. This is a normative-historical claim that Tom recognizes as central to the encyclopaedist worldview. The phrase 'genuine science' is doing evaluative work, and Tom is likely flagging this as the standard against which all disciplines — including moral enquiry — were judged in this tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is one of the core encyclopaedist assumptions MacIntyre must dismantle to make room for tradition-constituted moral enquiry; RC's project of recovering civility depends on showing that moral wisdom has a different kind of history — one of rupture, recovery, and tradition-dependent progress rather than linear accumulation.

annotations.content: v. sci revolutions (k)
annotations.anchor_text: _relatively continuous progress_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is cross-referencing Kuhn's 'The Structure of Scientific Revolutions' — the '(k)' almost certainly stands for Kuhn. He is noting that the encyclopaedist picture of continuous progress in science is precisely what Kuhn's account of paradigm shifts and revolutionary science disrupts. This is a sharp critical annotation: the encyclopaedists' self-image is empirically undermined by the history of science itself.
annotations.rc_relevance: This Kuhnian cross-reference supports MacIntyre's broader argument that tradition-constituted rationality applies even in natural science, not just moral enquiry — a point highly relevant to RC's attempt to show that MacIntyre's epistemology is not merely a retreat into pre-modern parochialism.

annotations.content: It is important to remark that an understanding of science thus conceived, an understanding both of at least the central details of each science and of the significance of the architectonic ordering of the sciences, was taken by Baynes and by many of his contributors to be an essential part of the general culture of the educated person.
annotations.anchor_text: _It is important to remark that an understanding of science thus conceived_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets this entire passage, indicating he regards it as a crucial, quotable claim. The argument is that for the encyclopaedists, scientific literacy — including architectonic understanding of how the sciences relate — was a civic and cultural obligation, not merely a professional one. Tom likely sees this as MacIntyre's clearest statement of the encyclopaedist ideal of an educated public, before the gap between expert and layperson widened.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's concern with the role of institutions in forming character and the concept of civic friendship — the encyclopaedist tradition held that a properly educated citizenry would share a common understanding of the sciences, which is a version of the shared rational culture RC seeks to recover on different grounds.

annotations.content: most educated all science included
annotations.anchor_text: _to be an essential part of the general culture of the educated person_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is paraphrasing and emphasizing MacIntyre's point: for the encyclopaedists, the educated person was expected to have inclusive scientific literacy — all sciences were part of general culture. The word 'most' may signal Tom's slight qualification or his noting that this was the aspiration for the educated class broadly, not just specialists. He is tracking the encyclopaedist ideal of the cultured generalist.
annotations.rc_relevance: This annotation connects to RC's theme of subsidiarity and the formation of character through institutions — the nineteenth-century ideal of general culture as the basis of civic life is a historical precedent that RC can use comparatively, even if MacIntyre ultimately finds its philosophical foundations defective.

annotations.content: 9th. public discourse on science is ted [essential?]
annotations.anchor_text: _There was not for them as there is for us a large gap_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is noting that the ninth edition's contributors assumed public discourse about science was essential — 'ted' almost certainly abbreviates 'essential' or possibly 'tended to be.' He is marking the contrast MacIntyre draws between the nineteenth-century situation (no gap between professional and public science) and the modern situation (large gap). Tom sees this as a historical-diagnostic point about the collapse of civic scientific culture.
annotations.rc_relevance: The fragmentation of public discourse about science mirrors the fragmentation of moral discourse that RC diagnoses — both involve the loss of a shared language for public reasoning. RC's project of resurrecting civility must grapple with whether a shared culture of enquiry is recoverable or whether the encyclopaedist model was itself flawed from the start.

annotations.content: an understanding of science thus conceived
annotations.anchor_text: _It is important to remark that an understanding of science_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this phrase to isolate the specific conception of scientific understanding at stake — it is not any understanding of science, but understanding 'thus conceived,' i.e., in the architectonically ordered, four-element form described above. This is a precision marker, ensuring he does not conflate this specific ideal with science in general.
annotations.rc_relevance: Precision about what 'understanding of science' means in the encyclopaedist tradition is essential for RC's comparative project — MacIntyre's critique targets this specific architectonic conception, not scientific rationality as such.

annotations.content: Agnes Mary Clerke
annotations.anchor_text: _nearer home in Edinburgh was Agnes Mary Clerke author_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines Clerke's name, likely flagging her as a concrete historical example of the encyclopaedist ideal of public scientific culture. She represents the possibility of serious scholarly work that bridges professional science and educated general readership — the kind of figure whose existence proves the encyclopaedists' cultural ideal was once realized.
annotations.rc_relevance: Clerke as a historical exemplar of integrated public intellectual culture is relevant to RC's interest in how institutions and individuals have historically embodied civic virtues; she shows that the gap between professional and public knowledge is not inevitable but historically produced.

pages.summary: This page situates Agnes Mary Clerke within a Victorian narrative of moral and scientific progress, showing how the Encyclopaedia Britannica's Ninth Edition embodied Humboldt's vision of academic disciplines mirroring the cosmos's order, progressively revealed through science. MacIntyre argues that the human sciences added a fifth self-referential characteristic to the natural sciences — that progress of all kinds (moral, scientific, technological, theological) is their central subject matter — making the emergence of enlightened figures like Gifford, Baynes, and Clerke the climax of a law-governed history. The primitive, savage, and superstitious are thus cast as inferior precursors to this enlightenment, with figures like Frazer and Sidgwick providing the moral-historical backdrop.

annotations.content: Clerke endorsed the educational views of Baron Friedrich Heinrich Alexander von Humboldt
annotations.anchor_text: _endorsed the educational views of Baron Friedrich_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is tracking the intellectual lineage connecting Clerke to Humboldt's encyclopaedic vision. This is significant because MacIntyre's encyclopaedist tradition is precisely characterized by the Humboldtian confidence that knowledge forms a unified, progressively ordered whole — and Tom is marking the personnel who embody that tradition. The underline suggests Tom sees this as a key node in MacIntyre's genealogy of the encyclopaedist.
annotations.rc_relevance: Central to RC's engagement with MacIntyre's tripartite structure: Humboldt and Clerke are avatars of the encyclopaedist rival version, whose assumption that moral and scientific progress are continuous is exactly what MacIntyre (and RC) contest.

annotations.content: it was Humboldt's vision of the ordering of the academic disciplines as mirroring the order of the cosmos itself
annotations.anchor_text: _vision of the ordering of the academic disciplines_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging this as a core claim about the encyclopaedist epistemology — the idea that disciplinary arrangement mirrors cosmic order is the theological-like confidence that MacIntyre will later critique. The underline signals that Tom recognizes this as a foundational assumption of the encyclopaedist project that RC must interrogate, since it displaces the Thomistic hierarchy of sciences with a secular analogue.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's engagement with MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism: the encyclopaedist claim that secular academic ordering mirrors reality parallels and competes with the Thomistic claim that theology orders all other sciences.

annotations.content: an ordering progressively revealed by the history of science, which informed not only Clerke's own historical and scientific work but the whole enterprise of the Ninth Edition
annotations.anchor_text: _progressively revealed by the history of science_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines 'progressively revealed' to flag the implicit philosophy of history embedded in the encyclopaedist project — the idea that history is the medium through which rational order becomes visible. This is the progressivist teleology MacIntyre will subject to genealogical critique, and Tom recognizes it as a structural assumption worth interrogating for RC.
annotations.rc_relevance: This progressivist narrative of history is precisely what RC (following MacIntyre) wants to challenge by recovering tradition-constituted enquiry, where history reveals the limits of rationalism rather than its triumph.

annotations.content: moral progress which they took to accompany scientific progress
annotations.anchor_text: _kind of moral progress which they took_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the conflation of moral and scientific progress as a key encyclopaedist assumption. The phrasing 'they took to accompany' is MacIntyre being carefully ironic — this is a belief, not a demonstrated truth — and Tom is likely flagging this as a claim RC needs to contest, since the RC project is premised on the inadequacy of progressive moral narratives divorced from virtue and tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: The identification of moral with scientific progress is exactly the Enlightenment error MacIntyre diagnoses and RC seeks to remedy by recovering Thomistic moral enquiry rooted in tradition rather than cumulative rational progress.

annotations.content: from the sheltered life of a girl in a Protestant family in West Cork
annotations.anchor_text: _sheltered life of a girl in a Protestant family_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this biographical detail, likely noting it as illustrative of the encyclopaedist narrative of personal moral development from parochialism to universal reason. MacIntyre is showing how Clerke's biography was read as a microcosm of civilization's progress from the particular to the universal — a move Tom likely finds both telling and problematic given RC's defense of local, particular communities as the proper site of moral formation.
annotations.rc_relevance: Relevant to RC's theme of subsidiarity and local community: the encyclopaedist narrative treats embeddedness in a particular family and tradition as a starting limitation to be overcome, whereas RC insists such particularity is constitutive of genuine moral formation.

annotations.content: She took her place among the foremost minds of the age
annotations.anchor_text: _She took her place among the foremost minds_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks this as an instance of the encyclopaedist canon-formation logic — 'foremost minds' is a meritocratic-progressive category that MacIntyre will later deconstruct. Tom is likely flagging how this kind of recognition operates within the encyclopaedist tradition's self-legitimating narrative, which RC must interrogate.
annotations.rc_relevance: Connects to RC's concern with how institutions form and recognize character: the encyclopaedist definition of 'foremost minds' excludes the virtue-formed practical wisdom that RC, following MacIntyre and Aristotle, sees as genuine intellectual excellence.

annotations.content: What the human sciences explained was how someone like Clerke had become possible
annotations.anchor_text: _human sciences explained was how someone like Clerke_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as perhaps the pivotal sentence on the page — MacIntyre is identifying the self-referential teleological claim of the human sciences: they explain their own preconditions. Tom likely sees this as the encyclopaedist tradition's most ambitious and most contestable claim, since it makes the tradition self-validating in a way that forecloses external critique.
annotations.rc_relevance: This self-referential move is precisely what MacIntyre's genealogist rival version attacks, and RC's recovery of Thomistic enquiry depends on showing that this self-validating closure is philosophically untenable — a tradition cannot adjudicate its own adequacy without appealing to standards outside itself.

annotations.content: What the human sciences explained was how someone like Clerke had become possible. To the four elements of the sciences of nature—data, unifying conceptions, methods, and a history of continuous progress—they added a fifth self-referential characteristic.
annotations.anchor_text: _fifth self-referential characteristic. The human sciences_
annotations.interpretation: The bracket isolates the structural argument about the fifth characteristic as a discrete unit worth extracting. Tom is likely marking this as a definition to be cited or returned to — the 'fifth self-referential characteristic' is MacIntyre's technical term for what distinguishes the human sciences within the encyclopaedist framework. The bracket suggests Tom sees this as a key argumentative move to track across the book.
annotations.rc_relevance: The self-referential characteristic of the human sciences is the epistemological mechanism by which the encyclopaedist tradition naturalizes its own moral assumptions, which is exactly the kind of ideological sleight-of-hand RC (following MacIntyre) must expose to recover genuine moral enquiry.

annotations.content: How w Sciences.
annotations.anchor_text: _What the human sciences explained was how_
annotations.interpretation: This appears to be Tom abbreviating 'How [the] Human Sciences [work/function/self-legitimate]' — a compressed pointer back to the structural claim MacIntyre is making. The period after 'Sciences' suggests this is a heading or label rather than a question, indicating Tom is categorizing this passage as illustrating the method or logic of the encyclopaedist human sciences. It may also be shorthand for a cross-reference to another section of the book where MacIntyre elaborates this point.
annotations.rc_relevance: Tom is flagging the epistemological self-description of the encyclopaedist human sciences as a key target for RC's critique — understanding how these sciences legitimate themselves is prerequisite to MacIntyre's (and RC's) project of articulating a rival, tradition-constituted alternative.

annotations.content: progress of all kinds, moral, scientific, technological, theological, is their central subject matter and conceptions of progress and of its inevitability are among their most important unifying conceptions
annotations.anchor_text: _progress of all kinds, moral, scientific, technological_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the list — moral, scientific, technological, theological — with special attention to 'theological' appearing in this catalogue, which suggests MacIntyre is showing that the encyclopaedist tradition has colonized even theological categories under the rubric of progress. Tom likely flags 'theological' as particularly significant because it shows the encyclopaedist tradition is not merely secular but actively re-defines theological enquiry in progressivist terms.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's concern with the role of theological tradition in moral enquiry: when theology is listed alongside technology as merely one more domain of 'progress,' the tradition-constituted character of theological reasoning is evacuated — precisely what MacIntyre's Thomistic rival version aims to restore.

annotations.content: progress of all kinds, moral, scientific, technological, theological, is their central subject matter and conceptions of progress and of its inevitability are among their most important unifying conceptions
annotations.anchor_text: _inevitability are among their most important unifying_
annotations.interpretation: The bracket reinforces the underline, suggesting Tom wants to isolate this as a quotable, citable passage. The combination of underline and bracket typically signals Tom intends to use this text directly in writing. The word 'inevitability' is particularly important — it signals the quasi-deterministic character of the encyclopaedist worldview, which forecloses the genuine contingency that tradition-based moral enquiry requires.
annotations.rc_relevance: The notion that progress is 'inevitable' is incompatible with the RC recovery of virtue ethics, which requires genuine moral agency and the possibility of regression — MacIntyre's whole argument depends on showing that the encyclopaedist narrative of inevitability is a retrospective construction, not a metaphysical law.

annotations.content: a law-governed history whose climax so far is their own emergence
annotations.anchor_text: _law-governed history whose climax so far is their own_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks 'climax so far' as a revealing phrase — MacIntyre is exposing the teleological self-congratulation embedded in the encyclopaedist narrative. The phrase 'so far' is ironic: it presents the tradition's own moment as the apex of history while leaving formal room for further progress, but the logic is self-serving. Tom likely sees this as a key instance of the encyclopaedist tradition's bad faith.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's engagement with MacIntyre's genealogist critique hinges on exposing this kind of self-congratulatory teleology: the encyclopaedist tradition declares itself the culmination of history, which is exactly the move Nietzsche's genealogist unmasks — and which RC must address without simply endorsing the genealogist's nihilism.

annotations.content: still producing best practitioners to date
annotations.anchor_text: _Adam Gifford, Thomas Spencer Baynes, and Agnes Mary Clerke_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is glossing MacIntyre's point about Gifford, Baynes, and Clerke by noting that within the encyclopaedist narrative these figures represent the tradition's best current exemplars — 'still producing best practitioners to date' captures the self-legitimating logic whereby the tradition validates itself by pointing to its own outstanding members. Tom may be noting this ironically, flagging that the tradition's self-assessment cannot be taken at face value.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's concern with how institutions form character and how traditions identify and authorize their own exemplars: MacIntyre's argument is that the encyclopaedist tradition's practitioners are not genuinely virtuous by the standards of tradition-constituted enquiry but are instead exemplars of a rival, ultimately incoherent moral framework.

annotations.content: Right-pointing arrow/chevron ('>') in the right margin
annotations.anchor_text: _In all history but perhaps particularly in the history of moral_
annotations.interpretation: A pointing arrow in MacIntyre's margin typically signals 'this is important — follow this argument forward.' Tom is likely marking the transition to the next paragraph's argument about the primitive and savage as inferior precursors, flagging it as a key moment where MacIntyre's critique of the encyclopaedist moral narrative becomes most explicit. It may also signal a connection to another passage earlier or later in the book.
annotations.rc_relevance: The passage being flagged — about primitive and savage as inferior precursors to enlightenment — is directly relevant to RC's concern with how the encyclopaedist tradition constructs moral hierarchy and excludes traditional communities, a construction MacIntyre and RC must contest by showing that 'primitive' traditions may encode genuine moral wisdom.

annotations.content: In all history but perhaps particularly in the history of moral beliefs and practices the primitive, the savage, and the superstitious are thus seen as the inferior precursors of enlightenment
annotations.anchor_text: _primitive, the savage, and the superstitious are thus seen_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as MacIntyre's sharpest statement of the encyclopaedist moral hierarchy. The phrase 'inferior precursors' is the key — it encodes a progressivist, developmental logic that renders traditional and pre-modern moral communities as merely preparatory to the enlightened present. Tom is likely flagging this as a claim RC must directly address, since RC's defense of tradition requires contesting the 'inferior precursor' status assigned to pre-modern moral frameworks.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the encyclopaedist move that RC most urgently contests: by classifying traditional moral communities as 'inferior precursors,' the Enlightenment narrative delegitimizes the very resources — classical virtue ethics, Thomistic moral theology, civic friendship — that RC seeks to recover.

pages.summary: MacIntyre situates Robert Flint — an unjustly neglected nineteenth-century philosopher — as the key theological contributor to the Ninth Edition of the Encyclopaedia Britannica, whose conception of theology as a science beginning from the data of nature and revelation aligned him with both his fellow contributors and Adam Gifford himself. Flint's central question — what constitutes genuinely scientific knowledge in theology — frames natural theology as a science still in the making, one whose method demands beginning at the right point or being forced to begin again. This places Gifford's project squarely within the encyclopaedist tradition's confidence in rational, progressive moral and scientific enquiry.

annotations.content: Robert Flint's article 'Theology.'
annotations.anchor_text: _cloth to Robert Flint's article Theology_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets Flint's name and article title at its first appearance, marking this as a key node in the encyclopaedist network MacIntyre is assembling. The bracket likely signals that Flint is a figure Tom wants to track — either because he is unfamiliar and needs following up, or because Flint's role as theological anchor to the encyclopaedist project is structurally important to MacIntyre's argument about the Ninth Edition as a unified moral-intellectual project.
annotations.rc_relevance: Flint's theology-as-science project exemplifies the encyclopaedist confidence in rational systematic enquiry that MacIntyre contrasts with tradition-constituted reasoning — directly relevant to RC's engagement with the tripartite structure of encyclopaedist, genealogist, and tradition.

annotations.content: Robert Flint is the most unjustly forgotten philosopher of the nineteenth century.
annotations.anchor_text: _Robert Flint is the most unjustly forgotten_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this evaluative claim — MacIntyre's strong assertion about Flint's historical neglect. This is the kind of bold historiographical claim Tom would flag either to contest or to use: it positions Flint as a recovered figure whose ideas are being rehabilitated, which mirrors RC's own project of recovering neglected traditions. Tom may also be noting the irony that MacIntyre, a critic of the encyclopaedist tradition, nonetheless rescues one of its key figures from obscurity.
annotations.rc_relevance: The recovery of neglected thinkers is central to RC's methodology — the project itself aims to resurrect civility traditions that have been marginalized. Flint's case is a micro-instance of that broader recovery logic.

annotations.content: Flint (Gifted) Assoc. Theo
annotations.anchor_text: _unjustly forgotten philosopher of the nineteenth century_
annotations.interpretation: Tom abbreviates a quick characterization of Flint: gifted, and associated with Theology (likely shorthand for his role as the theology article contributor). The parenthetical 'Gifted' may be Tom's own evaluative reaction to MacIntyre's claim that Flint is unjustly forgotten, or it could be shorthand for 'Gifford-associated.' This is a working note to remember who Flint is and his structural role without re-reading the passage.
annotations.rc_relevance: Tracking Flint as a theological figure within the encyclopaedist project is relevant to RC's analysis of how institutions (like Gifford Lectures) shape moral and theological enquiry — Flint embodies the encyclopaedist's theological arm.

annotations.content: Theo
annotations.anchor_text: _chair of divinity in Edinburgh in 1876_
annotations.interpretation: A secondary margin label, likely reinforcing or indexing Flint's disciplinary identity as a theologian distinct from a philosopher of science or morals. Tom may be distinguishing Flint's role from the other contributors (Tylor = anthropology, Robertson Smith = Bible) to map the division of labor within the encyclopaedist project.
annotations.rc_relevance: The disciplinary mapping of the Ninth Edition contributors is relevant to RC's concern with how institutions organize knowledge — who gets to speak about morality, theology, and science, and how those jurisdictions are drawn.

annotations.content: all the more impressive to find how much he, despite his atypicality, agreed with the consensus of the Ninth Edition.
annotations.anchor_text: _all the more impressive to find how much he despite his atypicality_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the paradox MacIntyre identifies: Flint's atypicality (home in French and Italian philosophy when Scots looked to Germany) makes his agreement with the encyclopaedist consensus more, not less, significant. Tom is tracking MacIntyre's argumentative move of using Flint's outlier status to strengthen the claim that the Ninth Edition had a genuine, coherent consensus — not just a parochial Scottish agreement.
annotations.rc_relevance: This speaks to RC's interest in tradition vs. reform: even a thinker who crosses intellectual traditions can converge on shared moral-epistemological commitments, suggesting the encyclopaedist project had internal coherence worth analyzing seriously before critiquing.

annotations.content: begins with the data, both the data of nature which furnish the starting point for natural theology and those provided by the Bible which furnish the starting point for a theology of revelation.
annotations.anchor_text: _begins with the data both the data of nature_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines Flint's epistemological method: a two-source data model (nature + Bible) as the foundation of theological science. This is structurally important to MacIntyre's argument that Flint's theology-as-science shares the encyclopaedist's empiricist starting point even while treating revealed data as legitimate. Tom is likely noting this as the moment where Flint's method is most precisely stated — it will contrast later with tradition-constituted or genealogical approaches to moral knowledge.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's engagement with subsidiarity and institutional formation of character requires understanding how different traditions ground moral knowledge — Flint's dual-data method is the encyclopaedist's answer, which MacIntyre will eventually contest on behalf of tradition-constituted enquiry.

annotations.content: The subject matter of theology is religion, but that delimitation does not exclude God from that subject matter.
annotations.anchor_text: _subject matter of theology is religion but that delimitation_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks Flint's careful philosophical distinction: theology's subject is religion (a human phenomenon), yet this doesn't reduce theology to mere anthropology — God remains within scope as the object of religious experience. This is a philosophically precise move that prevents the collapse of theology into sociology of religion. Tom may be noting this as the moment where Flint resists the reductionism that later genealogists (Nietzsche, Foucault) would champion.
annotations.rc_relevance: This distinction is relevant to RC's concern with the objectivity of moral and theological claims — whether civility and virtue have a ground beyond social construction. Flint's insistence that God is not excluded from theological subject matter parallels RC's resistance to pure sociological reductionism in ethics.

annotations.content: For religious experience is experience of God and the inescapability of the objectivity of God as the object both of religious experience and of theological reflection is reported as one of the findings of theology as a science.
annotations.anchor_text: _inescapability of the objectivity of God as the object_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles this as a climactic claim — Flint's assertion that the objectivity of God is not a presupposition but a finding of theological science. The circle marks this as a key proposition to return to, likely because it is the most philosophically contestable claim on the page. Tom may be flagging it to ask: is this a legitimate scientific finding or a smuggled theological assumption? MacIntyre himself will presumably scrutinize this from the perspective of tradition-constituted rationality.
annotations.rc_relevance: The question of whether moral and religious claims can be 'findings' of rational enquiry is central to RC's engagement with MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism — Flint represents the encyclopaedist optimism that they can, which MacIntyre will challenge.

annotations.content: the findings of theology as a science.
annotations.anchor_text: _findings of theology as a science_
annotations.interpretation: A secondary, more targeted underline within the larger circled passage — Tom isolates the specific phrase 'findings of theology as a science' as a key term of art. This phrase encapsulates the encyclopaedist epistemological claim and will likely serve as a contrast point when MacIntyre introduces the tradition and genealogy alternatives. Tom is flagging it as a term to track.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery of classical moral enquiry requires engaging with whether moral truths are 'found' through rational science (encyclopaedist), deconstructed as power (genealogist), or received through tradition (Thomist) — this phrase marks the encyclopaedist position at its clearest.

annotations.content: Gifford's agreement w/ Flint
annotations.anchor_text: _to a very large degree the project set out in Adam Gifford's will_
annotations.interpretation: Tom notes the alignment MacIntyre draws between Gifford's own views (in his will and lectures) and Flint's theological-scientific method. This is a structural annotation: Tom is marking the moment where MacIntyre closes the loop between the institutional patron (Gifford) and the intellectual contributor (Flint), showing the project had a unified vision. This matters for RC because it suggests the Gifford Lectures were not a neutral platform but an expression of a specific epistemological tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's interest in how institutions form character and enquiry is directly engaged here — the Gifford Lectures as institution embodied a particular encyclopaedist conception of rational moral progress, not a neutral space for theological debate.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the Enlightenment project of a unified, rationally contestable science of the whole has been radically undermined — not merely interrupted by external factors, but constitutively fractured by rupture and discontinuity. Drawing on Bachelard, Polanyi, Kuhn, and Foucault, he shows that the very history of rationality and science is itself a history of ruptures, making the assumptions underlying Adam Gifford's original project culturally uninhabitable. The central question becomes whether a systematically continuous and fruitful enquiry in natural theology is still possible, and MacIntyre suggests the answer is 'No' to the old form but 'Yes' to a radically renovated version.
