annotations.content: ② problem of unified rationality K, vs. oo, so shaped,
annotations.anchor_text: _tectonic of the sciences matched that of_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is numbering this as the second major problem MacIntyre identifies on this page: the collapse of unified rationality. The 'K' likely refers to Kuhn, 'vs. oo' may mean 'versus the encyclopaedist whole' or an infinite/universal claim, and 'so shaped' suggests Tom is noting that the very shape of enquiry was conditioned by a now-defunct assumption of rational unity. He is tracking MacIntyre's diagnostic argument that the Enlightenment's self-image was architecturally unsound.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly maps to the RC theme of MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism — specifically how the encyclopaedist tradition's foundationalist ambitions collapse under the weight of historical discontinuity, which is the precondition for recovering tradition-constituted enquiry.

annotations.content: ③ inevitable progress, is inevitable discontinuity
annotations.anchor_text: _history of inevitable progress judged by a standard_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is crystallizing MacIntyre's ironic inversion: the Enlightenment narrative of inevitable progress is itself undone by the equally inevitable reality of discontinuity. The numbering '③' suggests Tom is building a sequential list of MacIntyre's argumentative moves, and this entry captures the dialectical reversal at the heart of the page. Tom likely finds this rhetorically powerful and conceptually central.
annotations.rc_relevance: This speaks to the RC tension between tradition and reform — the assumption that moral and rational progress is linear is precisely what the genealogist (and MacIntyre) contests, and recovering Thomistic enquiry requires abandoning that progressivist self-understanding.

annotations.content: Kuhn, and Foucault, the importance of rupture and discontinuity,
annotations.anchor_text: _in recognizing the occurrence and in the light_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has highlighted 'Kuhn' (in orange/red) and then the phrase 'the importance of rupture and discontinuity' together, suggesting he sees these thinkers as the intellectual warrant for MacIntyre's anti-progressivist epistemology. The selective emphasis on 'rupture and discontinuity' as a phrase indicates Tom is flagging this as a key theoretical term — one that will recur and that distinguishes genealogical from encyclopaedist consciousness.
annotations.rc_relevance: Rupture and discontinuity are structurally important to RC's tripartite framework: the encyclopaedist denies them, the genealogist weaponizes them, and the tradition-bearer must account for them without being undone by them — this is the tightrope MacIntyre walks.

annotations.content: fruitful enquiry, historically continuous with those of traditional natural theology
annotations.anchor_text: _however it means Is a systematic and fruitful enquiry_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this phrase to mark MacIntyre's precise redefinition of what would count as a viable successor to Gifford's project. The words 'historically continuous' are crucial — MacIntyre is not abandoning continuity altogether but relocating it within tradition rather than within Enlightenment progress. Tom seems to be tracking this as a constructive moment in an otherwise diagnostic argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects directly to RC's interest in how institutions and traditions sustain moral enquiry across time — 'historical continuity' here is not encyclopaedist progressivism but tradition-constituted rationality, which is foundational to the RC recovery project.

annotations.content: we are confronted with an impressive contemporary case for answering 'No,' but also with a contemporary case
annotations.anchor_text: _then we are confronted with an impressive contemporary_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's double 'No/Yes' structure — 'No' to Gifford's original form, 'Yes' to a renovated enquiry. The underlining of the full phrase including 'albeit necessarily a very different one' (implied by the continuation) suggests Tom is marking this as the argumentative pivot of the chapter, where critique turns toward constructive possibility. This is a structural hinge Tom will likely return to.
annotations.rc_relevance: This pivot is central to RC's animating question: can classical traditions of civility and virtue be genuinely recovered, or only archaeologically described? MacIntyre's 'Yes, but differently' is the methodological template RC operates under.

annotations.content: the assumptions and beliefs characteristic of the Ninth Edition of the Encyclopaedia Britannica,
annotations.anchor_text: _ing the assumptions and beliefs characteristic of_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the specific identification of the Ninth Edition of the Encyclopaedia Britannica as the embodiment of the encyclopaedist tradition's assumptions. This is the concrete historical instantiation of the abstract epistemological critique above. Tom is likely marking this as the referent for 'encyclopaedist' throughout the book — a useful anchor for the tripartite structure.
annotations.rc_relevance: The Encyclopaedia Britannica (9th Edition) is MacIntyre's paradigm case for the encyclopaedist version of moral enquiry, which RC explicitly opposes as the dominant but now incoherent framework shaping contemporary liberal moral discourse.

annotations.content: A 3 9th
annotations.anchor_text: _case for answering No but also with a contemporary_
annotations.interpretation: Tom draws a boxed label with 'A', '3', and '9th' connected by a line to '3RV' at the bottom. This appears to be a cross-referencing notation: 'A' may denote the first rival version (encyclopaedist/Aristotelian), '3' refers to Three Rival Versions, and '9th' specifies the Ninth Edition of the Encyclopaedia Britannica as the textual locus of that version. The line to '3RV' confirms Tom is explicitly tagging this passage as architecturally central to the book's tripartite structure.
annotations.rc_relevance: This cross-reference is Tom doing structural cartography of MacIntyre's argument — identifying where the encyclopaedist version is formally introduced as a historical-textual object, which is essential for RC's deployment of MacIntyre's framework as a diagnostic tool for contemporary moral fragmentation.

annotations.content: 3RV — written at bottom left with a line connecting upward to the boxed A/3/9th label
annotations.anchor_text: _the possibility of a radical renovation of a project_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes '3RV' (Three Rival Versions) at the bottom margin and connects it via a drawn line to the boxed notation above, creating a visual anchor showing that this passage is a key locus in the book's overall argument. This is Tom's bibliographic-structural shorthand, confirming he sees this page as a load-bearing moment in MacIntyre's argument about the three rival versions.
annotations.rc_relevance: The notation '3RV' signals Tom is situating this page within the macro-structure of MacIntyre's project as a whole — a practice of reading that RC itself models, insisting that moral enquiry must be understood within the tradition that makes it intelligible.

pages.summary: MacIntyre sets up the three rival versions of moral enquiry by identifying the founding documents of two of the three traditions: Nietzsche's 'Zur Genealogie der Moral' (1887) as the charter of the genealogist project, which seeks to undermine the encyclopaedist's canonical ambitions and displace the very notion of a canon, and Pope Leo XIII's 'Aeterni Patris' (1879) as the charter of the Thomistic tradition, which calls for renewal of intellectual enquiry through Aquinas as a resource for critiquing modern rationality while preserving the canonical status of Scripture. MacIntyre then notes that Adam Gifford's will — the third document in play — understood ethics and morality as grounded not in human will or convention but in the nature and essence of the unchangeable God, aligning Gifford's project with natural theology. The page thus establishes the structural tripartite conflict that organizes the entire book, showing each rival tradition's self-understanding through its founding text.

annotations.content: CA
annotations.anchor_text: _way to his own, the other aspires_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has circled what appears to be his own earlier annotation or a printed mark near the phrase about one party writing an epitaph for the other's inquiry. The 'CA' may stand for 'Central Argument' or 'Canonical Authority' — Tom is flagging that this moment crystallizes the adversarial relationship between the traditions. It may also be a cross-reference marker to another page or note in his system.
annotations.rc_relevance: This flags the zero-sum framing of competing traditions, directly relevant to RC's concern with whether rival moral traditions can coexist or must displace one another — a tension MacIntyre himself later tries to resolve through tradition-constituted rationality.

annotations.content: construction of a type of subversive narrative
annotations.anchor_text: _provided was not only an argument in favor of_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's characterization of Nietzsche's genealogical method as producing a 'subversive narrative' rather than a straightforward argument. This marks a key methodological distinction: the genealogist doesn't refute the encyclopaedist on shared rational grounds but destabilizes the very framework of rational inquiry. Tom is likely tracking how MacIntyre frames genre as constitutive of the rival versions, not merely incidental.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with tradition vs. reform turns on exactly this: whether reform of moral community requires working within inherited rational frameworks or subverting them. The genealogist's subversive narrative is the anti-tradition move RC must understand and counter.

annotations.content: designed to undermine the central assumptions of the Encyclopaedia
annotations.anchor_text: _but a paradigm for the construction of a type_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the explicit statement of Nietzsche's oppositional project. This is not just critique but designed undermining — a hostile act toward Enlightenment rationalism. Tom is tracking MacIntyre's framing of the genealogist as parasitically defined against the encyclopaedist, which raises the question of whether genealogy can stand alone or only as negation.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's engagement with MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism finds its sharpest edge here: the genealogist doesn't offer an alternative moral community but dissolves the conditions for one. This is precisely the crisis RC is responding to.

annotations.content: A Nietzsche
annotations.anchor_text: _Zur Genealogie der Moral published by Nietzsche in 1887_
annotations.interpretation: Tom labels this 'A' in his structural scheme, identifying Nietzsche as the first of the three rival traditions. The letter 'A' suggests Tom is creating a numbered/lettered index of the three rivals as MacIntyre introduces them — likely A (Nietzsche/genealogist), B (Leo XIII/Thomism), with the encyclopaedist presumably labeled elsewhere. This is organizational reading, building a map of the book's argumentative architecture.
annotations.rc_relevance: Identifying Nietzsche as 'A' in the tripartite structure is foundational for RC's framework — MacIntyre's three rivals give RC its diagnostic vocabulary for understanding contemporary moral fragmentation as a conflict between genealogy, encyclopaedism, and tradition.

annotations.content: discredit the whole notion of a canon
annotations.anchor_text: _For the other rival party its charter document_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the most radical claim MacIntyre makes about the genealogist's ambition: not merely to propose a different canon but to invalidate canonicity as such. This is a sharper move than the encyclopaedist's displacement of the Bible — it attacks the very logic of authoritative texts. Tom is likely flagging this as the key structural asymmetry between the three rivals.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry depends on the legitimacy of canonical authority — Scripture, Aquinas, natural law tradition. The genealogist's attack on canonicity itself is the deepest threat RC must address, not merely competing content but competing meta-frameworks.

annotations.content: Aeterni Patris
annotations.anchor_text: _charter document is the encyclical letter Aeterni Patris published by_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the title of Leo XIII's encyclical, marking it as the founding document of the Thomistic tradition. This is a tracking underline — Tom wants to be able to locate the three rival charter documents quickly. It also signals that Tom recognizes the institutional and ecclesiastical weight of this document relative to Nietzsche's philosophical text.
annotations.rc_relevance: Aeterni Patris is the institutional origin of the Thomistic revival MacIntyre draws on. RC's engagement with subsidiarity and institution-forming character finds its deepest roots in this tradition, making this a key reference point.

annotations.content: B: Leo XIII (Thomism)
annotations.anchor_text: _Pope Leo XIII in 1879 four years after the Ninth Edition_
annotations.interpretation: Tom labels Leo XIII's Aeterni Patris as 'B' in his structural scheme — completing the pairing with 'A: Nietzsche.' The parenthetical '(Thomism)' shows Tom is not just noting the historical figure but categorizing the tradition. This confirms Tom is building a running index of MacIntyre's three rivals across the margins, enabling quick structural navigation of the book.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry is directly indexed here. By labeling this 'B: Thomism,' Tom signals that this is the tradition RC most closely aligns with — the one that can provide resources for radical criticism of modern rationality while preserving the basis for civic moral community.

annotations.content: a renewal of an understanding of intellectual enquiry as the continuation of a specific type of tradition
annotations.anchor_text: _Aeterni Patris summoned its readers to a renewal_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's summary of what Aeterni Patris called for — not merely a return to Aquinas but a reconceptualization of inquiry itself as tradition-constituted. This is a pivotal formulation for MacIntyre's entire project: rationality is not tradition-independent (the encyclopaedist's assumption) but requires a specific tradition to be intelligible. Tom is marking this as the positive alternative to both encyclopaedism and genealogy.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is perhaps the single most RC-relevant passage on the page: 'intellectual enquiry as the continuation of a specific type of tradition' is precisely the model RC proposes for recovering civility — not abstract principles but lived, transmitted, community-embedded practices of moral reasoning.

annotations.content: the writings of Aquinas, one the appropriation of which could not only provide the resources for radical criticism of the conception of rationality dominant in nineteenth-century modernity
annotations.anchor_text: _that which achieved definitive expression in the writings of Aquinas_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's claim that Thomism provides resources for radical criticism of modern rationality — not merely conservative defense. This is crucial: the Thomistic tradition is not reactionary but offers a substantive alternative rationality that can critique modernity on its own terms. Tom is flagging that this tradition is both historically grounded and critically potent.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's argument is not nostalgic but claims that classical and Thomistic traditions offer genuinely superior resources for moral community. This passage grounds that claim — Thomism can criticize liberal individualism from a position of rational strength, not mere tradition-loyalty.

annotations.content: also preserve and justify the canonical status of the Bible as distinct from, yet hegemonic over, all secular enquiry
annotations.anchor_text: _but also preserve and justify the canonical status_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the Thomistic tradition's double move: preserving Scripture's canonical authority while also engaging secular enquiry — distinct from but hegemonic over it. This is MacIntyre's account of how Thomism navigates the religion/reason divide differently from both the encyclopaedist (who subordinates religion to reason) and the genealogist (who dissolves both). Tom is tracking this as the tradition's structural claim about authority.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with civic friendship and moral community must navigate the public/private, sacred/secular divide. The Thomistic claim of Scripture as 'distinct from yet hegemonic over' secular enquiry models a way of grounding civic morality in transcendent authority without sectarianism — a key RC tension.

annotations.content: Each of these two documents bears the marks of its time and place of origin as clearly as does Adam Gifford's will
annotations.anchor_text: _Each of these two documents bears the marks_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's historicizing move — all three charter documents are historically situated, including Gifford's will. This is MacIntyre guarding against any claim that one tradition speaks from a view from nowhere. Tom is flagging this as methodologically significant: MacIntyre is applying his own tradition-constituted rationality thesis reflexively, acknowledging that the Thomistic tradition is also historically embedded.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC cannot claim that classical traditions of civility are timeless or culturally neutral — they too are historically constituted. This annotation signals Tom's awareness that RC must argue for the superiority of recovered traditions on tradition-constituted grounds, not on claims to universal validity.

annotations.content: It will therefore be important to ask why, after the transformations of the intervening hundred years, they are able to play a part in defining the conflicts of the present in a way in which Adam Gifford's will is not
annotations.anchor_text: _important to ask why after the transformations_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's central diagnostic question for the book: why have Nietzsche and Leo XIII's documents remained culturally potent while Gifford's Victorian liberal synthesis has not? This is the hinge of MacIntyre's argument — the failure of Gifford's ecumenical natural theology project to survive modernity's conflicts. Tom is marking this as the book's animating puzzle.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's project: if Gifford's attempt to recover natural theology for civic purposes failed, what makes RC's recovery of civility traditions different? Tom is implicitly asking whether RC faces the same fate as Gifford's project — a key self-critical question for the project.

annotations.content: eternal and unchangeable system and scheme of morality and ethics, founded not on the will, or on the devices, or in the ingenuity of man, but on the nature and essence of the unchangeable God
annotations.anchor_text: _that there is an eternal and unchangeable system_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines Gifford's own statement of his theological grounding for ethics — a strong natural law claim rooted in divine essence rather than human will or convention. This is Gifford's Thomistic moment, aligning his project with the tradition MacIntyre champions. Tom is likely marking the irony that Gifford's stated theological grounding was stronger than his Victorian liberal institutional framing, yet his project still failed to generate the kind of tradition-constituted moral community MacIntyre describes.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's grounding of moral community in something beyond human convention finds its classical articulation here. Gifford's formulation — morality founded on 'the nature and essence of the unchangeable God' rather than will or ingenuity — is the anti-voluntarist, anti-constructivist foundation RC seeks to recover for contemporary civic life.

pages.summary: MacIntyre identifies five key characteristics of morality as it functioned in Adam Gifford's Victorian culture: (1) morality as an autonomous, compartmentalized domain distinct from aesthetics, religion, economics, law, and science; (2) morality as primarily rule-following and ritualized response to transgressions; (3) moral rules as chiefly negative prohibitions, with condemnation attached especially to their breach; (4) a strong culture of propriety tied to compartmentalized social boundaries; and (5) widespread practical agreement on moral content coexisting with deep intellectual disagreement about rational justification, both underpinned by a shared conviction that morality must be rationally justifiable. This five-part typology sets up MacIntyre's diagnosis of what went wrong in Enlightenment moral culture — the conditions that made the 'encyclopaedist' project both plausible and ultimately incoherent.

annotations.content: ME Nath, Per G.lRed + (Inter) Mor Z
annotations.anchor_text: _Adam Gifford's Project in Context_
annotations.interpretation: Tom appears to be cross-referencing MacIntyre ('ME') to natural theology ('Nath'), Gifford ('Per G.lRed' likely shorthand for 'Per Gifford's Reading' or 'Per Gifford's Lecture'), and interlocutors or interferences ('Inter'), possibly also noting moral rationalism ('Mor Z'). This top-of-page note functions as a meta-index connecting this passage to a larger network of sources or arguments Tom is tracking — likely flagging this page as a node in a genealogy of natural theology and moral rationalism relevant to the book's tripartite structure.
annotations.rc_relevance: This cross-reference signals Tom mapping MacIntyre's critique of the encyclopaedist tradition back to its institutional origins in the Gifford Lectures, directly relevant to RC's concern with how institutions (like lecture series) shape and constrain moral enquiry.

annotations.content: If therefore in these lectures I focus upon questions about the nature and status of morality and about the nature of enquiry into the nature and status of morality
annotations.anchor_text: _If therefore in these lectures I focus_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets MacIntyre's self-situating methodological statement, marking the moment where MacIntyre announces his own project's scope. The bracket with what appears to be an 'S' or opening marker suggests Tom sees this as the start of a structurally significant unit — possibly marking where the book's explicit argument begins after contextual setup. Tom may be noting the reflexivity here: MacIntyre is himself doing what Gifford intended, yet critically.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's concern with how tradition-bound enquiry is always already situated — MacIntyre's self-awareness about occupying a Gifffordian institutional position mirrors the RC project's own need to locate itself within, rather than above, the traditions it studies.

annotations.content: G. on morality
annotations.anchor_text: _I begin then by asking what morality was for Adam Gifford's culture_
annotations.interpretation: Tom abbreviates 'Gifford on morality' — a simple structural label flagging that the following passage is MacIntyre's reconstruction of Gifford's (and his culture's) moral framework. The brevity suggests Tom is building a quick-reference index of this page's argumentative moves for later retrieval or citation.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's interest in how inherited cultural frameworks constitute moral understanding — MacIntyre's reconstruction of Gifford's moral world exemplifies the 'tradition as context of enquiry' theme central to RC.

annotations.content: ① autonomous area
annotations.anchor_text: _distinct and relatively autonomous area of beliefs, attitudes_
annotations.interpretation: Tom numbers this as the first of the five characteristics, tracking MacIntyre's enumeration. Labeling it 'autonomous area' compresses the idea that Victorian morality was sharply compartmentalized from religion, law, aesthetics, etc. Tom is likely building a numbered schema he can reference — possibly to later compare this Victorian moral structure against Thomistic or Aristotelian alternatives where such compartmentalization would be alien.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'autonomous area' characteristic is precisely what MacIntyre's Thomistic alternative contests — RC's project of recovering integrated virtue ethics directly challenges the compartmentalization of morality that this first characteristic names.

annotations.content: ② rule-based
annotations.anchor_text: _morality was primarily a matter of rule-following and of ritualized responses_
annotations.interpretation: Tom's second numbered annotation tags MacIntyre's identification of rule-following as the dominant mode of Victorian moral life. By labeling it simply 'rule-based,' Tom highlights the contrast with virtue-based or teleological ethics. This is a key term in MacIntyre's broader argument: the reduction of morality to rules is a symptom of the loss of the Aristotelian framework of virtues and telos.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry is precisely a recovery from rule-based morality toward virtue and character formation — Tom's annotation marks the diagnostic target that RC's project aims to address.

annotations.content: ③ chiefly prohibitive
annotations.anchor_text: _the rules, breaches of which chiefly invited such condemnations_
annotations.interpretation: Third in Tom's numbered schema, 'chiefly prohibitive' names the negative character of Victorian moral rules — morality as a system of 'thou shalt nots' rather than positive prescriptions for flourishing. Tom's choice of 'prohibitive' (over MacIntyre's 'negative prohibitions') suggests he is reframing slightly, perhaps to connect to a broader discourse about deontological versus eudaemonistic ethics.
annotations.rc_relevance: The prohibition-centered moral culture MacIntyre describes is precisely what civic friendship and subsidiarity in RC cannot operate within — positive civic virtues require more than rule compliance, and Tom is tracking this contrast.

annotations.content: ④ emph. on propriety
annotations.anchor_text: _strong notions of impropriety attached to violations of the compartmentalizing boundaries_
annotations.interpretation: Tom abbreviates 'emphasis on propriety,' capturing MacIntyre's fourth characteristic: the culture's obsession with knowing the appropriate behavior for each compartmentalized domain of social life. The annotation shows Tom noting that propriety functioned as a quasi-moral category, which MacIntyre treats with some irony — impropriety itself being seen as a species of immorality. Tom may be flagging this for its relevance to how civility can degenerate into mere etiquette.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's distinction between genuine civility rooted in virtue and the mere performance of propriety — a tension between authentic moral community and social conformism that RC must navigate.

annotations.content: ⑤ agreement on conduct action F not M yet ME Rational
annotations.anchor_text: _social agreement, especially in practice, on the importance and the content of morality coexisted with large intellectual disagreements_
annotations.interpretation: Tom's most complex annotation on the page. 'Agreement on conduct/action' captures the practical consensus; 'F not M' likely means 'Form not Matter' or 'Function not Meaning' — the agreement was behavioral/practical, not grounded in shared metaphysical or theoretical justification. 'Yet ME Rational' flags MacIntyre's point that this culture still assumed morality must be rationally justifiable 'somehow or other.' Tom is tracking the internal tension MacIntyre identifies: a culture that agrees on moral behavior but cannot agree on why it is binding — the very condition that generates the encyclopaedist crisis.
annotations.rc_relevance: This annotation goes to the heart of RC's central problematic: the fragmentation MacIntyre diagnoses is not merely practical but epistemic — the loss of a shared rational foundation for morality is what RC, following MacIntyre, seeks to recover through tradition-constituted enquiry.

annotations.content: what morality was for Adam Gifford's culture
annotations.anchor_text: _I begin then by asking what morality was_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the specific framing of MacIntyre's question — notably 'was,' past tense, and 'for Adam Gifford's culture,' emphasizing the historical particularity. This is MacIntyre doing cultural-historical moral archaeology, and Tom flags it because the method itself matters: understanding morality requires understanding the tradition and culture that gave it content, not abstracting it into universal principles.
annotations.rc_relevance: This historicizing move is foundational to RC's methodology — the project cannot recover civility without first understanding what civility meant in its formative contexts, mirroring exactly what MacIntyre does here for Gifford.

annotations.content: five important characteristics
annotations.anchor_text: _It had five important characteristics_
annotations.interpretation: Simple structural underline marking the beginning of MacIntyre's typological analysis. Tom uses this alongside his numbered margin annotations (①–⑤) to create a complete navigational system for this section. The underline confirms Tom read this as a structural signal, not merely descriptive content.
annotations.rc_relevance: The five-characteristic schema is MacIntyre's diagnostic of the encyclopaedist moral culture — RC's project implicitly requires a similar diagnostic of contemporary moral culture, and Tom is likely storing this as a methodological template.

annotations.content: distinct and relatively autonomous area of
annotations.anchor_text: _First, morality was a distinct and relatively autonomous area_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the defining phrase of characteristic one, 'autonomous area,' reinforcing his margin note. The word 'relatively' is important — MacIntyre is not claiming absolute separation but functional independence, and Tom may be tracking this qualification as relevant to debates about whether religious and moral reasoning were ever truly separate in this period.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with subsidiarity and integrated institutions directly contests the notion of morality as an 'autonomous area' — the underline marks a key target for RC's constructive argument.

annotations.content: morality was primarily a matter of rule-following and of ritualized responses to breaches of rules
annotations.anchor_text: _Secondly, morality was primarily a matter of rule-following_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the full definition of characteristic two. The inclusion of 'ritualized responses to breaches' is significant — Tom may be flagging that Victorian morality had quasi-liturgical enforcement mechanisms (condemnation by fathers or clergymen), which is an interesting structural parallel to religious practice even as it claimed autonomy from religion (characteristic one). This internal tension is something MacIntyre will exploit.
annotations.rc_relevance: The ritualized dimension of rule-enforcement points toward RC's interest in how practices and institutions form moral character — even in a degraded form, Victorian morality recognized that moral life requires social enactment, not merely individual reasoning.

annotations.content: Thirdly
annotations.anchor_text: _the rules, breaches of which chiefly invited such condemnations_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles the transition marker 'Thirdly' to maintain his structural tracking of MacIntyre's five-point enumeration. This is a navigational annotation consistent with his numbered margin schema. The circle rather than underline suggests Tom wanted to visually distinguish structural signposts from substantive content.
annotations.rc_relevance: Minimal direct RC relevance as a structural mark, but signals Tom's systematic approach to MacIntyre's argument — important for RC's use of MacIntyre, which requires precise command of his analytical categories.

annotations.content: negative prohibitions
annotations.anchor_text: _breaches of which chiefly invited such condemnations and reproaches, were negative prohibitions_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the key term 'negative prohibitions,' isolating it from its surrounding context. This term captures MacIntyre's claim that Victorian morality was fundamentally asymmetric — stronger on what not to do than on positive prescriptions for flourishing. The italics in the original text (which Tom preserves by underlining) signal MacIntyre's own emphasis on this as a technical characterization.
annotations.rc_relevance: Negative prohibitionism is the antithesis of virtue ethics and civic friendship — RC's positive vision of moral community requires precisely what this culture lacked: thick positive accounts of what human flourishing looks like together.

annotations.content: strong notions of impropriety attached to violations of the compartmentalizing boundaries of social life
annotations.anchor_text: _Fourthly, this was a culture in which strong notions of impropriety_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the full description of characteristic four, capturing both the 'strong notions of impropriety' and their attachment to 'compartmentalizing boundaries.' The phrase 'compartmentalizing boundaries' is doing important work here — it connects back to characteristic one (autonomous areas) and shows how the compartmentalization is enforced socially through shame and propriety norms rather than argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly illuminates RC's concern that the contemporary collapse of civility may not be simply a loss of rules but a loss of the social-enforcement mechanisms (shame, propriety, institutional authority) that made rule-based morality stable — a fragmentation MacIntyre traces through subsequent chapters.

annotations.content: social agreement, especially in practice, on the importance and the content of morality coexisted with large intellectual disagreements concerning the nature of its rational justification
annotations.anchor_text: _Fifthly and finally, social agreement, especially in practice_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the full articulation of the fifth and most philosophically significant characteristic — the coexistence of practical moral consensus with theoretical moral chaos. This is MacIntyre's diagnosis of the Victorian moral predicament that the encyclopaedist project tried and failed to resolve. Tom underlines the entire formulation, suggesting he sees this as the most important of the five characteristics for his purposes — the one that generates the crisis MacIntyre's book addresses.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is arguably the most RC-relevant passage in the page: contemporary social fragmentation may be diagnosed similarly — we retain some practical moral intuitions but have lost the shared rational framework to justify them, and RC's recovery project must address both levels simultaneously.

annotations.content: disagreements concerning the nature of its rational justification, both agreement and disagreement being underpinned by a shared conviction that morality, thus understood, must be such as to be rationally justifiable somehow or other
annotations.anchor_text: _underpinned by a shared conviction that morality must be such as to be rationally justifiable_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the paradoxical formulation that even the disagreement was held within a shared meta-conviction about rationality's authority over morality. This is MacIntyre identifying the deepest assumption of the encyclopaedist tradition — that moral claims must be rationally grounded — which MacIntyre will show the tradition could not fulfill. Tom likely sees this as the hinge on which MacIntyre's entire critique of Enlightenment moral rationalism turns.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's engagement with MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism is directly targeted here — the 'somehow or other' is MacIntyre's ironic signal that this meta-conviction was itself held without rational grounding, the very condition that makes tradition-constituted rationality necessary as an alternative.

pages.summary: MacIntyre, following Franz Steiner's analysis of taboo, argues that Victorian rationalists used the concept of taboo to simultaneously distance themselves from 'primitive' religious superstition and to manage the problem of irrational prohibitions within their own moral framework. By employing Tylor's concept of 'survivals,' they could classify residual taboo-like beliefs among Free Church members and Roman Catholics as primitive remnants, while framing their own Edinburgh bourgeois morality as the rational culmination of moral progress. This move reveals the self-serving circularity of encyclopaedist moral reasoning, which disguises its own contingent standards as 'timeless truths of morality.'

annotations.content: The problem of taboo,
annotations.anchor_text: _became extraordinarily prominent in the Victorian age_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging Steiner's diagnostic claim that taboo became a conceptual problem specifically because of the Victorian rationalist project. The underlining isolates the phrase as a technical term or thesis marker — Tom likely wants to track how 'the problem of taboo' functions as a lens MacIntyre uses to expose encyclopaedist self-deception. He may be noting that what counts as a 'problem' is itself tradition-relative.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects directly to MacIntyre's encyclopaedist critique: the Victorians framed taboo as a problem to be solved by rational morality, mirroring the RC concern about how Enlightenment rationalism dissolves inherited moral prohibitions by relabeling them as irrational survivals rather than carriers of genuine moral wisdom.

annotations.content: became extraordinarily prominent in the Victorian age for two reasons: the rationalist approach to religion and the place of taboo in Victorian society itself
annotations.anchor_text: _prominent in the Victorian age for two reasons_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is marking Steiner's two-part explanatory structure — the underlining suggests he wants to hold both reasons simultaneously in view. The internal tension is key: taboo is both an intellectual embarrassment for rationalism (reason one) and a social necessity for Victorian moral order (reason two). Tom may be noting that MacIntyre is showing encyclopaedism's bad faith — it needs taboo-like prohibitions to function even as it condemns them.
annotations.rc_relevance: This dual structure maps onto RC's concern with institutions forming character: Victorian moral institutions required quasi-taboo prohibitions to maintain civic order, yet their official rationalist ideology could not acknowledge this. This is exactly the kind of contradiction RC seeks to surface in post-Enlightenment liberalism.

annotations.content: moral rules from arbitrary and irrational prohibitions.
annotations.anchor_text: _by a failure to distinguish genuine moral rules_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is isolating the key diagnostic failure the Victorians projected onto primitive societies — and implicitly onto Free Church Christians and Catholics. The underlining likely signals Tom's recognition that this 'failure to distinguish' is precisely the charge MacIntyre reverses against the encyclopaedists themselves: it is they who cannot ground the distinction between genuine moral rules and arbitrary prohibitions.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is central to MacIntyre's genealogical critique and to RC's recovery project: if Enlightenment rationalism cannot distinguish genuine moral rules from arbitrary prohibitions without appeal to tradition, then the Thomistic tradition's account of natural law offers a more coherent foundation — a key RC argument.

annotations.content: they followed Robertson Smith in using Tylor's concept of a survival in order to distinguish what were mere crude survivals from primitivism from what were genuine anticipations of, or already identical with, their own apprehension of what they took to be the timeless truths of morality.
annotations.anchor_text: _they followed Robertson Smith in using Tylor's concept_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is marking the methodological move MacIntyre considers central to encyclopaedist self-legitimation: using 'survivals' theory to create a historical narrative in which their own morality is the telos. The extended underlining suggests Tom sees this as the argumentative crux of the page — the Victorians' use of Tylor is circular, defining 'genuine' moral anticipations as whatever agrees with what they already believe. The phrase 'timeless truths of morality' is likely ironic in MacIntyre's usage, and Tom is tracking that irony.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the encyclopaedist version of tradition-as-progress, which RC directly contests: the claim to access 'timeless truths' independent of tradition is itself a tradition-bound claim. MacIntyre's point here anticipates his argument for tradition-constituted rationality, a cornerstone of RC's retrieval of Thomistic moral enquiry.

annotations.content: the timeless truths of morality.
annotations.anchor_text: _what they took to be the timeless truths_
annotations.interpretation: The secondary or emphasized underlining of this specific phrase within the longer underlined passage suggests Tom wants to flag it as a term of art — almost certainly ironic in MacIntyre's usage. Tom is likely noting that 'timeless truths' is the encyclopaedist's self-description, which MacIntyre is exposing as tradition-relative. This may be a term Tom wants to return to or quote.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry depends on rejecting the encyclopaedist fantasy of tradition-independent moral truth. Tom flagging this phrase suggests he sees it as a rhetorical target MacIntyre dismantles — and one RC must also address when engaging contemporary liberal moral discourse.

annotations.content: just like K's "dd" history of science
annotations.anchor_text: _they followed Robertson Smith in using Tylor's concept_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is drawing a parallel between the Victorian use of 'survivals' to narrate moral progress and what he abbreviates as K's 'dd' history of science — almost certainly a reference to Thomas Kuhn's account of scientific development (possibly 'The Structure of Scientific Revolutions'), where 'dd' likely stands for 'disciplinary matrix' or possibly 'developmental' or even 'Whig' history. The point Tom is making is that both moves — Victorian moral progressivism and a certain history of science — construct teleological narratives that retroactively validate the current paradigm as the rational endpoint. Tom sees MacIntyre's critique of the encyclopaedists as structurally parallel to critiques of Whiggish history of science.
annotations.rc_relevance: This cross-reference is highly significant for RC: if MacIntyre's critique of encyclopaedist moral narrative parallels Kuhn's critique of triumphalist science history, then RC can draw on philosophy of science literature to reinforce the argument that 'timeless truth' claims are always tradition-embedded — strengthening the case for tradition-constituted rationality as the alternative.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that educated Victorians, like the Edinburgh scholars of the Ninth Edition, were profoundly ethnocentric in their misreading of Polynesian taboo—projecting their own moral scheme onto other cultures while remaining blind to this projection. This ethnocentrism was compounded by a falsifying 'encyclopaedist' history of morality, exemplified by Sidgwick's Britannica article, which anachronistically read back the modern concept of 'morality' as a timeless, autonomous subject matter onto Plato, Aristotle, Hobbes, and Kant alike. The result was a conceptual flattening that obscured the historically conditioned and tradition-dependent character of moral enquiry itself.

annotations.content: again, like * old history
annotations.anchor_text: _key respects projected upon the Polynesians_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging this as a recurring pattern he has already identified elsewhere in the book or in related reading — the phenomenon of projecting present moral frameworks backward onto the past or onto other cultures. The asterisk likely signals a cross-reference or a theme he is tracking across pages. The phrase 'old history' suggests he is connecting MacIntyre's critique of Victorian ethnocentrism to a broader historiographical problem he associates with the encyclopaedist tradition's false universalism.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly tracks MacIntyre's encyclopaedist rival version — the tendency to treat one's own tradition-bound morality as timeless and universal — which RC identifies as a root cause of modern moral incoherence and the breakdown of civic moral language.

annotations.content: their own moral scheme
annotations.anchor_text: _key respects projected upon the Polynesians_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the precise phrase that captures the mechanism of ethnocentric projection — not just bias, but the imposition of a complete moral framework. This is a technically important term in MacIntyre's argument: 'scheme' implies something systematic and historically embedded, not merely individual prejudice.
annotations.rc_relevance: Central to RC's concern with tradition: moral schemes are not free-floating but embedded in particular communities and practices; failing to recognize this leads to the kind of false universalism RC seeks to diagnose and correct.

annotations.content: enabling prescriptions, just because their own rules were primarily negative
annotations.anchor_text: _the negative side of enabling prescriptions_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks this to capture MacIntyre's specific logical point: the Victorians misread Polynesian taboo as merely prohibitive because their own moral rules happened to be formulated negatively, and they lacked the conceptual resources to see positive enabling functions within a different moral grammar. This is a precise epistemological failure, not just cultural prejudice.
annotations.rc_relevance: Relevant to RC's theme of how institutional and communal practices form moral character: the enabling, positive dimension of moral rules (closer to virtue ethics and Thomistic natural law) is exactly what the encyclopaedist tradition's negative, Kantian-inflected framework could not perceive.

annotations.content: rules and other elements in taboo customs as an arbitrary mingling
annotations.anchor_text: _they saw the connections between the rules_
annotations.interpretation: The underline flags 'arbitrary mingling' as a key diagnostic phrase — the Victorians saw integration of different moral elements as disorder because their own tradition demanded compartmentalization. Tom is likely noting that what looks arbitrary from outside a tradition is internally coherent, a point central to MacIntyre's defense of tradition-constituted rationality.
annotations.rc_relevance: This speaks directly to MacIntyre's epistemological argument that rationality is tradition-dependent: what appears irrational or arbitrary from one tradition's standpoint may be deeply rational within another. RC uses this to argue against thin, procedural accounts of public reason.

annotations.content: blind to the local and parochial nature of their own assimilation of impropriety to immorality
annotations.anchor_text: _And they were blind to the local_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as a key formulation of self-blindness — the Victorians collapsed a contingent social norm ('impropriety') into a universal moral category ('immorality') without recognizing this as a culturally local move. The word 'blind' is doing philosophical work here: it's not dishonesty but a structural incapacity born of uncritical immersion in one's own tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is deeply concerned with the conflation of manners and morals, and the way modern social fragmentation partly results from losing the distinction between etiquette, civic norms, and genuine moral obligation. This passage is a historical case study in that conflation.

annotations.content: All cultures are of course ethnocentric and few are genuinely aware of the degree of their own ethnocentricity
annotations.anchor_text: _they took to be the mark of rationality_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's concessive universalization — the claim softens the critique slightly but actually sharpens it: it's not that the Victorians were uniquely bad, but that ethnocentricity is the default human condition, making self-critical tradition-awareness all the more necessary and rare. Tom likely sees this as MacIntyre's acknowledgment that the problem is structural, not moral.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is foundational to RC's argument for institutional and communal structures that enable self-examination: if ethnocentricity is universal, then the recovery of civility requires not just good intentions but practices and institutions that cultivate reflexive moral awareness — precisely what MacIntyre's 'tradition' version of enquiry is meant to provide.

annotations.content: All cultures are of course ethnocentric and few are genuinely aware of the degree of their own ethnocentricity. In both respects educated Edinburgh of the Ninth Edition was no exception to the human norm.
annotations.anchor_text: _All cultures are of course ethnocentric_
annotations.interpretation: The bracket demarcates this as a self-contained, citable claim. Tom is treating these two sentences as a unit — a concise thesis statement about universal ethnocentricity. He may intend to quote or cite this passage directly in RC writing, or he is marking it as a moment where MacIntyre steps back from historical critique into a more general philosophical claim.
annotations.rc_relevance: This bracketed passage could serve as an epigraph-level statement for RC's critique of contemporary moral discourse: the problem of projecting one's own scheme as universal is not a Victorian artifact but a perennial human tendency that civic traditions and practices of humility must continually resist.

annotations.content: 2002 obscured=
annotations.anchor_text: _What was thereby obscured from their view_
annotations.interpretation: This is the most cryptic annotation on the page. '2002' may refer to a year of publication — possibly a book, article, or event from 2002 that Tom associates with the same obscuring dynamic MacIntyre describes. The equals sign after 'obscured' suggests Tom is drawing a direct equivalence between MacIntyre's historical case and something contemporary. Alternatively, '2002' could be a page number in another text. The annotation signals that Tom sees MacIntyre's critique as having a live contemporary referent, not merely a historical one.
annotations.rc_relevance: If Tom is dating a contemporary instance of the same obscuring mechanism MacIntyre identifies in Victorian moral history, this is highly relevant to RC's diagnostic project: showing that Enlightenment-encyclopaedist habits of mind are still operative in present moral and political discourse.

annotations.content: of posing two questions about their own morality
annotations.anchor_text: _What was thereby obscured from their view was the possibility_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the structural pivot: 'two questions' signals that MacIntyre is about to introduce a binary analytical framework. Underlining this phrase means Tom is tracking the logical architecture of the argument — he wants to know what the two questions are and why their suppression matters. This is the kind of structural annotation that suggests active outlining.
annotations.rc_relevance: The two questions (about the history and the rational justification of one's own moral scheme) are precisely the questions RC wants to re-open in contemporary civic discourse — MacIntyre's point is that encyclopaedism forecloses them by presupposing its own answers.

annotations.content: the word 'morality' as used by his contemporaries has no equivalent expression in biblical or medieval Hebrew or in either classical or koinē Greek, or in either classical or high medieval Latin
annotations.anchor_text: _does Sidgwick take notice, for example_
annotations.interpretation: This is one of the most heavily marked passages on the page, and rightly so — it is MacIntyre's most concrete linguistic-historical argument. Tom underlines the full claim to capture its specificity: the modern concept of 'morality' as a discrete, autonomous domain is a historically late construction, not a timeless category. The absence of equivalent terms in Hebrew, Greek, and Latin is evidence that the encyclopaedist project is projecting backwards.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's genealogical-diagnostic work: if 'morality' as a free-standing domain is a modern invention, then appeals to 'universal morality' in civic discourse are actually appeals to a particular, historically contingent tradition masquerading as neutral — exactly the kind of false foundation RC seeks to expose and replace with thicker, tradition-rooted civic virtue.

annotations.content: not yet recognized the distinctiveness or the autonomy of morality
annotations.anchor_text: _speakers of such languages had not yet recognized_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines 'not yet' — this is Sidgwick's (and the encyclopaedists') teleological framing: earlier cultures hadn't 'yet' arrived at the modern concept. MacIntyre is exposing this as question-begging progressivism. Tom flags it because the 'not yet' encodes the entire encyclopaedist fallacy: treating historical difference as developmental lag rather than genuine conceptual difference.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the heart of MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism, which RC takes up directly: the assumption that modern liberal moral categories represent the telos of human moral development is precisely what MacIntyre's 'tradition' version contests, and what RC seeks to dislodge from civic discourse.

annotations.content: Sidgwick's falsifying history thus projected back into the past the conceptual structuring of the author's present
annotations.anchor_text: _Sidgwick's falsifying history thus projected back_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines 'falsifying history' — a strong, polemical phrase from MacIntyre. This is not gentle critique; it is an accusation of historical distortion. Tom marks it because it names the exact epistemic vice MacIntyre is diagnosing: not mere error but a structurally distorting projection. The phrase also echoes the earlier margin note 'again, like old history,' suggesting Tom sees this as the culminating formulation of a theme he has been tracking.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, 'falsifying history' is the encyclopaedist tradition's signature failure — it produces a civic and moral discourse that cannot learn from the past because it has already decided what the past was trying (imperfectly) to be. Recovering civility requires, first, a more honest history.

annotations.content: suggested that Plato and Aristotle, Hobbes, Spinoza, and Kant and Sidgwick himself were all offering accounts, albeit rival accounts, of the rational status of one and the same timeless subject matter.
annotations.anchor_text: _suggested that Plato and Aristotle, Hobbes_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets this sentence as the clearest statement of the encyclopaedist flattening of moral history — the claim that all major moral philosophers were addressing the same timeless question. This is the position MacIntyre's entire book is written against. The bracket likely signals that Tom intends to use this as a foil statement in RC writing: 'the view we are contesting is this...'
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the encyclopaedist rival version in its purest form, and RC's entire project is predicated on rejecting it: moral enquiry is not the progressive refinement of answers to one timeless question but the contested, tradition-embedded pursuit of goods that are themselves historically and communally constituted.

annotations.content: still the dominant view, today.
annotations.anchor_text: _Plato and Aristotle, Hobbes, Spinoza, and Kant_
annotations.interpretation: Tom's bottom-margin note is his most direct editorial comment on the page — and arguably the most important for RC. He is asserting that the encyclopaedist view MacIntyre critiques historically is not a dead error but the reigning assumption of contemporary moral philosophy and public discourse. The period after 'today' gives it a declarative, almost exasperated finality. This is Tom positioning MacIntyre's historical diagnosis as a live contemporary problem, not a museum piece.
annotations.rc_relevance: This note is the RC project's raison d'être in miniature: if the falsifying, tradition-blind, timeless-subject-matter view is still dominant today, then the recovery of civility requires not just philosophical argument but cultural and institutional work to displace it — exactly what RC is attempting.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that late Victorian moral culture faced two unasked questions: whether morality was susceptible of rational justification at all, and whether morality as they understood it was itself merely a 'survival' in Tylor's anthropological sense — a fragment detached from the larger context that once gave it intelligibility and rational grounding. Both questions converge on whether the moral frameworks of the Encyclopaedist tradition and its rivals are genuine rational achievements or irrational remnants of a broken tradition, setting up the three-rival-versions structure of the book.

annotations.content: A question
annotations.anchor_text: _ford in his will called the Infinite_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'A question' to flag that MacIntyre is formally introducing what will be a structurally significant interrogative — this is not casual phrasing but the announcement of a specific philosophical problem. The circle likely marks this as Question 1 (Q1) in a sequence, coordinating with the margin note nearby. Tom is tracking the argumentative scaffolding MacIntyre is building.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project's engagement with MacIntyre's tripartite structure depends on understanding what questions that structure is designed to answer; Tom is identifying the first of the two 'unasked questions' that motivate the whole inquiry.

annotations.content: neither Sidgwick nor Gifford nor their culture in general was able to frame was whether the tasks of the rational elucidation of the status of moral rules and the rational justification of obedience to them had not been finally carried through
annotations.anchor_text: _neither Sidgwick nor Gifford nor their culture_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this extended clause to capture the core of Question 1: the failure of rational elucidation and justification of moral rules. The underline spans across the line break, suggesting Tom is treating this as a complete propositional unit worth extracting. He is likely noting that this is MacIntyre's diagnosis of the Encyclopaedist project's internal limitation — it could not complete what it set out to do.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly engages RC's MacIntyre theme: the Enlightenment rationalist project (Encyclopaedia) failed to provide adequate rational foundations for morality, which is the very crisis RC seeks to address through recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry.

annotations.content: not merely because some present or future philosopher had yet to succeed where Plato, Aristotle, Hobbes, Kant, and Sidgwick had all in some degree failed
annotations.anchor_text: _not merely because some present or future philosopher_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this to mark MacIntyre's crucial qualifier — the problem is not simply that the right philosopher hasn't come along yet. This is MacIntyre distinguishing a deeper structural failure from a merely contingent one. Tom is tracking the logical force of 'not merely because,' which sets up the more radical claim to follow.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is central to RC's argument against purely rationalist solutions to moral fragmentation — the problem is not a missing genius but a fractured tradition, pointing toward why institutional and communal recovery (subsidiarity, civic friendship) is necessary.

annotations.content: but because in fact morality, conceived as that late Victorian culture conceived it, is not in significant and central respects susceptible of rational justification
annotations.anchor_text: _but because in fact morality conceived as that late Victorian_
annotations.interpretation: This underline marks MacIntyre's positive thesis: the failure is not contingent but structural — Victorian moral culture's conception of morality is itself the problem. Tom likely underlines this because it is the argumentative payoff of the preceding qualifications and represents MacIntyre's most direct diagnosis. The emphasis on 'in fact' signals MacIntyre's confidence in this as a historical-philosophical finding, not mere speculation.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the heart of MacIntyre's genealogical critique that RC must grapple with: if Enlightenment morality is structurally incapable of rational justification, the RC project's appeal to classical and Thomistic alternatives gains its urgency.

annotations.content: Q1 is M a set of taboos?
annotations.anchor_text: _is not in significant and central respects susceptible_
annotations.interpretation: Tom labels this 'Q1' and reformulates MacIntyre's first unasked question in his own compressed terms: is morality (M) merely a set of taboos? This paraphrase connects to the Polynesian taboo customs mentioned in the text below. Tom is translating MacIntyre's academic prose into a sharp, memorable question-form, likely for use in RC writing or teaching. The abbreviation 'M' shows Tom is building a shorthand notation system across this reading.
annotations.rc_relevance: The taboo analogy is critical for RC: if contemporary moral rules function like decontextualized taboos — obeyed without understood rationale — then the RC project's call for recovery of tradition-embedded virtue ethics is the appropriate remedy.

annotations.content: A second unasked question was closely related. It was whether morality, understood as these late Victorians understood it, might not itself after all be what Tylor termed a survival, or a set of survivals, that is, rules, attitudes, and responses which had once been at home within some larger context in terms of which their intelligibility had been spelled out and their rationality justified but which had become detached from that context.
annotations.anchor_text: _A second unasked question was closely related_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the entire definition of Tylor's 'survival' concept as MacIntyre deploys it — this is Question 2. The thoroughness of the underline (spanning multiple lines) indicates Tom sees this as a passage of foundational importance, not just a passing reference. Tylor's anthropological concept is being recruited by MacIntyre to diagnose modernity's moral condition, and Tom recognizes this as a key theoretical move.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'survival' concept maps directly onto RC's concern with tradition vs. reform: if modern moral rules are survivals detached from their formative contexts, then RC's recovery project is not nostalgia but intellectual necessity — restoring the larger context (Thomistic tradition, civic institutions) that gives moral rules their intelligibility.

annotations.content: Q2 are these taboos fragments of some earlier whole?
annotations.anchor_text: _survival or a set of survivals that is rules_
annotations.interpretation: Tom labels this 'Q2' and again paraphrases in his own language, connecting it back to Q1's taboo framing. The phrase 'fragments of some earlier whole' is Tom's own formulation — sharper and more evocative than MacIntyre's prose — suggesting Tom is not just comprehending but actively rewriting for clarity. The word 'whole' is philosophically loaded in a Thomistic context (the common good, the polis) and Tom may be consciously invoking that resonance.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly engages RC's central problematic of social fragmentation: if moral practices are fragments severed from their wholes, then RC's project of resurrecting civility requires identifying and partially reconstructing those wholes — which is precisely what MacIntyre's tradition-based enquiry aims to do.

annotations.content: Both the contemporary contending parties of which I spoke earlier treat these two questions as crucial and they would agree in understanding the morality of the culture of Adam Gifford and the Ninth Edition as in important respects an irrational survival, to be understood in terms of a narrative of historical contingency.
annotations.anchor_text: _Both the contemporary contending parties of which I spoke_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this to capture MacIntyre's surprising claim: the Encyclopaedists and the Genealogists (Nietzscheans) actually agree on this diagnosis, even if they draw opposite conclusions. This paradox — that rivals share a premise — is structurally important for the book's argument. Tom is flagging a moment where MacIntyre shows unexpected common ground between adversaries.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this convergence is significant: both secular rationalism and postmodern genealogy agree that traditional morality lacks rational grounding, which means RC cannot borrow from either without engaging MacIntyre's third alternative — tradition-constituted enquiry.

annotations.content: back to A + B
annotations.anchor_text: _Both the contemporary contending parties of which I spoke_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes 'back to A + B' to cross-reference what are almost certainly the Encyclopaedist (A) and Genealogist (B) parties introduced earlier in the chapter or book. This is a navigational annotation — Tom is reminding himself that this passage returns to the two-party framework established previously, before the Tradition (C, MacIntyre's Thomism) is introduced as the third rival. The '+' rather than 'vs.' is interesting: Tom may be noting their unexpected agreement rather than opposition.
annotations.rc_relevance: This annotation shows Tom tracking MacIntyre's tripartite structure, which is foundational to RC's theoretical framework — understanding that both Enlightenment rationalism (A) and Nietzschean genealogy (B) are inadequate requires first seeing how they agree in their diagnosis even while differing in their prescriptions.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the standoff between Nietzsche's genealogical critique (Zur Genealogie der Moral) and the Thomistic encyclical (Aeterni Patris) reveals that both traditions, despite their radical opposition, agree in rejecting the moral world of Adam Gifford's Victorian milieu. He then advances a stronger thesis: not only are our conceptions of rationality and enquiry incompatible with Gifford's, but the very subject matter — morality itself — can no longer be understood as Gifford understood it, meaning any Gifford Lecture today conducts an enquiry into something fundamentally alien to what Gifford envisaged.

annotations.content: B
annotations.anchor_text: _of Aeterni Patris the morality of the Victorian_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is labeling a structural unit of MacIntyre's argument with the letter 'B,' corresponding to the Aeterni Patris / Catholic Thomistic standpoint. This likely pairs with the 'A' label lower on the page marking the Nietzschean genealogical standpoint, suggesting Tom is mapping the tripartite structure MacIntyre employs throughout the book — encyclopaedist, genealogist, tradition — onto discrete bracketed passages. 'B' here marks the Catholic/Thomistic rival version's characterization of Victorian morality.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly maps to the RC project's engagement with MacIntyre's 'three rival versions' framework; Tom is tracking where Thomistic tradition (central to the RC recovery project) enters as a diagnostic lens on moral decline.

annotations.content: of Aeterni Patris the morality of the Victorian nineteenth century was only the latest version of a primitive, herd morality in which enmity to the biologically vital was masked by the false pretensions of reason.
annotations.anchor_text: _Victorian nineteenth century was only the latest_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has bracketed the passage describing the Nietzschean genealogical verdict on Victorian morality — that it is 'herd morality' masking biological enmity behind rational pretension. The bracket likely corresponds to the 'A' label, isolating the genealogist's diagnosis as a discrete argumentative unit. Tom is tracking how MacIntyre characterizes the Nietzschean position as a foil to Thomism, and possibly flagging the rhetorical force of 'herd morality' as a term that reveals Nietzsche's own normative commitments.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project's concern with the 'genealogist' strand of MacIntyre's tripartite structure is directly engaged here; this passage shows why the genealogist cannot serve as a constructive resource for recovering civic virtue, only as a diagnostic.

annotations.content: A
annotations.anchor_text: _Victorian nineteenth century was only the latest version_
annotations.interpretation: The letter 'A' marks the Nietzschean/genealogical standpoint's characterization of Victorian morality, structurally paired with 'B' above for the Thomistic standpoint and likely 'C' further down. Tom is creating a visual index of the three rival positions as MacIntyre presents their respective verdicts on the same historical moment. This is careful structural annotation — Tom is not just reading but reconstructing MacIntyre's dialectical architecture.
annotations.rc_relevance: Tracking the genealogist position is essential for RC because MacIntyre argues that contemporary moral discourse is haunted by genealogical suspicion; Tom needs to know exactly where each rival voice speaks in order to articulate what a recovered Thomistic tradition must answer.

annotations.content: C
annotations.anchor_text: _in the perspective afforded by Aeterni Patris Nietzsche's doctrines_
annotations.interpretation: Tom labels a third structural unit 'C,' which corresponds to the passage where, from the Thomistic perspective, Nietzsche's doctrines are themselves 'one more variant of the characteristic errors of nineteenth-century modernity.' This completes Tom's A/B/C mapping of how each rival tradition views the others and their shared Victorian context. The 'C' annotation shows Tom recognizes that MacIntyre is not merely listing three views but showing their mutual condemnations.
annotations.rc_relevance: For the RC project, this third node is crucial: it shows that Thomism does not merely oppose liberalism but also diagnoses Nietzscheanism as a symptom of modernity's errors — supporting RC's argument that only a robust tradition-based virtue ethics can ground civic renewal.

annotations.content: conception of a science and the corresponding conception of rationality as unitary
annotations.anchor_text: _conception of a science and the corresponding conception of rationality_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's identification of the core Giffördian assumption — that science and rationality are unitary and continuous. This is the philosophical premise MacIntyre is dismantling, and Tom recognizes it as the hinge on which the book's entire argument about the impossibility of a neutral 'natural theology' turns. The underlining flags this as a term to track: 'unitary rationality' is the encyclopaedist's key commitment.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's critique of Enlightenment rationalism depends precisely on rejecting this 'unitary' conception; Tom is marking the exact claim MacIntyre will use to show that any contemporary Gifford Lecture must choose a tradition rather than pretend to neutral rationality.

annotations.content: also that that about which we are to enquire is, at least in the case of morality, something which we cannot understand as Adam Gifford understood it
annotations.anchor_text: _also that that about which we are to enquire is_
annotations.interpretation: This is the strongest claim on the page — that not just methodology but the very object of moral enquiry has changed — and Tom's underlining marks it as the argumentative climax. MacIntyre is making a point about conceptual incommensurability: 'morality' as a subject matter is itself tradition-dependent. Tom likely sees this as the most radical and most useful claim for RC, since it implies that moral enquiry cannot be conducted from a tradition-neutral standpoint.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is foundational for RC's argument that recovering civility requires recovering a specific tradition's account of what morality is, not just its methods; the RC project cannot simply borrow liberal moral vocabulary and fill it with Thomistic content.

annotations.content: G, vs. diff't understands of
annotations.anchor_text: _the conception of a science and the corresponding conception of rationality_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is summarizing MacIntyre's thesis about Gifford ('G') versus contemporary ('diff't understands of') conceptions across three domains: (1) Science, (2) Rationality, and (3) Morality. The numbered list mirrors the escalating structure of MacIntyre's argument — each adds a deeper layer of incompatibility. Tom is making this implicit structure explicit for his own use, likely because he plans to cite or teach this passage and needs the argument's architecture visible at a glance.
annotations.rc_relevance: This three-part schema is directly usable in RC's framing of why modern institutions cannot simply import classical virtue language without transforming their underlying conceptions of rationality and moral subject matter — all three levels of Gifford's assumptions must be interrogated.

annotations.content: the lecturers shall be under no restraint whatever in their treatment of their theme
annotations.anchor_text: _the lecturers shall be under no restraint whatever_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the quoted clause from Gifford's will granting lecturers complete freedom. This is ironic in context: MacIntyre is pointing out that Gifford's own generous open-endedness is what allows MacIntyre to conduct an enquiry so alien to Gifford's intentions. Tom likely marks this because it is rhetorically important — MacIntyre uses Gifford's liberality against Gifford's assumptions — and because it raises a question about the relationship between institutional founding intent and subsequent use.
annotations.rc_relevance: This tension between founding intent and institutional evolution is a core RC concern, particularly around subsidiarity and how institutions form (or deform) character over time; Tom may be noting that institutions can be 'captured' by traditions their founders never envisioned.

pages.summary: This page concludes MacIntyre's framing chapter on Adam Gifford's project by announcing the method of the ensuing lectures: MacIntyre will use Gifford's endowment as a platform to compare what natural theology would look like if guided by Nietzsche's 'Zur Genealogie der Moral' versus Leo XIII's 'Aeterni Patris,' thereby dramatizing the conflict between two rival traditions of moral enquiry. The page marks the transition from historical contextualization to the book's central tripartite rivalry. The underlined sentence signals MacIntyre's self-conscious acknowledgment that only Gifford's 'reckless generosity' in funding open intellectual inquiry makes this confrontational comparative project possible at all.

annotations.content: it is only in virtue of this reckless generosity that I am able to proceed to the ensuing lectures in order to enquire in some detail
annotations.anchor_text: _And it is only in virtue of this_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has underlined MacIntyre's ironic acknowledgment that the Gifford Lectures' own institutional openness — its 'reckless generosity' — is what permits a lecture series that will ultimately challenge the Enlightenment presuppositions underlying that same institution. This is a moment of performative self-awareness in MacIntyre's argument: the tradition-constituted enquiry he champions is being funded by an institution shaped by encyclopaedist assumptions. Tom likely flags this as a productive tension, perhaps even a contradiction worth exploiting in RC work.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly illuminates the RC theme of institutions forming character and enabling enquiry — but here the institution (the Gifford bequest) inadvertently enables a critique of its own founding rationalist assumptions, raising the question of whether institutions can transcend their own tradition-embedded purposes.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the Ninth Edition of the Encyclopaedia Britannica was the canonical text of a particular weltanschauung — a unified, progressivist conception of reason — and that the encyclopaedia article as a genre was perfectly matched to this worldview. He extends this claim to the university lecture of the same era: professors who wrote encyclopaedia articles also delivered lectures ex cathedra that functioned as 'spoken encyclopaedia articles,' a genre radically different from both medieval disputational lecture and modern academic discourse. The medieval lecture, by contrast, presupposed shared authoritative texts and culminated in disputation, grounding authority externally rather than in the lecturer's own rational synthesis.
