annotations.content: still able
annotations.anchor_text: _if and insofar as we are still able_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has highlighted this conditional phrase, which is doing significant philosophical work: MacIntyre is already signaling that the capacity to ask and answer questions about God and the good is not guaranteed — it is a question of whether we are still able. This marks a threshold claim about the condition of modern moral enquiry, not merely a historical observation.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly touches the RC theme of social fragmentation: the 'if and insofar' signals that the tradition of Gifford-style enquiry may be broken or occluded, which is precisely the crisis RC seeks to diagnose and address.

annotations.content: about God and the good
annotations.anchor_text: _to ask and to answer questions about God_
annotations.interpretation: Highlighting these two objects of enquiry together suggests Tom is tracking MacIntyre's pairing of theological and ethical questions as inseparable — a claim that cuts against Enlightenment attempts to ground morality without reference to God. Tom may be flagging this for RC's argument that the separation of these two domains is itself a symptom of fragmentation.
annotations.rc_relevance: Central to RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry, which refuses the modern severance of ethics from theology. The pairing 'God and the good' is the unity that Encyclopaedist rationalism tries to dissolve.

annotations.content: has to be from some standpoint alien to his and that of his Edinburgh contemporaries precisely in its rejection of their unitary conception of reason
annotations.anchor_text: _has to be from some standpoint alien to his Edinburgh contemporaries_
annotations.interpretation: This is a structurally pivotal sentence: MacIntyre is saying that any viable contemporary enquiry must come from outside the Encyclopaedist standpoint — it must be a rival, not a reform, of that tradition. Tom's highlighting suggests he sees this as the hinge on which the whole tripartite structure turns: you cannot fix the Encyclopaedia from within.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the crux of RC's anti-liberal-reformist stance: genuine renewal of civility and moral community cannot be achieved by tweaking Enlightenment frameworks but requires a genuinely alternative tradition, whether Thomistic or otherwise.

annotations.content: view of a developing world within which each part of the enquiry contributes to an overall progress
annotations.anchor_text: _view of a developing world within which each part_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's characterization of the Encyclopaedist worldview as progressivist and teleological in a secular sense — each inquiry contributes to cumulative rational progress. This is the ideology embedded in the genre of the encyclopaedia article, and Tom seems to be marking it as the target of MacIntyre's critique rather than a neutral description.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is deeply skeptical of this progressivist meta-narrative; the Thomistic tradition offers a different teleology grounded in natural law and divine order rather than historical accumulation. Tom is likely flagging this to contrast Encyclopaedist telos with Thomistic telos.

annotations.content: is an account of the progress of mankind
annotations.anchor_text: _whose supreme achievement is an account of the progress_
annotations.interpretation: The green highlight (distinct from the pink) on this phrase suggests Tom is using color-coding to distinguish types of claims — possibly separating descriptive characterizations of the Encyclopaedist worldview from MacIntyre's own argumentative moves. The phrase 'progress of mankind' is the ideological core of Victorian rationalism that MacIntyre is exposing as a standpoint, not a neutral truth.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project directly contests this narrative of progress as the framework for evaluating moral and civic life, preferring instead MacIntyre's account of tradition-constituted rationality.

annotations.content: The canonical books
annotations.anchor_text: _The canonical books of those who gave their allegiance_
annotations.interpretation: Highlighting 'canonical books' marks a key conceptual move: MacIntyre is using the language of canon — normally associated with religious or classical tradition — to describe the Encyclopaedia Britannica. This ironic application suggests that the Encyclopaedist tradition has its own quasi-religious authority structure, which Tom is flagging as a critical insight about the nature of rationalist ideology.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is interested in how traditions constitute authority and canonicity; this annotation marks the moment MacIntyre shows that even anti-traditional rationalism has a canon, pointing toward the inescapability of tradition-constituted enquiry.

annotations.content: The encyclopaedia article was the genre
annotations.anchor_text: _The encyclopaedia article was the genre whose form perfectly matched_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's genre-theory claim: that intellectual content and literary form are not separable — the encyclopaedia article as a genre embodies and transmits the weltanschauung. This is a McLuhan-adjacent point about the medium shaping the message, which Tom likely finds important for thinking about how institutional forms (universities, genres, lectures) shape moral formation.
annotations.rc_relevance: This speaks to RC's theme of institutions in forming character: if genres carry worldviews, then the recovery of civility requires attention to what genres and institutional forms are being used — not just what is being said but how it is being said.

annotations.content: E type Texts
annotations.anchor_text: _The canonical books of those who gave their allegiance to this weltanschauung_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is labeling the Encyclopaedia Britannica and its associated texts as 'E type Texts' — 'E' almost certainly standing for 'Encyclopaedist.' This is a shorthand taxonomy he is developing for the tripartite structure (Encyclopaedist / Genealogist / Tradition), allowing him to track which texts and genres belong to which rival version. The label suggests Tom is building a comparative schema across the book.
annotations.rc_relevance: This taxonomic note is directly relevant to RC's use of MacIntyre's tripartite framework: identifying 'E type Texts' is the first step toward identifying what texts a recovered Thomistic tradition would offer as counterparts — what would 'T type Texts' look like for RC?

annotations.content: 2 types of lectures
annotations.anchor_text: _ex cathedra, so their university lectures were spoken encyclopaedia articles_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is noting that MacIntyre has introduced a contrast between two types of lecture: the Encyclopaedist ex cathedra lecture (spoken encyclopaedia article) and the medieval disputational lecture. This binary is structurally important — it will likely expand into the tripartite as the chapter develops. Tom may be flagging this for RC's interest in pedagogical form and how different lecture traditions embody different conceptions of authority and knowledge.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with institutions forming character extends to pedagogy: how we teach is not neutral. Tom's note suggests he sees the lecture-type distinction as a model for thinking about what kind of educational institution could support the recovery of civility.

annotations.content: type of lecture in which they spoke ex cathedra, so their university lectures were spoken encyclopaedia articles
annotations.anchor_text: _type of lecture in which they spoke ex cathedra_
annotations.interpretation: The highlighting of 'ex cathedra' and the equation of university lectures with spoken encyclopaedia articles marks MacIntyre's claim that the authority structure of Encyclopaedist lecturing is dogmatic — the professor speaks from the chair as an authority synthesizing universal rational knowledge. Tom is likely struck by the irony that an ostensibly anti-dogmatic rationalism reproduces a quasi-papal authority structure.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's critique of Enlightenment rationalism: the supposedly emancipatory tradition of rational enquiry reproduces authoritarian forms. The recovery of genuine civic discourse may require more dialogical, disputational forms — closer to the medieval model MacIntyre is about to describe.

annotations.content: spoken encyclopaedia articles
annotations.anchor_text: _university lectures were spoken encyclopaedia articles. This type of lecture was_
annotations.interpretation: A second, distinct highlight on this exact phrase (possibly in a different color or emphasis) reinforces that Tom considers this the key formulation on the page — the conceptual payoff of MacIntyre's genre analysis. The phrase crystallizes the argument: genre, institution, and worldview are unified.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this phrase is a diagnostic tool: it names what went wrong with modern university formation. The recovery of civility may require recovering a different pedagogical genre — one closer to the disputatio — that the RC project could model or advocate.

annotations.content: medieval lecturer shared with his audience a background of beliefs concerning which texts were authoritative. The lecture as interpretative commentary upon such texts
annotations.anchor_text: _medieval lecturer shared with his audience a background of beliefs_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the defining features of the medieval lecture: shared authoritative texts and interpretative commentary. This is MacIntyre's positive alternative — the tradition-constituted model of enquiry in which authority is external (in the texts) and shared (between lecturer and audience), not concentrated in the sovereign rational individual. Tom is likely flagging this as the constructive countermodel.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the heart of RC's Thomistic recovery project: the idea that genuine moral enquiry requires a community of shared texts and interpretative practices. The medieval lecture model embodies subsidiarity (authority located in tradition, not the individual expert) and civic friendship (a shared horizon between teacher and student).

annotations.content: which texts were authoritative
annotations.anchor_text: _background of beliefs concerning which texts were authoritative_
annotations.interpretation: The separate or additional highlighting of this phrase within the already-highlighted sentence suggests Tom considers the question of textual authority to be particularly significant — not just background but the crux. Which texts count as authoritative, and who decides, is a question that divides all three rival versions of moral enquiry.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC must grapple directly with this question: what are the authoritative texts for a recovered tradition of civility? Aquinas, Aristotle, Augustine? Tom is likely flagging this as a foundational RC question.

annotations.content: the disputation
annotations.anchor_text: _had as its sequel the disputation in which the lec-_
annotations.interpretation: Highlighting 'the disputation' at the very bottom of the page, where the sentence is cut off, marks the third element of the medieval model: after shared texts and interpretative commentary comes structured adversarial dialogue. Tom recognizes this as MacIntyre's implicit model for genuine rational enquiry — not the ex cathedra monologue but the disputation.
annotations.rc_relevance: The disputation is directly relevant to RC's vision of civic friendship and public discourse: genuine civility is not mere politeness but the capacity for rigorous, tradition-grounded disagreement. RC might advocate for reviving disputational forms in public and educational life.

annotations.content: The medieval lecturer shared with his audience a background of beliefs concerning which texts were authoritative. The lecture as interpretative commentary upon such texts appealed to an authority beyond itself and had as its sequel the disputation
annotations.anchor_text: _medieval lecturer shared with his audience a background_
annotations.interpretation: The bracket around the entire final paragraph draws a box around what Tom has identified as the positive model — the medieval alternative to the Encyclopaedist lecture. The bracket (with what appears to be a pink oval or circle mark near it) signals that this passage is a unit to be extracted, cited, or returned to. It is the constructive core of the page.
annotations.rc_relevance: This bracketed passage is likely a candidate for direct quotation in RC materials, as it gives MacIntyre's clearest description of tradition-constituted pedagogy: shared texts, interpretative authority external to the self, and dialogical testing through disputation.

annotations.content: oval/circle mark near bracket
annotations.anchor_text: _medieval lecturer shared with his audience a background_
annotations.interpretation: A pink oval or circle drawn near the left margin bracket appears to be a visual emphasis marker — possibly Tom's way of flagging this as a 'must use' or 'key passage' beyond what the bracket alone conveys. It may also be a color-coded system where pink circles denote passages for RC direct use, distinct from mere note-taking. The ambiguity should be noted.
annotations.rc_relevance: If this is a 'priority' marker, it confirms that the medieval lecture model is one of Tom's anchor passages for RC — the positive vision against which contemporary fragmentation is measured.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the contemporary university has made the nineteenth-century lecture format obsolete: where that format presupposed shared deference to authoritative texts and rational standards, we now inhabit a condition of radical disagreement in which a lecture can only be a moment within an ongoing conflict. This is why Adam Gifford's will — which prescribed lectures as the vehicle for conveying universally accessible 'real' knowledge — is doubly alien to us: both its content (encyclopaedist rationality) and its form (the lecture as authoritative performance) have become problematic. Nietzsche's abandonment of the professorial chair is presented as the historically significant rejection of both the encyclopaedist conception of knowledge and the lecture-genre that embodied it, anticipating his new role as genealogist.

annotations.content: tested dialectically and demonstratively
annotations.anchor_text: _luter's theses were tested dialectically and_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the epistemological method characteristic of the medieval university — dialectical testing as a communal, rationality-governed procedure. This contrasts with the nineteenth-century lecture's authoritative monologue. Tom is likely tracking MacIntyre's tripartite structure: this marks the 'tradition' model of enquiry as dialogical rather than declarative, which is philosophically significant for how tradition-based rationality actually works.
annotations.rc_relevance: Central to RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry: genuine tradition-based rationality is dialogical and disputational, not merely received — a corrective to caricatures of tradition as passive deference.

annotations.content: both audience and lecturers accepted standards of truth and rationality independent of either
annotations.anchor_text: _just because both audience and lecturers accepted_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the shared epistemic framework that made medieval disputation possible — a rationality no party owned but all were accountable to. This is the condition of genuine rational enquiry MacIntyre admires. Tom may be asking: what is the contemporary analogue, if any, and can it be recovered? The highlight signals that this shared standard is precisely what has been lost.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's concern with civic friendship and shared moral vocabulary: genuine civil discourse requires a rationality held in common, not merely competing assertions of self-interest or identity.

annotations.content: intellectual equivalent of trial by ordeal
annotations.anchor_text: _other to test any particular thesis in_
annotations.interpretation: A striking metaphor MacIntyre uses to characterize nineteenth-century lecturing — the lecturer as sole arbiter, authority by performance rather than argument. Tom highlights this likely because the phrase crystallizes the degeneration from genuine dialectic to authoritative display. It may also resonate as a critique of contemporary academic culture where prestige substitutes for argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: Relevant to RC's critique of Enlightenment rationalism: the 'encyclopaedist' mode mistakes the performance of authority for the exercise of reason, a corruption that RC seeks to name and reverse.

annotations.content: authority resided in the lecturer himself and in the weltanschauung
annotations.anchor_text: _ity upon the standards in constituting himself as_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the locus of authority in the nineteenth-century lecture: not in shared rational standards but in the person and worldview of the lecturer. The retention of the German 'Weltanschauung' is itself significant — MacIntyre may be subtly pointing to the ideological, culture-bound nature of this authority. Tom is tracking how authority migrates from shared rational procedure to individual charisma or institutional position.
annotations.rc_relevance: This speaks to RC's concern with institutions forming character: when authority is personalized rather than grounded in shared rational standards, institutions cannot do the formative work tradition requires.

annotations.content: The audience came to hear and to learn from authoritative, encyclopaedic pronouncements, not to dispute
annotations.anchor_text: _lecture. The audience came to hear and_
annotations.interpretation: Tom flags the passivity of the nineteenth-century audience as constitutive of the encyclopaedist model. The contrast with medieval disputation is sharp: learners as receptors vs. learners as disputants. Tom may be considering how contemporary pedagogy unconsciously replicates this encyclopaedist posture even in supposedly progressive educational settings.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's theme of subsidiarity and character formation: if audiences are trained to receive rather than dispute, the formation of practical reasoning — essential to Thomistic moral enquiry — is undermined at the institutional level.

annotations.content: Deference on the part of the audience was one of the defining marks of the late nineteenth-century university
annotations.anchor_text: _encyclopaedic pronouncements, not to dispute. Deference on_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this as a diagnostic claim about institutional culture. 'Deference' here is a structural pathology, not a virtue — it names the corrosion of genuine enquiry by hierarchical performance. Tom is likely noting the irony that this 'deference' mimics the outward form of respect for tradition while hollowing out its rational substance.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC must distinguish genuine deference to authoritative tradition (which enables enquiry) from servile deference to institutional prestige (which forecloses it) — this passage marks exactly that distinction.

annotations.anchor_text: _century university. For us in the contemporary_
annotations.interpretation: A circled number '2' in the right margin, appearing twice in close proximity. This is almost certainly Tom's structural labeling of the argument's second major section or second key claim on this page. The first circled '2' appears to mark the transition to the 'contemporary university' paragraph, and the second may mark a sub-point within it. Tom is organizing the page's argument into numbered units, likely for cross-referencing in his own notes or for reconstructing MacIntyre's argumentative structure.
annotations.rc_relevance: Tom's structural mapping suggests he is tracking MacIntyre's argument step-by-step, likely to deploy it in RC's analysis of how contemporary institutions fail to support moral enquiry — the numbering indicates this passage is architecturally important.

annotations.content: the contemporary university the nineteenth-century lecture is a genre as impossible as the medieval
annotations.anchor_text: _For us in the contemporary university the_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks MacIntyre's bold diagnostic claim: both medieval disputation and nineteenth-century authoritative lecturing are unavailable to us. This is a crucial moment in the argument — not just nostalgia for one lost form, but recognition that we are in a genuinely novel and more difficult predicament. Tom is likely registering the weight of this claim for RC: recovery of tradition cannot be naive repetition.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's project of recovering tradition must grapple with this claim directly: if traditional forms of enquiry are genuinely 'impossible' rather than merely unfashionable, recovery requires creative reconstruction, not mere restoration.

annotations.content: we no more share the agreements presupposed by the deference of the nineteenth-century audience than we do the acknowledgment of authoritative texts by the twelfth or thirteenth century
annotations.anchor_text: _for we no more share the agreements presupposed_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's parallel construction: both forms of shared agreement — deference to authoritative persons AND deference to authoritative texts — are gone. This is a symmetrical diagnosis. Tom may be noting that MacIntyre is not simply nostalgic for the medieval but is making a structural claim about the loss of any shared epistemic framework. The question this raises for Tom: is there a third form of shared agreement still possible?
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the crux of RC's challenge: the project seeks to recover civic friendship and shared moral vocabulary in conditions where MacIntyre says the necessary agreements no longer exist — RC must either contest this claim or propose how to rebuild those agreements.

annotations.content: a lecture can only be an episode in a narrative of conflicts
annotations.anchor_text: _of radical disagreements a lecture can only_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks this as MacIntyre's key redescription of what a lecture IS in contemporary conditions — not a transmission of knowledge but a move in an ongoing conflict. The word 'narrative' is philosophically loaded for MacIntyre: this is not mere rhetorical conflict but a structured historical drama with protagonists. Tom is likely flagging this as the point where MacIntyre's narrative epistemology and his social diagnosis converge.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this reframes the challenge of civility: if every intellectual exchange is a moment in a conflict narrative, then civility cannot be achieved by procedural neutrality but only by a substantive account of what the conflict is about and what resolution would look like.

annotations.content: it is always a moment of engagement in conflict
annotations.anchor_text: _by a necessarily less than innocent bystander, but_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's insistence on the inescapability of conflict-engagement — even apparent neutrality ('report from the sidelines') is a position within the conflict. The phrase 'necessarily less than innocent bystander' is key: there is no view from nowhere. Tom may be noting this as a direct challenge to liberal proceduralism and its claims of neutrality, relevant to how RC positions itself.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC cannot claim a neutral standpoint above the conflicts it diagnoses — this annotation suggests Tom recognizes that RC itself must own its position within the tradition vs. Enlightenment conflict MacIntyre is mapping.

annotations.content: they were to convey "real" knowledge of a kind which "lies at the root of all well-being." A summons to the hearing of such a lecture is very different from a summons to participate in a conflict over what the reality of knowledge consists in, if anything.
annotations.anchor_text: _they were to convey real knowledge of a kind_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets this extended passage with a green bracket, marking it as a unit. The contrast MacIntyre draws — between being summoned to receive knowledge and being summoned to contest what knowledge is — is the sharpest formulation of the book's central problem on this page. The bracket suggests Tom sees this as a passage worth quoting or returning to, perhaps as a set-piece statement of the contemporary predicament.
annotations.rc_relevance: This bracketed passage crystallizes the RC project's motivating tension: Gifford's bequest assumed a shared understanding of knowledge's relation to well-being that no longer holds — RC seeks to argue that only the recovery of a Thomistic framework can restore that connection.

annotations.content: therefore unsurprising that one of the two most notable nineteenth-century rejections of the encyclopaedists' conception of knowledge and of rationality should have been accompanied, indeed preceded by an abandonment of the lecture itself as a genre
annotations.anchor_text: _It is therefore unsurprising that one of the two_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the logical link MacIntyre draws between rejecting encyclopaedist epistemology and rejecting its institutional form. This is a methodologically important claim: ideas and their institutional vehicles are internally connected, not contingently related. To reject the encyclopaedist worldview authentically requires rejecting its pedagogy. Tom is tracking how MacIntyre makes form and content mutually implicate.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with institutions forming character runs in both directions: if institutions embody epistemologies, then recovering Thomistic enquiry requires attending to which institutional forms can carry it — the lecture alone cannot.

annotations.content: abandonment of the lecture itself as a genre
annotations.anchor_text: _preceded by an abandonment of the lecture itself_
annotations.interpretation: A second, focused highlight on 'abandonment of the lecture itself as a genre' (appears to overlap with the longer highlight above, suggesting Tom double-marked it for emphasis). The genre point is philosophically significant: genres are not neutral containers but carry epistemological presuppositions. Tom is marking Nietzsche's gesture as not merely biographical but as a philosophical act about the relationship between form and content.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's question of what forms of discourse and institution can carry genuine moral enquiry — the RC project itself must think about its own genre and institutional vehicle, not just its content.

annotations.content: abandonment of both professorial chair and professorial stance, was an integral part of his preparation for the assumption of a new role, that of genealogist
annotations.anchor_text: _sche's breaking away from the university, his abandonment_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the culminating identification: Nietzsche's exit from the university is the precondition for becoming a genealogist. This is MacIntyre setting up the genealogist as one of his three 'rival versions' — and doing so by showing that the role required institutional rupture. Tom is tracking the introduction of the key term 'genealogist' as it formally enters the argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: The genealogist role is the second vertex of MacIntyre's tripartite structure that RC must engage; Tom's highlighting here shows he is mapping where each 'version' is formally introduced, essential for RC's use of MacIntyre's framework.

annotations.content: a new role
annotations.anchor_text: _preparation for the assumption of a new role_
annotations.interpretation: The phrase 'a new role' receives distinct highlighting, suggesting Tom wants to isolate the performative and social dimension of Nietzsche's move. A 'role' implies not just a set of ideas but a social position, an audience, a mode of address. Tom may be noting that MacIntyre understands intellectual traditions as constituted by social roles, not just doctrines — another instance of the form/content unity thesis.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with civic friendship and institutional formation is illuminated here: moral enquiry is always embodied in social roles, and recovery of Thomistic enquiry means recovering not just its arguments but the social roles — teacher, disputant, confessor — that give those arguments their life.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that Nietzsche was thoroughly formed within the orthodoxies of nineteenth-century classical philology under Ritschl, whose project united philological, archaeological, and institutional study to render antiquity serviceable to modern culture. Nietzsche's rupture with this tradition—expressed most scandalously in 'Die Geburt der Tragödie'—stemmed from his perception that classical philologists had, in pursuing their academic purposes, actually alienated themselves from the classical realities they claimed to study and concealed this alienation from themselves. The outrage greeting that book, from Ritschl's silence to Wilamowitz's pamphlet, confirmed that the offense was not merely scholarly but struck at the self-legitimating structure of academic enquiry itself.

annotations.content: what new role today? in TT? Contry blds?
annotations.anchor_text: _No one had been more thoroughly educated_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is asking whether the function Ritschl's philological project served—synthesizing antiquity into a resource for contemporary culture—has any analog today. 'TT' likely refers to a specific text or seminar Tom is tracking (possibly 'Three Traditions' or a reading group). 'Contry blds' almost certainly means 'country buildings' or 'country builders,' possibly shorthand for a subsidiary or local civic institution argument Tom is developing. He is probing whether the institutionalized transmission of classical learning has a contemporary equivalent or successor.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's concern with how institutions form character and transmit tradition: Tom is asking whether the university's culture-forming philological role (Altertumswissenschaft) has collapsed, and what, if anything, has replaced it at the level of civic or local community formation.

annotations.content: what was SS role? Ps? Mack?
annotations.anchor_text: _No one had been more thoroughly educated_
annotations.interpretation: This appears to be a second line of the same top marginal note cluster. 'SS' could refer to a scholar's initials or a shorthand for 'social science' or possibly 'subsidiarity structures.' 'Ps' may mean 'professors' or 'Platonists.' 'Mack' almost certainly refers to MacIntyre himself—Tom is asking what MacIntyre's own institutional role or position is relative to the dynamic being described, perhaps flagging that MacIntyre, like Nietzsche, was himself formed inside the academic tradition he critiques.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's MacIntyre theme: if MacIntyre is the 'tradition' voice critiquing the encyclopaedist university, Tom wants to know whether MacIntyre risks the same self-concealment he diagnoses in Nietzsche's philological predecessors—a crucial internal tension for RC's recovery project.

annotations.content: No one had been more thoroughly educated into the orthodoxies of nineteenth-century academia than had Nietzsche
annotations.anchor_text: _more thoroughly educated into the orthodoxies_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is marking the paradox that will drive the whole chapter: the most radical subverter of academic orthodoxy was also its most thoroughly formed product. This is not incidental biographical detail—MacIntyre is establishing that genealogical critique grows from within tradition, not outside it. Tom likely sees this as structurally important to the tripartite scheme: the genealogist is not an alien but a renegade insider.
annotations.rc_relevance: Central to RC's 'tradition vs. reform' tension: genuine subversion of a tradition requires deep formation within it—a point with implications for how RC frames its own reformist project vis-à-vis liberalism.

annotations.content: Ritschl
annotations.anchor_text: _Friedrich Wilhelm Ritschl who died in 1876_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging Ritschl as a key named figure to track. The circle isolates him as an institutional actor whose pedagogical role (forming Nietzsche) is causally significant. Tom may be noting the irony that the man who most shaped Nietzsche later publicly distanced himself from him, or he may be marking Ritschl as a type: the institution-builder whose synthesis eventually produces its own undoing.
annotations.rc_relevance: Ritschl exemplifies the RC theme of institutions forming character—and forming it in ways that exceed the institution's own intentions. RC must reckon with how tradition-transmitting institutions can inadvertently cultivate the critics who dismantle them.

annotations.content: Wilamowitz-Moellendorf
annotations.anchor_text: _Ulrich von Wilamowitz-Moellendorf who in 1877_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles Wilamowitz as a contrasting figure to Nietzsche—the man who stays fully within the philological orthodoxy and attacks Nietzsche's transgression. Wilamowitz represents the 'encyclopaedist' position in MacIntyre's tripartite: the defender of academic scientific method as the sole legitimate mode of enquiry. The circle signals Tom is tracking this contrast deliberately.
annotations.rc_relevance: Wilamowitz as encyclopaedist stands in for the RC target of Enlightenment rationalism institutionalized in the modern research university—the position MacIntyre's 'tradition' strand must argue against.

annotations.content: Nietzsche was appointed to the chair of classical philology at Basel in 1869 at the age of twenty-four
annotations.anchor_text: _appointed to the chair of classical philology at Basel_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this to anchor the chronology and to mark the institutional legitimation Nietzsche initially received. The appointment at twenty-four signals he was not a marginal figure but a certified insider—which makes his subsequent repudiation of the tradition more structurally significant. Tom may also be noting the irony of the Basel chair as the launchpad for anti-academic genealogy.
annotations.rc_relevance: Relevant to RC's theme of institutional formation: Nietzsche's institutional credentialing is the precondition of his credibility as a critic, raising the question of whether reformers of tradition must first be recognized by it.

annotations.content: classical philologists in fact to understand classical realities, he was to remark, they would recoil horrified. And they would do so in part at least because they would have to acknowledge that their own academic purposes had alienated them from their object of study and concealed it from them
annotations.anchor_text: _they would recoil horrified. And they would do so_
annotations.interpretation: This is the most heavily marked passage on the page, and Tom's bracket signals it is the argumentative core. MacIntyre is making the self-concealment claim: academic enquiry systematically hides from itself the gap between its stated object (classical reality) and its actual product (a modern cultural artifact). This is the genealogist's diagnostic move—exposing motivated self-ignorance. Tom's bracket says: this is the hinge of the argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's MacIntyre theme and to the encyclopaedist critique: the modern university's claim to objective enquiry is, on this account, a concealment of its own culture-serving distortions. RC's recovery of Thomistic enquiry must grapple with whether it too risks this self-concealment.

annotations.content: Kotnian
annotations.anchor_text: _they would recoil horrified. And they would do so_
annotations.interpretation: This is likely a surname—possibly a scholar Tom associates with this argument about academic self-concealment, or a student/colleague whose work engages this theme. It may also be a misspelling of a known theorist (e.g., 'Kontian' as a phonetic for 'Kantian,' though that seems less likely given the context). Tom is cross-referencing someone who has written on this self-concealment dynamic in academic institutions.
annotations.rc_relevance: If this names a contemporary scholar, it suggests Tom is building an RC bibliography around the critique of academic self-legitimation—a key move if RC aims to diagnose how modern institutions obscure their own character-forming (or deforming) functions.

annotations.content: their own academic purposes had alienated them from their object of study and concealed it from them
annotations.anchor_text: _academic purposes had alienated them from their object_
annotations.interpretation: Tom separately underlines this clause even though it falls within the bracket, indicating he wants this specific formulation—'alienated from their object of study and concealed it'—as a quotable claim. The double gesture (bracket + underline) marks this as a passage Tom expects to cite directly in RC writing. The language of alienation and concealment carries clear Marxian resonance that MacIntyre is deliberately invoking.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the diagnostic sentence for RC's critique of the modern university as a tradition-destroying institution: it does not merely fail to transmit virtue but actively alienates enquirers from the realities virtue-enquiry requires.

annotations.content: small left-pointing angle bracket or chevron in left margin
annotations.anchor_text: _Nietzsche was appointed to the chair_
annotations.interpretation: This chevron appears to be a 'point here' marker, directing attention to the Basel appointment sentence. Tom uses it as a lighter form of emphasis than a bracket—possibly indicating a fact to verify or a structural marker in his own notes rather than a contested claim.
annotations.rc_relevance: Ambiguous in RC terms, but likely marks institutional legitimation as a precondition of effective critique—a point RC must address when positioning its own project's authority.

annotations.content: small left-pointing angle bracket or chevron in left margin
annotations.anchor_text: _boundaries was integral to its judgment upon modernity_
annotations.interpretation: A second chevron lower on the page, near the discussion of 'Die Geburt der Tragödie' and its challenge to academic boundaries. Tom is flagging the claim that the book's boundary-violation was not incidental but constitutive of its argument about modernity. This is a structural point: form and content are unified in Nietzsche's offense.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's own project of crossing disciplinary and institutional boundaries (theology, philosophy, sociology) may need to own this transgressive posture rather than apologize for it—Tom may be noting this as a precedent.

pages.summary: This page presents MacIntyre's account of Nietzsche's genealogical critique of truth, knowledge, duty, and right as masks for unacknowledged motivations and will-to-power. Nietzsche argues that to participate in the late nineteenth-century discourse of truth — exemplified by the Encyclopaedia Britannica's Ninth Edition — is to be a 'deformed person,' shaped by drives whose inhibition produces complicity in systemic suppression. The page identifies four key aspects of Nietzsche's mature thought (psychological, epistemological, historical, literary) already present in his 1873 essay 'On Truth and Lies in a Nonmoral Sense.'

annotations.content: until 1879
annotations.anchor_text: _did not finally resign his Basel chair_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles the date of Nietzsche's formal resignation from Basel, likely to anchor the biographical-institutional timeline. This may be cross-referencing the tension between Nietzsche's critique of universities and his own prolonged membership in one — a tension MacIntyre exploits to show that genealogical critique emerges from within the very institutions it attacks.
annotations.rc_relevance: This bears directly on the RC theme of institutions forming (or deforming) character: Nietzsche remained institutionally embedded even as he theorized institutional deformation, a paradox Tom may want to use when discussing whether reform must come from within tradition.

annotations.content: 34 yrs old
annotations.anchor_text: _did not finally resign his Basel chair_
annotations.interpretation: Tom calculates Nietzsche's age at the 1879 resignation (born 1844, resigned 1879 = 35, though Tom writes 34, possibly rounding to the birthday within the year or referencing the 1873 essay date). This humanizing biographical note suggests Tom is tracking Nietzsche's intellectual development relative to life stage — asking how young Nietzsche was when he had already formulated these radical epistemological positions.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this may flag the question of whether youthful radicalism against tradition is philosophically sustainable — a point MacIntyre will use to contrast the genealogist's instability with the tradition-bearer's long formation.

annotations.content: No genuinely radical living for truth is possible in a university
annotations.anchor_text: _letter to Erwin Rohde December 15 1830_
annotations.interpretation: This is one of the most directly quotable lines on the page and Tom marks it emphatically. The underline signals he sees this as a core genealogist claim: institutional belonging is structurally incompatible with genuine truth-seeking. Tom may be flagging this for direct quotation or for contestation — MacIntyre himself will push back on whether this anti-institutional stance is coherent.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the sharpest challenge the genealogist poses to the RC project's reliance on institutions (universities, churches, civic associations) as sites of moral formation. Tom will need to answer Nietzsche's charge that such institutions systematically corrupt rather than form.

annotations.content: not only the university but truth itself had to be put in question
annotations.anchor_text: _it was not long before not only the university_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the escalation from institutional critique to epistemological critique — Nietzsche moves from 'universities corrupt truth-seeking' to 'truth itself is a fiction.' This is the hinge moment MacIntyre is narrating, and Tom marks it to track the logical progression from sociology of knowledge to radical epistemological nihilism.
annotations.rc_relevance: This escalation is precisely what MacIntyre's tradition-based enquiry must resist: RC's Thomistic framework depends on the possibility of genuine rational enquiry toward truth, which Nietzsche's move here would dissolve entirely.

annotations.content: Truths are illusions which we have forgotten are illusions, worn-out metaphors now impotent to stir the senses, coins which have lost their faces and are considered now as metal rather than currency
annotations.anchor_text: _Truths are illusions which we have forgotten_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets the extended Nietzsche quotation from 'Über Wahrheit und Lüge' — one of the most cited passages in postmodern epistemology. The bracket rather than underline suggests Tom is marking this as a block to be treated as a unit, possibly for quotation or for structural analysis. The 'coins' metaphor is precisely the kind of literary-rhetorical move MacIntyre will later analyze as characteristic of genealogical style.
annotations.rc_relevance: This passage is the genealogist's sharpest weapon against the encyclopaedist's confidence in rational progress. For RC, Tom needs to know how MacIntyre responds: can Thomistic realism absorb the force of this metaphor without dismissing it?

annotations.anchor_text: _By 1873 he was asking What then is truth_
annotations.interpretation: This appears to be a page cross-reference — Tom is linking this passage to page 328 of another text, possibly a secondary source on Nietzsche or another MacIntyre work. The ellipsis suggests an ongoing note or that Tom was uncertain of the full reference. This is classic scholarly cross-referencing behavior, tracking where MacIntyre's or Nietzsche's argument surfaces elsewhere.
annotations.rc_relevance: The cross-reference suggests Tom is building an intertextual argument for RC, tracking how Nietzsche's epistemology is treated across the secondary literature, or connecting this to another MacIntyre passage on the genealogist's self-refuting position.

annotations.content: four key aspects of Nietzsche's later developed thought, psychological, epistemological, historical, and literary
annotations.anchor_text: _passage there are already to be found four key aspects_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's explicit structural taxonomy of Nietzsche's thought. This is a navigational mark — Tom is flagging the four-part schema that will organize MacIntyre's subsequent analysis. The underline ensures Tom can return to this as the interpretive key for the chapters that follow.
annotations.rc_relevance: This four-part schema is MacIntyre's analytical grid for the genealogist position in his tripartite structure (encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition). Tom needs this to understand how MacIntyre will systematically dismantle each aspect, which RC must engage with to make its case for tradition-based enquiry.

annotations.content: Psych
annotations.anchor_text: _Psychologically what is taken to be fixed and binding about truth_
annotations.interpretation: Tom labels the first of the four aspects with a marginal abbreviation, creating his own navigational system parallel to MacIntyre's. This is active structural reading — Tom is not just marking content but building an outline in the margins. The placement beside the paragraph beginning the psychological analysis confirms he is tracking the four-part argument in real time.
annotations.rc_relevance: The psychological dimension of Nietzsche's critique — that claims to truth mask unacknowledged motivations — is the most directly relevant to RC's concern with virtue and character formation: if moral claims are psychological projections, MacIntyre's entire tradition-based ethics is undermined.

annotations.content: Nietzsche would of course have said the same about knowledge and duty and right — is an unrecognized motivation serving an unacknowledged purpose
annotations.anchor_text: _Nietzsche would of course have said the same_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the expansion of the psychological critique from truth to knowledge, duty, and right — the full range of normative discourse. This marks the scope of Nietzsche's genealogical suspicion as total: no normative claim escapes the charge of motivated self-deception. Tom may be flagging this as the maximalist position MacIntyre must counter.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the genealogist's challenge to RC at its most comprehensive: duty and right — the very vocabulary of civic virtue and moral community that RC seeks to recover — are here rendered as symptoms of will-to-power. RC cannot ignore this charge.

annotations.content: To be, and not to rebel against being, a member of the professoriate or of its disciples is to be a deformed person, deformed by whatever drive it is whose inhibition and distortion have led to an unacknowledged complicity in a system of suppressions and repressions
annotations.anchor_text: _To be and not to rebel against being a member_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets this extended claim about the professoriate as a deforming institution. The choice to bracket rather than merely underline suggests this is a passage Tom wants to treat as an argument-unit, possibly to contest or to use as evidence of the genealogist's self-undermining radicalism. The claim is extreme — university membership as moral deformation — and Tom likely flags it as the point where MacIntyre's critique of Nietzsche will land hardest.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's engagement with subsidiarity and institutional formation: if the professoriate is structurally deforming, what institutions can form virtuous persons? MacIntyre's answer (tradition-bearing communities) must overcome exactly this genealogical skepticism.

annotations.content: That drive turns out to be what Nietzsche was later to characterize as the will-to-power
annotations.anchor_text: _Nietzsche was later to characterize as the will-to-power_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the explicit naming of will-to-power as the culmination of the psychological analysis. This is a key term-tracking mark — MacIntyre is showing how the early essay anticipates the mature concept, and Tom flags the moment of conceptual arrival. This may also be where Tom connects to his margin note 'will to power' on the right.
annotations.rc_relevance: Will-to-power is the genealogist's alternative to tradition as the ground of human action. RC must articulate why telos and virtue provide a more adequate account of human motivation than Nietzsche's will-to-power — this underline marks where that contest is joined.

annotations.content: will to power

annotations.interpretation: Tom writes out the term in full in the margin, likely as a retrieval keyword or to signal this is a terminus technicus he is tracking across the book. The fact that he writes it out rather than abbreviating suggests he is making a deliberate conceptual note, possibly preparing to compare MacIntyre's treatment of will-to-power with other accounts (Foucault, perhaps, or MacIntyre's own earlier work).
annotations.rc_relevance: Will-to-power is the genealogist's counter-concept to the tradition's teleological account of human striving. Tom tracking it here suggests he is building toward an RC argument about what motivates civic virtue if not will-to-power.

This is systematic structural annotation.
annotations.rc_relevance: The epistemological dimension of the genealogist's critique is where MacIntyre's argument about rival versions of moral enquiry is most acute: if the encyclopaedist's epistemology is blind to its own motivated character, the tradition must offer an alternative epistemology of enquiry — precisely what RC's Thomistic framework attempts.

annotations.interpretation: The arrow beneath 'Epist' points downward, indicating that the epistemological discussion continues beyond this page. Tom is signaling that his 'Epist' label is not complete at this point — the argument extends and he will need to track it on subsequent pages. This is a reading-continuity mark.
annotations.rc_relevance: The incompleteness marker suggests Tom knows the epistemological critique is the longest or most important of the four, consistent with RC's core concern: can reason genuinely achieve moral truth, or is it always already distorted by inarticulate drives?

pages.summary: MacIntyre examines how commentators have responded to Nietzsche's perspectivism — the claim that all truth-claims are made from particular standpoints — and argues that such commentary inadvertently smuggles in a universal, non-perspectival theory of truth to make Nietzsche's position coherent. He then raises the deeper problem: if Nietzsche's denials of Judaism, Christianity, Kant, and utilitarianism seem to carry unconditional force, his affirmations may too, risking restoration of the very conventional academic philosophy his genealogy was meant to subvert.

The phrase 'what it takes to rec. nonperspectivity' suggests Tom is tracking the internal contradiction MacIntyre is exposing: that rendering perspectivism coherent requires the very non-perspectival stance it denies. Tom is flagging this as a structural crux in the genealogist's self-undermining.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly maps onto MacIntyre's tripartite structure (encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition) and the RC theme of Enlightenment rationalism's self-defeat: the genealogist cannot escape the rationalist framework he attacks, which is central to MacIntyre's case for tradition-constituted rationality.

Tom may be flagging that nihilism (Niho) is being equated with a kind of conceptual blindness (Keln possibly = 'Keinen,' German for 'none,' or a scholar's name), marking MacIntyre's implicit claim that perspectivism collapses into nihilism.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project's concern with social fragmentation is rooted in the claim that nihilism follows from the rejection of tradition-grounded rationality; Tom is likely marking where MacIntyre begins to expose this trajectory in Nietzsche.

This is a key irony MacIntyre is drawing out, and Tom is marking it as a pivot in the argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to the RC theme of how moral traditions form the perceptual horizon of agents — blindness to one's own tradition-embeddedness is the condition MacIntyre diagnoses, and which the RC project aims to remedy through recovered self-awareness.

annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry depends on a realist ontology — a single world that moral enquiry tracks. This underline marks the precise point where the genealogist's ontology conflicts with the tradition MacIntyre is defending.

This is likely a term Tom is tracking as a recurring concept through the chapter, possibly to map it against the encyclopaedist's version of rationality.
annotations.rc_relevance: MacIntyre's critique of the encyclopaedist tradition in RC terms rests on showing that both encyclopaedists and genealogists share an unacknowledged commitment to universal standards — this underline marks the genealogist's version of that failure.

This is a critical annotation where Tom is playing devil's advocate against MacIntyre.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project needs MacIntyre's critique of genealogy to hold; if perspectivism can be rendered coherent without self-contradiction, the case for tradition-constituted rationality weakens. Tom is marking a vulnerability in MacIntyre's argument that RC would need to address.

annotations.anchor_text: _it is rather the status of such commentary_

This is a meta-philosophical move Tom recognizes as central to MacIntyre's strategy of questioning the self-understanding of rival traditions.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to the RC theme of how traditions authorize their own interpretive frameworks — MacIntyre is asking whether genealogical commentary is parasitic on intentions it cannot acknowledge, which mirrors the RC argument that liberal moral discourse is parasitic on tradition it has abandoned.

annotations.content: The denials of truth to Judaism, to Christianity, to Kant's philosophy, and to utilitarianism do seem to have the force of unconditional and universal nonperspectival denials.
annotations.anchor_text: _denials of truth to Judaism to Christianity_

This is likely flagged because it is MacIntyre's strongest textual argument, and Tom wants to return to it when constructing RC's account of why genealogy is self-defeating.
annotations.rc_relevance: Central to RC's use of MacIntyre: the genealogist's attack on tradition inadvertently demonstrates the inescapability of tradition-grounded authority claims, reinforcing the case for recovering Thomistic moral community rather than abandoning normative frameworks entirely.

annotations.content: stood will have been restored to conventional academic philosophy
annotations.anchor_text: _stood will have been restored to conventional academic philosophy_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this ironic conclusion — that understanding Nietzsche as operating at two levels (author and implicit commentator) neutralizes his radicalism by folding him back into the academy he attacked. This is MacIntyre's reductio, and Tom marks it as a decisive moment in the argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: This maps onto the RC concern with how institutions domesticate radical critiques — the same process by which liberal institutions absorb and neutralize communitarian or virtue-ethics challenges, which MacIntyre treats as a structural feature of encyclopaedist culture.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the difference between encyclopaedic and Nietzschean modes of moral utterance is not merely about perspectivism versus unity of truth, but about a deeper contrast between utterance as fixed belief and utterance as a moment in a shifting play of forces. Crucially, Nietzsche himself did not fully surrender to his own genealogical mode — he retained a 'commentator self' that stood external to both modes, providing grounds for analytic anticipations others have found in him. The instability and ambiguity in Nietzsche's writing stems from his failure (or refusal) to demarcate a consistent boundary between this abstracted commenting self and the perspectival voice, leaving the question open whether such demarcation is even possible.

annotations.content: 2 goal: a loving cluster of fruitfully interacting perspectives? Christ in me, in you, in us.
annotations.anchor_text: _order the hostile inhabitants of a territory_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is writing a counter-vision to the Nietzschean 'play of forces' — not hostile territorial contest but a 'loving cluster' of fruitful perspectives. The invocation of 'Christ in me, in you, in us' is a Pauline-Johannine reference (Galatians 2:20, John 17) suggesting Tom sees a Trinitarian or kenotic model of intersubjective truth-seeking as the theological alternative to both encyclopaedic monism and Nietzschean perspectival conflict. This is Tom's own constructive voice breaking through in reaction to the text.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly addresses the RC project's recovery of civic friendship and moral community — Tom is sketching a theological grammar of community that transcends the encyclopaedist/genealogist binary by grounding perspectival plurality in Christological unity-in-difference.

annotations.content: es ist were a thought? child?
annotations.anchor_text: _points us towards what is in part ambiguity_
annotations.interpretation: This appears to be Tom wrestling with the German 'es ist' (it is), possibly querying whether the 'instability in that thought itself' MacIntyre identifies is actually a generative, even nascent idea — like a child or embryonic thought — rather than a mere defect. The phrasing suggests Tom is asking whether Nietzsche's ambiguity is a kind of productive aporia. The annotation is ambiguous in transcription but seems to probe the ontological status of the unstable thought.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to the RC theme of tradition vs. reform — Tom may be probing whether Nietzsche's instability is analogous to the generative tensions within living traditions that MacIntyre values, rather than a simple failure.

annotations.content: EvSN
annotations.anchor_text: _The difference between that mode of utterance_
annotations.interpretation: This abbreviation likely stands for 'Encyclopaedist vs. [Nietzsche]' or 'Encyclopaedist vs. Nietzschean' — Tom is flagging the structural contrast MacIntyre is developing at this paragraph break. It functions as a structural label organizing the comparison the paragraph introduces, consistent with Tom tracking MacIntyre's tripartite schema throughout the book.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly tracks MacIntyre's central tripartite structure (encyclopaedist / genealogist / tradition), which is a foundational RC framework for diagnosing contemporary moral fragmentation.

annotations.content: A)
annotations.anchor_text: _is not merely a matter of the latter's multiplicity_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is numbering the sub-points of MacIntyre's multi-part argument about what the difference between encyclopaedic and Nietzschean modes consists in. Point A is the rejection of the claim that the difference is merely about perspectivism vs. unity of truth/reason — MacIntyre is clearing away a reductive reading before giving his positive account.
annotations.rc_relevance: Tom's structural annotation signals he is mapping MacIntyre's argumentative architecture carefully, likely because the precise nature of the encyclopaedist/genealogist distinction is crucial to RC's diagnostic framework for modernity.

annotations.content: B)
annotations.anchor_text: _only a matter of the latter's stress upon the conflict_
annotations.interpretation: Point B marks MacIntyre's second negation — it is also not merely about conflict between rival interpretations vs. synthesis toward agreed truth. Tom is tracking that MacIntyre is proceeding by elimination, ruling out reductive characterizations of the Nietzschean mode before arriving at what is distinctive about it (the fixity/fluidity contrast in utterance itself).
annotations.rc_relevance: The careful parsing of what the encyclopaedist/genealogist difference is NOT about is important for RC's argument that contemporary moral discourse has misunderstood the genealogist challenge — reducing it to mere relativism rather than a deeper challenge to the grammar of moral utterance.

annotations.content: C)
annotations.anchor_text: _truth. It is also a matter of the contrast between utterance_
annotations.interpretation: Point C marks MacIntyre's positive claim — the real difference is the contrast between utterance as expressing warranted fixity of belief and utterance as a moment in the development of one position against others in a shifting play of forces. This is the crux Tom has been tracking through A and B.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the conceptual heart of MacIntyre's critique of the genealogist mode and is highly relevant to RC's concern with how institutions form character through stable, committal moral utterance — a mode the genealogist's 'shifting play' structurally undermines.

annotations.content: in that thought itself, features that can be illuminated by a comparison with the encyclopaedic mode of utterance so decisively rejected by Nietzsche.
annotations.anchor_text: _points us towards what is in part ambiguity_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the claim that Nietzsche's own thought contains features illuminated by comparison with the encyclopaedic mode he rejects — a dialectical irony MacIntyre is exploiting. This suggests Tom is tracking how Nietzsche remains entangled with what he opposes, which is a key move in MacIntyre's genealogy of genealogy.
annotations.rc_relevance: Relevant to RC's engagement with MacIntyre's critique of the Enlightenment — the genealogist inherits the very structures he critiques, which parallels RC's argument that secular liberal frameworks remain parasitic on the traditions they displace.

annotations.content: is not merely a matter of the latter's multiplicity of perspectives and relativization of truth to those perspectives, as against the former's underlying conception of the unity of truth and reason

This is a critical methodological point: MacIntyre refuses to let the debate be settled at the level of epistemology alone, pushing toward something deeper about the grammar of moral assertion.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is concerned with recovering a substantive account of moral truth against both encyclopaedic rationalism and genealogical relativism — Tom is marking where MacIntyre refuses both easy options.

annotations.content: to express what is taken to be a warranted fixity of belief and utterance construed as a moment in the development of one position against others

annotations.interpretation: This is the positive, defining contrast MacIntyre draws — and Tom underlines it as the argumentative payoff of the A/B/C sequence. 'Warranted fixity of belief' versus positional development in a shifting play: this is the grammar of encyclopaedist vs. genealogist utterance at its deepest level.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is foundational for RC's theory of civic character formation — stable, committal moral utterance ('warranted fixity') is what institutions and traditions transmit, and what the genealogist mode systematically corrodes.

annotations.content: as a moment in the development of one position against others
annotations.anchor_text: _warranted fixity of belief and utterance construed_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles this phrase within the already-underlined passage, indicating it is the most precise and important clause — the one that captures what is distinctively Nietzschean about genealogical utterance: it is inherently agonistic and developmental, never resting in a fixed claim.
annotations.rc_relevance: The circled phrase crystallizes the RC concern with agonistic vs. cooperative moral discourse — the genealogist's mode structurally precludes the civic friendship MacIntyre and RC seek to recover.

annotations.content: It is the movement between this abstracted commenting self and the self whose only voice and view is that of some perspective which is the source of the instability in Nietzsche's writing; it was his failure to demarcate any consistent boundary between those selves which is the source of the ambiguity. But 'failure' may be the wrong word. For it remains to be asked whether such demarcation is in fact pos-
annotations.anchor_text: _It is the movement between this abstracted commenting self_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets this entire concluding passage — it is the argumentative climax of the page, where MacIntyre names the structural source of Nietzsche's ambiguity (the oscillation between commentator-self and perspectival-self) and then immediately questions whether 'failure' is the right diagnosis. Tom is marking this as the key unresolved question the chapter hinges on, likely because it opens onto the question of whether any thinker can escape this double-bind.
annotations.rc_relevance: This bracketed passage speaks directly to RC's concern with self-knowledge and the integrity of the moral agent — the question of whether one can maintain a stable self that both comments on and inhabits a tradition is central to MacIntyre's Thomistic alternative, and to RC's project of character formation.

annotations.content: the source of the instability in Nietzsche's writing
annotations.anchor_text: _self whose only voice and view is that of some perspective_
annotations.interpretation: Double-underlined or strongly underlined, this phrase identifies the structural diagnosis MacIntyre offers for Nietzsche's textual instability. Tom is flagging this as a key explanatory claim — the instability is not accidental but structural, arising from the unresolved tension between two modes of selfhood.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this diagnosis of instability is important because it suggests that a coherent moral voice requires a settled self — which points back to the Thomistic account of practical identity that MacIntyre will develop as the alternative.

annotations.content: is the source of the ambiguity. But 'failure' may be the wrong word
annotations.anchor_text: _to demarcate any consistent boundary between those selves_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's self-correction — the scare-quotes around 'failure' and the pivot to asking whether demarcation is even possible. This is MacIntyre at his most generous to Nietzsche, and Tom marks it as significant, perhaps because it opens the question of whether the instability is a vice or a feature of any honest thinker navigating modernity.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's nuanced engagement with modernity — RC does not simply dismiss genealogical thinkers but asks whether their instability reveals something true about the condition of the self in a fragmented culture, a condition that only a recovered tradition can heal.

annotations.content: like a fighter constantly shifting styles?
annotations.anchor_text: _play of forces and use of metaphors. Yet Nietzsche_
annotations.interpretation: Tom introduces a martial arts or boxing analogy — a fighter who constantly shifts styles cannot be 'read' by an opponent but also has no settled form. This is Tom's own interpretive gloss on the Nietzschean oscillation between commentator-self and perspectival-self: it is strategically effective but constitutively unstable. The question mark indicates Tom is testing this analogy rather than asserting it.
annotations.rc_relevance: The fighter analogy connects to RC's concern with the formation of stable character and virtue — a practitioner of virtue has a settled style, a habituated form, whereas the genealogist's perpetual style-shifting is the opposite of the Thomistic virtuous agent. This is Tom probing MacIntyre's critique through an embodied metaphor.

annotations.content: Downward-pointing arrow or caret in the margin beside the paragraph beginning 'The difference between that mode'
annotations.anchor_text: _The difference between that mode of utterance and Nietzsche's_
annotations.interpretation: The arrow likely marks this as the paragraph Tom considers most important on the page — a 'go here' or 'this is the crux' signal. Given that Tom also labeled this paragraph with A/B/C structure, the symbol reinforces that this is the argumentative core he is dissecting.
annotations.rc_relevance: Tom is marking the structural center of MacIntyre's argument about the encyclopaedist/genealogist distinction, which is the diagnostic backbone of RC's account of contemporary moral fragmentation.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that Nietzsche deployed three distinct genres — academic treatise, aphorism, and prophetic/poetic mode — each as a deliberate philosophical strategy. The aphorism is not merely stylistic but constitutes an active, energy-bearing medium that outwits the reactive, academic mode of moral discourse. Crucially, Zur Genealogie der Moral poses a generic problem: it is neither aphorism nor poem but an academic treatise, and this is Nietzsche's chosen instrument for the genealogical task of exhibiting the historical genesis of the psychological and moral formations sustaining late nineteenth-century morality.
