annotations.content: So the genealogical genre is one in which present theses about what has been are presented in a genre open to what is not yet.
annotations.anchor_text: _genre open to what is not yet_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has highlighted the core definition of the genealogical genre as essentially open and non-teleological. This is likely a key term-tracking move: MacIntyre is distinguishing the genealogist's mode from the encyclopaedist's drive toward finality. Tom may be flagging this as a structural anchor for the tripartite contrast, or noting the irony that this 'openness' is itself a kind of normative commitment.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly engages the encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition tripartite structure central to RC — the genealogist's refusal of finality contrasts with the tradition-bearer's telos-oriented enquiry, which RC seeks to recover.

annotations.content: Such theses cannot have either the type of fixity or the type of finality to which the encyclopaedic mind always aspires
annotations.anchor_text: _encyclopaedic mind always aspires_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's pointed contrast between genealogical openness and encyclopaedic closure. The word 'always' is notable — MacIntyre is characterizing the encyclopaedist's aspiration as a structural feature, not merely an accidental one. Tom may be tracking how MacIntyre's critique of the Enlightenment encyclopedia (the Ninth Edition) operates by exposing its hidden metaphysical commitments.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's critique of Enlightenment rationalism is mirrored here — the encyclopaedist's false claim to neutral finality is precisely what MacIntyre (and RC) argue must be unmasked before genuine moral tradition can be recovered.

annotations.content: But this answer by the genealogist raises a further difficulty.
annotations.anchor_text: _raises a further difficulty_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this transitional sentence, likely because it marks the pivot to the self-undermining paradox of genealogy. MacIntyre is about to show that the genealogist's own practice of writing and publishing contradicts his theoretical commitments. This is a structural marker Tom is tracking — the moment when MacIntyre turns the genealogist's weapon against him.
annotations.rc_relevance: This self-refutation argument is central to RC's engagement with MacIntyre: the genealogist cannot escape the very standards of rational accountability that tradition-based enquiry openly embraces.

annotations.content: For the genealogist who has put the academic stance in question by writing and publishing his or her book is addressing whom?
annotations.anchor_text: _is addressing whom_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the rhetorical question that launches MacIntyre's tu quoque against the genealogist. The question exposes a performative contradiction: to publish is to appeal to a community of rational discourse, which the genealogist's position ostensibly deconstructs. Tom is likely flagging this as the crux of MacIntyre's anti-Nietzschean argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's interest in civic friendship and moral community — the very act of address presupposes the kind of shared rational standards that genealogy denies, pointing toward the necessity of tradition-constituted enquiry.

annotations.content: Yet this cannot be done without adopting a fixity of stance, a staying in place, a commitment to defend and to respond and, if necessary, to yield.
annotations.anchor_text: _commitment to defend and to respond_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's claim that rational discourse requires a kind of moral-epistemic commitment — a 'fixity of stance' — that the genealogist's official position forbids. This is one of MacIntyre's strongest moves: the genealogist who engages in debate has already conceded the game. Tom may be connecting this to virtues like intellectual honesty and the willingness to be accountable.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery of civility requires exactly this kind of committed, accountable stance in discourse — the opposite of the detached irony MacIntyre associates with genealogy. This passage supports RC's argument that genuine civic exchange requires virtue, not mere procedure.

annotations.content: A piece of writing, whenever it confronts a reader — or indeed a set speech, when it is reuttered to a new hearer — does so at a time which is not only 'now' for that reader or hearer, but becomes the author's coincident 'now'
annotations.anchor_text: _becomes the author's coincident 'now'_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's phenomenological claim about the temporality of texts — that writing creates a shared 'now' that is atemporal in a meaningful sense. This is philosophically dense: MacIntyre is building toward the claim that timeless rational standards are implicit in any communicative act. Tom may be flagging this as the metaphysical hinge of the argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's interest in how traditions transmit moral knowledge across time — the 'shared now' of text and reader is precisely the mechanism by which classical civility can speak to contemporary fragmentation.

annotations.content: the timelessness extends to the standards of reason-giving, reason-accepting, and reason-rejecting
annotations.anchor_text: _standards of reason-giving, reason-accepting_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the key claim that standards of rational discourse are themselves timeless — not historically contingent. This is MacIntyre's move against Nietzschean historicism: even the genealogist, in writing and arguing, implicitly appeals to these standards. Tom is almost certainly tracking this as the argumentative crux.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is foundational for RC: the recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry depends on the claim that standards of reason-giving are not mere cultural artifacts but have a timeless character — which MacIntyre here argues even the genealogist cannot escape.

annotations.content: This appeal to impersonal, timeless standards, so often taken for granted in the post-Enlightenment world by those who take themselves to have rejected metaphysics, is itself only to be understood adequately as a piece of metaphysics.
annotations.anchor_text: _is itself only to be understood adequately as a piece of metaphysics_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights what is arguably the most pointed claim on the page — MacIntyre's ironic reversal: those who claim to have rejected metaphysics are themselves making a metaphysical move every time they appeal to impersonal rational standards. This is the performative self-refutation at full force. The highlighting suggests Tom sees this as a passage worth citing or quoting directly.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's critique of post-Enlightenment moral fragmentation is grounded in precisely this MacIntyrean argument — the pretense of metaphysics-free rationalism is itself a metaphysical stance, and RC argues we must be honest about our metaphysical commitments in order to rebuild moral community.

annotations.content: The possibility of such an appeal is inseparable from the possibility of that atemporal 'now' at which writer and reader encounter each other
annotations.anchor_text: _writer and reader encounter each other_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the culminating move: the 'atemporal now' of writer-reader encounter grounds the possibility of rational appeal itself. MacIntyre is here making a quasi-transcendental argument — the conditions of possibility for rational discourse include a kind of timeless shared space. Tom may be connecting this to his interest in how tradition mediates between past and present.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this passage is significant because it suggests that recovery of classical civility is not merely antiquarian — the 'atemporal now' means ancient and early modern traditions of virtue and civic friendship can genuinely address contemporary interlocutors.

annotations.content: Arrow pointing right toward 'H=' in a circle or box, drawn in green ink in the right margin
annotations.anchor_text: _appeal to impersonal, timeless standards_
annotations.interpretation: This symbol — an arrow with 'H=' — is almost certainly a cross-reference or a shorthand label. 'H' may refer to Hume, Hegel, or more likely given the context, a section heading or another philosopher MacIntyre discusses. Alternatively, it could be Tom's own notation for 'Hermeneutics' or a specific page/chapter cross-reference. The equals sign may indicate that this passage is equivalent to or maps onto a claim made elsewhere. This is ambiguous but deliberate.
annotations.rc_relevance: If 'H=' refers to Hegel or a Hegelian claim about the timelessness of Geist or rational standards, this would connect to RC's engagement with idealist vs. Thomistic accounts of tradition. If it refers to Hume, it may flag the empiricist denial of metaphysics that MacIntyre is here countering.

annotations.content: Arrow drawn in green ink pointing right, in the right margin near the paragraph beginning 'For the genealogist'
annotations.anchor_text: _For the genealogist who has put the academic stance_
annotations.interpretation: A simple pointing arrow in the margin typically signals 'this is important' or 'return to this.' Tom may be marking the start of MacIntyre's key argumentative move against the genealogist. The green ink (used consistently for margin symbols on this page) distinguishes structural markers from the pink highlighting of key passages.
annotations.rc_relevance: This marks the beginning of MacIntyre's self-refutation argument against genealogy, which is one of RC's primary intellectual resources for arguing that post-modern dismissals of moral tradition are self-undermining.

annotations.content: Small sketch or doodle of a figure (appears to be a person with a lance or spear, possibly jousting), drawn in ink in the lower right margin
annotations.anchor_text: _writer and reader encounter each other_
annotations.interpretation: This is a figurative doodle — possibly a knight or jousting figure — placed near the passage about writer and reader encountering each other. This may be Tom's playful visual gloss on the 'encounter' between author and reader as a kind of intellectual combat or agon. Alternatively, it may reference Nietzsche specifically (given the context of MacIntyre's engagement with Nietzsche) — Nietzsche as warrior-philosopher. The placement is suggestive rather than accidental.
annotations.rc_relevance: The agonistic metaphor is resonant for RC: the recovery of civility is not a retreat from intellectual contest but a reframing of it within shared standards — the 'encounter' MacIntyre describes is precisely the kind of civic-intellectual engagement RC wants to rehabilitate.

annotations.content: And Nietzsche recognized the force of this objection.
annotations.anchor_text: _Nietzsche recognized the force of this objection_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the moment when MacIntyre credits Nietzsche with recognizing his own problem. This is significant because MacIntyre is not simply dismissing Nietzsche — he is engaging him seriously. Tom may be flagging this as evidence of MacIntyre's dialectical generosity, or as a pivot to how Nietzsche attempted to respond to the self-refutation charge.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's engagement with genealogy must reckon with the fact that Nietzsche was not naive about this objection — MacIntyre's acknowledgment here suggests the tradition vs. genealogy debate is genuinely dialectical, not a simple refutation, which has implications for how RC frames its recovery project.

annotations.content: fore the property of neither writer nor reader.
annotations.anchor_text: _property of neither writer nor reader_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this clause, which asserts that what is timelessly true belongs to neither party in the discourse — it transcends individual ownership. This is MacIntyre's implicit argument for a shared, impersonal rational order that neither genealogist nor encyclopaedist can privatize. Tom may be connecting this to questions of authority and tradition in RC.
annotations.rc_relevance: This speaks directly to RC's interest in subsidiarity and the common good — truth as 'property of neither writer nor reader' implies a shared moral-rational commons that institutions and traditions are meant to steward, not own.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that writer-reader encounters presuppose a minimal metaphysics: that both parties possess enough fixity of identity and relational continuity to enter into communicative relationships, even without sharing identical normative commitments. This 'modest, albeit metaphysical' claim is distinguished from Habermasian ideal-norm theories — it requires only logical, ontological, and evaluative commitments sufficient to recognize identities and differences, not agreement. The irony MacIntyre pursues is that post-Nietzschean genealogists who proscribe this metaphysics cannot eliminate its traces from their own writing practice, as evidenced by Heidegger's accusation of Nietzsche and Derrida's of Heidegger.

annotations.content: minimal metaphysics of encounter concept to M's recat[ation?] of N's genealogy central
annotations.anchor_text: _beings so assumed are fictitious among them_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is naming the structural concept of the entire page — MacIntyre's 'minimal metaphysics of encounter' — and linking it explicitly to MacIntyre's broader recasting (recharacterization) of Nietzsche's genealogical project. The word 'central' suggests Tom sees this as a load-bearing concept in the book's argument, not a subordinate point. He may be flagging this for later use as a summary label for this section of the tripartite structure.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'minimal metaphysics of encounter' directly bears on RC's concern with the preconditions for civic friendship and communication across difference — MacIntyre is arguing that even the genealogist cannot escape a form of relational ontology, which RC would want to recover and name positively as the ground of moral community.

annotations.content: beings so assumed are fictitious
annotations.anchor_text: _beings so assumed are fictitious among them_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights Nietzsche's own admission (from Der Wille zur Macht) that the ego of writer and reader are fictions, marking the tension MacIntyre is setting up: even the genealogist who deconstructs stable selfhood must invoke a functional version of it to write. This is the performative contradiction MacIntyre is diagnosing.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery of the self as a narrative and relational entity (contra atomistic liberal individualism) is illuminated here — MacIntyre shows that the denial of stable identity is self-undermining, which RC can leverage to argue for the necessity of a thicker account of the person.

annotations.content: unity to enter into those relationships constitutive of the act
annotations.anchor_text: _unity to enter into those relationships constitutive_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks 'unity' and 'those relationships' as key — MacIntyre is claiming that the act of writing-and-reading constitutively requires relational unity, not merely instrumental coordination. The phrase 'constitutive of the act' is philosophically loaded: the relationship is not external to the communicative act but internal to it.
annotations.rc_relevance: This maps directly onto RC's theme of institutions forming character through constitutive relationships — the communicative encounter is itself a practice in MacIntyre's sense, requiring virtues internal to it, anticipating the tradition-based account of enquiry RC wants to recover.

annotations.content: modest, albeit metaphysical
annotations.anchor_text: _claim that I am making is a modest_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's careful self-characterization of his thesis. The qualifier 'modest' is doing rhetorical and philosophical work against Habermas — MacIntyre is not claiming a full metaphysical system, only the minimum required. Tom likely sees this as MacIntyre's strategic positioning between encyclopaedist over-reach and genealogist under-reach.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is concerned with recovering tradition without over-claiming — MacIntyre's 'modest metaphysics' offers RC a model for how to make robust claims about human nature and community without falling into the encyclopaedist's rationalist overconfidence.

annotations.content: one, not to be confused, for example, with Habermas's neo-Kantian thesis that allegiance to one specific set of ideal norms is a necessary condition for acts of communication
annotations.anchor_text: _not to be confused for example with Habermas_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the explicit differentiation from Habermas as significant. MacIntyre is distinguishing his minimal relational ontology from Habermasian discourse ethics, which requires ideal communicative norms. This is a triangulation move: MacIntyre is carving out a position that is neither Nietzschean (no metaphysics) nor Habermasian (ideal-norm metaphysics).
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's engagement with MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism is sharpened here — Habermas represents a sophisticated Enlightenment position that RC must differentiate itself from, and MacIntyre's distinction provides the analytical tool.

annotations.content: commitments of the one need not be in all respects the same as those of the other
annotations.anchor_text: _commitments of the one need not be_
annotations.interpretation: Tom flags this as the crucial pluralist concession in MacIntyre's minimal metaphysics — difference is not only permitted but expected. The point is that shared ontological and evaluative commitments sufficient for communication do not require identity of content. This prevents MacIntyre's position from collapsing into a demand for homogeneity.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with civic friendship across difference — rooted in Aristotle's philia politike — finds support here: communication and moral community do not require sameness, only sufficient common ground in the structure of rational enquiry.

annotations.content: identities and differences without which each cannot
annotations.anchor_text: _identities and differences without which each cannot_
annotations.interpretation: The highlighting of 'identities and differences' as a paired concept suggests Tom sees this as a structural key — MacIntyre is arguing that both sameness and otherness are preconditions for communicative encounter, not obstacles to it. This has implications for how traditions engage one another in MacIntyre's epistemology.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's interest in subsidiarity and the integrity of particular communities is relevant here — identity and difference must both be preserved in any genuine encounter between traditions or communities, which is the structural basis for RC's pluralist communitarianism.

annotations.content: Yet even this is enough to engage both parties in the kind of metaphysics which Nietzsche and a variety of post-Nietzscheans have tried to proscribe
annotations.anchor_text: _Yet even this is enough to engage both parties_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks this as the pivotal irony of the page — even the minimal metaphysics MacIntyre defends is sufficient to implicate genealogists in what they claim to reject. 'Yet even this' signals a concessive-then-reversal argumentative move that Tom recognizes as structurally important. The phrase 'tried to proscribe' is damning: the prescription fails on its own terms.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is central to RC's engagement with the genealogist version of moral enquiry — MacIntyre shows that the genealogist's program is self-defeating, which RC uses to argue for the necessity of returning to tradition-constituted rationality rather than the impossible escape from it.

annotations.content: evidenced By Brown Studi[es?]
annotations.anchor_text: _physics of reading that those who proscribe_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is cross-referencing an external source — likely a scholar named Brown or a Brown University context — to this passage about how post-Nietzscheans fail to eliminate metaphysical traces from their own work. This suggests Tom has encountered empirical or scholarly evidence (possibly in cultural studies or literary theory) that corroborates MacIntyre's claim about the inescapability of this metaphysics in practice.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's argument that modern anti-traditionalist positions unconsciously rely on traditional moral resources would be strengthened by this kind of cross-disciplinary evidence — Tom seems to be building a dossier of cases where genealogical practice contradicts genealogical theory.

annotations.content: c[ore] of N's subversion / genealogy
annotations.anchor_text: _sche of retaining in his thought an unacknowledged_
annotations.interpretation: Tom labels this passage as identifying the 'core' of Nietzsche's subversive genealogical method — the very accusation Heidegger makes against Nietzsche (retaining unacknowledged metaphysical remnants) is what MacIntyre sees as structurally necessary. Tom is marking this as the deepest level of MacIntyre's critique: not just that genealogists fail, but that their failure is internally generated by the logic of their own project.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's tripartite structure (encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition) depends on showing that the genealogist position is not a stable third option but a self-undermining one — Tom's annotation marks the exact textual moment where MacIntyre makes this case most forcefully.

annotations.content: Nietz reply
annotations.anchor_text: _But to all this there is of course a Nietzschean_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging that MacIntyre is about to give the Nietzschean counter-response to his own argument — a dialectical move that Tom wants to track. The shorthand 'Nietz reply' suggests Tom is mapping the structure of the argument as a back-and-forth, anticipating that the Nietzschean response will be addressed and then overcome.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC must engage seriously with the strongest Nietzschean objections rather than dismissing them — Tom's annotation here signals his recognition that MacIntyre takes the genealogist's best reply seriously, which models the kind of dialectical integrity RC itself needs to practice.

annotations.content: research program of
annotations.anchor_text: _whether by Rembrandt research program of_
annotations.interpretation: This appears to be a partial highlight on the right-hand page, catching the phrase 'research program of' — likely connected to a discussion of whether Nietzsche's project constitutes a coherent research program. Tom may be flagging the use of Lakatosian language ('research program') in a MacIntyrean context, or marking the claim that genealogy fails as a research program by its own standards.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's interest in the institutional and programmatic dimensions of moral enquiry — whether tradition-based rationality can constitute a sustainable research program — is directly at stake in this terminology, which MacIntyre uses to evaluate competing traditions.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the genealogist's challenge is to combine the fixity of particular stances (embodied in standard genres like the academic treatise) with the mobility of transition between stances — assuming and discarding masks without ever assenting to the metaphysical fiction of a finally true representation. He identifies Michel Foucault as the most impressive post-Nietzschean attempt to carry through this research program systematically. He then complicates the picture by noting that Nietzsche himself was not as alien to encyclopaedist conventions as Foucault and Deleuze assumed, pointing to Karl Jaspers's identification of a 'positivist phase' in Nietzsche.

annotations.content: oddgph; things, understood in ways, by persons meaning of clusters(cnt'ys) perspectives: p-t = ontological p-w: perceptions commitments now ddu time — [illegible] code
annotations.anchor_text: _genre of the academic treatise is_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is constructing a schema: 'p-t' likely stands for 'practical-traditional' or 'perspective-tradition' mapped to ontological commitment, while 'p-w' stands for 'perspective-world' mapped to perceptions. 'ddu time' may abbreviate 'due to' or 'determined during time.' He seems to be abstracting MacIntyre's point about masks and stances into a general epistemological grid about how meanings cluster around persons, times, and perspectives — possibly to operationalize the genealogist/encyclopaedist distinction for RC.
annotations.rc_relevance: This schema-building connects directly to RC's interest in the tripartite structure (encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition) and how different moral frameworks embed different ontological commitments — a key question for whether Thomistic enquiry can engage contemporary fragmentation.

annotations.content: but only, so the reply runs, for must occur and endure for a time:
annotations.anchor_text: _so the reply runs, for must occur_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the temporal qualification MacIntyre places on the genealogist's apparent ontological presencing — it is 'only' momentary. This is the crucial hedge that distinguishes genealogical from metaphysical claims, and Tom is tracking exactly where MacIntyre draws that line.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is concerned with whether tradition-based moral enquiry can offer something more stable than the genealogist's deliberately provisional stances; flagging this 'only for a time' limit marks the boundary RC must cross.

annotations.content: Metaphysical theories are masks
annotations.anchor_text: _said Oscar Wilde, and Nietzsche would_
annotations.interpretation: Tom singles out this compressed aphorism — attributed via Wilde to Nietzsche — because it is the philosophical crux of the whole page: if theories are masks, then adopting and discarding them is a performative act, not a truth-seeking one. Underlining it signals that Tom sees this as the genealogist's core claim against the encyclopaedist's faith in rational representation.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'masks' claim is the genealogist's sharpest challenge to the Thomistic tradition's belief in rationally accessible moral truth; RC must have a direct response to this if it is to defend MacIntyre's third tradition.

annotations.content: The problem then for the genealogist is how to combine the fixiry of particular stances, exhibited in the use of standard genres of speech and writing, with the mobility of transition from stance to stance,
annotations.anchor_text: _how to combine the fixity of particular stances_
annotations.interpretation: This is the central structural problem MacIntyre poses for genealogy, and Tom highlights it in full. The tension between fixity and mobility is the internal contradiction the genealogist must manage — and MacIntyre is setting up Foucault as the best attempt to resolve it. Tom likely sees this as the diagnostic heart of the chapter.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with tradition vs. reform maps directly onto this fixity/mobility tension; a tradition must be stable enough to form character yet supple enough to respond to critique — the same problem, from the other side.

annotations.content: and then to discard
annotations.anchor_text: _assume the contours of a given mask_
annotations.interpretation: The specific act of discarding is highlighted separately from assuming the mask, suggesting Tom is noting the asymmetry: the genealogist can adopt any stance instrumentally but is committed to its eventual disposal. This is the move that most sharply differentiates genealogy from tradition, which cannot simply discard its inherited forms.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, the inability (or unwillingness) to 'discard' is precisely what makes a living tradition different from a genealogical performance — this annotation marks a key conceptual boundary.

annotations.content: without ever assenting to the metaphysical fiction
annotations.anchor_text: _for another, without ever assenting_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the non-assent clause because it is what keeps the genealogist from collapsing into the encyclopaedist. MacIntyre is describing a disciplined irony — using metaphysical-seeming language while privately withholding assent. Tom likely finds this philosophically unstable or disingenuous, which would be the Thomistic critique.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's Thomistic strand would argue that this perpetual non-assent is self-defeating for moral community — civic friendship and shared institutions require some form of genuine ontological commitment, not performative mask-wearing.

annotations.content: The research program of the post-Nietzschean enterprise,
annotations.anchor_text: _Can it be done? The research program_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's naming of the post-Nietzschean enterprise as a 'research program' — a deliberately encyclopaedist-sounding term applied to genealogy, which is an irony MacIntyre probably intends. Flagging this term suggests Tom is alert to MacIntyre's rhetorical strategy of using encyclopaedist vocabulary against itself.
annotations.rc_relevance: The question of whether genealogy constitutes a coherent 'research program' is directly relevant to RC's evaluation of whether post-Nietzschean critique can be a genuine interlocutor for Thomistic enquiry or merely a dissolution of enquiry altogether.

annotations.content: is to find out by try-
annotations.anchor_text: _endorsed by Nietzsche for his intellectual heirs_
annotations.interpretation: Highlighting the open-ended 'find out by trying' formulation marks Tom's attention to MacIntyre's empirical framing of the genealogical project — it is tested by whether it can be carried through, not by a priori argument. This is a significant methodological concession MacIntyre makes to the genealogist.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC must grapple with this pragmatic test: if Foucault's project is judged by whether it succeeds, then tradition-based enquiry must also be willing to be evaluated by its fruits — an important methodological self-challenge for the RC project.

annotations.content: Michel Foucault
annotations.anchor_text: _than that to which Michel Foucault devoted_
annotations.interpretation: Circling Foucault's name at the moment MacIntyre names him as the supreme post-Nietzschean is a key identification mark — Tom is flagging this as the node where the abstract argument becomes a concrete historical case. The circle distinguishes this from mere underlining, suggesting Foucault's name functions as an anchor for the whole genealogical tradition under discussion.
annotations.rc_relevance: Foucault is the primary genealogical interlocutor for RC's project; identifying where MacIntyre formally introduces him as the 'best attempt' is essential for structuring RC's own response to the genealogical challenge.

annotations.content: best post-N attempt so far: Foucault
annotations.anchor_text: _devoted so much of his life_
annotations.interpretation: Tom summarizes MacIntyre's evaluative claim in his own words, confirming he read this as MacIntyre's endorsement of Foucault's supremacy within the genealogical tradition. The phrase 'so far' is significant — it leaves open the question of whether a better post-Nietzschean attempt could exist, or whether it signals Tom's own reservation about MacIntyre's assessment.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, knowing that Foucault represents genealogy's strongest form sets the level of the philosophical challenge the project must meet; this margin note functions as a bookmark for the height of the bar.

annotations.content: the objection that Nietzsche's thought was not, and indeed could not have been, as radically alien to conventional modes as Foucault and Deleuze and I, following them, have taken it to be.
annotations.anchor_text: _requires a response to the objection_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets MacIntyre's self-correction — the admission that he himself, along with Foucault and Deleuze, over-radicalized Nietzsche. This is a moment of authorial candor that complicates the clean genealogist/encyclopaedist binary. Tom likely brackets it because it introduces a third, messier possibility: a Nietzsche who straddles both traditions.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's tripartite structure depends on relatively clean boundaries between the three versions of moral enquiry; a Nietzsche who partially inhabits the encyclopaedist scheme complicates the genealogist position and may actually open a path for tradition-based recovery of pre-genealogical Nietzschean insights.

annotations.content: it was for much of the time Foucault's own understanding of that project
annotations.anchor_text: _and it was for much of the time_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's concession that the continuity reading (Foucault as Nietzsche's heir) was Foucault's own self-understanding, not merely MacIntyre's external categorization. This matters because it means any critique of that continuity must grapple with Foucault's own self-interpretation, not just a scholar's taxonomy.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this means engaging Foucault's genealogical project requires meeting him on his own terms — a methodological point about how RC must conduct its interlocution with the genealogist tradition.

annotations.content: found a place within the encyclopaedists' scheme
annotations.anchor_text: _at times identified himself with positions which found a place_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this because it is the specific evidence for the complication MacIntyre introduced: Nietzsche was not purely genealogical. The encyclopaedists' scheme here likely refers to positivism, historicism, and natural science — modes Nietzsche flirted with. Tom is tracking where the tripartite structure gets blurry.
annotations.rc_relevance: This blurring is important for RC because if the genealogist position is internally unstable (partially reliant on encyclopaedist methods), it may be less capable of sustained critique of Thomistic tradition than it appears — a potential opening for RC's argument.

annotations.content: Nietzsche wrote history
annotations.anchor_text: _as his positivist phase. Nietzsche wrote history on_
annotations.interpretation: Highlighting 'Nietzsche wrote history' at the sentence break emphasizes the empirical, craft-bound dimension of Nietzsche's work — he was not only a philosopher of masks but a practicing historian. Tom may be noting that this grounds genealogy in something more substantial than pure performative irony.
annotations.rc_relevance: The fact that Nietzsche practiced history as a craft connects to RC's interest in how traditions are materially transmitted and studied — history-writing is itself a tradition-dependent practice, which MacIntyre will likely use against the genealogist.

annotations.content: Vertical bracket with arrow pointing down-right, alongside a checkmark or 'V' symbol
annotations.anchor_text: _understanding of that project requires a response_
annotations.interpretation: The bracket with downward arrow likely signals 'continued below' or 'this argument develops further on the next page,' while the checkmark indicates Tom endorses or confirms the point being made. Together they suggest Tom sees the self-corrective move MacIntyre is making here as important and worth following into the next page.
annotations.rc_relevance: The self-correction about over-radicalizing Nietzsche is structurally significant for RC because it opens space for a more nuanced genealogical interlocutor — one RC must engage rather than simply dismiss.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that attempts to 'coopt' Nietzsche by translating his thought into conventional academic idioms fundamentally misread him, because Nietzsche's project deliberately subordinates elucidatory treatise to poem and epigram precisely to escape the constraints of grammar, logic, and established values — constraints the encyclopaedist tradition treats as necessary features of any intelligible discourse. The objection that Nietzsche is incommensurable with rational enquiry fails only for those who refuse to equate intelligibility with translatability into one's own conceptual idiom. MacIntyre is building toward the claim that Nietzsche's final standpoint must be approached from within, not judged by external standards, which sets up the genealogist's challenge to both encyclopaedist and Thomistic traditions.

annotations.content: So it is possible by excision and reinterpretation and
annotations.anchor_text: _So it is possible by excision and_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the opening of MacIntyre's concession — that a domesticated, academically palatable Nietzsche can be constructed by selective reading. The pink highlight likely signals that Tom sees this as a pivotal methodological warning: the encyclopaedist impulse to 'save' Nietzsche by making him intelligible is itself a distortion. This may also flag the irony that the same move is tempting for any tradition trying to absorb a rival.
annotations.rc_relevance: This speaks directly to the RC theme of tradition vs. reform — specifically the danger of co-opting radical critiques rather than genuinely engaging them, which MacIntyre sees as the encyclopaedist's characteristic failure.

annotations.content: to construct an alternative account of Nietzsche's development in which Nietzsche did indeed attempt to break systematically with conventional modes of thought but failed to do so
annotations.anchor_text: _alternative account of Nietzsche's development_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's description of the coopting strategy — portraying Nietzsche as a failed systematic thinker. This is marked likely because Tom recognizes this as the encyclopaedist move par excellence: judging a rival tradition by whether it succeeds on your own terms. The underline tracks a specific argumentative claim Tom wants to return to.
annotations.rc_relevance: Connects to RC's MacIntyre-derived theme of incommensurability between traditions — the encyclopaedist cannot see Nietzsche's 'failure' as a chosen strategy, which is precisely the blindness MacIntyre's tripartite structure is designed to expose.

annotations.content: Asterisk (*) drawn in the left margin beside the passage about incommensurability
annotations.anchor_text: _which I and others have ascribed to Nietzsche, cannot occur_
annotations.interpretation: The asterisk is Tom's strongest emphasis marker — he uses it to flag a passage he considers a crux of the argument. Here it marks MacIntyre's self-referential claim that the incommensurability he and others attribute to Nietzsche is itself being dismissed by those who insist intelligibility equals translatability. Tom likely sees this as the hinge of the entire chapter: the performative contradiction in the encyclopaedist objection.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the core RC tension between civic rationalism (the encyclopaedist assumption that all discourse must be mutually translatable) and tradition-constituted rationality — the asterisk suggests Tom treats this as a load-bearing passage for the RC argument about incommensurable moral vocabularies.

annotations.content: vertical bracket in left margin spanning the paragraph beginning 'It is indeed from the standpoint of such a we'
annotations.anchor_text: _It is indeed from the standpoint of such a 'we'_
annotations.interpretation: The tall vertical bracket with what appears to be a tick or notch marks the entire paragraph as a structural unit worth preserving whole. Tom is likely noting that MacIntyre here names the epistemic community presupposed by the objection — the 'we' who equate intelligibility with translatability. Bracketing the whole paragraph suggests Tom wants to cite or return to it as a complete argument-unit, not just a phrase.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'we' of the encyclopaedist tradition is directly relevant to RC's concern with civic friendship and the question of who counts as a legitimate participant in moral discourse — MacIntyre's point is that this 'we' is not neutral but tradition-specific.

annotations.content: The objection fails of course for everyone unwilling to equate intelligibility with translatability into one's own initial language
annotations.anchor_text: _The objection fails of course for everyone unwilling_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines what is effectively MacIntyre's thesis sentence for this section. The highlighting in pink combined with underlining suggests double emphasis — Tom treats this as a quotable formulation. He may be noting it as the clearest statement of why genealogical thought cannot be dismissed on encyclopaedist grounds, and possibly planning to use it as an epigraph or citation in RC work.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's challenge to Enlightenment universalism — the claim that 'our' conceptual idiom is not universal but parochial is foundational to MacIntyre's critique and to RC's recovery of tradition-based moral enquiry.

annotations.content: what its attempt to coopt Nietzsche cannot reckon with is his subordination of the elucidatory academic treatise to the poem and the epigram
annotations.anchor_text: _subordination of the elucidatory academic treatise to the poem_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this because it identifies the formal-literary dimension of Nietzsche's genealogical project — the choice of genre is itself philosophical resistance. This is not merely a stylistic observation; MacIntyre is arguing that the poem/epigram form is designed to make the encyclopaedist coopting move impossible. Tom may be connecting this to questions about the form RC itself should take.
annotations.rc_relevance: Touches on RC's concern with institutions forming character — the question of whether moral enquiry requires a certain kind of discourse community, and whether academic treatise form is adequate to the task of recovering civility.

annotations.content: Small letter 'c' or check mark in left margin near bottom of page
annotations.anchor_text: _to dispense with elucidatory treatises altogether in favor_
annotations.interpretation: This small notation is ambiguous — it may be a 'c' for 'cite' or a check indicating agreement or completion of reading. Given its position near the passage about dispensing with elucidatory treatises in favor of mockery and celebration, Tom may be flagging this as a moment where MacIntyre's sympathy for Nietzsche's formal radicalism becomes most visible — and potentially most challenging to a Thomistic recovery project.
annotations.rc_relevance: The tension between Nietzsche's anti-treatise stance and the Thomistic tradition's investment in systematic moral enquiry is central to RC — Tom may be noting where the genealogist challenge is most acute for the RC project's own methodology.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that Nietzsche's writings cannot be adjudicated by neutral data, since competing interpretations rest on incommensurable standpoints — the disagreement is structural, not resolvable by appeal to the texts themselves. He then pivots to Foucault, whose trajectory from professor to genealogist intensified the problem of academic presentation as a mask, which Foucault addressed directly in his Nietzsche essay for the Hyppolite Festschrift. The page closes by introducing history's 'three uses,' all of which sever history from memorial veneration by generating a disruptive countermemory.

annotations.content: Nasanti-#tx31:7
annotations.anchor_text: _Genealogies and Subversions page 49 heading_
annotations.interpretation: This appears to be a bibliographic or cross-reference code Tom has written, possibly a personal filing system linking this passage to another text or note ('Nasanti' may be a shorthand for a proper name, title, or concept cluster). The alphanumeric suffix '#tx31:7' suggests Tom maintains a numbered archive of texts or excerpts. He is flagging this page as importantly connected to something already catalogued elsewhere in his research.
annotations.rc_relevance: The cross-referencing habit itself reflects the RC project's comparative methodology — Tom is weaving MacIntyre's argument about incommensurable standpoints into a broader web of sources, likely bearing on the encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition tripartite structure.

annotations.content: Drawn rectangle/box with diagonal lines in corner, resembling a flagged tab or pointer arrow
annotations.anchor_text: _what is communicated is the movement_
annotations.interpretation: This box-with-arrow symbol appears to function as a high-priority structural marker — Tom uses it to signal that the claim being made here is architecturally important to the chapter's argument, not merely a supporting detail. The diagonal slash within the box may indicate 'contradiction' or 'tension.' He seems to be flagging MacIntyre's point that Nietzsche's project operates at the level of communicative movement rather than propositional content.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects directly to the RC concern with tradition vs. reform: if what is transmitted in a tradition is a movement of utterances rather than a fixed theory, then reforming or recovering civility requires attending to the living transmission of practices, not just doctrines.

annotations.content: like k
annotations.anchor_text: _proposed an abandonment of theory_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes 'like k' twice in the right margin at slightly different vertical positions, each keyed to a separate passage. 'k' almost certainly abbreviates Kierkegaard, whose indirect communication and anti-systematic method parallels Nietzsche's abandonment of theory in favor of a mode of utterance that must be enacted rather than argued. Tom is noting a structural analogy MacIntyre does not make explicit.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this Kierkegaard parallel is significant: both thinkers represent the limits of rationalist moral discourse, which MacIntyre's encyclopaedist version embodies. Tom may be building a case that the genealogist tradition forces a rethinking of how moral formation — central to civility — is transmitted.

annotations.content: All that I am claiming is that it must be understood and judged initially in its own terms and that in these terms it is not evidently and at once self-defeating. Whether it can in fact be carried through successfully is a question posed by the history of Foucault's thought.
annotations.anchor_text: _not at this point claiming that the Nietzschean project_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this as MacIntyre's carefully hedged methodological claim — he is not endorsing the genealogist project but granting it internal coherence as a precondition for serious engagement. The highlighting signals Tom recognizes this as a move of intellectual charity that MacIntyre's own tradition-based enquiry requires. The deferral to 'the history of Foucault's thought' is also structurally important: it means the genealogist project is self-testing through its own historical unfolding.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's engagement with MacIntyre's tripartite structure: the tradition-based enquirer must understand rival versions on their own terms before claiming superiority. Tom may be noting this as a model for how RC should engage genealogical critics of civility rather than dismissing them.

annotations.content: the question of who the historian is and how he or she must be transformed by engagement with history if the work of history is to be informed by genuinely Nietzschean insights.
annotations.anchor_text: _not so much the question of what history is_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the shift from 'what history is' to 'who the historian is' — this is Foucault's Nietzschean move of relocating the problem from epistemology to the formation of the subject. Tom is tracking this as a key conceptual hinge: genealogy is not primarily a theory of history but a practice of self-transformation. The emphasis on 'transformed' is significant for understanding why genealogy is incommensurable with encyclopaedist enquiry.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with institutions forming character maps directly onto this question: if the historian must be transformed by engagement, then the institution (university, church, polis) is the site where such transformation occurs or fails. Tom is likely connecting Foucault's point to MacIntyre's argument about the university as a place of constrained disagreement.

annotations.content: History thus understood turns out to have three uses, all of which sever the connection of history with memory and with memorial veneration of the past by creating a disruptive countermemory.
annotations.anchor_text: _three uses all of which sever_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the culminating claim about history's three uses and their shared function of creating countermemory. This is a structurally pivotal passage because it names the genealogist's fundamental operation: not preservation but rupture. The double underline effect of green highlighting beneath suggests Tom considers 'disruptive countermemory' a key term of art he will need to return to.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, 'disruptive countermemory' is the genealogist's direct assault on the tradition-based project — civility, subsidiarity, and Thomistic enquiry all depend on living memory and veneration of inherited practices. Tom is marking the sharpest point of conflict between the genealogist version and the tradition version that RC seeks to recover.

annotations.content: Foucault on history
annotations.anchor_text: _Nietzsche's progress was from professor to genealogist_
annotations.interpretation: Tom labels this section with a simple organizational tag identifying the subject shift from Nietzsche to Foucault's philosophy of history. While apparently simple, this note suggests Tom is structuring his reading notes thematically and may be building a dossier on Foucault's relationship to history as distinct from Nietzsche's — a distinction MacIntyre is himself careful to maintain.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's engagement with the genealogist rival version requires distinguishing Nietzsche's and Foucault's versions of genealogy; Tom's note signals awareness that Foucault's institutional position (professor-genealogist simultaneously) creates a different set of tensions than Nietzsche faced, relevant to RC's concern with institutional formation.

annotations.content: 3 uses of history
annotations.anchor_text: _three uses all of which sever the connection_
annotations.interpretation: Tom flags the '3 uses' as a numbered structural element he will need to track across subsequent pages. The bracket drawn alongside this section in green confirms he sees this as a list-in-progress whose full content will be elaborated further in the chapter. He is preparing to follow the argument forward.
annotations.rc_relevance: The three uses of history in Foucault's Nietzschean framework are directly oppositional to the three rival versions MacIntyre presents — encyclopaedia, genealogy, tradition. Tom is likely mapping the structural parallels and contrasts for RC's argument about which model of historical memory supports genuine moral community.

annotations.content: countermemory
annotations.anchor_text: _disruptive countermemory_
annotations.interpretation: Tom isolates 'countermemory' as a standalone term in the margin, treating it as a concept requiring its own tracking. This is not mere summarizing — he is extracting a technical term for use elsewhere in his research. The placement suggests he may intend to place it in dialogue with other theorists of memory (Ricoeur, perhaps, or Weil) who figure in the RC project.
annotations.rc_relevance: Countermemory is the genealogist's weapon against tradition-constituted enquiry, and its appearance here marks the deepest challenge RC must answer: can a recovered tradition of civility survive genealogical deconstruction, or must it be reconstructed on different grounds than memorial veneration?

pages.summary: MacIntyre, drawing on Foucault's Nietzschean genealogical method, outlines three uses of history: (1) to expose official academic history as a parody that destabilizes fixed identities, (2) to comprehend persons as complex cultural composites rather than stable selves, and (3) to reveal in the historian a 'rancorous will to know' that demands destructive sacrifice rather than rational progress. MacIntyre then identifies a self-endangering paradox in Foucault's project — the genealogical insights that subvert official history equally threaten to subvert the genealogical project itself. He uses Les Mots et les choses as a case study, arguing that reading it merely as a Bachelardian account of discontinuities in the immature sciences (analogous to Kuhn on mature sciences) misses two key aspects Foucault himself foregrounds, including his interest in immature sciences as zones of obscurity between cultural codes and mature scientific order.

annotations.content: F: 3 uses of H
annotations.anchor_text: _The first of these uses is to understand_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is labeling the structural tripartite taxonomy MacIntyre attributes to Foucault's uses of history. The 'F' almost certainly stands for Foucault, and '3 uses of H' abbreviates 'three uses of history.' This is a scaffolding note — Tom is mapping the argumentative skeleton of the page so he can return to it quickly. The fact that he marks this at the top suggests he sees it as the organizing claim of the entire passage, not merely one paragraph.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project's core 'tripartite structure' (encyclopaedist / genealogist / tradition) maps directly onto MacIntyre's method of identifying rival versions through their internal logics. Tom is tracking how Foucault's three uses of history constitute the genealogist's distinctive relationship to the past — a relationship RC must reckon with when arguing for the recovery of tradition.

annotations.content: ①
annotations.anchor_text: _The first of these uses is to understand official_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has circled or drawn a numbered bubble '1' in the margin to mark the first of Foucault's three genealogical uses of history. This is structural annotation — he is visually segmenting the argument so the three uses can be located at a glance. The circled numeral indicates Tom is treating this as a list he intends to track through the paragraph, likely for teaching, citation, or comparative purposes.
annotations.rc_relevance: The first use — exposing academic history as parody that destabilizes 'significant figures of the past as solid' — is directly antagonistic to the RC project's recovery of tradition. Tom is marking the point at which the genealogist most aggressively challenges the traditionalist's appeal to authoritative historical figures.

annotations.content: ②
annotations.anchor_text: _Secondly, we go on to comprehend them and ourselves_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the second use of history — comprehending persons not as stable identities but as 'complex patterns of elements representative of their various differing cultures.' The circled '2' continues the structural tracking begun with '①'. Tom may be noting that this use is particularly relevant to debates about the self — whether persons are constituted by tradition and practice (MacIntyre/Aquinas) or are radically decentered composites (Foucault/Nietzsche).
annotations.rc_relevance: This second use strikes at the heart of RC's concern with character formation and civic friendship, both of which presuppose a sufficiently stable self capable of virtue. Tom is likely flagging this as the anthropological crux where genealogist and traditionalist versions of moral enquiry most sharply diverge.

annotations.content: ③
annotations.anchor_text: _The third of these uses of history is to reveal_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the third use — revealing in the historian 'a rancorous will to know' that does not recognize limitation and demands destructive sacrifice. The circled '3' completes the triadic structure. The third use is the most epistemologically radical and self-implicating, as it attributes a will-to-power motivation to the historian herself, including the genealogist. Tom may be flagging this because MacIntyre is about to exploit this self-referential problem.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'rancorous will to know' that 'demands destructive sacrifices' is precisely what MacIntyre will contrast with the Thomistic tradition's understanding of enquiry as ordered toward truth and the common good. Tom is marking the point where the genealogist's ethos is most starkly incompatible with RC's vision of civic and epistemic virtue.

annotations.content: Asterisk (*) in green ink
annotations.anchor_text: _There is of course for Foucault a self-endangering paradox_
annotations.interpretation: Tom places a green asterisk beside the paragraph introducing Foucault's 'self-endangering paradox' — the insight that genealogical uses of history are themselves liable to subvert the genealogical project. The asterisk signals this as a high-priority passage, likely one Tom regards as MacIntyre's most powerful critical move against the genealogist. Green ink (distinct from the highlighting colors) suggests this is a later or deliberate second-pass annotation, marking something he wants to return to or cite.
annotations.rc_relevance: The self-referential paradox of genealogy is central to MacIntyre's case for why the genealogist version of moral enquiry is ultimately self-defeating. For RC, this is the key argumentative resource for showing that Foucauldian critique cannot ground a positive account of moral community — it undermines itself before it can undermine tradition.

annotations.anchor_text: _His interest in the immature sciences, he makes it clear_
annotations.interpretation: This cryptic notation — '7(A)' — in the lower left margin likely functions as a cross-reference: '7' may refer to a chapter, section, footnote, or another page in MacIntyre or a secondary source, while '(A)' could indicate a sub-point or a specific author (possibly Aquinas, or Aristotle, or perhaps Ian Hacking who is mentioned nearby). Alternatively, it could be Tom's own outline notation for a section of his RC notes. The ambiguity is significant — it suggests Tom is actively weaving this passage into a larger organizational schema outside the book itself.
annotations.rc_relevance: If '(A)' references Aquinas or Aristotle, Tom is drawing a contrast between the Thomistic account of enquiry into 'immature' domains and Foucault's genealogical account — a contrast central to MacIntyre's 'tradition' version of moral enquiry and to RC's recovery of Thomistic frameworks for understanding knowledge and virtue.

annotations.content: parody of the past, so that instead of taking the identities of the significant figures of the past as solid
annotations.anchor_text: _parody of the past so that instead of taking_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the phrase 'parody of the past' and its explanatory extension about refusing to treat historical figures as having solid identities. This is the genealogist's first move: deflationary and destabilizing. By highlighting this specific phrase, Tom is flagging the genealogist's hermeneutic of suspicion as applied to historical exemplars — exactly the move that makes tradition-based moral enquiry seem naive.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery of classical civility depends on treating figures from the tradition (Cicero, Aquinas, Aristotle) as genuine moral authorities, not as parodies or ideological projections. Tom is marking the precise point where the genealogist would challenge the very foundation of RC's historical methodology.

annotations.content: we go on to comprehend them and ourselves, not as persons, as identities, but as complex patterns of elements representative of their various differing cultures
annotations.anchor_text: _comprehend them and ourselves not as persons_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the core anthropological claim of the genealogist's second use of history — the replacement of 'persons' and 'identities' with 'complex patterns of elements.' This is a highlighted passage because it states explicitly what Foucault means by the 'death of the subject.' Tom may be flagging this both to understand genealogy on its own terms and to prepare a counter-argument grounded in MacIntyre's or Aquinas's account of personal identity and moral agency.
annotations.rc_relevance: The dissolution of personal identity into cultural patterns directly undermines the RC theme of character formation and civic friendship, both of which require agents capable of sustained commitment, virtue, and rational deliberation. This is the anthropological premise Tom needs to contest for RC's project to succeed.

annotations.content: reveal in the consciousness of the historian a rancorous will to know
annotations.anchor_text: _reveal in the consciousness of the historian_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights 'rancorous will to know' — Nietzsche's concept (via Foucault) of the drive behind genealogical enquiry. This phrase is philosophically loaded: it attributes an affective, agonistic, and ultimately irrational motivation to what presents itself as historical scholarship. Tom is likely marking this as the moment MacIntyre most clearly exposes the ethos of genealogy, which will contrast with the Thomistic account of enquiry as ordered by love of truth.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'rancorous will to know' is the inverse of the RC ideal of civic friendship and the common pursuit of truth. MacIntyre's tradition version of enquiry requires that enquirers be formed by virtues — including intellectual humility — that are structurally excluded by the genealogist's rancorous drive. Tom is marking the affective and motivational incompatibility between the two traditions.

annotations.content: a will which does not make fact issue in any progress towards truth and reason, but which, because it does not recognize limitation, demands destructive and self-destructive sacrifices
annotations.anchor_text: _will which does not make fact issue in any progress_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's characterization of genealogical enquiry as antithetical to rational progress — a will that 'demands destructive and self-destructive sacrifices.' This is a key polemical moment: MacIntyre is attributing not just methodological difference but a kind of moral pathology to the genealogist's stance. Tom underlines or highlights this to mark where MacIntyre's critique becomes explicitly normative.
annotations.rc_relevance: This passage directly supports RC's argument that the genealogist's framework cannot sustain the civic and moral goods RC seeks to recover. The refusal to recognize limitation is the antithesis of the subsidiary and virtue-ordered account of community that RC defends. Tom is building his case that genealogy is self-destructive as a form of moral enquiry.

annotations.content: the insights conferred by this post-Nietzschean understanding of the uses of history are themselves liable to subvert the project of understanding the project
annotations.anchor_text: _insights conferred by this post-Nietzschean understanding_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the precise statement of Foucault's self-endangering paradox: the genealogical method undermines itself. This is the argumentative pivot of the page — MacIntyre's strongest philosophical objection to genealogy as a form of enquiry. By highlighting this, Tom signals he sees it as the crux of MacIntyre's critique and likely the passage he will cite most in RC work.
annotations.rc_relevance: The self-subverting character of genealogy is MacIntyre's key argumentative resource for showing that the encyclopaedist and tradition versions of enquiry cannot be dismissed by genealogy without genealogy sawing off the branch it sits on. For RC, this is the decisive moment in the case for recovering tradition-based moral enquiry.

annotations.content: doing for immature sciences, and notably for the human sciences, something akin to what Thomas Kuhn achieved for our understanding of the mature physical and chemical sciences
annotations.anchor_text: _doing for immature sciences something akin to what Thomas Kuhn_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the Kuhn analogy — the reading of Les Mots et les choses as doing for the human sciences what Kuhn did for natural sciences. MacIntyre is about to argue this reading is reductive and misses Foucault's deeper project. Tom flags this likely because the Kuhn reference is an important intellectual-historical landmark, and because the rejection of this reductive reading is key to MacIntyre's interpretation of what genealogy is really doing.
annotations.rc_relevance: The Kuhn analogy is relevant to RC's engagement with how paradigm shifts in moral enquiry occur — MacIntyre's own work is partly about the Kuhnian dynamics of tradition-constituted rationality. Tom is tracking how MacIntyre differentiates his account of tradition from both the encyclopaedist (Kuhn as progressive science) and the genealogist (Foucault as radical discontinuity).

annotations.content: they tend to exemplify a middle area, always of some obscurity and confusion, between the fixed codes of a culture, on the one hand, and the well-ordered enterprises of the mature sciences on the other
annotations.anchor_text: _exemplify a middle area always of some obscurity_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights Foucault's (via MacIntyre) account of why immature sciences matter: they occupy the obscure middle zone between cultural codes and mature scientific order — the zone where underlying epistemic structures can be identified. This is a key passage for understanding what Foucault's archaeology is actually doing. Tom may be flagging it because it parallels MacIntyre's own interest in traditions that are in crisis or transition.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'middle area' of obscurity between cultural codes and ordered knowledge resonates with RC's concern about contemporary social fragmentation — the breakdown of shared moral codes without the emergence of a new ordered framework. Tom may be reading Foucault's description of epistemic liminal zones as a diagnosis of the very condition RC seeks to address.

pages.summary: MacIntyre analyzes Foucault's Les Mots et les choses as a self-subverting text that enacts what it describes: it is organized as an ordered scheme of classification and representation while arguing against the possibility of such schemes. He then turns to Foucault's argument in 'Qu'est-ce un auteur?' that 'the author' names a role or function rather than a person, meaning the author's name assigns status to discourse rather than grounding it in personal intention — thereby ruling out any appeal to authorized intention behind the text. This move makes the text-as-authored a self-presenting unit, implicating Foucault in the liquidation of the conventional conception of authorship.
