annotations.content: sets of coherences,
annotations.anchor_text: _to the modes of being of order, an insight into_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has highlighted this phrase in pink, isolating it as a key conceptual unit. 'Sets of coherences' is MacIntyre's characterization of what epistemic orders provide — not unified Truth but internally consistent frameworks. Tom is likely tracking this as the genealogist's alternative to tradition-grounded rationality: coherence without foundation.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects directly to RC's engagement with MacIntyre's tripartite structure: the genealogist identifies 'sets of coherences' where the tradition-bearer sees unified moral enquiry. Tom may be flagging the genealogist's epistemological move as both a challenge and a foil for the RC recovery project.

annotations.content: resemblances, and correspondences,
annotations.anchor_text: _capable of being organized in alternative, incompatible_
annotations.interpretation: Highlighted in pink alongside 'sets of coherences,' Tom is tracking MacIntyre's vocabulary for the Classical episteme's organizing logic. The pairing suggests Tom is building a list of the epistemic terms MacIntyre borrows from Foucault to describe rival classificatory schemes.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this vocabulary of 'resemblances and correspondences' echoes pre-modern analogical reasoning — the kind of thinking MacIntyre associates with Thomistic moral enquiry. Tom may be noting the irony that genealogy recovers pre-modern epistemic categories only to relativize them.

annotations.content: cault distinguished three successive styles of thought, each with its own standards in respect of classification and the use of signs, divided historically by moments of rupture, one in which the Renaissance period is displaced by the Classical at the beginning of the seventeenth century, and another in which the Classical in turn loses its place at the end of the eighteenth
annotations.anchor_text: _cault distinguished three successive styles of thought_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has bracketed this passage with green, marking Foucault's three-epoch schema (Renaissance / Classical / Modern). This is the structural heart of Les Mots et les choses, and Tom is isolating it as a discrete analytical unit. The bracket suggests he wants to hold this tripartite historical structure in view — likely to compare it with MacIntyre's own tripartite structure of encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's engagement with MacIntyre's rival versions framework. Foucault's three epistemes mirror MacIntyre's three rivals structurally, and Tom is likely noting this parallel as evidence of how deeply MacIntyre's argument is shaped by — and in contest with — genealogical historiography.

annotations.content: Small curved mark resembling a circle or check near the left margin beside the bracketed passage
annotations.anchor_text: _cault distinguished three successive styles of thought_
annotations.interpretation: This symbol appears to be Tom's way of flagging special importance beyond the bracket itself — possibly a 'return to this' marker. It may signal that this passage requires cross-referencing with MacIntyre's own tripartite structure introduced earlier in the book.
annotations.rc_relevance: The tripartite structure of moral enquiry is central to RC's theoretical scaffolding; Tom appears to be building a map of structural parallels between Foucault and MacIntyre that RC must navigate.

annotations.content: But this book about incommensurable ordered schemes of classification and representation, is itself organized as an ordered scheme of classification and representation,
annotations.anchor_text: _But this book about incommensurable ordered schemes_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has underlined this in green — this is the self-referential paradox at the heart of MacIntyre's critique of Foucault. The genealogist's text performs what it critiques. Tom is marking this as a decisive logical point: Foucault cannot escape the epistemic conditions he is analyzing. This is MacIntyre's strongest weapon against genealogy.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery of tradition-based moral enquiry depends on exposing the self-undermining character of genealogical critique. This underline shows Tom tracking the exact moment MacIntyre demonstrates that genealogy cannot achieve the radical exteriority it claims — a move essential to RC's apologetic against postmodern fragmentation.

annotations.content: which have the implication in the recurrent methodological remarks, which have the surface qualities—from the standpoint of genealogy—of all such methodological remarks.
annotations.anchor_text: _a scheme not identified or justified within its own pages_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's point that Foucault's methodological remarks have the 'surface qualities' of standard academic methodology while actually functioning genealogically. This is a subtle argument about how genealogy disguises its own standards. Tom is flagging this as MacIntyre catching Foucault in an act of methodological bad faith or at least self-concealment.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's concern with institutional formation and intellectual honesty: MacIntyre is arguing that the genealogist's methodology is itself a kind of tradition-dependent practice that refuses to acknowledge its own conditions — precisely the move RC's recovery of Thomistic enquiry seeks to avoid.

annotations.content: it is the text which does this, not the author—requiring us to supplement and perhaps correct, how radically we cannot know in advance, our first-order understanding of Les Mots et les choses by a second-order understanding of what underlies and orders the ordering of Les Mots et les choses.
annotations.anchor_text: _it is the text which does this, not the author_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this crucial methodological claim with green. MacIntyre is saying that the self-subverting quality is structural, not intentional — it is in the text, not in Foucault's mind. This opens the move to Foucault's own 'author function' argument. Tom is tracking how MacIntyre uses Foucault's anti-intentionalism against him: if the author is not the source of meaning, then the text's self-contradiction is all the more damaging.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's concern with the relationship between author, tradition, and text. MacIntyre is implicitly defending a view of authorship and intentionality against Foucault's dissolution — a move relevant to RC's recovery of texts from classical and Thomistic traditions as bearers of authoritative moral teaching.

annotations.content: it is the text which does this, not the author—requiring us to supplement and perhaps correct, how radically we cannot know in advance, our first-order understanding of Les Mots et les choses by a second-order understanding
annotations.anchor_text: _it is the text which does this, not the author_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has added a right-margin bracket in green alongside this passage, reinforcing the underline. The bracket signals that this is not just an important phrase but a structurally significant unit of argument — possibly the hinge between MacIntyre's internal critique of Foucault and the turn to the author-function discussion. The double-marking (underline + bracket) suggests Tom sees this as pivotal.
annotations.rc_relevance: The distinction between first-order and second-order understanding here mirrors MacIntyre's broader epistemological framework of tradition-constituted rationality, where adequacy requires a higher-order standpoint. RC's project of recovering civility similarly requires this kind of reflexive, second-order engagement with inherited texts and practices.

annotations.content: Foucault's threefold Nietzschean indictment of history has become self-referential.
annotations.anchor_text: _Foucault's threefold Nietzschean indictment of history_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this sentence in green — it is MacIntyre's summary verdict on Foucault: the genealogical critique of history collapses back on itself. The word 'threefold' is notable; Tom may be cross-referencing this with Foucault's own tripartite genealogical method (parodic/dissociative/sacrificial history from 'Nietzsche, Genealogy, History'). MacIntyre is saying all three modes of genealogical critique are self-defeating.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is a cornerstone of RC's theoretical position: the genealogist cannot escape tradition-constituted rationality and ends up self-refuting. Tom is marking the moment MacIntyre clinches this argument, which RC must appropriate in its defense of tradition-grounded civic and moral reasoning.

annotations.content: 'the author' for him names a role or function, not a person,
annotations.anchor_text: _1969 'the author' for him names a role_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this in pink — Foucault's redefinition of authorship as functional rather than personal. This is a key genealogical move: the author is a discursive function, not a subject. Tom is marking this as the theoretical foundation for Foucault's anti-intentionalism, which MacIntyre will then turn against Foucault. The pink highlight (distinct from green underlines) may signal a definition or term to track across the text.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery of tradition depends on the meaningfulness of authorial intention and the continuity of moral teaching across texts and persons. Foucault's author-function directly threatens this, and Tom is flagging the exact formulation RC must engage with when defending the coherence of a living tradition.

annotations.content: Hence any possibility of moving beyond and behind the text to authorized intention is already ruled out by authorized intention.
annotations.anchor_text: _Hence any possibility of moving beyond and behind the text_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this in green — this is MacIntyre's formulation of Foucault's self-sealing argument: 'authorized intention' forecloses appeal to authorized intention. The apparent paradox is deliberate: MacIntyre is showing that Foucault's own concept of the author-function generates a closed circle that traps the genealogist. Tom is marking this as a logical structure to analyze, not merely a quotable line.
annotations.rc_relevance: This has direct relevance to RC's engagement with hermeneutics and tradition. If authorized intention is self-sealing in Foucault's way, then the recovery of classical texts for contemporary moral purposes is impossible — a conclusion RC must resist. Tom is marking the exact point where the stakes become highest for the RC project.

annotations.content: Hence any possibility of moving beyond and behind the text to authorized intention is already ruled out by authorized intention.
annotations.anchor_text: _Hence any possibility of moving beyond_
annotations.interpretation: This passage receives both green underline and what appears to be pink/yellow highlight — double-marked, suggesting it is among the most important on the page. Tom is flagging this not just as a key argument but as something requiring active response. The double-marking may indicate this is a passage Tom intends to quote, contest, or develop in his own RC writing.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project's entire hermeneutical strategy — reading classical and Thomistic texts as live resources for contemporary moral community — is directly challenged by this claim. Tom's double-marking suggests he recognizes this and is preparing a substantive response.

annotations.content: Text-and-author, text-as-authored, this is the self-presenting unit.
annotations.anchor_text: _Text-and-author, text-as-authored, this is the self-presenting unit_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this compressed formulation in green. MacIntyre is here crystallizing the Foucauldian position: the unit of analysis is not author or text separately but text-as-authored — a compound that excludes appeal to anything outside itself. Tom is marking this as MacIntyre's sharpest encapsulation of the genealogist's position on textual authority.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, which depends on recovering authoritative teaching from canonical texts (Aristotle, Aquinas, etc.), this formulation names the central hermeneutical challenge: can the text-as-authored still speak to a living tradition, or is it sealed in its own discursive moment? Tom is tracking where MacIntyre locates the problem before offering his solution.

annotations.content: Arrow or tick mark in right margin beside 'Hence any possibility of moving beyond' passage
annotations.anchor_text: _is already ruled out by authorized intention_
annotations.interpretation: This right-margin mark appears to be an arrow or emphasis tick directing attention to the 'authorized intention' passage. Combined with the underline and highlight, it suggests Tom is treating this as a passage of exceptional structural importance — possibly the climax of MacIntyre's internal critique of genealogy on this page.
annotations.rc_relevance: The concentration of marks here confirms that the question of textual authority and intention is Tom's primary RC-related concern on this page — the hinge between genealogical critique and the possibility of tradition-recovery.

pages.summary: MacIntyre distinguishes Foucault's genealogical project from structuralism: where structuralists sought fundamental elements underlying all discourse, Foucault consistently denied any such foundations, instead tracing how 'epistemes' — the discursive practices unifying bodies of claims at any given time — are themselves products of power-laden assemblages. Sciences, far from being privileged access to rationality, are secondary phenomena whose apparent rationality is the surface expression of aggressive and defensive strategies; truth and power are thus inseparable. This page establishes the genealogist's core methodological claim that ruptures and incommensurable transitions between epistemes (as identified by Bachelard and Kuhn) reveal distributions of power rather than progress toward truth.

annotations.anchor_text: _Structuralisms have been of different and_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is labeling this passage as articulating the 'structuralist alternative' — likely to contrast it with the genealogist position being developed. The abbreviation suggests he is tracking the tripartite structure of the book (encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition) and wants a quick visual flag for where the structuralist position is defined and then distinguished from Foucault's genealogy. The placement right at the paragraph on structuralisms confirms he is organizing the argument architecturally.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to the encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition tripartite structure central to RC's use of MacIntyre — Tom needs to keep the genealogist position distinct from structuralism to correctly deploy the three-rival-versions framework when diagnosing contemporary fragmentation.

annotations.content: appeal to some set of elements, structured in this or that way, as fundamental to explanation and understanding
annotations.anchor_text: _appeal to some set of elements structured in_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets the core structuralist commitment — the appeal to underlying fundamental elements — precisely because this is what Foucault rejects. The bracket isolates the defining claim of structuralism so it can be held against Foucault's denial of any such foundation. Tom is likely noting this as the crux of the disagreement, since 'foundation' is the pivot on which the genealogist/encyclopaedist contrast turns.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project's engagement with MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment foundationalism requires this distinction — the encyclopaedist assumes such foundations; the genealogist exposes them as fictions. Tom needs this passage to articulate what the tradition-based alternative is rejecting in both.

annotations.content: a movement towards the preconceptual, the presystematic, and the prediscursive which itself necessarily cannot but be comprehended in terms that are conceptual, systematic, and discursive
annotations.anchor_text: _movement towards the preconceptual the presystematic_
annotations.interpretation: This bracket flags a performative paradox MacIntyre identifies: structuralism reaches for a pre-discursive ground but can only articulate it discursively — a self-undermining move. Tom is likely flagging this as a philosophical vulnerability of structuralism that Foucault also inherits in a different form. The bracket may signal a point Tom wants to use as a critical lever against foundationalist projects generally.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project's Thomistic strand (via MacIntyre) holds that moral enquiry must be conducted within a tradition's own terms — this passage illustrates the incoherence of trying to ground enquiry in something prior to all tradition, which is a key RC argument against Enlightenment universalism.

annotations.content: To the set of relations which in any given time and place unify the discursive practices underlying any one such body of claims Foucault gave the name 'episteme,' mocking Plato's and Aristotle's uses of that word in so doing.
annotations.anchor_text: _Foucault gave the name episteme mocking Plato's_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the definition of 'episteme' and the pointed note that Foucault is deliberately mocking the classical use of the term. The dual highlighting (pink over the relational definition, yellow/orange over 'episteme') suggests Tom is tracking both the technical definition and the polemical gesture. The mockery of Plato and Aristotle is significant for RC — it marks exactly where the genealogist stands in hostile relation to the tradition MacIntyre wants to recover.
annotations.rc_relevance: Central to RC's engagement with MacIntyre: Foucault's appropriation and subversion of 'episteme' is a direct attack on the Thomistic-Aristotelian tradition Tom's project seeks to resurrect. This annotation likely marks the sharpest point of conflict between the genealogist and tradition-based moral enquiry.

annotations.content: encounters interactions contys(?) formation & practices, and thus 'bodies of knowledge,' claims
annotations.anchor_text: _incommensurable bodies of claims assigned in their assemblage_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is paraphrasing or glossing the episteme concept in his own sociological vocabulary — 'encounters,' 'interactions,' 'formation,' 'practices.' The word 'contys' may be an abbreviation for 'continuities' or 'contexts.' This suggests Tom is translating MacIntyre/Foucault's language into terms usable for the RC project, particularly around how institutions and practices form 'bodies of knowledge.' He is asking: what are the social mechanisms through which epistemes are constituted?
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's theme of institutions forming character — Tom appears to be mapping the Foucauldian account of discursive formation onto a more sociological model of practice-based knowledge, which connects to MacIntyre's own account of practices and institutions in 'After Virtue.'

annotations.content: ruptures in that history, as identified by Bachelard or Kuhn
annotations.anchor_text: _ruptures in that history as identified by Bachelard_
annotations.interpretation: The yellow highlight on Bachelard and Kuhn flags the key intellectual predecessors MacIntyre identifies for Foucault's concept of epistemic rupture. Tom is likely noting that the genealogist's account of scientific change borrows from philosophy of science (Bachelard) and history of science (Kuhn) to destabilize the encyclopaedist's narrative of rational progress. This may also be a cross-reference point — Tom may want to examine whether Kuhn's incommensurability thesis is compatible with MacIntyre's tradition-constituted rationality.
annotations.rc_relevance: The question of whether traditions can make rational progress across ruptures is central to RC's argument that Thomistic moral enquiry is not merely parochial but genuinely rational — Kuhn and Bachelard are the genealogist's best weapons against this claim, so Tom is right to flag them.

annotations.content: also secondary phenomena
annotations.anchor_text: _rationality are also secondary phenomena for they_
annotations.interpretation: The circle isolates the claim that 'forms of rationality' are secondary phenomena — the same status given to sciences earlier in the passage. Tom circles this to mark the escalation of the genealogist's argument: not just scientific practice but rationality itself is downstream of power assemblages. This is the most radical claim on the page and Tom recognizes it as such.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the genealogist's most direct challenge to the RC project: if rationality is secondary to power, then the Thomistic tradition's appeal to rational moral enquiry is itself a power move. Tom needs to engage this claim directly if RC is to defend tradition-based reason against genealogical critique.

annotations.content: distributions of power
annotations.anchor_text: _making of which distributions of power have been_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the key term 'distributions of power' — the genealogist's explanatory primitive. By isolating this phrase, he is tracking how power functions as the foundational explanatory category in the genealogist's framework, replacing the encyclopaedist's 'reason' and the traditionalist's 'virtue.' This is likely a term he will need to contest or engage in RC.
annotations.rc_relevance: The concept of 'distributions of power' as explanatory of moral and rational formations is what MacIntyre's tradition-based account must either incorporate (as subsidiarity and institutional analysis) or refute — RC's theme of subsidiarity and civic friendship requires an account of power that is not purely Foucauldian.

annotations.content: what appear at the surface level as forms of rationality both are and result from the implementation of a variety of aggressive and defensive strategies
annotations.anchor_text: _surface level as forms of rationality both are_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as the genealogist's master thesis on the relationship between rationality and strategy. The double claim — rationality 'both is and results from' strategic power — is philosophically precise and Tom recognizes it. He is likely flagging this as the claim most in tension with MacIntyre's own defense of tradition-constituted rationality, since MacIntyre wants to say traditions can adjudicate rationally between themselves.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the crux of RC's challenge from the genealogist: if the Thomistic tradition's claims to rational superiority are themselves aggressive strategies, then the RC project's appeal to that tradition is ideologically suspect. Tom must address this to make the RC argument credible.

annotations.content: Truth and power are thus inseparable
annotations.anchor_text: _Truth and power are thus inseparable_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as the compressed thesis statement of the entire passage — Foucault's most quoted and most contested claim. The underlining here (as opposed to a bracket or circle) suggests Tom treats this as a proposition to be directly engaged, not just noted. He is likely planning to position this claim as the genealogist's definitive challenge to the RC project's recovery of objective moral truth.
annotations.rc_relevance: The inseparability of truth and power is the genealogist's most direct attack on the RC project's core assumption — that there is a recoverable tradition of moral truth worth resurrecting. MacIntyre's response to this is what RC must articulate, and Tom marks this as the sentence where that response must be sharpest.

annotations.content: power driven discourse through ruptures from one to another incommensurable systems of truth, with it an... [partially obscured]
annotations.anchor_text: _Truth and power are thus inseparable And what appear_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes his own synthesis of the page's argument at the bottom — 'power driven discourse through ruptures from one to another incommensurable systems of truth.' This is not just transcription but genuine intellectual synthesis: he has compressed Bachelard/Kuhn's ruptures, Foucault's epistemes, and the power/truth thesis into a single formulation. The phrase 'with it an...' trails off and may continue on the next page. This note suggests Tom is actively building his own vocabulary for the genealogist position.
annotations.rc_relevance: This bottom-margin synthesis is Tom working out how to present the genealogist challenge in RC — the phrase 'incommensurable systems of truth' is the language he will likely use to describe what the RC project must navigate. The note suggests he sees the genealogist's position as coherent and formidable, not merely dismissible.

annotations.content: Vertical bracket or parenthesis mark on far left margin alongside the structuralism paragraph
annotations.anchor_text: _Structuralisms have been of different and incompatible kinds_
annotations.interpretation: The double parenthesis or large bracket on the far left marks the entire structuralism passage as a unit to be read together. This is Tom's architectural annotation — he is demarcating the section that defines structuralism before it is distinguished from genealogy. The mark likely corresponds to his 'Struct alt' note above it, forming a paired annotation that brackets the structuralist position as a defined foil.
annotations.rc_relevance: Tom is mapping the book's argument structure, which is essential for using MacIntyre in RC — he needs to keep the three rival versions cleanly separated so the tradition-based alternative can be presented as genuinely distinct from both encyclopaedism and genealogy.

annotations.content: 6 [numeral, possibly a page cross-reference or section marker]
annotations.anchor_text: _presystematic and the prediscursive which itself_
annotations.interpretation: The numeral '6' in the left margin is ambiguous — it may be a chapter cross-reference, a section counter Tom is maintaining across his reading, or a reference to another text (e.g., page 6, or a sixth numbered point in his notes). Given its placement next to the paradox of reaching for the pre-discursive, it may mark this as the sixth key claim Tom is tracking across the chapter.
annotations.rc_relevance: If Tom is numbering key claims, this suggests he is building a structured engagement with MacIntyre's argument — the kind of systematic tracking needed for the RC project's theoretical scaffolding, particularly for the section addressing the genealogist challenge.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the genealogist faces a self-undermining paradox: to carry out the genealogical project coherently, the genealogist must ascribe to himself a continuous, substantial self with deliberate purpose and commitment — yet the genealogical stance dissolves precisely such a self into nothing but what genealogy makes of it. The genealogist needs a persistent identity to move between masks and personae, but the logic of genealogy denies that any such identity exists beneath the masks. This page thus exposes a deep internal tension in the Foucauldian genealogical project.

annotations.content: consent or implied?
annotations.anchor_text: _did this regression from Nietzsche to Hobbes_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is questioning whether the reader's or the genealogist's relationship to the text's argumentative framework is one of explicit consent or merely implied complicity. This likely flags a concern about the conditions under which a reader participates in the genealogical enterprise — whether one is knowingly entering its terms or being drawn in without acknowledgment. It may also connect to MacIntyre's point that the relationship between reader and genealogical text is temporary and provisional.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project is deeply concerned with the conditions of legitimate intellectual and moral authority — whether institutions and traditions earn genuine assent or merely assume it. Tom's note probes whether genealogy smuggles in normative commitments it officially denies, a question central to MacIntyre's critique.

annotations.content: (recall)
annotations.anchor_text: _I noted earlier that the relationship which Moml requires_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging a cross-reference, reminding himself that MacIntyre has discussed the reader-text relationship earlier in the book (likely in the chapter on the encyclopaedist or in the introduction). The parenthetical format suggests this is a navigational note rather than a substantive comment — he wants to track how MacIntyre's argument about the genealogist's reader relationship compares with what was said earlier.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project's tripartite structure (encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition) requires careful attention to how each mode of enquiry positions its reader; this cross-reference note suggests Tom is actively building that comparative picture.

annotations.anchor_text: _committing the genealogist him or herself as that elusive figure_
annotations.interpretation: The '2021' suggests Tom added this note during a specific reading session or is dating a thought. 'Why not a creative chain perspectives?' is a substantive challenge to MacIntyre's argument: Tom is asking whether the genealogist could simply embrace a creative, ever-shifting chain of perspectives without needing the persistent self MacIntyre demands. This is a genuine philosophical objection — perhaps influenced by a more sympathetic reading of Foucault or by thinkers like Deleuze who celebrate productive multiplicity without a stable subject.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly challenges MacIntyre's refutation of genealogy and is important for RC: if genealogy can survive by embracing perspectival creativity without a stable self, then MacIntyre's critique loses force and the case for tradition-based moral enquiry must be made differently.

annotations.content: Drawn figure resembling a simplified human torso/body outline (ovoid shape with a circle at top suggesting a head), annotated with '(1)' inside
annotations.anchor_text: _Yet now the question arises as to whether_
annotations.interpretation: The body drawing adjacent to the paragraph about the genealogist's self is almost certainly a visual gloss on the argument that the genealogist must ascribe to himself a bodily, persistent self. The '(1)' may mark this as the first horn of a dilemma Tom is reconstructing. Tom may be visually externalizing MacIntyre's claim that the genealogist requires a substantial, embodied self even while the genealogical project theoretically dissolves such selfhood.
annotations.rc_relevance: The question of embodied, persistent selfhood is central to Thomistic moral anthropology and to RC's argument that character formation requires a stable subject capable of habituation — this drawing flags exactly the point where genealogy fails on MacIntyre's account.

annotations.content: a continuity of deliberate purpose
annotations.anchor_text: _the genealogist has to ascribe to the genealogical_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this phrase because it is the pivot of MacIntyre's internal critique: the genealogist must secretly rely on the very kind of purposive, continuous selfhood that genealogy theoretically dismantles. This is a key term in the argument — 'deliberate purpose' implies rational agency and narrative identity, both of which genealogy subverts.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, 'continuity of deliberate purpose' is precisely what tradition-constituted rationality provides and what Enlightenment and genealogical rationality cannot — this underline marks the conceptual crux MacIntyre uses to privilege tradition-based enquiry.

annotations.content: commitment
annotations.anchor_text: _and a commitment to that purpose which can only_
annotations.interpretation: Underlined separately from the longer phrase, suggesting Tom sees 'commitment' as a distinct and significant term. MacIntyre is arguing that commitment — a durable, binding orientation — is required even by the genealogist, yet is incompatible with genealogy's anti-essentialist logic. Tom may be tracking this as a key virtue-theoretic term that genealogy cannot coherently employ.
annotations.rc_relevance: Commitment is central to RC's account of civic friendship and institutional loyalty — traditions require and sustain commitments in ways that genealogy structurally cannot. This annotation marks where MacIntyre's argument converges with RC's positive case.

annotations.content: the genealogist's self nothing but what genealogy makes of it, and that self is dissolved to the point at which there is no longer a continuous genealogical project. Or so I am suggesting.
annotations.anchor_text: _nealogist's self nothing but what genealogy makes_
annotations.interpretation: This is the climactic sentence of MacIntyre's argument and Tom has highlighted it as such. The phrase 'Or so I am suggesting' is notable — MacIntyre acknowledges this is a contestable claim, not a demonstrated proof. Tom likely highlights this both as the thesis of the section and as a potential weak point: MacIntyre admits he is 'suggesting' rather than proving, which opens the door to the kind of objection Tom raised in his margin note about a 'creative chain of perspectives.'
annotations.rc_relevance: This sentence is the lynchpin of MacIntyre's refutation of genealogy as a rival to tradition-based enquiry — for RC, establishing this refutation is essential before making the positive case for Thomistic moral enquiry as the only coherent alternative.

annotations.content: why not
annotations.anchor_text: _self not perspectival, but persistent and substantial_
annotations.interpretation: This is Tom's most sustained objection on the page. He is asking: why couldn't the genealogist's 'purpose' simply emerge organically — chosen improvisationally, unfolding without a pre-existing stable self? The reference to 'power relationships (including persuasive power)' suggests Tom is drawing on Foucauldian resources to counter MacIntyre: perhaps power/knowledge relations themselves constitute a kind of ongoing purposive structure that does not require a pre-given self. 'Without power relationships' may be a condition Tom is testing — can genealogy work even bracketing power dynamics?
annotations.rc_relevance: This annotation captures the deepest challenge RC must grapple with: whether a post-Foucauldian subject constituted through power relations can sustain moral enquiry without the tradition-grounded self MacIntyre requires. Tom is stress-testing MacIntyre's argument before accepting it as an RC foundation.

annotations.content: commitments vs. exploration
annotations.anchor_text: _Is this indeed so? If we press this question_
annotations.interpretation: Tom distills the entire dialectic of the page into a binary: commitments (the persistent, purposive self MacIntyre argues is required) versus exploration (the genealogist's preferred mode of open-ended, maskable, provisional inquiry). This is a conceptual summary note, likely written after finishing the page, organizing the tension for later retrieval or argument construction.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'commitments vs. exploration' binary maps directly onto RC's central tension between tradition (which requires stable commitments to practices and communities) and modernity's valorization of open-ended self-reinvention — Tom is crystallizing MacIntyre's argument in RC-applicable terms.

annotations.content: self
annotations.anchor_text: _Is this indeed so? If we press this question_
annotations.interpretation: Likely 'self-underlying perspective' or possibly 'self-destroying perspective' — the handwriting is ambiguous. If 'underlying,' Tom is noting that the genealogist requires a self that underlies all perspectival shifts, which is MacIntyre's point. If 'destroying,' Tom may be noting that genealogy is a self-undermining project. Either reading captures the performative contradiction MacIntyre identifies.
annotations.rc_relevance: The self-undermining character of genealogy is precisely what MacIntyre needs to establish to show that tradition-based enquiry is not merely one option among equals — this note marks Tom's recognition of this structural move, which is foundational to RC's theoretical architecture.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that genealogy as a systematic project faces a self-undermining paradox: the intelligibility of genealogical enquiry requires the genealogist to draw upon standards, allegiances, and forms of recognition that genealogy itself aims to disrupt and subvert. He then diagnoses the history of genealogy — specifically Foucault's trajectory — as one of 'progressive impoverishment,' in which later work avoided collapse only by covertly importing non-genealogical sources and methods. This leaves genealogy currently resourceless before problems it cannot yet solve, though MacIntyre notes this gives no comfort to encyclopaedists either.

annotations.anchor_text: _Can the genealogical narrative find any place_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is numbering the first of two questions MacIntyre poses as the central aporia of genealogy: whether the genealogical narrative can find any place within itself for the genealogist as agent. This structural numbering indicates Tom is treating these as a formal pair — likely to track them across subsequent chapters or to use them as a diagnostic grid for evaluating genealogy's self-undermining character.
annotations.rc_relevance: This question directly maps onto RC's concern with the 'genealogist' version of moral enquiry: if the genealogist cannot locate herself within her own narrative, the tradition-constituted alternative MacIntyre defends becomes all the more compelling as the only stable framework for moral agency.

annotations.content: sgn
annotations.anchor_text: _Can the genealogical narrative find any place_
annotations.interpretation: This abbreviation appears to be Tom's shorthand for 'sign' or possibly 'significant' — marking the two questions as a pivotal signpost in the argument. Alternatively, 'sgn' could be a cross-reference notation to another section or thinker (e.g., signaling a connection to Derrida on signs, or to MacIntyre's earlier use of 'signs of contradiction'). Its placement next to the circled '(1)' suggests Tom read both questions as a structurally critical moment deserving retrieval.
annotations.rc_relevance: If 'sgn' means 'significant,' Tom is flagging this as a key node in MacIntyre's critique of genealogy — which is central to RC's tripartite structure argument and the project's case for why tradition-based enquiry is the superior alternative.

annotations.anchor_text: _can genealogy, as a systematic project, be made intelligible_
annotations.interpretation: Tom numbers the second question symmetrically with the first, treating MacIntyre's two-question structure as a formal dialectical pair. The second question — whether genealogy can be made intelligible to the genealogist and to others without tacit recourse to the standards it aims to subvert — is the more devastating of the two, as it implicates genealogy in performative self-contradiction. Tom is likely marking this for use as a summary of genealogy's fundamental instability.
annotations.rc_relevance: This second question is the crux of RC's argument against purely deconstructive approaches to moral discourse: if genealogy cannot make itself intelligible without borrowing from tradition, then civic friendship and shared standards of rationality are not optional extras but necessary conditions of any moral enquiry.

annotations.content: the intelligibility of genealogy requires it seems to be the case that the intelligibility of genealogy requires beliefs and allegiances of a kind precluded by the genealogical stance
annotations.anchor_text: _intelligibility of genealogy requires beliefs and allegiances_
annotations.interpretation: This is the core performative contradiction MacIntyre identifies, and Tom's highlighting marks it as the thesis of the section. The highlighted passage captures the self-referential trap: genealogy must presuppose the very commitments it debunks. Tom is almost certainly flagging this as a quotable formulation for RC, where the tension between genealogical skepticism and the conditions of intelligible discourse is a central diagnostic.
annotations.rc_relevance: This passage is the philosophical heart of RC's case against the genealogist mode: moral intelligibility — including the intelligibility of civility itself — requires shared allegiances that genealogy dissolves. MacIntyre's point supports RC's argument that civic friendship and tradition are preconditions, not products, of moral enquiry.

annotations.content: the history of genealogy has been, and could not have been other than, one of progressive impoverishment
annotations.anchor_text: _history of genealogy has been one of progressive impoverishment_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's boldest historical claim: that genealogy as a research program has not merely stalled but has necessarily deteriorated. This is a strong evaluative verdict, not just a descriptive observation, and Tom's mark suggests he finds it either powerfully apt or in need of scrutiny. The phrase 'could not have been other than' is especially significant — MacIntyre is claiming the impoverishment is structural, not contingent.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this claim about genealogy's progressive impoverishment is a counterpart to the encyclopaedist's failure MacIntyre diagnoses elsewhere — together they clear the ground for the Thomistic tradition as the viable third option. Tom is likely tracking this as evidence for RC's constructive argument.

annotations.content: Yet if genealogy now confronts problems for which it does not at least as yet seem able to devise solutions, a resourcelessness which may of course be only temporary
annotations.anchor_text: _genealogy now confronts problems resourcelessness which may be only temporary_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's careful qualification — 'which may of course be only temporary' — suggesting he is tracking MacIntyre's intellectual honesty or perhaps noting a potential weakness in the argument. MacIntyre is not claiming genealogy is permanently defeated, only currently resourceless. Tom may be flagging this as a place where RC's argument must not overclaim, or where a charitable interlocutor might push back.
annotations.rc_relevance: This qualification is important for RC's rhetorical posture: the project cannot simply declare genealogy dead. RC must engage the possibility that genealogy could recover, which means the constructive Thomistic argument must be made on its own merits, not merely by default.

annotations.content: vertical double line (||) in right margin
annotations.anchor_text: _Yet if genealogy now confronts problems resourcelessness_
annotations.interpretation: A double vertical line typically signals a passage of exceptional importance — stronger than a single underline or bracket. Tom is marking the entire passage about genealogy's resourcelessness and the encyclopaedist's false comfort as a section he will need to return to. The double line may also indicate a point of structural argument he intends to cite directly in RC writing.
annotations.rc_relevance: The double-marked passage is precisely where MacIntyre pivots from diagnosing genealogy to warning encyclopaedists — a move that clears space for the tradition-based alternative. pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the genealogical project, even if it has failed to fully overcome its own difficulties, has at minimum succeeded in impugning the encyclopaedist's form of moral enquiry. He contends that defects in genealogy's treatment of incommensurability among rival moral, scientific, physical, and theological standpoints do not diminish the force of that incommensurability itself — the moral enquirer is transformed from a neutral encyclopaedic participant into an engaged partisan of one standpoint against rivals. This dissolution is visible in the Fifteenth Edition of the Encyclopaedia Britannica, where Mortimer Adler's editorial faith in the unity of knowledge is exposed as mere fideism increasingly at odds with contemporary intellectual fragmentation.

pages.summary: This is the closing passage of the chapter 'Genealogies and Subversions' (page 57, not 62 as stated). MacIntyre concludes his comparative framing of the encyclopaedist and genealogist modes of moral enquiry by asserting that the adequacy of his critique depends entirely on whether a third alternative exists beyond these two rivals. He announces that the next four lectures will be devoted to investigating the possibility of precisely such a third alternative — which the reader understands will be the Thomistic tradition-constituted enquiry.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that despite their radical opposition, the encyclopaedist and the genealogist share a crucial hidden agreement: both conceive of the history of philosophy from Socrates to the nineteenth century as a unified history — the encyclopaedist as a history of rational progress, the genealogist as a history of reason's disguised service to the will to power. This shared commitment to a unified historical narrative puts both traditions at odds with any view that sees philosophy's history as fundamentally broken or divided. The page thus sets up MacIntyre's own tradition-based view as a third alternative that rejects this shared assumption.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that Kleutgen's key contribution was not merely his particular characterization of pre-modern versus modern philosophy, but his insistence on rupture where both encyclopaedists and genealogists had seen continuity. The chapter then pivots to show that Descartes functions as a symbol for the encyclopaedist's ideal of reason liberated from any particular moral or religious community — an impersonal, universal, disinterested rationality. MacIntyre identifies this as the shared premise that encyclopaedist and genealogist both accept, framing an either/or: reason is universal and disinterested, or it is merely a mask for particular interests — and this false dichotomy conceals the Thomist third possibility.

Tom is almost certainly extracting this as a clean, citable statement of the encyclopaedist's core epistemological claim — that rationality equals freedom from community-particularity. This is the position MacIntyre will argue is shared (in its negative valence) by the genealogist.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's critique of liberal civic culture depends on contesting exactly this claim — that genuine civility and rational enquiry require detachment from particular moral communities rather than formation within them.

annotations.content: declaration of independence by reason from the particular bonds
annotations.anchor_text: _Descartes symbolized for the nineteenth-century encyclopaedist a declaration_
annotations.interpretation: The phrase 'declaration of independence' is highlighted within the already-bracketed block, suggesting Tom is flagging this specific rhetorical framing. MacIntyre's use of 'declaration of independence' is politically loaded — it frames the encyclopaedist project as a kind of revolutionary secession from tradition. Tom may be noting both the argumentative content and the rhetorical strategy.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this phrase crystallizes the Enlightenment founding myth that civility and reason require emancipation from inherited moral bonds — the very assumption RC seeks to challenge by recovering classical accounts of civic friendship and virtue formation.

annotations.content: its objectivity is inseparable from its freedom from the partialities of all such communities
annotations.anchor_text: _It is on this view of the essence of rationality_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has highlighted the specific epistemological claim: objectivity = freedom from communal partiality. This is the premise MacIntyre is about to show is shared by both encyclopaedist and genealogist — and which the Thomist tradition denies. The highlight signals Tom's recognition that this is the crux of the argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly engages RC's interest in MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism — the claim that objectivity requires communal detachment is precisely what Thomistic and classical accounts of practical reason reject, and what RC's recovery of tradition-embedded civility must address.

annotations.content: this very same conception of reason as universal and disinterested that the genealogist rejects
annotations.anchor_text: _And it is of course this very same conception_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has highlighted the moment MacIntyre names the genealogist's shared premise (in its negative form). The genealogist and encyclopaedist agree on what reason would have to be if it were legitimate — they merely disagree about whether it exists. Tom is tracking the structural logic of the either/or that MacIntyre is about to expose as a false dilemma.
annotations.rc_relevance: This annotation marks the precise point where MacIntyre shows that postmodern genealogical critique and Enlightenment encyclopaedism are mirror images — a key theoretical move for RC, which seeks a position outside this binary.

annotations.content: conceals from view is a third possibility
annotations.anchor_text: _What this alternative conceals from view is a third possibility_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has highlighted what is arguably the most important phrase on the page — MacIntyre's announcement of the Thomist third possibility that the encyclopaedist/genealogist binary suppresses. The highlight at the very bottom of the page suggests Tom is flagging a major argumentative turn. The phrase 'conceals from view' is itself philosophically significant: it implies that the binary is not innocent but ideologically motivated.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the theoretical heart of RC's project — the recovery of a tradition-based rationality that is neither the encyclopaedist's disembodied universalism nor the genealogist's unmasking critique, but a genuinely third account of how reason moves within and through moral communities toward truth.

annotations.content: the possibility that reason can only move towards being genuinely
annotations.anchor_text: _What this alternative conceals from view is a third possibility, the possibility that reason_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has underlined the continuation of MacIntyre's announcement of the third possibility at the very foot of the page, suggesting he is tracking the sentence as it runs onto the next page. The underline beneath the highlight reinforces the sense that Tom treats this as a key passage requiring close attention — possibly a passage he intends to quote or build on.
annotations.rc_relevance: The phrase 'reason can only move towards being genuinely' points toward MacIntyre's processual, tradition-embedded account of rationality — directly foundational to RC's argument that civic reason is formed within communities of practice, not despite them.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that entering the Socratic dialectic already presupposes certain moral virtues and commitments, meaning the conclusions of Platonic-Aristotelian enquiry are partially predetermined by the initial moral stance of the enquirer. Nietzsche recognized this entrapment and therefore rejected the dialectic altogether, preferring the sophists — but MacIntyre notes that Plato himself was equally in conflict with the encyclopaedist assumption that truth is standpoint-independent and discoverable by any adequately intelligent person. The page thus sets up a three-way tension: the tradition-based enquiry of Plato/Aristotle, Nietzsche's genealogical rejection, and the encyclopaedist's pretension to neutral rationality.

annotations.content: nascent virtue...
annotations.anchor_text: _one should already possess and recognize certain_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging MacIntyre's claim that moral enquiry requires pre-existing, incipient virtue as a precondition — the enquirer must already be partly virtuous before philosophical dialectic can function. The ellipsis suggests Tom is holding this thought open, perhaps to connect it to another passage or to ask: what counts as 'nascent' enough? This may also signal tension with liberal educational assumptions that rationality alone suffices.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is central to RC's concern with character formation prior to civic participation — the project's interest in how institutions form virtue before individuals can engage in rational moral discourse maps directly onto this 'nascent virtue' precondition.

annotations.content: Ack by S
annotations.anchor_text: _something further acknowledged by Plato in the Republic_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is tracking that this point — the necessity of prior moral virtue for dialectic — is acknowledged by both Socrates (S) and Plato (P). He is structuring a parallel attribution, likely to show MacIntyre is not just reading Plato anachronistically but drawing on a consistent strand across both figures. This kind of double-attribution tracking suggests Tom is building an argument about the depth of this tradition's self-awareness.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, the fact that both Socrates and Plato explicitly acknowledge the moral preconditions of rational enquiry strengthens the case that tradition-based moral community is not a Thomistic invention but a classical consensus — relevant to RC's recovery of pre-modern civic virtue traditions.

annotations.content: nor is
annotations.anchor_text: _and by Aristotle in his account of the inseparability_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is extending his attribution schema — Aristotle also acknowledges this, and Tom notes this acknowledgment is equivalent to ('=') Socrates' acknowledgment but 'simplified.' The word 'simplified' is striking: Tom may be suggesting that Aristotle's version of the claim (inseparability of moral and intellectual virtues) is a more systematic, less aporetic version of what Socrates dramatized. This is a sophisticated reader's gloss, not just a content note.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's engagement with MacIntyre's tripartite structure depends on understanding how the Aristotelian-Thomistic tradition consolidates and clarifies what was nascent in Socratic practice — Tom's 'simplified' gloss tracks exactly this developmental logic within the tradition.

annotations.content: V added by S
annotations.anchor_text: _Hence of course arose Nietzsche's warning not to allow_
annotations.interpretation: This is Tom's most extended and complex annotation. 'V added by S to G' likely means Virtue (V) added by Socrates (S) to the Gorgias (G) — i.e., Socratic dialectic introduces virtue as a standard Callicles does not share. 'Is not V, could not be V to really prize' suggests Tom is working through why Callicles cannot genuinely value virtue — his framework prevents it. The final lines about vice and virtue being 'blended and mixed in a N show of rhetorical power' is Tom's interpretation of the Nietzschean/Calliclean position: what looks like virtue in the sophistic tradition is actually a performance blending virtue and vice through rhetoric. This is a philosophical reconstruction, not just a summary.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly addresses RC's concern with the corruption of civic discourse by rhetorical performance masquerading as virtue — the 'blending of vice and virtue in rhetorical show' is precisely the kind of pseudo-civility RC seeks to diagnose and resist.

annotations.content: Gorgias and in the Republic
annotations.anchor_text: _by Plato in initiating the philosophical tradition particularly_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the two Platonic texts MacIntyre centers his argument on, likely to keep them visually salient as reference points for the dialectic/virtue argument that follows throughout the page.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery of tradition-based enquiry requires engaging these foundational texts directly; highlighting them marks Tom's awareness that MacIntyre's argument is tethered to specific textual loci, not just abstract claims.

annotations.content: view therefore bound to be sterile if disinterested. A prior commitment is required and the conclusions which emerge as enquiry progresses will of course have been partially and crucially predetermined by the nature of this initial commitment.
annotations.anchor_text: _bound to be sterile if disinterested a prior commitment_
annotations.interpretation: This is the key thesis of the page and Tom has highlighted it in full. MacIntyre is making the strong claim that moral enquiry is necessarily tradition-constituted — disinterested enquiry is not just difficult but structurally sterile. The highlighting suggests Tom regards this as a passage to cite or return to, possibly as the strongest statement of MacIntyre's anti-encyclopaedist position in this section.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is foundational for RC: the encyclopaedist pretension to neutral enquiry is not merely epistemically naïve but practically self-defeating. RC's argument for tradition-embedded civility rests on exactly this claim — that moral community requires prior commitment, not just rational openness.

annotations.content: Nietzsche's warning not to allow oneself to be entrapped by the Socratic dialectic
annotations.anchor_text: _Hence of course arose Nietzsche's warning not to allow_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines Nietzsche's strategic insight as MacIntyre presents it — that the dialectic is a trap for those who don't already share its moral presuppositions. This underline likely signals that Tom sees this as MacIntyre's fairest characterization of the Nietzschean genealogical move, worth marking as an honest concession within the argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC must grapple with the genealogical challenge seriously — Tom's underlining of Nietzsche's 'warning' suggests he recognizes that any recovery of tradition-based civility must account for why the Nietzschean rejection has force, not just dismiss it.

annotations.content: Gorgias and the Republic was equally in conflict with one of the major presuppositions of the encyclopaedic stance, that truth not only is what it is, independent of standpoint, but can be discovered or confirmed by any adequately intelligent person, no matter what his point of view
annotations.anchor_text: _was equally in conflict with one of the major presuppositions_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's crucial structural move: Plato was not only opposed to Nietzsche's genealogy but also to the encyclopaedist's standpoint-neutrality. This creates the three-way opposition that structures the whole book. Tom's underlining here suggests he sees this as the pivot of the page's argument — the moment MacIntyre positions the Platonic-Aristotelian tradition as a genuine third option, not a proto-encyclopaedism.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's tripartite structure (encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition) depends on this exact point — the tradition is not a pre-critical version of liberal rationalism but a genuinely distinct alternative. Tom's marking confirms he is tracking MacIntyre's architecture carefully for RC's own constructive use.

annotations.content: Checkmark in left margin
annotations.anchor_text: _one should already possess and recognize certain moral virtues_
annotations.interpretation: A simple affirmation mark — Tom agrees with or strongly endorses the claim that moral virtues must be possessed prior to dialectical enquiry. Given the adjacent 'nascent virtue...' note, the checkmark likely signals both agreement and importance, distinguishing this as a claim Tom wants to affirm rather than merely record.
annotations.rc_relevance: The checkmark on the pre-condition of virtue for enquiry affirms one of RC's core pedagogical assumptions: that civic formation must precede civic argument, not follow from it.

annotations.content: universal and impersonal insofar as it is neither neutral nor disinterested, that membership in a particular type of moral community is a condition one from which fundamental dissent has to be excluded, and more especially for moral and theological enquiry.
annotations.anchor_text: _universal and impersonal insofar as it is neither neutral_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets the opening argument about the non-neutral character of what appears universal — MacIntyre's claim that tradition-based enquiry presents itself as universal while actually requiring community membership and excluding fundamental dissent. The bracket likely marks this as a structurally important passage that frames everything following on the page.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's critique of liberal proceduralism depends on exposing exactly this: that claims to universal rational standards are always already embedded in particular communities with exclusionary commitments — Tom is marking the argument's foundation.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that philosophy, properly understood in the tradition running from Socrates through Aquinas, is a techne — a craft — requiring both intellectual and moral virtues, with the master-craftsman possessing sophia as its telos. He contends that this craft-conception of philosophical enquiry, shared by the medieval liberal arts tradition (ars liberalis), stands in sharp contrast to modern encyclopaedic and genealogical rivals. The page then begins to unpack a key structural distinction within any craft: the difference between what genuinely is good to do and what merely seems good to a particular apprentice.

annotations.content: we need a commentary on 3RV
annotations.anchor_text: _Too Many Thomisms? page 61_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging a meta-scholarly need: the book itself lacks a sufficiently accessible secondary literature to guide readers through its argument. This is not a criticism of MacIntyre per se but a research gap Tom is identifying — likely for the RC project itself, which may need to produce or rely on such a commentary. The note at the very top of the page signals that this passage is dense enough to require interpretive scaffolding before it can be deployed in RC work.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's methodology: if the project draws heavily on 3RV's tripartite structure (encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition), the absence of a good commentary is an obstacle Tom is flagging as needing resolution before RC can proceed confidently.

annotations.content: icular kind of person if he or she is to move towards a knowledge of the truth about his or her good and about the human good
annotations.anchor_text: _move towards a knowledge of the truth_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is marking the anthropological precondition MacIntyre sets for genuine philosophical enquiry: it requires a certain kind of person, not merely a certain method. This is a direct challenge to the encyclopaedist assumption that enquiry is person-neutral. The highlight suggests Tom sees this as foundational to MacIntyre's whole argument about tradition-constituted rationality.
annotations.rc_relevance: Central to RC's concern with character formation: civility and moral enquiry are not techniques anyone can apply but require a prior formation of the self — connecting to subsidiarity and the role of institutions in shaping persons.

annotations.content: making oneself into an apprentice to a craft
annotations.anchor_text: _involved in making oneself into an apprentice_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the reflexive, self-transformative dimension of philosophical enquiry as craft. The emphasis is on 'making oneself' — this is not passive reception of knowledge but active self-formation. Tom is likely tracking MacIntyre's use of the apprenticeship model as a counter to the Enlightenment ideal of the autonomous rational agent who reasons from nowhere.
annotations.rc_relevance: Speaks directly to RC's theme of institutions forming character: the apprenticeship model implies that moral and intellectual formation happens within structured communities of practice, not in isolated individuals — a key plank of the Thomistic alternative MacIntyre defends.

annotations.content: conceiving philosophy as a craft, a techne, and its conception of what such a craft in good order is
annotations.anchor_text: _conceiving philosophy as a craft a techne_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the double claim: not just that philosophy is a craft, but that there is a normative standard — 'good order' — internal to the craft itself. This is MacIntyre's move against relativism: traditions are not arbitrary but have internal criteria of excellence. Tom is likely noting this as a hinge moment in the argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's engagement with MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism: the claim that philosophy has an internal telos challenges the encyclopaedist notion of universal, tradition-independent reason.

annotations.content: is the ergon of a techne
annotations.anchor_text: _Every good is the ergon of a techne_
annotations.interpretation: Tom isolates the Aristotelian technical term ergon — the characteristic work or function of a craft. This is the architectonic claim: every good is produced by some craft, and philosophy's ergon is the human good. Tom may be flagging this for its connection to Aristotle's function argument in the NE and its implications for how MacIntyre reconstructs Thomistic teleology.
annotations.rc_relevance: Connects to RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry: the ergon concept grounds MacIntyre's claim that moral enquiry has a determinate subject matter (the human good), resisting the genealogist's dissolution of teleology.

annotations.content: Science of Human Good
annotations.anchor_text: _which is a master-craft science episteme_
annotations.interpretation: Tom glosses MacIntyre's claim that enquiry into the good is both techne and episteme — craft and science — by labeling this composite 'Science of Human Good.' The red ink suggests emphasis or a cross-reference to another text (possibly Aristotle's Politics or NE I). Tom is collapsing the techne/episteme distinction into a single organizing concept, possibly to make it more tractable for RC's audience.
annotations.rc_relevance: This gloss is Tom's attempt to name the master-discipline that RC itself aspires to recover: a rigorous, tradition-constituted enquiry into what human flourishing requires — the intellectual backbone of the civility project.

annotations.content: a master-craft science (episteme) which is techne which enables us to discern unity in multiplicity and the master-craftsman who is the model of the person with sophia
annotations.anchor_text: _master-craftsman who is the model of the person with sophia_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the culminating claim of MacIntyre's craft analogy: sophia names the virtue of the person who has mastered the master-craft, able to perceive unity across the plurality of human goods. Tom is likely flagging this as the ideal against which both encyclopaedist and genealogist fall short — the person with sophia is the implicit standard throughout 3RV.
annotations.rc_relevance: The figure of the person with sophia is directly relevant to RC's concern with civic friendship and the formation of leaders capable of discerning the common good — a Thomistic alternative to both technocratic management and relativist pluralism.

annotations.content: embark on a techne, Aristotle of course only restated what he had learned from Socrates and Plato
annotations.anchor_text: _committed to becoming philosophos is to embark on a techne_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the continuity claim — Aristotle's craft-conception of philosophy is not original but inherited from Socrates and Plato. This is MacIntyre asserting the unity of the classical tradition against the tendency to treat Greek thinkers as radically discontinuous. Tom may be tracking this to counter a genealogical reading that would fragment the tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: Supports RC's theme of tradition vs. reform: MacIntyre is showing that the tradition is internally continuous, which strengthens the case that its recovery is possible rather than merely nostalgic.

annotations.content: ceptions of craft were used to characterize enquiry (the word 'ars' as used in 'ars liberalis' means precisely what 'techne' means; the liberal arts are the crafts of free persons)
annotations.anchor_text: _ars liberalis means precisely what techne means_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's philological move connecting Greek techne to Latin ars and specifically to ars liberalis. The parenthetical gloss — 'the crafts of free persons' — is politically loaded: liberal education is not mere technique but the formation of free citizens. Tom is likely flagging this for its implications about education and civic formation.
annotations.rc_relevance: Highly relevant to RC's concern with civic friendship and institutional formation: if the liberal arts are crafts of free persons, then the degradation of liberal education represents a loss of the very formation that civility requires.

annotations.content: totle that authors drew to inform that conception of their practice
annotations.anchor_text: _upon either Plato or Aristotle that authors drew_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's claim about the normative authority Plato and Aristotle held for medieval practitioners of philosophy-as-craft. This marks the transmission of the tradition — it is not merely historical but actively authoritative. Tom may be noting this as evidence that traditions require canonical authorities, a point relevant to RC's engagement with Thomism.
annotations.rc_relevance: Connects to RC's MacIntyrean theme that tradition-constituted rationality requires authoritative texts and exemplars — the kind of canonical inheritance that Enlightenment rationalism tried to bypass.

annotations.content: is it about the structure of a craft and of a craft guild which is important for philosophy? What is it that philosophy as a craft shares with other crafts
annotations.anchor_text: _structure of a craft and of a craft guild important for philosophy_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's explicit statement of the question he is about to answer — the structural analogy between craft guilds and philosophical communities. This is a methodological pivot in the text, and Tom marks it as the hinge on which the next argument turns. The double question signals that MacIntyre is about to introduce the distinctions that give philosophy-as-craft its specific content.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's concern with institutions forming character: the craft guild is MacIntyre's model for the kind of community that can sustain moral enquiry — a concrete institutional form that RC might draw on when thinking about civic institutions.

annotations.content: Philosophy as craft
annotations.anchor_text: _structure of a craft and of a craft guild_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes a clean heading-style gloss to label this section of MacIntyre's argument. The simplicity suggests this is an organizational note — Tom is structuring his reading of the page so he can retrieve this argument later. It also indicates Tom sees 'philosophy as craft' as a discrete, citable thesis within 3RV rather than a passing metaphor.
annotations.rc_relevance: This label is likely a section-header Tom will use when organizing RC's engagement with MacIntyre — marking the passage where the craft analogy is most systematically developed.

annotations.content: One arises from two key distinctions
annotations.anchor_text: _One arises from two key distinctions which apprentices_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'One' — the first of the salient characteristics MacIntyre identifies. This is a structural marker: Tom is tracking MacIntyre's numbered argument and wants to ensure he finds the corresponding 'Two' later. The circle is a navigational device, not necessarily a substantive one, though it signals Tom recognizes this as an argument with an enumerable structure he intends to map.
annotations.rc_relevance: Relevant to RC's use of MacIntyre's structural distinctions: identifying these numbered characteristics is essential for RC to accurately deploy MacIntyre's craft-analogy argument without collapsing its internal logic.

annotations.content: I
annotations.anchor_text: _One arises from two key distinctions which apprentices_
annotations.interpretation: Tom places a large 'I' in the margin corresponding to MacIntyre's first salient characteristic. This is a structural labeling device — Tom is building a personal outline of MacIntyre's enumerated argument. The 'I' likely pairs with a 'II' on a subsequent page, suggesting Tom is tracking an argument he plans to reproduce or cite in RC materials.
annotations.rc_relevance: Part of Tom's effort to make MacIntyre's dense argument tractable for RC: by numbering the structural features of craft-as-philosophy, he is creating a retrievable schema that can be applied to RC's analysis of civic institutions.

annotations.content: A. real vs. apparent goods
annotations.anchor_text: _what in particular situations it really is good to do and what only seems good_
annotations.interpretation: Tom labels the first of MacIntyre's 'two key distinctions' as 'A. real vs. apparent goods.' This is a philosophical gloss condensing MacIntyre's distinction between what genuinely is good and what merely seems good to the apprentice. The 'A' label suggests Tom expects a 'B' distinction to follow on the next page, and he is pre-organizing the argument. Tom is also signaling his recognition that this distinction is Aristotelian — the difference between real and apparent goods is foundational to NE Book III.
annotations.rc_relevance: Central to RC's concern with moral formation: the distinction between real and apparent goods is precisely what civic institutions must help citizens discern — and its erosion is what MacIntyre attributes to the failure of Enlightenment moral culture.

annotations.content: what in particular situations it really is good to do and what only seems good to do to this particular apprentice but is not in fact so
annotations.anchor_text: _it really is good to do and what only seems good_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the exact formulation of the real/apparent goods distinction. The precision of the highlight — catching the full contrast including the particular apprentice's limited perspective — shows Tom is marking this as a quotable passage, not just a structural marker. The phrase 'this particular apprentice' is key: moral error is indexed to the agent's developmental stage within the craft tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's argument that moral formation requires institutional guidance: the apprentice cannot self-correct without the craft community's authoritative judgment — an anti-individualist, anti-Enlightenment point that RC can deploy against liberal proceduralism.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the apprentice in any craft must learn to identify his or her own defects — corruptions of desire, taste, habit, and judgment — and understand what vices must be eradicated and what virtues cultivated in order to progress toward the craft's telos. He then extends this to moral enquiry specifically: because all rational powers (techné included) can be directed toward contrary ends, something beyond skill is needed — orexis or prohairesis, desire guided by right reason — to orient the exercise of the craft toward genuine good. The telos of moral enquiry is excellence in achieving the genuinely human good for the particular agent in his or her particular circumstances.
