annotations.content: IB.
annotations.anchor_text: _standards recognized to be the best available_
annotations.interpretation: The 'IB.' almost certainly abbreviates Isaiah Berlin, likely flagging a contrast or comparison point. MacIntyre's insistence on craft-embedded, tradition-constituted standards of excellence is in direct tension with Berlin's pluralism and his rejection of any single telos or summum bonum. Tom may be noting this as a site where MacIntyre implicitly argues against the Berlinian view that there is no single best account of human flourishing.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's engagement with MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment liberalism is sharpened here: the IB note suggests Tom is tracking where the tradition-based account of standards diverges from liberal pluralism, a key fault line for the Resurrecting Civility project's treatment of moral disagreement.

annotations.content: mistakes made by him or herself in applying the acknowledged standards, the standards recognized to be the best available so far in the history of that particular craft
annotations.anchor_text: _standards recognized to be the best available_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the full claim that standards are craft-internal and historically sedimented, not externally imposed or universally self-evident. This is MacIntyre's anti-foundationalist epistemology in miniature: what counts as a mistake is only intelligible within the ongoing history of a practice. Tom is likely marking this as a key premise in the argument against both Enlightenment rationalism and relativism simultaneously.
annotations.rc_relevance: This speaks directly to RC's concern with how institutions transmit and embody standards — the standards that make civility intelligible are not abstract but historically housed in particular traditions and communities.

annotations.content: between what it is good and best for me with my particular level of training and learning in my particular circumstances to do and what is good and best unqualifiedly
annotations.anchor_text: _good and best for me with my particular_
annotations.interpretation: This distinction — between the contextually best and the unqualifiedly best — is philosophically central to MacIntyre's Thomism. Tom underlines it likely because it captures the dual-level teleology: the agent's current developmental stage versus the ultimate telos. This distinction has important implications for how moral progress is conceived, and Tom may be flagging it as structurally important for the craft-of-moral-enquiry analogy.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this distinction maps onto the tension between where a civic community currently is and the excellence it is ordered toward — relevant to the question of how reform of civic institutions should be judged and paced.

annotations.content: What the correct and systematic application of these distinctions both by his or her teacher and by the apprentice him or herself results in initially is the identification of the defects and limitations of this particular person, as he or she is here and now, with respect to the achievement of that telos
annotations.anchor_text: _results in initially is the identification of the defects_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets the full methodological claim: that proper application of craft distinctions leads first to self-knowledge of defect. The bracket signals this is a structural hinge in the argument — it is the moment MacIntyre moves from epistemology (how standards work) to moral psychology (what the apprentice must confront about herself). Tom may be marking this as the point where the craft analogy becomes a model for moral self-examination.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's interest in how institutions form character — the process described here (structured self-confrontation guided by a teacher within a tradition) is a model for the kind of formative civic institutions RC wants to recover.

annotations.content: cies of desire, taste, habit, and judgment
annotations.anchor_text: _rooted in corruptions and inadequacies of desire, taste, habit_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the four-term list of corruption sites — desire, taste, habit, judgment — which maps closely onto Aristotelian faculty psychology and Thomistic moral anthropology. This is not merely a list; it is a claim about where moral formation must intervene. Tom likely marks it because it specifies the anthropological substrate that any tradition-based moral pedagogy must address.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's interest in character formation and civic virtue requires a theory of what faculties are being formed; this list gives MacIntyre's answer and anchors the project's anthropological commitments.

annotations.content: self-eval re craft...
annotations.anchor_text: _what it is about him or herself that has to be transformed_
annotations.interpretation: Tom abbreviates 'self-evaluation regarding/within craft.' He is flagging MacIntyre's claim that the craft framework generates a specific mode of self-evaluation — not abstract introspection but practice-internal self-assessment against craft standards. The ellipsis suggests Tom is holding a thought open, perhaps to connect it to something elsewhere in his notes or to a further implication he hasn't fully worked out.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this is a model of the kind of reflexive self-scrutiny that civic life requires — not liberal self-expression but tradition-guided self-examination; this has implications for RC's account of civic friendship as formative rather than merely associative.

annotations.content: what vices need to be eradicated, what intellectual and moral virtues need to be cultivated
annotations.anchor_text: _what vices need to be eradicated, what intellectual_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the paired claim about vice-eradication and virtue-cultivation, which together constitute the moral-developmental program MacIntyre is describing. This is not a casual observation — it is the core of what craft-based moral enquiry demands of the apprentice. Tom may be noting this as the operational content of what MacIntyre means by moral education.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's project of recovering formative institutions requires specifying what those institutions are supposed to do; this sentence gives MacIntyre's answer and is directly usable in RC's constructive proposals.

annotations.content: this need to identify such virtues and to acquire them in order to learn whatever it is in which, in this particular craft, one needs to be instructed has particularly important consequences when the craft in question is, or includes, moral enquiry
annotations.anchor_text: _particularly important consequences when the craft in question is_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the full pivotal sentence where MacIntyre makes the move from any craft to moral enquiry specifically. This is the argumentative crux of the chapter: moral enquiry is a craft, and therefore all the craft-apprenticeship logic applies to it. Tom's underlining here is likely not just noting the claim but marking the logical move as one he wants to track or cite.
annotations.rc_relevance: This sentence is foundational for RC's appropriation of MacIntyre: if moral enquiry is a craft, then the institutions that house it (schools, churches, civic communities) are the only legitimate sites of moral formation — which directly supports RC's emphasis on subsidiarity and institutional renewal.

annotations.content: the craft of moral enquiry
annotations.anchor_text: _consequences when the craft in question is, or includes, moral enquiry_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes out the central concept MacIntyre is developing as a clean label, with a downward arrow suggesting it governs the text below. This is an organizing annotation — Tom is naming the section's thesis for himself. The downward arrow likely indicates that what follows (the Aristotle passage on rational powers and orexis) is being read as flowing from this identification.
annotations.rc_relevance: This annotation confirms Tom is treating 'the craft of moral enquiry' as a key conceptual node for RC — the idea that moral enquiry has an internal telos, standards, and apprenticeship structure is central to RC's account of how civic virtue can be taught and sustained.

annotations.content: The virtues of course are required for the practice of any techné if that techné is to be directed towards a genuine good
annotations.anchor_text: _virtues of course are required for the practice of any techné_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's claim that virtues are not specific to moral enquiry but required for any techné directed at genuine good. This universalizes the argument: the relationship between virtue and craft is not a special feature of ethics but a structural feature of all purposive rational activity. Tom may be flagging this as the philosophical grounding for the broader civic application.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this is important because it means the recovery of virtue is not a sectarian or purely religious project — it is a condition of excellence in any domain of civic life, which supports RC's appeal to a broad audience beyond explicitly Thomistic communities.

annotations.content: the skills of a techné may be exercised with the purpose of achieving what is not in fact a good
annotations.anchor_text: _skills of a techné may be exercised with the purpose_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the dangerous implication MacIntyre acknowledges: skill without virtue can be directed toward evil. This is the Aristotelian point about rational powers having contrary effects. Tom likely marks this because it is the argumentative pressure point that makes virtues necessary rather than merely helpful — a move that resists the liberal separation of technical competence from moral character.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is acutely relevant to RC's critique of technocratic governance and expertise-without-virtue in contemporary institutions — the fragmentation of civility that RC diagnoses may be partly a consequence of exactly this: skills exercised without moral direction.

annotations.content: All rational powers, said Aristotle — and to possess a techné is to possess a rational power — can have contrary effects
annotations.anchor_text: _All rational powers, said Aristotle_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the explicit appeal to Aristotle (Metaphysics IX), flagging the philosophical pedigree of the argument. MacIntyre is not just making a point about craft; he is grounding it in Aristotle's metaphysics of potentiality. Tom may be tracking the Aristotelian sources as distinct from the Thomistic overlay, or simply confirming the philosophical authority being invoked.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's engagement with the 'encyclopaedist / genealogist / tradition' tripartite structure requires knowing which tradition MacIntyre is drawing on at each moment; this citation anchors the argument in Aristotelian rather than specifically Thomistic resources, which matters for RC's account of who can participate in this tradition.

annotations.content: the exercise of a techné does not of itself determine to what end that exercise will be directed. Something else is needed — orexis or prohairesis, felt desire alone or desire guided by reason
annotations.anchor_text: _Something else is needed — orexis or prohairesis_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the introduction of orexis and prohairesis as the bridging concepts between rational power and genuine good. This is philosophically dense: MacIntyre is arguing that rational skill requires appetitive orientation (orexis) or deliberate choice (prohairesis) to be morally directed. Tom is likely marking this because it specifies what virtues actually do — they don't just add moral content to skill; they provide the appetitive structure that directs skill.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC's account of character formation, this is crucial: institutions that form civility must cultivate not just correct beliefs or behaviors but the right appetitive dispositions — which points toward liturgical, habituative, and affective dimensions of formation that purely cognitive approaches miss.

annotations.content: the judgment of right reason informing such a desire will always refer implicitly or explicitly to that telos the achievement of which is the genuine good to be achieved for that particular agent in his or her particular circumstances
annotations.anchor_text: _judgment of right reason informing such a desire will always refer_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the full account of how right reason and desire are integrated in the practically wise person: judgment always refers to the telos appropriate to this particular agent. This is MacIntyre's Thomistic particularism — practical wisdom is not the application of universal rules but the orientation of judgment toward the agent's genuine good within their concrete circumstances. Tom may be marking this as the culminating claim of the argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's subsidiarity theme: the judgment of right reason is irreducibly particular and contextual, which means that no centralized authority can substitute for the practical wisdom formed in local communities and traditions — a key RC argument against bureaucratic approaches to civic renewal.

annotations.content: The telos of moral enquiry, which is excellence in the achieve-
annotations.anchor_text: _The telos of moral enquiry, which is excellence_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the opening of what appears to be a culminating definition of the telos of moral enquiry. The sentence is cut off by the page break, but Tom marks it as significant regardless — it is the point where MacIntyre names the end toward which the whole craft-of-moral-enquiry argument has been building. The underlining here has a definitive, summarizing quality.
annotations.rc_relevance: This sentence encapsulates one of the most important claims RC wants to appropriate from MacIntyre: that moral enquiry has a determinate telos, not just a procedure, which means RC's project of recovering civility is also a project of recovering a substantive account of human excellence.

annotations.content: small asterisk or star-like symbol near 'orexis or prohairesis' passage
annotations.anchor_text: _be directed. Something else is needed — orexis or prohairesis_
annotations.interpretation: The small symbol (appears to be an asterisk or stylized star) near the orexis/prohairesis passage functions as a high-priority flag, distinct from mere underlining. Tom is marking this as a passage he wants to return to or cite — possibly because the introduction of these technical Aristotelian terms is the argumentative pivot of the page.
annotations.rc_relevance: The orexis/prohairesis distinction is important for RC's anthropology of civic formation: the project must address not just the rational but the appetitive dimensions of persons if it is to recover genuinely virtuous civic life.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that moral enquiry is a virtue-guided craft requiring both theoretical understanding and practical embodiment, but this generates an apparent paradox (a version of Plato's Meno problem): one cannot develop the virtues needed for moral enquiry without already possessing some of those virtues. The resolution, partially drawn from the Meno, is that we have an innate potentiality toward moral truth, but we also require a teacher whose authority we must initially accept on faith within a community. From this emerges a conception of 'rational teaching authority' internal to the practice of moral enquiry, analogous to the master-craftsman/apprentice relationship in trades like furniture-making and fishing.

annotations.content: Moral enquiry, therefore not just a craft but a virtue-guided craft
annotations.anchor_text: _not just a craft but a virtue-guided craft_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is marking MacIntyre's key definitional move distinguishing moral enquiry from mere techne — it is not a neutral skill but one constitutively shaped by virtue. This is a load-bearing claim in the chapter's argument and Tom appears to be flagging it as a thesis statement worth returning to. The highlight intensity suggests this is a term of art Tom wants to track across the text.
annotations.rc_relevance: Central to RC's critique of proceduralist liberalism: if moral enquiry is virtue-guided rather than technique-governed, then institutions must form character, not merely adjudicate competing preferences.

annotations.content: one cannot learn how to move towards such conclusions without first having acquired some at least of those same virtues about which one is enquiring
annotations.anchor_text: _cannot learn how to move towards such conclusions_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is carefully marking the paradox MacIntyre is identifying — the hermeneutic circle at the heart of moral formation. This is the Meno problem restated in Thomistic-Aristotelian terms. Tom likely recognizes this as the philosophical crux the whole chapter turns on, and is flagging it for use in RC's account of why moral formation cannot be bootstrapped from neutral starting points.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly supports RC's argument against Enlightenment 'encyclopaedist' rationalism: you cannot construct civic virtue from scratch via reason alone; initiation into a tradition is logically prior.

annotations.content: Asterisk (*) in the right margin
annotations.anchor_text: _So we are threatened by an apparent paradox_
annotations.interpretation: The asterisk marks the moment MacIntyre names the paradox explicitly. Tom uses this symbol to flag structurally pivotal moments — this is likely a 'key hinge' marker rather than a note of agreement or disagreement. The asterisk sits precisely where the argument shifts from exposition to problem-statement, suggesting Tom is tracking the logical architecture of the chapter.
annotations.rc_relevance: The paradox MacIntyre identifies here is the same one RC must address when arguing for tradition-based moral formation: how do you invite someone into a tradition they don't yet have the virtues to appreciate?

annotations.content: wisr? p118
annotations.anchor_text: _apparent paradox in the understanding of moral enquiry_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is cross-referencing page 118 of this or another text, apparently linking the concept of 'wisdom' (abbreviated 'wisr') to this paradox passage. The question mark suggests he is tentatively connecting this to a discussion of wisdom elsewhere — possibly MacIntyre's own treatment of phronesis later in the book, or a cross-reference to Aquinas on prudentia. This is a scholarly tracking annotation, not a critique.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's treatment of practical wisdom (phronesis/prudentia) as the integrating virtue of civic life would need to reckon with this paradox — wisdom cannot be taught without already being partially possessed, which has implications for RC's institutional design proposals.

annotations.content: relativist p.130
annotations.anchor_text: _How is this threat of paradox — recognizably a version_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is anticipating or cross-referencing MacIntyre's engagement with the relativism objection on page 130. The label 'relativist' next to the paradox passage suggests Tom sees the Meno paradox as feeding directly into the relativism problem — if you must already be inside a tradition to reason morally, doesn't this make moral truth tradition-relative? He is flagging this as a philosophical vulnerability MacIntyre will need to address.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is one of RC's most pressing theoretical challenges: how to argue for the recovery of a specific moral tradition (Thomistic civility) without collapsing into relativism. Tom is tracking MacIntyre's own path through this problem.

annotations.content: unless we already have within ourselves the potentiality for moving towards and achieving the relevant theoretical and practical conclusions we shall be unable to learn.
annotations.anchor_text: _unless we already have within ourselves the potentiality_
annotations.interpretation: The bracket isolates MacIntyre's Meno-derived answer to the paradox: innate potentiality. Tom is marking this as a distinct philosophical move — the appeal to an in-built orientation toward moral truth that precedes explicit formation. He may be evaluating whether this is a satisfying answer or whether it simply defers the problem (how do we know the potentiality is there and pointing the right way?).
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's account of natural law and human dignity may depend on this claim about innate moral potentiality — it is what makes civic formation possible across cultural difference and what grounds RC's universalist aspirations within a traditionalist framework.

annotations.content: initial acceptance necessary
annotations.anchor_text: _we shall have to learn from that teacher and initially accept on the basis of his or her authority_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is glossing MacIntyre's key claim about epistemic authority with a compressed label. The word 'necessary' is doing philosophical work — Tom is not merely summarizing but affirming or noting that MacIntyre is making this a logical requirement, not a merely sociological observation. This suggests Tom sees 'initial acceptance' as a structural feature of all tradition-based enquiry, not a defect to be overcome.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's concept of subsidiarity and institutional authority: RC needs a theory of legitimate teaching authority in civic formation, and this annotation marks where MacIntyre grounds such authority in the logic of moral enquiry itself rather than in mere social convention.

annotations.content: we also need a teacher to enable us to actualize that potentiality, and we shall have to learn from that teacher and initially accept on the basis of his or her authority within the community of a craft
annotations.anchor_text: _we also need a teacher to enable us to actualize_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is highlighting the full statement of MacIntyre's solution: potentiality + teacher + community + authority. The highlight spans the complete argumentative unit, suggesting Tom wants to quote or closely analyze this passage. He is likely noting that MacIntyre combines an internalist answer (potentiality) with an externalist one (teacher, community), which is philosophically significant and potentially in tension.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the passage most directly relevant to RC's theory of civic institutions as 'teachers' — schools, parishes, local associations — that actualize moral potentiality through authoritative formation within community, which is the heart of RC's subsidiarity argument.

annotations.content: Hence there emerges a conception of rational teaching authority internal to the practice of the craft of moral enquiry
annotations.anchor_text: _Hence there emerges a conception of rational teaching authority_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is marking the payoff claim of the entire passage — the concept of 'rational teaching authority.' The highlight is emphatic and the right-margin annotation repeats the phrase, indicating this is a term Tom intends to use in RC. He recognizes that MacIntyre is doing something philosophically precise: grounding authority in rationality and practice rather than in mere tradition or power, which defends it against both liberal and postmodern critique.
annotations.rc_relevance: This concept is potentially the theoretical cornerstone of RC's account of civic institutions: they possess not merely social authority but rational teaching authority derived from their role in sustaining practices of moral enquiry. This is RC's answer to the question of why institutions deserve deference.

annotations.content: Rational teaching authority
annotations.anchor_text: _Hence there emerges a conception of rational teaching authority_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is writing out the key term in full in the margin, which is his way of flagging a concept he intends to adopt or engage with directly in RC. This is not merely a summary but a conceptual extraction — he is naming a tool he plans to use. The repetition of the printed phrase suggests he is committing it to memory and marking it for citation.
annotations.rc_relevance: Tom is likely building toward using 'rational teaching authority' as a technical term in RC to describe the kind of authority civic institutions and traditions can legitimately claim — distinguished from mere power or arbitrary convention.

annotations.content: soccer analogy: which club for my child? Any club? What coaches, what parents, coaches, authors... Your trust circle on these issues — for, soccer culture
annotations.anchor_text: _partially define the relationship of master-craftsman to apprentice_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has generated a concrete contemporary analogy to MacIntyre's master-craftsman/apprentice model. The soccer club question is asking: in a pluralist society where many 'craft communities' compete, how do parents choose which tradition of formation to enter their child into? 'Any club?' probes whether the specific tradition matters or whether the mere fact of initiation suffices. The 'trust circle' note suggests Tom is thinking about how authority is actually transmitted in modern life — through networks of trusted persons rather than formal institutions. This is a remarkably applied and pastoral annotation.
annotations.rc_relevance: This analogy is directly usable in RC's public-facing argument: the soccer club as a micro-institution of character formation illustrates subsidiarity in action and raises the question of how fragmented modern communities can recover coherent traditions of civic formation without becoming sectarian.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that standards of achievement within any craft or intellectual enquiry are historically constituted and justified — they emerge from criticism of predecessors, are guided by a conception of a 'finally perfected' telos, and are rationally justified as 'the best so far.' The schemes of deductively ordered sciences are themselves justified not by foundationalist certainty but by their ability to outperform rival competitors in organizing experience and supplying correction. The temporal reference of reasoning within a craft is thus fundamentally different from both the encyclopaedist's ahistorical rationalism and the genealogist's radical skepticism.

annotations.content: best so far... currents of persuasion...
annotations.anchor_text: _standards of achievement within any craft_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is distilling MacIntyre's key epistemological phrase 'best so far' and linking it to the idea of 'currents of persuasion' — suggesting he sees the justificatory standard not as static truth but as a persuasive, rhetorical, and tradition-borne force that flows through time. The ellipses imply Tom is holding this thought open, perhaps for cross-referencing elsewhere. He may be noting that MacIntyre's rationality is not algorithmic but dialogical.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly maps onto RC's concern with tradition vs. reform: civility norms are not timeless but are 'best so far' achievements within a living tradition, and the 'currents of persuasion' framing suggests civic discourse itself is the medium through which such standards travel.

annotations.content: historical justification of stds of craft
annotations.anchor_text: _justified because and insofar as they have_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is labeling the structural claim MacIntyre is making: that justification is inherently historical, not foundationalist. The abbreviation 'stds' (standards) shows Tom is tracking the argument's key term carefully. He is likely flagging this as the anti-Enlightenment epistemological move — standards are not self-evident but emerge from and are accountable to a tradition's history.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is central to RC's engagement with MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism: the encyclopaedist assumes ahistorical standards, but MacIntyre insists all standards are craft-embedded and historically justified — a claim RC uses to ground civility in formative communities rather than abstract principles.

annotations.content: T w/ sciences craft
annotations.anchor_text: _deductively ordered by derivation from first principles_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is abbreviating 'Thomism with sciences as craft' — noting that MacIntyre is here presenting the Thomistic tradition's view of the sciences as a craft with its own telos and historical standards. The shorthand suggests Tom has developed a personal notation for this cluster of ideas. He may be flagging this as the place where MacIntyre most explicitly integrates Thomism into his epistemology of enquiry.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry hinges on exactly this point: if the sciences (including moral science) are crafts with historically justified standards, then Thomism is not defeated by modernity but is a living competitor in an ongoing rational debate — precisely the posture RC wants to model for civic tradition.

annotations.content: So fa given science as craft
annotations.anchor_text: _all schemes themselves are justified by their ability to do better than any rival competitors so far_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is writing 'So far / given science as craft' — connecting the 'best so far' criterion explicitly to the framing of any given science as a craft. This annotation collapses the two key moves of the paragraph: (1) justification is always provisional ('so far') and (2) the unit of justification is the craft-tradition. Tom appears to be building a compressed formula he can deploy in RC writing.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's argument about institutions forming character: if institutions are crafts, their authority is 'so far' justified by their historical track record of organizing human experience well — not by natural law abstractions alone. This gives RC a pragmatic-historical argument for institutional recovery.

annotations.content: Asterisk or fleur-de-lis type symbol in left margin
annotations.anchor_text: _They have emerged from the criticism of their predecessors_
annotations.interpretation: This decorative or emphatic symbol marks what Tom considers the pivot sentence of the paragraph — the claim that standards emerge from internal criticism of predecessors. The ornate nature of the symbol (compared to simple checkmarks or arrows) suggests Tom regards this as a foundational methodological claim worth returning to. It may also serve as a cross-reference marker to another page in the book or to his RC notes.
annotations.rc_relevance: The idea that standards are self-correcting through internal critique is essential to RC's distinction between living traditions and dead dogmas — civility as a tradition must have this self-critical structure to avoid becoming mere conventionalism.

annotations.content: Triangle (delta) symbol at bottom left margin
annotations.anchor_text: _supplying correction and improvement where some need for these has been identified_
annotations.interpretation: A triangle in philosophical annotation often signals 'change,' 'difference,' or 'therefore' (delta notation). Tom may be using it to mark the logical conclusion of the paragraph — that self-correction is what distinguishes a living craft-tradition from a closed system. Alternatively, he may be flagging a tension: MacIntyre's tradition can correct itself, but who adjudicates when correction is needed? This is the problem of authority within tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is deeply concerned with subsidiarity and the role of institutions in forming character — this mark may signal Tom's awareness that self-correcting traditions require internal authority structures, linking to the RC question of what institutional forms enable genuine civic correction.

annotations.content: Large boxed diagram or word 'So far' written in large letters at bottom margin with box drawn around it
annotations.anchor_text: _temporal reference of reasoning within a craft thus differs_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has written 'So far' in large letters and boxed it at the bottom of the page — this is a visual anchor, a mnemonic device making the page's controlling concept unmissable. The box signals that 'so far' is not just a phrase but a technical term Tom wants to carry forward. This also functions as a page-end summary: the entire argument on this page is captured by that two-word criterion.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'so far' standard is RC's alternative to both relativism and absolutism in the civility debate — traditions of civility are justified not because they are eternal but because they are the best available account of human social flourishing to date, a position RC uses to defend tradition against both genealogical deconstruction and encyclopaedist dismissal.

annotations.content: standards of achievement within any craft are justified historically
annotations.anchor_text: _standards of achievement within any craft are justified_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the thesis sentence of the key paragraph. The highlighting of 'historically' in particular suggests he wants to flag the adverb as doing major philosophical work — it is not merely a descriptive point but an anti-foundationalist epistemological claim. This sentence is the clearest statement of MacIntyre's position that Tom will likely quote in RC writing.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the epistemological foundation for RC's entire project: if achievement standards are historically justified, then the recovery of classical civility norms is not nostalgia but rational enquiry — engaging the tradition as a living competitor in contemporary moral discourse.

annotations.content: guides to excellent achievement within that particular craft. Every craft is informed by some conception of a finally perfected
annotations.anchor_text: _guides to excellent achievement within that particular craft_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the telos-language here — 'finally perfected' is MacIntyre's Aristotelian-Thomistic move, importing teleology into epistemology. The highlighting suggests Tom wants to track how MacIntyre smuggles metaphysics into what looks like a pragmatic account of craft-rationality. This is the point where MacIntyre's account is most distinctively Thomistic and least acceptable to his rivals.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry depends on this teleological structure: civic friendship and civility are not merely useful habits but point toward a 'finally perfected' form of human community — the highlighting tracks this as the theologically loaded moment in MacIntyre's otherwise historicist argument.

annotations.content: rationally justified as the best so far, in the light of those formulations of the relevant standards of achievement which are rationally justified as the best so far
annotations.anchor_text: _rationally justified as the best so far in the light_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the recursive, almost circular formulation MacIntyre uses — 'best so far' justified by 'best so far.' He may be flagging this either as a moment of philosophical elegance (coherentism about tradition) or as a potential vulnerability (the bootstrapping problem: how do you exit the circle?). The double highlighting of the repeated phrase suggests Tom noticed MacIntyre's deliberate repetition.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is precisely the point where RC must address the charge of traditionalist circularity — the project needs to show that the 'best so far' of classical civility traditions is not self-sealing but genuinely open to rational challenge, which MacIntyre claims but must demonstrate.

annotations.content: all schemes themselves are justified by their ability to do better than any rival competitors so far, both in organizing the experience of those who have up to this point made the craft what it is
annotations.anchor_text: _all schemes themselves are justified by their ability_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's anti-foundationalist payoff: even the deductive schemes of a science are justified not by first principles alone but by competitive performance against rivals. This is MacIntyre's most pragmatist-sounding moment, and Tom may be tracking it because it opens a dialogue with non-Thomistic interlocutors. The phrase 'those who have up to this point made the craft what it is' highlights the communal, not individual, nature of justification.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with civic friendship and the role of institutions in forming character connects here: the 'competitors so far' framing suggests that civility traditions must be tested in genuine civic competition, not merely asserted — this has implications for how RC presents its argument to secular audiences.

annotations.content: temporality
annotations.anchor_text: _authority is related to temporality in a way_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'temporality' as a technical term, flagging it as MacIntyre's explicit naming of the temporal structure of authority within a tradition. The circle isolates it from surrounding text — Tom wants to return to this word. It may be a cross-reference to the genealogist's different use of temporality (Nietzsche's genealogy also temporalizes authority, but destructively rather than constructively).
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's tripartite structure (encyclopaedist / genealogist / tradition) hinges on different relationships to time: the encyclopaedist is ahistorical, the genealogist uses history to unmask, and the tradition-bearer uses history constructively. Circling 'temporality' suggests Tom is tracking this structural difference as key to RC's argument.

annotations.content: temporal reference
annotations.anchor_text: _The temporal reference of reasoning within a craft thus differs_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'temporal reference' at the very end of the page — mirroring his circle of 'temporality' above. This creates a bracket around the entire page: it opens with authority's relationship to temporality and closes with the temporal reference of craft-reasoning. Tom is likely marking this as the page's structural arc, possibly to use this framing in RC writing about how tradition-based reasoning differs from both encyclopaedic and genealogical modes.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'temporal reference' of reasoning is what distinguishes tradition-based civility from both timeless natural law claims and genealogical deconstruction — RC needs this distinction to argue that recovering classical civility is neither reactionary nor naive, but a temporally-situated rational enterprise.

annotations.content: the company of others, to some of whose authority
annotations.anchor_text: _the company of others to some of whose authority_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this phrase, which sets up the contrast between the genealogist's resistance to authority and the tradition-bearer's acceptance of communal authority. The phrase 'company of others' has civic resonance — it suggests that rationality is not solitary but communal, which Tom may be flagging as a point of contact between MacIntyre and civic republican or Aristotelian political theory.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's theme of civic friendship is implicit here: rationality exercised in 'the company of others' is the epistemological correlate of the civic community RC wants to recover — knowledge and virtue are both formed in community, not in isolation.

pages.summary: MacIntyre contrasts the encyclopaedist's conception of rationality — timeless, universal, tradition-independent — with the rationality of a craft tradition, which is historically situated, inseparable from the particular tradition through which it was achieved, and yet still aims at a timeless, perfected formulation of its own standards. He argues that to share in craft rationality requires sharing in the contingencies of its history and finding oneself as a character in its enacted dramatic narrative. The page closes by introducing the authority of a master within a craft as something more than merely exemplifying best standards — it involves knowing how to go further and directing others toward further progress.

annotations.content: physics & historically because (eg IER → 150) (2) personally transformative or identity (3) staging contributions
annotations.anchor_text: _Too Many Thomisms? page 65_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is constructing a threefold typology for why one engages with a tradition — epistemically (because it is historically validated), personally (because it transforms identity), and practically (because it positions one as a contributor). The reference 'IER → 150' is likely a cross-reference to another text, possibly MacIntyre's 'Idea of an Educated Reason' or a similar work. Tom seems to be asking: what are the legitimate entry points into a tradition, and is mere intellectual assent enough?
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly maps onto RC's concern with how individuals are formed by and enter into traditions of civility — it distinguishes cognitive uptake from the deeper identity-transforming and participatory dimensions that RC holds are necessary for genuine moral community.

annotations.content: Deceiving? the → timeless
annotations.anchor_text: _rationality like truth is independent of time place_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging skepticism about the encyclopaedist's claim that rationality transcends time — asking whether this aspiration toward timelessness is itself a kind of deception or self-deception. The arrow suggests a movement or transition he is tracking, possibly that the encyclopaedist is pretending to arrive at timelessness while being historically conditioned.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's critique of Enlightenment rationalism — the encyclopaedist stance is precisely what RC identifies as producing civic fragmentation by stripping away the tradition-embedded contexts that make moral reasoning intelligible.

annotations.content: rationality like truth is independent of time, place, and historical circumstances
annotations.anchor_text: _rationality like truth is indepen-dent of time place_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this as the core claim of encyclopaedist rationality that the whole chapter is positioned against. This is the thesis MacIntyre is dismantling, and Tom marks it as such — it is the foil against which craft rationality will be defined.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's foundational argument is that this Enlightenment conception of reason — detached from tradition, place, and history — has eroded the shared moral frameworks necessary for civil life; highlighting this passage marks the problem RC is responding to.

annotations.content: tradition as a guide to truth
annotations.anchor_text: _what view entails is an exclusion of tradition_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles this phrase to isolate it as a positive thesis — tradition as epistemically authoritative, not merely culturally contingent. This is the pivot point: if tradition is excluded as a guide to truth by encyclopaedist rationality, then craft rationality must rehabilitate it. Tom is likely marking this for use as a constructive RC claim.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is central to RC's recovery project — the argument that tradition is not an obstacle to truth but a vehicle for it, which grounds RC's defense of inherited civic and moral practices against modernist dismissal.

annotations.content: Tocs guide to truth...
annotations.anchor_text: _exclusion of tradition as a guide to truth_
annotations.interpretation: Tom abbreviates 'tradition' as 'Tocs' (or possibly 'Trad') and notes 'guide to truth' — he is extracting a propositional claim from the passage for later use. The ellipsis suggests he sees this as an incomplete thought to be developed, possibly in connection with how a tradition adjudicates truth claims internally.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's epistemological foundation depends on this claim — that traditions carry within them criteria of truth adequate to their own enquiry, which is what distinguishes RC's approach from both relativism and Enlightenment universalism.

annotations.content: Gifford counterposed what he took to be the truths of morality to the forces of custom and tradition, which are on his view as likely to be or more likely to be obfuscating than illuminating
annotations.anchor_text: _Gifford counterposed what he took to be truths of morality_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights Gifford's position as a concrete historical instantiation of encyclopaedist anti-traditionalism — the view that custom obscures rather than transmits moral truth. This serves as a named example Tom can deploy to show that the encyclopaedist stance has real intellectual champions with real consequences.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC needs historical cases where the rejection of tradition in favor of abstract rationality produced moral impoverishment; Gifford here functions as a test case for what RC is arguing against.

annotations.content: By contrast, just because at any particular moment the rationality of a craft is justified by its history so far, which has made it what it is in that specific time, place, and set of historical circumstances, such rationality is inseparable from the tradition through which it was achieved
annotations.anchor_text: _By contrast just because at any particular moment_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this as the central constructive claim of the page — craft rationality is historically justified and tradition-embedded, not despite its claim to rationality but because of it. The 'By contrast' signals the structural pivot in MacIntyre's argument. Tom is marking the positive thesis he will build on.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the theoretical core RC draws on to argue that civility, civic friendship, and moral community are not irrational residues of custom but rational achievements of tradition — their authority is justified precisely by their history.

annotations.content: the rationality of a craft is justified by its history so far
annotations.anchor_text: _at any particular moment the rationality_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles this specific formulation to isolate it as a quotable thesis — the rationality-history nexus is the key conceptual move. The circle suggests this is a passage Tom intends to cite or to test against objections. He may be asking: what does 'justified by history so far' actually mean epistemically?
annotations.rc_relevance: RC must answer the relativist objection that tradition-based rationality is merely historically contingent; this formulation is MacIntyre's (and RC's) response — historical situatedness is the ground of rationality, not its undermining.

annotations.content: Rationality of craft cf 9th T-bound rationality
annotations.anchor_text: _rationality of a craft is justified by its history_
annotations.interpretation: Tom cross-references the 'rationality of craft' with 'the 9th' — almost certainly the Ninth Edition of the Encyclopaedia Britannica discussed earlier in the book — and coins or borrows the term 'T-bound rationality' (tradition-bound rationality) as a shorthand. This suggests Tom is building a conceptual vocabulary for RC that distinguishes types of rationality structurally.
annotations.rc_relevance: The concept of 'T-bound rationality' is directly useful for RC's argument that the rationality operative in civic traditions is not inferior to encyclopaedist rationality but differently structured — bound to particular histories and yet genuinely truth-tracking.

annotations.content: To share in the rationality of a craft requires sharing in the contingencies of its history, understanding its story as one's own, and finding a place for oneself as a character in the enacted dramatic narrative which is that story so far
annotations.anchor_text: _To share in the rationality of a craft requires sharing_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this as the participatory and narrative condition for belonging to a rational tradition — it is not merely intellectual assent but narrative self-insertion. This passage is doing significant work: it links rationality to identity, story, and character, which are all themes Tom's top margin note anticipated with his 'personally transformative or identity' category.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is foundational for RC's account of civic formation — civility cannot be taught as a set of abstract rules but must be inhabited as a narrative in which one finds oneself a character; this is what institutions, guilds, and civic communities do.

annotations.content: yet objectivity
annotations.anchor_text: _aims at timeless formulation of its own standards_
annotations.interpretation: Tom adds 'yet objectivity' as a counterweight to the historical situatedness just described — he is noting that MacIntyre does not collapse into relativism; craft rationality still aims at objective, timeless standards even if it approaches them historically. The 'yet' signals Tom tracking the dialectical tension MacIntyre is holding together.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is critical for RC's credibility — RC must show that tradition-based civility makes genuine objective claims about human flourishing, not merely local preferences; 'yet objectivity' is Tom's marker that this distinction must be preserved.

annotations.content: unlike the rationality of the encyclopaedic mode, understood as a historically situated rationality, even if one which aims at timeless formulation of its own standards which would be their final and perfected form through a series of successive reformulations, past and yet to come
annotations.anchor_text: _unlike the rationality of the encyclopaedic mode_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the full contrast statement that holds together historical situatedness and the aspiration to timeless perfection — this is MacIntyre's resolution of the apparent tension between tradition-bound and objective rationality. Tom is marking this as the passage that prevents craft rationality from collapsing into relativism.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's entire project depends on this move — it can claim that inherited traditions of civility are both historically particular and genuinely normative, aiming at perfected forms that transcend their moment; this is what distinguishes RC from mere conservatism.

annotations.content: eg SM
annotations.anchor_text: _successive reformulations past and yet to come_
annotations.interpretation: Tom annotates 'eg SM' — almost certainly 'for example, Scotus/MacIntyre' or more likely 'Summa' or a specific thinker whose name begins SM (possibly Servais Pinckaers, or Saint MacIntyre as a shorthand joke, or more likely a reference to a specific text). This suggests Tom sees a concrete historical case of this pattern of successive reformulation aiming at perfection.
annotations.rc_relevance: If SM refers to a Thomistic or scholastic example, this directly supports RC's argument that the Thomist tradition exemplifies the craft-rational model — it has undergone successive reformulations while maintaining continuity of purpose.

annotations.content: The authority of a master within a craft is both more and other than a matter of exemplifying the best standards so far. It is also and most importantly a matter of knowing how to go further and especially how to direct others towards going further
annotations.anchor_text: _The authority of a master within a craft_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets this entire passage to mark it as a discrete, important claim about the nature of authority within a tradition. The bracket suggests this is a unit of argument he wants to preserve intact — the definition of mastery as prospective and directive, not merely retrospective and exemplary.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is deeply concerned with the role of institutions and persons in forming civic character; the 'master' figure here is precisely what RC means by the kind of authority that civility requires — not authoritarian imposition but the authority of one who can direct others toward further flourishing.

annotations.content: authority of master Craftsune ↓
annotations.anchor_text: _The authority of a master within a craft_
annotations.interpretation: Tom notes 'authority of master Craftsune' with a downward arrow — 'Craftsune' appears to be Tom's shorthand for 'craftsman' or possibly a coined term combining craft and a suffix. The arrow suggests this theme continues below or on the next page. Tom is flagging this as a concept node to track.
annotations.rc_relevance: The authority of the master-craftsman is RC's model for the kind of authority that civic institutions and moral exemplars should exercise — not bureaucratic rule-enforcement but the guiding authority of one who embodies and can extend a living tradition.

annotations.content: masters: 1. exemplify best available stds 2. know how to go further 3. — how to direct others
annotations.anchor_text: _knowing how to go further and especially how to direct others_
annotations.interpretation: Tom produces a numbered list summarizing MacIntyre's implicit account of mastery: (1) exemplary standard-bearing, (2) prospective advancement, (3) pedagogical direction. The dash before item 3 may indicate he sees item 3 as the most important or most neglected. This is Tom doing constructive philosophical work — extracting a structured definition from MacIntyre's prose.
annotations.rc_relevance: This tripartite structure of mastery maps directly onto RC's account of civic and institutional leadership — RC argues that functional civic communities need figures who don't merely model civility but know how to advance it and can teach others to do so; this is a blueprint for RC's pedagogical dimension.

annotations.content: knowing how to go further and especially how to direct others towards going further
annotations.anchor_text: _most importantly a matter of knowing how to go further_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this to extract the specifically forward-looking and pedagogical dimension of mastery — this is the claim that distinguishes MacIntyre's account from mere conservatism or traditionalism. The master is not just a custodian but an agent of development.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC must address the objection that tradition-based civility is static or nostalgic; this underlined passage provides the theoretical resource to show that genuine mastery within a tradition is inherently progressive and generative.

pages.summary: MacIntyre contrasts three rival stances toward moral enquiry — the encyclopaedist, the genealogist, and the craft-tradition — by showing how each relates differently to authority, historical situatedness, and rationality. The genealogist (Nietzschean and post-Nietzschean) reads tradition as a mask for domination and Christianity as a purveyor of illusions, while the craft-tradition holds that reason-giving is genuinely truth-tracking even if historically situated. The page ends by identifying the genealogist's deepest target: not merely Christian ethics but the metaphysical realism — the view that the world is what it is independently of human willing — that underlies Jewish and Christian theism.

annotations.content: eg perfecting / listening
annotations.anchor_text: _telos of fully perfected work it is_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is glossing MacIntyre's notion of craft-authority with a concrete example or pair: 'perfecting' and 'listening.' This suggests he is thinking about how the transmission of craft-knowledge involves both an exemplary end-point (the perfected work) and a receptive disposition in the learner. He may be connecting this to pedagogical or formative practices relevant to civility.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's interest in how institutions form character: the craft-tradition model of authority requires a learner who listens and a master who embodies the telos — a model of moral formation quite different from Enlightenment autonomy.

annotations.content: and future that those with authority are able to draw upon tradition to interpret and reinterpret it, so that its directedness towards the telos of that particular craft becomes apparent in new and characteristically unexpected ways.
annotations.anchor_text: _those with authority are able to draw_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the dynamic, reinterpretive function of authority within tradition — authority is not static repetition but creative application of the tradition's telos to new circumstances. This is a key structural claim MacIntyre makes to distinguish craft-tradition from mere conservatism, and Tom recognizes it as load-bearing for the whole tripartite argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: Central to RC's 'tradition vs. reform' tension: this passage shows how tradition can be genuinely reforming without becoming genealogical critique — a distinction RC needs to make when arguing for the recovery of Thomistic civility in contemporary contexts.

annotations.content: genealogy on / authority
annotations.anchor_text: _The genealogist has no way of understanding_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is labeling the section's pivot: after describing craft-authority positively, MacIntyre now shows what the genealogist does with the concept of authority — namely, reduces it to domination. The marginal label is a structural marker helping Tom track the argument's dialectical movement between the three rival versions.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC must account for the genealogist's challenge to authority claims; this note flags where MacIntyre articulates that challenge most sharply, which is exactly the philosophical obstacle RC's recovery of civic tradition must overcome.

annotations.content: more way of allowing the past to subjugate the present. And the central symptom of the sickness of this type of social existence, from the genealogical standpoint, is that, despite its recognition of the historical situatedness of all reason-giving and reason-offering, it understands the truth to which it aspires as timeless
annotations.anchor_text: _more way of allowing the past to subjugate_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's precise articulation of the genealogist's internal critique of craft-tradition: the tradition claims historical situatedness but then claims timeless truth — which looks, from the genealogical standpoint, like bad faith. Tom likely sees this as the sharpest version of the genealogist's challenge, worth returning to when building RC's counter-argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the core epistemological challenge RC must address: if tradition-based moral enquiry claims timeless truth while being historically embedded, how does it avoid the genealogist's charge of self-deception? RC's recovery of Thomism depends on answering this.

annotations.content: Genealogy / vs / Christianity
annotations.anchor_text: _The conflicts between these three are sharply evident in their contrasting attitudes to Christian theology_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the structural shift to the theology subsection of the tripartite comparison. The vertical arrangement ('Genealogy / vs / Christianity') suggests he is treating this as a binary confrontation within the broader three-way argument, possibly because he finds this pairing most directly relevant to RC's engagement with Christian moral tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is explicitly invested in recovering Christian (especially Thomistic) moral resources; this label marks where MacIntyre stages that recovery's most direct philosophical antagonist — the Nietzschean genealogist.

annotations.content: SHLeg / central to / R+trad+n
annotations.anchor_text: _But the fundamental hostility remains and among those features_
annotations.interpretation: Tom appears to be cross-referencing a secondary literature source or a specific scholar (possibly 'SHLeg' as an abbreviation for a name or text) as central to understanding the 'R+trad+n' (reason-and-tradition) question. This suggests Tom is tracking how other commentators have located this passage in the broader MacIntyre debate, which is consistent with building an academic research project like RC.
annotations.rc_relevance: If 'SHLeg' refers to a scholar who has written on genealogy and tradition, this cross-reference indicates Tom is assembling a scholarly apparatus around MacIntyre's critique — exactly the kind of secondary engagement RC's academic dimension requires.

annotations.content: My B / want of independent / Realism
annotations.anchor_text: _For such theism has as its core the view that the world is what it is independently of human thinking and judging and desiring and willing_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is glossing the passage's definition of theistic metaphysical realism — that the world is independent of human willing — with what appears to be a personal thesis note ('My B' likely meaning 'My point B' or a thesis labeled B in Tom's own outline). 'Want of independent Realism' may mean Tom sees contemporary society as lacking this kind of realism, which is the RC project's diagnostic claim.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly connects to RC's diagnosis of social fragmentation: if modernity has abandoned metaphysical realism (the view that there is a fact of the matter about the good), then moral discourse degenerates into the kind of interminable conflict MacIntyre diagnoses. Tom is flagging this as a structural pillar of his own argument.

annotations.content: vs interactive realism, stable thngs
annotations.anchor_text: _independently of human thinking and judging and desiring_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has written and circled his own counter-concept — 'interactive realism, stable things' — in the bottom margin, positioning it against the static metaphysical realism MacIntyre attributes to theism. This suggests Tom is developing a modified realism that is neither purely Nietzschean (no stable reality) nor naively correspondence-theoretic, but something more dynamic. 'Stable things' may gesture toward Aristotelian substance or natural kinds.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is potentially Tom's most original theoretical move visible in these annotations: RC may be building toward a 'interactive realism' that preserves the objectivity needed for moral tradition while acknowledging the participatory, dialogical character of moral knowledge — avoiding both genealogical anti-realism and rigid metaphysical essentialism.

annotations.content: iterative / emerging creativity emerging / God's K as causal definitive
annotations.anchor_text: _there is a single true view of the world and of its ordering_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is developing a theological-philosophical gloss: 'iterative, emerging creativity' suggests a process-like or developmental ontology, while 'God's K as causal definitive' (K likely = Knowledge or perhaps Knowing) positions divine knowledge as the ground of objective order — not a static blueprint but a causally efficacious knowing that generates stable reality. This is sophisticated philosophical theology being sketched in the margin.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry may depend on this kind of refined theological realism: God's knowing as creative and causal (a Thomistic theme) provides the objective grounding for moral truth without falling into the rigid 'timeless truth' that the genealogist attacks as self-deceptive. Tom is sketching the metaphysical foundation RC needs.

annotations.content: But the fundamental hostility remains
annotations.anchor_text: _But the fundamental hostility remains and among_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as a thesis sentence — MacIntyre's insistence that despite Foucault's moderation of Nietzschean savagery, the genealogical project remains fundamentally hostile to tradition-based realism. Tom likely marks this to signal that RC cannot expect genealogical thinkers to be mollified by Foucault's measured tone; the underlying antagonism is structural.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC must reckon with this structural, not merely rhetorical, hostility: the project of recovering civility cannot simply appeal to genealogists on their own terms, because the disagreement runs to incompatible metaphysical commitments.

annotations.content: not only the moral aberration which, on the genealogical view, Christianity inherited from Judaism, but also the metaphysical realism of Jewish and Christian theism
annotations.anchor_text: _not only the moral aberration which on the genealogical view_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's specification of the genealogist's two-level target: first-order ethics AND its metaphysical grounding. This double underline signals Tom recognizes that the genealogical challenge cannot be met by moral argument alone — it requires a defense of realism itself.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC cannot rehabilitate civic virtue without also defending the metaphysical realism that makes objective goods possible; this annotation marks where MacIntyre shows the debate is ultimately ontological, not merely normative.

annotations.content: it is independently of human thinking and judging and desiring and willing. There is a single true view of the world and of its ordering
annotations.anchor_text: _independently of human thinking and judging and desiring_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the core definition of theistic realism that the genealogist attacks. Given his bottom-margin note about 'interactive realism' and 'emerging creativity,' Tom appears to be questioning whether this is the only or best formulation of realism available — he may think MacIntyre's formulation is too static and that RC needs a more dynamic alternative.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the precise claim Tom seems to want to revise or supplement with his 'interactive realism' concept — RC may argue that theistic realism need not be as static as MacIntyre's formulation implies, and that a more dynamic account can better withstand genealogical critique.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the genealogist's accusation cuts deeper than simply charging theism with falsity: the very conception of rationality, language, and realism that the encyclopaedist employs is itself covertly theological, such that abandoning God requires abandoning the grammar of realist thought (Nietzsche's point in Götzendämmerung). Consequently, the genealogical challenge cannot be met by a nonrational fideism (like Kierkegaard's) but required looking back to a tradition-directed understanding of philosophy as techné — specifically Aquinas's account of sapientia in the Summa Contra Gentiles and the Metaphysics, where wisdom is explained through a hierarchy of crafts ordered to higher ends.

annotations.content: criterion for truth
annotations.anchor_text: _for human judgments to be true and_
annotations.interpretation: Tom isolates the epistemological stakes of MacIntyre's opening claim: that human judgments require conformity with a divinely created order to be genuinely true. By labeling this 'criterion for truth,' Tom is flagging that MacIntyre is not merely making a theological point but an epistemological one — what counts as a standard for truth is itself contested between the three rival versions. This is a load-bearing premise Tom will likely return to when comparing encyclopaedist, genealogist, and Thomist truth-criteria.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project's concern with whether shared moral reasoning is possible across fragmented traditions hinges precisely on this question: is there a common criterion for truth, or is each tradition incommensurable? Tom is tracking where MacIntyre locates that criterion.

annotations.content: v Gvs gth on C.
annotations.anchor_text: _criterion for truth arrow pointing down_
annotations.interpretation: This is almost certainly an abbreviated cross-reference: 'v' = 'versus' or 'see,' 'Gvs' = 'Genealogist versus,' 'gth' possibly 'growth' or more likely 'Genealogist's thesis,' and 'on C.' = 'on Christianity.' Tom is noting that the arrow of argument runs from the criterion-for-truth claim downward to the genealogist's specific charge against Christianity. The abbreviation style suggests Tom is in rapid note-taking mode, tracking the logical sequence of MacIntyre's move from epistemology to the genealogist's accusation.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's tripartite structure (encyclopaedist / genealogist / tradition): Tom is mapping how the genealogist's critique of Christianity is grounded in a prior epistemological disagreement, which is central to understanding why MacIntyre thinks tradition-directed enquiry is the only adequate response.

annotations.content: conception of rationality, indeed the conception of language and its mode of application to the world pre-supposed by that conception, is itself theological
annotations.anchor_text: _It is also and more fundamentally that that_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights what is arguably the most explosive claim on the page: not just that theism is false (genealogist's weak charge) but that the realist grammar underwriting encyclopaedist rationality is itself theological. Tom's highlighting here suggests he sees this as the crux of MacIntyre's argument — the point at which the genealogist becomes an unwitting ally of the Thomist by exposing the hidden theological commitments of secular reason.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's engagement with MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism: if secular rationality is covertly theological, then the Enlightenment project of neutral public reason is incoherent, which bears on RC's argument for tradition-embedded civic virtue.

annotations.content: we are not getting rid of God because we still believe in grammar
annotations.anchor_text: _I fear wrote Nietzsche in Götzendämmerung_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the Nietzsche quotation, which MacIntyre uses as the genealogist's own self-critique. By marking this, Tom signals he recognizes this as a pivotal dialectical move: MacIntyre is using Nietzsche against the genealogist's moderate versions — if you keep realist grammar, you keep God whether you like it or not. Tom may be noting the irony that the genealogist's own most radical thinker undermines the halfway position.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this passage supports the argument that attempts to ground civic discourse in purely secular, grammatically realist terms are philosophically unstable — a point relevant to RC's critique of liberal proceduralism.

annotations.content: the genealogical accusation is not just that theism is in part false because it requires the truth of realism, but that realism is inherently theistic
annotations.anchor_text: _So the genealogical accusation is a logic of identity_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's formal restatement of the genealogist's sharpened charge. The highlighting of the full logical structure ('not just… but that') shows Tom tracking MacIntyre's argumentative precision — this is a reversal: realism entails theism, not merely the other way around. Tom appears to be mapping the logical dependencies between the positions.
annotations.rc_relevance: This highlights a tension RC must address: if realism is inherently theistic, can RC's appeal to shared moral community be made intelligible to non-theist interlocutors, or must RC embrace an explicitly theological grounding for civic friendship?

annotations.content: which M seems to share?
annotations.anchor_text: _that realism is inherently theistic_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is asking whether MacIntyre himself endorses the claim that realism is inherently theistic, or merely reports it as the genealogist's accusation. The question mark signals genuine interpretive uncertainty: is MacIntyre agreeing that realism requires theism (which would be a strong Thomist commitment) or is he merely presenting the genealogist's stronger version of the critique? This is a sophisticated reader's question about the difference between MacIntyre's dialectical method and his own first-order commitments.
annotations.rc_relevance: This question is critical for RC: if MacIntyre shares the view that realism is inherently theistic, then RC's Thomistic framework must be explicitly theistic — it cannot be presented as a neutral philosophical option available to secular civic participants.

annotations.content: It is therefore unsurprising that some theologians of the nineteenth century, whose allegiance required them neither to constrict and constrain their creedal affirmations and their theological understanding of those affirmations within the limits imposed by the post-Kantian framework of the nineteenth century encyclopaedist nor to confront the challenge of the genealogist by a nonrational fideism

The bracket isolates the structural condition MacIntyre is identifying — a third path. Tom is likely marking this as the pivot point in the argument where MacIntyre introduces the Thomistic alternative as neither encyclopaedist nor Kierkegaardian.
annotations.rc_relevance: This bracket marks the structural moment where RC's preferred alternative — tradition-directed Thomism — is introduced as superior to both liberal rationalism and irrationalist responses to modernity, directly relevant to RC's constructive program.

annotations.content: Kierkegaard

This is not merely a reference identification — Tom is noting that MacIntyre is implicitly criticizing Kierkegaard's leap-of-faith response as inadequate to the genealogist's challenge. It shows Tom is reading MacIntyre's historical argument as a structured elimination of alternatives.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this is significant: it rules out a fideist grounding for civic virtue, confirming that RC's Thomistic approach must be rationally articulable and tradition-embedded rather than a leap of faith — which has implications for how RC addresses pluralist audiences.

annotations.content: should have looked back
annotations.anchor_text: _should have looked back as Kleutgen did_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles the key prescriptive verb phrase 'should have looked back,' which is MacIntyre's normative judgment about what nineteenth-century theologians ought to have done. The circle isolates this as a moment where MacIntyre steps from historical description into prescriptive argument — this is MacIntyre's thesis in miniature. Tom is marking the argumentative gear-shift from analysis to normative recommendation.
annotations.rc_relevance: This circles the heart of RC's methodological commitment: the recovery project ('looking back') to pre-modern traditions is not nostalgia but a rational necessity — precisely the move MacIntyre endorses here and that RC seeks to operationalize for contemporary civic discourse.

annotations.content: What sapientia is is there explained in terms of the hierarchy of crafts
annotations.anchor_text: _sapientia is there explained in terms of_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the introduction of the techné/sapientia framework, which will carry MacIntyre's positive argument about tradition-directed philosophy. By highlighting at this point, Tom marks the transition from critique to construction — MacIntyre is now grounding the Thomistic alternative in a specific account of wisdom as ordered within a hierarchy of practices. This is the beginning of the positive case.
annotations.rc_relevance: The hierarchy of crafts and sapientia is central to RC's account of how institutions form character and how subsidiarity works: practical wisdom is embedded in ordered practices, not abstract principles — a key RC theme about the role of institutions in moral formation.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the 'whole epistemological turn' of post-Cartesian philosophy is the outcome of a mistake — that the philosophy of craft-tradition exposes epistemology as secondary to the objects of knowledge, not primary. He then transitions to showing how late nineteenth-century theologians, unaware of the genealogical challenge, were nonetheless forced to respond to repeated attempts within Christian denominations to reshape doctrine to fit post-Enlightenment encyclopaedist culture, which provoked a variety of theological restatements — notably those of Kierkegaard and Newman.

The note suggests Tom is tracking how MacIntyre's three rival versions map onto epistemological commitments, not merely onto institutional or historical positions.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'philosophy of craft-tradition' is central to RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry — it grounds knowledge in practice and formation within a living tradition, which RC positions against Enlightenment-derived fragmentation.

annotations.content: Too Many Thomisms? (M.K is lock&key, vs hand&glove) (new) old model of catalysis CKM: Kes catalysis katalysis

The 'old model of catalysis' and the Greek term 'katalysis' (dissolution/breaking down) versus 'catalysis' (enabling reaction) suggest Tom is thinking about whether Thomism should be understood as dissolving modernity's errors or as fitting into its framework. annotations.rc_relevance: This note directly engages RC's tension between tradition vs. reform — Tom is interrogating which model of Thomism RC should adopt: one that catalyzes new synthesis or one that dissolves inadequate modern frameworks.

'Phil/Theo' signals the philosophy-theology boundary, which is exactly what MacIntyre is probing here. Tom is cross-referencing this page to a specific institutional or pedagogical context where this boundary is contested.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is concerned with the institutional formation of character and the role of philosophy-theology integration in civic and moral education — this note flags that MacIntyre's argument has direct application to that curricular or institutional debate.

annotations.content: the philosophy of craft-tradition presented the mind as inadequate until it had conformed itself to the object which theology presented for its attention

annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this as MacIntyre's clearest statement of how craft-tradition epistemology inverts the encyclopaedist priority: the mind conforms to reality (including theologically-disclosed reality), not vice versa. This is the core realist claim Tom wants to anchor for RC.
annotations.rc_relevance: This inverts the Enlightenment autonomy of reason that RC critiques — it positions the knower as formed by an object-ordered tradition, not as a self-grounding rational subject, which is foundational to RC's virtue-formation argument.

annotations.content: for the philosophy of craft-tradition, knowledge is a secondary phenomenon not to be understood in the light of the objects of knowledge and not vice versa
annotations.anchor_text: _knowledge is a secondary phenomenon not to be understood_
