The highlighting suggests he wants to use this exact phrasing, possibly in writing or teaching. The careful underlining within the highlight (visible in image) shows he is distinguishing the logical structure of the claim.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the anti-encyclopaedist epistemological claim RC depends on — knowledge is object-ordered and tradition-embedded, not a neutral method applied from outside. RC uses this to argue against purely procedural or liberal accounts of civic rationality.

annotations.content: epistemological turn
annotations.anchor_text: _The whole epistemological turn of philosophy is thus_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'epistemological turn' as a key term of art — MacIntyre is naming and critiquing a defining feature of post-Cartesian philosophy. The circle isolates the term for conceptual tracking, suggesting Tom may be building a glossary or mapping MacIntyre's critical vocabulary.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's critique of modernity hinges on identifying the epistemological turn as the source of moral fragmentation — circling this term suggests Tom sees it as a pivot point in the argument he will return to.

annotations.content: The whole epistemological turn of philosophy is thus the outcome of a mistake
annotations.anchor_text: _The whole epistemological turn of philosophy is thus_
annotations.interpretation: This is one of MacIntyre's most provocative claims and Tom marks it prominently. The highlight combined with the circle of 'epistemological turn' shows Tom registering this as a thesis statement, not merely a transitional remark. Tom likely sees this as the page's argumentative climax.
annotations.rc_relevance: If the epistemological turn is a mistake, then the entire post-Enlightenment framework RC is responding to is built on error — this gives RC's recovery project a diagnostic foundation, not merely a nostalgic one.

annotations.content: Where it itself is deeply at odds with the central theses of genealogy is, as I remarked earlier, over the genealogical reading of the history of philosophy in which post-Cartesian and post-Kantian philosophers are treated as if they were the genuine descendants of Socrates, Plato, Aristotle, Augustine, and Aquinas and in which the epistemological concerns of post-Cartesian philosophy are misleadingly projected back on to ancient and medieval thought.
annotations.anchor_text: _Where it itself is deeply at odds with the central theses_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets this passage to mark MacIntyre's differentiation of craft-tradition from genealogy — both reject encyclopaedism, but for different reasons. The bracket signals that Tom wants to preserve this distinction carefully: the craft-tradition critique of genealogy is that genealogy's historical narrative is itself distorted by the very anachronism it claims to expose.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC must navigate between genealogical critique (which RC finds useful for diagnosing modernity) and the tradition-based recovery MacIntyre advocates — this bracket shows Tom tracking exactly where those two moves diverge.

annotations.anchor_text: _as I remarked earlier, over the genealogical reading_
annotations.interpretation: A cross-reference to page 58, where MacIntyre presumably first introduced the distinction between craft-tradition and genealogy on this point. Tom is building a textual map of the argument's internal cross-references, likely for citation or lecture use.
annotations.rc_relevance: Tracking MacIntyre's internal argument structure helps Tom identify where the tradition vs. genealogy distinction is first established — crucial for RC's use of MacIntyre as a methodological resource.

annotations.content: But they could not but be responsive to the recurrent attempts within every major Christian denomination to reshape and to diminish central Christian doctrine in a way that would make it acceptable to post-Enlightenment culture, the culture of the encyclopaedia.
annotations.anchor_text: _But they could not but be responsive to the recurrent attempts_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the descriptive sociology here — MacIntyre is identifying a structural pressure on theology from encyclopaedist culture. Tom is likely noting the parallel to contemporary pressures on Christian institutions, and the word 'recurrent' is important: these are not one-time crises but systemic patterns.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is directly concerned with how institutions (churches, universities, civic bodies) either resist or capitulate to post-Enlightenment cultural pressure — this passage names the mechanism RC must address in its institutional reform proposals.

annotations.content: these recurrent attempts evoked a variety of theological restatements, of which Kierkegaard's and Newman's were among the most notable
annotations.anchor_text: _evoked a variety of theological restatements, of which Kierkegaard's_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks Kierkegaard and Newman as exemplary respondents to encyclopaedist pressure. This is a resource note — Tom is identifying figures RC might draw on. The juxtaposition of Kierkegaard (existentialist response) and Newman (development-of-doctrine response) suggests Tom is interested in the range of strategies available.
annotations.rc_relevance: Newman's model of doctrinal development and Kierkegaard's attack on Christendom-as-institution are both relevant to RC's question of how tradition renews itself without capitulating to modernity — Tom is building a comparative toolkit.

annotations.content: is it interested in realism such an attempt?
annotations.anchor_text: _evoked a variety of theological restatements_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is asking whether the theological restatements MacIntyre describes are genuinely committed to realism (in the metaphysical and epistemological sense) or are merely apologetic accommodations. The question mark signals genuine uncertainty — Tom is not sure MacIntyre has adequately distinguished realist restatements from merely rhetorical ones. 'Such an attempt' likely refers to one of the named restatements (Newman? Kierkegaard?) and whether it qualifies as truly within the craft-tradition's realist epistemology.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery project depends on identifying restatements of tradition that are genuinely realist — not merely culturally adaptive. Tom's question here is whether the historical models MacIntyre cites actually meet that bar, which is a methodological question RC must answer.

pages.summary: MacIntyre traces how Antonio Rosmini Serbati (1797–1855) attempted to defend Augustinian and Thomistic positions against Kantian critics, but in doing so absorbed substantial Kantian structures into his own system. Rosmini's central thesis—that our knowledge consists in more than awareness of particulars because we bring an a priori universal idea of being to understanding—locates the idea of being as neither a product of nor property of the knowing subject, but as a 'given' whose givenness must be referred to God's action. The page argues that Rosmini's project ultimately failed on its own terms: either he failed philosophically by introducing intuitive apprehension of God into Kantian structures, or he failed theologically by making God immanent within Kantian cognitive structures.

annotations.content: Rosmini
annotations.anchor_text: _Rosmini Serbati (1797–1855). Rosmini had been_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging Rosmini as a named figure worth tracking—a proper-noun anchor in the margin to help locate this thinker quickly on re-reading. The placement suggests Tom did not know or want to remember Rosmini's full name and is using the margin as an index entry. It may also signal that Rosmini is being introduced here for the first time in the chapter and Tom wants to mark that introduction structurally.
annotations.rc_relevance: Rosmini represents a failed attempt to reconcile tradition (Thomism/Augustinianism) with Enlightenment rationalism (Kant)—a central RC tension between inherited moral frameworks and modernizing reform. Tom may be assessing whether Rosmini's failure is instructive for the RC project's own effort to recover Thomistic enquiry without capitulating to liberal epistemological frameworks.

annotations.content: central Christian positions for apologetic purposes
annotations.anchor_text: _too much of the ground of debate to their opponents_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is marking the diagnostic claim that Catholic thinkers ceded ground by adopting apologetic strategy over philosophical integrity. The underline isolates the phrase as a key structural diagnosis MacIntyre is making about a recurring failure mode—subordinating truth-claims to rhetorical effectiveness.
annotations.rc_relevance: This maps directly onto RC's concern with institutions that compromise their formative character in order to appear relevant—a pattern MacIntyre identifies as the encyclopaedist's corruption of tradition-based enquiry.

annotations.content: The most important of the Catholic philosophers against whom they directed this rereading of Aquinas was Antonio Rosmini Serbati
annotations.anchor_text: _most important of the Catholic philosophers against_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is underlining the superlative claim—'most important'—which signals that MacIntyre is making a strong historical-philosophical ranking, not merely listing figures. Tom may be noting this to track whether MacIntyre's judgment is later vindicated or complicated, and to mark Rosmini as the chapter's primary case study for failed Thomistic synthesis.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is interested in which historical figures successfully recovered Thomistic moral enquiry and which failed; marking Rosmini as 'most important' among the failures helps Tom build the genealogy of attempted recoveries that the RC project must reckon with.

annotations.content: he genuinely believed to be Augustinian and sometimes Thomistic positions
annotations.anchor_text: _ing and defending what he genuinely believed to be_
annotations.interpretation: The word 'genuinely' is likely what drew Tom's underline—MacIntyre is attributing sincere belief to Rosmini while simultaneously implying those beliefs were mistaken or distorted. Tom may be flagging the gap between authentic intention and philosophical outcome as a structurally important move in MacIntyre's argument about how traditions can be corrupted from within by sincere actors.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's theme of tradition vs. reform: genuine allegiance to a tradition does not immunize a thinker from distorting it. Tom may be using this as a cautionary model for RC's own constructive proposals.

annotations.content: his central enterprise was to vindicate theology against its Kantian critics, he absorbed into his own system a good deal of Kant
annotations.anchor_text: _was to vindicate theology against its Kantian critics_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the irony MacIntyre is exposing: the very act of engaging Kantian critics on their own terms caused Rosmini to internalize Kantian structures. This is a classic MacIntyrean move—showing how a tradition's attempt to defend itself by adopting the rival's framework constitutes a form of self-defeat. Tom is likely tracking this as a structural argument about philosophical method.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the core RC tension between tradition and Enlightenment rationalism: engaging modernity on its own terms risks importing its assumptions. MacIntyre's critique of Rosmini is a template for understanding how civic and moral traditions are colonized by encyclopaedist epistemology.

annotations.content: distorted those older positions by reworking them in Kantian terms
annotations.anchor_text: _tingly, distorted those older positions by reworking_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the conclusion of MacIntyre's diagnosis of Rosmini—the word 'distorted' is the evaluative verdict. Tom is likely noting this as the payoff of the preceding analysis and perhaps flagging whether MacIntyre is being fair to Rosmini or is overdetermining the failure.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery project must avoid the same distortion—reworking classical virtue ethics or Thomistic categories in terms acceptable to liberal or proceduralist frameworks. This underline may be a warning Tom is cataloguing for RC's methodological commitments.

annotations.content: Rosmini's central thesis was that our knowledge consists in more than an awareness of particulars, because we bring to the understanding of those particulars the a priori and universal idea of being
annotations.anchor_text: _Rosmini's central thesis was that our knowledge_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the full statement of Rosmini's positive epistemological thesis—this is the philosophical content being evaluated, not just a diagnostic claim. Tom is tracking exactly what Rosmini argued in order to assess MacIntyre's subsequent critique. The underlining of the complete thesis suggests Tom wants a precise record of the claim being adjudicated.
annotations.rc_relevance: Rosmini's a priori idea of being is relevant to RC's interest in what kinds of pre-rational or trans-rational endowments ground moral community—whether MacIntyre's critique of Rosmini's 'given' has implications for RC's own appeal to natural law or common moral intuitions.

annotations.content: the idea of being is itself neither a product of, nor primarily a property of, the knowing subject, empirical or transcendental
annotations.anchor_text: _idea of being is itself neither a product of_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks Rosmini's crucial anti-Kantian move: the idea of being is not generated by the subject. This is the hinge of Rosmini's argument and Tom appears to be tracking it carefully—likely to see whether MacIntyre grants this point or argues it still fails to escape Kantian structures.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's interest in whether moral knowledge is constructed by rational agents or received from tradition, community, or divine givenness—a question about the sources of moral authority that runs through MacIntyre's entire project.

annotations.content: All ideas, except for the idea of being, are formed by abstraction
annotations.anchor_text: _ject, empirical or transcendental. All ideas, except_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines Rosmini's universal claim about abstraction with the single exception of the idea of being. The underlining isolates this as a logically precise and falsifiable claim—Tom may be noting it as either the strongest or most vulnerable point in Rosmini's system.
annotations.rc_relevance: The exceptionalism of the idea of being in Rosmini's system parallels debates in RC about whether there are moral truths that cannot be derived from social practice but must be received—a question about the limits of immanent critique within traditions.

annotations.content: Only the idea of being is not and cannot have been so formed; it is a given and its givenness is such that it must be referred to the action of God
annotations.anchor_text: _Only the idea of being is not and cannot have been_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the theological conclusion of Rosmini's epistemology—the move from 'given' to 'God as giver.' This is the moment where Rosmini's philosophical argument becomes theological, and it is precisely the move MacIntyre will argue is either philosophically illegitimate (by Kantian standards) or theologically compromised (by introducing God into a Kantian structure). Tom is marking the crux.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the RC question of whether moral and civic order requires a theological grounding or can be sustained immanently—Rosmini's attempt to ground the idea of being in God's action is a historical test case for that question, and its failure (per MacIntyre) is a data point for RC's constructive work.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that Rosmini's attempt to reconcile Catholic theology with post-Kantian modernity failed on both fronts: by accommodating Kantian philosophical standards, his thought ceased to be authentically Catholic, and by remaining Catholic, it failed to meet modern philosophical standards. This double failure made Rosmini a forerunner of Catholic modernism and much fashionable post-Vatican II Catholic thought. The Encyclopaedia Britannica's sympathetic treatment of Rosmini (unlike its hostile treatment of Aquinas and Scholasticism) is explained by his Kantian affinities, which aligned him with the encyclopaedist project.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the neo-Thomist revival predates Leo XIII's 1879 encyclical Aeterni Patris by roughly thirty years, tracing it through a network of Jesuit educators — particularly Taparelli d'Azeglio, Serafino Sordi, and the Pecci brothers — whose institutional influence in Perugia and Rome quietly rehabilitated Aquinas against the dominant post-Kantian idealism. The political success of Thomism under Leo XIII was, in an important sense, premature relative to its intellectual consolidation. This genealogy of institutional formation demonstrates that traditions are carried and transmitted through concrete persons and educational structures, not merely through texts or official decrees.

annotations.rc_relevance: This speaks directly to RC's concern with institutions as sites of tradition-formation and tradition-suppression; the tension between post-Kantian rationalism and Thomistic moral enquiry maps onto MacIntyre's encyclopaedist vs. tradition tripartite structure.

annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's core tension between tradition and reform: MacIntyre is warning that premature institutional victories can distort or freeze a tradition before it has fully worked out its own internal coherence — a lesson for any contemporary project of moral recovery.

pages.summary: MacIntyre contrasts Aquinas's and Kleutgen's understandings of the Thomistic tradition: Aquinas treats enquiry as an ongoing, collaborative, historically embedded enterprise — drawing on Augustinian and Aristotelian sources toward a single continuing enquiry — whereas Kleutgen misreads Aquinas through a Suarezian lens, presenting him as offering a finished, self-contained system. The deeper philosophical consequence is that Suarez's epistemology severs the mind's apprehension of universals from actual singular existence, a problem Aquinas did not have because his account of truth was embedded in a living tradition of enquiry rather than a static system.

annotations.content: mind moves to the achievement of that truth which completes the act and, in returning to itself, reflects upon and understands the principle informing that movement.

annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with how institutions and practices form character maps directly onto this reflexive, activity-based account of the mind. Aquinas's truth-as-achievement supports RC's claim that civic virtue is cultivated through participation in living traditions, not deduced from abstract principles.

annotations.content: The work is one of conceptual clarification, analysis, and description, not at all one of epistemological justification.
annotations.anchor_text: _work is one of conceptual clarification analysis_

Tom likely highlights this because it is MacIntyre's sharpest counter to the encyclopaedist misreading of Aquinas, and because it distinguishes the Thomistic mode of enquiry from the epistemological project Kleutgen and Suarez impose on it.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's critique of Enlightenment rationalism depends on this distinction: the encyclopaedist tradition demands epistemological justification from a neutral standpoint, while Thomistic enquiry operates within a tradition of conceptual clarification. This passage is a key textual anchor for RC's argument.

annotations.content: draw upon the resources of, to correct and to modify, and to integrate into Aquinas's own account the various relevant arguments and considerations advanced by a variety of writers within the Augustinian and the Aristotelian traditions, now understood as contributing to a single ongoing enterprise of enquiry.

The phrase 'single ongoing enterprise of enquiry' is MacIntyre's definition of what a living tradition looks like from the inside. Tom is almost certainly marking this as the positive model — the thing Kleutgen fails to see — and as a definition he will need to return to.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the RC project's ideal of tradition in action: plural voices (Augustinian, Aristotelian) unified by a shared telos of enquiry. It directly supports RC's argument that civic friendship and subsidiarity require participation in a tradition understood this way, not submission to a fixed system.

annotations.content: Kleutgen instead treats Aquinas as presenting a finished system whose indebtedness to earlier writers is no more than an accidental feature of it.
annotations.anchor_text: _Kleutgen instead treats Aquinas as presenting_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the pivot sentence that introduces Kleutgen's error. The word 'instead' is doing crucial work — MacIntyre is contrasting two reading strategies, and Tom marks the moment the contrast is made explicit. This is a structurally pivotal sentence and Tom likely wants it as a quotable summary of the Kleutgen problem.
annotations.rc_relevance: The Kleutgen error is paradigmatic of what RC calls the encyclopaedist misreading of tradition: treating inherited wisdom as a closed system strips it of its formative, civic power. This sentence crystallizes the failure mode RC is arguing against.

annotations.content: Red asterisk or fleur-de-lis symbol in left margin
annotations.anchor_text: _than Aquinas. For Suarez the mind in apprehending_
annotations.interpretation: The red symbol (asterisk or stylized mark) beside the Suarez passage signals that Tom considers this a high-priority passage — possibly the most important on the page. It marks the moment MacIntyre names the Suarezian epistemology explicitly and begins to describe its structure. The use of a distinct red symbol rather than simple underlining suggests Tom uses a hierarchy of annotation marks, and this is a top-tier flag.
annotations.rc_relevance: Suarez's epistemology — where universal concepts mediate apprehension of particulars — is the philosophical root of the problem RC diagnoses in modern moral discourse: the loss of contact with actual communities and singular persons in favor of abstract categories. This mark flags the genealogy of that problem.

annotations.content: For Suarez the mind in apprehending necessary truths about possible essences apprehends what may, but need not, exist.
annotations.anchor_text: _For Suarez the mind in apprehending necessary truths_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this compressed but devastating description of Suarezian essentialism: the mind grasps possible essences that need not correspond to existing things. This is the epistemological move MacIntyre identifies as corrupting the Thomistic tradition. Tom is tracking this as the precise philosophical error that severs universality from singular existence — the question MacIntyre raises at the bottom of the page.
annotations.rc_relevance: Suarez's severance of essence from existence parallels the RC diagnosis of how modern moral philosophy loses contact with the particular communities, persons, and practices that give moral life its content. This is a philosophical root of social fragmentation.

annotations.content: a hive mind... in progress...
annotations.anchor_text: _understood as contributing to a single ongoing enterprise of enquiry_
annotations.interpretation: Tom glosses MacIntyre's 'single ongoing enterprise of enquiry' with the phrase 'a hive mind... in progress...' — a striking contemporary metaphor for distributed, collaborative intelligence working toward a shared goal. The ellipses suggest Tom is thinking this through rather than asserting a finished interpretation. He is translating MacIntyre's dense philosophical language into a communicable image, possibly for use in RC presentations or writing. The 'hive mind' metaphor also implicitly critiques individualist epistemology — no single agent owns the enquiry.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'hive mind in progress' gloss is Tom's attempt to make the tradition-as-enquiry model accessible for RC's audience. It maps onto RC's subsidiarity theme: collective wisdom emerges from distributed, locally embedded contributions — not from a single authoritative system or a neutral universal standpoint.

annotations.content: What relates our apprehension of possibility and universality to actual singular existence?
annotations.anchor_text: _what relates our apprehension of possibility and universality_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets MacIntyre's rhetorical question — the philosophical problem that Suarez leaves open. The bracket isolates this as the crux of the epistemological difficulty: how does the mind's grasp of universals connect to real singular existing things? Tom is flagging this as an unresolved problem that MacIntyre is about to address, and possibly as a question he wants to bring into the RC framework explicitly.
annotations.rc_relevance: This question is the philosophical nerve of RC's concern with abstraction vs. embodied community. The failure to answer it adequately produces moral frameworks that cannot account for the particular persons, places, and practices that constitute civic life — exactly the fragmentation RC diagnoses.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the reception of Aeterni Patris was fatally shaped by Kleutgen's prior epistemologizing of Thomism: those who responded to the encyclical followed Kleutgen in making epistemological concerns central, thereby dooming Thomism to the same fragmentation that afflicts all post-Cartesian philosophies — the indefinite multiplication of disagreement. Giovanni Cornoldi (1822–1892) had already anticipated this danger by contrasting Thomism's integrative unity with the falling-apart of modern philosophy from Descartes onward. MacIntyre's verdict is that Thomism, by epistemologizing itself after Aeterni Patris, unwittingly re-enacted the very disagreements it was meant to overcome, generating multiple rival systematic Thomisms each contending with different erroneous tendencies.

annotations.content: the need to make a transition from apprehensions of essence to judgments of particular existence within his system is one which it is all too easy to interpret as requiring Cartesian foundations
annotations.anchor_text: _apprehensions of essence to judgments of particular existence_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the precise diagnostic claim: the Thomistic move from essence to particular existence was misread as requiring a Cartesian epistemological foundation. This is the hinge on which the whole argument turns — a structural misreading that infected subsequent Thomism. Tom is tracking how a philosophical error at the level of interpretation (not doctrine) produced institutional consequences.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects directly to the RC theme of tradition vs. reform: the corruption of the Thomistic tradition did not come from outside attack but from an internal misreading, a warning for any project attempting to recover classical moral resources.

annotations.content: It was a mark of the unusual philosophical ingenuity of Kleutgen that, having first misidentified Aquinas's central positions with those of Suarez, thus opening up a kind of epistemological question for which there is no place within Aquinas's own scheme of thought, he went on to supply an epistemological answer to that question by reading into texts in De Veritate an epistemological argument which was not in fact there.
annotations.anchor_text: _unusual philosophical ingenuity of Kleutgen_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks this as a key indictment of Kleutgen: his 'ingenuity' was actually a double error — misidentification followed by creative fabrication. The irony MacIntyre intends (calling it 'unusual philosophical ingenuity') is not lost on Tom. He is likely noting that institutional authority (Jesuit influence, papal reception) amplified a philosophical mistake into a tradition-defining error.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this illustrates how institutions can canonize distortions — a cautionary note about the relationship between institutional formation of character and the accuracy of the tradition being transmitted, a tension central to the subsidiarity theme.

annotations.content: Yet those who responded to Aeterni Patris all too often followed Kleutgen in making epistemological concerns central to their Thomism. And in so doing they doomed Thomism to the fate of all philosophies which give priority to epistemological questions: the indefinite multiplication of disagreement.
annotations.anchor_text: _doomed Thomism to the fate of all philosophies_
annotations.interpretation: This is perhaps the most heavily marked claim on the page — Tom is flagging MacIntyre's central thesis about epistemology-first philosophies. The phrase 'indefinite multiplication of disagreement' is a technical verdict that explains why there are 'too many Thomisms.' Tom likely sees this as the argumentative payoff of the chapter and possibly the book's core diagnostic.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the heart of MacIntyre's genealogical critique of Enlightenment rationalism as applied to Thomism itself — directly relevant to the RC tripartite structure (encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition) since epistemologized Thomism falls into the encyclopaedist trap MacIntyre diagnoses.

annotations.content: Yet those who responded to Aeterni Patris all too often followed Kleutgen in making epistemological concerns central to their Thomism. And in so doing they doomed Thomism to the fate of all philosophies which give priority to epistemological questions: the indefinite multiplication of disagreement.
annotations.anchor_text: _doomed Thomism to the fate of all philosophies_
annotations.interpretation: The bracket reinforces the highlight on the same passage, suggesting Tom flagged this with two independent marks — likely on separate reading occasions or to signal this passage deserves extraction for direct quotation in RC materials. The double-marking (highlight + bracket) is a strong signal of importance.
annotations.rc_relevance: The bracketed passage is the most quotable formulation of MacIntyre's argument about how traditions self-destruct by capitulating to alien epistemological frameworks — a key reference point for RC's engagement with MacIntyre's critique of modernity.

annotations.content: Cornoldi
annotations.anchor_text: _Giovanni Cornoldi 1822–1892 yet another Thomist Jesuit_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles the name Cornoldi, likely to flag him as a figure needing follow-up research or cross-reference. Cornoldi is introduced as someone who 'unwittingly prophetic' anticipated the problem — his name-circling suggests Tom wants to investigate whether Cornoldi's pre-Aeterni Patris diagnosis offers a version of Thomism less contaminated by Kleutgen's epistemologizing.
annotations.rc_relevance: If Cornoldi diagnosed the problem before it fully manifested, he might represent an internal corrective voice within the tradition — relevant to RC's interest in whether traditions carry resources for self-correction without abandoning their substance.

annotations.content: contrasting the unity of Thomist thought, its ability to integrate disparate elements within itself, with the falling apart into contention of those disparate elements in the history of philosophy from Descartes onwards
annotations.anchor_text: _unity of Thomist thought its ability to integrate disparate elements_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines Cornoldi's contrast between Thomist integrative unity and post-Cartesian fragmentation. This is a positive claim about what authentic Thomism does — it integrates rather than multiplies disagreement. Tom is tracking the normative standard against which epistemologized Thomism is found wanting, and this formulation may serve as a positive model for RC.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'integration of disparate elements' maps directly onto RC's theme of civic friendship and subsidiarity — the idea that a living tradition holds together what modernity tears apart, whether in philosophy or in social institutions.

annotations.content: Cornoldi was unwittingly prophetic. Thomism, by epistemologizing itself after Aeterni Patris, proceeded to reenact the disagreements of post-Cartesian philosophy.
annotations.anchor_text: _Cornoldi was unwittingly prophetic_
annotations.interpretation: The underlining of 'unwittingly prophetic' and the follow-on verdict captures MacIntyre's ironic historical judgment: Cornoldi praised Thomism for what it would soon lose. Tom is marking both the rhetorical force and the historical irony — the tradition diagnosed its own future failure without knowing it.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this is a model of how traditions can carry warnings about their own deformation — relevant to the question of whether contemporary projects of moral recovery can avoid repeating the same epistemological capitulation MacIntyre diagnoses.

annotations.content: ['s rep / enct of / leprecy / s4t.
annotations.anchor_text: _doomed Thomism to the fate of all philosophies_
annotations.interpretation: The handwriting is difficult but appears to read something like '[']s rep[etition] / enct[ment] of / l[e]g[a]cy / s4t [Suarez for Thomism?]' — Tom may be abbreviating 'reenactment of legacy' and flagging Suarez as a key figure. Alternatively 's4t' could be a personal shorthand. The bracket-arrow drawn next to the 'indefinite multiplication of disagreement' passage suggests this note glosses that claim. Tom seems to be summarizing the mechanism: the epistemologized Thomism's fate is a repetition/reenactment of the very fragmentation it sought to cure.
annotations.rc_relevance: If Tom is tracking the concept of 'reenactment' here, this connects to RC's concern with how institutions meant to form character can instead transmit the pathologies of the surrounding culture — a key tension in the subsidiarity and tradition-vs-reform themes.

pages.summary: MacIntyre surveys how different Thomist schools (Mercier/Louvain, Rousselot, Maritain) each responded to their particular secular philosophical rivals, showing that Thomistic interpretation was always inseparable from philosophical apologetics. He then focuses on Maritain's attempt to ground human rights in Aquinas's natural law framework, arguing this was a fundamental error: for Aquinas, law is primary and rights are derivative from divine law, whereas Enlightenment modernity reverses this priority, making rights prior to law. MacIntyre treats Maritain's UN Declaration project as a 'quixotic' and ultimately incoherent assimilation of Thomism to a liberal rights framework alien to Aquinas's actual thought.

annotations.content: two kinds of contest were closely connected
annotations.anchor_text: _enough these two kinds of contest were closely_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging MacIntyre's structural claim that the internal contest between Thomisms and the external contest with secular philosophy are not separate phenomena but mutually constitutive. This is a methodological point about how traditions define themselves through rivalry — a core MacIntyrean thesis. Tom may be tracking this as evidence that 'Thomism' is not a monolithic tradition but a field of competing interpretations each shaped by its apologetic context.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's concern with tradition vs. reform: the RC project must grapple with the fact that even within a single tradition, rival versions emerge from different apologetic confrontations with modernity.

annotations.content: rival and a corrector of Kant
annotations.anchor_text: _Mercier at Louvain, made out of Aquinas_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the specific characterization of Mercier's Aquinas as a 'corrector of Kant' — this is the Louvain neo-Thomist move of meeting Kantian epistemology on its own ground. By underlining 'corrector' rather than just 'rival,' Tom seems attentive to the distinction between wholesale opposition and internal correction, which has implications for how tradition engages modernity.
annotations.rc_relevance: This speaks to RC's MacIntyre critique of Enlightenment rationalism: the Louvain strategy of 'correcting' Kant from within represents one possible mode of engagement between tradition and modernity — one MacIntyre will likely find insufficient.

annotations.content: responded to the French academic philosophy
annotations.anchor_text: _different fashion responded to the French academic_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is tracking MacIntyre's comparative method: each Thomist school is defined by its particular secular interlocutor (Kant for Mercier, French academic philosophy for Rousselot). The underline reinforces the pattern MacIntyre is building — that Thomistic interpretation is always contextually reactive, never purely internal.
annotations.rc_relevance: For the RC project, this pattern raises the question of whether any recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry today can escape being shaped by its contemporary secular rival — a tension between authentic retrieval and apologetic distortion.

annotations.content: offering a rival and superior account of the same moral subject matter as do other modern nontheological doctrines of universal rights
annotations.anchor_text: _tempt to present Thomism as offering a rival_
annotations.interpretation: This is one of the most heavily marked phrases on the page — Tom is flagging MacIntyre's diagnosis of Maritain's error as strategic rather than merely philosophical. By trying to offer a 'rival and superior account of the same moral subject matter,' Maritain has conceded the liberal terms of debate. Tom likely sees this as the key failure mode: accepting the enemy's framing in order to beat them on their own ground.
annotations.rc_relevance: Central to RC's concern with civic friendship and moral community: if Thomism capitulates to the liberal rights framework in order to compete, it loses its distinctive claim that law and community are prior to individual rights — undermining the very alternative it claims to offer.

annotations.content: alleged to attach to individual persons
annotations.anchor_text: _doctrines of universal rights alleged to attach_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles this phrase, which is MacIntyre's carefully skeptical language — 'alleged to attach' signals that the attachment of rights to individuals is itself a contested philosophical claim, not a neutral description. The circle isolates MacIntyre's embedded critique of the ontology of rights. Tom is likely flagging this as a key piece of MacIntyre's genealogical debunking of rights-talk.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's engagement with MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism: rights 'alleged to attach to individuals' is the target formulation — the RC project must articulate what replaces this atomistic ontology with a communal/relational one.

annotations.content: plain persons are misled into giving moral expression to those capacities through assent to false philosophical theories
annotations.anchor_text: _tures and notably in that of modernity plain persons_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's claim that modernity systematically misleads ordinary moral agents by channeling their genuine moral capacities through false philosophical frameworks. This is a strong claim about ideology and moral distortion. Tom may be evaluating whether MacIntyre is being too harsh on Maritain here, or alternatively affirming it as the key diagnostic claim about modernity's corruption of natural moral knowledge.
annotations.rc_relevance: Core RC theme: the role of institutions in forming character. If plain persons are systematically misled by false philosophical theories, the RC project must address how institutions and practices can re-form moral perception without simply substituting one ideological framework for another.

annotations.content: For on Aquinas's view the rights which are normative for human relationships are derived from and warranted only by divine law, apprehended by those without the resources afforded by God's self-revelation as the natural law
annotations.anchor_text: _nas's thought. For on Aquinas's view the rights_
annotations.interpretation: This is perhaps the most heavily underlined passage on the page — Tom is marking MacIntyre's precise statement of Aquinas's actual position on the law/rights relationship. The extended underline suggests Tom wants this as a reference point, either to deploy in his own work or to test against other accounts of Thomistic natural law. The phrase 'without the resources afforded by God's self-revelation' is especially important: natural law is accessible to reason, but its warrant is still divine.
annotations.rc_relevance: Foundational for RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry: this passage establishes that rights are epistemically and ontologically secondary to law in Aquinas, which has direct implications for how RC frames civic friendship and moral community as law-constituted rather than rights-constituted.

annotations.content: Law is primary, rights are secondary
annotations.anchor_text: _God's self-revelation as the natural law. Law is primary_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this compressed thesis statement — one of the clearest propositional claims in the passage. This is MacIntyre's distillation of the Aquinas/Enlightenment contrast into a single aphorism. The underline suggests Tom treats this as a key quotable formulation, possibly for direct use in RC writing or as a structural anchor for understanding the chapter's argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the RC project's central tension in miniature: subsidiarity and civic friendship presuppose that moral community (law) is prior to individual claims (rights) — MacIntyre's 'law is primary' formulation is the philosophical backbone of that RC commitment.

annotations.content: Law is primary, rights are secondary. But for Enlightenment and post-Enlightenment modernity, human rights provide a standard prior to all law.
annotations.anchor_text: _Law is primary, rights are secondary. But for_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets the full antithesis — Aquinas's position and its Enlightenment inversion together. The bracket frames this as a unit, suggesting he sees it as a set piece or structural pivot in MacIntyre's argument. He may intend to cite both halves together, or he is marking the precise point where MacIntyre's genealogical contrast is most explicit.
annotations.rc_relevance: This bracketed antithesis is the philosophical hinge of the RC project's critique of liberal rights discourse: the RC argument that civic friendship and subsidiarity require law-primacy over rights-claims depends on exactly this Thomistic inversion of the Enlightenment priority.

annotations.anchor_text: _Maritain at a later date would formulate_
annotations.interpretation: This is almost certainly a date range Tom has noted for Maritain's influence or relevance — possibly the span of MacIntyre's own sustained engagement with Maritain, or the years of a particular debate about Thomism and human rights. It could also refer to a publication range for Maritain scholarship Tom is cross-referencing. The annotation suggests Tom is placing this argument in a historical context external to the book itself.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, knowing the temporal span of the Maritain debate helps Tom situate when and how the Thomistic rights-framework became institutionally dominant (e.g., through Catholic social teaching and the UN), which shapes what 'recovery' of authentic Thomism must undo.

annotations.content: M·A: [law] is primary?
annotations.anchor_text: _Law is primary, rights are secondary_
annotations.interpretation: Tom appears to be annotating the 'law is primary' thesis with a question mark — either questioning whether MacIntyre has correctly represented Aquinas, or flagging his own uncertainty about the claim's implications. The 'M·A:' prefix (MacIntyre or possibly Maritain/Aquinas abbreviated) suggests he is attributing the claim to a specific thinker and interrogating it. This is a critical annotation, not merely a summary.
annotations.rc_relevance: This questioning of 'law is primary' is vital for RC: Tom may be probing whether this Thomistic priority of law over rights is adequate for a contemporary civility project, or whether it risks authoritarianism — a tension RC must address directly.

annotations.content: law is primary (rights secondary)
annotations.anchor_text: _God's self-revelation as the natural law. Law is primary_
annotations.interpretation: This appears to be Tom restating MacIntyre's thesis in his own hand as a summary note — 'law is primary (rights secondary)' is a condensed version of the bracketed antithesis on the page. Tom is likely creating a retrieval anchor for this key claim, possibly for later indexing or outline use in RC writing.
annotations.rc_relevance: This self-summary note suggests Tom has identified this as one of the page's most RC-relevant claims — the ontological priority of law over rights is the philosophical foundation for RC's argument that moral community precedes individual rights claims.

annotations.content: Vertical lines with bracket marks in left margin alongside the Maritain human rights paragraph
annotations.anchor_text: _to be a Thomistic defense of the doctrine of human rights_
annotations.interpretation: Multiple vertical lines or bracket marks in the left margin alongside the Maritain/UN Declaration passage suggest Tom is flagging this as a high-importance section requiring close attention or extended note-taking. The use of multiple lines (rather than one) may indicate strong agreement or strong disagreement — given the questioning annotations nearby, this likely marks a site of critical engagement rather than simple endorsement.
annotations.rc_relevance: The Maritain/UN Declaration passage is directly relevant to RC's concern with how Thomistic moral thought gets co-opted by liberal institutional frameworks — Tom's heavy marking here suggests this is a key case study for the RC project's critique of rights-based civic discourse.

annotations.content: For Maritain this was an uncharacteristic lapse. We owe in part to him, as we do also to Maréchal and outstandingly to Rousselot, the development of a kind of understanding of Aquinas
annotations.anchor_text: _For Maritain this was an uncharacteristic lapse_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's qualified rehabilitation of Maritain — despite the 'lapse' on rights, Maritain is credited with contributing to a genuine advance in understanding Aquinas. This nuanced judgment (Maritain is both wrong on rights and valuable on Aquinas) is important: Tom may be tracking MacIntyre's dialectical method of internal critique that preserves what is valuable while excising what is distorted.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this nuance models how the project should engage thinkers who are partially right: the RC recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry cannot wholesale reject figures like Maritain but must discriminate between their genuine contributions and their accommodations to modernity.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the Thomistic tradition, represented by historical scholars recovering Aquinas's own developing thought, was challenged by modern epistemological and genealogical modes of enquiry. He contends that restoring Aquinas's thought requires confronting these rival modes not thesis-by-thesis but at the level of their overall 'modes of enquiry' — the craft-tradition of dialectical reasoning stemming from Socrates, Plato, and Aristotle. The true conflict between Thomism and modernity is therefore structural and methodological, not merely doctrinal.

annotations.content: 1 neg. post. Thomism
annotations.anchor_text: _if this were the whole story of Thomism_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is labeling the first of what appears to be a numbered sequence of positions or phases — here marking a 'negative' or defeated form of post-Thomism, likely the story of Thomism as a series of defeats against idealism, materialism, rationalism, empiricism, and positivism. The numeral '1' suggests he is building a structural schema of the argument's stages, and 'neg.' signals he reads this as the thesis MacIntyre is about to qualify or overcome.
annotations.rc_relevance: This maps onto the RC concern with institutional decline — how traditions of virtue can appear defeated by Enlightenment rivals, which is precisely the problem RC seeks to address by recovering classical modes of enquiry.

annotations.content: 2(?) historical Thomism
annotations.anchor_text: _retrieving stage by scholarly stage the historical understanding_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is labeling a second phase or version of Thomism — the historical-scholarly recovery of Aquinas (Grabmann, Mandonnet, Gilson, Van Steenberghen, Weisheipl). The question mark suggests Tom is uncertain whether MacIntyre fully endorses this as a discrete stage or whether it overlaps with something else. He is tracking MacIntyre's implicit typology of Thomisms, which connects to the book's title 'Too Many Thomisms.'
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project similarly must navigate between a naive traditionalism and a sophisticated historical recovery — Tom is alert to MacIntyre's distinction because RC must decide which version of 'Thomism' it draws upon.

annotations.content: if this were the whole story of Thomism it would at least appear as, and perhaps be, a story of defeat
annotations.anchor_text: _if this were the whole story of Thomism_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's concessive framing — the acknowledgment that on one reading, Thomism loses. This is a rhetorically important move: MacIntyre grants the strongest version of the opposition before reframing the terms. Tom likely highlights it to mark the dialectical structure — defeat acknowledged, recovery incoming.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC directly confronts the 'story of defeat' narrative for classical virtue traditions; highlighting this passage signals Tom sees MacIntyre's reframing strategy as a model for how RC should position itself against charges of irrelevance.

annotations.content: those also generated a quite different set of intellectual enterprises, those which, in retrieving stage by scholarly stage the historical understanding of what Aquinas himself said, wrote, and did, recovered for us an understanding of what is distinctive about the mode of enquiry elaborated in its classical and most adequate form by Aquinas
annotations.anchor_text: _retrieving stage by scholarly stage the historical understanding_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the key distinction MacIntyre introduces: alongside the defeated systematic Thomisms, a historical-scholarly tradition recovered Aquinas's actual mode of enquiry. The phrase 'mode of enquiry' is crucial — Tom is tracking this as a technical term that will become central to MacIntyre's argument. The highlighting suggests Tom sees this as the pivot point of the chapter.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery of civility traditions depends on exactly this kind of scholarly retrieval — not imposing a system but recovering a 'mode of enquiry.' Tom is noting the methodological template MacIntyre offers.

annotations.content: but in which a real progress appears, so that while systematic Thomisms apparently multiplied disagreement, the historical scholars of the Thomistic movement challenged them
annotations.anchor_text: _real progress appears so that while systematic Thomisms_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's ironic juxtaposition: systematic Thomisms multiplied disagreement while historical scholarship made real progress. This is a key structural claim — methodological rigor (historical recovery) outperforms systematic assertion. Tom is likely flagging this as evidence for MacIntyre's implicit critique of 'encyclopaedist' modes of doing philosophy.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's argument that civic traditions must be recovered through historical and practical understanding rather than abstract systematic prescription is directly supported here — Tom is building his case.

annotations.content: That account is one in which the developments within Aquinas's own thought throughout his career (about Aquinas as himself a continuously developing thinker, transforming and correcting his own initial positions in a variety of instructive ways
annotations.anchor_text: _Aquinas as himself a continuously developing thinker_
annotations.interpretation: The highlighting of 'continuously developing thinker' is significant — Tom is noting MacIntyre's portrait of Aquinas not as a static systematic authority but as a thinker engaged in ongoing dialectical self-correction. This counters the encyclopaedist image of Aquinas as a fixed doctrinal source and aligns him with the craft-tradition model.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this matters because a tradition of civility cannot be frozen — it must be alive and self-correcting. MacIntyre's Aquinas models the kind of living tradition RC wants to recover.

annotations.content: almost nothing was said by the epistemologically oriented Thomists) were the outcome of his own participation in, as well as his continuously more adequate representation of, a mode of enquiry which was a dialectically open-ended continuation of that craft-tradition stemming from Socrates, Plato, and Aristotle as well as from the church fathers
annotations.anchor_text: _dialectically open-ended continuation of that craft-tradition_
annotations.interpretation: This is one of the most densely highlighted passages — Tom recognizes this as MacIntyre's definition of the Thomistic mode of enquiry as a craft-tradition. The phrase 'dialectically open-ended' is crucial: the tradition is not closed or dogmatic but dynamically engaged. Tom is almost certainly flagging 'craft-tradition' as a key RC term — MacIntyre's concept of tradition-constituted rationality.
annotations.rc_relevance: The craft-tradition concept is foundational for RC: civility, like Thomistic enquiry, is a practice transmitted through institutions and formation, not a set of abstract principles. This passage is RC's philosophical backbone.

annotations.content: That account is one in which the developments within Aquinas's own thought throughout his career (about Aquinas as himself a continuously developing thinker, transforming and correcting his own initial positions in a variety of instructive ways, almost nothing was said by the epistemologically oriented Thomists) were the outcome of his own participation in, as well as his continuously more adequate representation of, a mode of enquiry which was a dialectically open-ended continuation of that craft-tradition stemming from Socrates, Plato, and Aristotle as well as from the church fathers, a tradition of which Aeterni Patris spoke with great accuracy but the character of which the readers of Aeterni Patris had often been unable to comprehend.
annotations.anchor_text: _That account is one in which the developments_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets this entire long passage, demarcating it as a coherent unit of argument. The bracket likely signals 'this is the positive counter-narrative' to the 'story of defeat' highlighted earlier. The closing reference to Aeterni Patris being accurately described but poorly understood by its readers is particularly significant — Tom may be noting MacIntyre's implicit critique of institutional reception failure.
annotations.rc_relevance: The bracket isolates MacIntyre's description of tradition-as-craft, which RC needs as a theoretical anchor. Tom is treating this as a quotable, self-contained argument unit.

annotations.content: It is then crucial, if Aquinas's thought is to be restored and continued so that it may confront what have been the sovereign modes of moral and theological enquiry in the last hundred years, the encyclopaedic and the genealogical modes, that we recognize
annotations.anchor_text: _It is then crucial if Aquinas's thought is to be restored_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the thesis statement of the chapter's second movement. MacIntyre names the two rivals explicitly — encyclopaedic and genealogical — and frames the task as confrontation, not accommodation. Tom is marking this as the argumentative crux: the tripartite structure (Thomism vs. Encyclopaedia vs. Genealogy) made explicit.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the core RC theoretical framework — the three rival versions. Tom is marking the moment MacIntyre names the battle lines that RC must navigate in reconstructing civility traditions.

annotations.content: the encyclopaedic and the genealogical modes
annotations.anchor_text: _the encyclopaedic and the genealogical modes that we recognize_
annotations.interpretation: A smaller bracket or underline isolating the two rival names. Tom is flagging these as technical terms requiring attention. The isolation suggests he may be cross-referencing these terms against earlier or later passages, or preparing to cite them in RC writing.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC must position itself against both encyclopaedist (liberal proceduralist) and genealogical (postmodern/Nietzschean) rivals — naming them precisely is the first step.

annotations.content: that it is not in respect of their individual theses, considered item by item, but only in respect of those theses understood in their relationship to each overall specific mode of enquiry
annotations.anchor_text: _not in respect of their individual theses considered item by item_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the methodological claim that distinguishes MacIntyre's approach: the conflict is not about individual propositions but about entire modes of enquiry. This is anti-foundationalist in a specific sense — you cannot adjudicate between traditions thesis-by-thesis; you must engage them at the level of their constitutive rationality. Tom underlines 'individual theses' and 'mode of enquiry' as the contrasting poles.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC cannot simply argue for specific civic norms against liberal rivals — it must contest at the level of the mode of enquiry itself. This passage gives Tom the theoretical warrant for RC's holistic approach.

annotations.content: mode of enquiry
annotations.anchor_text: _relationship to each overall specific mode of enquiry_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'mode of enquiry' — this is a technical term he is tracking as it recurs through MacIntyre's argument. The circle marks it as a term of art requiring precise understanding, distinct from 'method' or 'approach.' It is likely the third or fourth occurrence of this phrase on this page, and Tom is noting its cumulative weight.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's engagement with MacIntyre depends on correctly understanding what a 'mode of enquiry' is — not a set of procedures but an embodied, tradition-constituted practice. Tom is flagging this as a concept RC must define carefully.

annotations.content: So Aq's acct. must be seen 1st of all as a product of a craft nested in a tradition of enquiry
annotations.anchor_text: _it is not in respect of their individual theses_
annotations.interpretation: This is Tom's most substantive margin note — a paraphrase and synthesis of MacIntyre's argument in his own words. 'Seen 1st of all' suggests Tom is noting this as a priority claim: before evaluating Aquinas's theses, one must situate them within the craft-tradition that produced them. The phrasing 'nested in a tradition of enquiry' is Tom's own formulation, suggesting he is beginning to develop RC-specific vocabulary from MacIntyre's framework.
annotations.rc_relevance: This note is directly RC-generative: Tom is articulating the principle that any recovery of Thomistic (or classical) civility traditions must first locate them within their constitutive practice-context — a methodological commitment RC must make explicit.

annotations.content: Two vertical lines forming a bracket on the right margin, with a right-pointing arrow toward the paragraph beginning 'It is then crucial'
annotations.anchor_text: _It is then crucial if Aquinas's thought is to be restored_
annotations.interpretation: The double vertical lines with arrow signal high importance — Tom is marking this paragraph as exceptionally significant, likely for direct citation in RC writing. The arrow directs attention inward to the text, suggesting 'pay attention to this specific passage.' This is one of his strongest positive markers on the page.
annotations.rc_relevance: The paragraph being flagged contains the explicit tripartite framework and the anti-thesis-by-thesis methodological claim — both central to RC's theoretical architecture.

annotations.content: Right-pointing arrow (>) in red/dark ink beside 'the sovereign modes of moral and theological enquiry'
annotations.anchor_text: _the sovereign modes of moral and theological enquiry_
annotations.interpretation: A standalone directional arrow marks 'sovereign modes' as a phrase warranting attention. 'Sovereign' is MacIntyre's strong word — these modes don't merely compete, they claim authority. Tom may be noting the political valence of this word, or cross-referencing it to MacIntyre's discussion of sovereignty elsewhere.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's project is precisely to contest the sovereignty of encyclopaedist and genealogical modes in shaping contemporary civic discourse — Tom marks the claim about sovereignty as the problem RC must address.

annotations.content: another dimension of difference and conflict
annotations.anchor_text: _recognize yet another dimension of difference and con-_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the phrase signaling MacIntyre is introducing a new dimension of analysis beyond what has been established. This is a structural marker — Tom is tracking the layering of MacIntyre's argument as it adds dimension upon dimension to the analysis of inter-tradition conflict.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC must account for multiple dimensions of conflict between traditions — not just doctrinal but methodological and now (as the next page will reveal) formal. Tom is tracking this structure to use it in RC's own multi-level analysis.

annotations.content: the form which each standpoint
annotations.anchor_text: _That dimension is a matter of the form which each standpoint_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets 'the form' at the very end of the page — this is a cliffhanger annotation, marking the introduction of 'form' as the next level of analysis MacIntyre will develop on the following page. The bracket signals Tom is primed to continue tracking this argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: The question of 'form' in which a tradition presents itself — narrative, treatise, encyclopedia — is directly relevant to how RC should present its recovery of civility traditions. Tom is alert to this.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the encyclopaedist's narrative structure is governed by belief in the progress of reason, where individual lives gain meaning only insofar as they contribute to or obstruct that progress — canonization being the encyclopaedia's reward for contributors. He uses Henry Sidgwick's admission of 'chaos' in ethics (independent, incompatible fundamental principles) as the internal crisis that exposes the encyclopaedist tradition's failure: reason, on its own terms, discovers not cosmos but incoherence. The genealogist then exploits this appeal to timeless rational principles by revealing its function as concealment of a tradition-laden past.

annotations.content: on narrative structure → Encycl. narrative struct.
annotations.anchor_text: _The narrative structure of the encyclopaedia_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is labeling this passage with a structural tag — 'Encycl. narrative struct.' — that mirrors the tripartite framework MacIntyre is developing across the book. The arrow suggests Tom sees this as the pivot point where MacIntyre defines the encyclopaedist's distinctive narrative logic. He is likely cross-referencing this against how MacIntyre will later characterize the genealogist's and Thomist's rival narrative structures, so he can track all three in parallel.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project's engagement with MacIntyre's tripartite structure (encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition) requires precisely this kind of structural mapping; Tom is building a taxonomy of how each tradition narrates moral progress, which is foundational for RC's argument about tradition-constituted rationality.

annotations.content: the structure of such narratives
annotations.anchor_text: _in which each understands the structure_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging MacIntyre's claim that what divides the rival traditions is most clearly seen through their contrasting narrative structures — not just their conclusions. This is a methodological claim: narrative form, not propositional content alone, is the diagnostic tool. Tom may be noting this because it underwrites RC's interest in how institutions and practices embody and transmit narratives of moral life.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is concerned with how traditions form character through narrative and practice; this highlight marks the moment where MacIntyre grounds inter-tradition comparison in narrative structure, which is the theoretical engine behind RC's recovery of Thomistic moral formation.

annotations.content: The narrative structure of the encyclopaedia is one dictated by belief in the progress of reason
annotations.anchor_text: _The narrative structure of the encyclopaedia is_
annotations.interpretation: This is a definitional claim — MacIntyre's formal characterization of the encyclopaedist tradition — and Tom has highlighted it as a key thesis sentence. The highlighting suggests Tom wants this as a quotable anchor point for the encyclopaedist position. He may also be noting the implicit critique: 'belief in progress' is itself a narrative commitment, not a neutral rational stance, which is exactly MacIntyre's debunking move.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's critique of Enlightenment rationalism depends on exposing 'progress of reason' as a tradition-specific narrative rather than a universal standard; this sentence is the lynchpin of that argument and Tom recognizes it as such.

annotations.content: Articles of a certain length and tone were the cyclopaedist's equivalent of canonization
annotations.anchor_text: _Articles of a certain length and tone were_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this striking sociological observation: the encyclopaedia has its own quasi-liturgical authority structures — canonization through article format. This is MacIntyre at his most institutionally analytical, showing that the supposedly neutral encyclopaedia is actually a tradition with its own practices of recognition and exclusion. Tom may be drawn to this because it parallels how RC thinks about institutions forming and legitimating moral authorities.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's theme of institutions in forming character and moral community is directly illuminated here: MacIntyre reveals the encyclopaedia as an institution with authoritative practices that shape who counts as a moral exemplar, analogous to how RC examines how civic and ecclesial institutions canonize models of civility.

annotations.content: So what reason discovers, when it is successful, is always in some sense itself, some instantiation of the coherence of reason
annotations.anchor_text: _discovers, when it is successful, is always_
annotations.interpretation: Tom flags this as MacIntyre's diagnosis of the encyclopaedist's circular epistemology: reason can only ever confirm itself, never encounter genuine otherness. This is a crucial critique — it means the encyclopaedist tradition cannot genuinely learn from traditions it deems irrational. Tom likely sees this as the philosophical core of MacIntyre's anti-foundationalism and its relevance to why Sidgwick's 'chaos' result is so damaging.
annotations.rc_relevance: This speaks directly to RC's engagement with MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism: the self-referential closure of encyclopaedist reason is what makes it incapable of the kind of tradition-mediated moral learning that RC, following MacIntyre and Aquinas, advocates.

annotations.content: independent, not always compatible fundamental principles, whose brute givenness could neither be denied nor transcended
annotations.anchor_text: _to discover cosmos rational order he had instead found chaos_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights Sidgwick's 'chaos' result — incompatible foundational principles that reason cannot resolve — as the empirical evidence for the encyclopaedist tradition's internal collapse. This is MacIntyre's immanent critique: the tradition refutes itself by its own standards. Tom likely sees this as a model for RC's engagement with contemporary moral fragmentation — the 'chaos' Sidgwick found is precisely the fragmentation RC is responding to.
annotations.rc_relevance: Sidgwick's 'chaos' of incompatible principles is a historical precursor to the contemporary moral fragmentation RC addresses; Tom is connecting MacIntyre's historical diagnosis to RC's present-day concern with the collapse of shared moral language and civic friendship.

annotations.content: present principles which owe their status as principles to reason and not at all to the past
annotations.anchor_text: _whose place in the progress of reason is never more than a stage towards_
annotations.interpretation: Tom flags this as the encyclopaedist's explicit repudiation of tradition: principles derive authority from reason alone, never from historical inheritance. This is the sharpest statement of what MacIntyre is arguing against, and Tom likely marks it as the clearest articulation of the position RC must contest. The phrase 'not at all to the past' is almost polemically stated — Tom may be noting its rhetorical force.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the direct antithesis of RC's tradition-constituted rationality: RC argues that moral principles are always already tradition-laden and that severing them from the past produces exactly the incoherence Sidgwick encountered. This sentence is a target for RC's counter-argument.

annotations.content: Principles will soon be everything, and tradition nothing . . .
annotations.anchor_text: _as Sidgwick had declared in 1865_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights Sidgwick's 1865 declaration as a historical marker — a moment when the encyclopaedist tradition stated its anti-traditionalism with maximum clarity and confidence, just before its own internal collapse. The ellipsis MacIntyre uses is telling: it invites the reader to note the irony that 'tradition' did not in fact become 'nothing.' Tom is likely flagging this as a quotable irony for RC's argument about the inevitable return of tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: This quote is a gift for RC's rhetorical arsenal: the confident Enlightenment proclamation that tradition would be superseded by principle turns out to be historically false, vindicating RC's claim that tradition is ineliminable from moral enquiry.

annotations.content: a denigration of the past and an appeal to principles purportedly timeless
annotations.anchor_text: _tive of the encyclopaedist issues in a denigration_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this as MacIntyre's summary verdict on the encyclopaedist narrative: it structurally requires both a dismissal of historical authority and a claim to atemporal validity. The word 'purportedly' is MacIntyre's debunking qualifier — Tom likely noticed this as the moment where MacIntyre most concisely encapsulates the encyclopaedist's self-deception. This may also be circled or bracketed given its summary force.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery of tradition-constituted civility requires dismantling the claim that timeless principles can ground civic life without historical community; this sentence names the two moves RC must counter: denigration of the past and false claims to timelessness.

annotations.content: the function of concealing the burden of a past which has not in fact been discarded at all
annotations.anchor_text: _For the genealogist this appeal to timeless rational principles has_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the genealogist's counter-move: the appeal to timeless principles is exposed as a concealment strategy. This is where MacIntyre pivots from the encyclopaedist's self-description to the genealogist's critique of it. Tom likely marks this as the hinge point of the dialectic — the genealogist scores a point against the encyclopaedist that the Thomist will then also use, though differently. The distinction between genealogist and Thomist uses of this critique is crucial for RC.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC must navigate carefully here: it agrees with the genealogist's unmasking of false timelessness but refuses the genealogist's nihilistic conclusion. Tom is marking the point where RC must appropriate the genealogist's insight while rejecting its ultimate trajectory.

annotations.content: Geneal. narrative struct.
annotations.anchor_text: _For the genealogist this appeal to timeless rational principles_
annotations.interpretation: Tom mirrors his earlier 'Encycl. narrative struct.' label with 'Geneal. narrative struct.' — he is systematically tagging the structural definitions of each tradition as MacIntyre introduces them. This is clearly a reading strategy for comparative tracking across the book, likely to be used in RC documents that lay out the three rival versions in parallel. The consistency of the labeling convention suggests Tom planned this from the start of the chapter.
annotations.rc_relevance: Tom is building a structural map of MacIntyre's tripartite argument that will serve as the backbone of RC's theoretical framework; this systematic annotation practice reflects RC's need to present MacIntyre's three traditions clearly and comparatively to audiences unfamiliar with the book.

annotations.content: Upward arrow with vertical line and tick marks on left margin
annotations.anchor_text: _present principles which owe their status as principles to reason_
annotations.interpretation: This symbol appears to mark a directional movement in the argument — possibly Tom indicating the escalation or trajectory of the encyclopaedist's claim (principles ascend, tradition descends). Alternatively it may be a reading aid marking 'this is where the argument climaxes before the reversal.' The tick marks suggest multiple layers or steps he is tracking. Given its position near the Sidgwick material, it likely flags the ironic arc: confidence ascending just before the fall.
annotations.rc_relevance: The symbol may be tracking the internal dialectic of the encyclopaedist tradition's rise and collapse — a structural pattern RC would want to document as a model of how overconfident Enlightenment rationalism generates its own crisis, which is a core RC historical narrative.

pages.summary: MacIntyre contrasts three narrative stances toward the past: the encyclopaedist reduces it to mere prologue, the genealogist struggles against it as hidden rationality, while the Thomist treats the past as a source from which we must learn in order to move toward our telos. The Thomist reappropriation operates at two interrelated levels — theoretical enquiry (a sequenced progression of questions building on prior answers) and the practical embodiment of that enquiry — making the reader of Aquinas a participant in an ongoing narrative of moral inquiry. This inquiry, on the Thomistic view, discloses answers to foundational questions about telos, right action, virtues, and laws.
