annotations.content: Thomist (collective street)
annotations.anchor_text: _The Thomists' narrative, by contrast with both_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is labeling the Thomist position as a distinct 'street' or tradition-community, distinguishing it from the encyclopaedist and genealogist. The word 'collective' is significant — Tom seems to be flagging that Thomist enquiry is not individualist but communally constituted, perhaps contrasting it with the genealogist's essentially solitary critical stance. The informal word 'street' may be Tom's own idiom for a living tradition or school of practice, suggesting he finds MacIntyre's Thomism sociologically grounded.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly maps onto RC's interest in tradition-as-community: the Thomist 'collective street' is precisely the kind of moral community RC seeks to recover against atomized liberal individualism.

annotations.content: 2 levels of reappropriation
annotations.anchor_text: _reappropriation of the past takes place at two interrelated levels_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging MacIntyre's structural claim that Thomist reappropriation is bimodal — theoretical and practical. By labeling it explicitly, Tom is treating this as a load-bearing architectural point in the argument, not merely a descriptive aside. He may be noting this to track how MacIntyre avoids the charge that Thomism is merely antiquarian: the two levels ensure the past is both intellectually interrogated and embodied in practice.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with institutions forming character maps exactly onto this distinction: theoretical enquiry alone is insufficient; practical embodiment is what converts inherited doctrine into civic virtue.

annotations.content: Theor. enquiry (like Kuhn-like? structured)
annotations.anchor_text: _Theoretical enquiry is constituted by a sequence of questions_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is drawing a comparison between MacIntyre's sequenced theoretical enquiry and Thomas Kuhn's structure of scientific revolutions — paradigms generating puzzles in an ordered sequence. The question mark suggests Tom is not certain this comparison holds but finds it generative. This is a sophisticated cross-reference: Tom may be probing whether Thomist enquiry is paradigm-bound in the Kuhnian sense, or whether MacIntyre is deliberately echoing Kuhn to make tradition-bound rationality appear epistemically respectable.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's engagement with MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism turns partly on whether tradition-constituted enquiry can claim cognitive authority — the Kuhn comparison is a way of asking whether tradition-rationality has structural parallels to normal science.

annotations.content: Inq. is thus structured
annotations.anchor_text: _outcome of some particular learner's debate with all those distinct pasts_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is summarizing MacIntyre's point that enquiry is not free-floating but structurally constrained by the tradition's own sequence of questions. The phrase 'thus structured' suggests Tom is tracking MacIntyre's anti-voluntarist epistemology — one cannot simply choose one's questions but must engage them as they arise from within the tradition. This may also be a cross-reference note pointing back to the 'sequence of questions' point above.
annotations.rc_relevance: This speaks directly to RC's interest in how institutions form character: structured enquiry implies that the learner is shaped by the tradition before she can reshape it — a point with clear implications for civic education and institutional design.

annotations.content: 1. telos
annotations.anchor_text: _a set of answers to such questions as: What is the telos of human beings? What is right_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is completing and organizing MacIntyre's list of Thomistic foundational questions into a numbered sequence. This is not merely transcription — by numbering them 1–4, Tom imposes a hierarchical order (telos → right action → virtues → laws/customs) that suggests he reads this as a derivation chain: telos grounds right action, which specifies virtues, which are institutionalized in laws and customs. The ellipsis after 'customs' signals Tom knows the list continues on the next page.
annotations.rc_relevance: This four-part schema is the backbone of RC's substantive agenda: recovering a telos-grounded account of civility means working through exactly this chain — from human ends down to the customary and legal forms that embody them in communities.

annotations.content: della - disregarding / undermining / monopolising
annotations.anchor_text: _Too Many Thomisms?_
annotations.interpretation: This appears at the very top of the page, likely a carry-over note from the chapter's argument or a prior page. Tom seems to be cataloging three strategies by which a dominant discourse (perhaps the encyclopaedist or secular academy) relates to rival traditions: disregarding them, undermining their authority, or monopolizing the terms of debate. This tripartite taxonomy is Tom's own analytical frame overlaid on MacIntyre's text.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is directly concerned with how civility and Thomistic moral vocabulary have been marginalized in public discourse — Tom's three-part schema names the mechanisms of that marginalization, which RC must diagnose before proposing remedies.

annotations.content: the genealogists' narrative has two strands to it: one a history of that which the genealogists aspire to undermine by such disclosure; the other the history of the genealogists' own project and of the evasions and stratagems without which the genealogist would inevitably fall back into just those modes which he or she is concerned to repudiate and to expose.
annotations.anchor_text: _the genealogists' narrative has two strands_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has highlighted the internal self-reflexivity of the genealogist's narrative — it must simultaneously attack its target and police its own evasions. Tom is likely flagging this because it reveals a structural vulnerability in genealogy: it requires continuous self-critique that the encyclopaedist's narrative does not. This is a key point for MacIntyre's argument that genealogy is ultimately self-undermining.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's engagement with postmodern critiques of tradition (the 'genealogist' position) depends on showing that genealogy cannot sustain its own normative commitments — this passage is the textual ground for that argument.

annotations.content: The Thomists' narrative, by contrast with both of these, treats the past neither as mere prologue nor as something to be struggled against, but as that from which we have to learn if we are to identify and move towards our telos more adequately and that which we have to put to the question if we are to know which questions we ourselves should next formulate and attempt to answer, both theoretically and practically.
annotations.anchor_text: _treats the past neither as mere prologue nor as something to be struggled against_
annotations.interpretation: This is the definitional core of the Thomist narrative position and Tom has highlighted it in full, likely as a passage he will quote or build on directly. The double negation ('neither...nor') followed by the positive ('but as that from which we have to learn') is MacIntyre's rhetorical pivot. Tom may also be tracking the phrase 'telos more adequately' — 'adequately' implies progressive approximation, not static repetition of tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: This passage is arguably the single most important for RC's theoretical foundation: it defines the posture RC advocates toward inherited traditions of civility — not nostalgia (mere prologue) and not deconstruction (struggle against), but disciplined learning oriented toward telos.

annotations.content: reappropriation of the past in a way which directs the present towards a particular — and yet eternal — future takes place at two interrelated levels: that of theoretical enquiry and that of the practical embodiment of such enquiry.
annotations.anchor_text: _directs the present towards a particular — and yet eternal — future_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has highlighted the paradoxical phrase 'particular — and yet eternal — future,' which is philosophically dense. MacIntyre is claiming that the Thomist telos is both historically particular (embedded in a specific tradition) and transhistorically valid (eternal). Tom is likely flagging this as a key move in MacIntyre's defense against relativism — the tradition-constituted enquiry can still aim at universal truth. The 'two levels' reinforces his margin note.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is crucial for RC's anti-relativist argument: recovering civility through tradition is not mere parochialism because the telos aimed at has universal normative force — a claim RC must defend against both liberal universalists and postmodern relativists.

annotations.content: At every stage what emerges is the outcome of some particular learner's debate with all those distinct pasts. Hence in theoretical enquiry the readers of Aquinas, like his original hearers, both enact the narrative of their own enquiry and make that narrative a continuing part of a larger narrative of enquiry in which they are only the latest actors, who also understand that what they are able to contribute will lead on beyond them.
annotations.anchor_text: _readers of Aquinas, like his original hearers, both enact the narrative_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has highlighted the passage describing how readers of Aquinas become participants in an ongoing tradition rather than mere consumers of a text. The phrase 'latest actors' is important — it positions the contemporary Thomist as historically situated but not historically trapped. Tom may be thinking about RC's own position as a contemporary project that must see itself as one stage in a longer tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC explicitly positions itself as continuing a tradition of civility discourse — this passage gives MacIntyrean philosophical grounding for that self-understanding: RC is not merely reviving the past but enacting its own chapter in an ongoing narrative of moral enquiry.

annotations.content: the genealogists' narrative has two strands
annotations.anchor_text: _designed to disclose what its authors take the encyclopaedists' narrative to conceal_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has circled the word or phrase marking the structural duality of the genealogist's project. Circling (as opposed to underlining) often signals a term Tom wants to hold in mind as a recurring concept. He may be tracking 'two strands' as a structural pattern to compare with the 'two levels' of Thomist reappropriation — noting that both genealogy and Thomism have bipartite structures, but for very different reasons.
annotations.rc_relevance: Understanding the internal architecture of rival traditions (genealogy's two strands vs. Thomism's two levels) is methodologically important for RC's comparative work on tradition and reform.

annotations.content: sequence of questions, ordered both so that questions are generated in accordance with the direction of enquiry, and so that, at each stage, what needs to be presupposed or otherwise appealed to in answering the questions of that stage has already been provided.
annotations.anchor_text: _Theoretical enquiry is constituted by a sequence of questions_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has underlined MacIntyre's definition of what makes theoretical enquiry properly ordered — questions must be sequentially generated and each stage must presuppose only what has already been established. This is MacIntyre's account of rational progress within a tradition, and Tom is marking it carefully, perhaps to use as a methodological standard against which to measure RC's own argumentative structure.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's project of recovering Thomistic moral enquiry must itself be sequentially ordered in this way — Tom may be using this underline as a self-imposed methodological standard for how the RC argument should be built.

annotations.content: In the posing of each detailed set of questions, the principal answers made available by the various and conflicting traditions which have contributed to the making of this enquiry are set out and evaluated.
annotations.anchor_text: _principal answers made available by the various and conflicting traditions_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has bracketed this passage, which describes how Thomist enquiry engages competing traditions by laying out and evaluating their best answers at each stage. The bracket suggests Tom sees this as a methodological commitment he wants to hold up — possibly as a model for how RC should engage liberal, communitarian, and postmodern rivals rather than dismissing them.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's methodology of engaging 'rival versions' directly mirrors this Thomistic procedure — bracketing this passage suggests Tom is consciously modeling RC's comparative approach on MacIntyre's description of how Thomist enquiry handles competing traditions.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the moral life is not merely theoretical but is embedded in the narratives of particular communities — farmers, craftsmen, families, cities — whose traditions of moral, religious, and intellectual life constitute the living context of moral enquiry. He then makes the pivotal claim that modern moral philosophy has been 'blind' to the complementary relationship between narrative and theory: until we can specify what enacted narrative a moral theory corresponds to in actual social life, we do not yet understand that theory adequately. The page closes by framing the three rival versions — encyclopaedic, genealogical, and Thomistic — as not merely competing moral theories but competing projects for constructing rival moral narratives.

annotations.content: wheres your community
annotations.anchor_text: _action directed towards the telos? What_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is interrupting MacIntyre's abstract opening questions about telos and virtue with a sharp, grounding challenge: before we can answer what virtues or laws order human relationships, we must locate ourselves in an actual community. This is less a summary of the text and more Tom pushing back — or pushing forward — asking the reader (or himself) to apply the framework personally and institutionally. The question mark makes it feel like a Socratic probe.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is one of the core diagnostic questions of the RC project: contemporary social fragmentation means many people lack a functioning moral community, which is precisely why MacIntyre's framework feels both urgent and practically blocked. Tom is flagging the practical precondition MacIntyre's theory assumes but cannot supply.

annotations.content: EFRepx Dates! Flor
annotations.anchor_text: _Too Many Thomisms? What are the virtues_
annotations.interpretation: This appears to be a list of abbreviations or proper names — possibly 'EF' (Early Fathers?), 'Repx' (Republic?), 'Dates' and 'Flor' (possibly Florentine humanism or Dante/Florence). Tom may be cross-referencing other texts or thinkers that exemplify 'too many Thomisms' — tracking the historical exemplars MacIntyre will invoke. 'Flor' almost certainly refers to Florence and Dante, who is named just below as 'philosopher par excellence of the practical life itself.'
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project's engagement with tradition-constituted rationality requires identifying which historical communities successfully embodied Thomistic moral enquiry — Dante's Florence being a key test case MacIntyre uses. Tom is mapping the historical instantiations.

annotations.content: a practically well-ordered life is to embody the universal concepts
annotations.anchor_text: _live a practically well-ordered life is to embody_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the phrase that captures MacIntyre's central move: the universal (moral concepts, the telos) is only knowable through the particular (a well-ordered life actually lived). This is the anti-Kantian, anti-encyclopaedist heart of the page — moral knowledge is not abstract but embodied. The underline suggests Tom sees this as a thesis statement worth anchoring.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project's interest in character formation through institutions depends precisely on this claim: civility and virtue cannot be taught as abstract principles but must be embodied in ordered social practices. This underline marks the philosophical warrant for that pedagogical conviction.

annotations.content: may often not be recognized as a theory, the product of the theorizing of very different craft, but which nevertheless is one
annotations.anchor_text: _may often not be recognized as a theory_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks MacIntyre's subtle point that practical moral knowledge — the kind embedded in crafts and trades — IS a theory, just not a recognizable one to academic philosophy. This challenges the encyclopaedist assumption that theory is the property of professional reasoners. Tom seems to be tracking MacIntyre's vindication of tacit, tradition-embedded knowledge against explicit systematic theory.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this is crucial: the moral wisdom embedded in guilds, families, local institutions, and professions is theoretically serious even when it cannot articulate itself as such. This justifies RC's attention to vernacular and institutional traditions of civility that predate Enlightenment systematization.

annotations.content: continue the traditions, moral, religious, and intellectual, of such communities as those of the family, the city, the clan, and the nation
annotations.anchor_text: _continue the traditions moral religious and intellectual_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's enumeration of the nested communities — family, city, clan, nation — that are the carriers of moral tradition. The underline signals that Tom sees this as MacIntyre's answer to his own top-margin question 'where's your community?' — the communities are these overlapping, tradition-bearing institutions. The list also has a subsidiarity structure moving from smallest to largest.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly maps onto RC's subsidiarity theme: moral formation happens through nested communities at different scales, and the collapse of any layer (especially family and city/local community) constitutes the fragmentation RC seeks to address.

annotations.content: political narratives of success or failure in the making and sustaining of such communities are also inescapably narratives of embodied moral enquiry
annotations.anchor_text: _political narratives of success or failure in the making_
annotations.interpretation: This is one of the most heavily marked passages on the page. Tom underlines MacIntyre's claim that political history — the rise and fall of communities — is simultaneously moral history. There is no politically neutral narrative; every account of civic success or failure carries moral weight. This connects the narrative turn to political philosophy directly.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this means that the story of American civic decline (or any community's fragmentation) is not merely sociological but constitutes a moral narrative of failure — and its recovery would be a moral narrative of success. This gives the RC project an inherently narrative, not just programmatic, character.

annotations.content: community as test-bed of moral charity + success
annotations.anchor_text: _political narratives of success or failure in the making_
annotations.interpretation: Tom translates MacIntyre's abstract point into an operational concept: the community is the 'test-bed' — a scientific/empirical metaphor — for moral charity and success. This is a striking formulation because it converts MacIntyre's historical-narrative point into a quasi-experimental one. Tom seems to be thinking about how RC could use existing communities as sites for observing whether traditions of virtue actually produce flourishing.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is perhaps the most operationally significant annotation on the page for RC. If community is the test-bed, then RC's methodology must include empirical engagement with actual communities — studying where moral traditions persist or collapse, not merely theorizing about them. The word 'charity' (caritas) adds a Thomistic/theological register.

annotations.content: Aquinas was the philosopher par excellence of theoretical enquiry into the practical life, Dante was the philosopher par excellence of the practical life itself
annotations.anchor_text: _Aquinas was the philosopher par excellence_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks MacIntyre's pivotal distinction between Aquinas (theorist of practice) and Dante (practitioner-philosopher). This is the complementarity MacIntyre is building: neither pure theory (encyclopaedist) nor pure narrative (genealogist) but the two in productive tension. The distinction also implies that full Thomistic moral enquiry requires BOTH — the Summa AND the Commedia.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's interest in the Thomistic tradition cannot rest with academic theology; it needs embodied literary and civic expression. Dante as the 'philosopher of the practical life itself' suggests that RC may need its own narrative artists, not just its theorists.

annotations.content: moral enquiry can only therefore extend itself by drawing both upon Aquinas and upon Dante
annotations.anchor_text: _moral enquiry can only therefore extend itself_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's methodological conclusion: Thomistic moral enquiry is not complete without both theoretical and narrative dimensions. This is a prescription, not just a description. The word 'only' is doing heavy lifting — MacIntyre is closing off purely theoretical approaches as insufficient.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this provides a methodological warrant for combining philosophical argument with narrative, historical, and community-based approaches. RC cannot be a purely academic project; it needs the Dante-dimension of embodied civic narrative.

annotations.content: Modern moral philosophy has in general been blind to the complementary character of narrative and theory both in moral enquiry and in the moral life itself
annotations.anchor_text: _Modern moral philosophy has in general been blind_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines what is arguably the most polemical claim on the page — 'blind' is a strong word, and MacIntyre is indicting the entire Enlightenment tradition of moral philosophy for a structural failure of vision. Tom's marking suggests he sees this as the pivot of the chapter's argument and possibly the book's central diagnostic claim.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the diagnosis that motivates the RC project: the institutions, universities, and public discourses shaped by modern moral philosophy are structurally unable to see the narrative dimension of moral life — which is why civic renewal requires recovering pre-modern frameworks.

annotations.content: narrative embodies theory of theory is essential for understanding
annotations.anchor_text: _complementary character of narrative and theory both in moral enquiry_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is glossing MacIntyre's complementarity claim in his own words, noting that the relationship runs both ways: narrative embodies theory, AND theoretical enquiry is essential for understanding narrative. This mutual dependence is what MacIntyre means by 'complementary,' and Tom is making it explicit as a bidirectional formula. The phrasing 'theory of theory' may be a slip or abbreviation for 'theory of [narrative / moral life].'
annotations.rc_relevance: This annotation captures the methodological heart of what RC must do: it cannot simply tell community stories (narrative without theory) or simply construct philosophical arguments (theory without narrative embodiment). The project must hold both in tension.

annotations.content: what type of enacted narrative would be the embodiment, in the actions and transactions of actual social life, of this particular theory?
annotations.anchor_text: _what type of enacted narrative would be the embodiment_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks MacIntyre's key diagnostic question — the test of any moral theory's adequacy. This is the operationalization criterion: a theory that cannot specify its corresponding enacted narrative is not yet fully understood. Tom seems to recognize this as a methodological touchstone that could structure RC's evaluative framework.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this becomes a powerful evaluative question to ask of modern liberal institutions and the theories behind them: what enacted narrative corresponds to, say, Rawlsian justice or utilitarian policy? MacIntyre implies the answer reveals these theories' inadequacy.

annotations.content: we are each engaged in enacting our own narrative, so revealing implicitly, and sometimes also explicitly, the not always coherent theoretical stance presupposed by that enactment. Hence differences between rival moral theories are always in key part differences in the corresponding narrative.
annotations.anchor_text: _we are each engaged in enacting our own narrative_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets this passage, suggesting it is a unit he wants to treat as a whole — possibly for quotation or as a section summary. The claim that individual moral lives reveal (often incoherently) a theoretical stance is both diagnostic and somewhat humbling: none of us fully knows our own moral theory until we examine our narrative. The conclusion — that rival theories are rival narratives — is the logical payoff of the whole page.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this means that participants in fragmented communities are already enacting rival moral narratives, often without knowing it. RC's work of 'resurrecting civility' involves making those implicit narratives explicit and evaluating them — a form of practical moral enquiry in MacIntyre's sense.

annotations.content: rival moral theories but also as projects for constructing rival forms of moral narrative
annotations.anchor_text: _rival moral theories but also as projects for constructing rival_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the closing reframing: the three rivals are not just epistemological positions but narrative projects — they are competing to author the story of morality itself. This is MacIntyre's most ambitious claim on the page, elevating the stakes from academic debate to cultural-historical contestation.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the RC project's competitive landscape made explicit: RC is not merely arguing for a position in moral philosophy but entering a contest over which narrative of moral community will shape public life. Recognizing the encyclopaedist and genealogist as rival narrative projects (not just rival theories) clarifies why RC cannot win through argument alone.

annotations.content: Cmty as testbed
annotations.anchor_text: _rival moral theories but also as projects for constructing rival forms_
annotations.interpretation: Tom repeats and amplifies his earlier 'community as test-bed' note at the bottom of the page, this time in larger, underlined script — suggesting this is a major takeaway or organizing concept for his reading of this page. The repetition at top and bottom of the page frames his entire reading of it through this lens. 'Cmty' is his consistent abbreviation for 'community.'
annotations.rc_relevance: This bottom-of-page repetition confirms that 'community as testbed' is Tom's central RC appropriation of this page: the community is both the site where moral narratives are enacted and the empirical criterion by which rival moral theories are evaluated. This could become a structural principle of the RC methodology.

annotations.content: *illustrate D[ante?]
annotations.anchor_text: _Dante was the philosopher par excellence of the practical life_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks a note to himself to find illustrations or examples from Dante — presumably for use in RC materials or writing. The asterisk signals a task or action item, not just a reading note. This suggests Tom is actively thinking about how to operationalize the Aquinas/Dante complementarity in RC's own narrative or pedagogical work.
annotations.rc_relevance: If RC is to follow MacIntyre's prescription that Thomistic moral enquiry requires both Aquinas and Dante, Tom is already planning the 'Dante side' — the narrative, literary, or case-study dimension of the project.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the superiority of one narrative tradition over its rivals is demonstrated by its capacity to incorporate and retell competing narratives as episodes within its own larger story — not merely to refute them, but to account for the very telling of those rival stories. He then pivots to the specific case of Aquinas, arguing that Aquinas's distinctive philosophical achievement lay in his craft-skill of integrating two deeply antagonistic traditions — Augustinianism and Aristotelianism — and that this integration was not incidental but constitutive of Thomistic moral enquiry. The Thomistic project, MacIntyre insists, must be understood as a response to conflict, and any adequate account of it must begin by examining what those two warring traditions actually claimed.
pages.ocr_text: aft-skill asa pht-

annotations.content: Dante's standard for judging the superiority of narratives
annotations.anchor_text: _One possible answer was supplied by Dante_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is naming the criterion MacIntyre extracts from Dante — that a narrative tradition proves its superiority by its capacity to subsume rivals. The label 'narratives' added at the end suggests Tom is generalizing the principle beyond Dante's specific literary case to a broader epistemological standard. This is a key structural move: Tom recognizes MacIntyre is offering a meta-criterion for adjudicating between rival traditions, not just describing Dante's poetics. Tom may intend to cite this as a methodological touchstone in RC writing.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly maps to the RC project's engagement with MacIntyre's tripartite structure: the 'tradition' version of moral enquiry wins by incorporation, not refutation — a model RC can use to argue that Thomistic civic tradition can absorb and reframe liberal and genealogical critiques rather than simply opposing them.

annotations.content: that narrative prevails over its rivals which is able to include its rivals within it, not only to retell their stories as episodes within its story, but to tell the story of the telling of their stories as such episodes.
annotations.anchor_text: _One possible answer was supplied by Dante_
annotations.interpretation: This is the core definitional claim Tom is capturing — the precise Dantean criterion for narrative superiority. The highlighting signals Tom sees this as a quotable, foundational passage, likely for direct use in RC writing or teaching. The layered logic (retelling the story AND retelling the telling of the story) is philosophically dense, and Tom's marking suggests he wants to preserve this exact formulation rather than paraphrase it.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this passage is crucial because it provides a non-coercive, non-relativist standard for why a tradition-constituted moral community might claim authority — it wins by comprehensiveness and generosity toward rivals, not by suppression. This speaks directly to RC's concern with civility as intellectual hospitality.

annotations.content: Yet we cannot hope even to pose the question of how Dante's standard might be fruitfully applied without first elaborating an adequately full account of the Thomistic understanding of moral enquiry.
annotations.anchor_text: _yet we cannot hope even to pose_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets this as a methodological hinge — MacIntyre is flagging that the Dantean criterion cannot be applied abstractly; it requires a fully elaborated Thomistic framework first. Tom likely recognizes this as MacIntyre setting up the rest of the chapter's work, and the bracket may signal a note-to-self: 'this is why the next section matters.' It also suggests Tom is tracking the book's argumentative scaffolding, not just its conclusions.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's concern that civic recovery cannot be achieved through surface-level appeals to tradition — it requires the hard philosophical work of reconstructing the internal logic of Thomistic moral enquiry before applying it to contemporary fragmentation.

annotations.content: what made Aquinas preeminent in the exercise of his craft-skill as a philosopher was his ability to integrate two quite different traditions, not only distinct but in their first confrontations deeply at odds with one another
annotations.anchor_text: _made Aquinas preeminent in the exercise_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the claim that Aquinas's genius was specifically integrative — a craft-skill, not merely intellectual brilliance. The emphasis on 'craft-skill' is significant: MacIntyre is using the Aristotelian vocabulary of techne to describe philosophical excellence, which Tom is likely tracking as a key terminological move. This also sets up the claim that Thomism is not a stable system but an achievement wrested from conflict.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's interest in character formation through institutions maps directly here: if Thomistic moral enquiry is itself a craft-constituted practice achieved through conflict-integration, then RC's recovery project must involve forming practitioners capable of that same integrative skill — not just transmitting doctrines.

annotations.content: asterisk with arrow pointing right toward margin
annotations.anchor_text: _not only to retell their stories as episodes_
annotations.interpretation: The asterisk with rightward arrow appears to cross-reference the bottom margin note (also marked with an asterisk). Tom is flagging this passage as the textual anchor for the note below about 'competing narratives.' This is Tom's system of internal cross-referencing — linking a key theoretical claim in the text to his own synthetic note at the bottom of the page.
annotations.rc_relevance: The cross-reference suggests Tom is actively building a network of RC-relevant concepts from this page — the Dantean criterion for narrative superiority is being linked to his own formulation about competing narratives as a usable RC framework.

annotations.content: small asterisk or tick mark
annotations.anchor_text: _without this integration none of the distinctive features_
annotations.interpretation: A secondary symbol near the claim that Aquinas's standpoint would not have appeared without the integration of rival traditions. Tom marks this as a key argumentative moment — MacIntyre is making a strong historical-philosophical claim that Thomism is not just enhanced by integration but is ontologically dependent on it. Tom may be flagging this for its anti-foundationalist implication: there is no 'pure' Thomism prior to conflict.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's argument against nostalgic traditionalism — the tradition worth recovering is not a pre-conflict purity but the tradition forged through engagement with rivals, which is precisely the civic model RC wants to recommend.

annotations.content: Aquinas's philosophy and theology were a response to conflict and it is from that conflict therefore that all Thomistic argument has to begin
annotations.anchor_text: _Aquinas's philosophy and theology were a response_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's explicit statement of the agonistic origin of Thomism. This is a thesis sentence and Tom knows it — the highlighting is deliberate and precise. The claim that 'all Thomistic argument has to begin' from conflict is methodologically radical: it means no Thomistic argument can be properly conducted without first re-enacting the encounter with Augustinianism and Aristotelianism. Tom may be flagging this as a challenge to neo-scholastic Thomism that presents the system as complete and self-standing.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this passage grounds the project's engagement with contemporary rivals (liberalism, genealogical critique) not as a concession but as a methodological necessity — Thomistic civic renewal must begin from honest confrontation with what opposes it, not from retreat into confessional insularity.

annotations.content: for Aquinas was certainly an Augustinian, and of Aristotelianism, for Aquinas was equally certainly an Aristotelian. What then were these two forms of moral enquiry which Aquinas encountered as warring rivals during his own education?
annotations.anchor_text: _Aquinas was certainly an Augustinian and of Aristotelianism_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets the closing question of the passage — MacIntyre's pivot to the next section. The bracket signals Tom is tracking the book's argumentative structure: this question is not rhetorical but genuinely programmatic, setting up the historical reconstruction that follows. Tom may be using brackets throughout the book to mark 'section transition sentences' as a navigational aid for later review.
annotations.rc_relevance: This transition matters for RC because it models the kind of genealogical self-awareness RC advocates: before making claims about civic virtue, one must understand the rival moral frameworks that shaped and challenged the tradition one is recovering.

annotations.content: * Hostile D's competing narratives →
annotations.anchor_text: _that narrative prevails over its rivals_
annotations.interpretation: This is Tom's most complex annotation and likely a pedagogical or writing note. The asterisk links back to the Dantean criterion passage above. 'Hostile D's competing narratives' may mean 'hostile/competing narratives within Dante' or may abbreviate a person's name (a student, colleague, or interlocutor identified as 'D'). 'SE/SF bar square off + N mechanics' is cryptic — possibly 'SE' and 'N' are abbreviations for specific thinkers or positions that Tom wants to set in opposition using MacIntyre's narrative-superiority mechanics. '(Scholastic?): write your own' may be a self-directive: apply the Dantean criterion by writing a narrative that incorporates rivals. The note is working-through in character, not just descriptive.
annotations.rc_relevance: This bottom-margin cluster suggests Tom is actively translating MacIntyre's abstract criterion into a practical RC methodology — possibly designing an exercise, argument structure, or RC text that demonstrates narrative superiority by incorporation rather than mere refutation, which is central to RC's non-adversarial model of civic engagement.

chapters.title: The Augustinian Conception of Moral Enquiry

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that in medieval Augustinian culture, the relationship between reader and text is reciprocally constitutive: the reader interprets the text, but the text also interprets and transforms the reader. This creates a version of the Meno paradox — you cannot read the texts aright until you are transformed, but you cannot be transformed until you read them aright — which requires resolving through obedient trust in a teacher and a tradition of interpretive commentary. The pre-rational reordering of the self must precede the cognitive capacity to recognize good reasons, meaning moral formation is logically prior to moral reasoning.

annotations.content: the relationship between the key texts of that culture and their reader was twofold
annotations.anchor_text: _In medieval Augustinian culture the relationship_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the opening claim because it establishes the structural premise of the whole chapter: the text-reader relationship is not one-directional (reader masters text) but mutually constitutive. This is the hinge on which the Augustinian model differs from Enlightenment encyclopaedism, where the reader stands over the text as sovereign reasoner. Tom is likely flagging this as the foundational contrast MacIntyre is building.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly maps onto RC's concern with how institutions and traditions form character — the Augustinian model insists that formation precedes comprehension, which challenges liberal assumptions that individuals come to traditions as autonomous evaluators.

annotations.content: eg Petritz
annotations.anchor_text: _the relationship between the key texts of that culture_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is noting a concrete example or parallel figure — likely 'Petritz' is a shorthand for a thinker, scholar, or case study that illustrates this twofold reader-text relationship. The 'eg' signals Tom is thinking comparatively, perhaps connecting MacIntyre's abstract claim to a historical or contemporary exemplar he has encountered elsewhere in his research. The name may refer to a medievalist or an Augustinian commentator Tom has been reading alongside MacIntyre.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's method involves recovering historical exemplars of civic and moral formation; Tom's note suggests he is already building a roster of figures who embody the Augustinian model of tradition-embedded reading as a counterpoint to encyclopaedist autonomy.

annotations.content: interpreting the text
annotations.anchor_text: _the task of interpreting the text_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this phrase to isolate the first of the two roles assigned to the reader. By marking it separately, he is tracking MacIntyre's careful analytic structure: role one (active: reader interprets) versus role two (passive: reader is interpreted). This is a setup for the paradox that follows, and Tom seems to be parsing the argument's logical scaffolding.
annotations.rc_relevance: The distinction between interpreting and being interpreted maps onto RC's tension between individual agency and institutional formation — a key fault line in debates about subsidiarity and the role of tradition in shaping civic actors.

annotations.content: What the reader, as thus interpreted by the texts, has to learn about him or herself is that it is only the self as transformed through and by the reading of the texts which will be capable of reading the texts aright.
annotations.anchor_text: _What the reader as thus interpreted by the texts_
annotations.interpretation: This is the most heavily marked passage on the page, and Tom's bracket signals he considers it the argumentative core — the precise statement of the self-referential loop. The bracketing (rather than just underlining) suggests Tom sees this as a unit of argument to be extracted and cited. He is likely noting that this is MacIntyre's clearest articulation of why Augustinian moral enquiry cannot be entered from the outside by a neutral reasoner.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is central to RC's critique of Enlightenment rationalism: the encyclopaedist pretends to approach tradition from a view from nowhere, but MacIntyre (and Augustine) insist the self must already be undergoing transformation to read aright. This is the philosophical grounding for RC's claim that character formation must precede civic reasoning.

annotations.content: only the self as transformed through and by the reading of the texts which will be capable of reading the texts aright
annotations.anchor_text: _only the self as transformed through and by_
annotations.interpretation: The highlight reinforces the bracket, singling out the exact formulation of the paradox's resolution: transformation is the precondition of right reading. Tom appears to be color-coding this as a quotable thesis statement. The emphasis on 'only' suggests Tom is attuned to MacIntyre's exclusivity claim — this is not merely one path to good reading but the only one within this tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this sentence is weaponizable against both liberal civic education models (which assume rational deliberation can occur prior to character formation) and against purely procedural accounts of democratic discourse.

annotations.content: the paradox of Plato's Meno
annotations.anchor_text: _encounters apparent paradox at the outset a Christian version of the paradox of Plato's Meno_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the explicit philosophical reference because it names the structure of the problem MacIntyre is deploying. The Meno paradox (you cannot search for what you don't know, and if you know it you don't need to search) is being given an Augustinian inflection. Tom is tracking MacIntyre's move of translating a Greek aporetic puzzle into a Christian pedagogical problem, which is a key moment in MacIntyre's tradition-rivalry thesis.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's tripartite structure (encyclopaedist / genealogist / tradition) depends on showing that each tradition has its own way of handling foundational paradoxes. Tom marking the Meno parallel suggests he sees this as evidence that the Augustinian tradition does not avoid Greek philosophy but transforms it — relevant to RC's argument about the continuity and reform of traditions.

annotations.content: paradox
annotations.anchor_text: _encounters apparent paradox at the outset a Christian version_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes 'paradox' in the left margin as a structural label — a navigational flag so he can quickly locate MacIntyre's statement of the central aporia when returning to this page. The simplicity of the label suggests this is a retrieval tool rather than a new thought. However, it also signals that Tom considers the paradox structure the organizing concept of this section, not merely an illustrative aside.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC must grapple with its own version of this paradox: how do you invite people who have been formed by liberal individualism into a tradition of civic virtue they cannot yet recognize as good? Tom flagging this paradox suggests he is thinking about RC's pedagogical and rhetorical challenge.

annotations.content: a teacher and an obedient trust
annotations.anchor_text: _The person in this predicament requires two things a teacher_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the two-part solution to the Meno paradox: teacher and obedient trust. The dual underlining (or single underline capturing both terms) shows Tom isolating MacIntyre's answer as a discrete claim. 'Obedient trust' is a philosophically loaded phrase — it is not mere deference but a reasoned submission to authority that has not yet been verified by the student's own transformed understanding. Tom likely finds this either compelling or in need of defense.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's account of subsidiarity and institutional authority — the Augustinian model requires that individuals submit to institutional wisdom before they can evaluate it, which is a hard sell in contemporary liberal culture and a central challenge RC must address.

annotations.content: pred noncognitive reason
annotations.anchor_text: _The person in this predicament requires two things_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is labeling the epistemological structure MacIntyre is describing: 'pred' almost certainly abbreviates 'pre-rational' or 'pre-cognitive,' and 'noncognitive reason' signals Tom is identifying this as a case where moral formation operates prior to and independent of cognitive justification. This is a sophisticated philosophical annotation — Tom is mapping MacIntyre's argument onto debates in moral epistemology about whether practical reason requires prior dispositional formation (virtue ethics) versus whether reasons are always available to any rational agent (Kantian/encyclopaedist view).
annotations.rc_relevance: This is one of RC's core philosophical commitments: that civic virtue cannot be generated by rational argument alone, that there must be pre-rational formation of character and disposition. Tom's note shows he sees MacIntyre's Augustinian model as philosophical grounding for RC's resistance to purely deliberative models of democratic civility.

annotations.content: Wilson Woldt
annotations.anchor_text: _The person in this predicament requires two things_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes two names — likely scholars, collaborators, or interlocutors he associates with the argument on this page. 'Wilson' and 'Woldt' may be colleagues in the RC project, or thinkers whose work parallels or challenges MacIntyre's claim about pre-rational formation and obedient trust. The placement near the 'noncognitive reason' note suggests Tom is thinking about who else in his intellectual network has grappled with this problem and whose work he needs to synthesize with MacIntyre's.
annotations.rc_relevance: If these are RC collaborators or interlocutors, Tom is already doing the work of building a scholarly conversation around this specific claim — which suggests the pre-rational formation argument is one RC needs to either adopt, defend, or modify in dialogue with these thinkers.

annotations.content: standing of the texts which becomes available only to the transformed self
annotations.anchor_text: _standing of the texts which becomes available only to the transformed self_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the recurrence of 'only to the transformed self,' reinforcing his earlier bracket and highlight. The repetition of the mark suggests Tom sees this phrase as MacIntyre's refrain — the argumentative pivot that keeps returning. By highlighting it again here, Tom may be noting that MacIntyre is deepening the claim: it is not just reading aright that requires transformation, but the very understanding (standing-under) of the texts.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this phrase carries weight in debates about who can participate in civic discourse and on what terms — the Augustinian answer is that civic understanding is not democratically available to all comers but requires formation within a tradition, which has both conservative and radical implications for RC's project.

annotations.content: certain attitudes and dispositions, certain virtues, before he or she can know why these are to be accounted virtues
annotations.anchor_text: _certain attitudes and dispositions certain virtues before_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the precise formulation of the priority thesis: virtues must be inculcated before they can be recognized as virtues. This is the sharpest statement of the anti-encyclopaedist position on the page, and Tom is flagging it as a quotable crux. The word 'before' is doing enormous philosophical work here, and Tom's highlight suggests he recognizes it as the load-bearing term in MacIntyre's argument against liberal moral epistemology.
annotations.rc_relevance: This sentence is potentially the single most important sentence on the page for RC: it provides MacIntyre's philosophical warrant for the claim that civic formation must precede civic reasoning, grounding RC's entire project in Augustinian moral epistemology rather than Enlightenment proceduralism.

annotations.content: prerational reordering of the self
annotations.anchor_text: _So a prerational reordering of the self has to occur_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles this phrase as a key technical term — 'prerational reordering' is MacIntyre's coinage for the Augustinian process of self-transformation that must precede rational engagement with moral texts. The circle (rather than underline or highlight) suggests Tom sees this as a term of art to be tracked across the book, possibly to appear in his own writing. He may be noting that MacIntyre is here naming something that other traditions (encyclopaedist, genealogist) deny is possible or necessary.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC needs a vocabulary for talking about formation that precedes deliberation; 'prerational reordering' is a candidate term Tom may be considering adopting or engaging critically. It also connects to RC's interest in how institutions (family, church, civic associations) perform this reordering in practice.

annotations.content: this reordering requires obedient trust, not only in the authority of this particular teacher, but in that of the whole tradition of interpretative commentary
annotations.anchor_text: _this reordering requires obedient trust not only in the authority_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the extension of 'obedient trust' from teacher to tradition — this is MacIntyre's move from the personal pedagogical relationship to the institutional and historical one. The highlight captures what is arguably the most politically and theologically charged claim on the page: submission is owed not to an individual but to a whole tradition of interpretation. Tom is likely flagging both the power of this claim for RC and its potential vulnerabilities (who defines the tradition? what about reform?).
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's tension between tradition and reform: if obedient trust is owed to 'the whole tradition of interpretative commentary,' how does that tradition ever correct itself? Tom flagging this suggests he is already anticipating the question RC must answer about whether Augustinian moral enquiry is inherently conservative or contains resources for self-critique.

annotations.content: double vertical line (||) in left margin
annotations.anchor_text: _What the reader as thus interpreted by the texts has to learn_
annotations.interpretation: The double vertical bar is a classic scholarly symbol for 'extremely important passage' — stronger than a single line. Tom uses this to flag the bracketed section as the most significant passage on the page. The symbol functions as a retrieval and priority marker: when skimming later, this passage should be the first stop. The double bar may also signal that Tom intends to quote or closely engage with this passage in his own writing.
annotations.rc_relevance: The fact that Tom uses his strongest annotation symbol here confirms that the self-transformation / right-reading paradox is the argumentative core he sees as most generative for RC — it is the passage he will likely build from in constructing RC's account of moral formation.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that for Augustine, reading sacred Scripture is not merely an intellectual act but a transformative one in which the reader discovers their own life re-enacted in the narrative the text speaks — the paradigmatic record of this being Augustine's own Confessions. He then examines whether Augustine's doctrine of interpretation extended to nonsacred texts, noting that the powers of discrimination required to judge secular literature rightly can themselves become an expression of distinctively Christian virtues. Finally, MacIntyre begins to argue that the relationship of reader to both sacred and secular texts is mediated by a tradition of teaching and initiation into which no merely human teacher can on their own successfully initiate a learner.

annotations.content: the texts are talking about me - testimony
annotations.anchor_text: _the reading of texts is part of the history_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is glossing MacIntyre's claim that the reader discovers him or herself inside the texts that Scripture speaks — he condenses this into the phenomenological insight that authentic reading is testimonial in character: the text addresses me personally and I am a witness within its narrative. The word 'testimony' likely signals Tom's awareness of this as a specific epistemological category (perhaps invoking Coady or Reformed epistemology), not just an affective response. He may be flagging this as a bridge concept between Augustine's hermeneutics and contemporary debates about the authority of tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly illuminates the RC theme of tradition as the medium through which moral identity is formed — if texts speak about me, then initiation into a reading tradition is constitutive of selfhood, not merely instrumental to it. This connects to MacIntyre's broader argument that tradition-constituted enquiry is not optional but identity-forming.

annotations.content: morled uctum of abeeme [likely: 'ordered reordering of experience' — partially obscured handwriting at top]
annotations.anchor_text: _to be initiated through his or her reordering_
annotations.interpretation: This annotation appears at the very top of the page and seems to be Tom glossing or paraphrasing the concept of conversion as an 'ordered reordering' — a paradox he finds worth naming. The handwriting is difficult and may include a word like 'experience.' Tom may be noting that conversion (metanoia) has its own internal rational structure even though it ruptures prior commitments, which is relevant to MacIntyre's account of how one enters a tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: This maps onto the RC tension between tradition and reform: transformation is not arbitrary rupture but a reordering that is itself intelligible within a tradition's narrative logic. This is central to MacIntyre's rejection of Enlightenment accounts of rational autonomy.

annotations.content: the reading of texts is part of the history of which the same texts speak
annotations.anchor_text: _the reading of texts is part of the history_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's most compressed and structurally important claim on this page — a hermeneutical circle in which the act of reading is itself embedded in the narrative that the text narrates. This is not merely circularity but a claim about the ontological thickness of tradition: the reader is always already inside the story. Tom likely sees this as a key premise for MacIntyre's entire account of tradition-constituted enquiry.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to the RC project's engagement with MacIntyre's critique of encyclopaedist rationalism: the encyclopaedist pretends to stand outside all narratives, but Augustine shows that reading is always already a traditioned act. Civic and moral formation cannot be neutral.

annotations.content: The paradigmatic record of such a discovery was Augustine's Confessions and it was of course Augustine who had formulated in classical form the doctrine of understanding which came to inform the medieval tradition, a Platonic conception to which Augustine gave a Christian form.
annotations.anchor_text: _paradigmatic record of such a discovery was Augustine's Confessions_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets this passage to isolate MacIntyre's identification of the Confessions as both the paradigm case of self-discovery through reading and the source of the medieval doctrine of understanding. The bracket likely signals that Tom sees this as a structural hinge in MacIntyre's argument — moving from the phenomenology of reading to the historical transmission of an interpretive tradition. He may also be flagging the Platonic-to-Christian transformation as a model for how traditions absorb and transform rival frameworks.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to the RC theme of the 'tradition' version of moral enquiry and its Augustinian-Thomistic lineage. The Confessions as paradigm case models exactly what RC seeks to recover: moral self-knowledge mediated through a narrative tradition rather than abstract principle.

annotations.content: The recognition of the Platonism which the tradition thus inherited raises at once the question of whether Augustine's doctrine of interpretation extended to nonsacred texts.
annotations.anchor_text: _raises at once the question of whether Augustine's doctrine_
annotations.interpretation: Tom places a box/bracket around this sentence and writes '(Rep)' in the margin — likely short for 'reprise' or 'repetition,' signaling that this question will be returned to or has been raised before. He is tracking the structural argument: MacIntyre is flagging that the extension of Augustinian hermeneutics to secular texts is the crux question for whether his tradition can resource a full account of moral enquiry, not just sacred reading. Tom recognizes this as a pivot point.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is central to RC's concern with whether classical and Augustinian moral resources can speak to secular civic life — not just ecclesial communities. If Augustine's interpretive doctrine does not extend to nonsacred texts, the RC project faces a serious limitation in using this tradition to address social fragmentation.

annotations.content: (Rep)
annotations.anchor_text: _raises at once the question of whether Augustine's doctrine of interpretation extended to nonsacred texts_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes '(Rep)' next to the bracketed sentence about whether Augustine's doctrine extends to nonsacred texts. This almost certainly abbreviates 'reprise' — Tom is noting that MacIntyre will return to or has already flagged this question, and he wants to track it as a recurring structural node in the argument. It could also mean 'repetition' in a critical sense, if Tom thinks MacIntyre is circling back without advancing the argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: The question of whether Augustinian interpretation extends beyond sacred texts is precisely the RC problem of whether a tradition-formed virtue ethics can address plural, secular civic contexts — a key tension in the project between ecclesial and civic applications of MacIntyre's framework.

annotations.content: on Augustine's view the powers of discrimination which are required to judge rightly in understanding secular literature can themselves become an expression of peculiarly Christian virtues,
annotations.anchor_text: _powers of discrimination which are required to judge rightly_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's answer to the 'nonsacred texts' question: secular judgment is not abandoned but transfigured — the virtues required to read Cicero or Vergil well are themselves Christianized. Tom likely sees this as MacIntyre's most important move here: not a retreat from secular culture but its transformation from within. This has direct bearing on whether classical civic virtues survive or are sublated in Augustine's framework.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is perhaps the most RC-relevant passage on the page: it models exactly how the RC project might argue that classical republican virtues (Cicero, Vergil, Sallust on Roman virtue) are not simply abandoned by the Christian tradition but re-formed as expressions of charity-ordered virtue. This speaks directly to civic friendship and institutional character formation.

annotations.content: The relationship of reader to both sacred and secular texts is then mediated by a certain kind of teaching and a certain kind of initiation into a tradition of reading and interpretation. But no merely human teacher
annotations.anchor_text: _mediated by a certain kind of teaching and a certain kind of initiation_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the transition into MacIntyre's next argument: that tradition-mediated reading requires initiation through teaching, but that no merely human teacher suffices. Tom is likely tracking this as the moment where MacIntyre introduces a theological or epistemological excess — something beyond human pedagogical capacity is required. This sets up the Augustine/illumination argument and connects to the limits of purely institutional or human accounts of formation.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly implicates the RC theme of the role of institutions in forming character: if no merely human teacher can accomplish initiation into the relevant reading tradition, then purely institutional or civic accounts of formation are incomplete. This is a serious challenge to any secularized RC framework and Tom appears to be tracking it carefully.

annotations.content: Arrow pointing downward with asterisk/star symbol beside 'no merely human teacher' at bottom right margin
annotations.anchor_text: _no merely human teacher can by him or herself succeed_
annotations.interpretation: The downward arrow and asterisk mark signal that Tom is flagging this claim as one that continues onto the next page and is highly significant — the arrow says 'follow this,' the asterisk says 'this matters.' Tom is alerting himself that the argument about the insufficiency of merely human teaching is the structural key to the page's conclusion and he wants to carry it forward in his reading.
annotations.rc_relevance: The insufficiency of merely human teaching is directly relevant to RC's engagement with subsidiarity and institutional formation: if human institutions alone cannot initiate learners into the tradition of moral reading, what is the role of the church, the polis, or other mediating institutions? This is a live RC question.

pages.summary: MacIntyre reconstructs Augustine's Platonic-derived epistemology, arguing that learning requires the mind to find within itself a source of intelligibility beyond itself — a movement toward timeless forms apprehended only in the light of God. Because the will is initially perverse and cannot naturally move toward the good, faith in authority must precede rational understanding, humility is the necessary first step in education, and rational justification is therefore essentially retrospective rather than foundationalist. The reading of texts mirrors this structure: movement toward what a text says and what it speaks generates a tradition of commentary that progressively raises the burden of dissent.
