annotations.content: Pink/red checkmark or 'k/s' notation in upper left margin
annotations.anchor_text: _What was at stake for medieval Platonism_
annotations.interpretation: This 'k/s' marking (possibly 'key section' or a personal shorthand) signals that Tom regards the opening question about what was at stake for medieval Platonism as a structurally pivotal moment. It may indicate that this passage is one he intends to excerpt or quote directly in RC materials. The brevity suggests a rapid tagging rather than a developed thought — Tom is flagging the whole opening paragraph as load-bearing for his argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: The question of what was at stake for medieval Platonism directly feeds the RC project's engagement with MacIntyre's genealogy of tradition-constituted enquiry — specifically how a tradition's internal debates (not external critiques) drive its development.

annotations.content: What was at stake for medieval Platonism in that controversy
annotations.anchor_text: _What was at stake for medieval Platonism_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the framing question itself, not an answer, suggesting he is tracking MacIntyre's method of posing internal-to-tradition questions. By marking the question rather than a thesis, Tom may be noting MacIntyre's dialectical pedagogy — the way understanding a tradition requires reconstructing what its participants took to be at stake, not imposing external criteria.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects directly to MacIntyre's account of tradition-constituted rationality: RC's argument against Enlightenment universalism depends on showing that moral enquiry is always shaped by what a community perceives to be at stake internally.

annotations.content: Augustine had transformed Plato's eide into exemplars of created things in the mind of God
annotations.anchor_text: _Augustine had transformed Plato's eide into exemplars_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the Augustinian transformation of Platonic forms into divine exemplars as a key doctrinal hinge. This is not merely historical notation — it signals that Tom recognizes this move as the theological grounding for a specifically Augustinian epistemology where truth-conformity is conformity to God's mind, not to abstract forms. He may be cross-referencing this against later RC discussions of how institutions mediate access to moral truth.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry depends on understanding how Augustinian Platonism feeds into Aquinas; this annotation tracks the precise point where that lineage is established in MacIntyre's argument.

annotations.content: To progress in understanding the world of creation as it truly is is to move away from the initial judgments of everyday life
annotations.anchor_text: _to move away from the initial judgments of everyday life_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this because it represents the Augustinian epistemological program in miniature: moral and cognitive progress requires estrangement from pre-theoretical common sense. This is a point of tension for RC, which must negotiate between MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism and the Augustinian suspicion of ordinary language and judgment — a suspicion that could seem to undercut the very civic common ground RC wants to recover.
annotations.rc_relevance: This tension — between the tradition's demand for correction of everyday judgment and RC's hope for accessible civic virtue — is a live problem for the project. Tom may be flagging where MacIntyre's Augustinianism risks elitism.

annotations.content: the truth of things lies in their conformity to the exemplars
annotations.anchor_text: _the truth of things lies in their conformity_
annotations.interpretation: Underlining the conformity-to-exemplars thesis isolates the core Augustinian-Platonic epistemological claim. Tom is almost certainly marking this as the thesis Abelard will complicate — and thus as a before-and-after pivot in the argument. It may also resonate with RC's interest in how norms of civility require grounding in something beyond mere convention.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery of virtue ethics needs a metaphysical grounding for moral norms; this annotation tracks whether that grounding can survive the Abelardian critique and still support the kind of shared moral community RC envisions.

annotations.content: Platonism in this as in all its versions is antagonistic to ordinary language
annotations.anchor_text: _Platonism in this as in all its versions is antagonistic_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks MacIntyre's sweeping claim about Platonism's structural hostility to ordinary language. This is a strong thesis that has implications for RC: if all Platonism is antagonistic to ordinary language, then any tradition-based moral vocabulary must either embrace that hostility or find a non-Platonic grounding. Tom may be questioning whether MacIntyre's own Thomistic tradition escapes this charge.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's project of resurrecting civility depends on language that can do civic work — be heard and understood across communities. If the moral tradition RC draws on is inherently at war with ordinary language, that is a foundational problem for public moral discourse.

annotations.content: William of Champeaux
annotations.anchor_text: _what William of Champeaux had done was to transform_
annotations.interpretation: Circling William of Champeaux's name marks him as a key actor in the argument whose role Tom wants to keep clearly in view. This is a character-tracking annotation — Tom is ensuring he can follow the dramatis personae of the universals controversy as MacIntyre reconstructs it. The circle (rather than underline) suggests Tom wants to visually isolate the agent of the philosophical move being described.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC's engagement with MacIntyre's tradition-genealogy framework, understanding which figures represent which positions in the universals debate is necessary to track how the tradition developed through internal crisis and response — MacIntyre's central account of how traditions progress.

annotations.content: Platonism into a theory of meaning for ordinary language
annotations.anchor_text: _Platonism into a theory of meaning for ordinary language_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks William's specific philosophical move — transforming Platonism into a semantic theory — as the key innovation (and error) that Abelard will refute. This is the crux: William tried to make Platonism do the work of explaining how ordinary nouns mean what they mean, which creates the absurdities Abelard exposes. Tom recognizes this as the structural hinge of the page's argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: This move anticipates RC's concern with how moral language actually functions in communities: RC needs a theory of moral meaning that doesn't collapse into either Platonic abstraction or nominalist relativism — precisely the impasse this paragraph dramatizes.

annotations.content: So the apprehension of the true forms turns out to be something involved in comprehending ordinary meanings, not something that provides a telos for a long process of enquiry
annotations.anchor_text: _apprehension of the true forms turns out to be something involved_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as the problematic consequence of William's position: it collapses the distinction between ordinary semantic competence and metaphysical insight, making Platonic form-apprehension trivially present in everyday language use rather than the goal of a long moral-intellectual journey. This is precisely what MacIntyre wants to criticize — it short-circuits the kind of extended tradition-constituted enquiry he champions.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's account of moral formation: MacIntyre's tradition requires a long process of enquiry and habituation to approach moral truth; any shortcut (whether Williamite or Enlightenment) that bypasses that process is the target of the critique RC inherits.

annotations.content: Abelard's achievement in exhibiting the absurdities which followed from William's position
annotations.anchor_text: _Abelard's achievement in exhibiting the absurdities_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks MacIntyre's characterization of Abelard's work as an 'achievement' — a term of art in MacIntyre's virtue-and-tradition vocabulary where achievements are historically situated advances within a tradition's ongoing enquiry. Tom may be noting how MacIntyre's evaluative language encodes his philosophical commitments even in ostensibly historical description.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's method involves recovering past intellectual achievements as resources for present moral reconstruction — MacIntyre's framing of Abelard's work as an 'achievement' (not merely a historical event) models exactly the kind of retrieval RC attempts.

annotations.content: a movement towards a comprehension of the genuinely universal, but it does not of itself yield a knowledge of true essences
annotations.anchor_text: _movement towards a comprehension of the genuinely universal_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the careful qualification MacIntyre introduces: intellectual progress moves toward the universal without arriving at essential knowledge. This asymptotic structure — genuine movement without final arrival — is characteristic of MacIntyre's account of tradition-constituted enquiry and distinguishes it from both relativism (no progress) and rationalism (complete arrival). Tom is tracking MacIntyre's epistemological modesty.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is crucial for RC's project: it allows MacIntyre (and RC) to claim genuine moral progress and shared standards without claiming Enlightenment-style finished rationality. The 'movement towards' structure is what makes tradition-based civility possible without requiring metaphysical certainty.

annotations.content: key→
annotations.anchor_text: _Platonism in this as in all its versions_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks this section as 'key' with an arrow pointing to the text about Platonism's antagonism to ordinary language and William's transformation of it. This is a navigation annotation — Tom is building a personal index of load-bearing passages. The placement near the universals-as-meaning-theory passage suggests this is the passage he would assign or quote in RC materials.
annotations.rc_relevance: Flags the core of MacIntyre's account of the universals controversy as essential background for RC's argument about how moral traditions handle the gap between philosophical vocabulary and ordinary civic language.

annotations.content: Abelard distinction between intellectus universalis + essences → Ag Rel[ation?]
annotations.anchor_text: _intellectus universalis, the mind's everyday grasp of properties_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is summarizing in his own words the distinction MacIntyre attributes to Abelard: between the intellectus universalis (everyday conceptual grasp) and genuine apprehension of essences. The 'Ag Rel' notation likely abbreviates 'Aquinas's relation' or 'Augustinian relation' — Tom is making a cross-reference forward to the Aquinas synthesis mentioned later in the paragraph, tracking how Abelard's distinction anticipates the Thomistic solution. This is active theoretical work, not mere summarizing.
annotations.rc_relevance: This distinction is foundational for RC's account of moral epistemology: the intellectus universalis explains how ordinary civic participants can share moral concepts without requiring philosophical expertise, while the distinction from essences preserves the tradition's aspiration toward something more than mere convention.

annotations.content: anticipation of Aquinas's synthesis of a particular version of Augustinian Platonism with Aristotle
annotations.anchor_text: _anticipation of Aquinas's synthesis of a particular version_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets the claim about Aquinas's synthesis because it is structurally pivotal for MacIntyre's entire argument in the book — Aquinas represents the highest achievement of tradition-constituted enquiry, and showing Abelard as his anticipator establishes historical-rational continuity. Tom may also be noting that MacIntyre says 'a particular version' — the qualification is significant and might be marked as something to interrogate.
annotations.rc_relevance: The Abelard-to-Aquinas line is the backbone of MacIntyre's 'tradition' version of moral enquiry that RC draws on. Tom brackets it because RC's credibility depends on this genealogy being persuasive to contemporary audiences skeptical of medievalism.

annotations.content: Abelard rescued the controversy over universals from the earlier sterile debates between nominalists and realists
annotations.anchor_text: _Abelard rescued the controversy over universals from the earlier sterile debates_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines 'rescued' and 'sterile debates' — MacIntyre's evaluative language again. 'Sterile' is a term of art: debates that generate no progress within a tradition are sterile because they cannot be rationally adjudicated by internal standards. Tom is likely noting both MacIntyre's philosophical judgment and his rhetorical strategy of casting the pre-Abelardian impasse as a kind of epistemic dead end that tradition-based enquiry overcomes.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's project implicitly claims to rescue contemporary moral discourse from its own sterile debates — the parallel structure (sterile impasse → tradition-constituted breakthrough) is a model RC wants to replicate for present social fragmentation.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the Augustinian epistemology underlying Bernard of Clairvaux's condemnation of Abelard reveals something structural about tradition-based enquiry: the integrity of the life of enquiry requires submission to authority, because heresy is always a sign of pride — elevating one's own judgment above that of genuine authority. Authority is not merely an external constraint but an internal necessity of the Augustinian scheme, because rationally unjustified belief must precede understanding; belief accepted on authority is the precondition of moral and intellectual formation.

annotations.content: Abelard↓ (with arrow pointing downward)
annotations.anchor_text: _To understand Abelard in this perspective_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging Abelard as a structural marker — the downward arrow likely signals that Abelard's trajectory in this section is one of descent or submission, not vindication. This is not merely a name-check; Tom is tracking the arc of Abelard's story as MacIntyre uses it to illustrate the epistemological stakes of authority within tradition. The bracket shape around the name suggests Tom may be mapping this as a case study node.
annotations.rc_relevance: Abelard functions in RC as the archetype of the brilliant reformer whose rationalism outstrips his formation — directly relevant to RC's tension between tradition and reform, and to MacIntyre's warning about enquiry untethered from institutional authority.

annotations.content: What Bernard and Abelard agreed upon, and no consistent Augustinian could have held otherwise, was that the integrity of the life of enquiry requires such interventions by authority.
annotations.anchor_text: _integrity of the life of enquiry requires_
annotations.interpretation: This is the thesis sentence of the entire page and Tom has correctly identified it as such. The highlighting signals that Tom sees this as the load-bearing claim: authority is not heteronomous to enquiry but constitutive of it. Tom is likely flagging this for direct quotation in RC work, since it crystallizes MacIntyre's anti-Enlightenment epistemology in a single sentence.
annotations.rc_relevance: This sentence is the core RC counter-claim against liberal autonomy: genuine enquiry requires institutional submission, not freedom from it — directly relevant to RC's recovery of Thomistic and Augustinian moral epistemology against Enlightenment individualism.

annotations.content: * role of authority
annotations.anchor_text: _requires such interventions by authority_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is naming the thematic concept this passage instantiates. The asterisk signals priority — this is not a casual observation but a load-bearing RC theme. 'Role of authority' is Tom's shorthand for the structural function authority plays in the Augustinian epistemology MacIntyre is reconstructing.
annotations.rc_relevance: The role of authority in forming enquirers — rather than merely constraining them — is a central RC concern, connecting to subsidiarity and the institutional formation of character. Tom is building a conceptual vocabulary here.

annotations.content: Will is more fundamental than intelligence
annotations.anchor_text: _Will is more fundamental than intelligence_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has underlined this as a counter-intuitive but pivotal claim. MacIntyre (via Bernard/Augustine) is inverting the Enlightenment hierarchy: rationality does not govern the will; the will's orientation (pride vs. humility) determines whether rationality functions rightly. Tom is likely flagging this because it directly contests the intellectualist assumptions of modern moral philosophy.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is a direct challenge to RC's dialogue partners in secular liberal ethics: if will precedes intelligence, then moral formation cannot be achieved through argument alone — it requires the transformation of desire. This connects RC to virtue ethics and the Augustinian account of disordered love.

annotations.content: it is only through the transformation of the will from a state of pride to one of humility that the intelligence can be rightly directed.
annotations.anchor_text: _transformation of the will from a state of pride_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this as the anthropological premise of the entire Augustinian epistemology. The claim is that right knowing requires prior moral conversion — not just better arguments. This is the heart of why Abelard's condemnation was, for Bernard, epistemically justified and not merely a power move. Tom is tracking this as the alternative to Enlightenment rationalism.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's engagement with MacIntyre depends heavily on this premise: civil discourse and moral enquiry are not primarily cognitive achievements but require the formation of character, specifically the virtue of humility. This directly grounds RC's institutional and formative ambitions.

annotations.content: primacy of love why humility?
annotations.anchor_text: _transformation of the will from pride to humility_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is asking a generative question: why is humility the specific virtue required? He supplies his own answer — 'primacy of love' — suggesting he is connecting this to the Augustinian ordo amoris. Humility is what rightly ordered love looks like when directed toward authority and truth. The question mark signals genuine philosophical inquiry, not just annotation — Tom is not sure MacIntyre makes this connection explicit.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects RC to Augustine's theology of love as the ground of right social order — civility as rightly ordered love, not merely procedural courtesy. Tom may be developing this as a RC thesis: civic friendship requires the primacy of love, not just rational agreement.

annotations.content: clearly pride of will which Bernard discerned in Abelard
annotations.anchor_text: _pride of will which Bernard discerned in Abelard_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this to mark the diagnostic moment: Bernard's condemnation was not arbitrary but represented a trained reader of the will recognizing disordered self-elevation. Tom is tracking how MacIntyre rehabilitates Bernard's judgment as epistemically serious rather than merely authoritarian.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, the capacity to discern pride within intellectual culture is a civic and not merely theological competence — relevant to diagnosing the failures of contemporary discourse that RC aims to address.

annotations.content: it is the underlying epistemology of Augustinian enquiry which requires the condemnation of heresy
annotations.anchor_text: _underlying epistemology of Augustinian enquiry_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this because it is MacIntyre's most controversial move: heresy-condemnation is not a theological add-on but a structural requirement of the epistemology. Tom is flagging this as either the most powerful or the most contested claim on the page — it will need to be handled carefully in RC work because it risks alienating pluralist audiences.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC must navigate the tension between recovering Augustinian epistemology and engaging pluralist civil society. This sentence marks the sharpest edge of that tension: can RC draw on Augustinian authority structures without endorsing their coercive implications?

annotations.content: trust is root of humility, love is root of trust
annotations.anchor_text: _heresy is always a sign of pride_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is constructing a philosophical chain: love → trust → humility → right enquiry. This is his own synthesis, not a direct quote from MacIntyre, suggesting active philosophical engagement rather than passive annotation. He is building a conceptual ladder that grounds epistemic humility in affective and relational categories — love and trust — rather than in mere deference to authority.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is potentially one of Tom's most original RC contributions: reframing the Augustinian epistemology not as authoritarianism but as a relational ontology of trust and love. This could serve as RC's bridge between classical authority-structures and contemporary relational ethics.

annotations.content: but to some another rests to the lord / the heresy of Jesus? You've had it and (written in blue ink over printed text near 'genuine authority')
annotations.anchor_text: _sign of pride in choosing to elevate one's own judgment_
annotations.interpretation: This is Tom's most personal and theologically charged annotation, written in a different ink color, suggesting it was added at a different moment of reading. He appears to be wrestling with the paradox that Jesus himself was condemned as a heretic by the legitimate religious authority of his day — which would seem to undermine MacIntyre's/Bernard's claim that authority-submission is always epistemically correct. Tom is flagging a serious counter-example from within the tradition itself.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is a critical RC aporia: if authority-structures can themselves be disordered (as in the condemnation of Jesus), then RC cannot simply endorse Augustinian authority-submission without a theology of prophetic reform. This annotation marks the most unresolved tension in Tom's engagement with MacIntyre.

annotations.content: the conception of authority thus appealed to is itself a necessary feature of the Augustinian scheme of understanding.
annotations.anchor_text: _conception of authority thus appealed to is itself_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this to mark MacIntyre's meta-level move: he is not just describing authority as used by Augustinians but arguing it is logically necessary to the scheme. Tom is tracking this as a structural argument, not a historical one — this is MacIntyre doing philosophical reconstruction, not just history of ideas.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this establishes that the recovery of Augustinian moral enquiry necessarily includes recovery of its authority structures — RC cannot selectively borrow the epistemology while jettisoning the institutional framework. This is a structurally important constraint on the RC project.

annotations.content: rationally unjustified belief has to precede understanding, belief has to be accepted on authority.
annotations.anchor_text: _rationally unjustified belief has to precede understanding_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this as the epistemological inversion at the heart of the Augustinian scheme — credo ut intelligam. This directly contradicts the Enlightenment demand for rational justification prior to belief. Tom likely sees this as a key passage for RC's argument that moral formation precedes moral argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is foundational for RC's pedagogical and institutional claims: if belief must precede understanding, then civic formation cannot begin with rational debate — it must begin with practices, habits, and communities that form the will prior to philosophical justification.

annotations.content: ①
annotations.anchor_text: _belief has to be accepted on authority. What_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has placed a circled number at the bottom right of the page, likely indicating this is the first of a numbered series of key structural points he is tracking across the section or chapter. This is an organizational mark suggesting Tom is building a numbered argument structure — possibly for RC writing — from MacIntyre's text.
annotations.rc_relevance: The numbering suggests Tom is systematizing MacIntyre's Augustinian epistemology into enumerable claims for RC use — this is active scholarly appropriation, not just reading.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that within the Augustinian scheme, the initial acceptance of authority is not based on prior rational evaluation of evidence, but on trust in authorized persons — a trust whose apparent arbitrariness (e.g., Augustine hearing 'Tolle, lege') is only adequately understood retrospectively. By accepting authority one acquires a teacher who educates one into reading certain texts, and this process embeds one's personal story within a larger narrative of history moving toward intelligibility. Continuous authority is thus justified not as a permanent substitute for reason but as indispensable to a continuing progress confirmed by dialectical enquiry.

annotations.content: prelim cognitive interaction w/ noncognitive context later, historical [or experimental] recognition of authority always still affecting contest one hears not in told cases
annotations.anchor_text: _authority provides at this point is testimony_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging the temporal and epistemic structure MacIntyre is describing: the initial uptake of authority is 'preliminary' and involves non-rational (noncognitive) contextual factors — chance, affect, social accident — while later stages involve historical and experiential recognition. The phrase 'always still affecting' suggests Tom is noting that these early noncognitive conditions do not simply disappear; they continue to shape even later rational enquiry. 'Contest one hears not in told cases' seems to be Tom noting that one cannot yet evaluate the testimony as good or bad at this stage — one is not yet in a position to contest it.
annotations.rc_relevance: This annotation maps directly onto RC's concern with how tradition-formation begins — not in rational consensus but in pre-rational submission to authority, a key tension MacIntyre identifies between the Augustinian and Enlightenment models. Tom is tracking the noncognitive substrate of moral formation, which is central to the RC critique of purely procedural or rationalist accounts of civic formation.

annotations.content: when I first believe in order that I may go on to understand
annotations.anchor_text: _So in the Augustinian scheme when I first believe_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles this classic Augustinian formula ('crede ut intelligas') because it is the epistemological hinge of the entire page. The circle distinguishes this as a key propositional claim rather than mere contextual description. He is marking the precise logical structure MacIntyre is attributing to Augustine: belief precedes and enables understanding, not the reverse.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the Augustinian anti-Enlightenment epistemology that RC wants to recover — the idea that moral and civic formation requires prior commitment to a community and its authorities before one can critically evaluate them. It directly challenges the encyclopaedist demand for reason-first validation.

annotations.content: I do not evaluate evidence, but put my trust in certain persons as authorized to represent the apostolic testimony
annotations.anchor_text: _I do not evaluate evidence but put my trust_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this to mark MacIntyre's bold claim: in the Augustinian scheme, the first move is fiduciary, not evidential. Trust precedes evaluation. This is likely being marked both as the core argument of the page and as something Tom wants to be able to cite precisely. The underlining also suggests Tom is alive to how provocative this claim is — it reverses the standard Enlightenment epistemology entirely.
annotations.rc_relevance: Central to RC's argument that civic and moral communities cannot be founded on neutral rational procedures alone — they require inherited trust structures, institutional authority, and personal relationships that predate and enable critical reasoning. This is the anti-encyclopaedist move.

annotations.content: this initial acceptance of authority is itself something that is to be adequately understood only later
annotations.anchor_text: _apparent arbitrariness of this initial acceptance_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this to mark MacIntyre's retrospective justification structure. The key insight is that the apparent irrationality of initial trust is not a permanent epistemic flaw but a stage whose rationality becomes visible only through the subsequent progress of enquiry. Tom is tracking this as a sophisticated response to the obvious objection that Augustinian epistemology is simply fideism.
annotations.rc_relevance: This speaks to RC's interest in how tradition justifies itself not through prior proof but through retrospective vindication — a point relevant to defending civic institutions and inherited practices against rationalist critique. It also connects to MacIntyre's broader argument that traditions are self-correcting over time.

annotations.content: authority is reencountered in a very different way
annotations.anchor_text: _understood only later and in that later understanding_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines 'reencountered' specifically — this word signals a transformation in the relationship to authority, not its abandonment. The 're-' prefix marks a second, more mature engagement with the same authority one originally accepted naively. Tom is likely noting the developmental, stage-like structure of MacIntyre's epistemology here.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is interested in how institutions and traditions can be both authoritative and reformable — this annotation captures the moment where initial deference gives way to informed, dialectically-shaped re-engagement, which is precisely the kind of 'living tradition' MacIntyre champions against both rigid conservatism and Enlightenment rupture.

annotations.content: nota w (or not) select leve in this indiv accepted [illegible] humility
annotations.anchor_text: _By accepting authority as we saw at the outset_
annotations.interpretation: This marginal note appears to be Tom questioning whether MacIntyre's account specifies *which* level of authority is being accepted by which individual — 'select leve[l] in this indiv[idual]' suggests Tom is probing the personalization of authority-acceptance. The word 'humility' at the end suggests Tom is connecting the acceptance of authority to the virtue of humility, which may be the noncognitive or affective condition that makes the initial belief possible. This may also be a note about whether MacIntyre is being descriptive or normative here.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is deeply concerned with how civic virtue — including humility — functions as a precondition for genuine participation in moral community. Tom is flagging that MacIntyre's account of authority-acceptance may implicitly require a virtue-theoretic account of the agent, which connects to RC's Thomistic anthropology.

annotations.content: noncognitivism
annotations.anchor_text: _one acquires a teacher who both introduces one_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes 'noncognitivism' in the left margin beside the passage about accepting authority and acquiring a teacher. This is a significant theoretical label — he is asking whether MacIntyre's account of initial authority-acceptance functions like a form of noncognitivism (where attitudes or commitments rather than beliefs or evidence do the foundational work). This may be a critical flag: Tom may be wondering if MacIntyre has adequately distinguished his position from a mere emotivist or voluntarist account of moral uptake.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC must address the charge that tradition-based moral formation is ultimately noncognitive — i.e., that it amounts to socialization rather than rational justification. Tom is tracking whether MacIntyre successfully navigates this charge, which is crucial for RC's claim that civic formation through institutions is genuinely rational.

annotations.content: By accepting authority, as we saw at the outset, one acquires a teacher who both introduces one to certain texts and educates one into becoming the sort of person capable of reading those texts with understanding, texts in which such a person discovers the story of him or herself, including the story of how he or she was transformed into a reader of these texts.
annotations.anchor_text: _one acquires a teacher who both introduces one to certain texts_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this entire passage — it is the most heavily marked section of the page. The passage articulates the full circle: authority → teacher → texts → formation of self → discovery of self in texts → narrative identity. Tom is marking this as the core positive account of Augustinian moral formation, where the teacher, text, and self are mutually constitutive. The reflexive structure ('story of how he or she was transformed into a reader') is especially significant — the very capacity for reading is itself a product of the process.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the heart of MacIntyre's account of tradition-constituted enquiry as character formation, directly relevant to RC's interest in how civic institutions form citizens. The teacher-text-self triad maps onto RC's concern with subsidiarity (the teacher as local authority), civic friendship (the educational relationship), and the role of narrative in moral identity.

annotations.content: This story of oneself is embedded in the history of the world, an overall narrative within which all other narratives find their place.
annotations.anchor_text: _story of oneself is embedded in the history of the world_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this sentence separately, suggesting he sees it as a distinct and important claim from the preceding passage. MacIntyre is here making the move from personal narrative to universal narrative — individual formation is not merely private but located within a cosmological-historical story. Tom is likely marking this as the point where MacIntyre's account goes beyond mere communitarianism to something more metaphysically ambitious.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's project involves recovering a sense of shared civic narrative — a common story within which particular communities and institutions make sense. This sentence is the theoretical warrant for that aspiration: it is not merely that individuals have stories, but that those stories are intelligible only within a larger, tradition-constituted account of history's direction.

annotations.content: in learning this we learn that authoritative testimony, to point us forward from where we now are, can never in our present bodily life be dispensed with. So continuous authority receives its justification as indispensable to a continuing progress, the narrative of which we first learned how to recount from that authority and the truth of which is confirmed by our own further progress including that progress made by means of dialectical enquiry.
annotations.anchor_text: _can never in our present bodily life be dispensed with_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the concluding argument of the page — the justification of continuous authority. MacIntyre's claim is that authority is not a ladder to be kicked away once reason matures; it is permanently necessary 'in our present bodily life.' Tom highlights the full circular structure of justification: we learned to tell the narrative from authority, and then our own progress confirms it. He is clearly marking this as the culminating argument he needs to engage with or deploy.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's defense of civic institutions against the liberal view that mature citizens should transcend institutional authority. MacIntyre's argument that authority is indispensable to continuing progress — not merely to initial formation — provides RC with a principled defense of ongoing institutional and traditional authority structures.

annotations.content: like N's narrative adm. in some respects...
annotations.anchor_text: _overall narrative within which all other narratives find their place_
annotations.interpretation: Tom compares MacIntyre's 'overall narrative' to someone else's narrative — 'N' is likely Nietzsche, given the genealogist-tradition-encyclopaedist framework of the book. Tom is noting a structural similarity: both Nietzsche and MacIntyre operate with a grand narrative that situates all other stories, though they differ radically in content and direction. The 'adm[ittedly] in some respects' qualification suggests Tom is being careful — he sees the parallel but does not want to overstate it.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC must distinguish its tradition-based narrative from Nietzschean or other genealogical narratives that also claim to situate all other stories. Tom is flagging a potential objection to MacIntyre: if the Augustinian tradition and Nietzsche both offer 'overall narratives,' what makes one preferable to the other? This is a live tension in the RC project's engagement with pluralism.

annotations.content: continuous authority
annotations.anchor_text: _So continuous authority receives its justification_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes 'continuous authority' as a standalone term in the left margin, functioning as a conceptual label or keyword for the argument concluding the page. This is a term-tracking annotation — he is naming the concept MacIntyre introduces so he can cross-reference it elsewhere. The isolation of this term suggests Tom sees 'continuous authority' as a technical concept worth flagging separately from the surrounding argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's institutional theory depends on exactly this concept — authority that is not merely foundational but ongoing, not merely pedagogical but constitutive of progress. Tom is labeling this for use in constructing RC's positive account of how civic institutions maintain their claim on citizens throughout life, not just in formation.

annotations.content: illustrate w/ transformations in scientific understanding
annotations.anchor_text: _progress made by means of dialectical enquiry_
annotations.interpretation: Tom notes an illustration strategy at the bottom of the page: use transformations in scientific understanding as an analogy for MacIntyre's account of how initial authority-acceptance gives way to dialectical progress. This is a pedagogical or rhetorical note — Tom is thinking about how to make MacIntyre's abstract epistemological argument concrete for an audience. Scientific revolutions (perhaps Kuhn?) or the history of science offer a parallel to the Augustinian pattern.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC needs to communicate its arguments to contemporary audiences skeptical of religious or traditional authority. Using scientific understanding as an analogy — where early training in paradigms precedes and enables critical revision — could make MacIntyre's account of continuous authority more accessible and less threatening to secular interlocutors.

annotations.content: N: Erication [Education?] Ex: good, time R: model + slow Q: big G: accidental [initials?] Ar: carbon build this out
annotations.anchor_text: _progress made by means of dialectical enquiry_
annotations.interpretation: This appears to be Tom's working notes for a project structure or presentation related to the RC project. The letters (N, Ex, R, Q, G, Ar) likely correspond to categories or variables in a framework Tom is developing — possibly: N=Nietzsche or Narrative, Ex=Example, R=Reform or Reason, Q=Question, G=Genealogist, Ar=Aristotle or Argument. 'Build this out' is a self-directive. The specific terms ('good, time,' 'model + slow,' 'big,' 'accidental,' 'carbon') are cryptic but suggest Tom is sketching a comparative or taxonomic framework to apply MacIntyre's argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: These notes suggest Tom is actively building out the RC framework using this page as a springboard — the letters and categories may correspond to the RC project's own tripartite or multi-variable structure for analyzing civic formation. 'Build this out' confirms this page is a generative node in Tom's RC research rather than merely a reading note.

annotations.content: Small asterisk or star symbol in left margin
annotations.anchor_text: _the apparent arbitrariness of this initial acceptance_
annotations.interpretation: A small symbol (asterisk or similar mark) appears in the left margin beside the passage about the apparent arbitrariness of initial authority-acceptance. This is likely a priority marker — Tom is flagging this passage as especially important or as something to return to. The asterisk beside 'apparent arbitrariness' suggests Tom sees this as one of MacIntyre's most philosophically vulnerable but also most important claims.
annotations.rc_relevance: The arbitrariness of initial authority-acceptance is the most obvious target for critics of tradition-based moral formation — including both Enlightenment rationalists and genealogists. Tom marking this with a priority symbol suggests RC must directly address this vulnerability: how does one defend apparently arbitrary starting points without collapsing into relativism?

pages.summary: MacIntyre examines two rival modern views on the relationship between texts and their subject matter: (a) that texts are creative falsifications of their subject matter, so independent enquiry will reveal a lack of correspondence between text and world (Tolstoy, Sartre); and (b) that we have no access to a text's subject matter except through the text itself, making questions of external truth or falsity unanswerable. Both views, despite their mutual incompatibility, converge on the claim that no systematic correspondence between text and subject matter can be made sense of — a convergence MacIntyre uses to set up his broader argument about tradition-constituted enquiry and the Augustinian alternative.

annotations.content: and this He scientific method? (add Slaughter + typing relates to styles too) (80)
annotations.anchor_text: _practice of specifically Augustinian dialectic and_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is connecting MacIntyre's discussion of Augustinian dialectic to the 'scientific method' as a rival framework — likely cross-referencing a passage on page 80 and a source by 'Slaughter' (possibly Sheila Slaughter or another scholar on academic or scientific epistemology). The parenthetical suggests Tom is building a reading list or annotation cluster around the theme of method and style of enquiry, flagging this passage as evidence for a broader argument he is constructing.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects directly to RC's engagement with MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism: Tom appears to be tracing how the 'encyclopaedist' model of scientific method stands in tension with tradition-constituted enquiry, a central RC tension.

annotations.content: this practice is a movement towards a truth never as yet wholly grasped thus presupposes the guidance of authority. Hence when the very same authority places restrictions upon dialectical enquiry, it would be unreasonable not to submit.
annotations.anchor_text: _it would be unreasonable not to submit_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has highlighted the core claim that Augustinian dialectic is internally committed to deference to authority — not as an external imposition but as a logical consequence of the practice itself. This is MacIntyre's strongest statement of how authority and enquiry are internally related rather than opposed, which Tom likely finds crucial for distinguishing tradition-constituted rationality from liberal autonomy.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is central to RC's theme of institutions forming character and the role of legitimate authority in moral community — the claim that submission to authority can be rational within a tradition directly challenges the Enlightenment assumption that authority and reason are opposed.

annotations.content: Abelard's submission, therefore, unlike Galileo's, was of a piece with his enquiries. The acknowledgment of authority was already an essential element in those enquiries.
annotations.anchor_text: _Abelard's submission unlike Galileo's was of a piece_
annotations.interpretation: The contrast between Abelard and Galileo is highlighted as a key historical illustration: Abelard's deference to authority was constitutive of his enquiry, not a suppression of it, whereas Galileo's conflict with authority is typically read as authority suppressing reason. Tom is tracking MacIntyre's revisionary reading of intellectual history, which reframes the Abelard/Galileo contrast to challenge the standard Enlightenment narrative.
annotations.rc_relevance: This speaks directly to RC's 'tradition vs. reform' theme — MacIntyre is arguing that the Galileo model of heroic individual reason against institutional authority is not the only or best model, and that tradition-internal submission can be epistemically virtuous.

annotations.content: the place of authority within enquiry
annotations.anchor_text: _This view of the place of authority within enquiry_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has underlined this phrase as a key conceptual node — 'the place of authority within enquiry' is a technical formulation that encapsulates the Augustinian thesis MacIntyre is defending. By underlining just this phrase, Tom signals he sees it as a term to track or deploy, not merely a passing claim.
annotations.rc_relevance: Authority within enquiry is a foundational RC concept: the project is concerned with how legitimate institutional authority shapes moral formation, and this phrase gives Tom a precise MacIntyrean formulation for that relationship.

annotations.content: presupposes a particular understanding of how texts relate to that of which they speak and of how progress in the reading and understanding of texts changes the relationship of the reader to that of which the texts speak.
annotations.anchor_text: _presupposes a particular understanding of how texts relate_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this to mark MacIntyre's claim that the Augustinian view of authority is inseparable from a specific hermeneutics — a theory of how reading transforms the reader's relationship to reality. This is not merely an epistemological point but an ontological one about the reader's formation through textual practice.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's interest in how institutions and practices form character: MacIntyre is arguing that reading within a tradition is itself a formative practice, which aligns with RC's Thomistic emphasis on habituation and moral formation.

annotations.content: since the reader him or herself is part of the subject matter about which the texts speak, what is changed is the relationship of the reader to other elements of that subject matter.
annotations.anchor_text: _the reader him or herself is part of the subject matter_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this because it is MacIntyre's most striking claim on this page: the reader is not an external observer but is themselves constituted by and within the subject matter under enquiry. This collapses the subject/object distinction in a specifically Augustinian way — moral enquiry transforms the enquirer.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is pivotal for RC's account of civic friendship and moral community: if enquirers are internal to the subject matter of moral enquiry, then moral knowledge is necessarily communal and self-involving, not detached and universal — a direct counter to encyclopaedist neutrality.

annotations.content: now imagine the reader sto becomes an author mission yes on texts
annotations.anchor_text: _reader to other elements of that subject matter_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is extrapolating from MacIntyre's claim: if the reader is transformed into part of the subject matter, the next step is that the reader becomes a kind of author — not of the text, but of themselves or of the tradition's ongoing enquiry. The phrase 'becomes an author' suggests Tom is thinking about the creative or constitutive role of the reader within tradition, possibly connecting to debates about authority, authorship, and transmission.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's concern with how traditions are transmitted and renewed — if readers become authors, the question of who has authority to re-describe the tradition (reform vs. continuity) becomes acute, a central RC tension.

annotations.anchor_text: _Every text is thus to some degree a creative falsification of_
annotations.interpretation: Tom labels this section '(a.)' to mark the first of two rival modern views MacIntyre is presenting: the view that texts are creative falsifications of their subject matter (associated with Tolstoy and Sartre). This is a structural annotation organizing MacIntyre's dialectic, showing Tom is tracking the argument's architecture.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's tripartite structure (encyclopaedist / genealogist / tradition) is relevant here: Tom may be mapping MacIntyre's two modern views onto the encyclopaedist and genealogist positions, preparing to show how both fail compared to tradition-constituted enquiry.

annotations.anchor_text: _By contrast there are those other modern writers who have argued_
annotations.interpretation: Tom labels this section '(b.)' to mark the second rival modern view: that we have no access to a text's subject matter except through the text itself. Paired with '(a.)', Tom is explicitly tracking MacIntyre's two-part dialectic, setting up the convergence argument that follows ('a + b agree').
annotations.rc_relevance: This structural labeling reflects RC's interest in MacIntyre's tripartite structure — Tom is identifying the two positions that will be shown to converge in their rejection of systematic correspondence, before MacIntyre introduces the tradition-constituted alternative.

annotations.content: no access to the peculiar and idiosyncratic subject matter of any text except that afforded by the text itself.
annotations.anchor_text: _no access to the peculiar and idiosyncratic subject matter_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as the precise formulation of view (b.) — a strong textualist or hermeneutic anti-realism. The underline likely signals Tom is tracking this as a position he will want to contrast with the Augustinian view of texts as pointing beyond themselves to a reality that transforms the reader.
annotations.rc_relevance: This textualist view is a challenge RC must address: if texts are self-enclosed, then appeal to tradition as a guide to external moral reality becomes incoherent — Tom is marking the position RC (and MacIntyre) must refute.

annotations.content: the question of truth or falsity in relation to some genuinely external subject matter cannot arise.
annotations.anchor_text: _truth or falsity in relation to some genuinely external subject matter_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as the most radical consequence of view (b.): the elimination of the question of truth altogether in relation to external reality. This is likely flagged as the position MacIntyre will show leads to genealogical nihilism or the collapse of enquiry as such.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is deeply concerned with the possibility of moral truth against genealogical and relativist critiques — this underline marks the precise philosophical danger MacIntyre's project (and RC) must overcome.

annotations.content: perhaps that each text must be judged in its own terms, perhaps that texts are nothing but what we, the readers, make of them.
annotations.anchor_text: _texts are nothing but what we the readers make of them_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as the reductio of view (b.) — reader-constructivism. This formulation collapses moral enquiry into subjective construction, which is the genealogist's endpoint. Tom likely marks this as the position MacIntyre's tradition-constituted enquiry is designed to refute.
annotations.rc_relevance: Reader-constructivism is the antithesis of RC's recovery of Thomistic moral realism — if texts (and by extension moral traditions) are only what readers make of them, the RC project of recovering civility through tradition becomes incoherent.

annotations.content: other
annotations.anchor_text: _By contrast there are those other modern writers_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'other' to flag that MacIntyre is distinguishing a second group of modern writers from the first (Tolstoy/Sartre group). The circle draws attention to the contrastive structure — this is not a continuation of view (a.) but a genuinely different, incompatible position. Tom may also be noting that 'other' is doing significant argumentative work by implying a symmetry between two equally modern, equally mistaken views.
annotations.rc_relevance: The symmetry between two rival modern positions is central to MacIntyre's argument that both the encyclopaedist and genealogist traditions are internally incoherent — RC draws on this to argue for the tradition-constituted alternative.

annotations.content: except
annotations.anchor_text: _of any text except that afforded by the text itself_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'except' to highlight the logical structure of view (b.): the only access is through the text itself. This is a key logical qualifier — 'except' marks the boundary of what is epistemically permissible on this view. Tom may be noting that this 'except' is both the view's strength (it takes textual integrity seriously) and its limitation (it forecloses reference to external reality).
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's concern with subsidiarity and the limits of institutional authority: just as view (b.) forecloses appeal beyond the text, an overly self-enclosed tradition risks becoming unable to evaluate itself by external standards — a danger RC must navigate.

annotations.content: agree in
annotations.anchor_text: _mutually incompatible ways of thinking about texts agree in_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'agree in' because this is the pivotal move in MacIntyre's argument: two mutually incompatible views converge on a shared negative conclusion. The circle marks the logical surprise — incompatibility does not prevent agreement on a deeper point. Tom is tracking MacIntyre's dialectical method, which regularly finds unexpected convergences between rival positions to reveal a shared presupposition.
annotations.rc_relevance: MacIntyre's technique of finding convergence between encyclopaedist and genealogist positions is foundational to RC's argument structure — both fail in the same way, creating space for the tradition-constituted alternative RC champions.

annotations.content: no sense can be made of the nature of a systematic correspondence between
annotations.anchor_text: _no sense can be made of the nature of a systematic correspondence_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as the shared conclusion of views (a.) and (b.) — the denial of systematic correspondence between text and subject matter. This is the negative thesis MacIntyre attributes to both modern positions before introducing the Augustinian alternative, which does affirm such correspondence (mediated through tradition and transformed readership).
annotations.rc_relevance: The recovery of 'systematic correspondence' between moral enquiry and moral reality is precisely what RC's Thomistic project attempts — Tom is marking the problem RC must solve: restoring intelligibility to the idea that tradition-constituted enquiry can track moral truth.

annotations.content: a + b agree:
annotations.anchor_text: _What these very different and mutually incompatible ways of thinking about texts agree in_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes 'a + b agree:' to explicitly annotate the logical structure he labeled earlier with '(a.)' and '(b.)' — confirming that his numbering was preparation for tracking this convergence. This is Tom the systematic reader: he laid out the structure in advance and now marks where MacIntyre closes the dialectical trap.
annotations.rc_relevance: This structural annotation shows Tom is reading MacIntyre as providing RC with a dialectical tool: the convergence of rival positions on a shared failure creates the argumentative space for the tradition-constituted alternative that RC is built on.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the Augustinian tradition avoids the correspondence-theory objection by treating nature itself as a text — a series of signs authored by God — so that Scripture and the created world are both legible only within a tradition of authoritative interpretation. God is the ultimate interpreter of meaning, and ecclesiastical and secular authorities appointed by God are therefore authoritative in interpreting nature, history, Scripture, and the conclusions of dialectical enquiry. Progress in reading and understanding texts — including the text of nature — consists first in identifying discrepancies and incoherences and formulating hypotheses to resolve them.

annotations.content: modern (arb) vs correspondence this of textual interp in may only be Cathiec of Truth
annotations.anchor_text: _dence theory of truth, in which truth_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging the contrast between modern (possibly arbitrary or constructed) theories of meaning and the correspondence theory of truth — noting that the Augustinian move is to situate textual interpretation within a framework that transcends both. The abbreviated 'Cathiec' likely refers to 'Catholic' or possibly 'catechetical,' suggesting Tom is tracking how confessional commitment shapes epistemology. He may be questioning whether the Augustinian position itself smuggles in a kind of authority-grounded arbitrariness that mirrors what it criticizes in modernism.
annotations.rc_relevance: This annotation directly engages MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism — specifically the encyclopaedist's reliance on a neutral correspondence theory — and flags the Augustinian counter-claim that tradition-embedded authority is the only coherent alternative. For RC, this is foundational: civility and moral discourse require a shared framework of interpretive authority, not merely procedural agreement.

annotations.content: The arguments advanced in support of this contention have much in common with those objections which have been cogently urged against any version of the correspondence theory of truth
annotations.anchor_text: _arguments advanced in support of this contention_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this because it marks the moment MacIntyre acknowledges a structural parallel between the Augustinian position and broader anti-correspondence critiques — a surprising concession that strengthens the tradition's philosophical credentials. By highlighting this, Tom is noting that MacIntyre is doing something sophisticated: showing that the Augustinian view is not naive realism but anticipates postmodern critiques of representation.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this matters because it shows that tradition-based moral enquiry is not simply pre-critical — it has resources to engage and absorb modern epistemological challenges, which is central to the project's argument that classical traditions remain viable interlocutors in contemporary fragmented discourse.

annotations.content: The Augustinian standpoint is not however open to this type of objection. Particular texts do indeed characterize, refer to, stand in different types of relationships of correspondence to something beyond and outside themselves, but that something is always another text.
annotations.anchor_text: _Augustinian standpoint is not however open_
annotations.interpretation: This is the pivotal claim of the page — that for Augustine, the referent of any text is always another text, not a raw non-linguistic fact. Tom's highlighting signals he sees this as the crux of the Augustinian epistemology: correspondence is preserved but radicalized by making the world itself a semiotic system. He is tracking the move that dissolves the nature/text binary.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's concern with how traditions constitute the intelligibility of moral reality — if nature is itself a text, then reading nature morally requires initiation into a tradition of reading, which supports the RC argument for institutions and communities as necessary for moral formation.

annotations.content: nature as text
annotations.anchor_text: _ture, including human nature and human history, is itself on this view a text_
annotations.interpretation: Tom distills the key philosophical move of this passage into a compact formula. 'Nature as text' is a summary concept he is likely building into his own analytical vocabulary for the RC project. This is not merely descriptive — it signals Tom is treating this as a theoretical resource, not just a historical curiosity. He may be considering how this Augustinian semiotics could ground a richer account of moral ecology than secular naturalism provides.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'nature as text' concept is deeply relevant to RC's engagement with subsidiarity and moral community — if nature is authored and meaningful, then local institutions and practices that cultivate reading of that meaning are not arbitrary but are participating in a real moral order. This challenges purely proceduralist or contractarian accounts of civic life.

annotations.content: ture, including human nature and human history, is itself on this view a text
annotations.anchor_text: _including human nature and human history_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this specific extension of the 'nature as text' thesis to human nature and history — the most politically and morally charged application. He is noting that MacIntyre's Augustinianism does not merely aestheticize the world; it makes human social life itself a legible moral text requiring authoritative interpretation.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this is crucial: if human social history is a text authored by God, then the deterioration of civic friendship and the rise of social fragmentation are not merely sociological facts but signs that require interpretation within a tradition. This grounds RC's normative critique of contemporary culture.

annotations.content: asterisk or star-like symbol
annotations.anchor_text: _Particular texts do indeed characterize, refer to, stand_
annotations.interpretation: Tom uses this symbol to flag a passage of special importance — likely the precise moment where MacIntyre formulates the Augustinian solution to the correspondence objection. The symbol suggests Tom wants to return here, possibly to use this passage as a citation anchor or as a structural node in his own argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: The starred passage is the philosophical hinge of MacIntyre's Augustinian semiotics, which RC needs to engage directly when arguing that tradition-embedded interpretation is not relativism but a more coherent form of realism than Enlightenment alternatives.

annotations.content: also: Rn ref Scripture + Tradition
annotations.anchor_text: _texts of Scripture themselves speak of this speaking to us in both_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is cross-referencing another source — 'Rn' likely abbreviates 'Romans' or possibly a scholar's name — in connection with the relationship between Scripture and Tradition. He is building an intertextual web, noting that the authority structure MacIntyre describes here has precedent or parallel in other texts he is reading. This suggests Tom is constructing a dossier of sources on the Scripture-Tradition nexus.
annotations.rc_relevance: The Scripture-Tradition dyad is central to RC's engagement with how authoritative interpretation is transmitted institutionally — a key question for the project's account of how civic and ecclesial institutions sustain moral tradition across generations.

annotations.content: God, as author of both, speaks to us in both, and the texts of Scripture themselves speak of this speaking to us in both and of us as hearing or failing to hear. God is thus the authoritative interpreter of his meanings, and the authorities which he has appointed to speak in his place, both ecclesiastical and secular, are therefore authoritative in interpretation and evaluation
annotations.anchor_text: _God, as author of both, speaks to us in both_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this extended passage because it is the culminating claim of MacIntyre's Augustinian account of interpretive authority — God as both author and authoritative interpreter, with delegated authority flowing to ecclesiastical and secular institutions. Tom is tracking the precise mechanism by which tradition-grounded authority is justified, and the inclusion of 'secular' authority alongside ecclesiastical is noteworthy and likely of special interest.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is perhaps the most directly RC-relevant passage on the page: it provides the theological grounding for subsidiarity — authority is delegated downward from God through institutions — and for the RC argument that civic institutions are not merely functional but carry genuine moral authority. The inclusion of secular alongside ecclesiastical authority is important for RC's engagement with non-confessional civic contexts.

annotations.content: incarnate God
annotations.anchor_text: _ecclesiastical and secular, are therefore authoritative in interpretation_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes 'incarnate God' alongside the passage about God as authoritative interpreter speaking through institutions. This is a theological gloss: Tom is connecting the abstract claim about divine authorship to the specifically Christian doctrine of Incarnation — the idea that God's interpretive authority becomes most fully embodied in the person of Christ, and by extension in the Church as Christ's body. This is not merely a pious aside; it suggests Tom is tracking how the metaphysics of the text argument cashes out in Christology.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, the Incarnation as interpretive event is significant because it means that the tradition's authority claim is not abstract theism but a historically specific community-forming event — which connects to MacIntyre's broader argument that traditions are constituted by formative narratives, and to RC's interest in how founding stories shape civic communities.

annotations.content: The determinacy of meaning of texts requires and receives the exercise of interpretative authority, authority of just the kind to which Abelard submitted.
annotations.anchor_text: _determinacy of meaning of texts requires_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this concluding sentence of the argument, which ties the abstract semiotic claim to a concrete historical case — Abelard's submission to ecclesial interpretive authority. Tom sees this as the payoff of the whole argument: meaning requires authority, and that authority is institutionally embodied. The reference to Abelard functions as a historical proof of concept for MacIntyre's theoretical claim.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's theme of tradition vs. reform — Abelard represents the reforming rationalist who must ultimately submit to traditional authority for meaning to be determinate. RC uses this structure to argue that contemporary moral reformers operating outside tradition face an analogous problem of indeterminacy.

annotations.content: in what sens — a/13
annotations.anchor_text: _determinacy of meaning of texts requires and receives_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is questioning the sense in which 'determinacy of meaning' is achieved — asking whether MacIntyre's claim is fully coherent or requires qualification. The 'a/13' notation is ambiguous but likely refers to a cross-reference: either page 13 of another text, annotation 13 in his own notes, or a numbered argument point. Tom is not simply accepting the claim; he is flagging it for critical scrutiny.
annotations.rc_relevance: The question of what 'determinacy of meaning' means in practice is crucial for RC, which must address how tradition-embedded interpretation avoids the charge of mere authoritarianism — a challenge the project needs to answer to make its case for recovering classical traditions in contemporary civic discourse.

annotations.content: progress in reading 'texts'
annotations.anchor_text: _What then does progress in reading and understanding texts_
annotations.interpretation: Tom isolates the concept of 'progress in reading texts' with scare quotes around 'texts' — signaling that he is tracking MacIntyre's extended use of 'text' to cover nature, history, and Scripture, not just written documents. The scare quotes suggest Tom is either marking this as a technical term he wants to interrogate or noting that the concept of 'progress' within a tradition-bound hermeneutic is itself a live question.
annotations.rc_relevance: Progress in moral enquiry is a core RC concern: the project must show that recovering classical traditions is not regression but a form of genuine progress. MacIntyre's account of what constitutes progress within a tradition directly funds this argument, making this annotation a potential structural node in RC's constructive proposal.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that in the Augustinian tradition, theoretical and practical enquiry were inseparable, unified under the monastic ideal exemplified by the Rule of St. Benedict. However, in the twelfth century, new social pressures — from centralizing royal and papal administrations, changing occupational demands on clergy, and developments in logic and the artes liberales — forced the Augustinian tradition to provide a broader intellectual framework for new kinds of clergy and laypeople. In response, a new genre of comprehensive 'conspectus' writing emerged, exemplified by Hugh of St. Victor's Didascalion, which attempted to subordinate all secular learning to Scripture and practical perfection, echoing Augustine's own project in De Doctrina Christiana.
