annotations.content: interrelatedness of Theoretic & practical pursuits as perfection
annotations.anchor_text: _tical enquiry were so closely interrelated_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is naming the core structural claim MacIntyre is making at the top of the page: that in the Augustinian monastic ideal, theoretical and practical enquiry are not separate domains but co-constitutive paths toward perfection. The note condenses this into a portable conceptual label — 'interrelatedness' — suggesting Tom sees this as a key premise to carry forward. He may be flagging that this unity is precisely what later modernity (the encyclopaedist tradition) will shatter.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly maps to RC's concern with the bifurcation of civic and moral life in modernity — the RC project seeks to recover a tradition where intellectual enquiry and practical virtue formation are unified rather than separated into academic and political silos.

annotations.content: The rational justification of belief in the object of faith is internal to the life of faith
annotations.anchor_text: _rected by faith towards its true perfection_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has highlighted MacIntyre's epistemologically loaded claim that the justification of faith is internal — not external or foundationalist. This is a direct shot at Enlightenment rationalism, which demands that beliefs be justifiable from a standpoint outside any tradition. Tom is flagging this as a core anti-encyclopaedist move.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is central to MacIntyre's 'tradition-constituted rationality' argument that RC draws on — justification is always internal to a living tradition, not available from a neutral Archimedean point. RC's critique of contemporary liberal discourse depends on this exact move.

annotations.content: is conceived as a progress towards perfection through an ever more adequate apprehension of perfection that theoretical enquiry and practical enquiry were so closely interrelated
annotations.anchor_text: _conceived as a progress towards perfection_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is marking the teleological structure of Augustinian enquiry — perfection as the telos that binds theory and practice together. The highlighting suggests he sees this as architecturally important for understanding why the later dissolution of this unity (under encyclopaedist pressure) is a kind of moral catastrophe, not just an intellectual reorganization.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry depends on restoring this teleological architecture — the idea that moral formation and intellectual enquiry are ordered toward a common end. This passage gives the Augustinian root of what Aquinas will synthesize.

annotations.content: the two types of ideal were held together
annotations.anchor_text: _Victor, the two types of ideal were held together_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is marking the historical moment of unity — the Victorine canons as the site where monastic and secular-clerical ideals had not yet split. This is the 'before' in a before/after narrative MacIntyre is constructing. Tom seems to be tracking the fragmentation story carefully, noting where the synthesis still holds.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project is fundamentally about recovering synthesis — of civic and religious, theoretical and practical. This annotation marks the historical exemplar of that synthesis before its dissolution, which is the narrative RC wants to reverse.

annotations.content: the question of what Augustinianism involved for the practical life began to assume new dimensions and did so precisely as the Augustinian tradition came under pressure to provide the intellectual foundation and framework for a type of education directed both at those who were not clergy and at new kinds of clergy
annotations.anchor_text: _the course of the twelfth century that_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has highlighted the pivotal causal claim: external social pressure forces internal transformation of the tradition. The word 'precisely' is important — MacIntyre is arguing for a tight causal link between social-institutional pressure and the intellectual mutation of Augustinianism. Tom appears to be tracking how traditions evolve under stress, not just how they transmit doctrine.
annotations.rc_relevance: This speaks to RC's theme of tradition vs. reform — how does a tradition respond to new social conditions without losing its identity? The Augustinian case is a historical test case for whether external pressure produces genuine development or distortion, a question RC must answer for contemporary institutions.

annotations.content: Pressure
annotations.anchor_text: _pressure to provide the intellectual foundation_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has written 'Pressure' in the left margin as a standalone label, capitalizing it as if it were a section heading or analytical category. This suggests he is structuring his reading around what forces drive institutional and intellectual change — 'pressure' becomes a variable in his analytical framework, not merely a narrative detail.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is concerned with how contemporary social fragmentation generates institutional pressure on churches, universities, and civic bodies. Tom is perhaps building a typology of pressure-points that MacIntyre's historical cases can illuminate for contemporary RC analysis.

annotations.content: centralizing administrations
annotations.anchor_text: _centralizing administrations of both royal and papal courts_
annotations.interpretation: The circle with the number '1' marks this as the first in a list of pressure-sources MacIntyre enumerates. Tom is organizing MacIntyre's argument into a structured list, suggesting he intends to compare or deploy these factors analytically. The circling of 'centralizing administrations' specifically — not just the number — indicates Tom sees this as a key concept, perhaps connecting to subsidiarity debates.
annotations.rc_relevance: Centralizing administration is directly opposed to the RC principle of subsidiarity. Tom is likely flagging this as a historical precedent for the kind of top-down institutional pressure that RC argues deforms moral community — the twelfth-century papacy and monarchy as early modern analogues to the contemporary administrative state.

annotations.content: relation between these two, and in the other artes liberales had raised in various ways questions about the overall structure and purpose of purely secular knowledge
annotations.anchor_text: _developments in logic, in grammar, in the relation between_
annotations.interpretation: The circle with '2' marks the second pressure-source: internal intellectual development within the liberal arts raising questions about the structure of secular knowledge itself. Tom is tracking that the crisis is not only external (administrative) but internal (logic and grammar generating their own autonomous questions). This is MacIntyre's point that the encyclopaedist impulse has intellectual, not merely social, roots.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the seed of what MacIntyre calls the 'encyclopaedist' tradition — secular knowledge developing its own internal logic independent of theological teleology. RC's critique of modern moral fragmentation needs to understand this internal intellectual dynamic, not just blame secularization as an external force.

annotations.content: new pre-encycl. genre
annotations.anchor_text: _appeared a genre of writing which in providing_
annotations.interpretation: Tom abbreviates 'pre-encyclopaedist' to label the genre of comprehensive conspectus writing that emerges in response to the pressures enumerated above. He is naming this as a transitional genre — not yet the encyclopaedist tradition MacIntyre will critique, but its ancestor. The label is analytical and forward-looking, showing Tom is reading this page in light of the book's larger tripartite structure.
annotations.rc_relevance: The encyclopaedist tradition is one of MacIntyre's three rival versions of moral enquiry, and RC needs to understand its genealogy to contest it. Tom is marking the precise historical moment of its emergence, which is crucial for RC's argument that this tradition has a contingent rather than necessary origin.

annotations.content: Bonaventure, to later...
annotations.anchor_text: _What Hugh wanted to achieve was the subordination of all learning_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is making a cross-reference, flagging that Hugh of St. Victor's project will be continued or transformed by Bonaventure later in MacIntyre's argument (or in Tom's broader reading). The ellipsis suggests an open thread — Tom is not yet sure how to complete the connection but wants to track it. This is a classic reading annotation that marks an argument in motion.
annotations.rc_relevance: Bonaventure represents a key figure in the Augustinian-Franciscan alternative to the Dominican-Thomistic synthesis, and RC's engagement with MacIntyre must account for this internal differentiation within the tradition. Tom is tracking whether Bonaventure represents recovery or further fragmentation of the Augustinian synthesis.

annotations.content: the subordination of all learning to the reading of Scripture in order that study might issue in the perfecting of practical life
annotations.anchor_text: _What Hugh wanted to achieve was_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines Hugh's explicit goal as MacIntyre states it — the subordination of all learning to Scripture for the sake of practical perfection. This is the Augustinian telos stated at its most programmatic. The underlining suggests Tom sees this as a quotable, foundational claim — perhaps the clearest statement on the page of what the tradition is trying to do before it gets deformed.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the positive vision RC wants to recover in secular form — not necessarily the subordination to Scripture per se, but the subordination of intellectual enquiry to a shared telos of practical moral flourishing. RC's institutional design question is: what plays the role Scripture played for Hugh in contemporary civic institutions?

pages.summary: MacIntyre contrasts two medieval university models: Paris, an Augustinian institution where all learning was subordinated to theology and designed to re-educate students toward a more adequate knowledge of ends, and Bologna, where teachers were employed by students and education was oriented toward utility and political power, with law as its central discipline. He draws on Ferruolo's scholarship to argue that Paris's organizational forms and curriculum cannot be explained except as the outcome of a debate over educational ideals, not merely corporate self-interest. Paris thus represents an institutionalized Augustinian conception of enquiry in which theology governs the ordering of all other knowledge.

annotations.content: debate about curriculum universities
annotations.anchor_text: _became a debate not only about_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging that the opening of this section explicitly frames the founding of medieval universities as a curricular and institutional debate — not merely a political or economic one. This is a key structural move by MacIntyre: the university is a site of contested moral formation, not neutral knowledge transmission. Tom likely wants to return to this as evidence that institutional design is always already ideologically loaded.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly maps to RC's concern with institutions as character-forming structures. If curriculum debates at Paris were really debates about human ends, then contemporary university reform debates are similarly moral and not merely administrative.

annotations.content: 2 types University
annotations.anchor_text: _Paris was a university of masters Bologna was a university of students_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is distilling MacIntyre's contrast into a clean binary: the two archetypal university models. The brevity suggests this is a structural note — he's organizing the argument for later retrieval or teaching. The phrasing '2 types' implies he sees this as a generalizable taxonomy, not just a historical observation.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is deeply concerned with what kinds of institutions can sustain moral enquiry. The Bologna/Paris distinction maps onto a tension between consumer-driven education (utility, student as employer) and tradition-directed education (the teacher as moral authority shaping ends) — a tension very much alive today.

annotations.content: Paris was a university of masters Bologna was a university of students
annotations.anchor_text: _It is a commonplace to contrast these two_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the defining structural contrast. This is a foundational definitional claim MacIntyre is building on, and Tom recognizes it as load-bearing. He may also be noting that MacIntyre calls this a 'commonplace' — i.e., a received distinction he will then complicate or deepen.
annotations.rc_relevance: The master/student governance distinction encodes two different theories of authority and deference — crucial to RC's interest in subsidiarity and the locus of moral authority in institutions.

annotations.content: teachers were employed by their pupils
annotations.anchor_text: _university controlled by teachers while Bologna_
annotations.interpretation: This underline isolates the economic and power relationship at Bologna — teachers as hired professionals answerable to student-clients. Tom is likely flagging this as the prototype of the modern 'customer satisfaction' model of education that MacIntyre implicitly critiques.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with the corruption of institutions by market logic finds a historical precursor here. Bologna represents education as a service transaction rather than a formative relationship — exactly the model RC resists.

annotations.content: liberal arts Universities
annotations.anchor_text: _teaching at Bologna was designed to serve the purposes of the students_
annotations.interpretation: Tom seems to be labeling Bologna-type institutions as 'liberal arts universities' in a loose or ironic sense — or perhaps noting that the Bologna model, despite its utility-focus, is an ancestor of certain liberal arts traditions. Alternatively, he may be distinguishing Bologna from what we now call liberal arts colleges by contrast. The note is slightly ambiguous but likely reflects Tom thinking about modern institutional analogues.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC would be interested in whether contemporary liberal arts colleges are closer to the Paris or Bologna model — whether they form students toward ends or merely serve student preferences. This annotation suggests Tom is actively mapping historical types onto present institutions.

annotations.content: teaching at Paris was designed to reeducate students into a more adequate knowledge of ends and purposes, so that the desires with which they came to study might be corrected
annotations.anchor_text: _designed to reeducate students into a more adequate knowledge_
annotations.interpretation: This is the most heavily marked claim on the page, and for good reason: it is MacIntyre's sharpest statement of the Augustinian pedagogical ideal. 'Reeducate' and 'corrected' imply that students arrive with disordered desires, and the university's job is moral rectification, not preference satisfaction. Tom's underline signals he recognizes this as a central claim — possibly one he wants to either champion or scrutinize for RC.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the heart of RC's interest in moral formation through institutions. The idea that education should correct disordered desires rather than serve existing ones is Augustinian and Thomistic — and directly challenges both liberal neutrality and the Bologna/consumer model. RC would build on this claim extensively.

annotations.content: Education at Bologna was designed to be useful in terms of a standard of utility established in the realm of political power
annotations.anchor_text: _standard of utility established in the realm of political power_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the explicit linkage between Bologna's utility standard and political power. MacIntyre is making a critical point: 'useful' is not a neutral category but is defined by whoever holds political authority. Tom likely sees this as MacIntyre's implicit critique of modern technocratic and state-serving education.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's critique of Enlightenment rationalism includes its tendency to subordinate moral enquiry to political or instrumental ends. Bologna here becomes a historical type of what happens when education loses its orientation to transcendent goods.

annotations.content: Unsurprisingly the most important academic subject was held to be law
annotations.anchor_text: _most important academic subject was held to be law_
annotations.interpretation: The underline with 'Unsurprisingly' included is telling — Tom is noting MacIntyre's ironic tone. Law as Bologna's crown discipline follows logically from its utility-for-political-power orientation. Tom may be thinking about the modern analogue: economics or STEM as the 'unsurprising' top disciplines of today's utility-driven universities.
annotations.rc_relevance: This speaks to RC's concern about what disciplines anchor a university's conception of human flourishing. If law serves political power at Bologna, theology serves transcendent ends at Paris — the choice of master discipline encodes a complete moral anthropology.

annotations.content: law
annotations.anchor_text: _most important academic subject was held to be law_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'law' as a key term, reinforcing the underline. The circle suggests he wants this word to stand out as a conceptual marker — possibly for cross-reference with Bologna's utility orientation or for contrast with theology at Paris. Circling a single word often signals it will reappear or needs tracking.
annotations.rc_relevance: The law/theology opposition in medieval universities is a compressed version of the natural law vs. positive law debate that RC engages. Circling 'law' may indicate Tom is tracking where positive legal authority ends and moral enquiry begins.

annotations.content: B: law P: theo
annotations.anchor_text: _most important academic subject law Education at Paris_
annotations.interpretation: Tom abbreviates the key contrast: Bologna's master discipline is law (B: law), Paris's is theology (P: theo). This is a condensed structural summary of MacIntyre's argument, written for quick recall. The colon notation suggests Tom is building a parallel structure he may use in writing or teaching.
annotations.rc_relevance: This two-line note encodes one of RC's most important historical data points: that the choice of a university's highest discipline is a moral and theological decision, not an administrative one. RC would use this to argue that modern universities' elevation of STEM or economics reflects an implicit (and contestable) theology of human ends.

annotations.content: Education at Paris was designed to put in question just such standards of utility by enquiring how the useful ought to be related to the quest for human perfection
annotations.anchor_text: _Paris was designed to put in question just such standards of utility_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's claim that Paris's purpose was explicitly critical and interrogative — not just to transmit doctrine but to question utility-standards by reference to human perfection. This is MacIntyre at his most programmatic: the university as a site of tradition-based moral critique. Tom likely sees this as a model for what RC argues universities should still do.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's recovery project. If Paris was designed to interrogate utility by reference to human perfection — an Augustinian/Thomistic telos — then RC is arguing for something analogous: resurrecting institutions capable of asking what human flourishing really requires.

annotations.content: conceived in Augustinian terms, a quest the point of which is necessarily invisible to those not already engaged in it
annotations.anchor_text: _quest for human perfection conceived in Augustinian terms_
annotations.interpretation: This underline targets one of MacIntyre's most philosophically dense claims: that the Augustinian quest is only intelligible from within the tradition. Tom recognizes this as the epistemological crux — the tradition-constituted rationality thesis applied to the university. It is also potentially the most controversial claim on the page, since it implies that outsiders cannot evaluate the tradition on neutral grounds.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is central to RC's engagement with MacIntyre's epistemology. The 'invisibility' claim explains why RC cannot simply argue for civility and virtue on neutral liberal grounds — one must be formed within a tradition to see what it is aiming at. This has profound implications for RC's rhetorical and pedagogical strategy.

annotations.content: Hence all education at Paris was subordinated to and directed towards the study of theology
annotations.anchor_text: _all education at Paris was subordinated to and directed towards_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the summative claim about Paris's curricular hierarchy. 'Hence' signals this is a logical conclusion from the preceding argument. Tom may be noting both the strength of the claim and its challenge to modern secular universities, which have no equivalent organizing telos.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC asks what could replace theology as the integrating discipline in a post-Christian university. This underline marks the historical moment when theology performed that integrating function — and implicitly raises the RC question: what does moral community look like without it?

annotations.content: theology
annotations.anchor_text: _subordinated to and directed towards the study of theology_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'theology' as the culminating term of MacIntyre's argument about Paris. This is a key word circle — marking the telos of the entire Parisian curricular structure. It may also signal Tom's awareness that this is the most contestable element of MacIntyre's account for a contemporary secular audience.
annotations.rc_relevance: Circling 'theology' here connects to RC's central question about whether Thomistic moral enquiry requires theological commitment or can be accessed by those outside the faith tradition. MacIntyre's answer seems to be yes — which RC must grapple with honestly.

annotations.content: although the corporate interests of masters in securing their autonomy and security from external authority was certainly important at Paris, as it was at Bologna, both the forms of organization of the university at Paris and its curriculum cannot be adequately explained except as the outcome of a debate over educational ideals
annotations.anchor_text: _Ferruolo has recently reexamined the question of how the University of Paris_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines Ferruolo's corrective thesis at length: institutional forms cannot be explained by corporate self-interest alone — educational ideals drive organizational structure. This is MacIntyre using a historian to validate his philosophical point. Tom is likely flagging this as empirical support for an otherwise philosophical argument, and noting that MacIntyre is careful to acknowledge the corporate interest factor before subordinating it.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's institutionalist argument. If Paris's organizational forms were shaped by debates over educational ideals, then RC's claim that institutions embody moral visions — not just power arrangements — has historical precedent. This challenges purely sociological or economic accounts of institutional formation.

annotations.content: debate over educational ideals *
annotations.anchor_text: _outcome of a debate over educational ideals_
annotations.interpretation: Tom repeats the phrase 'debate over educational ideals' in the margin with an asterisk — a strong signal that this is a key phrase he wants to carry forward. The asterisk elevates it above a mere summary note to a flagged concept. He is almost certainly planning to use this phrase in his own writing or to return to it as a structural marker.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is itself a contribution to a 'debate over educational ideals' in the contemporary sense. Tom naming this phrase with an asterisk suggests he sees it as a RC thesis-generator: that the project of resurrecting civility is inseparable from contesting what education is fundamentally for.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the Augustinian conception of moral enquiry is not merely a historical relic but pervades modern thought in disguised forms: Descartes' cogito, Berkeley's sign-theory of nature, and Wittgenstein's account of ostensive learning all reproduce Augustinian structures, even when their authors believed they were repudiating them. Nietzsche's insight that 'we still believe in grammar' is read as an inversion rather than escape of the Augustinian standpoint — without God, there is no genuine objectivity of interpretation or conceptualization. The section closes by introducing Umberto Eco's critique of Porphyry's context-independent semantics, setting up the contrast between context-free and tradition-embedded meaning.

annotations.content: Prelim inarily Acceptance Thesis PAT
annotations.anchor_text: _moral enquiry and of rationality those conceptions_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is naming a structural thesis he is tracking across this chapter — a 'Preliminary Acceptance Thesis' (PAT). This suggests he is reading MacIntyre's argument as requiring that any rational enquiry presupposes prior acceptance of a framework of beliefs and practices before critical engagement is possible. The abbreviation 'PAT' indicates he intends to reference this concept repeatedly, likely as a named argumentative unit in his own work.
annotations.rc_relevance: PAT maps directly onto the RC theme of tradition as a precondition for rationality — that civic and moral formation requires prior initiation into practices before reform or critique is meaningful, contra Enlightenment views of context-free reason.

annotations.content: without acceptance at some fundamental level of those beliefs and initiation into the form of life defined by those practices, rational counter with Augustinianism is ruled out
annotations.anchor_text: _without acceptance at some fundamental level_
annotations.interpretation: This is almost certainly the textual basis for Tom's PAT label above. He is isolating MacIntyre's claim that rational engagement with Augustinianism requires prior acceptance — not bracketed neutrality. The underline signals this as a foundational claim Tom wants to be able to cite precisely, likely because it bears on how traditions can be critiqued from within vs. from without.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly supports the RC argument that civic and moral traditions cannot be evaluated from a tradition-neutral standpoint — a central MacIntyrean premise Tom is deploying against Enlightenment proceduralism.

annotations.content: Paris as Augustinian
annotations.anchor_text: _counter with Augustinianism is ruled out in the most limited way_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is cross-referencing the historical episode of the University of Paris, likely the 13th-century condemnations or the Augustinian theological tradition dominant there before Thomism. This note suggests he is tracking MacIntyre's implicit narrative about institutional sites of tradition — Paris as an Augustinian stronghold matters for understanding what kind of 'acceptance' was required for entry into that tradition-constituted enquiry.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project's interest in institutions as character-forming environments finds a historical anchor here — the University of Paris as a site where the PAT was institutionally enforced, relevant to subsidiarity and the role of educational institutions in transmitting tradition.

annotations.content: Contemp[orary] practice against [in]accessibility [of] A's [Augustine's] system from [our?] point
annotations.anchor_text: _between particular Augustinian theses and a number of positions which have been advanced_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is noting the contemporary problem of accessing Augustinianism — the system feels inaccessible from modern standpoints precisely because modern practices have replaced the forms of life that made Augustine's enquiry intelligible. This is a worry about translation across tradition-boundaries, not just historical distance. The word 'against' may signal that Tom sees contemporary practice as actively working against retrieval of Augustinian resources.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is central to the RC project's challenge: how to recover classical and early modern traditions of civility when contemporary social practices have severed the conditions of intelligibility those traditions presuppose. Tom is flagging the accessibility problem explicitly.

annotations.content: a certain relationship to the Augustinian standpoint can be discerned even in some of those who reject it most trenchantly
annotations.anchor_text: _discerned even in some of those who reject it most trenchantly_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this claim because it is the pivot of MacIntyre's argument about covert Augustinianism — that rejection does not escape the framework. This is the logical engine behind the Nietzsche analysis that follows. Tom likely sees this as a model for how RC can argue that secular liberal critics of tradition are themselves operating within tradition-shaped assumptions they have not acknowledged.
annotations.rc_relevance: This supports the RC argument that contemporary social fragmentation is not a post-traditional condition but a misdirected or covert continuation of tradition-shaped moral structures — a key move in MacIntyre's genealogical critique.

annotations.content: Nietzsche meant by belief in grammar was belief that the structure of language somehow mirrors and presupposes belief in an order of things
annotations.anchor_text: _Nietzsche meant by belief in grammar was belief_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this definition carefully, distinguishing Nietzsche's actual claim from a superficial reading. The emphasis is on 'mirrors and presupposes' — language is not merely conventional but ontologically committed. Tom appears to be tracking how even anti-metaphysical thinkers carry metaphysical commitments in their grammar, which is a point he can use against purely procedural or linguistic accounts of civility.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this connects to the critique of Enlightenment rationalism's pretense of neutrality — if language itself presupposes an order of things, then civic discourse is never tradition-neutral, and 'civility' as a thin procedural norm is philosophically untenable.

annotations.content: It was Nietzsche's insight that so long as reference to such a reality is still presupposed, belief in God is covertly present
annotations.anchor_text: _reference to such a reality is still presupposed belief in God_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks this as Nietzsche's diagnostic insight — that secularism inherits theological commitments it disavows. The underline on 'covertly present' is the key phrase: covert presence is exactly the structure MacIntyre is diagnosing throughout the chapter. Tom is likely building toward an argument that modern civic discourse similarly carries covert Augustinian or Thomistic moral commitments.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's recovery project: if modern moral discourse covertly presupposes the very traditions it rejects, then the RC project is not introducing alien resources but making explicit what is already latent in civic life.

annotations.content: And in so asserting Nietzsche simply inverted the Augustinian standpoint: without God there is no genuine objectivity of interpretation or conceptualization
annotations.anchor_text: _Nietzsche simply inverted the Augustinian standpoint without God_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the inversion thesis — that Nietzsche is not outside Augustine but is Augustine's photographic negative. 'Simply inverted' is doing heavy philosophical work: inversion preserves the structure while reversing the values. Tom is likely tracking this as a template for how all modern anti-traditionalist positions relate to the traditions they reject — they invert rather than escape.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this inversion thesis is a powerful rhetorical and philosophical tool: the genealogist's critique of tradition is itself tradition-dependent, which undermines the authority of purely secular or anti-institutional critiques of civic virtue.

annotations.content: I save point of context
annotations.anchor_text: _without God there is no genuine objectivity of interpretation_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging this passage as one he intends to deploy — 'I save' suggests he is bookmarking this for use in his own writing or argumentation. 'Point of context' indicates he sees the context-dependence of interpretation (as opposed to Nietzsche's attempted context-free nihilism) as the key takeaway. This is a self-directed note about where to place this argument in his RC framework.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'point of context' Tom is saving is the MacIntyrean claim that genuine interpretation requires tradition-embedded context — directly applicable to RC's argument that civic friendship and moral community require shared narrative contexts, not procedural abstractions.

annotations.content: Porphyry's error, according to Eco, is not to have given an account of categorial predication in which the meanings of the predicates were context-independent
annotations.anchor_text: _Porphyry's error according to Eco is not to have given_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this to capture the Eco-on-Porphyry critique: context-independence is the error, not a virtue. This reinforces the chapter's central argument that meaning is always tradition- and context-embedded. Tom is likely tracking the Eco reference as a modern confirmation of MacIntyre's Augustinian-Thomistic point about the analogical, context-dependent nature of predication.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's critique of liberal proceduralism: just as Porphyry's context-independent semantics fails philosophically, context-independent norms of civility fail to constitute genuine moral community — meaning and virtue require shared practices and traditions.

annotations.content: Arrow pointing from margin note toward underlined text about Augustinian standpoint being discerned in those who reject it
annotations.anchor_text: _discerned even in some of those who reject it most trenchantly_
annotations.interpretation: The arrow connects Tom's margin notes about contemporary practice and inaccessibility to the textual claim about covert Augustinianism. It is a structural connector indicating that Tom sees his own worry about inaccessibility as being answered — or at least complicated — by MacIntyre's point that rejection does not equal escape. The arrow may also connect the 'Paris as Augustinian' note to this claim.
annotations.rc_relevance: This structural connection is important for RC: the arrow suggests Tom is working through the tension between the tradition's apparent inaccessibility and its covert persistence — the very tension the RC project must navigate in recovering civility for contemporary audiences.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the Augustinian scheme of moral enquiry cannot be evaluated piecemeal from an external standpoint: each thesis stands or falls as part of an integrated whole, and the scheme must be believed before it can be fully understood. This 'all-or-nothing' character shaped the form of the University of Paris curriculum, where each part contributes to a movement toward perfection encompassing both uneducated and educated participants. The uneducated grasp truth through scriptural images interpreted by the educated, making preaching and the university institutionally inseparable.

annotations.content: Obj: to Plat:
annotations.anchor_text: _independent of ontology Porphyry was not_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging what he reads as an objection directed at Platonism — likely noting that MacIntyre's account of Augustinianism as a rival to Porphyry's linguistics implies a specific critique of Platonic a priori ontology. The abbreviation 'Plat:' suggests Tom is cross-referencing Plato (or a Platonic position) as the real target underneath the Porphyry discussion, perhaps to track where MacIntyre distances Augustine from Neoplatonic inheritances.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project's concern with tradition vs. reform is directly implicated: Tom is asking whether the Augustinian tradition is genuinely distinct from the Platonic one it superficially resembles, which matters for whether it can serve as a living alternative to Enlightenment rationalism.

annotations.content: linguistics taken to be science by Eco, a semiformal a priori linguistics independent of ontology
annotations.anchor_text: _semiformal a priori linguistics independent of ontology_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the core characterization of Eco's project to keep the tripartite rival-versions structure in view — Eco represents the encyclopaedist tradition's aspiration to a science of language free from ontological commitment. This is a load-bearing contrast for MacIntyre's argument that Augustinianism is not simply a competing linguistics but a rival ontological enterprise.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's engagement with MacIntyre's encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition triad depends on understanding exactly what the encyclopaedist version claims; this underline shows Tom tracking the defining feature of that rival position.

annotations.content: he was engaged in an alternative and rival enterprise
annotations.anchor_text: _alternative and rival enterprise_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this phrase to anchor the key claim that Augustinianism is not a variant of the same project as Eco/Porphyry but a genuinely incommensurable rival. This is structurally important because it sets up why external evaluation is impossible — you cannot judge from inside one enterprise what counts as success in the other.
annotations.rc_relevance: The incommensurability of rival traditions is central to RC's argument that modern liberal discourse cannot serve as a neutral arbiter of civility debates — different traditions have different internal standards of rationality.

annotations.content: It might be supposed that, since Augustinianism is thus committed to a variety of agreements and disagreements with the protagonists of other positions, we could find in the subject matter of those agreements and disagreements grounds for evaluating Augustinianism without having first to enter into and accept Augustinian faith and practice.
annotations.anchor_text: _without having first to enter into and accept_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets the entire 'suppose' clause, which MacIntyre is about to refute. The bracket likely marks this as the key objection that the following pages will dismantle — that comparative agreement/disagreement could provide tradition-neutral evaluation criteria. Tom is flagging the structure of the argument: this is the tempting but mistaken view.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the epistemic crux for RC: the claim that you must inhabit a tradition before you can evaluate it challenges modern assumptions about neutral civic discourse and directly informs RC's argument about why institutional formation of character precedes rational assessment.

annotations.content: object + practice
annotations.anchor_text: _faith and practice Is Augustine's treatment_
annotations.interpretation: Tom distills MacIntyre's 'faith and practice' into his own shorthand 'object + practice,' suggesting he is translating the Augustinian epistemological requirement into a more general formula: that both the object of enquiry and the practical form of life are preconditions for understanding. This may also be Tom's way of connecting this to Aristotelian phronesis or to MacIntyre's broader point about practices in 'After Virtue.'
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC theme of institutions forming character maps directly onto this: civic friendship and subsidiarity require both the right 'object' (human flourishing, the common good) and the right 'practice' (habituated participation in community), not merely propositional agreement.

annotations.content: For each specific Augustinian thesis stands or falls, from the Augustinian standpoint, as part of the overall scheme of belief
annotations.anchor_text: _each specific Augustinian thesis stands or falls_
annotations.interpretation: This is one of the most heavily marked passages — Tom underlines the holism claim. The point is that Augustinian theses are not individually falsifiable from outside; they form a web where each element depends on the whole. Tom likely sees this as the philosophical heart of the page and possibly of the chapter, since it explains why piecemeal critique fails.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this holism is crucial: proposals to 'recover' civility cannot cherry-pick Thomistic or Augustinian elements while abandoning the theological and institutional framework — the parts require the whole, which challenges reformist or liberal appropriations of classical virtue.

annotations.content: Abstract the parts from that whole, treat them as if they are not parts, and you no longer have the Augustinian theses but only a counterfeit version of them.
annotations.anchor_text: _counterfeit version of them_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines 'counterfeit version' — a strong polemical phrase MacIntyre uses to delegitimize selective appropriation. Tom is likely flagging this as a key argumentative move that has direct implications for contemporary virtue ethics projects that borrow from Augustine or Aquinas without the metaphysical or theological scaffolding.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly challenges any RC project that would deploy classical civility discourse without its full theological and communal context — Tom may be wrestling with whether RC itself risks producing a 'counterfeit' version of tradition-based civility.

annotations.content: accept coherently is a whole... Reg—
annotations.anchor_text: _it is the scheme as a whole which requires_
annotations.interpretation: Tom notes 'accept coherently is a whole' paraphrasing MacIntyre's point, and adds 'Reg—' which likely abbreviates a cross-reference — possibly to a person (Regis?), a concept, or another text in Tom's reading network. The notation suggests Tom is connecting MacIntyre's holism claim to another thinker or to a prior note in his research, possibly tracking the requirement of coherent acceptance as a condition of rational enquiry.
annotations.rc_relevance: The coherence condition maps onto RC's concern with subsidiarity and institutional wholeness: a civic culture cannot be reconstructed piecemeal; it requires the coherent restoration of nested institutions and practices that mutually support each other.

annotations.content: it is the scheme as a whole which requires that it be believed before it can be understood
annotations.anchor_text: _believed before it can be understood_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the Augustinian epistemological formula ('crede ut intelligas') as MacIntyre deploys it structurally. This is the inversion of Enlightenment epistemology (understand before you commit) and Tom recognizes it as a pivotal claim. The underline with no marginal comment may mean Tom finds this self-explanatory as a key thesis.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is foundational for RC's argument against the Enlightenment liberal model of civic discourse: if moral and civic understanding requires prior formation and commitment, then the project of building civility must focus on institutional habituation rather than rational persuasion from a neutral standpoint.

annotations.content: recommends bldg of Aug scheme suggested—
annotations.anchor_text: _no way to pass a verdict on the Augustinian scheme_
annotations.interpretation: Tom reads MacIntyre here as not merely describing but implicitly recommending the construction (building) of an Augustinian scheme. 'Suggested—' with the dash implies Tom sees this as a normative recommendation embedded in what appears to be a descriptive argument — a classic MacIntyrean move where the genealogy of a tradition slides into advocacy for its recovery.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is perhaps the most directly RC-relevant annotation: Tom is identifying the moment where MacIntyre's historical-philosophical analysis becomes a practical proposal, which is precisely what RC aims to do — build on MacIntyre's diagnostic work to propose institutional reconstruction.

annotations.content: It is to this wholeness, this all-or-nothing character of the Augustinian scheme
annotations.anchor_text: _all-or-nothing character of the Augustinian scheme_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'wholeness' and the 'all-or-nothing' characterization, marking this as a key term or structural marker for the argument. The circle distinguishes this phrase as a concept to return to — perhaps for use as a section heading or thematic anchor in his own writing. It may also flag Tom's ambivalence: 'all-or-nothing' could be read as a strength (integrity) or a weakness (rigidity).
annotations.rc_relevance: RC must grapple with whether the 'all-or-nothing' character of tradition-based moral enquiry is a feature or a bug for contemporary civic recovery: it guarantees coherence but may make the tradition inaccessible to those not already formed within it — the central tension in RC's public-facing mission.

annotations.content: the form of the university curriculum at Paris was to owe its shape
annotations.anchor_text: _university curriculum at Paris was to owe its shape_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the institutional claim — that the 'all-or-nothing' character of Augustinianism was architecturally expressed in the Paris curriculum. This connects the abstract epistemological argument to a concrete historical institution, which is likely significant for Tom's interest in how institutions embody and transmit traditions.
annotations.rc_relevance: The university as an institution that forms character by embedding individuals in a totalized scheme of enquiry is directly relevant to RC's subsidiarity theme and to the question of what contemporary institutions could perform analogous functions in recovering civic virtue.

annotations.content: Edn (Aug inquiry into own good
annotations.anchor_text: _enquiry of that particular person about his or her own_
annotations.interpretation: Tom abbreviates 'Education (Augustinian) inquiry into own good' — he is flagging that the Paris curriculum's purpose, as MacIntyre describes it, is essentially self-knowledge oriented toward the good, not mere information transfer. This connects Augustinian education to the Socratic/Platonic tradition of self-examination while noting its distinctly Augustinian inflection toward perfection and God.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with character formation through institutions is enriched here: education, on this model, is not civic training in a thin sense but a participant's enquiry into their own good within a shared framework — a much thicker account of what 'civility education' would need to be.

annotations.content: The uneducated understand themselves through the images of scriptural narrative which the educated interpret to them. Hence derives one aspect at least of the crucial importance of preaching.
annotations.anchor_text: _crucial importance of preaching_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the two-tier epistemology (uneducated via images, educated via interpretation) and its institutional corollary (preaching). He may be flagging this as both descriptively interesting and normatively challenging — it implies a hierarchical structure of moral understanding that sits uneasily with democratic assumptions about civic discourse.
annotations.rc_relevance: This passage is highly relevant to RC's theme of tradition vs. reform and civic friendship: the Augustinian model assumes a mediating class (the educated/clergy) between tradition and populace, which raises the question of what analogous mediating institutions RC proposes for contemporary civil society.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the Augustinian tradition, though it claims authority grounded in scripture and divine illumination rather than shared rational premises, is nonetheless vulnerable on its own terms in at least two ways: (1) an internal problematic inherited from Platonism concerning the metaphysical relationship between particulars and universals, and (2) the doctrine of divine illumination, which generates deep difficulties about how fallen human beings can access knowledge at all without grace. These vulnerabilities are self-recognized within the tradition, not merely external critiques, and MacIntyre uses them to set up his tripartite diagnosis of Augustinian/Thomistic tradition as a rational enterprise capable of confronting and resolving its own crises.

annotations.content: 2 cases of self-recognized Augustinian vulnerability
annotations.anchor_text: _fact is and was vulnerable in two different ways_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is labeling MacIntyre's structural move: the tradition has not one but two internally acknowledged weaknesses. The phrase 'self-recognized' is key — Tom is tracking that MacIntyre is not mounting an external attack on Augustinianism but showing that the tradition's own rational standards expose these gaps. This is important for distinguishing MacIntyre's method from genealogical debunking.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's engagement with MacIntyre's tradition-constituted rationality: a tradition is genuinely rational precisely because it can identify and grapple with its own failures, which is what distinguishes it from mere ideology.

annotations.content: internal problematic
annotations.anchor_text: _it has its own internal problematic that set_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is isolating the technical term 'internal problematic' as a load-bearing concept. MacIntyre uses it to mean the set of questions a tradition generates from within its own commitments — questions that cannot be dissolved by switching frameworks but must be answered on the tradition's own terms. Circling this suggests Tom recognizes it as a key methodological term he will need to apply or contest elsewhere.
annotations.rc_relevance: Central to RC's argument structure: recovering civility requires understanding which problems are internal to a tradition (solvable by deepening it) versus which require genuine reform — this distinction maps onto RC's tradition vs. reform tension.

annotations.content: even from the standpoint of its own adherents. In fact is and was vulnerable in two different ways. First like all developed traditions of intellectual and practical enquiry, it has its own internal problematic
annotations.anchor_text: _even from the standpoint of its own adherents_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is bracketing the full statement of the vulnerability thesis to mark it as the core argumentative claim of this section. The bracket likely signals a passage worth citing directly in RC materials, or a claim he wants to hold in tension with MacIntyre's more positive account of tradition elsewhere in the book.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC needs to account for the fact that tradition-constituted enquiry is not self-validating — even the best traditions carry internal problems. This passage supports RC's nuanced stance that recovering tradition requires critical engagement, not nostalgia.

annotations.anchor_text: _The first is one which arises for every type of Platonism_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is assigning the alphanumeric label '1.(c)' to the first type of internal problematic — the Platonist problem of universals and particulars. This likely fits into a broader outline Tom is building across pages, where he is tracking MacIntyre's enumeration of problems. The '(c)' may indicate this is the third sub-item under a larger first heading in his notes, suggesting a multi-level structural map of the chapter.
annotations.rc_relevance: The Platonist problem of universals has direct implications for RC's concern with the metaphysical underpinnings of moral community — can shared moral norms be grounded in anything beyond convention if the status of universals is philosophically unstable?

annotations.content: 2. mcny / [entanglement] / [metaphys]
annotations.anchor_text: _William of Champeaux's arguments reinforces the view that Augustinian Platonism_
annotations.interpretation: Tom abbreviates 'mcny' likely as 'modernity' or possibly 'MacIntyre' — context suggests modernity, noting that this Platonist entanglement is what makes the Augustinian tradition legible as a historical problem in modernity. '[metaphys]' tags this as a metaphysical issue, and '[entanglement]' suggests Tom is reading this as a case where the tradition became enmeshed in problems not entirely of its own making. This is a sophisticated reading: the Augustinian tradition inherited Platonic baggage it could not easily shed.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with how classical traditions survive (or fail) their encounters with modernity is directly at stake: the metaphysical entanglement Tom flags is precisely the kind of inherited burden that makes tradition recovery difficult without Thomistic reconstruction.

annotations.anchor_text: _A second set of problems concerns the part played by divine illumination_
annotations.interpretation: Tom labels the second internal problematic as '(b)', continuing his structural outline. This is the divine illumination problem — how can fallen humans access knowledge requiring grace? The label pairs with the earlier '1.(c)' structure, suggesting Tom is organizing MacIntyre's argument into a nested outline for use in his own writing or teaching. The placement immediately next to 'A second set of problems' confirms Tom is tracking enumerated argumentative moves.
annotations.rc_relevance: The divine illumination problem is directly relevant to RC's interest in the role of institutions (especially church and university) in forming moral knowledge — if illumination requires grace mediated through community, then institutional formation is not incidental but necessary for moral enquiry.

annotations.content: illumination / (or 1: many / contradiction / personal ly, -) / [ethics] / needed / here
annotations.anchor_text: _all understanding requires divine illumination De Civitate Dei x 2_
annotations.interpretation: This is Tom's most complex annotation on the page. He appears to be flagging the one-to-many problem embedded in divine illumination: if all understanding requires illumination, is that illumination a single universal gift or individuated for each knower? 'Contradiction' suggests Tom sees a tension between Augustine's agreement with Plotinus (mind has no active principle of its own) and the claim that grace restores illumination personally. '[ethics] needed here' is striking — Tom may be arguing that without a clear account of how illumination connects to moral agency, the doctrine floats free of ethical application. This could be a note toward a RC critique or a gap he wants MacIntyre to fill.
annotations.rc_relevance: Highly relevant to RC's concern with moral formation: if illumination is personal and grace-dependent, how does a civic or institutional account of moral community function? This tension maps onto the subsidiarity theme — does moral formation happen at the level of the person, the community, or the institution?

annotations.content: Modernity asks for arguments. And the Augustinian responds that in this area there can be no shared premises unless and until the word of the scriptural preacher is heard as authoritative
annotations.anchor_text: _Modernity asks for arguments And the Augustinian_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's sharp formulation of the incommensurability thesis: the Augustinian tradition does not meet modernity on modernity's terms because it rejects the premise that shared rational premises are necessary or available apart from scripture. This is a key passage for understanding why MacIntyre sees the Augustinian/Thomistic tradition as a genuine rival rather than a subset of Enlightenment rationalism.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly engages RC's MacIntyre theme: the Enlightenment encyclopaedist demands universal rational justification, which the Augustinian tradition structurally refuses. RC must decide whether to adopt, qualify, or work around this refusal in making a case for civility in pluralist public space.

annotations.content: divine illumination in generating knowledge in the created mind
annotations.anchor_text: _A second set of problems concerns the part played by_
annotations.interpretation: Tom isolates the precise phrase defining the second problematic. This underline is more surgical than the broad underlining above — he is pinning down the exact locus of the problem as the interface between divine action and created intellect. This suggests Tom is tracking the epistemological stakes carefully, not just noting the topic.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, the question of how knowledge is generated in the created mind has institutional implications: if universities or civic communities are to serve as sites of moral formation, they must have some account of how illumination (or its analogues) operates in and through them.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the Augustinian tradition is internally committed to predicting the failure of rival rationalist projects — specifically those of the Enlightenment encyclopaedist, empiricist, Kantian, Hegelian, and positivist traditions — to vindicate their claims even on their own terms. The Augustinian must also claim that Adam Gifford's foundationalist assumptions (universally available, rationally warranted first principles requiring no prior religious commitment) are among the projects predicted to fail. This sets up a direct structural clash: Augustinianism requires initial commitment to a specific tradition as the only basis for genuine moral and theological enquiry, which is precisely what Gifford's will was designed to circumvent.

annotations.content: Is Ag's conception committed to failure of all enquiry internally, or externally, or both? (note: In Broad it's not so clear.)
annotations.anchor_text: _the Augustinian is committed to predicting are_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is pressing MacIntyre on the precise modal scope of the Augustinian prediction-of-failure claim: does the Augustinian say rival traditions fail by their own internal standards (immanent critique), by external Augustinian standards, or both simultaneously? The parenthetical note about 'Broad' likely refers to a comparison text or argument where this distinction is less sharp, suggesting Tom has encountered a version of this claim elsewhere and finds it cleaner or murkier. This is a genuine philosophical pressure point — MacIntyre's argument depends heavily on the 'even in its own terms' formulation, and Tom is interrogating whether that framing is consistently maintained.
annotations.rc_relevance: This goes to the heart of RC's engagement with MacIntyre's tripartite structure: if the Augustinian critique of encyclopaedism is only external, it risks being merely sectarian rather than genuinely dialectical — a key tension in whether tradition-constituted rationality can claim universal relevance for civic discourse.

annotations.content: I: W [nature / of / love?] [epist?]
annotations.anchor_text: _intellect is or was related to the good_
annotations.interpretation: This cryptic note appears to be Tom flagging a structural question marker — likely 'I: W' as shorthand for 'Issue: What is the nature of...' with 'love' and 'epist[emology]' as two candidate completions. It sits beside MacIntyre's discussion of how the intellect relates to the good apart from its being misdirected by the will, suggesting Tom is identifying this as a crux about the epistemology of love or the love-directed character of Augustinian cognition. The bracketed uncertainty in transcription reflects genuine ambiguity in the handwriting.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly connects to RC's concern with the formative role of desire and love (eros/caritas) in moral epistemology — the Augustinian claim that the intellect cannot operate well without rightly-ordered love is foundational to any RC account of character formation in civic institutions.

annotations.content: Triangle/arrow pointing downward with the label 'very strong interp...' written beside it
annotations.anchor_text: _neither Cartesian nor empiricist nor Kantian nor Hegelian_
annotations.interpretation: The downward triangle or arrow is Tom marking this as a 'very strong interpretation' — flagging that MacIntyre is making a maximally bold claim here, namely that none of the major post-Enlightenment rationalist traditions can vindicate themselves even internally. Tom's 'very strong interp...' suggests he finds this either impressively ambitious or potentially overreaching, and is noting it as a claim that requires careful scrutiny or defense. This is likely a point Tom intends to either leverage or contest in RC writing.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this is the decisive move: if MacIntyre is right, then Enlightenment-derived frameworks for public civility and civic reason are structurally incapable of grounding themselves — making the recovery of tradition-constituted rationality not merely one option but the only viable one for addressing social fragmentation.

annotations.content: will exhibit its own failure
annotations.anchor_text: _in its own historical development, so the Augustinian_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has isolated the operative prediction MacIntyre attributes to Augustinianism: that rival traditions will not merely be shown wrong from outside but will collapse from within. Underlining this phrase singles it out as the lynchpin of the entire argumentative structure of the chapter — the Augustinian doesn't just oppose encyclopaedism, it prophesies its self-destruction. Tom is likely marking this for direct quotation or close analysis.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's engagement with institutional decline and social fragmentation finds in this phrase a diagnostic tool: contemporary civic and moral institutions built on Enlightenment premises may be exhibiting precisely the 'own failure' MacIntyre predicted, which is the opening RC needs to argue for the recovery of older frameworks.

annotations.content: ineradicable incoherence
annotations.anchor_text: _exhibit its own failure either by falling into_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the first of the two modes of failure MacIntyre identifies — that a tradition either becomes incoherent beyond repair or is forced into unjustified belief. Isolating 'ineradicable incoherence' specifically suggests Tom is tracking the distinction between recoverable intellectual crisis and terminal breakdown, which is important for understanding how MacIntyre distinguishes productive tradition-internal tensions from tradition-ending collapse.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this distinction matters enormously: a tradition in crisis is not necessarily dying, but one in 'ineradicable incoherence' is — and diagnosing which condition contemporary liberal civic culture is in determines whether RC's project is one of reform or replacement.

annotations.content: universally available rationally warranted first principles
annotations.anchor_text: _the Augustinian denies that there can be any such principles_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles this phrase as a key technical term — Gifford's foundationalist epistemological commitment — which the Augustinian is committed to denying. Circling rather than underlining suggests Tom sees this as a defined term that needs to be carried through the argument, perhaps to be contrasted with the 'initial commitment' framework that follows. This is the exact point of rupture between the Gifford Lectures' presuppositions and MacIntyre's Augustinian counter-argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's treatment of subsidiarity and tradition: if there are no universally available first principles, then moral community cannot be built top-down from abstract rational foundations but must be cultivated through particular inherited traditions — the RC thesis in miniature.

annotations.content: initial commitment to one specific type of
annotations.anchor_text: _it is only through initial commitment to one specific_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets this phrase — likely because it is the Augustinian counter-thesis to Gifford's universalism. The bracket marks it as a structural counterpoint to the circled 'universally available first principles' above, and Tom may be noting that MacIntyre is here articulating the positive Augustinian epistemological requirement, not merely the negative critique. The numbering '2' visible below the bracket suggests Tom may be organizing a list of Augustinian commitments.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the most directly RC-relevant claim on the page: that genuine moral and theological enquiry requires prior particular commitment — which is the Augustinian (and Thomistic) basis for RC's argument that civic friendship and moral community cannot be rebuilt without recovery of substantive traditional commitments, not merely procedural neutrality.

annotations.content: shared by Ag P.125: only one winner.--
annotations.anchor_text: _initial commitment to one specific type of_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is cross-referencing page 125 of the same text (or possibly another work, but most likely MacIntyre's own text given the 'Ag' abbreviation for Augustinian/Augustine), noting that the claim about 'only one winner' — i.e., only one tradition can ultimately be vindicated — recurs or is reinforced there. The double dash suggests resignation or irony, as if Tom finds this conclusion either uncomfortably strong or philosophically bracing. This is a reader tracking the argumentative arc across the book.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'only one winner' formulation is a critical challenge for RC: if MacIntyre's Augustinianism entails that tradition-constituted rationality is zero-sum — only one tradition can be true — then RC must decide whether to embrace this exclusivism or soften it, which has direct implications for how RC engages pluralistic civic discourse.

annotations.content: Aus 6
annotations.anchor_text: _Where Adam Gifford held that moral and theological enquiry_
annotations.interpretation: This appears to be a cataloging or organizational note — likely marking this as the sixth key 'Augustinian' claim or passage Tom has tracked through the book. It functions as a structural index marker, suggesting Tom is building a numbered list of Augustinian commitments or theses across his reading. This kind of notation is typical of a researcher building a taxonomy of arguments for later synthesis.
annotations.rc_relevance: Tom's systematic indexing of Augustinian claims suggests he is constructing a structured account of Augustinian rationality for RC — likely to contrast it with the encyclopaedist and genealogist positions in building RC's own constructive argument.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that Augustinianism requires not only internal progress in solving its own problems but also that rival traditions — particularly Nietzschean genealogy — must be shown to exhibit incoherence or resourcelessness by comparison. The rediscovery of Aristotle's philosophy in its full integrity posed a challenge of the first order to the Augustinian tradition, raising questions about key theological doctrines and the epistemological presuppositions of the Augustinian curriculum. The first specific challenge concerned the Augustinian claim — expounded by Hugh of St. Victor — that all types of secular knowledge, new or old, could be integrated within its scheme, a claim now stressed by the recovery of Aristotelian natural science through Islamic commentators.
