annotations.content: failure of rivals
annotations.anchor_text: _The Augustinian Conception of Moral Enquiry_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has titled this page at the top margin with a thematic label: 'failure of rivals.' This is not MacIntyre's own section title but Tom's interpretive gloss, capturing the page's core argumentative function — that Augustinianism must demonstrate the failure of competing traditions (Nietzsche, Aristotelian-Islamic synthesis) to vindicate itself. The phrase economically encodes MacIntyre's tripartite rivalry structure and signals Tom sees this page as pivotal to understanding how traditions compete.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly maps to the RC theme of MacIntyre's 'encyclopaedist / genealogist / tradition' tripartite structure — Tom is flagging the moment where the tradition-vs.-rival dynamic becomes explicit, central to RC's argument that modern civility crises reflect failed rival frameworks.

annotations.content: A vs N
annotations.anchor_text: _contrast with and opposition between Augustine and Nietz-_
annotations.interpretation: Tom abbreviates 'Augustine versus Nietzsche,' marking the structural opposition MacIntyre is drawing. The notation is analytical shorthand suggesting Tom is tracking the A/N axis as a recurring structural pair throughout the book, not merely a one-off comparison. It may also signal that Tom wants to return to this contrast for RC purposes — the two represent opposite evaluations of pride, humility, and the fallen will.
annotations.rc_relevance: The Augustine/Nietzsche opposition is central to RC's concern with competing genealogies of virtue — Augustine's account of pride as vice versus Nietzsche's rehabilitation of it touches RC's core question of what kind of character formation a civil society requires.

annotations.content: sonality which manifested the perversity of the fallen will in the arrogance of the vice of pride
annotations.anchor_text: _What Augustine condemned, Nietzsche praised_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the specific content of Augustine's condemnation — pride as the perversity of fallen will — which Nietzsche revalued as nobility of instinct. The underline isolates the anthropological crux: the diagnosis of the human condition that divides Augustinian from Nietzschean moral psychology. Tom is likely flagging this as a key doctrinal claim that needs to be carried forward into RC's analysis of character.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's project of recovering civic virtue must navigate this fault line — the Augustinian account of pride as disorder versus modern (Nietzschean-influenced) celebrations of self-assertion. This is foundational to RC's treatment of humility as a civic virtue.

annotations.content: humility
annotations.anchor_text: _Nietzsche perceived a perverse weakness and sickness_
annotations.interpretation: The separate underlining of 'humility' (with what appears to be a circle or emphasis mark) isolates it as a key contested term. Tom is tracking this virtue-term specifically, likely because it is one of the most contested between Augustinian and Nietzschean frameworks. The isolation of the single word suggests Tom wants to use it as a conceptual anchor in RC.
annotations.rc_relevance: Humility is a cardinal virtue in the Augustinian-Thomistic tradition that RC seeks to recover; its dismissal by Nietzsche as sickness is precisely the kind of genealogical attack on tradition that RC must address.

annotations.content: Augustinianism requires for its fullest rational vindication not only progress in the solution of its own problems but further confirmation by the way in which such rival projects of intellectual and practical enquiry exhibit incoherence or resourcelessness
annotations.anchor_text: _So Augustinianism requires for its fullest rational vindication_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets the core methodological claim of the page — that a tradition's vindication is dual: internal progress AND demonstrated failure of rivals. The bracket marks this as a key structural thesis, possibly to be cited directly in RC. The word 'key' written in the margin nearby confirms Tom sees this as the argumentative hinge of the chapter.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is MacIntyre's central criterion for tradition-victory, directly relevant to RC's argument that modern liberal and postmodern frameworks have shown their resourcelessness — and that this creates space for recovering Augustinian-Thomistic civility.

annotations.content: key
annotations.anchor_text: _such rival projects of intellectual and practical enquiry exhibit incoherence or resourcelessness_
annotations.interpretation: A simple but emphatic label: Tom marks this passage as the key claim of the page, or possibly of the chapter. This likely corresponds to his bracketing of the same passage. The economy of the notation suggests Tom intends this as a citation anchor — a passage he will return to repeatedly in building RC's theoretical framework.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'key' notation signals Tom treats MacIntyre's dual-vindication criterion as foundational to RC's theoretical architecture — it is the warrant for claiming that rival modern frameworks have failed.

annotations.content: it itself is put in question by the success of such rival projects
annotations.anchor_text: _correspondingly it itself is put in question by the success_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the reciprocal vulnerability: Augustinianism is itself endangered if rival projects succeed. This is the other edge of the dual-vindication sword — the tradition cannot merely progress internally but must watch its rivals. Tom may be flagging this as a self-critical moment, acknowledging the stakes of the RC project itself.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC implicitly accepts this vulnerability — it is not merely recovering tradition for nostalgia but arguing that Augustinian-Thomistic frameworks can outperform rivals. This underline marks Tom's awareness of the argumentative risk RC must accept.

annotations.content: the challenge of Aristotelianism
annotations.anchor_text: _challenge was posed by the rediscovery of Aristotle's philosophy in all its integrity_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes out the full label for what MacIntyre calls the challenge posed to Augustinianism: the recovery of Aristotle. The spelling out of 'Aristotelianism' rather than abbreviating suggests Tom wants clarity on the structural label for this challenge, possibly for use as a section heading or thematic marker in RC notes. It also marks the transition from the Augustine/Nietzsche contrast to the historical challenge from within the intellectual tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: The Aristotelian challenge to Augustinianism is the historical prototype for RC's own situation — any recovery of Augustinian civic virtue must reckon with how Aquinas resolved this challenge through synthesis, which is MacIntyre's deeper argument.

annotations.content: rediscovery of Aristotle's philosophy in all its integrity
annotations.anchor_text: _challenge was posed by the rediscovery of Aristotle's philosophy_
annotations.interpretation: The phrase 'in all its integrity' is key — MacIntyre means not a partial or filtered Aristotle but the full corpus including natural science, which created the epistemological crisis. Tom underlines this to mark the specificity of the challenge: it was not Aristotle's ethics alone but the whole system that threatened Augustinian integration claims.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery project must similarly engage Aristotelian-Thomistic synthesis in its integrity, not selectively. This underline signals Tom's awareness that partial recoveries of tradition are insufficient — a theme central to MacIntyre's critique of liberal appropriations of virtue ethics.

annotations.content: doctrines
annotations.anchor_text: _raised not only questions of the truth of key theological doctrines_
annotations.interpretation: Tom singles out the word 'doctrines,' likely flagging that the Aristotelian challenge was not merely philosophical but struck at specific theological truth-claims. This micro-underline tracks a key term — doctrine as the site where philosophy and theology collide — relevant to RC's concern with how institutional teaching and character formation are grounded.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with institutions forming character depends on doctrinal coherence; this annotation tracks where MacIntyre locates the vulnerability of tradition to intellectual challenge — a vulnerability RC must acknowledge and address.

annotations.content: curriculum
annotations.anchor_text: _the Augustinian curriculum and the account of knowledge_
annotations.interpretation: The underlining of 'curriculum' marks an institutionally-loaded term — the curriculum is where the tradition's epistemological and theological commitments are embedded and transmitted. Tom is likely tracking this as a key institutional concept, distinct from abstract doctrine, pointing to how traditions are carried through pedagogical structures.
annotations.rc_relevance: Curriculum is a key RC concept connecting subsidiarity and institutional character formation — the question of what is taught, by whom, and in what order is central to how civic virtue is reproduced across generations.

annotations.content: First, it was the claim of the Augustinian scheme, as expounded not only by Hugh of St. Victor but also by other writers on the curriculum, that within that scheme all types of secular knowledge, new or old, could be integrated.
annotations.anchor_text: _First, it was the claim of the Augustinian scheme_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets the first numbered challenge to Augustinianism. The circled '1' in the margin confirms this is the first of what Tom expects to be a series — he is organizing MacIntyre's list of challenges structurally. The claim bracketed is the integration thesis: that Augustinianism can absorb all secular knowledge. Tom is likely tracking this as a claim RC must either defend or qualify.
annotations.rc_relevance: The integration claim is foundational to RC's vision of a unified moral community — if Augustinian-Thomistic frameworks cannot integrate contemporary knowledge (sciences, social sciences), RC's project faces the same challenge Hugh of St. Victor faced with Aristotle.

annotations.anchor_text: _First, it was the claim of the Augustinian scheme_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles the number '1' he has written in the margin, signaling the start of an enumerated list of challenges. This organizational mark suggests Tom is reading MacIntyre's argument as a structured set of specific challenges and wants to track them discretely. It likely anticipates further numbered challenges on subsequent pages.
annotations.rc_relevance: Tom's structural annotation practice here reflects his approach to building RC — he is extracting MacIntyre's argument into a usable analytical framework, mapping the challenges to tradition that RC must respond to.

annotations.content: that within that scheme all types of secular knowledge, new or old, could be integrated
annotations.anchor_text: _writers on the curriculum, that within that scheme_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the specific integration claim — 'all types of secular knowledge, new or old' — highlighting the universality of the Augustinian ambition. The phrase is the precise claim that Aristotelian natural science (via Islamic commentators) would stress. Tom is isolating the exact thesis that is under challenge.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC must make an analogous integration claim — that Augustinian-Thomistic moral frameworks can encompass contemporary knowledge — making this underline a direct marker of RC's own theoretical vulnerability and ambition.

annotations.content: recovery of Aristotelian science
annotations.anchor_text: _but the recovery of Aristotelian science, as expounded by the Islamic commentators_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the specific historical mechanism of challenge — it was not abstract Aristotelianism but Aristotelian science as mediated by Islamic commentators (Averroes, Avicenna) that forced the crisis. This is a historically precise underline, suggesting Tom wants to be accurate about the transmission history, not just the philosophical content.
annotations.rc_relevance: The Islamic mediation of Aristotle is a detail relevant to RC's concern with how traditions encounter rivals across cultural boundaries — a prototype for contemporary cross-cultural challenges to Western moral traditions.

annotations.content: today, that challenge is sciences, all of it: phys-[?], bio-[?] sciences, comp sciences
annotations.anchor_text: _recovery of Aristotelian science, as expounded by the Islamic commentators_
annotations.interpretation: Tom draws an explicit contemporary parallel: just as the recovery of Aristotelian natural science challenged 13th-century Augustinianism, today the challenge comes from the full range of modern sciences — physical, biological, and computer/computational sciences. The abbreviations are difficult to read precisely but the pattern is clear. This is Tom's most direct application of MacIntyre's historical argument to RC's present situation. He is arguing that RC faces an analogous integration challenge from contemporary science.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the single most RC-relevant annotation on the page — Tom explicitly maps MacIntyre's historical analysis onto RC's contemporary challenge. RC must show that Augustinian-Thomistic moral frameworks can integrate not just Aristotelian physics but modern physics, biology, and computational science. This annotation may indicate a planned RC section or chapter.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that Aristotle's accounts of what a science is and of the telos of all enquiry were fundamentally alien to Augustinian epistemology, which had no need for such frameworks given its reliance on divine illumination. This created an unavoidable dilemma for thirteenth-century Augustinians at Paris: admitting Aristotle's scheme of studies meant confronting students with mutually exclusive claims, while excluding it meant undermining both the university's universal integrative claims and Augustinianism's own institutional authority. The chapter closes by insisting that before the Augustinian response can be properly told, the full dimensions of the Aristotelian challenge must first be evaluated.

annotations.content: what a science is
annotations.anchor_text: _Aristotle provided accounts of what a science_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles this phrase to isolate MacIntyre's claim that Aristotle's formal definition of scientific knowledge is the specific point of incompatibility with Augustinian epistemology. This is not merely a historical observation — Tom is likely flagging the epistemological fault line: what counts as 'science' determines what counts as legitimate enquiry. The circle may also signal a cross-reference to MacIntyre's broader encyclopaedia/tradition argument elsewhere in the book.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly engages RC's concern with rival epistemologies and the question of what authorizes moral knowledge — a central tension between Enlightenment rationalism and tradition-constituted enquiry.

annotations.content: the telos of all enquiry
annotations.anchor_text: _accounts of what a science is of the telos_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines 'telos of all enquiry' as a load-bearing phrase — it names what is at stake when two rival traditions of enquiry confront each other. The teleological framing of knowledge itself is what Augustinianism resists or cannot absorb without self-contradiction. Tom is likely tracking how MacIntyre uses 'telos' to frame the incompatibility as not merely methodological but metaphysical.
annotations.rc_relevance: The telos of enquiry is foundational to RC's recovery of Thomistic moral reasoning — the project cannot proceed without adjudicating what enquiry is for, which is precisely MacIntyre's point here.

annotations.content: notably at odds with Augustine's version of Platonism
annotations.anchor_text: _that were notably at odds with Augustine's version_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the contrast between Aristotelian and Augustinian-Platonic epistemology as a structural marker in the argument. He is noting that the incompatibility is not incidental but deep — rooted in Augustine's Platonist inheritance. This may also flag a point Tom wants to contest or nuance: was Augustine's Platonism monolithic enough to be simply 'at odds' with Aristotle?
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's engagement with the Augustinian tradition requires precisely this kind of genealogical clarity about what Augustine actually inherited — the Platonist substrate shapes how civic and moral virtue were understood before Aquinas's synthesis.

annotations.content: in its account of the genesis of knowledge
annotations.anchor_text: _more especially in leaving no place and in having_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the specific epistemological mechanism MacIntyre identifies as the crux: how knowledge originates. Augustine's divine illumination model has no room for Aristotelian abstraction from sensory experience. Tom is likely tracking this as the point where the two traditions are structurally incompatible rather than merely in disagreement about conclusions.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, the 'genesis of knowledge' question underlies the contrast between tradition-constituted rationality and Enlightenment foundationalism — MacIntyre's whole project turns on this, and Tom is marking where it enters the historical argument.

annotations.content: in having no need, its no place and in having no need, for divine illumination
annotations.anchor_text: _leaving no place and in having no need for divine illumination_
annotations.interpretation: Tom double-marks the absence of divine illumination in Aristotle's account, emphasizing that the problem is not that Aristotle contradicts Augustine but that Aristotle's framework makes Augustine's central explanatory resource — divine illumination — simply unnecessary. This is a stronger claim than mere disagreement, and Tom appears to be registering its force.
annotations.rc_relevance: The redundancy of divine illumination within Aristotelian science is precisely what makes the Augustinian tradition vulnerable as an institutional authority — a tension RC must navigate when arguing for tradition-grounded moral formation.

annotations.content: it seemed to be the case that the Augustinian scheme could be true only if the Aristotelian was false, and vice versa
annotations.anchor_text: _So that in certain respects it seemed to be the case_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets the logical crux of the dilemma: mutual exclusivity. This is the hinge of MacIntyre's argument on this page, and the bracket signals Tom recognizes it as such. He may also be flagging this for contestation — is the mutual exclusivity as tight as MacIntyre claims, or does Aquinas's synthesis prove it was not truly zero-sum? The bracket isolates the claim as one requiring either endorsement or refutation.
annotations.rc_relevance: This mutual exclusivity claim is central to RC's engagement with MacIntyre's 'three rival versions' structure — it is the historical instantiation of incommensurability between traditions, which RC must either accept or argue against in proposing a recovery of civility.

annotations.content: an Augustinian dilemma
annotations.anchor_text: _Hence at once an Augustinian dilemma Admit_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'an Augustinian dilemma' as a structural label — MacIntyre is naming the logical trap explicitly, and Tom marks it as a key argumentative move. The circle may also signal that Tom is tracking MacIntyre's rhetorical technique of naming dilemmas as a way of forcing a choice between traditions, which is itself a methodological feature of 'Three Rival Versions.'
annotations.rc_relevance: The naming of an 'Augustinian dilemma' is structurally parallel to the 'encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition' tripartite that RC draws on — Tom is tracking how MacIntyre builds his case that traditions face genuine, not merely apparent, crises of authority.

annotations.content: Admit the Aristotelian scheme of studies and you thereby confront the students with not one, but two claims upon his allegiance, claims which at key points are mutually exclusive.
annotations.anchor_text: _Admit the Aristotelian scheme of studies and you_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the first horn of the dilemma in full, suggesting he wants to hold the complete logical structure in view rather than just the label. The emphasis on 'mutually exclusive' reinforces the bracket above — Tom is building a reading that tracks how MacIntyre establishes incommensurability through historical argument rather than mere assertion.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with institutional formation of character is directly implicated here: the university as an institution cannot simultaneously form students in two mutually exclusive traditions — this is the historical root of the fragmentation RC seeks to address.

annotations.content: Exclude the Aristotelian corpus from the scheme of studies and you put in question both the universal, integrative claims of Augustinianism and the claims of the university, at least as understood at Paris.
annotations.anchor_text: _Exclude the Aristotelian corpus from the scheme_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the second horn of the dilemma, completing the logical structure. The pairing with the previous underline suggests Tom is reading this page as presenting a formal dilemma — he is annotating both horns with equal weight. The phrase 'universal, integrative claims' may particularly interest Tom as it names the institutional ambition of Augustinianism, which RC is implicitly trying to recover in a different register.
annotations.rc_relevance: The failure of Augustinianism's 'universal, integrative claims' at Paris is a historical warning for RC — any project of moral and civic recovery must grapple with how institutional authority and intellectual comprehensiveness relate, which is a core RC concern.

annotations.content: before the story of the Augustinian response can be adequately told, it is necessary to evaluate the dimensions of the Aristotelian challenge
annotations.anchor_text: _before the story of the Augustinian response can_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the transitional methodological claim that closes the chapter. This is MacIntyre announcing his next move, and Tom marks it as a structural signpost. The underline suggests Tom is reading carefully for MacIntyre's argumentative sequence — understanding the challenge precedes understanding the response, which is itself a claim about the logic of tradition-constituted enquiry.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this methodological principle has direct application: before proposing a recovery of civility, the dimensions of the challenge posed by social fragmentation and Enlightenment individualism must be fully mapped — MacIntyre's historical procedure mirrors RC's own theoretical obligation.

annotations.anchor_text: _before the story of the Augustinian response_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has written a circled '2' in the right margin, likely as a structural label organizing this page within a sequence — possibly marking this as the second major point or the second phase of an argument he is outlining across multiple pages. Given RC's systematic approach to MacIntyre, this may correspond to a second thesis in the chapter, or the second of the three rival versions receiving extended treatment. The circle distinguishes it as a label rather than an annotation of printed text.
annotations.rc_relevance: The numbered labeling suggests Tom is building a schematic reading of MacIntyre's argument structure, which aligns with RC's need to extract a usable architecture from 'Three Rival Versions' for application to contemporary civility debates.

pages.summary: This page (104) consists entirely of bibliographic endnotes for the chapter on the Augustinian conception of moral enquiry, citing secondary scholarship on medieval monastic culture, the twelfth-century renaissance, and the intellectual world of Hugh of St. Victor. The references point to foundational works on the origins of universities, monastic love of learning, and twelfth-century theological renewal, situating MacIntyre's Augustinian 'tradition' version of moral enquiry in its historical-institutional context. No substantive argument is made here; the page functions as a scholarly apparatus anchoring the chapter's claims about pre-modern tradition-constituted rationality.

annotations.content: Jerome Taylor, New York, 1961
annotations.anchor_text: _Victor has been translated by Jerome Taylor_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has circled the Taylor translation of the Didascalion, likely flagging it as a primary text he intends to consult or already owns. The Didascalion of Hugh of St. Victor is a foundational text for understanding medieval conceptions of ordered learning and the unity of knowledge — directly relevant to MacIntyre's argument about tradition-constituted enquiry. The circle may indicate Tom wants to source this text for the RC project, or that he recognizes it as a key interlocutor for MacIntyre's Augustinian tradition strand.
annotations.rc_relevance: Hugh of St. Victor's Didascalion represents precisely the kind of pre-modern, institutionally embedded, virtue-ordered pedagogy that MacIntyre contrasts with Enlightenment encyclopaedism — central to RC's interest in how institutions form character and transmit tradition.

annotations.content: Phen Ue Fem
annotations.anchor_text: _Victor has been translated by Jerome Taylor_
annotations.interpretation: This handwritten note is difficult to transcribe with confidence — 'Phen Ue Fem' may be an abbreviation or shorthand Tom uses, possibly 'Phenomenon of...' or a personal reference code. It could also be a misread of cursive abbreviations like 'Phen. U. Fem.' pointing to a phenomenological or feminist philosophical reference Tom is cross-referencing. The ambiguity is significant: it may be a reminder to compare MacIntyre's account of medieval learning with another framework entirely, suggesting Tom is reading MacIntyre against a contrasting lens.
annotations.rc_relevance: If this note gestures toward a contrasting theoretical framework (phenomenological, feminist, or otherwise), it may reflect Tom's interest in stress-testing the RC project's reliance on MacIntyre's Augustinian tradition against critiques from outside that tradition — a methodologically important move for the project's credibility.

annotations.content: Renaissance and Renewal in the Twelfth Century
annotations.anchor_text: _eds., Renaissance and Renewal in the Twelfth Century_
annotations.interpretation: The bracketing of this reference to Benson and Constable's edited volume on twelfth-century renewal suggests Tom recognizes this as particularly important among the citations on this page. The twelfth-century renaissance is the precise historical moment when the Augustinian tradition MacIntyre describes was being consolidated and institutionalized — before the Aristotelian disruption MacIntyre traces in subsequent chapters. Tom may be flagging this as background reading that illuminates the 'tradition' version's strongest historical moment.
annotations.rc_relevance: The twelfth-century renewal represents a high-water mark of tradition-constituted rationality operating through monastic and cathedral institutions — exactly the model of character-forming institutional community that RC seeks to recover as an antidote to contemporary fragmentation.

chapters.title: Aristotle and/or/against Augustine: Rival Traditions of Enquiry

pages.summary: MacIntyre traces how the challenge of reconciling Aristotle's doctrines with theistic belief in divine creation and the soul's immortality was first posed within Islamic philosophy before reaching Western Christianity. The Latin Averroists inherited their problems from Islamic and Judaic predecessors (particularly Averroës and Maimonides) but could not accept those predecessors' solutions at crucial points. Averroës' own dilemma — that unlike Plato, the Islamic philosopher cannot simply contrast philosophical truth with mere popular belief but must affirm the truth of mass religious belief — sets up the structural tension that will define the chapter's inquiry into rival traditions.

annotations.content: The problems of the relationship of Aristotle's doctrines to those of a theism whose doctrines included belief in divine creation of the world and in the immortality of the soul had of course been posed within Islam before they were confronted by the theologians of Western Christianity.
annotations.anchor_text: _problems of the relationship of Aristotle's doctrines_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is marking the foundational historical claim that opens the chapter: the Aristotle-theism problem is not originally a Christian one. By underlining the full arc from 'problems' to 'Western Christianity,' he seems to be tracking MacIntyre's insistence on the priority of Islamic intellectual history — a move that destabilizes any Eurocentric account of scholasticism. This likely resonates with Tom's interest in how traditions inherit problems rather than generate them ex nihilo.
annotations.rc_relevance: This maps directly onto RC's concern with how traditions of enquiry are constituted by inherited problems; the claim that Western Christianity received a pre-formed problematic from Islam is a concrete instance of MacIntyre's thesis that no tradition is self-originating.

annotations.content: was not only the greatly enlarged body of Aristotelian texts made available to them in the twelfth and thirteenth centuries but also the accompanying Islamic commentary
annotations.anchor_text: _greatly enlarged body of Aristotelian texts_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the 'not only… but also' construction, flagging MacIntyre's point that the transmission was double: texts AND commentary arrived together. This is significant because it means Latin scholastics didn't receive raw Aristotle but Aristotle already interpreted through an Islamic lens — the tradition came pre-packaged with its own interpretive framework.
annotations.rc_relevance: This bears on RC's theme of how institutions and traditions shape the very reception of ideas; the 'commentary tradition' as a vehicle of transmission is an example of how no text enters a tradition nakedly but always through prior interpretive mediation.

annotations.content: the accompanying Islamic commentary
annotations.anchor_text: _accompanying Islamic commentary culminating in that of Abu_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles this phrase specifically within the already-underlined sentence, adding a second layer of emphasis. He is isolating 'Islamic commentary' as a distinct concept — not just noting the historical fact but flagging the term itself as analytically important. This may signal that he wants to return to the notion of commentary as a tradition-bearing genre.
annotations.rc_relevance: The commentary tradition is central to MacIntyre's account of how traditions reproduce and develop; Tom may be thinking about analogues in the Augustinian tradition or in the RC project's own attempt to recover a living commentary tradition.

annotations.content: Since Jewish thinkers too had had to define their positions in relation to both Aristotle and Islam
annotations.anchor_text: _Since Jewish thinkers too had had_
annotations.interpretation: The 'too' is key here — Tom is likely marking MacIntyre's structural move of triangulating the problem: it's not a bilateral Islam-Christianity issue but a tripartite one involving Jewish thinkers as a third tradition forced into the same problematic space. Maimonides becomes a parallel case to Averroës, and Tom seems to be tracking how each tradition's response to Aristotle is shaped by its own internal commitments.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's tripartite structure (encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition) finds a historical precursor in this three-way encounter; Tom may be reading Maimonides and Averroës as case studies in what it means for a tradition to define itself under pressure from rival rationalities.

annotations.content: The difficulty which confronted most of the Latin appropriators of Aristotle, the so-called Latin Averroists, was that, while they inherited their problems from their Islamic and Judaic predecessors, they were unable to accept at certain central and crucial points the solutions proposed by those predecessors.
annotations.anchor_text: _difficulty which confronted most of the Latin appropriators_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets this as a thesis statement — the precise formulation of the Latin Averroist dilemma. The bracket signals structural importance: this is the hinge sentence of the opening section, defining the exact nature of the inheritance problem. Tom is likely flagging this as a model for how any tradition can inherit a problem-set without being able to adopt the accompanying solutions, which is a recurring MacIntyrean motif.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is perhaps the most RC-relevant sentence on the page: it is a concrete historical instantiation of MacIntyre's general claim that traditions can be constituted by their inability to resolve inherited problems — directly relevant to RC's analysis of contemporary moral fragmentation as an inheritance problem.

annotations.content: while they inherited their problems from their Islamic and Judaic predecessors, they were unable to accept at certain central and crucial points the solutions
annotations.anchor_text: _inherited their problems from their Islamic and Judaic predecessors_
annotations.interpretation: Tom double-marks this phrase (it falls within the bracket AND is underlined), suggesting he considers the inherited-problems/unacceptable-solutions dyad to be a key analytical structure. He may be thinking about how this maps onto contemporary moral philosophy's inheritance of Enlightenment problems without being able to accept Enlightenment solutions — MacIntyre's own diagnosis of modernity.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the core RC problematic stated historically: tradition-constituted rationality means you cannot simply discard inherited problems, but you may be forced to seek new solutions — exactly what the RC project proposes to do with civility and Thomistic moral enquiry.

annotations.content: Averroës had contrasted his own situation as a philosopher in twelfth-century Cordoba with that of Plato in fourth-century Athens.
annotations.anchor_text: _Averroës had contrasted his own situation_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets the opening of the Averroës-Plato contrast, flagging the structural move MacIntyre is making: using Averroës' own self-analysis as a window into the tradition-philosopher problem. The bracket here may also be a reading aid marking the start of a new sub-argument. Tom is likely interested in how Averroës' situation — philosophically aware but religiously embedded — anticipates later tensions.
annotations.rc_relevance: The philosopher's relationship to the beliefs of the community is central to RC's concern with civic friendship and subsidiarity; Averroës' dilemma — cannot stand outside common belief as Plato did — is a historical case study in the tension between philosophical enquiry and communal solidarity.

annotations.content: Plato had been able to contrast the conclusions of the philosopher with mere popular belief. Truth belongs to the former, whereas the latter cannot be true.
annotations.anchor_text: _Plato had been able to contrast the conclusions_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines Plato's position as a foil for Averroës — the Platonic philosopher can stand apart from and above popular belief. Tom seems to be marking this as a position MacIntyre is about to complicate or reject, given that the next sentence introduces Averroës' constraint. There may be an implicit question here about whether MacIntyre himself endorses a Platonic separation of philosophical and popular truth.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with civic friendship and the formation of character within communities implies skepticism about the Platonic philosopher-above-the-masses model; Tom is likely tracking this as a position that Thomistic and Augustinian traditions will challenge.

annotations.content: But the Islamic philosopher has to share the beliefs of the masses, affirming the truth of those same beliefs pre-
annotations.anchor_text: _Islamic philosopher has to share the beliefs of the masses_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the constraint on Averroës that distinguishes him from Plato: the Islamic philosopher cannot treat popular religious belief as merely false. The underline seems to mark this as the crux — the philosophical problem that Islam poses for a would-be Aristotelian. Tom may be asking: is this constraint a limitation, or is it actually closer to MacIntyre's own view that tradition-embedded belief is not simply 'mere popular belief' to be transcended?
annotations.rc_relevance: This tension is directly relevant to RC's recovery of tradition: the question of whether the philosopher must share the community's beliefs, or can stand above them, maps onto debates about the role of the intellectual in civic life and the nature of authority in tradition-constituted enquiry.

annotations.content: Vertical line or tick mark in the right margin beside the paragraph beginning 'Since Jewish thinkers too'
annotations.anchor_text: _Since Jewish thinkers too had had to define_
annotations.interpretation: A marginal symbol (appears to be a '2' or a tick with a downward curl) in the right margin beside the Jewish thinkers passage. This may be a structural number indicating this is the second of a sequence Tom is tracking (Islam = 1, Judaism = 2, Latin Christianity = 3?), or it may be a priority mark indicating he wants to return to this passage. The ambiguity between '2' and a check-mark matters: if it's '2', Tom is organizing the three-tradition structure explicitly.
annotations.rc_relevance: If Tom is numbering the three traditions as they enter MacIntyre's argument, this maps directly onto RC's tripartite framework and suggests he is reading the historical narrative as an analogue or precursor to MacIntyre's systematic three-versions structure.

pages.summary: MacIntyre examines how Averroës and Maimonides each negotiate the relationship between philosophical enquiry and religious authority — for Averroës, the philosopher's faithfulness to Islam is judged by religious authorities appealing to the Qur'an rather than to philosophical rationality; for Maimonides, there is a crucial genre distinction between the Mishneh Torah (authoritative commentary standard) and The Guide for the Perplexed (rational philosophical enquiry), with Maimonides founding a distinctive philosophical genre for systematically reconciling philosophy with Halakhic authority. The page concludes by noting that for both thinkers, religious doctrines constrain philosophical activity without themselves being philosophically informed — a situation that changes dramatically for the Latin Averroists.

annotations.content: Religious phil): facing popular belief, authoritative text, authoritative religious interpretation of those texts
annotations.anchor_text: _Aristotle and/or against Augustine_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is constructing a tripartite definition of what constitutes 'religious philosophy' as a distinct mode of enquiry: it must navigate (1) popular/folk belief, (2) canonical authoritative texts, and (3) the authoritative interpretive tradition around those texts. This is not merely descriptive — Tom appears to be building a working schema, possibly for use in RC, that characterizes the structural constraints any tradition-embedded philosophy faces. The abbreviation 'Phil)' suggests this is a term being formally defined for later use.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly maps onto MacIntyre's concept of tradition-constituted rationality: RC's recovery of Thomistic enquiry similarly requires navigating popular moral intuitions, authoritative texts (Aquinas, Scripture), and the interpretive tradition of commentators — Tom is identifying the structural parallel.

annotations.content: the whole community of Islam, masses and learned alike
annotations.anchor_text: _the whole community of Islam masses_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the universality claim — that philosophical truth must be reconciled not just for the learned elite but for the entire community. This is a tension MacIntyre is tracking: philosophy serves the few, but religious authority must serve all. Tom likely flags this as a problem of subsidiarity and the role of mediating institutions between the philosopher and the masses.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with civic friendship and community formation is directly implicated here — the question of how elite philosophical insight translates into communal moral formation is central to MacIntyre's project and to RC's interest in institutions that shape character across social strata.

annotations.content: the truths of philosophical theory have to be reconciled with those of the prophetically authorized and authored stories
annotations.anchor_text: _truths of philosophical theory reconciled prophetically_
annotations.interpretation: This marks one of the book's core structural tensions: narrative (prophetic story) versus theoretical argumentation. Tom underlines this as a site of productive conflict — the word 'reconciled' is doing enormous work, and MacIntyre is careful not to say which subordinates the other. Tom may be flagging this as an analogue to the Thomistic synthesis of faith and reason.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the tradition vs. reform tension in RC's framework: prophetic narrative represents the inherited moral tradition, while philosophical theory represents the reforming rational impulse. MacIntyre's point that reconciliation is the philosopher's task resonates with RC's recovery project.

annotations.content: the philosopher remains faithful to Islam and to its law
annotations.anchor_text: _philosopher remains faithful to Islam and its law_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles this phrase, likely because it crystallizes a paradox: faithfulness is the criterion of the philosopher's standing, but that faithfulness is judged externally by religious authorities, not by philosophical self-assessment. This means the philosopher cannot be the final judge of their own adequacy — a radical constraint on autonomous rational enquiry. Tom may be flagging this as a key moment where tradition-constituted rationality is shown to be non-self-certifying.
annotations.rc_relevance: This speaks directly to MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism in RC's framework — the encyclopaedist's assumption that reason is self-authorizing is precisely what this passage denies. The philosopher's rationality is accountable to a community and its authorities, not to reason alone.

annotations.content: by the religious authorities
annotations.anchor_text: _ascertained by the religious authorities_
annotations.interpretation: The separate circling of 'religious authorities' as distinct from the larger circled phrase above suggests Tom is isolating the locus of judgment — it is institutional, not merely textual or rational. This may connect to Tom's interest in who holds legitimate authority in tradition-constituted communities, and whether that authority is accountable to reason or prior to it.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's subsidiarity theme: religious authorities here function as the mediating institutional layer between the philosopher and the community. Tom appears to be mapping the authority structure MacIntyre describes onto RC's concern with legitimate institutional formation of moral character.

annotations.content: the philosopher remains faithful to Islam and to its law has to be ascertained by the religious authorities, but those authorities appeal to the Qur'an and to the law itself in so deciding, not to any philosophical understanding of truth or of rationality
annotations.anchor_text: _not to any philosophical understanding of truth_
annotations.interpretation: The bracket around this full passage marks it as a unified argumentative unit Tom wants to preserve intact. The key move MacIntyre makes here — that religious authorities appeal to text and law, not to philosophical rationality — is being flagged as a structural claim about the asymmetry between religious and philosophical authority. Tom likely sees this as the crux of the Averroëan position and wants to return to it.
annotations.rc_relevance: This asymmetry is central to RC's MacIntyrean framework: the genealogist and the encyclopaedist each claim rational self-sufficiency, but the tradition-holder here explicitly subordinates rationality to textual and legal authority — Tom is marking where the tradition's self-understanding most clearly diverges from Enlightenment assumptions.

annotations.content: a crucial distinction of genres
annotations.anchor_text: _So there is a crucial distinction of genres_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as a methodologically pivotal claim. MacIntyre's argument about genre distinction — that different kinds of writing operate under different rational standards — is one of the book's most important structural moves. Tom is tracking this because genre determines what counts as consistency, authority, and truth within a tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: The genre distinction maps onto RC's concern with how different institutional forms (legal, narrative, philosophical) each carry their own standards of rationality — this is MacIntyre's point that tradition-constituted enquiry is not monolithic but internally differentiated.

annotations.content: the appeal to authority in the Mishneh Torah provides in the mode of commentary a standard with which nothing written in any other genre can be allowed to be inconsistent
annotations.anchor_text: _appeal to authority Mishneh Torah provides standard_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks this as the definitive statement of how authoritative commentary functions as a normative standard across genres. The phrase 'cannot be allowed to be inconsistent' is almost juridical — it implies enforcement, not merely preference. Tom likely sees this as illustrating how tradition sets the outer bounds of permissible philosophical argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is a precise illustration of MacIntyre's tradition-constituted rationality operating in practice: the Mishneh Torah functions analogously to how Aquinas functions in Thomistic enquiry — as the authoritative standard against which all other enquiry must be checked. RC's recovery project depends on recovering exactly this kind of normative genre hierarchy.

annotations.content: But philosophical enquiry itself and the understanding of truth and rationality involved in such enquiry are reserved for the philosopher
annotations.anchor_text: _philosophical enquiry itself understanding of truth reserved_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the exclusivity claim — that philosophical rationality is not democratized but reserved for a specialized class. This creates a two-tier community: the masses governed by authoritative narrative and law, the philosopher governed additionally by rational enquiry. Tom may be flagging this elitism as a tension MacIntyre is either endorsing or describing critically.
annotations.rc_relevance: This two-tier structure is in tension with RC's civic friendship ideal, which requires some shared rational ground across social levels. MacIntyre's Thomistic recovery must grapple with whether philosophical truth can be genuinely communal or remains the property of a learned class.

annotations.content: a distinctive philosophical genre for the discussion of the systematic relationship of philosophy to the acceptance of Halakhic authority and all that such acceptance entails
annotations.anchor_text: _distinctive philosophical genre systematic relationship philosophy_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks Maimonides' achievement as genre-creation — The Guide for the Perplexed is not just philosophy but a new kind of philosophical writing designed specifically to negotiate the philosophy/authority interface. Tom likely sees this as a model for what MacIntyre himself is doing in this book: creating a genre adequate to the task of tradition-recovery.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is itself a genre-creating project in some sense — it seeks a mode of enquiry adequate to recovering civility within contemporary fragmentation. Tom's marking of Maimonides' genre innovation suggests he sees a structural parallel between Maimonides' task and MacIntyre's (and RC's) own.

annotations.content: Thus for both Averroës and Maimonides the religious doctrines and practices with which Aristotelian philosophy and philosophical enquiry more generally have to be reconciled and integrated might place constraints upon the activities and conclusions of the philosopher but were not themselves informed by philosophical themes, theories, and arguments
annotations.anchor_text: _religious doctrines practices not themselves informed philosophical_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks this as the summary statement of the Averroës/Maimonides position before MacIntyre pivots to the Latin Averroists. The key word is 'constraints' — religion constrains philosophy from outside without itself being philosophically constituted. Tom is likely noting this as the structural feature that distinguishes Islamic/Jewish philosophical theology from Latin Scholasticism, where the relationship becomes more mutually constitutive.
annotations.rc_relevance: This one-directional constraint model is precisely what MacIntyre's Thomism seeks to overcome — in RC's framework, the recovery of Thomistic enquiry requires a relationship where philosophical argumentation and authoritative tradition are genuinely internally related, not merely externally constraining one another.

annotations.content: More particularly they did not contain within themselves any well-articulated understanding of truth and rationality
annotations.anchor_text: _did not contain well-articulated understanding truth rationality_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks this as the philosophical deficiency MacIntyre identifies in the religious traditions as Averroës and Maimonides received them — they lacked internal philosophical self-understanding. This is not a criticism but a structural observation: the tradition cannot adjudicate its own rational claims without importing philosophical tools from outside. Tom likely sees this as the opening MacIntyre uses to justify why philosophical enquiry is necessary within tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's project: a tradition that cannot articulate its own understanding of truth and rationality is vulnerable to the genealogist's critique. MacIntyre's recovery project — and RC's — requires traditions to develop internal philosophical self-understanding, not merely external authority.

annotations.content: T. Loughran (signature/initials at bottom left)
annotations.anchor_text: _confronted not only the problems of consistency_
annotations.interpretation: This appears to be Tom's ownership mark or reading signature at the bottom left margin. Its placement at the end of the page rather than the beginning may indicate a habit of marking pages he has actively engaged with rather than merely read.
annotations.rc_relevance: Ownership/identification mark; no direct RC thematic content, but consistent with a scholar marking pages of active interpretive engagement.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the encounter between Augustinian Christianity and Aristotelian philosophy produced radical inconsistencies at three levels, the most visible being theological antitheses over the eternity of the world, the immortality of the soul, and resurrection. However, he contends that ecclesiastical authority's response was not blanket condemnation but selective restriction and partial prohibition, which paradoxically allowed Aristotle's works to be read piecemeal rather than as a systematic challenge to the whole Augustinian mode of enquiry. This tactical prudence by the Church delayed recognition of the deeper, structural incompatibility between the two traditions — a conflict not just over particular doctrines but over styles of thought, standards of rationality, and the very structuring of moral enquiry.

annotations.content: the dogmatic theology of Latin Christianity was pervasively informed by philosophical theories and conclusions and carried within its own Augustinian understanding of truth and rationality an understanding apparently irreconcilable with that of Aristotle as presented by the best commentators and interpretations so far.
annotations.anchor_text: _dogmatic theology of Latin Christianity was pervasively_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is highlighting MacIntyre's thesis that the conflict is not merely theological but epistemological — the Augustinian framework carries its own embedded rationality that is structurally incompatible with Aristotle's. This is not just a doctrinal dispute; it is a clash of rival epistemologies. Tom likely sees this as foundational to MacIntyre's whole tripartite argument and wants to flag it as a key diagnostic passage.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's engagement with MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism — the idea that rationality is always tradition-embedded. The passage exemplifies the 'tradition vs. tradition' dynamic RC traces, showing that even within Latin Christianity, philosophical assumptions shape what counts as rational enquiry.

annotations.content: those two sets of standards apparently incommensurable as well as incompatible.
annotations.anchor_text: _those two sets of standards apparently incommensurable_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has highlighted the exact moment MacIntyre deploys 'incommensurable' — a term with heavy philosophical freight from Kuhn and MacIntyre's own earlier work in 'After Virtue.' Circling or highlighting this word suggests Tom is tracking how MacIntyre uses incommensurability not as a relativist concession but as a diagnostic of what tradition-rivalry actually means. The pairing 'incommensurable as well as incompatible' is precise: incompatible means they conflict; incommensurable means there is no neutral standard to adjudicate.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with social fragmentation maps directly onto this: modern moral disagreement is not merely disagreement but incommensurability — parties lack shared standards of rationality. This passage is a key theoretical anchor for RC's argument that civility requires recovering a common tradition of enquiry, not just procedural agreement.

annotations.content: Radical inconsistencies
annotations.anchor_text: _Radical inconsistencies between the two standpoints_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has circled the opening phrase of the paragraph that begins MacIntyre's three-level analysis. This functions as a structural marker — Tom is flagging the pivot point where MacIntyre moves from describing the general conflict to unpacking its internal architecture. The circle likely signals: 'this is the claim I need to track across what follows.'
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's tripartite framework (encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition) is itself a diagnosis of radical inconsistencies between moral traditions. Tom is likely noting that MacIntyre's method here — identifying levels of incompatibility — mirrors the analytical structure RC will use to diagnose contemporary fragmentation.

annotations.content: Number '1' in margin beside paragraph about central Christian doctrines and eternity of the world
annotations.anchor_text: _the attention of ecclesiastical and theological authority concerned central Christian doctrines_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is numbering the first of MacIntyre's three levels of inconsistency — the most visible level concerning central theological doctrines (eternity of the world, immortality of the soul, resurrection). By numbering it, Tom is building his own structural map of the argument, likely anticipating that the other two levels will be less obvious and more important to MacIntyre's deeper thesis. This is careful argumentative tracking.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's interest in institutional formation of character is relevant here: this first level is where ecclesiastical authority focused its attention, which MacIntyre suggests was a kind of institutional misdiagnosis. The institution policed surface doctrines while missing deeper structural challenges — a pattern RC would recognize in contemporary institutions that manage symptoms without addressing the underlying collapse of shared moral frameworks.

annotations.content: Number '2' or letter with downward arrow/bracket beside text about individual soul and Averroes
annotations.anchor_text: _separate immaterial existence of the individual soul, and where Averroes'_
annotations.interpretation: This marks the second level of inconsistency, related to the soul's immaterial existence and Averroes' interpretation of the De Anima, which denied individual soul-survival apart from the body. Tom is continuing his structural enumeration. The placement here suggests he is tracking how Averroism specifically sharpened the conflict — not just Aristotle abstractly but Aristotle as filtered through Islamic philosophy.
annotations.rc_relevance: The role of Averroes as mediating figure — making Aristotle more systematically dangerous to Christianity — anticipates RC's concern with how traditions are transmitted and transformed through rival communities of interpretation. This connects to MacIntyre's argument that traditions are not static but develop through encounters with rivals.

annotations.content: cause / eff s [possibly 'effects' or 'effets']
annotations.anchor_text: _levels of encounter, types of encounter both made possible in part_
annotations.interpretation: Tom appears to be noting a causal relationship — likely flagging that the ecclesiastical strategy of selective prohibition was both a cause and an effect of how Aristotle was received. Alternatively, 'cause/effects' may be Tom's shorthand for MacIntyre's implicit argument that the Church's tactical prudence had unintended effects: it caused Aristotle to be read selectively rather than systematically, which in turn delayed the full recognition of the structural challenge. This is Tom reading the argument causally, not just descriptively.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's interest in how institutions shape moral formation — the Church's partial prohibitions had downstream effects on how entire generations structured their enquiry. The unintended consequences of institutional intervention in intellectual life is a live concern for RC's analysis of how contemporary institutions either enable or foreclose genuine moral reasoning.

annotations.content: levels of encounter, types of encounter both made possible in part and also delayed by the extraordinary degree of tact and prudence shown in the first half of the thirteenth century by ecclesiastical authority
annotations.anchor_text: _levels of encounter, types of encounter both made possible_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has underlined MacIntyre's key claim that the Church's tact and prudence simultaneously enabled and delayed the full philosophical encounter. The underlining of 'levels of encounter' and 'types of encounter' suggests Tom is tracking MacIntyre's distinction — these are not synonyms but analytically different categories. The underlining of the entire causal chain shows Tom sees this as a crucial pivot: institutional prudence as a double-edged instrument in the history of ideas.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's theme of subsidiarity and institutional formation is directly implicated: MacIntyre is showing that ecclesiastical authority's prudential management of intellectual life had profound unintended consequences for the development of moral tradition. This is a case study in how institutions can both preserve and distort the traditions they are meant to steward — a central tension in RC's analysis.

annotations.content: a variety of restrictions, partial prohibitions, and cautions, leading to a selective reading, so that Aristotle's works do not at first make their impact as a whole, as a systematic mode of thought challenging the dominant Augustinianism not only in respect of this or that particular dogma or philosophical or theological thesis but in respect of style of thought, of the structuring of enquiry, and of fundamental standards.
annotations.anchor_text: _selective reading, so that Aristotle's works do not at first make their impact as a whole_
annotations.interpretation: This is the most heavily annotated passage on the page, and Tom's underlining of the full sentence underscores that he recognizes this as the argumentative crux: the Church's strategy prevented Aristotle from being read as a rival system of enquiry. Tom is tracking MacIntyre's distinction between surface-level doctrinal conflict and deep structural rivalry — 'style of thought,' 'structuring of enquiry,' 'fundamental standards' are exactly the categories MacIntyre uses throughout the book to define what tradition-rivalry really means. Tom likely sees this as the key to understanding why the encyclopaedist tradition failed: it read texts without traditions.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is perhaps the most RC-relevant passage on the page. RC's entire project involves recovering not just particular moral conclusions but a 'style of thought' and 'structuring of enquiry' — Thomistic moral enquiry as a living tradition. MacIntyre's point here is that fragmentation occurs precisely when works are read in independence from each other and from their tradition, which is the condition RC identifies in contemporary moral discourse.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that even before 1240, the introduction of Aristotle's corpus into the Parisian curriculum was already destabilizing the Augustinian framework — not merely at the level of individual theses, but at the structural level of how enquiry itself was organized and how theology related to other disciplines. The deeper challenge was that incorporating Aristotle fully would have granted philosophy a relative independence from theology, transforming what had been a mere prologue to theological study into a self-sufficient mode of enquiry. This constituted a second-level challenge to Augustinianism: not just disagreement on particular questions, but a rival conception of the architecture of knowledge itself.

annotations.anchor_text: _even before 1240 it was beginning_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has circled a numeral '2' he wrote in the margin, likely as part of a sequential numbering scheme tracking distinct levels or phases of the Augustinian-Aristotelian conflict. This '2' appears to correspond to MacIntyre's claim that there was a 'second level' at which Augustinianism was challenged — not the first-order level of particular disputed theses, but the structural level of how enquiry itself is organized. Tom is mapping MacIntyre's layered argument about the nature of intellectual challenge.
annotations.rc_relevance: This maps directly onto RC's concern with tradition vs. reform — the distinction between surface-level disagreement and deep structural conflict over what counts as legitimate enquiry is central to MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism and to RC's diagnosis of contemporary social fragmentation.

annotations.content: SEE [something illegible, possibly a page number or abbreviation]
annotations.anchor_text: _established organization and structure of enquiry_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has written a cross-reference note — likely pointing to another page in the book or a related text — beside the passage about the established organization and structure of enquiry being put in question. This suggests he recognizes this as a pivotal concept that recurs elsewhere, possibly MacIntyre's earlier discussion of the 'encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition' tripartite or his analysis of institutional rationality. The illegible characters may be a page number or author abbreviation.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'structure of enquiry' being challenged is precisely the MacIntyrean concept RC draws on when arguing that modern moral fragmentation is not merely a matter of bad values but of a broken institutional and intellectual architecture for moral reasoning.

annotations.content: 2a
annotations.anchor_text: _level at which Augustinianism came to be challenged_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has marked '2a' — a sub-item within his numbering scheme — suggesting he is distinguishing between the primary second-level challenge (the structural/organizational conflict) and a specific sub-manifestation of it. The 'a' qualifier indicates Tom is being precise about MacIntyre's layering: the second-level challenge has its own internal distinctions, perhaps between the challenge to the curriculum's hierarchy and the challenge to theology's authority over philosophical questions.
annotations.rc_relevance: This granular annotation reflects RC's interest in subsidiarity and institutional hierarchy — Tom is tracking exactly how authority structures break down when a rival tradition enters an established curriculum, which is analogous to RC's analysis of how Enlightenment rationalism disrupted inherited moral communities.

annotations.content: even before 1240 it was beginning to become clear that it was the established organization and structure of enquiry that was being put in question
annotations.anchor_text: _even before 1240 it was beginning_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has underlined this as a thesis statement — MacIntyre's key claim that the Aristotelian challenge was not about particular doctrines but about the organizational structure of knowledge itself. The underline signals Tom's recognition that this is the hinge of MacIntyre's argument: the crisis is epistemological and institutional, not merely theological. This is the 'second level' his margin numbers are tracking.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is foundational for RC: the argument that intellectual and moral crises manifest first as institutional crises — the breakdown of the structures that make coherent enquiry possible — before they appear as doctrinal disputes.

annotations.content: the relationship of theology to the other disciplines within that organization and structure
annotations.anchor_text: _more particularly the relationship of theology_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this phrase to mark MacIntyre's specification of what exactly was being challenged: not theology as such, but the hierarchical relationship between theology and other disciplines. This is a crucial precision — the Aristotelian challenge was to theology's architectonic role, its position as the organizing science of the curriculum. Tom likely sees this as MacIntyre's most pointed institutional claim.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with the role of institutions in forming character depends on this hierarchical model — if theology loses its architectonic authority, the whole system of formative education is disrupted. This parallels RC's critique of how secular institutions have displaced virtue-forming ones.

annotations.content: This then was a second level at which Augustinianism came to be challenged, increasingly after 1240, by Aristotle and his interpreters
annotations.anchor_text: _This then was a second level_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's explicit naming of the 'second level' — confirming that his margin numbers (2, 2a) are tracking this exact structural distinction in MacIntyre's argument. The underline here functions as an anchor: this is the sentence that names what the preceding argument has been building toward. Tom is ensuring he can locate the precise moment MacIntyre formalizes the two-level structure of the challenge.
annotations.rc_relevance: The two-level structure — particular theses vs. organizational architecture — is crucial for RC's argument that modern moral disagreement operates at both levels simultaneously, and that recovering civility requires addressing the deeper structural level, not just adjudicating surface disputes.

annotations.content: not only would those studies either have been incorporated into or themselves understood as Aristotelian sciences, integrated into the hierarchical structure of those sciences, but in being so incorporated and understood they would have acquired a greater independence of theology
annotations.anchor_text: _would have acquired a greater independence of theology_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this long conditional to mark MacIntyre's explanation of why full incorporation of Aristotle was threatening: it would have granted the liberal arts a structural independence from theology, reversing the Augustinian hierarchy. The underline spans the whole logical structure of the claim, suggesting Tom is tracking the argument's internal logic rather than just flagging a key phrase. He may be thinking about analogous modern cases where disciplines claim independence from any overarching moral framework.
annotations.rc_relevance: This passage speaks directly to RC's concern with subsidiarity and the proper ordering of disciplines — the question of whether practical and ethical sciences can operate independently of a theological or metaphysical foundation is live in contemporary debates about secular civic education that RC engages.

annotations.content: They would have functioned less as mere prologue. Moreover questions which within the Augustinian curriculum had hitherto been part of the exclusive province of theology would have fallen within their competence, notably, a large range of questions in ethics, politics, psychology, and metaphysics.
annotations.anchor_text: _functioned less as mere prologue_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this to capture the concrete disciplinary consequence MacIntyre is identifying: ethics, politics, psychology, and metaphysics would have been liberated from theological jurisdiction. The phrase 'mere prologue' is particularly charged — it suggests that under Augustinianism, philosophy was instrumentalized, and Aristotle's arrival threatened to end that subordination. Tom may be connecting this to MacIntyre's broader critique of how modernity resolved this tension by simply expelling theology.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry depends on resisting exactly this expulsion — the claim that ethics and politics can be conducted without theological or metaphysical grounding is the Enlightenment move MacIntyre contests, and RC contests with him.

annotations.content: Such a violation of the Augustinian division of intellectual labor in the curriculum seemed to challenge not only the institutional arrangements of the Augustinianism of the University of Paris but the intellectual presuppositions of those arrangements. And hence there arose and continued the underlying Augus-
annotations.anchor_text: _violation of the Augustinian division of intellectual labor_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the closing sentence of the page, which introduces the phrase 'division of intellectual labor' — a sociological/institutional framing of what was at stake. The dual challenge — institutional AND intellectual presuppositions — is precisely MacIntyre's point that you cannot separate the social organization of enquiry from its epistemic content. Tom's underline here likely marks this as a concluding thesis he will track into the next page.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is a cornerstone RC claim: that intellectual fragmentation and institutional fragmentation are inseparable. The 'division of intellectual labor' concept anticipates MacIntyre's later argument about how modernity's fragmented curriculum produces fragmented moral agents — a central RC diagnosis.

annotations.content: 2b
annotations.anchor_text: _violation of the Augustinian division of intellectual labor_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has marked '2b' — a second sub-item under the 'second level' challenge — beside the passage about the violation of the Augustinian division of intellectual labor. This suggests Tom is distinguishing between 2a (the challenge to theology's hierarchical position relative to other disciplines) and 2b (the challenge to the institutional arrangements and intellectual presuppositions of the Augustinian curriculum as a whole). Tom is building a precise map of MacIntyre's layered argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: The distinction between 2a and 2b reflects RC's methodological concern with precision in diagnosing tradition-conflict: identifying whether a challenge is to authority structures (2a) or to foundational presuppositions (2b) determines what kind of response is adequate — reform or wholesale retrieval.

pages.summary: MacIntyre contrasts Aristotle and Augustine on three key issues: (1) the mind's capacity for knowledge — Aristotle sees the mind as self-sufficient in achieving knowledge, while Augustine requires divine illumination to compensate for an otherwise impotent mind, leading to the Cartesian problematic on the Augustinian path but not the Aristotelian; (2) the nature of truth — for Aristotle truth is located in the mind's relationship to objects, for Augustine it is located in God as the source of 'veritas,' making truth of statements tertiary; and (3) the nature of defect and error — for Augustine the will is the primary cause of error, the intellect being liable to perversion by a will uninformed by charity, so that Augustinian enquiry always suspects intellectual failure of being rooted in moral defect.
