annotations.content: rival forms of learning (modes of inquiry)
annotations.anchor_text: _ARISTOTLE AND/OR/AGAINST AUGUSTINE_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is glossing MacIntyre's central tripartite framing at the chapter heading level — 'rival forms of learning' maps onto the book's three rival versions (encyclopaedia, genealogy, tradition). By writing 'modes of inquiry' in parentheses he is connecting MacIntyre's terminology to a broader epistemological vocabulary, possibly Dewey's or Peirce's, suggesting Tom sees this chapter as being about how different epistemic communities constitute themselves through distinct inquiry practices.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly names the RC project's foundational tension: that contemporary social fragmentation arises partly because rival epistemic communities — secular liberal, Augustinian, Aristotelian-Thomistic — cannot agree on the very mode of inquiry by which disagreements should be adjudicated.

annotations.content: histories
annotations.anchor_text: _ARISTOTLE AND/OR/AGAINST AUGUSTINE_
annotations.interpretation: Tom isolates two structural features MacIntyre says every tradition must have: a history and institutional embodiment. The vertical listing suggests he is building a checklist or taxonomy of what constitutes a tradition, which he will later test against contemporary institutions. The pairing of 'histories' and 'institutions' implies Tom sees these as co-constitutive — institutions carry and transmit histories, and histories only persist through institutional form.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project's concern with subsidiarity and institution-formation is directly engaged here: if traditions require institutional embodiment, then the erosion of civic institutions is simultaneously the erosion of the moral traditions they carry. Tom is flagging the precondition for MacIntyre's entire argument.

annotations.content: modes of inquiry
annotations.anchor_text: _rival incompatible and apparently incommensurable_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles this phrase to mark it as a key technical term. By circling rather than underlining, he is likely signaling definitional importance — this is a term he wants to track across the text or cross-reference with other works. The phrase appears in the context of two traditions each developing their own mode of inquiry, which is the heart of the incommensurability problem.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project's engagement with MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism hinges on whether 'modes of inquiry' are genuinely incommensurable or whether there is a meta-rational standpoint — Tom circling this term suggests he is holding that question open.

annotations.content: Rival Ts
annotations.anchor_text: _two rival incompatible and apparently incommensurable traditions_
annotations.interpretation: Tom abbreviates 'Rival Traditions' as 'Rival Ts' — a shorthand he is likely using consistently across his reading of the book. The placement next to the text about two rival traditions developing their own modes of inquiry confirms this. It also suggests Tom is tracking the book's structural argument about which traditions are in contest in this chapter specifically.
annotations.rc_relevance: This shorthand is Tom's way of flagging the core RC tension: that modern public discourse involves not a unified civic tradition but multiple rival traditions whose conflict cannot be resolved by appeal to neutral reason. The 'Rival Ts' notation appears to be a recurring annotation key for the RC project.

annotations.content: two rival, incompatible and apparently incommensurable traditions, each with its own history and its own developing mode of enquiry and each requiring its own institutionalized embodiment in certain highly specific forms
annotations.anchor_text: _two rival incompatible and apparently incommensurable traditions_
annotations.interpretation: This is the most extensively underlined passage on the page, suggesting Tom identifies it as the chapter's thesis sentence or the most citable formulation of MacIntyre's position. The underline spans the full claim: rivalry + incommensurability + history + mode of inquiry + institutional embodiment. Tom is not just flagging a definition but marking what he takes to be MacIntyre's complete account of what a tradition is.
annotations.rc_relevance: This sentence is likely a foundational quotation for the RC project — it articulates why civility cannot be restored by procedural liberalism alone, since rival traditions require their own institutional forms and cannot be adjudicated from a neutral standpoint.

annotations.content: History
annotations.anchor_text: _Every tradition which embodies a distinctive conception_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is building a structural outline of MacIntyre's account of what constitutes a tradition: (1) History, (2) Characteristics + starting point, (3) Rationality of a Tradition. The abbreviated 'Char. + pt' likely means 'characteristics and starting point,' and 'rationality of Trad.' refers to MacIntyre's concept of tradition-constituted rationality. Tom is constructing a definitional schema he can apply elsewhere.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this schema is crucial: if traditions have a constitutive rationality, then the recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry is not merely antiquarian but is an attempt to re-activate a living tradition with its own rational standards. Tom's outline maps directly onto what the RC project is trying to recover.

annotations.content: rationality as a tradition
annotations.anchor_text: _certain characteristics relevant to its rationality as a tradition_
annotations.interpretation: Tom isolates this exact phrase, which is one of MacIntyre's most important technical contributions — the idea that rationality is not universal and context-free but is itself tradition-constituted. By underlining just this phrase among the surrounding sentence, Tom signals he recognizes it as the pivotal philosophical claim on this page.
annotations.rc_relevance: This phrase is the philosophical hinge of the RC project's engagement with MacIntyre: the claim that civility and civic friendship are not procedural virtues but tradition-embedded ones directly follows from the concept of 'rationality as a tradition.'

annotations.content: ① common
annotations.anchor_text: _by being challenged from some alternative point of view_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is numbering MacIntyre's three types of challenges that can destabilize a tradition: (1) external challenge from a rival point of view, (2) internal incoherence discovered within the tradition's own beliefs, and (3) resourcelessness before some critical theoretical problem — sometimes a combination. This numbered list mirrors exactly the text's 'sometimes... sometimes... sometimes by some combination.' Tom is structuring MacIntyre's argument for later retrieval and application.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, identifying these three challenge-types is practically important: contemporary moral fragmentation can be diagnosed as combinations of all three — external challenge from secular liberalism, internal incoherence in received Christian social teaching, and resourcelessness before problems like pluralism and technology. Tom's numbering sets up a diagnostic framework.

annotations.content: discovered resourcelessness in the face of some theoretical or practical problem
annotations.anchor_text: _discovered resourcelessness in the face of some theoretical_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this specific phrase — 'discovered resourcelessness' — which is MacIntyre's term for a tradition's internal crisis when it cannot answer problems generated by its own premises. This is more internal than external challenge and more structural than mere incoherence. Tom underlines it separately from the numbered margin note, suggesting he finds the precise terminology philosophically significant.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project must grapple with whether the Aristotelian-Thomistic tradition faces 'resourcelessness' before modern problems of pluralism, democratic legitimacy, and scientific naturalism — or whether it has underdeveloped resources. Tom marking this term suggests he is testing MacIntyre's confidence that the tradition is not in this condition.

annotations.content: From then on a tradition will move through stages
annotations.anchor_text: _From then on a tradition will move through stages_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the sentence that begins MacIntyre's account of how a tradition progresses after its formative crisis. The phrase 'From then on' marks a temporal pivot in the argument — before this point MacIntyre described what constitutes a tradition; after this he describes its developmental logic. Tom is tracking the argumentative structure.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this 'stages' model is important because it suggests that traditions can be at different points of development — the recovery of a tradition for civic purposes requires knowing what stage it is at and what its next rational move should be.

annotations.content: justification of the scheme of belief as a whole could be applied in terms of its rational superiority to the formulations of its predecessor
annotations.anchor_text: _justification of the scheme of belief as a whole_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the core criterion for rational progress within a tradition: not correspondence to mind-independent facts, but rational superiority to the tradition's own prior formulations. This is MacIntyre's alternative to both foundationalism and relativism. Tom is flagging this as the key epistemological move.
annotations.rc_relevance: This criterion is central to the RC project's argument that recovering Thomistic moral enquiry is a rational rather than merely sentimental or fideistic project — it can be shown to be rationally superior to its Enlightenment competitors on their own best terms, which is MacIntyre's central claim in the book.

annotations.content: the availability of this type of reference from the present to the past is not by itself sufficient to constitute a tradition of rational enquiry
annotations.anchor_text: _availability of this type of reference from present to past_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's crucial qualification: historical continuity alone does not make a tradition. There must also be directedness toward theoretical and practical goals. This is a guard against mere antiquarianism or nostalgia. Tom is flagging this as a constraint on what counts as a living tradition versus a dead one.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is a direct challenge to the RC project: recovering classical traditions of civility requires not just historical retrieval but showing that those traditions have ongoing directedness toward live problems. Tom marking this suggests he is aware of the anti-nostalgic demand MacIntyre places on tradition-recovery projects.

annotations.content: directedness emerge, so that theoretical and practical goals
annotations.anchor_text: _directedness emerge so that theoretical and practical goals_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the positive condition MacIntyre adds: a tradition must have directedness — an active orientation toward theoretical and practical goals — not just historical depth. The word 'emerge' is significant: MacIntyre does not say directedness is imposed but that it emerges from the tradition's internal dynamic.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this means that recovering civic friendship as a tradition-constituted virtue requires showing it has directedness — that it is answering live questions about social fragmentation, not merely commemorating past forms. Tom's underline here is a self-challenge as much as a textual note.

annotations.content: Ts constituted
annotations.anchor_text: _enquiry are formulated and at later stages reformulated_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is constructing a full schema of what MacIntyre says constitutes a tradition: it is constituted by (a) a history of stages, (b) a set of standards for identification and progress, (c) directedness toward theoretical and practical goals. The '(T+P)' abbreviates 'theoretical and practical,' and 'Tr Goals' synthesizes the whole. The downward arrow indicates a logical chain. This is Tom building a usable analytical framework from MacIntyre's argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: This schema is the RC project's operational definition of a 'living tradition' — it gives Tom the criteria by which to evaluate whether contemporary Thomistic, Augustinian, or civic humanist traditions are genuinely alive or merely antiquarian. The 'standards for ID and progress' criterion is especially relevant to institutional design in the RC project.

annotations.content: what are these 3 challenges to IT [i.e., the tradition]?
annotations.anchor_text: _which at each stage those participating in that particular_
annotations.interpretation: At the bottom of the page Tom shifts from reconstructing MacIntyre's general argument to applying it: he is asking what the three challenges are specifically for the tradition MacIntyre is defending (Thomistic/Aristotelian). His three candidates are (1) scientism and observer-dependency (likely referencing philosophy of science challenges), (2) something abbreviated as 'F/G in Tl/AC' — possibly 'Foucault/Genealogy in the Thomistic/Augustinian context,' or 'Foundationalism/Genealogy' — and (3) reordering and institutionalizing, which likely refers to the challenge of rebuilding moral communities. This is Tom's own analytical work, not a transcription of MacIntyre.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is one of the most RC-relevant annotations on the page: Tom is directly applying MacIntyre's three-challenge schema to the specific project of recovering Thomistic moral enquiry for contemporary civic life. Challenge 3 ('reordering, institutionalizing') maps directly onto the RC project's concern with subsidiarity and institution-formation as the practical task of tradition-recovery.

annotations.content: But the availability of this type of reference from the present to the past is not by itself sufficient to constitute a tradition of rational enquiry. It is necessary also that a certain continuity of directedness emerge
annotations.anchor_text: _availability of this type of reference from present to past_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets the full passage distinguishing mere historical continuity from genuine tradition — this is a longer structural bracket, not just a single phrase. Bracketing rather than underlining suggests Tom wants to cite or use this entire passage as a unit, perhaps as a block quotation for the RC project or for a paper. The bracket encompasses MacIntyre's most important anti-nostalgic argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: This passage is likely a key RC citation: it establishes that tradition-recovery is not nostalgia but requires demonstrating active rational directedness. Tom bracketing it suggests it will do argumentative work in the RC project's case for why recovering civic friendship is a rational, not merely sentimental, enterprise.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues on this page that the question of what constitutes the rationality of a tradition cannot be answered from a neutral standpoint — every evaluative framework is itself tradition-bound. He then makes a pivotal self-disclosure: he himself speaks as a partisan, and the academic neutrality claimed by the encyclopaedist is a fiction. Because one's presentation of any conflict — including the 13th-century clash between Augustinianism and Aristotelianism — is inescapably framed by one's own tradition's beliefs and purposes, rival traditions will generate rival histories of the same events.

annotations.content: Final incommensurability of tradition
annotations.anchor_text: _rationality of a tradition and how particular traditions_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is naming the logical endpoint of MacIntyre's opening move: if each tradition supplies its own criteria of rationality, there is a terminal incommensurability between traditions that cannot be dissolved by appeal to a neutral meta-standard. The word 'Final' suggests Tom sees this not merely as a methodological observation but as a substantive thesis about the limits of cross-tradition dialogue. He may be flagging this as the crux from which MacIntyre's whole argument about encyclopaedist vs. tradition-bearer follows.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly engages RC's core tension between tradition and reform: if traditions are finally incommensurable, what grounds the hope of recovering civic friendship or Thomistic moral enquiry across fractured contemporary communities? Tom needs this point to argue why the RC project cannot simply appeal to 'reason in general.'

annotations.content: And so from the standpoint of the Augustinian tradition in the thirteenth century and again from that of the contemporary Aristotelian, the view of what constituted truth and practical and theoretical rationality was in important ways, as we have already noticed, idiosyncratic to each tradition.
annotations.anchor_text: _view of what constituted truth and practical and theoretical rationality_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets this sentence as a crystalline summary of the incommensurability thesis — each tradition has its own account of truth and rationality that is 'idiosyncratic' to it. The bracket signals Tom wants to return to this formulation, likely because it is the most explicit statement of why cross-tradition adjudication is structurally problematic rather than merely practically difficult.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is foundational for RC's engagement with MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism: if rationality is tradition-constituted, then RC cannot appeal to a universal civic reason but must argue from within a recoverable tradition — exactly the Thomistic move MacIntyre recommends.

annotations.content: MacIntyre as Partisan; neutrality as fiction
annotations.anchor_text: _I too must have been and will be speaking as a partisan_
annotations.interpretation: Tom flags MacIntyre's famous self-disclosure as a structurally significant move. By naming himself partisan, MacIntyre performs the very thesis he is arguing — he cannot exempt himself from the tradition-boundedness of enquiry. Tom's phrasing 'neutrality as fiction' is close to MacIntyre's own language, suggesting Tom found this formulation worth preserving verbatim as a quotable claim. He may also be noting the rhetorical courage of the move: MacIntyre undermines his own apparent authority as author in order to validate the argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this annotation is crucial to the project's self-understanding: any RC researcher or practitioner who claims a neutral civic standpoint would be, on MacIntyre's terms, adopting the encyclopaedist fiction. RC must be explicitly tradition-located — a point Tom seems to be tracking carefully.

annotations.content: I too must have been and will be speaking as a partisan
annotations.anchor_text: _I too must have been and will be speaking as a partisan_
annotations.interpretation: The underlining of this phrase alongside the margin note reinforces that Tom treats this as one of the key sentences in the chapter — perhaps in the whole book. The first-person confession is philosophically loaded: it is MacIntyre refusing the encyclopaedist's god's-eye view and placing himself within the tradition-constituted framework he has been analyzing. Tom likely intends to cite or quote this line directly.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's methodological integrity depends on whether it can honestly locate itself within a tradition rather than claim neutral civic expertise; this underlined sentence is the warrant for that methodological stance.

annotations.content: The neutrality of the academic is itself a fiction of the encyclopaedist
annotations.anchor_text: _neutrality of the academic is itself a fiction of the encyclopaedist_
annotations.interpretation: This is one of MacIntyre's most polemical lines, and Tom underlines it separately from the partisan confession, suggesting he sees two distinct claims: (1) MacIntyre is partisan, and (2) academic neutrality as such is a genre-specific illusion of the encyclopaedist tradition. Tom may be distinguishing the personal admission from the structural critique of an entire mode of intellectual inquiry.
annotations.rc_relevance: This speaks directly to RC's engagement with contemporary academic discourse on civility and social cohesion, which often presents itself as tradition-neutral. MacIntyre — and by extension Tom — would diagnose that neutrality as itself a partisan position within Enlightenment encyclopaedism.

annotations.content: in recording the history of a conflict, in particular, how one recounts that history will depend upon what from one's own point of view one takes to have been the outcome
annotations.anchor_text: _recording the history of a conflict in particular how one recounts_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as MacIntyre's epistemological point about historiography: there is no tradition-neutral way to narrate a historical conflict because the very selection of what counts as the 'outcome' is tradition-determined. This has implications for how MacIntyre can write about the 13th-century conflict without simply begging the question in favor of Thomism. Tom may be flagging this as a potential vulnerability or as a sophisticated acknowledgment of the problem.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's own historical narrative — recovering traditions of civility and virtue — faces exactly this problem: which tradition's account of what was lost and what must be recovered does RC adopt? This annotation marks where Tom is wrestling with the historiographical stakes of tradition-based enquiry.

annotations.content: 2d — 1st competency is possible but still other
annotations.anchor_text: _able to provide a presentation of it of just the kind that one of its own adherents would give_
annotations.interpretation: Tom appears to be constructing a numbered schema — likely labeling a 'first' and 'second' order competency. The 'first' competency is the ability to understand and present a rival tradition as its own adherents would (MacIntyre's point about imaginative understanding). The 'second' is something additional — perhaps the capacity to evaluate or critique from within one's own tradition. 'But still other' likely means: even achieving first-order competency does not dissolve the otherness of the rival tradition; the gap remains. This is a subtle but important nuance Tom is drawing.
annotations.rc_relevance: This maps onto RC's theme of civic friendship across difference: genuine understanding of the other tradition is a necessary but not sufficient condition for reconciliation or productive dialogue. The 'still other' notation suggests Tom is resisting a naive hermeneutic optimism.

annotations.content: but still other
annotations.anchor_text: _rival views of the outcome will generate rival histories_
annotations.interpretation: This appears to be a second instance of the 'still other' notation, or a continuation of the thought from the margin note above, now anchored specifically to the claim about rival histories. Tom seems to be emphasizing that even sympathetic cross-tradition understanding cannot overcome the fundamental alterity that generates competing historiographies. The repetition suggests this is a thematic marker Tom is building across the page.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this is the hardest problem: if rival traditions generate rival histories, then the RC project's own historical recovery of civility traditions will itself be a tradition-specific narrative — which raises the question of whom it can persuade and on what terms.

annotations.content: 2d — 1st comp: difficult, possible, still other
annotations.anchor_text: _rival views of the outcome will generate rival histories_
annotations.interpretation: This bottom-margin note appears to be Tom synthesizing the schema he developed in the right margin into a compressed formula: cross-tradition competency (understanding the other as they understand themselves) is (1) difficult, (2) possible, but (3) leaves the other still other. This tripartite formula — difficult / possible / still other — may be Tom's own theoretical contribution, distilling MacIntyre's nuanced position into a usable RC framework. The notation style suggests it is meant as a working thesis statement.
annotations.rc_relevance: This three-part formula is directly actionable for RC: the project can claim cross-tradition dialogue is genuinely possible without claiming it dissolves difference — a position that avoids both relativist despair and naive universalism, and that maps onto the subsidiarity principle of honoring local tradition while seeking broader civic solidarity.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues on this page that what appeared to be incommensurable disagreements between Aristotelian and early modern scientific traditions — as diagnosed by Kuhn and Feyerabend — were actually resolved through the internal development of the scientific tradition itself. He challenges Feyerabend's claim that Galileo's victory was achieved through rhetorical manipulation rather than rational argument, contending that Feyerabend's assumptions about rational resources available across traditions are defective. The deeper point is that genuine reconciliation between rival traditions is possible, but only when understood from within a tradition that has the resources to represent and overcome its predecessor's failures.

annotations.content: 3 CT Tradins Aug GA AQ
annotations.anchor_text: _ARISTOTLE AND/OR AGAINST AUGUSTINE_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is labeling this page as belonging to the 'Three Rival Versions' (3 CT Tradins) framework, with 'Aug' for Augustine, 'GA' likely for Genealogy/Against, and 'AQ' circled for Aquinas — the three traditions MacIntyre identifies. The circle around 'AQ' suggests Aquinas is the focal tradition on this page, the one with resources to reconcile the others. Tom is using shorthand to track which of the three rival traditions is operative at each moment in the argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly maps to the RC tripartite structure (encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition) and signals Tom's tracking of how the Thomistic tradition is positioned as the reconciling framework — central to RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry.

annotations.content: how is reconciliation possible?
annotations.anchor_text: _how this type of reconciliation could even be possible_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is lifting MacIntyre's own implicit question and making it explicit as a philosophical problem. This is not mere summarizing — by framing it as a question, Tom is flagging that MacIntyre's answer will be non-obvious and contestable. He likely wants to stress-test whether MacIntyre's account of tradition-internal reconciliation is genuinely satisfying or whether it begs the question against Feyerabend.
annotations.rc_relevance: The question of how reconciliation is possible between rival moral traditions is foundational to RC's project — if traditions are truly incommensurable, the recovery of civility through Thomistic resources cannot work. Tom is marking the hinge on which the entire RC argument turns.

annotations.content: recognizing & rationally overcoming incommensurability
annotations.anchor_text: _recognized and rationally overcome within the context of_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is distilling MacIntyre's key move: that incommensurability is not an absolute barrier but something that can be both recognized and rationally transcended from within a sufficiently developed tradition. The phrase 'rationally overcoming' suggests Tom sees this as MacIntyre's answer to Feyerabend and wants to hold it as a distinct thesis. The ampersand construction suggests Tom sees these as two sequential steps, not one act.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the epistemological backbone of RC: the claim that traditions can recognize their own limitations and reform themselves rationally. Without this, the RC project's appeal to tradition as a resource for addressing fragmentation collapses into relativism.

annotations.content: early modern science
annotations.anchor_text: _how in the history of early modern science what have been_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights 'early modern science' as the specific historical case MacIntyre is using to test the general thesis about incommensurability and reconciliation. This is the evidential terrain — the Galileo/Newton/impetus theory debate — and Tom wants to keep it visible as a case study rather than letting the argument become purely abstract.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is concerned with recovery of pre-modern and early modern traditions of civility; the early modern period is the pivot where MacIntyre locates the break. Flagging this period marks where the genealogical and Thomistic traditions diverge from encyclopaedist modernity.

annotations.content: Kuhn
annotations.anchor_text: _by writers as different as Kuhn and Paul Feyerabend_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles Kuhn specifically, distinguishing him from Feyerabend who is not circled. This likely signals that Tom sees Kuhn and Feyerabend as playing different roles in MacIntyre's argument, or that Kuhn is the more important interlocutor at this moment. It may also flag a cross-reference to notes elsewhere in Tom's research on Kuhn's concept of paradigm incommensurability versus MacIntyre's tradition-based rationality.
annotations.rc_relevance: Kuhn's incommensurability thesis is a challenge RC must answer: if moral traditions are like scientific paradigms, cross-tradition civic friendship may be impossible. Tom's isolation of Kuhn suggests he is tracking this challenge specifically.

annotations.content: Sci: ↓
annotations.anchor_text: _development of the scientific tradition. From the standpoint_
annotations.interpretation: Tom abbreviates 'Science' with a downward arrow, likely indicating that the scientific tradition example is being used as a subordinate illustration — an analogy pointing down to a more general claim about tradition-rationality. The arrow may also indicate that the scientific case descends from (or feeds into) the broader philosophical argument above it. This is Tom tracking argumentative structure.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's argument is primarily about moral and civic traditions, not scientific ones. Tom's 'Sci: ↓' may flag that the scientific case is merely illustrative and should not be over-weighted when translating MacIntyre's argument into RC's domain of moral community.

annotations.content: how Δoft looks a rational to K. Feyerabs
annotations.anchor_text: _Feyerabend was able to argue with at least some appearance of plausibility_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is noting that what 'often looks rational' to Kuhn and Feyerabend is in fact being contested by MacIntyre as a misreading — the 'appearance of plausibility' in Feyerabend's account is precisely what MacIntyre is dismantling. The delta (Δ) likely abbreviates 'that' or 'change/difference,' and 'oft' means 'often.' Tom is flagging that the Feyerabendian picture of Galileo's rhetorical manipulation rests on a flawed criterion of rationality that MacIntyre will expose.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's engagement with MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism: the genealogist (Feyerabend/Nietzsche) mistakes tradition-embedded rationality for mere power, which is exactly the diagnosis RC wants to reverse when recovering civic tradition.

annotations.content: if adequate reasoning from a starting point within one system of thought and practice to conclusions within another, incommensurable
annotations.anchor_text: _adequate reasoning from a starting point within one system_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the conditional structure of MacIntyre's argument against Feyerabend: the 'if' clause sets up what Feyerabend assumes is impossible (reasoning across incommensurable systems), which MacIntyre will show is possible under certain conditions. Tom is marking the logical fulcrum of the page — the point where MacIntyre's alternative account of rational tradition-crossing is introduced against Feyerabend's impossibility claim.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the key epistemological claim for RC: if reasoning across traditions is possible, then civic dialogue between fragmented communities is not merely rhetorical but genuinely rational. Tom is marking the philosophical license for RC's entire cross-tradition recovery project.

annotations.content: able to the first, could have no resources but those provided within the first of these systems
annotations.anchor_text: _could have no resources but those provided within_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines Feyerabend's premise as MacIntyre reconstructs it: that a system of thought is resource-limited to its own internal standards. This is the claim MacIntyre will refute by showing how traditions can develop resources to represent and critique rival traditions. Tom is isolating the exact premise to be demolished.
annotations.rc_relevance: If traditions are truly self-enclosed resource systems, subsidiarity and civic friendship across different moral communities would be philosophically groundless. Tom marks this as the adversarial claim RC must defeat to justify cross-community moral dialogue.

annotations.content: Feyerabend would have been right to insist at least this, that Galileo's reasoning could not have been effective rational reasoning but only in some other nonrational rhetorical way
annotations.anchor_text: _Feyerabend would have been right to insist at least this_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's conditional concession to Feyerabend — 'would have been right IF his assumptions hold.' This is a dialectical move where MacIntyre grants Feyerabend's conclusion follows validly from his premises, before attacking the premises themselves. Tom is tracking this as a sign of MacIntyre's argumentative rigor and intellectual honesty, not weakness.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project depends on rejecting the reduction of tradition-crossing argument to rhetoric or power. Tom marks MacIntyre's conditional concession as the moment where the stakes are highest — if Feyerabend's premises stand, RC's recovery of civility is exposed as nostalgic rhetoric rather than rational recovery.

annotations.content: reasoning but only in some other nonrational rhetorical way
annotations.anchor_text: _nonrational rhetorical way of reasoning_
annotations.interpretation: A second, reinforcing underline on the phrase 'nonrational rhetorical way' suggests Tom found this specific formulation particularly important — possibly because it echoes debates in RC about civility as either genuine rational virtue or mere social performance. The doubling of the underline signals conceptual weight beyond the surrounding context.
annotations.rc_relevance: The distinction between rational argument and nonrational rhetoric is foundational to RC's claim that civility is a genuine moral practice, not merely social lubrication. MacIntyre's refusal to concede this distinction to Feyerabend is what makes the RC project philosophically defensible.

annotations.content: Feyerabend's assumptions about the rational resources which could be provided in such cases are defective in three ways
annotations.anchor_text: _Feyerabend's assumptions about the rational resources_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the thesis sentence that announces a structured tripartite refutation of Feyerabend. The phrase 'defective in three ways' signals that what follows (on subsequent pages) will be a systematic demolition. Tom is marking this as the argumentative roadmap for the next section, likely to be cross-referenced when reading those pages.
annotations.rc_relevance: MacIntyre's systematic critique of Feyerabend's assumptions is the philosophical foundation on which RC's recovery of tradition-based rationality stands. Tom flags this as the beginning of MacIntyre's positive constructive argument — exactly what RC needs to borrow.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that epistemological crises within a tradition can be resolved through 'imaginative occupation' of an alien standpoint — one imaginatively inhabits another tradition's point of view prospectively, then concludes retrospectively that the new scheme is superior. He illustrates this with Aquinas, who invited both Augustinians and Averroists to occupy his synthesized standpoint, which integrated and then corrected the defects of both Augustinianism and Aristotelianism as judged from within their own respective criteria. This achievement was possible precisely because the incommensurable differences between the traditions made mutual self-understanding from the rival point of view otherwise impossible.

annotations.content: 3a: time to look around for my own rec[ourse?]
annotations.anchor_text: _one fundamental standpoint to another incommensurable_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is labeling this section '3a' as part of a structured enumeration of the argument's moves — likely tracking MacIntyre's numbered conditions for tradition-transcendence. The phrase 'time to look around for my own rec[ourse]' suggests Tom is noting the moment in the argument where a tradition-member must actively seek alternatives, connecting this to his own research situation or a thesis he is building.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's interest in how traditions reform themselves under crisis — this is the moment of productive instability MacIntyre describes, which RC maps onto contemporary civic fragmentation.

annotations.content: 3b: poss[ibility] to view your prob[lem] imaginatively from another POV, 1st dialectically & then (and only on reasonable conversion) decisively
annotations.anchor_text: _possibility of learning to understand the other_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is glossing MacIntyre's second condition (3b) for resolving an epistemological crisis: the imaginative inhabiting of an alien standpoint must proceed in two stages — first dialectically (experimentally, without commitment) and then decisively (upon conversion). Tom's parenthetical 'and only on reasonable conversion' signals he is tracking MacIntyre's epistemological rigor and the non-arbitrary nature of tradition-switching.
annotations.rc_relevance: This two-stage logic — dialectical engagement then reasonable conversion — is central to RC's argument that civic renewal requires genuine imaginative encounter with rival traditions, not mere tolerance or superficial pluralism.

annotations.anchor_text: _First the onset of an epistemological crisis_
annotations.interpretation: A structural label in the left margin marking the beginning of MacIntyre's first condition for tradition-transcending epistemological resolution. Tom is using number-letter notation (3a, 3b) to track the sub-steps within what is likely MacIntyre's third major argumentative move in the chapter, suggesting Tom is outlining the book's logic for later retrieval.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's engagement with MacIntyre requires understanding the precise structure of his argument about tradition-rationality; Tom's outlining practice here signals he intends to deploy this structure in his own work.

annotations.content: A of 2[?] 19-L carpentry 3b.
annotations.anchor_text: _surable point of view from within imaginatively_
annotations.interpretation: This cryptic notation likely cross-references an earlier passage or another text — '19-L' may be a page or line reference, and 'carpentry' may be a metaphor Tom is tracking (possibly MacIntyre's own language about constructing standpoints, or a note to himself about a structural analogy). The '3b' label confirms this is the second sub-condition. This is one of the more opaque annotations and may be a personal shorthand.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'carpentry' metaphor, if Tom's own, may relate to RC's theme of institution-building as craft — the idea that traditions are constructed through practical reasoning, not abstract theorizing.

annotations.content: key distinction: imaginative engagement vs. intellectual occupation
annotations.anchor_text: _occupied intellectually can never be ruled out_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging what he reads as a terminological distinction MacIntyre is making — or one Tom is imposing on the text — between 'imaginative engagement' (a prospective, exploratory inhabiting of another standpoint) and 'intellectual occupation' (a more settled, committed dwelling within it). This distinction matters for the epistemology of conversion: one can imaginatively engage without yet being intellectually occupied.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this distinction is crucial: civic friendship and tradition-recovery require imaginative engagement with rival accounts of the good before intellectual conversion is possible — this maps onto the RC theme of subsidiarity and gradual formation of character.

annotations.content: possibility of learning to understand the other incommensurable point of view from within imaginatively
annotations.anchor_text: _ondly the possibility of learning to understand_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the core epistemological claim of the passage: that incommensurability does not preclude understanding, because imagination can bridge what logic cannot. This is a key anti-relativist move by MacIntyre that Tom clearly finds central.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is foundational to RC's response to contemporary fragmentation: incommensurable traditions can still achieve mutual intelligibility through imaginative occupation, which is a model for civic engagement across deep disagreement.

annotations.content: So one can be invited
annotations.anchor_text: _ture's point of view. So one can be invited_
annotations.interpretation: The highlight isolates the verb 'invited' — Tom recognizes that MacIntyre's language here is deliberate. The epistemological move is not coercive or purely rational; it is an invitation, which implies a relational, even rhetorical dimension to tradition-transcendence. This is underlined as well, suggesting double emphasis.
annotations.rc_relevance: The language of invitation resonates with RC's interest in civic friendship and the role of rhetoric and hospitality in forming moral community — conversion between traditions is not algorithmic but interpersonal.

annotations.content: So one can be invited
annotations.anchor_text: _ture's point of view. So one can be invited_
annotations.interpretation: Combined with the highlight, the underline marks 'invited' as a term Tom considers load-bearing. The invitation structure implies that the tradition-holder extends something — an offer, a demonstration — rather than merely asserting superiority. Tom may be tracking this against a contrast with the encyclopaedist's purely argumentative mode.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is concerned with how traditions make themselves accessible to outsiders — the 'invitation' model is directly relevant to RC's institutional and pedagogical proposals for recovering civility.

annotations.content: imaginative identification with a process of prospective reasoning to be justified retrospectively and to conclude to the superior adequacy of the new scheme
annotations.anchor_text: _tion with a process of prospective reasoning_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the full logical structure of MacIntyre's conversion epistemology: prospective imaginative identification → retrospective justification → conclusion of superior adequacy. This is the core of MacIntyre's non-relativist account of tradition-transcendence, and Tom is marking it as the argumentative payoff of the passage.
annotations.rc_relevance: This prospective-retrospective structure is directly relevant to RC's argument that tradition-recovery is not mere nostalgia but a rationally justifiable orientation — one reasons forward from within the tradition and justifies the path only retrospectively.

annotations.content: CONVERSION
annotations.anchor_text: _conclude to the superior adequacy of the new scheme_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes 'CONVERSION' in large letters at the bottom of the highlighting bracket around the prospective-retrospective reasoning passage. This is a conceptual label — Tom is naming what MacIntyre is describing. The capitalization signals this is a key term for Tom's own project, not merely a gloss.
annotations.rc_relevance: Conversion is a central category for RC: the project asks whether and how contemporary persons can be converted to richer traditions of civility and virtue. MacIntyre's epistemological account of conversion provides RC with a non-fideistic model.

annotations.content: It was a precisely similar invitation to both the Augustinians and the Averroists of his time which was issued by Aquinas
annotations.anchor_text: _It was a precisely similar invitation to both the Augustinians_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the application of the abstract argument to the historical case of Aquinas. The word 'precisely similar' is doing heavy lifting — MacIntyre is claiming that Aquinas's move is not unique to him but is the general pattern of tradition-transcendence. Tom tracks this as the confirmatory illustration of the theoretical claim above.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, Aquinas's synthesis is the paradigm case of tradition-renewal through integration rather than rejection — directly relevant to RC's Thomistic strand and its argument that moral enquiry requires a living tradition, not merely abstract principles.

annotations.content: both the achievements of Augustinianism and Aristotelianism had been integrated in such a way that what were, or should have been, recognized as the defects and limitations
annotations.anchor_text: _both the achievements of Augustinia ism and Aristotelianism had been integrated_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the integrative logic of Aquinas's synthesis — not a simple eclecticism but an integration that also exposes defects. The phrase 'or should have been' is notable: MacIntyre is claiming that the defects were in principle visible from within each tradition, but were not recognized. Tom tracks this because it is MacIntyre's answer to the charge that he is smuggling in an external standard.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's argument that civility traditions can be recovered requires this same logic: the defects of Enlightenment liberalism are in principle visible from within its own criteria, but require an alternative standpoint to be fully recognized.

annotations.content: defects and limitations of Aristotelianism as judged from an Aristotelian standpoint had both been first more adequately characterized and then corrected or transcended
annotations.anchor_text: _defects and limitations of Aristotelianism as judged_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the crucial qualifier: the defects are judged from within each tradition's own standpoint, not from an external Archimedean point. This is MacIntyre's defense against the charge of arbitrariness. Tom underlines it to preserve this anti-relativist nuance for his own use.
annotations.rc_relevance: This internal-criteria point is essential for RC: the recovery of civility cannot be argued from a neutral liberal standpoint but must be shown to satisfy the internal criteria of the traditions being addressed.

annotations.content: any standpoint which enables one in this way to judge between the claims of fundamentally different and rival traditions and standpoints rather than only to judge from within one
annotations.anchor_text: _Notice first that any standpoint which enables one_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's meta-criterion for a superior standpoint: it must enable judgment between traditions, not merely from within one. This is the definition of what Aquinas achieved. Tom marks it because it is the normative conclusion of the historical argument and the criterion RC must itself aspire to meet.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's project of 'resurrecting civility' implicitly claims to occupy such a standpoint — one that can judge between liberal, communitarian, and Thomistic accounts. This annotation marks the criterion RC must satisfy to be credible.

annotations.content: "new initiate" generalization or a gift of revolutionary insight.
annotations.anchor_text: _any standpoint which enables one in this way to judge_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is glossing the two ways one can come to occupy a tradition-transcending standpoint: either as a 'new initiate' (someone formed within the synthesizing tradition from the start) or through a 'gift of revolutionary insight' (the exceptional thinker like Aquinas who constructs the synthesis). The quotation marks around 'new initiate' suggest Tom is borrowing MacIntyre's own language. This tracks an important asymmetry in MacIntyre's account.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this distinction matters practically: if only 'revolutionary insight' can produce tradition-transcending synthesis, RC's institutional and pedagogical proposals face a problem — they aim to form 'new initiates,' not to produce Aquinases.

annotations.content: vs K's generalization or a gift of revolutionary insight
annotations.anchor_text: _any standpoint which enables one in this way_
annotations.interpretation: The 'vs K' likely refers to Kuhn (Thomas Kuhn), whose account of scientific revolutions also involves a kind of 'revolutionary insight' but in a different epistemological register. Tom is flagging a contrast or comparison between MacIntyre's account of tradition-transcendence and Kuhn's account of paradigm shifts — a standard philosophical comparison in this literature.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC must navigate the MacIntyre-Kuhn comparison: Kuhn's relativism about paradigms is precisely what MacIntyre resists, and RC's recovery project depends on MacIntyre's non-relativist alternative being defensible.

annotations.content: imaginative occupation of alt[ernative] pos[ition]
annotations.anchor_text: _any standpoint which enables one in this way to judge_
annotations.interpretation: Written in large letters at the bottom of the page, this is Tom's own conceptual synthesis of the page's argument — a shorthand label for the core idea. 'Imaginative occupation of alternative position' is Tom's preferred formulation of MacIntyre's epistemological proposal. The placement at the bottom suggests it is a concluding gloss on the whole page.
annotations.rc_relevance: This phrase — 'imaginative occupation of alternative position' — may become a key RC concept: the capacity for citizens and institutions to genuinely inhabit rival moral traditions as a precondition for civic renewal and reasoned disagreement.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that any scheme of belief and enquiry contains an ineliminable reference beyond itself — to truth and being as such — that cannot be reduced to warranted assertibility within that scheme. This requires a 'metaphysics of being,' of esse over and above what can be said about particular entia. He then shows how Aquinas, responding to this type of problem (avant la lettre, before Bachelard, Kuhn, Feyerabend, Davidson), began defining his distinctive position in De Ente et Essentia and De Veritate — works that function as philosophical dictionaries spelling out the analogical uses of 'true,' 'truth,' 'being,' and 'essence,' all anchored in God as primary analogate. The page culminates in the insight that to understand the analogical relationships one must reverse the generative causal order: beginning from the derivative (finite beings) to work back toward what is first in being and truth.

annotations.content: eg: obs dependence of measurement in Ms acct of TT?
annotations.anchor_text: _ineliminable reference beyond the criteria which_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is cross-referencing MacIntyre's point about the ineliminable reference beyond any scheme to a parallel question about observation-dependence of measurement in someone's account of 'TT' — likely 'tradition-transcendent' truth or possibly Thomas Kuhn's theory of truth. He is asking whether MacIntyre's Thomistic escape from scheme-relativism (positing esse beyond warranted assertibility) has a structural analogue or disanalogue in the philosophy of science debate about theory-laden observation. The abbreviation 'Ms acct' likely means MacIntyre's account, and 'TT' probably stands for tradition-transcendent truth, a key MacIntyrean term.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly engages MacIntyre's tripartite structure — specifically how the 'tradition' version of enquiry claims access to tradition-transcendent truth without collapsing into encyclopaedist foundationalism. For RC, the question is whether civility norms can be grounded in something beyond the schemes that generate them.

annotations.content: to set all possible schemes?
annotations.anchor_text: _ineliminable reference beyond the criteria_
annotations.interpretation: Tom appears to be pressing on the scope of MacIntyre's claim: does the metaphysics of esse function to 'set' or adjudicate between all possible schemes, or only between rival traditions that have already achieved a certain internal rationality? He may be questioning whether this move is as tradition-neutral as MacIntyre implies, or whether it smuggles in a particular (Thomistic) metaphysical framework as universally binding.
annotations.rc_relevance: This touches the RC tension between tradition-particularity and universal moral claims — a central problem for any recovery of Thomistic civic ethics in pluralist modernity.

annotations.content: Mind md P reel ain
annotations.anchor_text: _speak coherently and thus truly. Hence in judging_
annotations.interpretation: This is difficult to transcribe with certainty — 'md P' may be an abbreviation for 'and perhaps' or a name, and 'reel ain' may be 'reclaim.' Tom may be noting that 'mind' needs to be reclaimed or re-grounded in something beyond scheme-internal criteria, tracking MacIntyre's implicit Augustinian/Thomistic epistemology of intellect. Alternatively this could be a phonetic note or a cross-reference to another text. The interpretation should be held lightly.
annotations.rc_relevance: If 'reclaim' is the right reading, this connects to RC's recovery project — the idea that rationality itself must be recovered from Enlightenment distortion through return to a tradition that can ground it.

annotations.content: distinction esse vs essentia
annotations.anchor_text: _metaphysics of being, of esse, over and above_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging the scholastic distinction between esse (the act of existing) and essentia (what a thing is) as technically significant. This is the core Thomistic metaphysical move MacIntyre is invoking: Aquinas's De Ente et Essentia is precisely about this distinction. Tom recognizes this as the hinge of the argument — the reason Aquinas's account can reach beyond any particular conceptual scheme.
annotations.rc_relevance: The esse/essentia distinction is foundational to Thomistic natural law and moral theory; for RC, it grounds the claim that civic virtues and moral norms are not merely conventional but track real structures of being.

annotations.content: seems an invalid inference plurality of rival schemes conflict felbrize? seems possible
annotations.anchor_text: _two rival and incommensurable schemes of belief and enquiry_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is registering a logical objection: he suspects MacIntyre may be making an invalid inference from the premise that there are plural, rival, incommensurable schemes to some conclusion — perhaps that therefore a metaphysics of esse is required, or that tradition-transcendent truth is accessible. The word 'felbrize' is unclear — possibly a proper name, a neologism, or a mis-transcription; it may refer to a philosopher Tom is thinking of who makes a similar or counter-argument. 'Seems possible' may indicate Tom is not fully convinced either way.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism as applied to RC: if the inference from scheme-plurality to tradition-embedded rationality is invalid, the entire scaffolding for recovering Thomistic civic enquiry is weakened.

annotations.content: ineliminable reference beyond the criteria which provide the warrants for assertibility within that scheme
annotations.anchor_text: _there is always some ineliminable reference beyond_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this as the epistemological crux of the page: the claim that no scheme is self-sealing, that there is always a reference beyond internal criteria. This is MacIntyre's counter to both Rortyan anti-realism and Davidsonian coherentism. Tom likely sees this as the key premise from which the Thomistic metaphysics follows.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this 'ineliminable reference beyond' is analogous to the claim that civic friendship and moral community require grounding in goods that transcend any particular community's self-understanding — the anti-relativist core of the project.

annotations.content: a metaphysics of being, of esse, over and above what ever can be said about particular entia in the light of particular conceptual
annotations.anchor_text: _required, that is, a metaphysics of being_
annotations.interpretation: This is highlighted as the explicit conclusion MacIntyre draws from the ineliminability claim. Tom marks it because it is the moment MacIntyre commits to a realist, Thomistic metaphysics as the solution — not merely methodological but ontological. This is the move that distinguishes MacIntyre from post-liberal theologians who might stay at the level of narrative without making metaphysical commitments.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry depends on exactly this move — that civility and virtue are grounded in real structures of being, not merely in communal agreement or narrative tradition.

annotations.content: Kuhn
annotations.anchor_text: _was not responding to Bachelard, Kuhn, Feyerabend, and Davidson_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles Kuhn specifically among the four named philosophers of science/language. This likely signals that Kuhn is the most important interlocutor for Tom's purposes — possibly because Kuhn's incommensurability thesis is the most direct parallel to MacIntyre's account of rival traditions, and because the top-margin note about 'TT' (tradition-transcendent truth) and 'measurement' echoes Kuhn's concerns. Tom may be tracking Kuhn as a bridge figure between MacIntyre's argument and contemporary debates.
annotations.rc_relevance: Kuhn's paradigm-incommensurability is structurally analogous to the conflict between rival moral traditions in RC — the question is whether MacIntyre's Thomistic resolution is more successful than Kuhn's own (arguably relativist) resolution.

annotations.content: But he was responding to a type of problem which we now understand much better, thanks to those and kindred writers.
annotations.anchor_text: _responding to a type of problem which we now_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as a methodologically significant claim — MacIntyre is asserting a kind of retrospective intelligibility: Aquinas was solving a problem that we can now name better than he could. This is MacIntyre's characteristic move of using modern analytic categories to illuminate medieval thought without anachronism. Tom may be assessing whether this hermeneutic strategy is legitimate or whether it distorts Aquinas.
annotations.rc_relevance: This hermeneutic move is central to RC's project of retrieving classical traditions for contemporary use — the idea that tradition-embedded reasoning can be made intelligible across historical distance without losing its integrity.

annotations.content: each of these sets of uses is related analogically, but that there is a primary application of each and that it is to God
annotations.anchor_text: _underlying recognition not only that each of these sets_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the doctrine of analogical predication with God as primary analogate — this is the theological anchor of Aquinas's semantic and metaphysical project. Tom likely sees this as the point where MacIntyre's argument becomes most distinctively Thomistic and most contested for contemporary readers. The highlighting suggests Tom finds this both crucial and potentially the most difficult claim to defend in a pluralist context.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this is the deepest challenge: can a recovery of Thomistic civic ethics work without the theological grounding? Or does subsidiarity, civic friendship, and virtue ultimately require this analogical-theological foundation?

annotations.content: the derivative that we have to begin
annotations.anchor_text: _it is from the derivative that we have to begin_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this as a methodological principle — epistemological order runs opposite to ontological order. We must start from what is derivative (finite beings, our experience) to recover what is primary (God, esse). This 'ascending' epistemology is Thomistically orthodox but has significant implications for how moral enquiry proceeds in practice.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this principle justifies beginning from the concrete failures of contemporary civic life (fragmentation, incivility) and working backward to recover the goods that ground authentic civic community — a methodological starting point for the project.

annotations.content: we reverse and retrace the analogical order by which they were generated
annotations.anchor_text: _reverse and retrace the analogical order by which_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this reversal-of-order claim as structurally pivotal. The arrow Tom draws from this text down to his bottom-margin note confirms he sees this as the key methodological insight of the page. The reversal is not arbitrary — it follows the structure of analogy itself, where the primary term (God, esse) is known last by us but first in reality.
annotations.rc_relevance: This reversal structure is a model for RC's recovery project: civic virtues and institutions must be understood by retracing their generative logic, not merely by describing their current degraded states.

annotations.content: asterisk or star with arrow pointing to 'we reverse and retrace' line, connected by a curved arrow to the bottom margin note
annotations.anchor_text: _we reverse and retrace the analogical order_
annotations.interpretation: Tom draws a connecting arrow from the printed text about reversing the analogical order down to his bottom-margin formulation 'REVERSING CAUSAL ORDER in uncovery.' The arrow makes explicit that Tom is distilling this into a technical term or concept. The connection is deliberate and shows Tom is extracting a generalizable methodological principle from MacIntyre's specific claim about Aquinas.
annotations.rc_relevance: The arrow-and-star combination is Tom's way of flagging a concept he intends to carry forward into RC — 'reversing causal order in uncovery' as a recovery methodology applicable beyond Thomistic metaphysics.

annotations.content: + so ↑ from s Tferences to inter ate
annotations.anchor_text: _reverse and retrace the analogical order by which they were generated_
annotations.interpretation: This partially legible note appears to say something like 'and so upward from [surface/scheme] references to inter[pret/relate]' — Tom is glossing the reversal methodology as a movement from surface-level linguistic or scheme-internal references upward to something deeper. The 's Tferences' may be 'scheme-references' or 'surface references.' This is Tom tracking how the epistemological ascent works in practice.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this gloss suggests Tom is thinking about how civic enquiry must move from observable social phenomena (incivility, fragmentation) upward to the underlying moral and metaphysical structures — the practical application of MacIntyre's reversal principle.

annotations.content: REVERSING CAUSAL ORDER in (generative) uncovery
annotations.anchor_text: _we reverse and retrace the analogical order by which they were generated_
annotations.interpretation: This is Tom's most significant conceptual distillation on the page, written large at the bottom in block capitals. He coins or adopts the term 'uncovery' (as opposed to 'discovery') to describe what happens when we reverse the generative causal order — we are not finding something new but un-covering what was always already primary. The parenthetical '(generative)' modifies 'order,' clarifying that the causal order being reversed is the order of generation (from primary to derivative). This suggests Tom may be developing 'uncovery' as a technical term for the RC project.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is potentially a core RC methodological concept: the recovery of civility traditions is not discovery of novelty but 'uncovery' — a reversal of the process by which those traditions were obscured or degraded, retracing the generative logic of moral community.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues on this page that Aquinas achieved a genuine synthesis of Aristotelian and Augustinian frameworks by using each tradition's internal standards to correct the other's deficiencies, rather than merely harmonizing them. The Quinque Viae serve as the structural hinge: rejecting them would mean rejecting the shared conception of enquiry that both Aristotelians and Augustinians already presupposed. Retrospectively, Aquinas rescued both standpoints from imminent epistemological crises, showing that Aristotelian natural philosophy required Augustinian supernatural theology for its completion, and that Augustine's account of the human knower required Aristotle's rational framework as its prologue.

annotations.content: So finite beings are made intelligible, both as moved and as moving, and the structures through which they are made thus intelligible are those which in a variety of aspects relate all beings to their first cause as unmoved mover, itself not further determined or determinable by anything else.
annotations.anchor_text: _So finite beings are made intelligible both_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets the entire opening statement about the unmoved mover as the condition of intelligibility for finite beings. This is the Aristotelian cosmological claim that MacIntyre is about to show Aquinas integrates with Augustine. Tom is isolating the precise metaphysical claim — not just motion but intelligibility through relation to first cause — as the load-bearing premise for everything that follows on this page. The bracket suggests he wants to hold this passage as a unit for comparison or citation.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly maps onto RC's concern with the grounding of moral order in a teleological cosmos — without a first cause as end, the 'perfected end' toward which beings move collapses, and with it the entire framework of virtue ethics that RC seeks to recover.

annotations.content: structures of generated intelligibility revealed in 5 ways
annotations.anchor_text: _these structures which are analyzed and disclosed_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is glossing MacIntyre's point that the Quinque Viae are not merely proofs of God's existence but are the analytical structures through which the intelligibility of finite beings is disclosed — i.e., they are epistemological as much as theological. The phrase 'generated intelligibility' is Tom's own compression, suggesting he sees the 5 Ways as a generative grammar of understanding rather than a set of conclusions. This is a sophisticated philosophical reading that goes beyond the standard apologetic use of the Quinque Viae.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this reframes the 5 Ways as relevant to civil and moral enquiry, not just theology — they are the conditions under which a tradition-constituted rationality can make claims about the common good, directly relevant to MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism.

annotations.content: To reject the Quinque Viae would be to reject the conception of enquiry shared by both Aristotelians and Augustinians.
annotations.anchor_text: _To reject the Quinque Viae would be to reject_
annotations.interpretation: This is one of the most highlighted passages on the page, and rightly so — MacIntyre is making the strongest possible claim: that the 5 Ways are not optional theological extras but constitutive of the shared rational framework. Tom's highlighting signals he sees this as a thesis-level statement. It would be useful in RC contexts to show that rejecting classical metaphysics is not a neutral move but entails rejecting the conditions of coherent moral enquiry altogether.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the anti-Enlightenment thesis in concentrated form: the encyclopaedist project's rejection of teleological metaphysics is simultaneously a rejection of the common framework of enquiry, which is precisely MacIntyre's genealogical critique that RC must engage.

annotations.content: Aquinas integrated both rival schemes of concepts and beliefs in such a way as both to correct in each that which he took by its own standards could be shown to be defective or unsound and to remove from each, in a way justified by that correction, that which barred them from reconciliation.
annotations.anchor_text: _Aquinas integrated both rival schemes of concepts_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the full mechanism of Aquinas's synthesis: correction by internal standards, not external imposition. This is MacIntyre's key methodological point — traditions can be corrected without capitulating to a tradition-neutral standpoint. Tom likely flags this because it is the positive model of tradition-reform that RC needs: not nostalgia or mere retrieval, but immanent critique leading to reconciliation.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the RC model for how tradition and reform can coexist — the very tension that RC names as central. Aquinas's method here is the template for how classical virtue traditions might be brought into dialogue with modern institutional failures without either abandoning the tradition or ignoring the critique.

annotations.content: having rescued both standpoints from imminent, even if unrecognized, epistemological crises.
annotations.anchor_text: _having rescued both standpoints from imminent_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles this because 'epistemological crises' is a technical term in MacIntyre's broader corpus (from 'Epistemological Crises, Dramatic Narrative, and the Philosophy of Science') and its appearance here signals the structural parallel between how traditions survive crises and how Aquinas navigated this one. The word 'unrecognized' is especially important — neither side knew they were in crisis, which is MacIntyre's point about tradition-blindness. Tom circles it likely to flag for citation or to connect to the earlier MacIntyre essay.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's diagnosis of contemporary social fragmentation as an unrecognized epistemological crisis is directly prefigured here — modern liberal institutions may be in the same position as pre-Thomistic Aristotelians and Augustinians, unable to see the crisis from within.

annotations.content: Arist req Aug
annotations.anchor_text: _was not merely harmonized with an Augustinian supernatural theology but shown to require it_
annotations.interpretation: Tom abbreviates the directional dependency claim: Aristotelianism requires Augustinianism for its completion. This is the stronger-than-harmony thesis — not peaceful coexistence but structural necessity. Tom's shorthand suggests he is tracking the asymmetry and directionality of the relationship, which matters for RC because it means natural reason is not self-sufficient but points beyond itself.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this is crucial: civic friendship and natural virtue (Aristotelian goods) are not self-sustaining without a deeper account of human ends that only a theological tradition can provide — subsidiarity requires something above it to be subsidiarity toward.

annotations.content: Aug req Aristotle
annotations.anchor_text: _Augustine's account of the relationship of the human being as natural intelligence and agent_
annotations.interpretation: This is the reciprocal dependency note, paired with the one above. Tom is tracking that MacIntyre argues mutual necessity, not one-way dependence. Augustine's account of the human knower needs Aristotle's rational framework as its 'prologue.' Together with the 'Arist req Aug' note, Tom is building a structural diagram of mutual implication, which is more sophisticated than a simple synthesis narrative.
annotations.rc_relevance: This mutual dependency model is important for RC's account of institutions forming character: neither pure natural virtue ethics nor pure theological anthropology alone can ground the account of the moral community RC seeks to recover.

annotations.content: showing this req modes of inquiry combined from both...
annotations.anchor_text: _Part of the work involved in achieving this integrated account made use of the techniques of interpretation_
annotations.interpretation: Tom notes that Aquinas's synthetic achievement was not merely doctrinal but methodological — it required combining interpretive techniques (distinctiones, quaestiones from Augustine) with dialectical and demonstrative argument (from Aristotle). The word 'req' (required) continues Tom's tracking of necessity claims. He seems interested in the idea that the synthesis demanded a new mode of enquiry, not just new conclusions. The ellipsis suggests Tom was mid-thought or ran out of margin space.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this is the methodological lesson: recovering Thomistic moral enquiry is not just about retrieving conclusions but about practicing a combined mode of rational discourse — which has direct implications for how RC frames its own interdisciplinary methodology.

annotations.content: in the three major areas in which the Augustinian tradition had confronted its central problems Aquinas developed new positions by both interpretation and argumentative means.
annotations.anchor_text: _in the three major areas in which the Augustinian tradition_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the summary statement at the bottom of the page that introduces what will presumably be developed next. pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that within Aquinas's scheme, particular theses are justified dialectically or demonstratively, but the overall scheme itself is justified by its capacity to remedy defects, transcend limitations, and extend the reach of its tradition. The 'conception of truth' embedded in Thomism commits its adherents to engaging any alternative standpoint — including encyclopaedist and genealogist rivals — by rendering those standpoints intelligible from within Thomism better than they can render themselves, exposing their defects and limitations. This commitment to truth thus opens Thomism to radical self-judgment, making intellectual vulnerability the mark of worthwhile theorizing.

annotations.rc_relevance: This is central to RC's 'tradition vs. reform' theme — MacIntyre is showing that authentic tradition is not opposed to change but internally generates the criteria for legitimate change, which is the RC project's answer to the charge that recovering classical civility is merely nostalgic.

Tom wants to remember this as a bifurcated architecture.
annotations.rc_relevance: This distinction is critical for RC's engagement with MacIntyre's anti-encyclopaedist critique: the encyclopaedist demands that everything be justified by the same neutral standard, but MacIntyre shows that overall schemes require a different kind of justification — one grounded in historical performance and problem-solving capacity.

annotations.content: the overall scheme itself is, as I have already suggested, not to be justified in this way, but by its capacity to remedy the defects, transcend the limitations, and extend the reach of the tradition of which it is so far the best outcome

This is the passage that separates tradition-constituted rationality from both foundationalism and relativism.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the philosophical backbone for RC's argument that classical traditions of civility should be recovered — not because they are old, but because they have demonstrated superior capacity to remedy the defects of rival frameworks (liberal individualism, postmodern genealogy). annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this conception of truth is what distinguishes the Thomistic tradition from both encyclopaedist positivism and genealogical perspectivism — it is a conception that binds the community of enquirers together in shared obligation, which is itself a form of civic friendship.

annotations.content: Aquinas's dialectical synthesis will be able to render those standpoints intelligible in a way that cannot be achieved by their own adherents from their own point of view and to distinguish their defects and limitations from their insights and merits in such a way as to explain the occurrence of what they themselves would have to take to be their defects and limitations at points at which their own explanatory capacities are resourceless.

Tom is preserving this as a key passage likely for direct quotation or paraphrase in his own work. The passage describes a very specific dialectical move: not refutation but recontextualization — explaining why the rival fails on its own terms by showing its 'resourcelessness' at key explanatory moments.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the passage RC needs most: it models how a tradition-constituted enquiry can engage contemporary rivals (liberal individualism, identity politics, technocratic governance) without either capitulating to them or dismissing them — by showing where their own internal logic breaks down and why.
