This suggests Tom is extracting an action-oriented methodology from what is an abstract philosophical argument. He may be thinking about how to operationalize MacIntyre's framework for the RC project's practical engagements.
annotations.rc_relevance: This formula — march toward, adapt, overcome — could serve as RC's model for engaging institutions, movements, or ideologies that rival classical conceptions of civility. It embodies the spirit of subsidiarity (engage at the level of the problem) and civic friendship (genuine engagement before critique).

annotations.content: exhibits the kinds of intellectual vulnerability which is the mark of all worthwhile theorizing.
annotations.anchor_text: _exhibits the kinds of intellectual vulnerability which is the mark_
annotations.interpretation: Green highlight on the concluding evaluative claim of the paragraph. Tom is marking MacIntyre's normative criterion: intellectual vulnerability — openness to being wrong, to radical self-judgment — is what distinguishes genuine theorizing from ideology. This is both a virtue epistemology claim and a criterion for evaluating traditions. Tom may be flagging this as a standard the RC project itself must meet.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is crucial for RC's self-understanding: if the project of recovering classical civility is to be credible, it must itself exhibit intellectual vulnerability — acknowledging where the tradition has failed, where it has been complicit in exclusion, and where contemporary challenges genuinely expose its limitations.

The exclamation mark of 'any rival' suggests Tom finds the universality of this commitment striking or even provocative.
annotations.rc_relevance: This bottom-margin synthesis is Tom's working thesis statement drawn from this page: that truth-commitment in the classical tradition is not triumphalist but costly and open-ended. For RC, this models the kind of courageous civic engagement that recovers civility not as politeness but as intellectual and moral seriousness in the face of genuine disagreement.

pages.summary: On this page, MacIntyre is transitioning from his comparative critique of the encyclopaedist and genealogical traditions to a more precise articulation of what the Thomistic tradition requires by way of moral enquiry. He argues that the task now is to specify more carefully how both the form of moral enquiry and the form of the moral life are to be understood within the Thomistic scheme, and how that understanding relates — and has proven superior — to the rival claims of encyclopaedia and genealogy. This sets up the positive constructive phase of the book after the critical comparative work.

chapters.title: Aquinas and the Rationality of Tradition

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that mastering a craft requires learning two key distinctions: between what merely seems good to me and what really is good, and between what is good for me now given my limited education and what is good unqualifiedly. Crucially, these distinctions cannot be applied in a fixed, once-and-for-all manner because every craft has an ongoing, incomplete history that continually generates new demands on how distinctions are applied. Therefore, learning to make these distinctions adequately is itself a historical and progressive achievement — one must acquire a practical 'knowing how' that enables movement from past achievements toward deeper understanding.

annotations.content: seems to me good and what really is good
annotations.anchor_text: _activity or product merely seems to me good_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has highlighted the first of MacIntyre's two craft-distinctions, the gap between apparent and real good. The orange highlighting suggests this is a load-bearing phrase for Tom, not merely a point to note but a concept he is tracking across the argument. This distinction — between subjective seeming and objective reality — is the epistemological core of MacIntyre's anti-subjectivist moral theory, and Tom likely sees it as the foundation for critiquing liberal individualism's reduction of the good to personal preference.
annotations.rc_relevance: This distinction is central to RC's challenge to Enlightenment subjectivism: if civility and civic friendship are grounded only in what 'seems good to me,' they collapse. The RC project's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry depends on rehabilitating precisely this distinction between apparent and real goods.

annotations.content: D1
annotations.anchor_text: _seems to me good and what really is good_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is labeling this as the first of two distinctions ('D1') MacIntyre identifies as essential to craft-mastery. This is structural annotation — Tom is parsing MacIntyre's argument into enumerable components, likely because he plans to map these distinctions onto a broader framework or compare them to distinctions in other thinkers (e.g., Aristotle's phronesis, or Aquinas's treatment of practical reason). The labeling suggests Tom wants these distinctions retrievable as discrete argumentative units.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, identifying these two distinctions as separable units is crucial: D1 maps onto the question of moral realism (is there a real good beyond preference?), which underpins RC's critique of contemporary civility discourse that reduces civic norms to mere social convention.

annotations.content: always applied retrospectively
annotations.anchor_text: _a distinction always applied retrospectively as part_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the retrospective character of D1 — we only recognize what really was good by looking back at our mistakes. This is a philosophically significant claim about moral epistemology: the good is not fully knowable in advance but only through the accumulated experience of a tradition. Tom may be flagging this because it complicates any attempt to apply MacIntyre's framework prescriptively — you cannot simply 'know' the good and act on it; you learn it through practice and correction over time.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's theme of tradition vs. reform: the retrospective character of moral learning implies that tradition is not merely conservative inertia but a necessary epistemic resource. Reform without retrospective wisdom — i.e., Enlightenment-style rationalism — is blind to its own limitations.

annotations.content: what is good and best for me to do here and now given the limitations of my present state of education into the craft and what is good and best as such, unqualifiedly
annotations.anchor_text: _what is good and best for me to do here and now_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the second distinction ('D2'), between the situationally-constrained good and the unqualified good. This is a distinction between prudential and absolute goodness, or between developmental and perfective good. Tom's bracketing of this passage alongside the D2 label suggests he sees this as a structurally parallel but conceptually distinct move from D1 — where D1 is about realism vs. subjectivism, D2 is about developmental stage vs. perfected standard. This maps onto Aquinas's teleological framework of moral development toward an end.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's interest in subsidiarity is implicated here: 'what is good for me here and now' given my current formation is a subsidiarity-inflected claim — moral agents at different stages of formation require different kinds of guidance, and institutions must be calibrated to those stages. This also speaks to RC's concern with character formation through institutions.

annotations.content: D2
annotations.anchor_text: _what is good and best for me to do here and now_
annotations.interpretation: This confirms Tom's structural labeling scheme, marking the second of the two craft-distinctions. Together D1 and D2 form a paired analytical structure that Tom is evidently treating as a key argumentative unit in MacIntyre's account of practical rationality. The fact that Tom labels both suggests he may be building toward a synthesis or critique that requires holding both distinctions in view simultaneously — perhaps to argue that contemporary civility discourse fails on both counts.
annotations.rc_relevance: The D1/D2 pair structures the RC project's implicit epistemology: recovering civility requires both moral realism (D1) and a developmental account of formation (D2), neither of which is available within the encyclopaedist tradition MacIntyre critiques.

annotations.content: Every craft has a history and characteristically a history not yet completed
annotations.anchor_text: _Every craft has a history and characteristically_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights what is arguably the most important claim on this page: that craft-rationality is inherently historical and open-ended. This is MacIntyre's move against any attempt to freeze the standards of a craft (or moral enquiry) into a timeless system. The incompleteness of the history is not a deficiency but a structural feature — the tradition is alive precisely because it is unfinished. Tom is likely flagging this as the hinge between the account of craft-distinctions and MacIntyre's larger argument about tradition-constituted rationality.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the beating heart of RC's theoretical foundation: if moral enquiry is tradition-constituted and historically incomplete, then the RC project is itself a contribution to an ongoing tradition, not merely an antiquarian retrieval. It also directly addresses the 'tradition vs. reform' tension — reform is legitimate only within a living, historically-aware tradition.

annotations.content: apply two kinds of distinction
annotations.anchor_text: _To become adept in a craft one has to learn how to apply_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the structural announcement that there are two kinds of distinction to be learned — this is the topic sentence for the D1/D2 labeling scheme he develops in the margin. The underline here functions as an orienting mark, signaling that what follows is a list-like structure that requires careful parsing. Tom is likely ensuring he can locate the beginning of this argumentative sequence when cross-referencing.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, the two-distinction structure of craft-rationality provides a pedagogical template: civic formation (resurrecting civility) requires training people to make both D1 and D2 distinctions, which means institutions must be designed to cultivate both moral realism and developmental self-awareness.

annotations.content: distinctions are to be applied within some particular craft is rarely fixed once and for all
annotations.anchor_text: _But the way in which these distinctions are to be applied_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's anti-foundationalist claim that the application of distinctions is not fixed. This is a crucial anti-encyclopaedist move: there is no neutral, timeless algorithm for applying moral distinctions. Tom is tracking this because it distinguishes MacIntyre's traditionalism from mere rule-following conservatism — the tradition is rational precisely because it keeps the application of its own distinctions open to revision.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly engages RC's concern with MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism: the encyclopaedist project fails because it assumes that once you have the right distinctions, their application is fixed. RC's recovery of Thomistic enquiry must embrace the historically open character of moral application.

annotations.content: So learning how to make these distinctions adequately involves learning how to go on learning how to apply them
annotations.anchor_text: _So learning how to make these distinctions adequately involves_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this recursive, self-referential formulation — learning to make distinctions involves learning to keep learning how to apply them. This is MacIntyre at his most Aristotelian: practical wisdom is not a static possession but a dynamic, self-correcting capacity. Tom likely sees this as one of the most quotable and argument-clinching sentences on this page — the recursive structure mirrors the structure of tradition-constituted rationality itself.
annotations.rc_relevance: This sentence is directly applicable to RC's institutional theme: institutions that form character must themselves be structured to keep learning — they cannot be designed once and left static. It also speaks to civic friendship as an ongoing achievement rather than a fixed social contract.

annotations.content: if we are to understand the relevance of these two characterizations to Aquinas's conception of moral enquiry, it is first necessary to show how they relate to each other
annotations.anchor_text: _first necessary to show how they relate to each other_

The bracket suggests Tom recognizes this as a structural signpost in the argument — a promise MacIntyre is making about what the chapter will deliver. Tom may be checking later whether MacIntyre actually fulfills this promise.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, the connection between Aquinas as craft-philosopher and Aquinas as tradition-synthesizer is precisely the model RC seeks to emulate: recovering civility requires both a craft-based account of moral formation and a synthetic engagement with multiple intellectual traditions.

annotations.content: ①Re
annotations.anchor_text: _To become adept in a craft as we noticed earlier_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes '①Re' in the margin, likely meaning 'first reminder' or 'recall point 1' — signaling that this paragraph is returning to ('Re' = return/recall) a point made earlier in the book about craft. The circled '1' suggests this is the first of a series of such backward references Tom is tracking. This is a sophisticated cross-referencing notation: Tom is building a map of where MacIntyre's argument loops back on itself, which is essential for understanding the cumulative structure of the book.
annotations.rc_relevance: The recursive structure of MacIntyre's argument — returning to craft to now apply it to Aquinas — mirrors the RC project's own method of returning to classical sources not as antiquarianism but as live resources for contemporary enquiry.

annotations.content: traditions
annotations.anchor_text: _carried forward two hitherto independent traditions_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'traditions' in the opening paragraph where MacIntyre describes Aquinas as having carried forward two independent traditions (Aristotelianism and Augustinian Christianity) and merged them. The circle likely flags this as a key term — Tom is tracking how MacIntyre uses 'tradition' as both a descriptive and normative concept. The circle may also signal a tension Tom wants to return to: what does it mean to 'merge' traditions, and does this synthesis create a new tradition or subordinate one to another?
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's tripartite structure (encyclopaedist / genealogist / tradition): Aquinas is MacIntyre's exemplar of tradition-constituted rationality, and the merging of two traditions is the historical model for how living traditions grow. RC's own synthetic project — drawing on classical, early modern, and contemporary sources — implicitly follows this Thomistic model.

annotations.content: means
annotations.anchor_text: _differences in the means by which form is imposed upon matter_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'means' in the passage about how craft histories generate differences in materials, means, and conceptions of form. The circle on 'means' specifically — rather than 'materials' or 'conceptions' — may reflect Tom's interest in the instrumental dimension of craft: how the tools and methods of moral enquiry change over time. Alternatively, this may be a word-level flag noting that 'means' here carries Aristotelian hylomorphic freight (form imposed on matter), which has implications for how MacIntyre understands moral formation.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, the question of 'means' — what institutional and pedagogical means are available for forming civic character today — is urgent. If the means by which moral formation is achieved change historically, RC must identify what means are available in contemporary conditions of social fragmentation.

annotations.content: con-
annotations.anchor_text: _differences in the conceptions of the forms to be achieved_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles what appears to be the hyphenated line-break of 'conceptions' — this may be a mark noting the word 'conceptions' as a key term, or it may be a reading-continuity mark helping Tom track the sentence across the line break. Given the density of Tom's other annotations, this is more likely a genuine flagging of 'conceptions' as a philosophically loaded term: MacIntyre is saying that historical craft development changes not just tools and materials but the very conceptions of what the craft is for. This is a strong anti-essentialist claim about craft identity.
annotations.rc_relevance: If the 'conceptions of the forms to be achieved' change historically, then what civility means — what it looks like as an achieved form — is also historically variable. RC must navigate this tension: is there a trans-historical conception of civility, or only historically-situated ones?

annotations.content: m
annotations.anchor_text: _differences in the materials differences in the means differences in the conceptions_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes three descending letters — 'm', 'm', 'C' — in the right margin alongside MacIntyre's three-item list of what changes during a craft's history: materials, means, conceptions. This is a structural labeling of a tripartite list, with 'm' for 'materials', 'm' for 'means', and 'C' for 'conceptions' (capitalized perhaps because 'conceptions' is the most philosophically weighty of the three). Tom is again parsing MacIntyre's argument into discrete, recoverable components — a habit evident throughout this page's annotations.
annotations.rc_relevance: The materials/means/conceptions triad maps onto RC's concern with institutional design: resurrecting civility requires attending to all three levels — the social materials (people, communities), the means (institutions, practices), and the conceptions (what civility actually means and aims at). Tom may be building this triad into his RC framework explicitly.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that craft-traditions require the embodied transmission of a capacity to recognize, in the past, what guides the future — and that to be initiated into a craft is to be initiated into a tradition in good order. He then extends this to moral philosophy: the lives of craftspersons are themselves the subject matter of moral enquiry, and both the philosophical theorist (asking 'what is the good for human beings?') and the individual agent (asking 'what is my good?') are mutually dependent — no purely theoretical answer can be given that is not translatable into practical answers, and no practical answer can be given that does not presuppose some philosophical framework. Finally, MacIntyre introduces Aquinas's account of connatural moral knowledge — virtuous practice itself yields a form of knowing distinct from theoretical knowledge.

annotations.content: ① craft ② tradition
annotations.anchor_text: _possession and transmission of this kind_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is numbering the two central concepts MacIntyre links in the opening passage: craft and tradition. By separating them with numbered labels, he is tracking the logical structure of MacIntyre's equation — that craft-knowledge and tradition-knowledge are not merely analogous but constitutively interdependent. This suggests Tom wants to hold them as distinct terms that must then be reunited, perhaps to test whether MacIntyre's argument is circular.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly maps to RC's core tension between tradition as living inheritance versus tradition as mere conservatism. The numbered separation signals Tom is watching for how MacIntyre defines 'tradition in good order' as a normative, not merely descriptive, concept.

annotations.content: recognize in the past what is and what is not a guide to the future which is at the core of any adequately embodied tradition. A craft in good order has to be embodied in a tradition in good order. And to be adequately initiated into a craft is to be adequately initiated into a tradition.
annotations.anchor_text: _recognize in the past what is and what is not_
annotations.interpretation: This is the most heavily marked passage on the page — the orange highlight signals Tom treats this as a foundational definitional claim. MacIntyre is arguing that practical wisdom within a craft requires a retrospective, discriminating gaze: recognizing which past elements remain action-guiding. Tom likely sees this as the hinge-point between MacIntyre's epistemology of tradition and his moral ontology — you cannot know the good without being inside a living tradition that has already done the discriminating work.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the engine of RC's argument against Enlightenment rationalism: civility and virtue cannot be reconstructed from scratch by autonomous reason; they require initiation into a tradition that already embodies practical wisdom. The phrase 'adequately embodied tradition' is a key RC diagnostic term for evaluating institutional health.

annotations.content: Angled bracket / less-than sign pointing right, labeled 'oRa A.'
annotations.anchor_text: _possession and transmission of this kind of ability_
annotations.interpretation: The bracket with 'oRa A.' appears to be Tom's structural label for the first of two sub-arguments on the page. 'oRa' likely abbreviates a recurring RC analytical category — possibly 'order of rationality' or a personal shorthand. The 'A.' indicates Tom is tracking a multi-part argument, expecting a 'B.' to follow. This is Tom doing architecture-mapping of MacIntyre's text rather than content annotation.
annotations.rc_relevance: Structural mapping of this kind suggests Tom is building an outline of MacIntyre's argument about tradition-constituted rationality to deploy in RC's own theoretical framework — distinguishing the different rational orders at work in craft, tradition, and moral enquiry.

annotations.content: Label 'oRa B' with similar bracket structure
annotations.anchor_text: _someone who has been initiated into a craft_
annotations.interpretation: This is the paired structural marker to 'oRa A.' above. The 'B' section begins with the argument about how initiation into a craft makes one part of a history, so that one's actions as a craftsperson are intelligible only within that history. Tom is marking that MacIntyre has shifted from the epistemological definition of tradition (A) to the ontological consequence for the agent (B): your identity and intelligibility are constituted by the tradition you inhabit.
annotations.rc_relevance: This maps directly to RC's concern with how institutions form character — the 'B' argument is MacIntyre's account of how belonging to a practice-tradition is not optional for moral identity, which grounds RC's subsidiarity thesis about the necessity of local, embodied communities.

annotations.content: it is in terms of that history that their actions qua craftsperson will be intelligible or otherwise
annotations.anchor_text: _actions qua craftsperson will be intelligible or otherwise_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the intelligibility criterion — the claim that actions become meaningful only within a historical narrative of a practice. This is MacIntyre's Aristotelian-Thomistic answer to the Enlightenment's demand for context-independent rational justification. Tom is likely flagging this as a key move against the encyclopaedist position: intelligibility is not universal but tradition-indexed.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's critique of contemporary social fragmentation depends precisely on this: when shared traditions collapse, actions lose intelligibility and civic friendship becomes impossible. This underline marks the theoretical foundation for that diagnosis.

annotations.content: no one who engages in a craft is only a craftsperson
annotations.anchor_text: _no one who engages in a craft is only a craftsperson_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's move to multiple overlapping histories — the craftsperson is also a family member, community member, etc. This is an anti-reductionist claim: moral identity cannot be collapsed into a single role or practice. Tom may be noting this as evidence against liberal individualism's tendency to define persons by a single social function or autonomous will.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is foundational to RC's account of civic friendship and subsidiarity: persons inhabit nested communities simultaneously, and civility operates at the intersection of these overlapping memberships, not in a single sovereign sphere.

annotations.content: the actions of someone who engages in a craft are at the point of intersection of two or more histories, two or more enacted dramatic narratives.
annotations.anchor_text: _point of intersection of two or more histories_
annotations.interpretation: The underline of 'intersection of two or more histories' is Tom tracking MacIntyre's narrative ontology — that a self is constituted by the overlap of multiple enacted stories, not by a single autobiography. The phrase 'enacted dramatic narratives' is MacIntyre's signature language from 'After Virtue,' and Tom is clearly alive to its reappearance here in the context of craft-traditions.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's account of civility as a civic virtue depends on this: civility is the practice by which persons navigate their multiple narrative memberships in public — it is not a single-tradition virtue but an inter-traditional one, which is why its recovery requires understanding tradition-constituted rationality.

annotations.content: eg. family, mestaksts [possibly 'mistakes' or a name], or physics + catholicism
annotations.anchor_text: _we come to the practice of a craft with a history qua family member_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is glossing MacIntyre's abstract point with concrete examples of overlapping histories: family membership, another category (possibly 'contexts' or a proper name — hard to read), and the combination of a scientific discipline (physics) with a religious tradition (Catholicism). The physics+Catholicism pairing is particularly interesting — Tom may be thinking of a specific intellectual biography (perhaps his own, or a figure like Stanley Jaki) where these two traditions intersect in a person's moral identity.
annotations.rc_relevance: This annotation shows Tom thinking about RC's audience and exemplars — the project likely needs to demonstrate how persons in modern secular-professional contexts still inhabit multiple traditions, and the physics+Catholicism pairing suggests Tom is thinking about how scientific and religious traditions can be co-constitutive rather than competing.

annotations.content: philosophical theorist / philosophical person bracket
annotations.anchor_text: _philosophical theorist has to enquire: What is the good specific to human beings? Each individual has to enquire: What is my good_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets the distinction between the philosophical theorist's question ('what is the good for human beings?') and the individual agent's question ('what is my good?'). The curly brace with 'phle[sophical] pers[on]?' suggests Tom is questioning whether MacIntyre is drawing a clean distinction or whether the two questions ultimately collapse — can any individual ask 'what is my good?' without already being a philosophical theorist of some kind?
annotations.rc_relevance: This tension is central to RC: the project must speak to ordinary agents, not just philosophers, but MacIntyre's argument implies that practical wisdom already contains implicit philosophical commitments. RC's challenge is to recover civility for non-specialist citizens who are nonetheless implicitly tradition-constituted reasoners.

annotations.content: phle[sophical] pers[on]?
annotations.anchor_text: _Each individual has to enquire: What is my good as a human being_
annotations.interpretation: This is Tom's shorthand query — possibly 'philosophical person?' — written as a genuine question mark, suggesting uncertainty or skepticism about MacIntyre's category. Tom may be probing whether the 'individual' MacIntyre describes who asks 'what is my good?' is already implicitly a philosophical person, which would make MacIntyre's distinction between theorist and ordinary agent less stable than it appears.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC needs to address whether its target audience — citizens experiencing social fragmentation — can engage in tradition-constituted moral enquiry without explicit philosophical training. This annotation marks Tom's awareness of that accessibility problem.

annotations.content: no true answers can be given to their practical questions by ordinary human individuals, no true answers can be given to their questions by such individuals which do not presuppose some particular type of answer to the philosopher's question.
annotations.anchor_text: _no true answers can be given to their practical questions_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's mutual-dependence claim: practical and theoretical questions are co-constitutive. No purely practical answer stands without philosophical presuppositions, and no purely theoretical answer stands without practical translation. This is MacIntyre's argument against both pure theoreticism (encyclopaedist) and pure pragmatism (genealogist). Tom likely sees this as the core epistemological warrant for RC's integrative approach.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the anti-fragmentation argument at the epistemological level: civility cannot be recovered by practical programs alone (policy, etiquette) or by theory alone (academic philosophy) — it requires the integration MacIntyre describes, which is precisely what RC attempts.

annotations.content: There is no form of philosophical enquiry — at least as envisaged from an Aristotelian, Augustinian, or Thomistic point of view — which is not practical in its implications, just as there is no practical enquiry which is not philosophical in its presuppositions.
annotations.anchor_text: _no form of philosophical enquiry which is not practical in its implications_
annotations.interpretation: This is one of MacIntyre's most programmatic sentences in the book, and Tom underlines the whole of it. The tripartite 'Aristotelian, Augustinian, or Thomistic' invokes the tradition-stream MacIntyre is defending against encyclopaedist and genealogist rivals. Tom is marking this as a thesis statement — the collapse of the theory/practice distinction within tradition-constituted enquiry — that will anchor multiple RC arguments.
annotations.rc_relevance: This sentence is potentially a direct epigraph or anchor for RC's methodology: the project's integration of historical scholarship, philosophical argument, and practical civic proposals is justified precisely by this Thomistic refusal to separate theory from practice.

annotations.content: T, Pare inspectte [possibly 'T, Pare inseparable' or 'T/P are inseparable']
annotations.anchor_text: _no form of philosophical enquiry which is not practical in its implications_
annotations.interpretation: Tom abbreviates 'Theory' and 'Practice' (T, P) and notes they are 'inseparable' — a direct gloss on MacIntyre's sentence above. The slight illegibility ('inspectte' vs 'inseparable') suggests rapid writing. This is Tom crystallizing MacIntyre's point into an RC working principle. The abbreviation style matches his 'oRa' notation above — Tom has a personal shorthand system for tracking key structural claims.
annotations.rc_relevance: The T/P inseparability principle is foundational to RC's rejection of both pure academic theorizing and pure activist practice as responses to social fragmentation — it grounds the project's hybrid methodology.

annotations.content: experiencial K [possibly 'experiential K' = experiential knowledge]
annotations.anchor_text: _practice of the virtues yields knowledge the experience of having one's will directed by the virtues_
annotations.interpretation: Tom abbreviates 'experiential knowledge' as 'experiencial K' — glossing MacIntyre's introduction of Aquinas's connaturality doctrine. The 'K' for knowledge is consistent with Tom's shorthand. He is flagging that Aquinas posits a third form of moral knowing beyond theory and practice — a knowledge that comes from having one's will ordered by virtue, which is pre-theoretical yet genuinely epistemic.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is crucial for RC's account of how civility is learned: if connaturality is a real form of moral knowledge, then civic virtue can be transmitted through habituation and community participation without requiring explicit philosophical education — which makes RC's proposals for institutional and cultural renewal epistemologically coherent.

annotations.content: practice of the virtues yields knowledge the experience of having one's will directed by the virtues
annotations.anchor_text: _practice of the virtues yields knowledge_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the connaturality claim — that virtuous practice is itself cognitively productive, yielding knowledge unavailable to pure theoretical reflection. This is Aquinas's answer to the Kantian problem of moral motivation: you don't need to derive duty from reason alone because virtuous habituation already gives you direct moral knowledge. Tom marks this as a distinct epistemic category.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's argument for the necessity of institutions and communities in forming civic character depends on this: connaturality means that civility as moral knowledge cannot be taught only through argument — it must be embodied through practice in actual communities, which grounds RC's focus on subsidiarity and local institutions.

annotations.content: connaturality
annotations.anchor_text: _what Aquinas calls connaturality educated into that practice_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'connaturality' — MacIntyre's translation of Aquinas's technical term for the knowledge that comes from having one's affective and volitional nature ordered by virtue. The circle marks this as a key term to track, not merely a passage to remember. Tom is likely flagging this for the RC glossary or as a concept requiring elaboration in his own writing.
annotations.rc_relevance: Connaturality is the Thomistic bridge between RC's institutional and philosophical arguments: it explains how participation in civic traditions and communities generates genuine moral knowledge, not merely behavioral conformity — which is why the recovery of such traditions matters epistemically, not just sociologically.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues on this page that initiation into the moral life is simultaneously initiation into the complex history of moral enquiry — the two are inseparable. Drawing on Aquinas's account of self-education (from the Quaestiones Disputate De Veritate), he contends that all genuine teaching mirrors the order of the learner's own discovery, and that the Summa Theologiae enacts this by leading the reader through the narrative history of enquiry up to its highest achieved point. The Summa thus sets out a framework of universal moral concepts not as static doctrine but as a tradition to be rationally inhabited and enacted by individuals formed within it.

annotations.content: initiation into a transformative tradition
annotations.anchor_text: _AQUINAS AND THE RATIONALITY OF TRADITION_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has written a synthetic title or heading at the top of the page — this is an interpretive gloss on the chapter's central claim, not a transcription of MacIntyre's language. The phrase 'transformative tradition' signals that Tom is reading initiation here not as passive socialization but as a process that changes the person entering it. This is a conceptually loaded move: it distinguishes MacIntyre's Thomistic account from mere indoctrination or conservative transmission, emphasizing that the tradition itself is alive and reforming.
annotations.rc_relevance: This note goes to the heart of the RC project's tension between tradition and reform: Tom is flagging that for MacIntyre, a living tradition transforms its initiates while being transformed through their enquiry — a model RC needs to distinguish from both nostalgic conservatism and Enlightenment progressivism.

annotations.content: the moral life from time to time inescapably raises theoretical questions
annotations.anchor_text: _moral life from time to time inescapably raises_
annotations.interpretation: Highlighting this clause suggests Tom sees it as a key methodological premise: ordinary moral living is not philosophically innocent. The claim resists both the encyclopaedist's separation of theory from practice and the genealogist's reduction of moral claims to power. Tom is likely noting that the need for philosophical reflection arises from within the tradition, not from an external Archimedean standpoint.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly supports RC's critique of purely procedural or liberal accounts of civic life — the argument that civility and moral community inherently raise substantive philosophical questions that cannot be bracketed in the public square.

annotations.content: So theoretical and practical enquiry are intertwined
annotations.anchor_text: _So theoretical and practical enquiry are intertwined_
annotations.interpretation: The underlining of this sentence, positioned as a conclusion marker ('So'), suggests Tom is tracking MacIntyre's structural argument: this is a thesis statement emerging from the prior analysis of the practical intellect. Tom may be flagging it as a direct counter to Kantian or Humean separations of theoretical from practical reason, and as the premise that grounds the subsequent account of initiation.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry depends precisely on this intertwining — civility as a practice cannot be understood without its theoretical substrate, and moral theory has no life apart from the practices of actual communities.

annotations.content: The history of the moral life and the history of moral enquiry are aspects of a single, albeit complex, history
annotations.anchor_text: _history of moral enquiry are aspects of a single_
annotations.interpretation: This is one of the most heavily marked passages on the page, and rightly so — it is MacIntyre's most compressed statement of his historiographical thesis. Tom is identifying this as a load-bearing claim: that you cannot separate the narrative of how people have lived from the narrative of how they have thought about living. This has implications for how RC frames its own historical recovery work.
annotations.rc_relevance: This claim is foundational for RC's methodology: recovering civility cannot be done by extracting timeless principles from classical texts but requires attending to the full historical entanglement of moral practice and moral philosophy in specific communities.

annotations.content: to be initiated into the moral life is to be initiated into the tradition whose history is that complex history
annotations.anchor_text: _initiated into the moral life is to be initiated_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has highlighted the definitional equivalence MacIntyre draws here. The repetition of 'initiated into' is deliberate — MacIntyre is collapsing the distinction between moral formation and historical-intellectual initiation. Tom is likely tracking this as the answer to the structural question MacIntyre has been building toward about what tradition-constituted rationality actually requires of its members.
annotations.rc_relevance: This speaks directly to RC's concern with institutional formation: joining a civic community is not merely signing a social contract but being initiated into a living moral-historical tradition, with all the obligations of enquiry that entails.

annotations.content: learning who
annotations.anchor_text: _to be initiated into the moral life_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is generating concrete exemplars of initiation — figures who embody the kind of moral-historical formation MacIntyre is describing. Lincoln and St. Francis are suggestive: they represent different traditions (civic-republican American and Franciscan Catholic), both involving personal transformation through engagement with a complex moral history. The repetition of 'St Francis' may indicate Tom writing while thinking, or emphasizing that Francis is a particularly apt case of someone whose moral life was inseparable from a transformative tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is concerned with how moral exemplars function within traditions to transmit and renovate civic and spiritual life — Tom's list suggests he is thinking about how RC might use biographical narrative to illustrate MacIntyre's abstract account of initiation.

annotations.content: Initiation
annotations.anchor_text: _How is that initiation to be achieved_
annotations.interpretation: This is a partial, possibly interrupted note — 'lobe' is likely a truncation or mishearing of 'life' or possibly a phonetic shorthand. Tom seems to be restating MacIntyre's question in his own terms as he reads. The dash suggests an incomplete thought, possibly continuing on the next line or abandoned. It may also be a cue to himself to return to this question with further annotation.
annotations.rc_relevance: The question 'how is initiation to be achieved' is the operational question for RC: not just what tradition-constituted moral enquiry is in theory, but how institutions, teachers, and communities actually produce it in practice.

annotations.content: long as they inhabit a tolerably well-ordered form of social life
annotations.anchor_text: _For most persons at least so long as they inhabit_
annotations.interpretation: The highlighting of this conditional clause is significant — Tom is noting that MacIntyre's entire account of practical moral education depends on a prior social condition: a tolerably well-ordered community. This is an assumption that MacIntyre does not fully argue for on this page, and Tom may be flagging it as a vulnerability or as a premise that RC must either defend or problematize, given that RC addresses precisely contexts where social order has fragmented.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is one of the most RC-relevant marks on the page: if moral initiation requires a tolerably well-ordered social life, then social fragmentation — RC's central concern — threatens the very conditions of moral formation, not just its content.

annotations.content: fight To
annotations.anchor_text: _tolerably well-ordered form of social life_
annotations.interpretation: This cryptic note likely abbreviates 'fight to' [restore/maintain/create] such a social order — Tom is drawing a practical-political implication from MacIntyre's conditional. Alternatively 'fight To' could be a person reference (e.g., 'fight Tom' as a debate point), but the lowercase 'o' in 'To' suggests it is a verb phrase. Tom seems to be noting that the prior condition MacIntyre assumes must itself be fought for, which is precisely RC's programmatic claim.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is motivated by the conviction that the conditions for moral formation must be actively recovered and defended — Tom is marking the place where MacIntyre's descriptive account implies a normative-political imperative.

annotations.content: at the hands of some teacher
annotations.anchor_text: _in the course of their practical education at the hands_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is isolating the phrase 'at the hands of some teacher' — emphasizing the relational, personal, and embodied character of moral education against any purely textual or institutional account. This prepares for Aquinas's immediately following claim that all education is ultimately self-education, creating a productive tension Tom seems to be tracking.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's account of subsidiarity and character formation depends on the irreplaceable role of particular teachers and mentors — this underline flags that moral initiation is not scalable or algorithmic but requires personal transmission.

annotations.content: all education is in an important way self-education
annotations.anchor_text: _But Aquinas held that all education is in an important_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines Aquinas's thesis as a distinct and contestable claim — it is not obvious, and MacIntyre presents it as held by Aquinas, not as a universally accepted truth. Tom is tracking the Thomistic epistemological commitment: the teacher does not transfer knowledge but occasions the learner's own act of understanding. This has significant implications for how RC thinks about pedagogy and institutional design.
annotations.rc_relevance: If all genuine education is self-education, then RC's project of recovering civic virtue cannot rely on top-down institutional imposition but must create conditions in which individuals genuinely re-enact the enquiry — this is a design principle for RC's programming.

annotations.content: St Tom
annotations.anchor_text: _a teacher leads someone else to the knowledge_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is cross-referencing Aquinas's teaching method with Plato's Socratic method — both are characterized by leading the learner to discover what they already in some sense can know. This is a philosophically rich connection: it suggests MacIntyre's Thomistic account of education has a Platonic genealogy (recollection, maieutics) even while being framed in Aristotelian terms. Tom may be noting both the resonance and the difference.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's engagement with classical traditions of civility requires attention to how Platonic and Thomistic pedagogical models converge and diverge — this annotation suggests Tom is building a comparative account of tradition-transmission across the ancient and medieval periods.

annotations.content: the order of good teaching is ideally the same as the order of effective learning and a book which is well designed to teach
annotations.anchor_text: _order of good teaching is ideally the same as_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this methodological principle because it explains the structure of the Summa Theologiae — and by extension, justifies MacIntyre's own compositional strategy in Three Rival Versions. Tom may be noting a reflexive or self-referential dimension: MacIntyre is implicitly claiming that his own book should follow the same order, which is a significant rhetorical-methodological commitment.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's own output — whether books, curricula, or public programs — faces the same challenge: how to order content so that participants re-enact genuine enquiry rather than receive conclusions. This passage gives Tom a classical warrant for that design principle.

annotations.content: will follow the order of exploratory learning through which the pupil relives the history of enquiry up to the highest point of achievement which it has reached so far
annotations.anchor_text: _will follow the order of exploratory learning through which_
annotations.interpretation: This is the heart of MacIntyre's account of how the Summa works — not as a system to be memorized but as a reenactment of historical enquiry. Tom's underline suggests he sees this as a key claim about what tradition-constituted rationality actually demands: not assent to conclusions but participation in the living movement of enquiry. The phrase 'relives the history' is particularly significant.
annotations.rc_relevance: This speaks directly to RC's methodology: the project cannot simply present classical civility as a set of conclusions but must lead participants to relive the enquiry that produced those conclusions, so that the conclusions become genuinely their own.

annotations.content: will follow the order of exploratory learning through which the pupil relives the history of enquiry up to the highest point of achievement which it has reached so far
annotations.anchor_text: _follow the order of exploratory learning through which the pupil_
annotations.interpretation: The bracket reinforces the underline, suggesting this passage is being marked for a specific structural or argumentative purpose beyond mere importance. Tom may be intending to quote this passage directly in RC writing, or to use it as an organizing principle for a section of the project. The combination of underline and bracket is Tom's signal that this text deserves extended treatment.
annotations.rc_relevance: This bracketed passage could serve as an epigraph or organizing framework for RC's pedagogical philosophy — it captures in MacIntyre's own words the difference between tradition-as-living-enquiry and tradition-as-dead-orthodoxy.

annotations.content: surely
annotations.anchor_text: _the order of good teaching is ideally the same_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is testing MacIntyre's claim against the domain of physics — asking whether the order of good teaching in physics also requires the student to relive the history of enquiry (from Aristotelian physics through Newton to relativity). This is a genuinely interesting philosophical probe: if true, it would support MacIntyre's account as a general epistemological principle; if false in physics, it might suggest the model is specific to moral enquiry. Tom seems to affirm it ('surely in physics') rather than contest it.
annotations.rc_relevance: This note reveals Tom thinking about the scope of MacIntyre's claim — whether tradition-constituted enquiry is universal or peculiar to the moral domain. RC must address this question because its argument about civic virtue will be stronger if the pedagogical principle generalizes across disciplines.

annotations.content: Hence the Summa sets out in its ordering of universal concepts the framework for a type of narrative of moral enquiry to be enacted by individuals who do and will exhibit their rationality by participating in the forms of rationality established by rationality by participating in the forms of rationality established by tradition
annotations.anchor_text: _Hence the Summa sets out in its ordering of universal concepts_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this concluding sentence of the paragraph — it is MacIntyre's most explicit statement of what the Summa is and does. The phrase 'enacted by individuals' is crucial: moral rationality is not possessed abstractly but performed in and through participation in a tradition's forms. Tom may also be noting the slightly awkward repetition in the text ('rationality by participating in the forms of rationality') as either a typographical error or a deliberate rhetorical intensification.
annotations.rc_relevance: This sentence crystallizes the RC argument about civic formation: civility is not a set of rules to be transmitted but a form of rationality to be enacted — and its enactment requires institutional structures (traditions) that make such participation possible.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that to learn how to read the Summa Theologiae rightly, one must already possess certain intellectual and moral virtues — particularly prudentia/phronesis — which themselves cannot be possessed unless the moral virtues are already in place. This circular structure recapitulates Meno's paradox at the level of moral enquiry: we must already be, to some degree, what we are trying to become through the enquiry. The resolution lies in beginning with nascent, ordered passions and initial rule-following experiences, so that formal systematic moral enquiry (including reading the Summa) can build upon and refine those already-forming virtues.

annotations.content: Paradox of Reasoning (Meno)
annotations.anchor_text: _quiry is integral to the moral life_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has titled the page's central problematic at the very top, identifying MacIntyre's argument as a direct instantiation of Meno's paradox — the epistemological puzzle of how one can inquire into what one does not yet know. By writing this as a header, Tom is framing the entire page as a case study in that paradox applied to moral reasoning and virtue acquisition, not just abstract knowledge. This suggests Tom sees MacIntyre as doing something philosophically ambitious: transplanting a Platonic epistemological problem into the domain of Thomistic practical reason.
annotations.rc_relevance: Meno's paradox is directly cited in the margin as linking to virtue formation — central to RC's concern with how institutions and traditions can initiate moral formation in people who do not yet possess the virtues that would allow them to fully understand why those institutions matter.

annotations.content: p.63 Difficulty: Meno's paradox, re/ virtue
annotations.anchor_text: _already familiar type of difficulty reemerges_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is cross-referencing page 63 of the same book, where MacIntyre presumably introduced the 'already familiar difficulty' — Meno's paradox — in an earlier context. The notation 're/ virtue' specifies that Tom is tracking how the paradox migrates from an epistemological problem to a specifically virtue-theoretic one. This is careful structural tracking: Tom is noting that MacIntyre is returning to an earlier argumentative node and deepening it.
annotations.rc_relevance: This cross-reference reveals Tom is tracking the book's recursive argument structure — precisely the kind of 'tradition-constituted rationality' MacIntyre champions, where earlier insights are reformulated at higher levels. For RC, this maps onto how civic and moral traditions must be inhabited before they can be fully understood.

annotations.content: already familiar type of difficulty reemerges
annotations.anchor_text: _here an already familiar type of difficulty_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this phrase because it signals MacIntyre's own meta-commentary — he is explicitly flagging a recursive structure in his argument. The underlining captures the moment MacIntyre says 'we've been here before,' which is philosophically significant: it means the paradox is not a local problem but structural to tradition-based moral enquiry.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'already familiar difficulty' is the paradox of tradition itself — you must be inside a tradition to understand it, which RC identifies as the challenge for any project of civic renewal that cannot simply impose virtue from outside.

annotations.content: prudentia, phronesis, that virtue of practical intelligence
annotations.anchor_text: _virtues is prudentia, phronesis, that virtue_
annotations.interpretation: The underlining of both the Latin and Greek terms for practical wisdom signals that Tom is flagging MacIntyre's deliberate bilingualism — the move to show that Thomistic and Aristotelian traditions converge on the same concept. Tom is likely noting this as a key term for the RC project, where practical wisdom is the virtue that bridges rule-following and situation-responsive judgment.
annotations.rc_relevance: Phronesis/prudentia is the hinge virtue for RC's interest in character formation within institutions — it is the virtue that cannot be taught by rules alone, which is why RC must think carefully about the kinds of communities and practices that cultivate it.

annotations.content: once again involved in an apparent circularity akin to that of the Meno
annotations.anchor_text: _So we are once again involved in an apparent_
annotations.interpretation: The green highlight on this phrase marks the explicit statement of Meno's paradox as applied to moral virtue — this is the argumentative crux of the page. Tom has visually distinguished this as the thesis sentence, not just a supporting detail. The word 'apparent' is significant and Tom likely noticed it: MacIntyre is saying the circularity is apparent, not vicious, which means the resolution matters enormously.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the core RC epistemological problem: how does a project of civic renewal address people who lack the very virtues that would allow them to value such renewal? MacIntyre's 'apparent' circularity points toward the answer — nascent virtue and initial formation.

annotations.content: The key to the resolution of this
annotations.anchor_text: _The key to the resolution of this_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this transitional phrase because it signals MacIntyre is about to offer his answer to the paradox. This is structurally important — after establishing the circularity, the reader needs to track exactly how MacIntyre claims to dissolve it. Tom is flagging the argumentative pivot.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, the resolution MacIntyre offers — beginning with ordered passions and initial experiences of rule-following — is directly applicable to the question of how institutions can initiate moral formation before full virtue is present.

annotations.content: g (why students are intellectually selected) esp moral crts
annotations.anchor_text: _We have to begin by acquiring enough_
annotations.interpretation: This note — partially obscured but readable as a reflection on selective admission criteria — suggests Tom is reading MacIntyre's argument about pre-conditions for moral enquiry as having institutional implications: not everyone is equally positioned to enter the enquiry. The 'esp moral crts' (especially moral criteria) suggests Tom is thinking about what criteria of selection or formation institutions should use. The 'g' may be a section label or a reference to another note.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly engages RC's theme of institutions forming character — Tom is asking whether MacIntyre's argument implies that moral education must be selective or tiered, which connects to subsidiarity and the proper ordering of educational communities.

annotations.content: acquiring enough of the virtues to order our passions aright
annotations.anchor_text: _We have to begin by acquiring enough_
annotations.interpretation: This underline marks MacIntyre's proposed resolution to the Meno paradox: the 'enough' is crucial — not full virtue, but a nascent sufficiency. Tom is tracking that MacIntyre does not require complete virtue to begin, only enough to enter the enquiry. This is the anti-perfectionist move that makes tradition-based moral education practically possible.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this 'enough' is the opening through which civic formation can begin — institutions do not require morally perfected citizens, only those with sufficiently ordered passions to be receptive to formation.

annotations.content: initial experiences of rule-following and action-guiding from which we can begin to learn
annotations.anchor_text: _so that we acquire the initial experiences of rule-following_
annotations.interpretation: The green highlight marks MacIntyre's description of the first stage of moral formation: rule-following experience prior to full understanding of the rules. Tom is visually marking this as the practical entry point into the tradition. This connects to the craft analogy MacIntyre uses throughout — you apprentice before you theorize.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with how traditions initiate newcomers is directly illuminated here — civic friendship and participation in civic institutions provide exactly these 'initial experiences of rule-following' before citizens can articulate why the rules matter.

annotations.content: a knowing how which is both a knowing how to act and a knowing how to learn how to act
annotations.anchor_text: _a knowing how which is both a knowing how_
annotations.interpretation: This underline marks MacIntyre's important distinction between first-order practical knowledge (knowing how to act) and second-order practical knowledge (knowing how to learn how to act). Tom is flagging this as philosophically precise — it distinguishes mere habituation from the kind of reflective practical wisdom that can correct and develop itself. This is Ryle's 'knowing how' put to Thomistic use.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's interest in tradition vs. reform is illuminated here — a tradition that only transmits first-order knowing-how becomes rigid; one that transmits second-order knowing-how-to-learn can reform itself from within, which is MacIntyre's model of healthy tradition.

annotations.content: nascent virtues
annotations.anchor_text: _putting our nascent virtues those_
annotations.interpretation: The green highlight on 'nascent virtues' marks a key technical term — MacIntyre's solution to the Meno paradox depends on the concept of virtues that are real but incomplete, sufficient to begin but not yet fully formed. Tom is flagging this as a term of art that does philosophical work. The concept allows MacIntyre to say the circularity is 'apparent' because nascent virtue is genuinely virtue, not just simulation.
annotations.rc_relevance: Nascent virtue is the RC concept of the 'civic seed' — the minimal moral formation that institutions must cultivate and assume. RC's project of resurrecting civility depends on identifying what nascent civic virtues remain in contemporary fragmented communities.

annotations.content: virtues in more adequate form
annotations.anchor_text: _acquiring those same virtues in more adequate_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this phrase to mark the teleological structure of MacIntyre's argument: the same virtues that were nascent at the start are the goal, but in 'more adequate form.' This is the spiral structure of moral development — not a linear progression to different virtues, but a deepening of the same ones. Tom likely sees this as the answer to the Meno paradox's apparent circularity.
annotations.rc_relevance: This spiral structure maps directly onto RC's vision of civic renewal — not replacing existing civic virtues with new ones, but recovering and deepening the same virtues that tradition has partially formed in citizens.

annotations.content: formal systematic moral enquiry, including the reading of the Summa Theologiae, has the place that it has in the overall order of individual development and training
annotations.anchor_text: _formal systematic moral enquiry, including the reading_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the full concluding sentence of the argument because it states MacIntyre's final claim: that formal enquiry (including philosophical texts like the Summa) has its place determined by the prior order of character formation, not the other way around. This is MacIntyre's anti-Enlightenment point — reason does not ground virtue; virtue makes reason possible.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the central RC counter to Enlightenment encyclopaedism: the MacIntyre argument that formal systematic enquiry is downstream of character formation, not its foundation. RC's project must therefore prioritize institutional and communal formation over theoretical persuasion.

annotations.content: see p9 bottom 2134
annotations.anchor_text: _formal systematic moral enquiry, including the reading_
annotations.interpretation: Tom cross-references another page (likely p.9 of this book or another text, with '2134' possibly a line, note, or catalog number) in connection with the argument about formal enquiry's place in moral development. This suggests Tom has a web of cross-references being built across the book or across his research notes, connecting MacIntyre's argument to an earlier or related passage.
annotations.rc_relevance: The cross-reference habit itself reflects RC's method — building a tradition of reading that connects texts relationally, which is precisely what MacIntyre argues tradition-constituted rationality requires.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the Summa Theologiae represents the highest stage of Aquinas's integrated curricular vision, in which theology provides the framework within which all subordinate disciplines — possessing real but limited autonomy — are to be understood. This position was in direct tension with the dominant Augustinian curriculum, which, by grounding all understanding in divine exemplars yet leaving the links between theology and other disciplines practically unexplained, had produced a de facto disciplinary fragmentation and loss of curricular unity. Hugh of St. Victor's Didascalion was an earlier attempt to restore that unity, but MacIntyre implies it was insufficient to resolve the underlying Augustinian epistemological problem.

annotations.content: rules of i-ference/ralstr'n
annotations.anchor_text: _sufficient practical experience of pursuing and reformulating goals_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is shorthandedly noting 'rules of inference/ratiocination' — the logical procedural dimension of the stage MacIntyre describes. The abbreviation suggests Tom is tracking the epistemological ladder MacIntyre constructs: experience → rule-following → systematic moral enquiry → metaphysics/theology. He is likely flagging this as the structural mechanism by which tradition-constituted rationality advances, distinguishing it from the Enlightenment picture of context-free reason.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly maps onto the RC theme of tradition-constituted rationality versus encyclopaedist rationalism — Tom is marking the internal logic by which a tradition's standards of reasoning develop and self-correct, a core MacIntyrean claim relevant to RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry.

annotations.content: now applied to experience: logic, math. moral enquiry
annotations.anchor_text: _systematic moral enquiry And achievement in this_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is constructing his own taxonomy of the disciplines to which rules of inference are 'now applied,' listing logic, mathematics, and moral enquiry as the progression MacIntyre is tracing. This suggests Tom is mapping the sequence of enquiry stages onto recognizable academic disciplines, perhaps preparing to use this schema to explain to a non-specialist audience how Aquinas's hierarchy of sciences works in practice.
annotations.rc_relevance: Relevant to the RC theme of subsidiarity and the ordering of disciplines — Tom is noting how different forms of enquiry relate hierarchically, which mirrors the RC concern with how institutions (academic, ecclesial, civic) should be properly ordered rather than fragmented.

annotations.content: and extended analogically to: metaphysics/theology ↓
annotations.anchor_text: _metaphysical and theological studies which complete all enquiry_
annotations.interpretation: The arrow pointing downward suggests Tom sees this as the culminating move of the argument on this page — that the extension of rational enquiry 'analogically' into metaphysics and theology is the telos of the entire Thomistic curriculum. The word 'analogically' is Tom's own interpretive insertion, likely drawing on Aquinas's doctrine of analogical predication, which he is reading into MacIntyre's account of how lower disciplines point upward to theology.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is central to the RC project's engagement with MacIntyre's tripartite structure: the 'tradition' version of moral enquiry culminates in theology as the architectonic science, which RC contrasts with the encyclopaedist severing of moral enquiry from its metaphysical and theological grounding.

annotations.content: The autonomy of subordinate disciplines is real but limited and Aquinas's understanding of both that autonomy and those limitations was at odds with the dominant Augustinian curriculum.
annotations.anchor_text: _providing also the framework within which the other disciplines_
annotations.interpretation: This is the most heavily marked passage on the page — highlighted in green and likely also underlined — indicating Tom considers this the thesis sentence of the entire page. He is flagging the precise claim that Aquinas's position is a via media: neither collapsing disciplines into theology (full heteronomy) nor allowing them complete independence (full autonomy). The contrast with the Augustinian curriculum signals that this is a historical-structural claim, not merely a philosophical one.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the single most RC-relevant passage on the page: it captures the subsidiarity principle avant la lettre — subordinate disciplines have genuine but bounded authority — and it frames the RC project's argument that Thomistic integration is a corrective to both secular fragmentation and theocratic absorption of civil life.

annotations.content: sufficient practical experience of pursuing and reformulating goals, of following rules and of disciplining the passions to provide a basis for entering upon systematic moral enquiry
annotations.anchor_text: _reached the stage in which we have had sufficient practical experience_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is marking the full description of the preparatory stage that precedes systematic moral enquiry — experience, rule-following, and passion-discipline as prerequisites. This is not merely a passage summary underline; Tom is likely tracking MacIntyre's developmental, virtue-ethical account of how enquirers are formed before they can engage in higher-order enquiry, which is structurally important for understanding why the tradition model resists shortcutting to abstract principles.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's theme of character formation through institutions — the passage describes a pedagogical-formative process that institutions must sustain, and its underlining suggests Tom sees it as evidence for RC's claim that moral enquiry cannot be separated from the social practices that form enquirers.

annotations.content: making explicit what is presupposed by the intelligibility discovered in all enquiry
annotations.anchor_text: _metaphysical and theological studies which complete all enquiry in part by_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the functional definition of what metaphysics and theology do within the Thomistic curriculum: they make explicit the presuppositions of intelligibility that lower disciplines rely on but cannot themselves justify. This is a key epistemological claim — theology is not extrinsic to enquiry but completes its internal logic. Tom may be preparing to use this formulation in RC writing to explain why the secular bracketing of theology produces incoherence rather than neutrality.
annotations.rc_relevance: This speaks to RC's critique of Enlightenment rationalism's pretension to presuppositionless enquiry — MacIntyre (via Aquinas) is arguing that all enquiry has theological presuppositions, and Tom is marking this as a resource for RC's argument that recovering those presuppositions is necessary for genuine civility and moral community.

annotations.content: The Summa Theologiae was not misnamed; it is itself a work of instruction at this highest stage, comprehending and integrating into itself however that in the other disciplines which theology needs, and itself providing also the framework within which the other disciplines have providing also the framework
annotations.anchor_text: _The Summa Theologiae was not misnamed it is itself a work_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's characterization of the Summa as a pedagogical instrument of the highest integrative stage, not merely a speculative theological text. The emphasis on 'comprehending and integrating' suggests Tom is noting the architectonic function of the Summa — it does not merely top the curriculum but actively organizes it. This may be a cross-reference point for RC discussions of what a genuinely unified intellectual tradition looks like institutionally.
annotations.rc_relevance: Relevant to RC's concern with institutional formation of character and the role of traditions in sustaining coherent intellectual communities — the Summa here models what RC seeks at the level of civic and moral life: an integrating framework that preserves the real contributions of subordinate practices without letting them fragment into incommensurable specialisms.

annotations.content: a theological account of the relationship of God to His creation was required by every enquiry which aimed at understanding
annotations.anchor_text: _So a theological account of the relationship of God to His creation_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the Augustinian Platonist claim that theological grounding is a precondition for genuine understanding in any discipline. This is marked probably to contrast it with Aquinas's more differentiated view highlighted above — the Augustinian position makes theology a necessary presupposition of all enquiry, but (as MacIntyre goes on to show) this creates a practical problem when those links remain obscure. Tom may be tracking the structural weakness in the Augustinian position that Aquinas corrects.
annotations.rc_relevance: This tension between Augustinian totalization and Thomistic differentiation is directly relevant to RC's engagement with the genealogist/tradition debate — RC must navigate between a theocratic model that absorbs civil life into theology and a secular model that severs them entirely, and Tom is mapping that fault line here.

annotations.content: remained unexplained and obscure, in practice the links between theology and the other disciplines were minimal
annotations.anchor_text: _exemplars in the divine mind themselves not directly accessible to finite minds_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the practical consequence of the Augustinian epistemological failure: despite the theoretical claim that all understanding requires theology, the actual intellectual links remained obscure, producing de facto disciplinary autonomy without principled grounding. This is a diagnostic claim about institutional intellectual life — theory and practice diverged. Tom is likely marking this as a historical case study in the kind of fragmentation RC is trying to address.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's theme of social and intellectual fragmentation — this passage describes how a nominally unified tradition can in practice dissolve into disconnected specialisms when its integrating principles are not operationally available, which is MacIntyre's diagnosis of the modern academy and which RC applies to contemporary civic life.

annotations.content: so that the curriculum lost any real unity
annotations.anchor_text: _increasing tendency for those disciplines to proceed with a de facto autonomy_
annotations.interpretation: A short, pointed underline on what is effectively the diagnostic conclusion of the Augustinian section. Tom isolates this phrase as the key outcome — loss of curricular unity — which is the problem Aquinas's framework is designed to solve. The brevity of the marked passage suggests Tom is tagging a quotable phrase, possibly for use in RC writing as a description of what happens when tradition-constituted rationality loses its integrating center.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'loss of real unity' is the intellectual analogue to the social fragmentation RC is diagnosing in contemporary civic life — Tom is implicitly connecting curricular disintegration to civic disintegration, which is a central RC thesis about the relationship between intellectual traditions and the health of moral communities.

annotations.content: Hugh of St. Victor has written the Didascalion in order to restore genuine sovereignty to scriptural theology within a reunified curriculum
annotations.anchor_text: _It was in response to just this tendency in its earlier forms that Hugh_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the introduction of Hugh of St. Victor as a historical interlocutor — someone who recognized the same problem as Aquinas but attempted an earlier, ultimately insufficient solution. By marking this, Tom is tracking MacIntyre's historical narrative structure: problem recognized → insufficient prior solution → Aquinas's more adequate response. This is important for understanding why MacIntyre presents Aquinas as not merely conservative but genuinely innovative within the tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: Relevant to RC's theme of tradition versus reform — Hugh represents the kind of reform that attempts to restore unity by reasserting hierarchical sovereignty of theology, which RC must distinguish from Aquinas's more differentiated subsidiarity model that grants real autonomy to subordinate disciplines while maintaining their ordered relationship to the whole.

pages.summary: MacIntyre reconstructs the institutional crisis at the medieval University of Paris occasioned by the reception of Aristotle's physical and metaphysical works: if those works were assigned to the Faculty of Arts, the Arts faculty would gain autonomous authority over questions previously governed by theology, yet if assigned to Theology, theology itself would have to become philosophical in a radically new way. Only after Albertus Magnus set new standards of Aristotelian presentation did it become clear that theology might have to become a philosophical discipline — a break with conventional Augustinian understanding. Aquinas's Summa then embodies this resolution: by integrating metaphysical commentary into theology's own argumentative structure, theology is no longer sovereign dictator over subordinate disciplines but must argue with them, producing a work whose genre separated it from both conventional orthodoxies and the Averroist program.
