annotations.content: At the level of university organization the question was: in which faculty should the works of Aristotle be studied and taught
annotations.anchor_text: _At the level of university organization the question_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets the opening institutional framing of the Aristotle-reception problem, marking this as a key structural moment in MacIntyre's argument. The bracket signals that the question of which faculty owns Aristotle is not merely administrative but epistemologically loaded — it determines who has jurisdiction over reason itself. Tom is likely flagging this as the concrete institutional embodiment of the tradition-vs.-reform tension MacIntyre is tracing.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly maps onto the RC theme of institutions as the site where traditions are either preserved or transformed; the university is here functioning as a subsidiary institution whose internal ordering reflects deeper questions about the authority of reason vs. revelation.

annotations.content: physical and metaphysical works were assigned to the Faculty of Arts
annotations.anchor_text: _physical and metaphysical works were assigned to the Faculty_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the specific consequence of assigning Aristotle's core works to Arts — this is the crux of the jurisdictional crisis. He is tracking the logic of how disciplinary assignment creates or forecloses intellectual authority, noting that the 'merely' administrative act of curricular placement has profound consequences for the sovereignty of theology.
annotations.rc_relevance: Connects to RC's interest in subsidiarity: the question of which institutional level (faculty) governs which domain of enquiry is itself a subsidiarity question about competence and legitimate authority within a tradition-structured community.

annotations.content: pendently on matters on which theology had been sovereign
annotations.anchor_text: _pendently on matters on which theology had been sovereign_
annotations.interpretation: The underline isolates the word 'sovereign' and the phrase establishing theology's prior supremacy. Tom is noting the specific nature of what is at stake — not merely influence but sovereignty, a strong claim about ultimate jurisdiction. This word choice by MacIntyre signals a political as much as an epistemological relationship between disciplines, which Tom appears to find significant.
annotations.rc_relevance: Relevant to RC's engagement with MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism: the pre-modern model had theology as sovereign over enquiry, and the Averroist/Arts challenge prefigures the modern fragmentation of disciplines away from any integrating telos.

annotations.content: But were Aristotle's physical and metaphysical works instead to be assigned to the Faculty of Theology, theology itself would have to become philosophical in a quite new
annotations.anchor_text: _But were Aristotle's physical and metaphysical works instead_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the dilemma's other horn with particular care — the word 'But' is circled or marked, suggesting he sees this as a structural pivot in MacIntyre's argument (the dilemma's second term). The underline extends through 'in a quite new way,' indicating Tom registers that MacIntyre is pointing toward genuine transformation, not merely accommodation. This is the moment where reform-from-within becomes the only viable path.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the RC tradition-vs.-reform tension at its sharpest: tradition (Augustinian theology) must either reject Aristotle or transform itself. MacIntyre's point — that Aquinas chose transformation — is central to RC's argument that living traditions can integrate genuine rational challenges without capitulating to them.

annotations.content: But
annotations.anchor_text: _But were Aristotle's physical and metaphysical works_
annotations.interpretation: The circling of 'But' marks it as a logical pivot or adversative hinge in MacIntyre's argument structure. Tom frequently circles such structural connectives to map the skeleton of an argument — here he is noting the exact point where MacIntyre presents the forced choice. This is a reading habit oriented toward argument reconstruction rather than content extraction.
annotations.rc_relevance: Tracks MacIntyre's dialectical method, which RC uses as a model for how traditions reason: the 'but' marks the point at which a tradition is forced to confront an aporia it cannot resolve by simply restating prior commitments.

annotations.content: ogy itself would have to become philosophical in a quite new way. It had at first been generally unacceptable among the theologians
annotations.anchor_text: _ogy itself would have to become philosophical in a quite new way_
annotations.interpretation: Tom continues his underline through the initial rejection by theologians, tracking the historical sequence: proposal → resistance → eventual acceptance via Albertus Magnus. He is reading this as a case study in how traditions handle disruptive intellectual challenges — the initial 'unacceptability' is not terminal but a stage in rational development.
annotations.rc_relevance: Illuminates the RC theme of how tradition-constituted enquiry progresses: resistance to innovation is not mere conservatism but reflects the tradition's need to test new claims against its own standards before integrating them — a process MacIntyre celebrates as rational.

annotations.content: only after Albertus Magnus had set new standards in the presentation of Aristotle's own views that the extent to which theology itself might have to become a philosophical discipline became clear
annotations.anchor_text: _only after Albertus Magnus had set new standards_
annotations.interpretation: This is one of the most heavily marked passages on the page. Tom underlines the entire Albertus Magnus clause, marking the historical turning point where the problem became undeniable. He is interested in Albertus as the transitional figure who makes Aquinas's synthesis possible — not Aquinas alone but the tradition of enquiry he inherited. This resists a 'great man' reading of Aquinas in favor of a tradition-constituted one.
annotations.rc_relevance: Central to RC's interest in how traditions develop: Albertus represents the stage of tradition-constituted enquiry where a new standard of rational articulation forces a tradition to re-examine its own assumptions — MacIntyre's model of rational progress within traditions.

annotations.content: this would have involved a break with the conventional Augustinian understanding of theology
annotations.anchor_text: _this would have involved a break with the conventional Augustinian_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this with care, as it names what is actually at stake: not merely a methodological adjustment but a break with Augustinianism — the dominant tradition. He is marking MacIntyre's acknowledgment that Aquinas's project was genuinely revolutionary, not merely conservative synthesis. The word 'break' is key — Tom may be probing whether MacIntyre can coherently call this tradition-constituted enquiry if it involves a break.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's tension between tradition and reform: if Thomism involves a 'break' with Augustinianism, this complicates simple appeals to 'the tradition' and raises the question of which tradition counts as authoritative — a tension RC must address when invoking Aquinas against modernity.

annotations.content: It was the systematic character of Aquinas's insistence upon giving, within the same extended structures of argument, their due both to Pauline doctrine and to Aristotelian theory which resulted in his producing a work whose genre separated it both from the conventional orthodoxies of the thirteenth-century curriculum and from the Averroist program.
annotations.anchor_text: _systematic character of Aquinas's insistence upon giving_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets this entire passage as a key structural summary — this is MacIntyre's thesis statement about what the Summa is and what makes it generically novel. The bracket signals 'this is the payoff argument.' Tom is tracking the claim that Aquinas's achievement is formal (genre) as much as doctrinal — the Summa's structure IS the argument about how theology and philosophy relate.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's use of Aquinas as a model for moral enquiry: MacIntyre's point that the Summa separates itself from both conventional orthodoxy AND Averroism maps onto RC's project of distinguishing itself from both traditionalist nostalgia and modern rationalism.

annotations.content: both to Pauline doctrine and to Aristotelian theory
annotations.anchor_text: _both to Pauline doctrine and to Aristotelian theory_
annotations.interpretation: The specific underline of this paired phrase marks the precise duality MacIntyre sees Aquinas holding together. Tom is noting the specific intellectual commitments being integrated — not theology-in-general and philosophy-in-general, but specifically Pauline (scriptural, revelatory) and Aristotelian (rational, teleological). This pairing is the heart of the Thomistic synthesis RC draws on.
annotations.rc_relevance: The Pauline/Aristotelian pairing is foundational for RC's argument that moral community requires both revealed sources of obligation and rational accounts of human flourishing — neither alone is sufficient, which is why both secular liberal and fundamentalist alternatives fail.

annotations.content: a work whose genre separated it both from the conventional orthodoxies of the thirteenth-century curriculum and from the Averroist program
annotations.anchor_text: _a work whose genre separated it both from the conventional orthodoxies_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the 'genre separation' claim, which is MacIntyre's most distinctive move on this page. He appears to find the genre argument important — that Aquinas's innovation was not just in content but in form, in how argument is structured. The 'both/from' structure positions Aquinas between two failed alternatives, which maps onto MacIntyre's tripartite structure of encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: Maps directly onto MacIntyre's three rival versions framework that is central to RC: Aquinas occupies the 'tradition' position precisely by being neither the orthodox curriculum (encyclopaedist tendency) nor Averroism (a kind of internal genealogist challenge). RC's project similarly seeks a third way.

annotations.content: so a theology which has integrated metaphysical commentary into itself is now to stand, but this theology has to argue with and cannot merely dictate to the subordinate
annotations.anchor_text: _so a theology which has integrated metaphysical commentary_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the concluding thesis of the page — theology can no longer merely dictate but must argue. He is marking what may be the single most important claim on the page for RC: the shift from theology as sovereign dictator to theology as participant in argument. This is MacIntyre's account of how Thomistic tradition-constituted enquiry works — not by authority alone but by rational engagement.
annotations.rc_relevance: Core to RC's engagement with the role of institutions in forming character: if even theology cannot merely dictate but must argue, then RC's project of recovering civility through tradition must itself take the form of rational argument rather than nostalgic assertion — a methodological commitment MacIntyre is here modeling.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the Summa Theologiae is not a neutral text accessible to any rational reader, but rather requires the reader to be a certain kind of person — formed morally and theologically — in order to read it aright. This constitutes a direct challenge to the liberal assumption that every rational adult is free to read any book. He then introduces two contrasting ways of reading Aquinas's questions on law (Ia-IIae, qq. 90–97): one that isolates the text from Aquinas's broader corpus, and one that reads it within the full tradition — showing that the first method produces irresolvable disagreement because readers import alien philosophical frameworks.

annotations.content: formation — Di's prior to intellectual formation + reading = text
annotations.anchor_text: _Aquinas and the Rationality of Tradition_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is glossing MacIntyre's core claim that reading the Summa properly requires prior moral and intellectual formation. The abbreviation 'Di's' likely refers to 'disciplines' — the opening argument that the disciplines themselves form the reader. Tom is tracking a sequence: formation precedes intellectual formation, which precedes correct reading of the text. This is a structural note about the order of causation MacIntyre is establishing, possibly to use as a talking point or thesis anchor.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly maps onto RC's interest in the role of institutions in forming character — the idea that civic and moral formation must precede participation in a tradition's intellectual life, not follow from neutral individual encounter with texts.

annotations.content: dialectical encounter
annotations.anchor_text: _disciplines in a form of active dialectical encounter_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'dialectical encounter' as a key technical term. MacIntyre is contrasting this form of engaged, tradition-embedded reading against both the Averroist autonomous philosophy and Augustinian theology. The circle suggests Tom sees this phrase as load-bearing — the Summa is not a static repository of doctrine but a living argumentative form requiring active engagement.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, 'dialectical encounter' speaks to civic friendship and the dialogic nature of tradition — the opposite of the fragmented, monological discourse MacIntyre diagnoses in modernity. It suggests civility as a practice of encounter, not mere coexistence.

annotations.content: Averroist insistence on the autonomy of philosophy and the conventional Augustinian theology found no room for
annotations.anchor_text: _autonomy of philosophy and the conventional Augustinian theology_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the two institutional positions that the Summa's dialectical form simultaneously challenged. This is MacIntyre's historical situating of Aquinas — the Summa was not simply theology or philosophy but a third form that disrupted both established camps. Tom likely flags this to show how tradition-based enquiry defines itself against rival frameworks.
annotations.rc_relevance: This maps onto RC's tripartite structure (encyclopaedist / genealogist / tradition) — Aquinas's tradition-based enquiry is here shown emerging in explicit contrast to two rival frameworks, anticipating MacIntyre's broader typology.

annotations.content: forces us back upon the question of what kind of persons we will have to be or become, either in the thirteenth century or now, in order to read it aright
annotations.anchor_text: _forces us back upon the question of what kind of persons_
annotations.interpretation: This is likely Tom's most important underline on the page — it is the thesis claim that reading requires moral formation. The phrase 'either in the thirteenth century or now' is crucial: MacIntyre is making a transhistorical claim, not a historical footnote. Tom underlines this to mark it as the crux of the whole section's argument about tradition and readership.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is central to RC's concern with character formation and subsidiarity — the argument that participation in a moral tradition requires being shaped by it, not merely intellectually accessing it. It challenges the liberal individualist model of moral enquiry that RC is positioned against.

annotations.content: having to be a certain sort of person, morally or theologically, in order to read a book aright
annotations.anchor_text: _concept of having to be a certain sort of person_
annotations.interpretation: A reinforcing underline of the same thesis, focusing on the 'concept' as MacIntyre names it. Tom may be marking this as a named concept to extract for citation or analysis — MacIntyre is identifying this idea as philosophically significant enough to name explicitly. The double underline of this theme suggests Tom sees it as essential to his own argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this concept is directly relevant to the idea that civic institutions must form persons capable of participation in public life — not simply inform them. The idea that readership (and by extension citizenship) is a virtue, not a capacity, is a foundational RC claim.

annotations.content: is alien to the assumption of liberal modernity that every rational adult should be free to and is able to read every book
annotations.anchor_text: _alien to the assumption of liberal modernity_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's explicit naming of the liberal assumption being contested. The word 'alien' is pointed — this is not merely a different view but an incompatible one. Tom is tracking MacIntyre's direct confrontation with liberal epistemology as it applies to texts and readership.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is one of RC's central targets — the Enlightenment assumption of universal rational access to moral truth independent of formation. MacIntyre's critique here parallels RC's critique of the 'neutral public square' assumption in contemporary civility discourse.

annotations.content: Yet this liberal assumption consorts uneasily with the idiom of recent literary interpretation
annotations.anchor_text: _liberal assumption consorts uneasily with the idiom_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the pivot where MacIntyre turns to literary theory (Paul de Man) as an unexpected ally. The word 'consorts uneasily' signals MacIntyre's irony — even secular literary criticism, through its attention to moral relations between author, text, and reader, implicitly concedes the tradition-based point. Tom may be noting this as a rhetorical move useful for RC audiences skeptical of Thomistic premises.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this is a bridging move — showing that even non-Thomistic frameworks (contemporary literary theory) recognize the moral dimensions of reading. This could support RC's engagement with secular interlocutors without retreating to purely theological arguments.

annotations.content: the power to disrupt and undermine academic explications de texte
annotations.anchor_text: _power to disrupt and undermine academic explications_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's claim that the Summa shares with de Man's literary concepts the power to destabilize purely academic, detached readings. The French phrase 'explications de texte' signals a specific tradition of close textual analysis — MacIntyre is arguing that such methods are insufficient for the Summa.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this supports the argument that tradition-based texts resist reduction to technical scholarly analysis — they demand existential and moral engagement, which is precisely what RC argues civic life requires of citizens engaging with their own inherited traditions.

annotations.content: two very different ways of reading questions 90 to 97 of the Ia-IIae, those which concern law, divine, natural, and human
annotations.anchor_text: _two very different ways of reading questions 90 to 97_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the structural pivot where MacIntyre introduces his two-method contrast. This is a key argumentative marker — Tom is noting the beginning of a case study that will demonstrate the abstract point about formation through concrete textual examples. The specific citation of qq. 90–97 on law is also practically useful for RC work on natural law.
annotations.rc_relevance: The questions on divine, natural, and human law are directly relevant to RC's engagement with subsidiarity and the grounding of civic norms — MacIntyre's treatment of how they are read speaks to whether natural law can serve as a shared foundation for civic discourse.

annotations.content: One way is to focus
annotations.anchor_text: _divine, natural, and human One way is to focus_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'One way' to mark the first of MacIntyre's two contrasting reading methods. This is a structural annotation — he is visually organizing the argument so he can quickly locate the first methodological option (isolationist reading) versus the second. The circle here functions like an outline marker.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, identifying this as the 'bad' reading method matters because it represents the encyclopaedist approach — treating Aquinas's text as a self-contained, technically analyzable document rather than a tradition-embedded one.

annotations.content: a reading whose assumption is that with sufficient scholarly understanding of the particular words of this particular text we could elucidate Aquinas's doctrine on law
annotations.anchor_text: _reading whose assumption is that with sufficient scholarly_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's characterization of the first (inadequate) reading method. This is the encyclopaedist assumption — that the text is transparent to sufficiently trained scholarly analysis. MacIntyre is about to show this produces disagreement, not consensus. Tom flags this as the target claim that the rest of the paragraph will refute.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is MacIntyre's critique of the encyclopaedist tradition applied to textual reading — directly relevant to RC's use of the tripartite framework. The assumption that scholarly method alone suffices mirrors the broader Enlightenment error RC is critiquing.

annotations.content: prereqs to read the Summa
annotations.anchor_text: _forces us back upon the question of what kind of persons_
annotations.interpretation: Tom distills MacIntyre's complex argument into a practical formulation: 'prerequisites to read the Summa.' This is a teaching or presentation note — he is translating a philosophical argument into a communicable concept. It suggests he plans to use this point in RC materials or discussions, framing moral formation as a prerequisite rather than an optional supplement to intellectual engagement.
annotations.rc_relevance: This note crystallizes RC's argument that formation is prior to participation — whether in reading a text, engaging in civic life, or participating in a tradition. It connects to the RC theme that institutions must cultivate the character prerequisites for genuine civic engagement.

annotations.content: modern parallels eg applied ↓ AST
annotations.anchor_text: _liberal assumption consorts uneasily with the idiom of recent literary_
annotations.interpretation: Tom notes that MacIntyre's point has modern parallels and should be 'applied' — the arrow and 'AST' likely refer to a specific thinker, text, or RC working concept (possibly 'Associative Social Theory' or a named theorist). This is a cross-referencing note connecting MacIntyre's argument to contemporary applications Tom is tracking elsewhere in the RC project. The 'eg' signals he has specific examples in mind.
annotations.rc_relevance: This note shows Tom actively bridging MacIntyre's historical argument to contemporary RC contexts — identifying where the liberal assumption about reading/participation manifests today. This is the core RC methodology: using MacIntyre's genealogy to diagnose present fragmentation.

annotations.content: bad ways to read the Summa ↓
annotations.anchor_text: _two very different ways of reading questions 90 to 97_
annotations.interpretation: Tom labels the upcoming section as cataloguing 'bad ways to read the Summa,' with an arrow pointing down into the text. This is a structural outline annotation — he is pre-labeling the argument before reading it or labeling it in retrospect. The downward arrow suggests he sees what follows as an extended illustration of inadequate reading methods.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, identifying the 'bad ways' of reading tradition-based texts is methodologically important — it parallels the project of identifying bad ways of engaging with inherited civic traditions (reducing them to technical rules, historical artifacts, or personal preferences).

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues on this page that Aquinas's questions 90–97 on natural law are not self-interpreting: they can only be properly understood after working through questions 1–89, because the earlier discussion of 'good' establishes that identifying something as good is a factual judgment about what moves a being toward its natural end. Evaluative judgments are therefore a species of factual judgment about the final and formal causes of a species' activity. The concept of good applies only to beings insofar as they are members of some species or kind, and our understanding of those goods is cumulative, developmental, and subject to error — meaning questions 90–97 represent a mature understanding that corrects earlier, albeit genuine, misunderstandings of natural law.

annotations.content: (M₂)
annotations.anchor_text: _only be asked and answered in the way_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is labeling this passage as 'M₂' — almost certainly a sequential marker in a series (M₁, M₂, M₃...) tracking what he has identified as distinct 'modes of reading' Aquinas that MacIntyre enumerates. The bracketed passage just below describes an 'alternative mode of reading' in which questions 90–97 are only intelligible after 1–89. Tom is structuring MacIntyre's argument about hermeneutical method into a numbered list, likely to compare with other modes elsewhere in the chapter or book.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly maps onto RC's concern with the 'encyclopaedist / genealogist / tradition' tripartite structure: the 'alternative mode of reading' MacIntyre defends here is the tradition-constituted mode, and Tom is tracking how MacIntyre distinguishes it from rival hermeneutics — central to RC's argument that Thomistic enquiry cannot be extracted from its tradition.

annotations.content: What is insisted upon by an alternative mode of reading is that questions 90 to 97 could only be asked and answered in the way that Aquinas asks and answers them after questions 1 to 89 have been asked and answered
annotations.anchor_text: _alternative mode of reading is that questions_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets the entire claim that questions 90–97 are hermeneutically dependent on 1–89. This is the structural thesis of the section, and bracketing it suggests Tom sees it as a load-bearing claim he wants to return to or quote. The bracket signals 'this is the proposition I must either defend or contest' — likely he agrees with it, given his interest in tradition-constituted rationality, but he may also be flagging it as needing grounding.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is central to RC's argument against Enlightenment 'encyclopaedist' readings of Aquinas that strip natural law from its narrative and teleological context. MacIntyre's claim here is that the rationality of natural law is tradition-internal — you cannot parachute into Q. 90 without Q. 1–89.

annotations.content: when someone identifies a good as being the true good, that is, the end to which by virtue of his or her essential nature moves, he or she, unless hindered or directed in some way, moves toward it
annotations.anchor_text: _when someone identifies a good as being_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the core teleological claim: that identifying a true good is simultaneously a recognition of one's essential nature's directedness toward an end. This is the anti-Humean move MacIntyre is making — that fact and value are not separate when the 'fact' is a teleological fact about a natural kind. Tom likely underlines this because it is the philosophical pivot that makes evaluative judgments factual rather than merely emotive or prescriptive.
annotations.rc_relevance: This passage is foundational for RC's recovery of civic friendship and virtue ethics: the teleological account of good grounds the claim that social institutions can genuinely form character toward real goods, not merely preference-satisfaction — directly countering the Enlightenment liberal self Tom's project critiques.

annotations.content: always a factual judgment, which when recognized as true by some one moves that person toward that good
annotations.anchor_text: _factual judgment, which when recognized as true_
annotations.interpretation: A second underline reinforcing the fact-value unity thesis. Tom is tracking MacIntyre's move to collapse the post-Humean distinction. The underline of 'always a factual judgment' suggests Tom is marking this as a quotable thesis statement, possibly for use in RC writing where he needs to show that Thomistic moral enquiry does not accept the Humean fact-value gap.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's engagement with MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism depends precisely on this: that the fragmentation of modern moral discourse is a consequence of accepting the Humean fact-value split, and that tradition-constituted enquiry offers an alternative. This sentence is Tom's evidence.

annotations.content: Evaluative judgments are a species of factual judgment concerning the final and formal causes of activity of members of a particular species
annotations.anchor_text: _Evaluative judgments are a species of factual judgment_
annotations.interpretation: This is arguably the most philosophically dense sentence on the page, and Tom marks it carefully. By underlining this, he is flagging the Aristotelian-Thomistic metaphysical machinery (final and formal causation) that MacIntyre uses to dissolve the is-ought problem. Tom may be thinking about how to present this to a non-specialist RC audience — or he may be noting the dependency of the whole argument on a contested metaphysics of natural kinds.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this sentence is foundational: it justifies why institutions (family, guilds, civic associations) are not merely conventional but have genuinely evaluable forms — their goodness is factual relative to their nature and final cause. This supports RC's subsidiarity theme.

annotations.content: The concept of good, then, has application only for beings insofar as they are members of some species or kind
annotations.anchor_text: _concept of good, then, has application only_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the species-membership condition for the application of 'good.' This is a strong metaphysical claim that limits the universalizability of 'good' to natural-kind membership. Tom may be flagging this because it raises the question of how 'human good' is constituted — whether by biological species membership alone or also by membership in a tradition or community, which is MacIntyre's broader point.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's concern with the social constitution of persons: if 'good' only applies to beings qua members of a kind, then the erosion of natural community (family, civic association) undermines the very conditions under which moral evaluation is possible — a key RC argument against atomistic liberalism.

annotations.content: we are guided through the arguments which might have entangled us in error, so that when we arrive at questions 90 to 97 we are able to characterize our earliest and most primitive, albeit genuine, understandings of the natural law from the standpoint of the mature understanding which we have now acquired
annotations.anchor_text: _guided through the arguments which might have entangled_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the full developmental/corrective thesis: that questions 1–89 function as a guided enquiry that corrects earlier, primitive understandings, so that Q. 90–97 represents epistemic maturity. Tom is likely struck by this because it frames Thomistic enquiry as genuinely historical and self-correcting — not a deductive system from fixed axioms but a tradition that learns. The word 'entangled' is philosophically loaded: it implies that Enlightenment readers who skip the preparatory enquiry are trapped in error.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the heart of RC's argument about tradition vs. reform: moral progress is possible, but only from within a tradition that has the resources to identify and correct its own errors. It directly opposes the encyclopaedist assumption that reason can start fresh from neutral first principles.

annotations.content: In-Issue development
annotations.anchor_text: _concept of good, then, has application only_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes 'In-Issue development' beside the passages on how understanding of goods changes over time and is subject to error. This phrase suggests Tom is conceptualizing MacIntyre's point as a model of 'development within an issue' — that is, how a tradition handles internal disagreement and error-correction over time rather than rupture. 'In-Issue' may be Tom's own RC terminology for intra-traditional refinement as opposed to revolutionary overhaul.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's theme of tradition vs. reform: Tom appears to be theorizing a category of legitimate change that is neither conservative stasis nor Enlightenment-style revolutionary break — development that is continuous with, and accountable to, the tradition's own standards.

annotations.content: See again / Postan p.12
annotations.anchor_text: _our earliest and most primitive, albeit genuine, understandings_
annotations.interpretation: Tom cross-references 'Postan p.12,' likely M.M. Postan (medieval economic historian) or possibly another Postan. This is a striking cross-reference: Tom is connecting MacIntyre's epistemological point about the development of understanding to a historical or social-scientific text. He may be tracking a parallel between MacIntyre's account of how moral enquiry matures and Postan's account of how medieval institutions or concepts developed. The 'see again' suggests he has noted this connection before.
annotations.rc_relevance: This cross-reference suggests Tom is building an RC argument that bridges philosophical and historical-sociological evidence — using Postan's historical work to ground MacIntyre's abstract claims about tradition-constituted development in concrete medieval institutional history, a methodological move central to RC's project.

annotations.content: characterize
annotations.anchor_text: _90 to 97 we are able to characterize our earliest_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles the word 'characterize,' which is doing quiet philosophical work: MacIntyre says we 'characterize' our earlier understandings from the standpoint of the mature understanding — not that we simply replace or refute them. 'Characterize' implies retrospective interpretation, not elimination. Tom's circle suggests he notices this word is load-bearing for MacIntyre's developmental, non-revolutionary model of tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: The verb 'characterize' encapsulates RC's non-Whiggish model of tradition: earlier stages are not simply errors to be discarded but genuine, if incomplete, understandings that the mature tradition can retrospectively situate and value. This is the alternative to both nostalgic conservatism and progressive rupture.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the Summa Theologiae must be read as a unified whole rather than as a collection of extractable theses, because abstracting particular arguments — comparing them to Kant, Hume, or Hegel — fundamentally deforms Aquinas's thought. Contemporary curricular habits (e.g., publishing Questions 90–97 as a standalone 'Treatise on Law') perpetuate this deformation by inviting readers to engage Aquinas without first inhabiting the dialectical universe of discourse the Summa constitutes. A genuinely critical engagement with the Summa therefore requires first being a participant in its processes of enquiry and discovery, a condition nineteenth- and early twentieth-century Thomism largely ignored.

annotations.content: Summa can only be read as a whole
annotations.anchor_text: _the Summa can only be read as a whole_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is extracting MacIntyre's central methodological thesis and restating it at the top of the page as a header or thesis-flag — almost as a running title. This is not mere summary; by writing it at the top before engaging the body of the page, Tom is treating it as a hermeneutical rule he wants to carry into the rest of his reading. It signals he sees this claim as foundational, not incidental, to MacIntyre's project.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly names the RC theme of tradition as a living whole vs. the encyclopaedist habit of extracting propositions from their formative context. The claim that the Summa can only be evaluated from within mirrors MacIntyre's broader argument that moral traditions cannot be judged by tradition-external rationality — a cornerstone of the RC critique of Enlightenment fragmentation.

annotations.content: the Summa can only be read as a whole and can only be evaluated as a whole
annotations.anchor_text: _the Summa can only be read as a whole_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the exact sentence he then paraphrases in the top margin note, confirming this is the page's load-bearing claim for him. The double highlighting (both the margin note and the text) signals this is a quotable thesis, likely destined for direct citation in RC writing. He is also implicitly endorsing MacIntyre's anti-reductionist hermeneutics.
annotations.rc_relevance: Connects to RC's concern with institutional integrity — just as the Summa cannot be fragmented without distortion, civic and moral institutions (the Church, the polis, the guild) lose their formative power when their practices are abstracted from their tradition-embedded context.

annotations.content: is thus on this view a good deal more difficult to encounter, let alone evaluate, Aquinas's thought
annotations.anchor_text: _good deal more difficult to encounter, let alone evaluate_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the epistemological consequence of the holistic thesis: genuine encounter with Aquinas is harder than Thomists and their opponents have assumed. The highlight isolates the rhetorical escalation — 'encounter' before 'evaluate' — which implies that even recognizing what Aquinas is doing requires formation within the tradition. Tom may be tracking this as a challenge to RC's practical task: how do you introduce Aquinas to a contemporary audience without this prior formation?
annotations.rc_relevance: Speaks directly to RC's pedagogical challenge: if civic virtue and Thomistic moral enquiry require inhabiting a tradition before they can be evaluated, how does the RC project create the conditions for that encounter in a fragmented, post-traditional public culture?

annotations.content: Yet such deformation is inherent in a great many of our contemporary curricular and publishing habits
annotations.anchor_text: _Yet such deformation is inherent in a great many_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's structural diagnosis: the problem is not individual misreading but institutionalized habits of curriculum and publishing. The word 'deformation' is strong — it implies a violence done to the text's integrity — and Tom's underline suggests he finds this sociological-institutional critique as important as the philosophical one. He may be connecting this to his own institutional context.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's theme of how institutions form (or deform) character and understanding. MacIntyre's point that curricular habits encode epistemological commitments parallels RC's argument that civic institutions either sustain or erode the conditions for moral community.

annotations.content: deforms the reading of Aquinas's theses
annotations.anchor_text: _or whomever deforms the reading_
annotations.interpretation: Tom singles out the verb 'deforms' — a notably physical, almost violent word for a hermeneutical act. By underlining this specific word rather than the whole sentence, Tom appears to be tracking MacIntyre's polemical vocabulary. 'Deforms' implies that the encyclopaedist mode of reading does not merely misunderstand but actively disfigures what it touches, which is a stronger claim than mere error.
annotations.rc_relevance: Resonates with RC's MacIntyrean critique of Enlightenment rationalism: the encyclopaedist project does not neutrally survey moral traditions but actively reshapes them into unrecognizable forms, making genuine recovery of civic virtue harder.

annotations.content: as though each of them offered rival answers to one and the same set of questions about the nature of law, questions which can be formulated, so it is assumed, without already having committed oneself by the presuppositions of their formulation to speak either from outside or from within, that universe of discourse which is the Summa taken as a whole
annotations.anchor_text: _without already having committed oneself by the presuppositions_
annotations.interpretation: This is the longest underlined passage, and Tom marks it in full — suggesting he sees it as the argumentative heart of MacIntyre's critique of neutral comparison. The key phrase is 'without already having committed oneself': MacIntyre is denying that there is a neutral metalanguage for comparing Aquinas to Hegel or Kant on natural law. Tom likely sees this as directly contesting the encyclopaedist assumption of tradition-neutral rationality.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the core MacIntyrean epistemological claim undergirding RC: all moral enquiry is tradition-constituted, and the pretense of neutrality is itself a tradition (encyclopaedism) with its own commitments. RC's recovery of Thomistic civility must reckon with this — you cannot advocate for Aquinas from a supposedly neutral civic standpoint.

annotations.content: is how one can be a critic of the Summa without first having been a genuine participant in its processes of dialectical enquiry and discovery
annotations.anchor_text: _how one can be a critic of the Summa without_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the participatory requirement for legitimate criticism — you must first be a dialectical participant, not merely an external observer. This is underlined rather than highlighted, suggesting Tom is tracking it as a structural argument-move rather than a quotable thesis. He may also be asking: does this demand make the tradition impervious to reform from within, or does it rather describe how reform legitimately proceeds?
annotations.rc_relevance: Central to RC's theme of civic friendship and formation: genuine participation in a tradition's practices precedes the ability to critique or reform it. This has direct implications for RC's vision of how civic institutions should work — membership before critique, formation before judgment.

annotations.content: did the curricular and interpretative habits of the thirteenth century
annotations.anchor_text: _did the curricular and interpretative habits of the thirteenth century_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the historical parallel MacIntyre draws: just as modern curricular habits distort the Summa, so did thirteenth-century curricular habits. This underline may signal that Tom finds the historical symmetry important — MacIntyre is not simply nostalgic for the medieval university but is making a structural point about how any period's institutional habits shape reading. This complicates a simple 'return to Aquinas' narrative.
annotations.rc_relevance: Nuances RC's historical recovery project: if even thirteenth-century readers could domesticate Aquinas through their own curricular habits, then RC cannot simply invoke 'the tradition' as a pristine resource. The recovery must be critically self-aware about how institutional contexts shape reception.

annotations.content: to read it in its own terms from within the tradition which Aquinas reconstituted in the course of writing it is the only way to reckon with it in other than mock and distorting encounter
annotations.anchor_text: _to read it in its own terms from within the tradition_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the normative conclusion: reading from within the tradition is not one option among many but 'the only way' to avoid a 'mock and distorting encounter.' The phrase 'mock encounter' is striking — it implies that external readings are not just inadequate but are a kind of intellectual bad faith. Tom may be flagging this as MacIntyre's strongest and most contestable claim.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly articulates RC's foundational methodological commitment: the recovery of Thomistic moral community cannot be accomplished by borrowing Aquinas's conclusions for liberal or secular frameworks. It requires entering the tradition's own universe of discourse — which is precisely what RC attempts to facilitate.

annotations.content: To this it may be said
annotations.anchor_text: _To this it may be said, indeed should be said_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles this transitional phrase, which introduces an objection to MacIntyre's holistic reading thesis. Circling a dialectical pivot ('to this it may be said') rather than a substantive claim suggests Tom is mapping the argument's structure — he wants to track where MacIntyre acknowledges and responds to a counterposition. This is a reader marking the moment of genuine dialectic.
annotations.rc_relevance: Relevant to RC's engagement with the encyclopaedist / genealogist / tradition tripartite: MacIntyre's method here — stating a thesis, then introducing objections — enacts the tradition-based dialectical enquiry he is describing. Tom may be noting this as a structural model for RC's own argumentative approach.

annotations.content: try it whole, or tear it upon contest
annotations.anchor_text: _Yet if our dominant curricular and interpretative habits make it difficult_
annotations.interpretation: This cryptic margin note appears to be Tom's compressed paraphrase of the dilemma MacIntyre is posing: either engage the Summa as a whole (on its own terms) or your encounter will be a tearing-apart that produces only contestation without genuine understanding. The word 'contest' echoes MacIntyre's 'mock and distorting encounter.' The terseness suggests Tom is writing a mnemonic for a dialectical fork he wants to return to, possibly for RC writing.
annotations.rc_relevance: Captures the RC methodological stakes with precision: the project cannot selectively harvest Thomistic concepts for contemporary civic discourse without committing to the tradition as a whole. The 'tear it / contest' branch names the risk RC is trying to avoid.

annotations.content: Obj?
annotations.anchor_text: _To this it may be said, indeed should be said_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes 'Obj?' (presumably 'Objection?') next to the circled transitional phrase, confirming he is tracking MacIntyre's dialectical structure using scholastic terminology — the very terminology Aquinas uses in the Summa. The question mark suggests Tom is uncertain whether what follows is a genuine objection MacIntyre takes seriously or one he will quickly dismiss. This is a close reader interrogating the author's dialectical honesty.
annotations.rc_relevance: The use of 'Obj?' as a structural label mirrors the Summa's own sed contra / respondeo / objectio format, suggesting Tom is consciously reading MacIntyre through an Aquinian structural lens — which is itself an RC practice of entering the tradition's universe of discourse.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the plain person's knowledge of natural law is expressed not through explicit philosophical formulation but through the practical judgments they make, which presuppose the principium. Philosophical disagreements about how to formulate the principium parallel disagreements about non-contradiction — both require a web of interrelated concepts spelled out through their range of uses and applications. The movement from primitive apprehension of the good toward mature understanding requires engagement with concepts like telos, happiness, action, passion, and virtue, and this understanding is fundamentally communal and teachable, achieved through encounter with others in community.

annotations.content: On Natural Law as obj'n to wholistic thesis --
annotations.anchor_text: _136 Aquinas and the Rationality of Tradition_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is framing this entire page as addressing a potential objection: that natural law theory, because it posits universal precepts knowable by plain persons, resists or undermines the 'holistic' thesis that moral knowledge is tradition-dependent and community-embedded. He is using this page as a test case for whether MacIntyre can reconcile universalist natural law claims with his communitarian-traditionalist epistemology. The double dash suggests he sees tension here that needs further working out.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is central to RC's engagement with the encyclopaedist/tradition tension: if natural law is universally accessible, does that vindicate Enlightenment rationalism? Tom is probing whether MacIntyre's Thomism can hold together universal moral truth and tradition-dependent enquiry — a core RC question about the foundations of civic moral community.

annotations.content: M's response
annotations.anchor_text: _Consider then Aquinas's portrait of the plain person_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is marking this paragraph as MacIntyre's direct answer to the objection he identified at the top of the page. The 'M' almost certainly stands for MacIntyre, and Tom is tracking the dialectical structure of the argument — flagging where the author pivots from stating a problem to providing his solution. The downward arrow extending from this note suggests he is tracking how far the response extends into the text below.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, identifying where MacIntyre mounts his defense of Thomistic natural law against rationalist objections is critical — this is the hinge point between the encyclopaedist challenge and the tradition-based reply, directly relevant to RC's retrieval of Thomistic moral enquiry.

annotations.content: Downward-pointing arrow or triangle extending from 'M's response' label downward along the left margin
annotations.anchor_text: _Consider then Aquinas's portrait of the plain person_
annotations.interpretation: This bracket-like arrow marks the full extent of MacIntyre's response, tracing the argument across several paragraphs. Tom is visually demarcating a unit of argument for later retrieval. The arrow's length suggests he wants to know exactly how much text constitutes MacIntyre's reply before the argument shifts.
annotations.rc_relevance: Structurally important for RC project documentation — it identifies the precise argumentative unit Tom considers MacIntyre's answer to the natural law objection, useful for citation and synthesis.

annotations.content: the principium of practical reasoning
annotations.anchor_text: _which is the principium of practical reasoning_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is tracking the term 'principium' as a key technical concept. The underline signals either that this is the first or a significant use of the term in this passage, or that Tom wants to fix its definition: the principium is the foundational principle of practical reasoning (pursue good, avoid evil), known implicitly before it can be articulated.
annotations.rc_relevance: The principium is foundational for RC's interest in pre-theoretical moral knowledge — the idea that civic moral formation begins with implicit practical wisdom, not philosophical instruction, directly supports RC's argument for community-embedded character formation.

annotations.content: by showing a potentiality to do just that, in the way in which the truth of the principle is presupposed in a multiplicity of particular practical judgments
annotations.anchor_text: _not in any ability to formulate the principle explicitly_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this to capture the epistemological core of MacIntyre's argument: implicit knowledge is demonstrated through action, not articulation. This is the anti-Cartesian move — knowledge is shown in practice before it is stated in theory. Tom likely sees this as the key to answering the 'holistic thesis' objection marked at the top.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's critique of Enlightenment rationalism: civic virtue and moral knowledge are not primarily propositional but practical, demonstrated in the texture of community life — a claim RC needs to make against both liberal proceduralism and catechetical reductionism.

annotations.content: the principium enjoins the pursuit of our good, and the good of human beings has various aspects or parts
annotations.anchor_text: _For the principium enjoins the pursuit of our good_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks this because it establishes the pluralist structure of the good within natural law — the principium is not a single thin precept but points toward a complex, multi-aspected human good. This is important for resisting reductive readings of natural law and for connecting it to Aristotelian eudaimonism.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's account of civic friendship and subsidiarity requires a rich, pluralist conception of the human good — not merely procedural rights — and this passage grounds that in Thomistic natural law, which is a key move for the project.

annotations.content: apprehension of those goods and ends will be implicit in the plain person's particular practical judgments
annotations.anchor_text: _apprehension of those goods and ends will be implicit_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this to secure the claim that ordinary practical life already embeds a grasp of the full range of human goods — this is the non-elitist, non-academic dimension of Thomistic natural law that MacIntyre wants to recover. It connects the philosophical account back to the plain person introduced earlier.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is crucial for RC's subsidiarity theme: moral wisdom is distributed throughout the community, not concentrated in academic or ecclesiastical elites. The plain person's practical judgment is already a form of participation in natural law.

annotations.content: Disagreements about how the principium is to be formulated and understood at the level of philosophical enquiry are then going to be as apt to occur as similar disagreements about the principle of non-contradiction
annotations.anchor_text: _Disagreements about how the principium is to be formulated_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks this analogy between the principium and the principle of non-contradiction as structurally significant. MacIntyre is arguing that philosophical disagreement about first principles is normal and does not invalidate the principles themselves — just as logicians disagree about non-contradiction without undermining logic. Tom may be noting this as a move against the encyclopaedist who demands foundational certainty.
annotations.rc_relevance: This passage directly addresses RC's engagement with the 'encyclopaedist' version of moral enquiry, which demands neutral, tradition-independent first principles. MacIntyre's analogy shows that even the most basic logical principles are subject to tradition-embedded interpretation — supporting RC's anti-foundationalist argument.

annotations.content: a web of concepts, concepts which can only be spelled out in terms of their range of uses and applications
annotations.anchor_text: _must involve a web of concepts_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks 'web of concepts' as a key phrase capturing MacIntyre's holism about practical concepts — no single concept in the principium can be understood in isolation; each requires the others. This is the 'holistic thesis' from the top of the page, now shown to apply within natural law itself.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'web of concepts' image is important for RC because it supports the argument that civic virtue cannot be taught piecemeal or reduced to rules — it requires initiation into an interconnected form of life, which is precisely what institutions of civil community are for.

annotations.content: see again p. 130, 134. --
annotations.anchor_text: _in moving from the earliest and most primitive apprehensions of our good_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is cross-referencing earlier pages where the same theme of movement from primitive to mature moral understanding appears. This suggests he sees this passage as the culmination or restatement of an argument MacIntyre has been building across several pages, and he wants to track the development. The double dash again signals an open question or ongoing thread.
annotations.rc_relevance: This cross-reference helps Tom build a continuous reading of MacIntyre's argument about moral maturation — important for RC's account of how individuals and communities develop from basic civic intuitions to articulate traditions of virtue.

annotations.content: this movement is maturation
annotations.anchor_text: _in moving from the earliest and most primitive apprehensions_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is glossing MacIntyre's technical language with a single clarifying word: 'maturation.' He is interpreting the movement from primitive apprehension to mature understanding of the good as a developmental, educative process — not merely logical but biographical and communal. This is a significant interpretive move that aligns MacIntyre with virtue ethics traditions of moral formation.
annotations.rc_relevance: Maturation as the frame for moral development is central to RC's project: civic character is formed over time through institutions, practices, and community encounter — not delivered through rational instruction alone. This annotation marks where MacIntyre's argument most directly supports RC's formation thesis.

annotations.content: the meaning and use of such concepts as those of end (telos), happiness, action, passion, and virtue
annotations.anchor_text: _have to explore the meaning and use of such concepts_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this list of Aristotelian-Thomistic concepts because it specifies the vocabulary of mature moral understanding — the 'web of concepts' MacIntyre invokes. The enumeration (telos, happiness, action, passion, virtue) maps the full terrain of Aristotelian ethics that must be internalized for the principium to be properly understood.
annotations.rc_relevance: This list is effectively a syllabus for the kind of moral education RC argues civic institutions should support — the formation of citizens who can reason with these concepts, not merely follow rules, is the core of RC's educational and institutional vision.

annotations.content: What is constant in this movement is the core of our initial apprehension, that if we are to achieve an understanding of good in relation to ourselves as being, as animal, and as rational we shall have to engage with other members of the community in which our learning has to go on
annotations.anchor_text: _What is constant in this movement is the core_
annotations.interpretation: Tom gives heavy underline treatment to this sentence — it is likely the most important passage on the page for him. MacIntyre here makes the communal dimension of natural law knowledge explicit: understanding the good requires community engagement, not solitary reflection. The threefold description of the human person (being, animal, rational) is a Thomistic anthropological claim.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the RC project's philosophical cornerstone: moral understanding is inherently communal, requiring what RC calls 'civic friendship' and the context of local institutions. MacIntyre here provides the Thomistic grounding for RC's anti-individualist account of moral formation.

annotations.content: in such a way as to be teachable learners
annotations.anchor_text: _our learning has to go on in such a way_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks 'teachable learners' as a phrase that captures the dispositional requirement for moral education — one must be formed in the right kind of openness before one can learn. This has implications for institutional design: civic and educational institutions must create conditions for teachable reception, not just transmit information.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's institutional theme is engaged here: what kinds of communities, schools, parishes, and civic associations produce 'teachable learners'? This phrase suggests that subsidiarity is not just organizational but formational — institutions must cultivate receptivity.

annotations.content: we accomplish the first realization of our good by in the most elementary way respecting the good of those others in encounter with whom we have to learn
annotations.anchor_text: _we accomplish the first realization of our good_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as the ethical payoff of the page's argument: the most basic moral act is respecting others in the learning community. MacIntyre grounds elementary moral obligation not in abstract rights but in the concrete conditions of shared learning. This is a relational, not contractarian, foundation for morality.
annotations.rc_relevance: This passage directly supports RC's account of civic friendship as the foundation of moral community: the first civic obligation is to respect the conditions of our shared moral education — a claim that stands against both liberal autonomy and authoritarian instruction models.

annotations.content: What we grasp initially in understanding the binding force of the precepts of the natural law are the conditions for entering a com-
annotations.anchor_text: _the precepts of the natural law are the conditions_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the page-ending sentence, which cuts off at 'com-' (presumably 'community' on the next page). This is a culminating claim: natural law precepts are not abstract commands but conditions of entry into moral community. Tom likely sees this as MacIntyre's definitive answer to the holistic thesis objection noted at the top.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the hinge claim for RC: natural law and community membership are co-constitutive. The precepts of natural law are not pre-social but are the very terms on which persons can participate in the kind of community where human goods are pursued — a claim RC needs to make its case for institutional civility.

annotations.content: Community as context for discovery of one's own good
annotations.anchor_text: _members of the community in which our learning has to go on_
annotations.interpretation: Tom synthesizes the paragraph's argument in his own words: community is not merely the setting for moral life but the epistemological condition for discovering one's own good. The phrase 'one's own good' is significant — it is not just the common good that is discovered communally, but the individual's particular good. Tom is pressing MacIntyre's argument further than the text explicitly goes.
annotations.rc_relevance: This gloss is one of the most RC-relevant annotations on the page: it articulates the subsidiarity principle in epistemological terms — the individual's good is discovered within and through community, not prior to it. This directly opposes liberal individualism and supports RC's communitarian account of self-knowledge.

annotations.content: how would you like it, if Johnny pushed you?
annotations.anchor_text: _respecting the good of those others in encounter with whom we have to learn_
annotations.interpretation: This is a deliberately colloquial, parentlike example Tom is using to illustrate MacIntyre's abstract philosophical point about the most elementary form of moral learning. By imagining a mother scolding a child, Tom is showing how the 'first realization of our good' through 'respecting others' is actually instantiated in the most mundane scenes of childhood moral education. This is a pedagogical gloss, possibly for use in teaching or writing.
annotations.rc_relevance: This annotation is significant for RC's communication strategy: Tom is translating MacIntyre's technical argument into accessible, lived examples — precisely the kind of retrieval RC aims to perform for public audiences. It also connects to the 'plain person' theme on this page: ordinary parental correction is already an instance of Thomistic natural law formation.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues on this page that the movement toward the ultimate human good must be initiated in the household and wider community through moral formation, and that Aquinas—working through Aristotle's own criteria—turns those criteria against Aristotle to show that the ultimate good cannot be found in any created thing: wealth, honor, pleasure, the virtues, power, or bodily goods. The Aristotelianism that results has a 'tragic character' because, without rationally warranted belief in divine goodness, there can be no genuine knowledge of the perfect good toward which the soul is oriented. Aquinas thus positions himself as a better Aristotelian than Aristotle by invoking Scripture and Augustine, not to abandon Aristotelianism but to complete it.

annotations.content: reply to particulars moral formation starting w/ parents needed...
annotations.anchor_text: _taught by parents within the household_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging this passage as a direct resource for responding to a specific interlocutor or argument about moral formation—the ellipsis and exclamation suggest urgency or excitement. He is tracking MacIntyre's claim that moral formation begins in the household as an empirical and normative claim, not merely a historical observation. The note implies Tom sees this as actionable for RC: the recovery of civility requires particular, local, embodied moral formation starting with parents, not abstract principles.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly engages the RC theme of subsidiarity and the role of institutions (especially the family) in character formation. It also connects to MacIntyre's critique that Enlightenment rationalism bypasses the particular communities—households, local traditions—that are the actual sites of moral education.

annotations.content: munity in which we may discover what further specifications our good has to be given.
annotations.anchor_text: _community in which we may discover_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this as a key thesis sentence: the community is the epistemic site where the good gets specified. This is not merely a sociological point but an epistemological one for MacIntyre—you cannot know your good apart from communal tradition. Tom likely sees this as foundational for RC's argument that social fragmentation destroys the very conditions under which moral knowledge is possible.
annotations.rc_relevance: Central to the RC project's claim that civic community is not merely instrumental but constitutive of moral enquiry. This supports the RC argument that the recovery of civility is a precondition for practical wisdom, not merely a byproduct of it.

annotations.content: taught by parents within the household or if that is unfortunately lacking, within the wider community
annotations.anchor_text: _taught by parents within the household_
annotations.interpretation: The highlighting of this specific passage, combined with the top margin note, shows Tom is carefully marking the hierarchy of moral formation: household first, community second. The phrase 'if that is unfortunately lacking' signals MacIntyre's awareness of social breakdown—a condition Tom sees as directly relevant to the present. Tom is likely thinking about how RC must account for contexts where the family has already failed as a moral formation institution.
annotations.rc_relevance: This maps onto the RC theme of subsidiarity: moral formation flows from the most local institution outward. It also raises the RC problem of what happens when primary institutions collapse—a question central to the project's diagnosis of contemporary fragmentation.

annotations.content: What that teaching points towards is a discrimination of the ends which one may pursue in the light of that ultimate end or good, which is the true good of one's kind. What is that good?
annotations.anchor_text: _discrimination of the ends which one may pursue_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this as the pivot question of the page—MacIntyre is setting up the Thomistic interrogation of Aristotle's answer. The rhetorical question 'What is that good?' is highlighted because it is the hinge on which the entire argument turns: from community-based moral formation to the metaphysical question of the ultimate end. Tom is tracking the argumentative structure carefully.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project must eventually answer this same question in order to offer a constructive vision, not merely a critique. Marking this question signals Tom's awareness that RC cannot stop at diagnosing fragmentation—it must engage the teleological question MacIntyre is raising here.

annotations.content: wealth, honor, pleasure, and even the virtues, the peculiar excellences of the human soul, cannot be that good
annotations.anchor_text: _wealth honor pleasure and even the virtues_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights Aquinas's (via Aristotle's) negative theology of the good—the systematic elimination of candidates. The inclusion of 'even the virtues' is significant and likely caught Tom's attention: MacIntyre is pushing beyond Aristotle's own answer (eudaimonia through virtue) to a Thomistic transcendence of that answer. This is a critical moment where Aquinas becomes more Aristotelian than Aristotle.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this passage is important because it shows that a recovery of virtue ethics is not itself sufficient—the virtues point beyond themselves toward something they cannot supply. This guards RC against a merely neo-Aristotelian position that stops short of the theological dimension MacIntyre is insisting upon.

annotations.content: power
annotations.anchor_text: _to this list he adds power and the goods_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'power' specifically among the list of goods Aquinas adds beyond Aristotle. This is likely because power is the most politically charged item on the list—the one most relevant to contemporary social and political life. Tom may be flagging this for RC's critique of political and institutional power as a pseudo-telos that contemporary culture pursues in place of genuine goods.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project's critique of contemporary civility's collapse partly implicates the pursuit of power as a substitute for genuine common goods. Circling 'power' here connects MacIntyre's metaphysical argument to the political sociology RC is attempting.

annotations.content: A's methodology turned against him by S.T.
annotations.anchor_text: _Aquinas turns the criteria for an ultimate good_
annotations.interpretation: Tom abbreviates: A's = Aristotle's, S.T. = Sanctus Thomas (Aquinas). He is crystallizing MacIntyre's central argumentative move on this page: Aquinas does not abandon Aristotle's methodology but weaponizes it against Aristotle's own conclusions. This is a sharp, structurally aware note—Tom is tracking the dialectical strategy, not just the content. It shows he is reading MacIntyre's argument about how tradition-constituted enquiry works: you extend a tradition by using its own tools more rigorously than its founder did.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the RC theme of 'tradition vs. reform' at its most precise: genuine reform within a tradition uses the tradition's own rationality to transcend its current formulation. MacIntyre's tripartite structure (encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition) depends on this move—Tom is marking it as a key structural example.

annotations.content: Aquinas turns the criteria for an ultimate good to which Aristotle had appealed against Aristotle and uses them to show, first, that the ultimate good must lie in the relationship of the soul to something outside itself and, secondly, that in no state available in this created world can the type of good in question be found.
annotations.anchor_text: _Aquinas turns the criteria for an ultimate good_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this as the culminating argumentative claim of the section—the two-part Thomistic demonstration. The two criteria (relational good, not available in created world) are the logical structure that drives Aquinas from Aristotle toward Augustine and Scripture. Tom is tracking the syllogistic architecture here, likely because RC needs to understand how MacIntyre thinks tradition-internal argument can reach theological conclusions without abandoning rational enquiry.
annotations.rc_relevance: This passage is central to understanding MacIntyre's model of tradition-constituted rationality as applied to RC: the tradition's own internal logic, when followed rigorously, leads to conclusions that transcend the tradition's original horizon. RC's recovery of civility may require a similar move—following civic tradition's own logic toward a ground it cannot supply from within.

annotations.content: he was trying to be a better Aristotelian than Aristotle
annotations.anchor_text: _he was trying to be a better Aristotelian_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as a key interpretive claim about Aquinas's project—it is the clearest single-sentence summary of the argumentative strategy MacIntyre is describing. The underline suggests Tom wants this as a quotable formulation, likely for use in RC writing or presentations. It captures the paradox that fidelity to a tradition can require going beyond the tradition's founder.
annotations.rc_relevance: This formulation is directly usable in RC's theoretical framing: the recovery of civility is not antiquarianism but an attempt to be better at what the tradition was trying to do than the tradition itself managed. It models the kind of tradition-constituted reform RC is advocating.

annotations.content: the Aristotelianism which results has something of a tragic character to it
annotations.anchor_text: _Aristotelianism which results has something of a tragic character_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the phrase 'tragic character'—a striking and unusual evaluative term in a work of analytical philosophy. MacIntyre is saying that the Thomistic Aristotelianism, shorn of theological warrant, leaves the rational agent knowing what the good must be like but unable to attain it through natural reason alone. Tom likely sees this as both philosophically important and rhetorically powerful for RC's diagnosis of modernity.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project's diagnosis of contemporary fragmentation has a tragic dimension: modernity has inherited Aristotelian moral concepts (rights, virtues, justice) stripped of their teleological and theological context, leaving them incoherent. MacIntyre's 'tragic character' thesis here gives RC a precise vocabulary for naming what is lost.

annotations.content: tragic character of the good
annotations.anchor_text: _tragic character to it. Without some rationally warranted belief_
annotations.interpretation: Tom restates and slightly reformulates MacIntyre's phrase as 'tragic character of the good'—shifting the emphasis from the character of Aristotelianism to the character of the good itself. This is a philosophical refinement: Tom may be thinking that it is not just the intellectual tradition that is tragic, but that the good, as MacIntyre describes it, has an inherently tragic structure for natural reason. This is a sophisticated reading that goes slightly beyond what MacIntyre explicitly says.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this reformulation is important: if the good itself has a tragic character (knowable in outline, unattainable without grace or divine revelation), then civic life built around purely natural goods will always be haunted by an incompleteness it cannot name. This gives RC's project a theological depth that distinguishes it from secular neo-Aristotelianism.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that for an Aristotelian (whether Thomist or otherwise), what is good for any being is determined by its essential nature and the flourishing proper to its kind. Virtues are dispositions to judge particulars in light of truths about one's species, and being practically well-educated means taking pleasure and pain in the right things. Crucially, once you remove the notion of essential nature — as metaphysical nominalism does — the entire Aristotelian scheme of the self, good, and pleasure collapses into a bare individual self with only its pleasures and pains, foreshadowing Hobbesian conclusions about endless desire.
