annotations.content: as when a loved one dies, in absence of hope of resurrection
annotations.anchor_text: _underlying and ordering the range of uses_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is glossing the Hobbesian soul's permanent dissatisfaction — the soul that has embraced materialism and finds nothing beyond finite goods to satisfy it. He reaches for a concrete existential illustration: the grief of losing a loved one without the hope of resurrection is precisely what such a soul would experience as its permanent lot. This suggests Tom is reading MacIntyre's argument not just philosophically but theologically and pastorally, connecting the abstract critique of materialism to lived human suffering.
annotations.rc_relevance: This annotation connects directly to RC's concern with what happens to civic and moral community when transcendent hope is removed — the tradition MacIntyre defends is not merely intellectual but existential, and Tom is tracking what is lost when that tradition is abandoned.

annotations.content: or, following Plato, the soul of the tyrant
annotations.anchor_text: _foreshadowing of much else to come_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is cross-referencing Plato's Republic, where the tyrant is the paradigm case of the soul enslaved to insatiable desire — precisely the Hobbesian 'perpetuall and restless desire of Power after power.' He sees MacIntyre's Hobbesian shadow as having an even deeper Platonic root: the disordered soul that MacIntyre warns Aquinas's revision foreshadows is already anatomized by Plato. This suggests Tom is building a genealogy of the disordered self that runs Plato → Hobbes → modernity.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this annotation flags that the fragmentation MacIntyre diagnoses in modernity has ancient precursors — civic friendship and ordered community are always threatened by the tyrannical soul, making the recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry not merely academic but politically urgent.

annotations.content: A
annotations.anchor_text: _are dispositions to judge concerning those particularities_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has drawn a circled 'A' in the margin, which appears to label a structural element — likely the first of two key claims or steps in MacIntyre's argument on this page. Given the placement near the discussion of virtues as dispositions tied to species-truths, 'A' likely marks the positive Aristotelian account (virtues as species-relative dispositions) before the negative consequence (collapse under nominalism) which may be labeled 'B' or 'P' below.
annotations.rc_relevance: Tom is tracking the logical architecture of MacIntyre's argument — a skill central to RC's project of reconstructing the Thomistic tradition's internal coherence as a counter to Enlightenment fragmentation.

annotations.content: virtues concern pleasures + pains // right likes + dislikes
annotations.anchor_text: _are dispositions to judge concerning those particularities_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is distilling MacIntyre's (and Aristotle's) claim that virtues are not merely cognitive but affective — they concern what one takes pleasure in and is pained by. The double-slash suggests Tom sees two separable but related points: (1) virtues are trained responses to pleasure and pain, and (2) they are therefore about having the right likes and dislikes. This is a note for future use, likely to contrast with modern deontological or emotivist accounts that sever virtue from trained affect.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with how institutions form character connects here — if virtues require trained affective responses, then the institutions (family, church, civic bodies) that shape what we like and dislike are morally formative in a deep sense, not merely conventional.

annotations.content: P
annotations.anchor_text: _Take away the notion of essential nature_
annotations.interpretation: The circled 'P' likely stands for 'nominalism' or 'particulars-only' position, marking the pivot point where MacIntyre introduces the nominalist counter-move. Paired with the circled 'A' above, Tom seems to be charting a two-part structure: (A) the Aristotelian positive account, and (P) the nominalist premise that destroys it. The 'P' may also stand for 'Premises' of the nominalist collapse argument, or possibly 'Particulars' as the only remaining ontological category under nominalism.
annotations.rc_relevance: This structural labeling shows Tom tracking MacIntyre's dialectical method — central to RC's project of understanding how rival traditions argue past each other, since the Encyclopaedist tradition's nominalist commitments make it structurally unable to receive the Thomistic account of the good.

annotations.content: primacy of species in moral learning
annotations.anchor_text: _and of pleasure necessarily collapses_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is naming a key philosophical claim he has extracted from this section: that moral learning is not just individual but species-relative — you learn what is good qua member of a rational kind, not merely qua this particular self. The word 'primacy' is strong: Tom is noting that MacIntyre is not just saying species-membership is relevant but that it is foundational. This has implications for how moral education is conceived — it cannot be purely individualistic.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects directly to RC's theme of subsidiarity and institution-formation: if moral learning requires a species-level framework, then communities (not isolated individuals) are the primary loci of moral education, vindicating the RC project's communitarian and Thomistic commitments.

annotations.content: metaph. nominalism = there are no natures → thus none and (here) thus none to guide you.
annotations.anchor_text: _Thus inescapably from the Aristotelian point of view_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is writing out the logical consequence of metaphysical nominalism in his own words, in a compressed syllogism: no natures → no natural ends → no normative guidance. The '(here)' parenthetical suggests he sees this as a specifically moral application of a broader metaphysical thesis. He is essentially reconstructing MacIntyre's argument in stripped-down form for later retrieval or use in his own writing. The phrase 'to guide you' personalizes the stakes — this isn't abstract but about whether moral life is navigable.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is perhaps the most RC-central annotation on the page: it names the precise metaphysical move that MacIntyre sees as catastrophic for moral community. RC's entire project of recovering civility depends on resisting this move — if there are no natures, there is no shared basis for civic friendship, subsidiarity, or tradition-based moral enquiry.

annotations.content: for an Aristotelian, whether Thomist or otherwise, what is good or best for anyone or anything is so in virtue of its being of a certain kind, with its own essential nature and that which peculiarly belongs to the flourishing of beings of that kind
annotations.anchor_text: _I remarked earlier that for an Aristotelian_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has underlined the core metaphysical claim of Aristotelian ethics: goodness is kind-relative and nature-relative, not preference-relative. The underlining of 'whether Thomist or otherwise' is notable — MacIntyre is insisting this is not a narrowly Thomistic position but a broadly Aristotelian one, which broadens the coalition MacIntyre can invoke. Tom likely underlines this as the positive thesis against which the nominalist collapse will be measured.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the foundational claim RC needs to recover — that civility and civic virtue are not merely conventional but grounded in the kind of being humans are. Without this, the RC project's appeal to tradition is merely nostalgic rather than rationally warranted.

annotations.content: one brings to each particular situation, if practically well-educated, are dispositions to judge concerning those particularities in the light of truths about the good of one's species
annotations.anchor_text: _one brings to each particular situation_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's claim that practical wisdom (phronesis) is not situational intuition alone but judgment informed by species-level truths. The phrase 'truths about the good of one's species' is philosophically loaded — it grounds practical judgment in something objective and trans-individual. Tom is likely marking this as a key move against both relativism and pure situational ethics.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this connects to the theme of tradition as the carrier of species-level moral knowledge — tradition is not mere convention but the accumulated practical wisdom about what it means for beings of our kind to flourish, making its recovery essential for civic formation.

annotations.content: educated is to have learned to take pleasure in doing and judging rightly in respect of goods and to have learned to be pained by defect and error in the same respect
annotations.anchor_text: _educated is to have learned to take pleasure_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's Aristotelian account of moral education as affective re-training — the practically educated person does not merely know what is right but takes pleasure in it and is pained by its absence. This is a direct challenge to intellectualist accounts of ethics. Tom's earlier margin note about 'virtues concern pleasures + pains' is the distilled version of this underlined passage.
annotations.rc_relevance: This passage is directly relevant to RC's concern with institutional formation of character — civic institutions, liturgical practices, and educational communities are the sites where this affective re-training occurs, making them morally indispensable rather than merely instrumental.

annotations.content: Take away the notion of essential nature, take away the corresponding notion of what is good and best for members of a specific kind who share such a nature, and the Aristotelian scheme of the self which is to achieve good of good and of pleasure necessarily collapses
annotations.anchor_text: _Take away the notion of essential nature_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the pivotal destructive argument: nominalism's rejection of essential natures does not merely modify Aristotelian ethics but collapses it entirely. The underlining here is emphatic and comprehensive — Tom wants to preserve the full logical chain. This is MacIntyre's central diagnostic move, and Tom recognizes it as such.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the hinge of MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment morality and directly relevant to RC's argument about why modern liberal frameworks cannot sustain genuine civic friendship — they have already surrendered the metaphysical preconditions for it.

annotations.content: There remains only the individual self with its pleasures and pains
annotations.anchor_text: _There remains only the individual self_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as the terminal diagnosis — the bare individual of modern moral philosophy, stripped of essential nature and species-membership, left only with subjective preferences. The starkness of 'only' is key: MacIntyre is not saying modern ethics is incomplete but that it is a different and diminished thing entirely. Tom likely sees this as the description of contemporary moral culture that RC is responding to.
annotations.rc_relevance: This phrase encapsulates the social fragmentation RC is designed to address — a culture of bare individuals with pleasures and pains cannot sustain civility, civic friendship, or subsidiary institutions, which require shared teleological commitments.

annotations.content: inescapably
annotations.anchor_text: _Thus inescapably from the Aristotelian point of view_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this single adverb, which signals MacIntyre's confidence that the Aristotelian critique of nominalism is not merely a preference but a logical necessity. The word 'inescapably' is doing significant argumentative work — MacIntyre is claiming that from within the Aristotelian tradition, the collapse under nominalism is not contingent but necessary. Tom marks it to flag the strength of the claim.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this word matters because it means the choice between Aristotelian and nominalist frameworks is not a matter of taste but of rational commitment — strengthening RC's claim that recovering the Thomistic tradition is not nostalgia but rational necessity.

annotations.content: Aristotelian
annotations.anchor_text: _Thus inescapably from the Aristotelian point of view an under_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights 'Aristotelian' in yellow at the bottom of the page, possibly to flag the perspective-dependence of the claim that follows — MacIntyre is about to say what follows 'from the Aristotelian point of view,' which invites the question of what follows from rival points of view. This may be Tom flagging the structure of MacIntyre's 'three rival versions' method.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's engagement with MacIntyre's tripartite structure — the Aristotelian tradition is one of three rival versions, and Tom is tracking how MacIntyre marks perspectival claims to keep the dialectic clear.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the discovery of sin and wilful evil — far from leading to Hobbesian despair or Averroist denial — is, for Aquinas, the very condition that makes the human end achievable, because it opens the agent to the theological virtues of faith, hope, and charity. Only the kind of knowledge faith provides, the expectation hope provides, and the capacity for friendship with God that charity provides can complete and correct what natural virtue and natural law left unfinished. The self-revelation of God in scriptural history is thus not a supplement to moral enquiry but its necessary retrospective completion.

annotations.content: Misone/. rolls <w interpreter of Aot-narratives her... like NTW in JNG
annotations.anchor_text: _Aquinas and the Rationality of Tradition_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is identifying MacIntyre as a kind of meta-interpreter or narrator of the Thomistic tradition — someone who 'rolls' or translates Aristotelian narrative frameworks. The comparison 'like NTW in JNG' almost certainly refers to N.T. Wright in 'Jesus and the New Creation' or a similar Wright text, suggesting Tom sees a structural parallel between MacIntyre rehabilitating Aquinas and Wright rehabilitating Paul: both are ressourcement moves that use narrative to recover suppressed rationalities. The abbreviation 'Aot-narratives' may mean 'Aristotle-of-Tradition narratives' or 'acts-of-tradition narratives.'
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's engagement with MacIntyre's 'tradition' strand: Tom is situating MacIntyre within a broader theological-ressourcement movement, suggesting RC should read MacIntyre alongside Wright as parallel recoveries of classical moral community against Enlightenment fragmentation.

annotations.content: limits of prudence: sin
annotations.anchor_text: _tues which are required if one is to have_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is crystallizing one of the page's central structural claims: prudence, the master virtue of the natural order, has an internal limit, and that limit is revealed by sin. This is a philosophical compression — Tom is noting that MacIntyre's argument shows prudence is not self-sufficient, which is exactly what distinguishes Aquinas from a purely Aristotelian account. The arrow pointing downward likely traces this claim into the body of the text.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is a key RC tension: the insufficiency of civic virtue alone to sustain community. The 'limits of prudence: sin' note flags where MacIntyre parts from secular neo-Aristotelianism and why RC cannot rest on civic republicanism alone.

annotations.content: surd and unaccountable
annotations.anchor_text: _is something surd and unaccountable in terms_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines 'surd and unaccountable' as a philosophically loaded phrase — 'surd' meaning irrational or irreducible to rational explanation. MacIntyre is arguing that human moral failure cannot be fully explained within a naturalistic framework; it exceeds the rational scheme. Tom likely marks this because it is the diagnostic term that distinguishes the Thomistic account of human nature from both Hobbesian pessimism and Enlightenment optimism.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's critique of Enlightenment rationalism depends on exactly this: that human moral disorder is not merely institutional or educational failure but something 'surd' — which is why tradition-constituted rationality and not mere procedural reform is needed.

annotations.content: rooted tendency to disobedience in the will and distraction by passion, which causes obscuring of the reason and on occasion systematic cultural deformation
annotations.anchor_text: _rooted tendency to disobedience in the will_
annotations.interpretation: This long underline tracks MacIntyre's three-level account of moral disorder: volitional disobedience, passional distraction, and systemic cultural deformation. Tom marks all three levels, suggesting he sees this as a structural taxonomy of how communities fail — not just individuals. The phrase 'systematic cultural deformation' is especially significant for RC as it points to institutional and civilizational breakdown, not just personal sin.
annotations.rc_relevance: This taxonomy maps directly onto RC's concern with social fragmentation: individual moral failure is embedded in cultural and institutional deformation, which is why recovery requires tradition-constituted communities, not merely individual virtue cultivation.

annotations.content: This type of disruption of the moral life is very different from that which results from a Hobbesian denial of there being any ultimate end
annotations.anchor_text: _This type of disruption of the moral life_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets this comparative claim, which is MacIntyre's explicit differentiation of the Thomistic account of moral disruption from Hobbes. This is a structural pivot in the argument. The bracket suggests Tom wants to hold this distinction analytically — likely to use it in his own writing as the key contrast between a tradition-constituted account of failure and a modern social-contract account that simply eliminates teleology.
annotations.rc_relevance: Central to RC: the argument against reducing social order to Hobbesian contract. Tom is marking the exact point where MacIntyre shows that Thomistic moral failure is teleologically structured (the end is 'at least partly in view') while Hobbesian failure eliminates the end entirely.

annotations.content: just as an Averroist denial of any achievable state beyond those of this present life pointed forward towards a Hobbesian reduction of good and evil to pleasure and pain
annotations.anchor_text: _just as an Averroist denial of any achievable state_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the historical-philosophical genealogy MacIntyre is tracing: Averroism → Hobbesianism. This is MacIntyre showing that the denial of a transcendent end has a long intellectual history that terminates in modern materialism. Tom likely marks this to track MacIntyre's genealogical method — showing how philosophical positions have historical consequences — which mirrors MacIntyre's own critique of the 'genealogist' tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: Relevant to RC's engagement with the tripartite structure: MacIntyre is here doing genealogical work to show how the denial of tradition-constituted teleology produces Enlightenment reductionism — exactly the encyclopaedist/genealogist dynamic RC needs to map.

annotations.content: the discovery of sin points forward to a kind of existential despair which was completely unknown in the ancient world but which has been a recurrent malady of modernity
annotations.anchor_text: _discovery of sin points forward to a kind of existential despair_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this passage — likely in green — as one of MacIntyre's most rhetorically powerful claims: that existential despair is a distinctively modern pathology, not a timeless human condition. This is a direct challenge to secular therapeutic culture that treats despair as a psychological problem rather than a civilizational symptom. Tom is flagging this as a quotable diagnostic claim about modernity.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's diagnosis of contemporary social fragmentation: despair is not a personal malfunction but the cultural product of abandoning teleological community. This gives RC a philosophical vocabulary for why civility collapse is a civilizational problem.

annotations.content: yet for Aquinas, by contrast, it is in fact this discovery of wilful evil which makes the achievement of the human end possible
annotations.anchor_text: _yet for Aquinas, by contrast, it is in fact_
annotations.interpretation: This is the argumentative reversal — the 'how so?' moment — and Tom highlights it as the pivotal claim of the page. MacIntyre is making the counterintuitive argument that moral failure, rightly understood within a Thomistic framework, is generative rather than merely destructive. Tom is marking the exact hinge of the argument where despair is transfigured into the condition for theological virtue.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is foundational for RC: the recovery of civility is not a matter of restoring lost goods but of passing through acknowledged failure. The Thomistic move here — failure as condition of possibility — gives RC a non-nostalgic account of tradition recovery.

annotations.content: repentence as key to charity (SH)
annotations.anchor_text: _a necessary condition for one's reception of the virtues of faith, hope and charity_
annotations.interpretation: Tom notes 'repentence as key to charity' with the citation '(SH)' — likely referring to Stanley Hauerwas or possibly a specific text (perhaps 'The Peaceable Kingdom' or similar). Tom is cross-referencing MacIntyre's Thomistic claim about the structural role of repentance with a theologian who makes a similar argument about the moral community of the Church. This suggests Tom is building a dossier of converging sources on this point.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this is significant: if repentance is the gateway to civic friendship (charity), then RC cannot be built on secular virtue ethics alone — it requires an account of moral repair, not just moral formation. The Hauerwas cross-reference suggests Tom is thinking ecclesially about community recovery.

annotations.content: It is only the kind of knowledge which faith provides, the kind of expectation which hope provides, and the capacity for friendship with other human beings and with God which is the outcome of charity which can provide the other virtues with what they need to become genuine excellences
annotations.anchor_text: _only the kind of knowledge which faith provides_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets the entire theological-virtue triad passage and labels it 'f, h / love / friendship' in the left margin. This is Tom organizing the three theological virtues analytically: faith = knowledge, hope = expectation, charity = friendship/love. The bracket marks this as a structural definition that Tom will likely cite or build upon. The reduction to 'love / friendship' suggests Tom is especially interested in charity as civic friendship — the bridge concept between Aquinas and political community.
annotations.rc_relevance: The theological virtues as conditions for genuine excellence map directly onto RC's core argument: civic friendship (charity) is not a merely natural achievement but requires the kind of moral formation that only tradition-constituted communities — and ultimately theological communities — can provide.

annotations.content: f, h / love friendship
annotations.anchor_text: _knowledge which faith provides, the kind of expectation which hope provides_
annotations.interpretation: This is Tom's shorthand label for the bracketed theological-virtue passage: faith, hope, then love/friendship as the Thomistic triad. The compression into 'love friendship' rather than 'charity' is telling — Tom is translating the theological term into a civic-philosophical vocabulary, likely because RC needs to make the case for these virtues to non-theological audiences.
annotations.rc_relevance: This translation move — charity as civic friendship — is one of RC's central conceptual bridges between the Thomistic tradition and contemporary political philosophy. Tom is already doing the translation work in his annotations.

annotations.content: Acq: ↑ take and ??? [illegible]
annotations.anchor_text: _informing a way of life in and through which the good and the best can be achieved_
annotations.interpretation: This marginal note is partially illegible, but 'Acq' likely abbreviates 'Aquinas' or 'Acquisition,' and 'take' may refer to MacIntyre's interpretive 'take' on Aquinas. Tom may be noting that MacIntyre's Aquinas is a specific interpretation — a constructed 'take' — which relates to the top-of-page note about MacIntyre as 'interpreter of Aot-narratives.' The note signals Tom's critical distance: he is not simply accepting MacIntyre's Aquinas as the historical Aquinas.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC must navigate between the historical Aquinas and MacIntyre's Aquinas; this annotation suggests Tom is self-consciously tracking where MacIntyre is doing constructive work rather than pure retrieval — relevant to RC's own constructive vs. retrieval tensions.

annotations.content: reads to sobriety? conditions, identifying grieving?, yearning. Do all -ISMs want together? Yes, no, both. how far can current resources take you? [underlined:] entrone? to a city
annotations.anchor_text: _Pauline and Augustinian account retrospectively placed_
annotations.interpretation: This is Tom's most expansive and exploratory annotation — a string of live questions he is formulating at the bottom of the page. 'Reads to sobriety' may mean: does this argument land on a sober, realistic account of community? 'Do all -ISMs want together?' is a remarkable question — Tom is asking whether all ideological and philosophical traditions ultimately share a common desire for community (the 'city'). 'How far can current resources take you?' is the practical RC question: what can we actually build with these materials? 'Entrone? to a city' — possibly 'entrance to a city' — suggests Tom is thinking about the threshold or gateway concept: what is the condition of entry into genuine civic community?
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the RC project's generative tension in raw form: can the resources of Thomistic moral enquiry be translated into current conditions to rebuild civic community ('a city')? Tom's questions about whether all traditions 'want together' connect to RC's subsidiarity and civic friendship themes — and his uncertainty ('Yes, no, both') shows he is not assuming a simple convergence thesis.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that Aquinas's corrected Aristotelianism — supplemented by Augustine and Scripture — provides a richer account of the moral life than either pure Aristotelianism or its Lutherian rejection, especially through the cardinal and theological virtues (with charity as their form). A key structural point emerges: Aquinas holds that knowledge of God is not optional but foundational to moral enquiry from the outset, since the unifying framework of natural law — tracing formal, final, efficient, and material causality — always refers back to a unified first cause. This positions Thomistic enquiry against modern natural law theorists who claim we can understand and obey the natural law without any knowledge of God.

annotations.content: a first understanding our incomplete receving, a account reaching... (why we need music... eg Ambrosic: the psaller and pes Briewater
annotations.anchor_text: _Aristotle which had provided a first understanding_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is glossing MacIntyre's phrase 'first understanding' by linking it to the idea of an incomplete or partial reception of moral truth — our grasp is always reaching, never fully achieved. The reference to Ambrose and the Psalter suggests Tom is thinking about how liturgical and musical forms (psalmody, hymnody) functioned historically as pedagogical vehicles for this incomplete but genuine moral formation. The 'why we need music' parenthetical is a striking RC-relevant aside: aesthetic tradition as moral formation.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects directly to RC's concern with how tradition is transmitted — not only through argument but through formative practices like music and liturgy. It anticipates the subsidiarity theme: formation happens in communities of practice before it happens in philosophical treatises.

annotations.content: the core of the moral life
annotations.anchor_text: _Aristotle which had provided a first understanding of_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this phrase to mark MacIntyre's claim that Aristotle provides only a first, incomplete understanding — not the whole. The highlight signals that 'first understanding' is a technical term in the argument's structure, distinguishing Aristotle's contribution from what Aquinas adds via Augustine, Scripture, and charity.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is interested in how classical frameworks are necessary but insufficient — this highlight flags the exact hinge where Thomistic tradition must supplement Aristotelianism, a central RC concern about tradition vs. reform.

annotations.content: leave the despair of moral achievement and the gratuitousness of grace, and what is foreshadowed is Luther
annotations.anchor_text: _Take away or reject the Aristotelianism in the Thomist_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's claim that without Aristotle's corrective, Thomism collapses toward Lutheran despair and sola gratia. This is a structural diagnostic: remove the rational scaffolding and you get fideism. Tom is likely flagging this as a key historical-theological fork in the road.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is concerned with the genealogy of social fragmentation; MacIntyre traces one root to Luther's rejection of Aristotelian moral formation. This annotation marks the moment where RC's narrative of decline begins.

annotations.content: forced is to deny the incomplete receving
annotations.anchor_text: _what is foreshadowed is Luther_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is interpreting MacIntyre's point about Luther: rejecting Aristotelianism forces one to deny the gradual, incomplete but real reception of moral truth through rational enquiry. The word 'forced' is important — Tom sees this not as a free choice but as a logical consequence of the Lutherian move. This is a critical note against voluntarist or fideist traditions.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with tradition as ongoing, incomplete reception of moral truth is directly at stake here. Tom reads the Lutheran move as a rupture in the tradition of moral formation that RC seeks to recover.

annotations.content: an opportunity for showing how the understanding of prudence, justice, temperance, and courage in the light afforded by charity, hope, and faith, and more especially charity, which is the form of all the virtues
annotations.anchor_text: _is an opportunity for showing how the understanding_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this dense phrase about the theological virtues illuminating the cardinal virtues, with charity as the 'form of all the virtues.' This is the heart of MacIntyre's account of Aquinas's synthesis. Tom is tracking the architecture of the moral life: Aristotelian virtues are not replaced but re-ordered and elevated by charity.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's vision of civic friendship and moral community cannot be purely Aristotelian — charity, as the form of the virtues, is the binding force. This annotation marks where RC's classical-Christian synthesis must go beyond mere civic republicanism.

annotations.content: So, in the best accounts of the virtues to be given so far, inadequacies are remedied by using the Bible and Augustine to transcend the limitations not only of Aristotle but also of Plato
annotations.anchor_text: _So, in the best accounts of the virtues_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's cumulative argument: each tradition (Aristotle, Plato, Augustine) is partial, and the 'best accounts' are those that consciously use earlier resources to transcend earlier limitations. Tom is tracking the method of tradition-as-argument, not tradition-as-museum.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is central to RC's methodological stance: recovering civility is not antiquarianism but an active, critical engagement with tradition that transcends earlier inadequacies. MacIntyre's method here is the model RC wants to deploy.

annotations.content: (or, shows the inadequacy of Arist + Plato... (or encounter with)
annotations.anchor_text: _transcend the limitations not only of Aristotle but also of Plato_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is paraphrasing MacIntyre's point in two alternative framings — either we 'show inadequacy' or we 'encounter' something that exceeds classical philosophy. The parenthetical alternatives suggest Tom is testing which framing is more accurate or useful: is it a critique of Aristotle/Plato, or is it a positive encounter with something (Scripture, grace, God) that reveals their limits from outside?
annotations.rc_relevance: RC must decide whether to frame its recovery of tradition as exposing the inadequacy of Enlightenment alternatives or as a positive encounter with a richer tradition. Tom's alternatives here rehearse that same strategic question.

annotations.content: Two features
annotations.anchor_text: _Two features of that detailed treatment are of crucial importance_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'Two features' to mark a structural hinge in MacIntyre's argument — the text is about to enumerate two specific claims about Aquinas's account. Circling structural markers is Tom's way of keeping track of the argument's skeleton for later retrieval.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC writing will need to reproduce this structure — identifying the two features of Thomistic natural law enquiry that distinguish it from modern natural law theory is essential to RC's critique of liberal moral discourse.

annotations.content: we can fully understand and obey the natural law without any knowledge of God
annotations.anchor_text: _Modern Catholic protagonists of theories of natural law_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the claim Tom is identifying as the target of MacIntyre's critique: modern natural law theorists who sever moral knowledge from knowledge of God. This is a contested contemporary position (associated with figures like Grisez, Finnis) that MacIntyre is arguing against via Aquinas.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC must navigate this debate: can a pluralist civic project appeal to natural law without theological grounding? Tom is flagging the exact fault line. RC cannot simply adopt Finnis-style natural law without engaging this critique.

annotations.content: But God is an essential progres. of adero morelik
annotations.anchor_text: _But according to Aquinas all the moral precepts of the Old Law_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is noting (in abbreviated form) that for Aquinas, knowledge of God is not an add-on but an essential feature ('essential progres.') of moral progress. The word 'adero' may be a personal shorthand or a partial word (possibly 'adequate' or 'a developed'); 'morelik' likely abbreviates 'moral life' or 'morality.' Tom is pushing back against the modern natural law position underlined above.
annotations.rc_relevance: This annotation directly supports RC's argument that civic friendship and moral community cannot be constructed on a purely secular basis — the theological dimension is constitutive, not decorative.

annotations.content: A knowledge of God is, on Aquinas's view, available to us from the outset of our moral enquiry and plays a crucial part in our progress in that enquiry
annotations.anchor_text: _A knowledge of God is, on Aquinas's view, available_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as the positive thesis that answers the modern natural law position he underlined earlier. This is MacIntyre's strongest statement of Aquinas's position: knowledge of God is not the conclusion of moral enquiry but its precondition and companion from the beginning.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this means any recovery of civility that ignores God-talk is working with an artificially truncated moral framework. Tom is marking the theoretical warrant for RC's resistance to purely secular civic discourse.

annotations.content: the unifying framework within which our understanding of ourselves, of each other, and of our shared environment progresses
annotations.anchor_text: _the unifying framework within which our understanding_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines 'unifying framework' — a key structural concept in MacIntyre's argument. This framework is what makes moral enquiry coherent across the self, social relations, and the natural world. It is not merely epistemological but ontological: the framework is grounded in the first cause.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's project of resurrecting civility needs exactly such a unifying framework — one that integrates self-understanding, civic relations, and shared environment. Tom is marking MacIntyre's provision of the philosophical architecture RC needs.

annotations.content: by tracing the sequences of final, formal, efficient, and material causality, always refers us back to a unified first cause from which flows all that is good and all that is true in what we encounter
annotations.anchor_text: _by tracing the sequences of final, formal, efficient_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the fourfold causal analysis and its theological terminus — the first cause. This is MacIntyre's Thomistic metaphysics in compressed form: all four Aristotelian causes converge on God as their source. Tom is tracking the metaphysical backbone of the argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's appeal to tradition must ultimately ground out in a metaphysics of the good — not just historical argument. This annotation marks where MacIntyre supplies that grounding, which RC will need to engage or adapt.

annotations.content: (e.) love, revealed in the going down of both Jesus + Socrates (Rep VII)
annotations.anchor_text: _from which flows all that is good and all that is true_
annotations.interpretation: Tom draws a remarkable parallel: the 'first cause from which flows all good' is identified with love, and that love is revealed paradigmatically in two 'descents' — Jesus's incarnation/death and Socrates's descent in Republic Book VII (the philosopher returning to the cave). Tom is connecting MacIntyre's metaphysics of the good with Platonic and Christian kenosis. This is a synthetic, cross-traditional move that goes beyond MacIntyre's own text.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is one of the most RC-significant annotations on the page: it suggests that the 'unifying framework' MacIntyre describes is love, and that RC's recovery of civility must be grounded in a tradition of self-giving (kenotic) love that spans Platonic and Christian sources. Civic friendship, for RC, cannot be merely contractual — it must be ordered toward this kind of love.

annotations.content: This unifying framework -- integrally for our many selves; internally, the same -- is love friendship, the coupling constant, Healthy persons are loving communities, in loving Communities as the caring persons
annotations.anchor_text: _So in articulating the natural law itself in understanding the peculiar character_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes an extended synthesis at the bottom of the page, identifying MacIntyre's 'unifying framework' with love and friendship — described as the 'coupling constant' (a physics metaphor for the force that binds a system). The note insists this framework is integral across the 'many selves' (individual, social) and is internally consistent. The final claim — 'Healthy persons are loving communities, in loving Communities as the caring persons' — is a bidirectional formula: healthy persons make loving communities, and loving communities make healthy (caring) persons.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is Tom's most explicit RC synthesis on the page: the unifying framework RC seeks is love-as-friendship (Aristotelian philia elevated by charity), which functions as the binding force of civic life. The 'coupling constant' metaphor is striking — Tom may intend it as a rhetorical or explanatory device in RC writing to make the classical-Christian synthesis legible to contemporary readers.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that Aquinas's moral philosophy is irreducibly political: the conflicts of moral life are bound up with conflicts of competing jurisdictions (university, episcopal, royal, imperial, papal), and it was through these concrete historical conflicts that universal moral truths found their highly particular embodiments. The individual moral life continues and extends the tradition that formed it, appropriating teachings from a variety of pasts, so that the rationality of individual moral enquiry is the rationality embodied in and transmitted through tradition. The Summa itself is a definitive theoretical statement of what that tradition has reached so far, with its sequences portraying possibilities awaiting embodiment in particular persons, circumstances, times, and places.

annotations.content: why Lo, not just Ko? Lo completes the coupling, applies the framework, fixes the guidestars, configures the person through the information processing = rationality + algorithm for action
annotations.anchor_text: _Aquinas and the Rationality of Tradition_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is asking why MacIntyre focuses on Lonergan ('Lo') rather than or in addition to Korsgaard ('Ko') as the contemporary interlocutor for this account of rationality and tradition. He is working out a functional schema: Lo provides the cognitive-operational architecture (information processing, algorithm) while Ko provides the normative-reflective layer. This suggests Tom is building a two-philosopher scaffold and testing which thinker 'completes' MacIntyre's framework for forming persons within tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry: Tom is asking which contemporary philosopher best operationalizes MacIntyre's tradition-constituted rationality into a model of character formation and civic agency — a core RC design question.

annotations.content: The history of Israel and the church, as members of communities with secular political histories
annotations.anchor_text: _history of Israel and the church as members_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the dual-membership claim — sacred and secular community histories intersecting — as a key structural point. This is the passage establishing that moral life is not purely ecclesial or purely civic but always lives at the intersection, which is crucial for MacIntyre's political dimension argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly maps to RC's subsidiarity and institutional formation themes: the person is always already embedded in overlapping communities (sacred and secular) whose histories and conflicts shape moral character.

annotations.content: a second crucial feature of Aquinas's detailed treatment of the moral life is its political dimension
annotations.anchor_text: _second crucial feature of Aquinas's detailed treatment_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has highlighted what MacIntyre explicitly labels a 'second crucial feature,' signaling this as a numbered structural point in the argument. The political dimension of moral life — not merely its theological or metaphysical dimension — is being foregrounded, likely because Tom sees this as MacIntyre's strongest counter to purely individualist ethics.
annotations.rc_relevance: Central to RC's civic friendship and institutional character-formation themes: if moral life is inherently political, then resurrecting civility requires attending to the political structures and conflicts within which virtue is practiced and contested.

annotations.content: Circled number '2' in left margin
annotations.anchor_text: _second crucial feature of Aquinas's detailed treatment_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is numbering the structural features MacIntyre identifies in Aquinas's moral account. This '2' marks the political dimension as the second key feature, suggesting he has already marked a '1' elsewhere (likely the narrative/teleological structure). This is an organizational move to map MacIntyre's argument architecture for later retrieval.
annotations.rc_relevance: Tom's structural mapping supports RC's framework-building: knowing that the political dimension is MacIntyre's second feature helps position it within a ranked account of what Thomistic moral enquiry requires for contemporary recovery.

annotations.content: tories of sacred and secular communities intersect at key points, in part because of the internal structures of both types of communities, the conflicts of the moral life are on occasion bound up with the conflicts of competing jurisdictions
annotations.anchor_text: _internal structures of both types of communities conflicts of the moral life_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the causal chain: overlapping community structures → jurisdictional conflicts → moral conflicts. This is MacIntyre's empirical-historical claim that moral life is not abstract but embedded in institutional power struggles. Tom likely finds this both compelling and useful for RC's argument that fragmentation is not merely cultural but structural.
annotations.rc_relevance: This passage is foundational for RC's diagnosis of contemporary social fragmentation: competing jurisdictions (now including regulatory agencies, social media platforms, and market institutions) generate the same kind of moral conflict MacIntyre locates in Aquinas's Paris, requiring analogous craft-skills of practical reason.

annotations.content: the individual moral life continues and extends that tradition which provided it with its initial context for appropriating and extending teachings out of a variety of pasts
annotations.anchor_text: _individual moral life continues and extends that tradition_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's thesis that the individual moral life is not self-originating but is a continuation and extension of tradition — and crucially, that this involves appropriating from *multiple* pasts. This is an anti-parochialism point within tradition-constituted enquiry: tradition is not monolithic but a dynamic synthesis.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's 'tradition vs. reform' tension: the individual can extend and reform tradition without abandoning it, which gives RC a principled basis for recovering classical civility while adapting it to contemporary pluralism.

annotations.content: noncogx
annotations.anchor_text: _enquiries of the individual moral life are continuous with those of past tradition_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes 'noncogx' — likely shorthand for 'non-cognitive' or a related term, possibly flagging a contrast with noncognitivist metaethics (Ayer, Stevenson, Hare). He may be noting that MacIntyre's account of tradition-rationality is being positioned against the noncognitivist claim that moral judgments are not truth-apt. The 'x' may mean 'crossed out' or 'excluded' — i.e., MacIntyre is ruling out noncognitivism here.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry requires a cognitivist metaethics — that virtues and civic practices can be rationally justified, not merely expressed. Tom's flag suggests he is tracking where MacIntyre establishes the epistemological ground for RC's normative claims.

annotations.content: embodied in
annotations.anchor_text: _rationality of that life is the rationality both embodied in_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'embodied in' separately from 'transmitted through,' which is also circled, suggesting he is distinguishing two modes by which tradition carries rationality: embodiment (in practices, persons, institutions) versus transmission (through texts, teaching, narrative). This is a fine-grained analytical distinction that matters for how RC thinks about passing on civic virtue.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC's institutional character-formation theme, the embodiment/transmission distinction is crucial: it asks whether civility is recovered primarily through lived practice (embodiment) or through pedagogy and cultural memory (transmission) — or both simultaneously.

annotations.content: transmitted through
annotations.anchor_text: _rationality both embodied in and transmitted through tradition_
annotations.interpretation: Paired with the circle on 'embodied in,' this circle isolates the transmission vector of tradition-rationality. Tom is analytically decomposing MacIntyre's compressed phrase into two distinct processes, likely for use in RC's framework where he needs to specify mechanisms of cultural-moral inheritance.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with how classical traditions of civility can be recovered today turns precisely on this: what are the transmissive vehicles (texts, institutions, rituals, friendships) and what are the embodying practices that make rationality live in persons rather than merely in archives?

annotations.content: quences portray are a set of possibilities awaiting further embodiment in particular persons, circumstances, times, and places
annotations.anchor_text: _possibilities awaiting further embodiment in particular persons_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's claim that the Summa's sequences are not a closed system but an open set of possibilities awaiting particulars. This is a key anti-scholastic-rigidity point: the tradition is generative, not merely archival. Tom likely sees this as MacIntyre's answer to the charge that Thomism is anachronistic.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the philosophical hinge for RC's entire project: if the tradition portrays possibilities rather than fixed prescriptions, then recovering Thomistic civility is not antiquarianism but a creative, context-sensitive act — precisely what RC proposes to do.

annotations.content: how to live out the Summa?
annotations.anchor_text: _possibilities awaiting further embodiment in particular persons circumstances times and places_
annotations.interpretation: Tom poses the practical question that MacIntyre raises but does not fully answer: how does one actually embody the Summa's possibilities in concrete life? This is Tom's most direct RC project question — he is marking the gap between MacIntyre's theoretical account and the practical-pastoral question of formation. His note reflects frustration or productive dissatisfaction with MacIntyre stopping at theory.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the generative question for RC: the project is precisely an attempt to answer 'how to live out the Summa' in contemporary civic contexts, translating MacIntyre's tradition-rationality into actionable frameworks for civic character formation and institutional design.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the individual human being is a unity whose spiritual and social existence must be ordered hierarchically into a unified mode of life, yet each role (biological unit, family member, citizen, monk, etc.) carries its own importance and generates genuine tensions. The practical problems of integrating the self are the direct counterpart to the practical problems of competing jurisdictions in political community, and the virtues required for a well-integrated self are identical to those required for a well-integrated polity. Aquinas himself is offered as a living exemplar of this unity: his single-minded rejection of political entanglement and insistence on his intellectual vocation underwrote the quality Dante most admired — discrezione — the practical wisdom to make right moral and intellectual distinctions.

annotations.content: roles —
annotations.anchor_text: _individual qua family member, the individual qua_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging MacIntyre's enumeration of the multiple social roles that constitute a person's identity — biological unit, family member, citizen, subject, religious. The dash suggests he is holding the thought open, possibly intending to extend the list or connect it to another text. He may be tracking how role-plurality is not reducible to liberal individualism but is instead a structured hierarchy of obligations.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly maps onto RC's subsidiarity theme: the self is not an atomic liberal individual but a nested set of role-obligations across overlapping communities, and civility requires navigating — not collapsing — those layers.

annotations.content: tensions
annotations.anchor_text: _Hence arises a variety of tensions, and the practical problems_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is naming what he sees as the central phenomenological problem MacIntyre is raising: not merely abstract jurisdictional conflict but the lived tension of holding multiple social identities together across time. The addition of 'histories' is Tom's own extension — he is noting that each role carries a narrative history, not just a present duty, and that integrating the self means integrating those histories too.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's concern with social fragmentation: modern fragmentation is not just institutional but intra-personal — people lack the narrative and moral resources to unify their multiple role-identities, which is precisely what a tradition-constituted community is supposed to supply.

annotations.content: the practical problems of the integrity of the self are the counterpart to the practical problems of competing jurisdictions
annotations.anchor_text: _integrity of the self are the counterpart to the practical_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is underlining MacIntyre's structural homology between personal integration and political integration — the argument that self-governance and community governance are not separate problems but mirror images of each other. This is a key load-bearing claim in MacIntyre's account of why virtue ethics is inherently political.
annotations.rc_relevance: This homology is foundational to RC's argument that civic friendship and institutional character-formation are not merely political goods but are constitutive of personal moral development — you cannot have the one without the other.

annotations.content: The virtues which conjointly inform the actions of an integrated self are also the virtues of a well-integrated political community
annotations.anchor_text: _virtues which conjointly inform the ac- tions of an integrated_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is underlining the explicit thesis statement that bridges personal and civic virtue. This is the sentence that most directly states MacIntyre's claim about the unity of ethics and politics in Thomistic moral enquiry. The underline signals this is a quotable, citable claim.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this sentence is the philosophical grounding for the project's core claim: that recovering civility is not a matter of manners but of virtue, and that virtue is irreducibly both personal and civic — which is why the Enlightenment's separation of private morality from public politics was so corrosive.

annotations.content: like
annotations.anchor_text: _Aquinas himself seems to have negotiated these problems with a rare singleness of purpose_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is drawing an explicit parallel between Aquinas's singleness of purpose and Socrates — likely the Socrates of Plato's Republic who famously subordinates all other concerns to philosophy and justice. The 'Rep →' notation suggests he is cross-referencing a passage in the Republic (possibly the philosopher-king who refuses political power, or the account of the just soul in Books IV/IX). This is a substantive interpretive move: Tom sees Aquinas's refusal of the archbishopric as structurally analogous to Socratic philosophical withdrawal from conventional political ambition.
annotations.rc_relevance: This cross-reference raises a tension RC must engage: is the model of civic virtue one of engaged political participation, or of principled withdrawal that paradoxically produces greater civic fruit? MacIntyre's Aquinas and Plato's Socrates both model the latter, which complicates RC's emphasis on institutional engagement.

annotations.content: the first lay medieval university
annotations.anchor_text: _the University of Naples, the first lay medieval university_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging the institutional context of Aquinas's formation — Naples as a lay, not ecclesiastical, university founded by a politically controversial emperor. This detail matters because it places Aquinas at the intersection of competing authorities (papal vs. imperial, sacred vs. secular) from the very start of his intellectual life.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC's theme of institutions forming character, the University of Naples is a case study in how institutional context shapes the thinker — and how Aquinas's discrezione was forged precisely by navigating, rather than simply inheriting, a tradition.

annotations.content: a family deeply involved in the Italian conflicts between the papacy and Frederick II
annotations.anchor_text: _he was a member of a family deeply involved in the Italian_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is marking the biographical grounding for the political tensions MacIntyre is analyzing. Aquinas's family background embeds him in the very jurisdictional conflicts (papal vs. imperial authority) that the theoretical section of the page describes. Tom likely sees this as MacIntyre's deliberate move to show that Aquinas's philosophy was not abstract but emerged from lived political complexity.
annotations.rc_relevance: This supports RC's argument against the Enlightenment view of moral philosophy as context-free: Aquinas's moral and political thought was shaped by and responsive to specific historical conflicts — which is exactly what MacIntyre means by tradition-constituted enquiry.

annotations.content: he always rejected any political role
annotations.anchor_text: _It was therefore notable that he always rejected any political role_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is highlighting MacIntyre's interpretive emphasis on Aquinas's consistent refusal of institutional power. The word 'always' is key — this is not occasional reluctance but a principled, repeated pattern. Tom may be flagging this as evidence for the 'singleness of purpose' thesis and also as a counterintuitive claim: the most politically influential medieval thinker systematically refused political office.
annotations.rc_relevance: This creates a productive tension for RC: the recovery of civic virtue may require precisely the kind of persons who refuse to instrumentalize it for career or power — a point that has implications for RC's own positioning within contemporary institutions.

annotations.content: this single-minded insistence on the intellectual character of his vocation underlay the quality for which Dante most admired Aquinas, his discrezione, his ability to make the right moral and intellectual distinctions
annotations.anchor_text: _this single-minded insistence on the intellectual char- acter of his vocation_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is underlining the culminating evaluative claim of the Aquinas biographical section: that discrezione — practical wisdom in making moral and intellectual distinctions — is the fruit of integrated, vocation-focused selfhood. The underline encompasses both the causal claim (single-mindedness underwrites discrezione) and Dante's external validation of it.
annotations.rc_relevance: Discrezione as the civic virtue par excellence is central to RC: it is not rule-following but the cultivated capacity for judgment, which is exactly what fragmented, procedure-dependent modern civic culture lacks and what RC argues must be recovered through tradition and character formation.

annotations.content: Prudentia
annotations.anchor_text: _his discrezione, his ability to make the right moral and intellectual distinctions_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is glossing Dante's Italian term discrezione with its Latin Thomistic equivalent, prudentia — practical wisdom. This is a scholarly translational note: Tom wants to anchor Dante's poetic vocabulary in Aquinas's own philosophical terminology, preventing discrezione from being read as mere cleverness or tact rather than the cardinal virtue of prudence.
annotations.rc_relevance: The identification of discrezione with prudentia is crucial for RC: prudence (phronesis/prudentia) is the master virtue of the tradition MacIntyre is recovering, and its loss is precisely what RC diagnoses as the core deficit of contemporary civic culture — replaced by proceduralism, metrics, and rule-following.

annotations.content: he constructed poetically and philosophically from the Augustinian and Aristotelian traditions a synthesis that was genuinely his own
annotations.anchor_text: _he constructed poetically and philosophically from the Augustinian_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is underlining MacIntyre's characterization of Dante's creative synthesis as 'genuinely his own' — distinguishing authentic tradition-inheritance from mere repetition. This is MacIntyre's implicit argument that tradition-constituted rationality is not conservative mimicry but involves genuine creative appropriation.
annotations.rc_relevance: This speaks directly to RC's challenge: how to recover Thomistic and classical traditions without becoming antiquarian or nostalgic. MacIntyre's Dante models the answer — genuine ownership of a tradition involves transformation, not just transmission.

annotations.content: Dantes
annotations.anchor_text: _from the Augustinian and Aristotelian traditions a synthesis that was genuinely his own_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is labeling the precise intellectual-historical claim: Dante's synthesis is specifically Augustinian-Aristotelian, not simply Thomistic. This note differentiates Dante's project from Aquinas's while also explaining their convergence — both are working within the same dual inheritance. The abbreviations suggest Tom has used this shorthand before, indicating this Aug/Arist synthesis is a recurring analytical category in his reading.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, the Aug/Arist synthesis is the intellectual backbone of the tradition MacIntyre is defending against both the Enlightenment encyclopaedist and the Nietzschean genealogist — and understanding that Dante independently arrived at a version of it strengthens the case that this tradition has genuine intellectual vitality beyond any single thinker.

annotations.content: the vices of those who inhabit the Inferno, the virtues of those in the Paradiso, and the virtues and vices of those engaged in the transformations of the Purgatorio do indeed together particularize the accounts of the virtues and vices in the Ia–IIae and IIa–IIae of the Summa
annotations.anchor_text: _vices of those who inhabit the Inferno, the virtues of those_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is underlining MacIntyre's specific structural claim about how Dante's Commedia functions as a poetic particularization of Aquinas's Summa — with the three canticles mapping onto the moral psychology of the Summa's second part. This is a bold interpretive thesis and Tom is marking it as a key claim to either develop or test against the texts.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this mapping is important because it suggests that the recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry does not require dry philosophical treatises — it can be embodied in narrative and imaginative literature, which has direct implications for RC's own communicative strategy and audience.

annotations.content: Psychological
annotations.anchor_text: _and the practical problems of the integrity of the self_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is adding 'Psychological' as a gloss above or near 'practical problems of the integrity of the self,' flagging that MacIntyre's language here has a psychological as well as a philosophical dimension. Tom wants to distinguish this from purely political or metaphysical discourse — the self's integration problem is also an interior, psychological struggle, not just a normative question.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC must address the psychological as well as the institutional dimensions of civic fragmentation: it is not enough to reform institutions if persons lack the interior resources to inhabit them well, which is exactly the point MacIntyre is making about the virtues needed for integrated selfhood.

annotations.content: wondering, righteous, creative, calculating, witty, competing, empathetic... reasonable, kind, just, ferocious, attractive selves... seems more only about how? P; I'm soul, is it not? through — only community — network (HIFN)
annotations.anchor_text: _individual human being is a unity in whom the directedness_
annotations.interpretation: This is a dense top-margin note where Tom is generating his own list of human qualities or role-attributes — a kind of brainstorm about what constitutes the multiplicity of the self that MacIntyre wants to unify. The question 'seems more only about how?' suggests Tom is probing whether MacIntyre's account is merely procedural (how to integrate) rather than substantive (what to integrate toward). 'P; I'm soul, is it not?' appears to be an internal address — possibly to a person (P?) or a self-reminder that the tradition's answer to integration is the soul's orientation toward God. 'HIFN' is likely an abbreviation for a text, concept, or interlocutor Tom is tracking across his reading — possibly 'Human Is a Fundamentally Networked' being or a specific text abbreviation.
annotations.rc_relevance: This top-margin note captures Tom working out RC's central problem: how to articulate the richness of human social identity (the list of qualities) without reducing it to either liberal individualism or communitarian collectivism — the 'community-network' formulation suggests Tom is reaching for a model that is neither.

pages.summary: MacIntyre uses Dante's placement of Brunetto Latini and Emperor Frederick II in Hell to illustrate Aquinas's thesis that the badness of any part of a self entails the badness of the whole: doing good deeds is perfectly compatible with a perverse fundamental choice of self, and hell is simply the persistence in that defection. He then contrasts Nietzsche's praise of Frederick II's ruthless self-affirmation with Dante and Aquinas's condemnation of the very same disposition, arguing that what counts as achievement of the good versus emasculation depends entirely on which larger narrative framework — the Commedia's scriptural vision or Nietzsche's genealogical one — one's individual story is embedded within.

annotations.content: Drewfees illustrates to whd nor + metappoint of godhood
annotations.anchor_text: _to be read as its partial counterpart_
annotations.interpretation: Tom appears to be cross-referencing a thinker or text ('Drewfees' may be a personal shorthand or a proper name — possibly 'Dreyfus' as in Hubert Dreyfus) who illustrates the same point about the self's metaphysical orientation toward a kind of self-apotheosis or 'godhood.' The phrase 'metappoint of godhood' suggests Tom is tracking the theme of self-deification as the ultimate form of the perverse fundamental choice MacIntyre is describing — the self that chooses itself as its own final end. This connects the Thomistic notion of disordered will to a broader phenomenological or existentialist framing Tom may be developing.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly illuminates the RC theme of how Enlightenment and post-Enlightenment moral frameworks substitute the autonomous self for God as the telos of action — a core MacIntyrean critique Tom is apparently mapping onto a specific contemporary interlocutor.

annotations.content: inforces is both Aquinas's thesis that the badness and failure of any one part of a self entails the badness of the whole and his thesis that there is no badness of any part which is not the redeemable corruption or distortion of some good
annotations.anchor_text: _What that particularization reinforces is both Aquinas's thesis_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has underlined the double thesis — holism of the self's moral condition and the redeemability of all badness as distorted good — which is the metaphysical backbone of the whole Dante discussion. This is a foundational Thomistic claim: evil has no independent ontological status, it is always privation of good. Tom underlines it likely because it is the hinge on which the Frederick II contrast will turn, and because it has direct normative implications for how one diagnoses social fragmentation.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is central to RC's recovery of Thomistic moral anthropology: social fragmentation and civic vice are not simply 'bad' in a thin sense but are privations of a determinate good — civic friendship and ordered community — which means they are in principle redeemable through recovery of tradition.

annotations.content: The answer is clearly given by Aquinas: the doing of many good deeds is perfectly compatible with the perverse choosing of something in oneself which is defect and error and affirming it as what one intends unalterably to be
annotations.anchor_text: _The answer is clearly given by Aquinas_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this because it is MacIntyre's sharpest formulation of the compatibility between outward moral achievement and inward disordered will — the Brunetto Latini paradox. This is not merely a point about Dante's cosmology; it is a claim about the insufficiency of deontological or consequentialist accounts of virtue that focus only on actions rather than the fundamental orientation of the self. Tom likely sees this as a key diagnostic tool for understanding figures who perform civic goods while being fundamentally disordered.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is concerned with institutions that form character, not merely regulate behavior — this passage gives the Thomistic warrant for insisting that civic performance without ordered selfhood is insufficient, and that tradition must form the inner orientation of persons, not just their external conduct.

annotations.content: And it is this choice which is one's own choice of exclusion from the community of the perfected. So hell is persistence in defection from the integrity both of a self and of its communities.
annotations.anchor_text: _hell is persistence in defection from the integrity_
annotations.interpretation: The left-margin bracket with what appears to be an arrow or chevron mark signals that Tom considers this definition of hell — as persistent self-exclusion from community — to be a key structural or definitional moment in the argument. The bracketing of 'community of the perfected' alongside 'integrity both of a self and of its communities' suggests Tom is noting the inseparability of personal integrity and communal belonging in Aquinas/MacIntyre's framework. Hell is not punishment imposed from outside but the logical extension of one's own choice of self over community.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's theme of civic friendship and subsidiarity: the disintegration of community is not merely sociological but moral-ontological, and the recovery of civility requires addressing the inner dispositions that produce self-chosen exclusion from ordered community.

annotations.content: What Nietzsche praised in each of them was what he perceived as a ruthless affirmation of the self and its powers; what Dante saw as Frederick's self-condemnation was, so it seems, the very same affirmation
annotations.anchor_text: _What Nietzsche praised in each of them_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the exact moment of the Nietzsche/Dante inversion — same phenomenon, diametrically opposed moral evaluations — because this is MacIntyre's clearest demonstration of rival incommensurable frameworks producing contradictory verdicts on the same data. Tom is tracking this as the genealogist/tradition opposition made concrete and biographical rather than abstract. The underline suggests he wants to use Frederick II as a paradigm case in his own writing.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the RC project's central tension made vivid: MacIntyre's tripartite structure (encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition) is not merely theoretical — it produces real, irreconcilable differences in how we evaluate historical and contemporary figures. RC must grapple with which framework it inhabits when assessing civic virtue.

annotations.content: And is there then a single history of the world within which all other stories find their place and from which the significance of each subordinate story derives?
annotations.anchor_text: _is there then a single history of the world_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this rhetorical question because it is MacIntyre's pivot to the metanarrative question — the question of whether any tradition can claim comprehensive narrative authority. This is the moment where the argument moves from the Dante/Nietzsche comparison to the deepest philosophical stakes: can there be a single story within which all human stories are intelligible? Tom likely sees this as the crux of the tradition vs. genealogy debate and the foundation for the Commedia's claim to authority.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's entire project depends on whether a recoverable tradition can provide the kind of comprehensive narrative framework MacIntyre is describing here — if not, civility becomes merely local and provisional; if yes, there is a principled basis for the recovery of civic order.

annotations.content: tell me your story and I will show you that it only becomes intelligible within the framework provided by the Commedia, or rather within some framework provided by that scriptural vision which the Commedia
annotations.anchor_text: _tell me your story and I will show you_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines Dante's challenge — 'tell me your story' — because it is MacIntyre's formulation of how tradition-based enquiry engages rivals: not by abstract argument but by showing that the rival's own narrative only becomes coherent within the tradition's framework. This is an assertive, almost polemical claim about the Thomistic/scriptural tradition's comprehensiveness. Tom may be thinking about how RC can issue a similar challenge to contemporary secular frameworks of civility.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is RC's methodological challenge: can the tradition of civility grounded in Thomistic moral enquiry show contemporary interlocutors that their own fragmented civic stories become intelligible only within a richer framework? This passage gives Tom the rhetorical and philosophical template for that move.

annotations.content: passional inclusive narrative
annotations.anchor_text: _tell me your story and I will show you_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes 'passional inclusive narrative' in the left margin alongside Dante's challenge, suggesting he is categorizing Dante's approach as a kind of narrative that is both passionate/embodied ('passional') and comprehensive ('inclusive'). This may be Tom's own theoretical vocabulary being applied to MacIntyre — he is perhaps distinguishing the Commedia's mode of argument (narrative, affective, all-encompassing) from purely propositional or analytical modes. The phrase may also gesture toward the emotional and communal dimensions of tradition-bearing narratives.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with institutions forming character requires attention to the affective and narrative dimensions of civic formation — Tom's phrase 'passional inclusive narrative' points toward a mode of civic pedagogy that is story-based and emotionally engaging, not merely rule-following, which is a key implication of the Thomistic account MacIntyre is developing.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that from a Thomistic standpoint, rational justification operates at four distinct levels depending on the interlocutor's epistemic and moral formation: (1) the genuinely uninstructed person asking 'What is my good?', (2) the person already sharing the Aristotelian-Thomistic larger scheme who debates within a common framework, and (3) the antagonist who systematically rejects first principles, creating incommensurability. The chapter uses Nietzsche's admiration for Dante to show how a Nietzschean reading inverts Dante's moral intentions, which sets up the question of what Thomistic resources can resist such a genealogical appropriation. The four-levels framework is MacIntyre's structural answer to the challenge of rational justification across rival traditions.
