annotations.content: 4 levels: (1) become the person + [something] the person (2) compare [?] [antagonists?] to [?] from [?] (3) engage w/ [rival?] on multiple [?] [less than?] their [?] (4) [illegible] on multiple [?] [illegible]
annotations.anchor_text: _questions of rational justification may arise_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is pre-mapping all four levels of rational justification before reading the enumeration in the text — a structural preview note. The handwriting is partially illegible but Tom is clearly building a schematic of the argument's architecture. This suggests he is reading MacIntyre analytically, tracking the logical skeleton so he can apply it outside the text. The fact that he numbered them at the top before encountering all four in the body indicates he either already knew the argument or was reading ahead.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly maps to RC's concern with how moral argument must be calibrated to the interlocutor's formation — a key subsidiarity insight: you meet people where they are in their moral development, not where you wish they were.

annotations.content: N supplies what Vmeta-narrative [?]
annotations.anchor_text: _Nietzsche greatly admired Dante while viewing_
annotations.interpretation: Tom appears to be noting that Nietzsche supplies a meta-narrative or counter-narrative function — possibly shorthand for 'Nietzsche supplies what a [Thomistic] meta-narrative cannot do genealogically.' The abbreviation 'Vmeta-narrative' may mean 'via meta-narrative' or 'versus meta-narrative,' flagging that the genealogical move is itself a kind of narrative counter-claim. This is Tom tracking the epistemological stakes of Nietzsche's appropriation of Dante.
annotations.rc_relevance: In RC terms, this touches the encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition tripartite: Tom is noting that the genealogist (Nietzsche) doesn't merely reject tradition's narratives but replaces them with his own, which is precisely the threat MacIntyre is diagnosing.

annotations.content: 5 Post [?] [illegible — possibly 'Postcritique' or '5 Postulates']
annotations.anchor_text: _yet Nietzsche greatly admired Dante_
annotations.interpretation: This circled number '5' with partial text likely marks a section or sub-argument number in Tom's own outline of the chapter, or possibly a cross-reference to a fifth point in a series he is tracking across the book. The word after '5' is ambiguous but may relate to 'postulates' or 'postcritique,' suggesting Tom is charting MacIntyre's argumentative moves as numbered propositions.
annotations.rc_relevance: Tom's practice of numbering MacIntyre's argumentative moves reflects his RC project's need to extract a usable framework for contemporary civic discourse — he is essentially creating a deployable schematic of Thomistic apologetics.

annotations.content: from a Thomistic standpoint
annotations.anchor_text: _recognize that from a Thomistic standpoint as I have_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the explicit standpoint claim — MacIntyre is not pretending to view from nowhere, but from within a tradition. This is a philosophically loaded moment: the tradition-constituted nature of rationality is being enacted, not just argued. Tom likely highlights this as a key methodological marker.
annotations.rc_relevance: Central to RC: MacIntyre's rejection of Enlightenment view-from-nowhere rationalism. Highlighting 'from a Thomistic standpoint' signals Tom's awareness that all rational justification is tradition-indexed — a core RC thesis about why civility must be recovered through tradition, not abstract procedure.

annotations.content: questions of rational justification may arise at four different levels
annotations.anchor_text: _questions of rational justification may arise at four_
annotations.interpretation: This is the thesis sentence for the entire structural section that follows. Tom highlights it as the load-bearing claim of the page. The four-levels framework is MacIntyre's answer to the Nietzschean challenge, and Tom is marking it as such — this is not merely descriptive but is the architectonic move of the chapter.
annotations.rc_relevance: The four-levels structure is directly applicable to RC's practical program: any project of resurrecting civility must know which level of moral interlocution it is operating at. Highlighting this is Tom identifying a usable analytical tool.

annotations.content: 4 levels of [?] rational [?] [?] 1 common [?] of [?] [?] [?] [?] at [?] intuition [?]
annotations.anchor_text: _questions of rational justification may arise at four_
annotations.interpretation: This margin note appears to be Tom's own compressed gloss on level 1 — the uninstructed plain person operating from bare moral intuitions. The phrase 'common [?] at intuition' suggests Tom is noting that the first level of justification begins with pre-theoretical moral apprehensions, which aligns with the text's description. This is an interpretive compression of the argument into Tom's own vocabulary.
annotations.rc_relevance: Level 1 in MacIntyre's schema maps to RC's concern with moral formation: the uninstructed person needs a teacher, a community, an institution — this is exactly the subsidiarity and character-formation theme that drives RC.

annotations.content: that of the genuinely uninstructed plain person
annotations.anchor_text: _There is first of all that of the genuinely_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the formal designation of Level 1 interlocutor. The word 'genuinely' is significant — MacIntyre means someone not yet formed by any tradition's moral grammar, not merely someone who hasn't been taught Thomism. Tom is likely flagging this as the starting condition for moral pedagogy.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'genuinely uninstructed plain person' is a type RC must address — contemporary citizens who have been stripped of traditioned moral formation by liberal proceduralism. This is the RC project's primary audience.

annotations.content: 2 apologists [?] of [?] nature w/i same common framework
annotations.anchor_text: _secondly the person who shares that larger scheme_
annotations.interpretation: Tom labels Level 2 as 'apologists' — those who debate within a shared framework. His gloss 'w/i same common framework' precisely captures MacIntyre's point about shared first principles and natural enquiry. The word 'apologists' is interesting: it suggests Tom sees this level as the domain of classical apologetics — reasoned defense within a common rational vocabulary.
annotations.rc_relevance: Level 2 corresponds to RC's interest in civic friendship and shared moral vocabulary — the condition MacIntyre says made Aquinas's debates with Islamic and Jewish thinkers possible. This is the RC ideal of substantive civic discourse.

annotations.content: son who shares that larger scheme
annotations.anchor_text: _There is secondly the person who shares that larger scheme_
annotations.interpretation: Underline of the Level 2 subject designation, parallel to the Level 1 underline above. Tom is systematically marking each level's defining subject. 'Shares that larger scheme' is the key differentiator from Level 1 — this person is already within a tradition's moral framework.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'shared larger scheme' is the condition RC seeks to recover — a common enough moral framework to make civic argument possible. Its absence is precisely the contemporary crisis RC addresses.

annotations.content: so that demands for rational justification are framed in terms of a shared understanding of natural enquiry and a shared conception of first principles
annotations.anchor_text: _demands for rational justification are framed in terms_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the epistemological conditions for Level 2 debate. The shared conception of first principles is Thomistic natural law — this is MacIntyre saying Aquinas could debate Averroists because they shared enough Aristotelian framework. Tom is marking this as the positive case for tradition-internal rational justification.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the RC thesis about what civility requires: not merely procedural agreement but substantive shared first principles. Tom is marking the conditions of possibility for the kind of civic discourse RC wants to resurrect.

annotations.content: It was from within this kind of agreement that Aquinas conducted his debate with some rival Islamic, Jewish, and Latin Averroist positions
annotations.anchor_text: _Aquinas conducted his debate with some rival Islamic_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the historical exemplification of Level 2 debate. MacIntyre uses Aquinas's actual historical practice as evidence for the framework. Tom marks this because it is the concrete instantiation of the abstract claim — it shows that Thomistic rational engagement across difference is historically possible, not merely theoretically.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is significant for RC: the Thomistic tradition has actual historical precedent for substantive cross-traditional dialogue. Tom is collecting evidence that civility-through-tradition is not nostalgic fantasy but demonstrated historical practice.

annotations.content: interested in [?] 3 detected [?] [?]
annotations.anchor_text: _tween antagonists each of whom systematically rejects_
annotations.interpretation: Tom labels Level 3 as involving detection of some kind — possibly 'detected incommensurability' or 'detected rejection.' The word 'interested in' before the number suggests Tom is flagging this as the level most relevant to his project. Level 3 is where antagonists systematically reject each other's first principles — the hardest case for rational justification, and the one most relevant to contemporary pluralism.
annotations.rc_relevance: Level 3 is the condition RC faces in contemporary culture: systematic rejection of shared first principles. Tom flagging interest here suggests RC must develop strategies for this hardest level of engagement, not just preach to the already-convinced.

annotations.content: In this third type of debate, characterized by some large degree of incommensurability
annotations.anchor_text: _In this third type of debate characterized by some large_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the formal designation of Level 3 and its defining characteristic — incommensurability. This is the crux: when interlocutors share no common first principles, what form can rational justification take? The highlight signals this as the theoretical problem MacIntyre is building toward, and the one most urgent for contemporary application.
annotations.rc_relevance: Incommensurability is the central challenge RC must confront: contemporary moral discourse often operates at Level 3, where participants not only disagree but lack shared criteria for resolving disagreement. RC's program must address whether and how Thomistic resources can operate at this level.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that Aquinas's synthesis can be shown rationally superior to prior traditions by demonstrating that it can explain the limitations and errors of rival views in their own terms, while incorporating what is cogent within them. A key historical mistake of nineteenth- and early twentieth-century Thomism was treating the task of rational justification against Cartesian, Humean, and Kantian adversaries as a 'second-type' problem (shared first principles) rather than a 'third-type' problem (tradition-constituted rationality from incommensurable starting points). Against Nietzschean opponents especially, this error is compounded because Nietzsche reveals how a philosophical tradition can be so institutionally organized that its adherents become systematically blind to their own errors.

annotations.content: nice overview
annotations.anchor_text: _rational superiority of Aquinas's philosophical and theological_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging this passage as a clean, usable summary of MacIntyre's core thesis about tradition-constituted rational superiority. The word 'nice' suggests pedagogical or presentational value — this is likely a passage Tom would excerpt for RC audiences or lectures. He's marking it as a moment where MacIntyre states his argument with unusual clarity.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly maps to the RC project's engagement with MacIntyre's tripartite structure: the claim that traditions can be shown superior through internal critique is foundational to RC's argument that Thomistic moral enquiry offers a genuine alternative to Enlightenment fragmentation.

annotations.content: see w/ 360 [number/reference]
annotations.anchor_text: _superiority of Aquinas's philosophical and theological synthesis_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is cross-referencing this passage with page 360, likely another location in the book or a related text where this argument about tradition-constituted rational superiority is developed further or contrasted. The 'see with' phrasing indicates he wants to read these passages in conjunction, suggesting he detected a structural echo or tension between the two locations.
annotations.rc_relevance: Cross-referencing within MacIntyre's text is consistent with RC's methodology of tracing the internal logic of the tradition/encyclopaedia/genealogy tripartite structure across the whole argument, particularly how the 'third type' of rational justification unfolds.

annotations.content: precise and detailed terms what it is about the opposing view which engenders just these particular limitations, defects, and errors and also what it is about that view which must deprive it of the resources required for understanding, overcoming, and correcting them.
annotations.anchor_text: _precise and detailed terms what it is about_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is highlighting the precise formulation of what 'tradition-constituted rational superiority' actually requires: not just pointing out errors in rivals, but explaining WHY those errors are structurally necessary given the rival tradition's own commitments. This is the hardest and most specific criterion MacIntyre sets, and Tom recognizes it as the crux.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is central to RC's argument that recovering Thomistic civility is not mere nostalgia but a genuine explanatory advance — it must show why modern liberal frameworks systematically produce civic fragmentation rather than merely asserting it.

annotations.content: A mistake of much nineteenth- and early twentieth-century Thomism was to suppose that the task of rational justification against their Cartesian, Humean, or Kantian adversaries was of the second, rather than of this third type.
annotations.anchor_text: _mistake of much nineteenth- and early twentieth-century Thomism_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's self-critical moment within the Thomistic tradition — acknowledging that Thomists themselves made a strategic error by conceding shared rational ground to Enlightenment opponents. This is a crucial admission because it shows the tradition is capable of self-correction, which is itself a marker of a living tradition vs. a dead one. Tom likely sees this as directly relevant to how RC must position itself differently from neo-scholastic apologetics.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC must avoid repeating this mistake: it cannot argue for civic virtue and subsidiarity on Enlightenment liberal grounds (e.g., utility or rights-language alone) but must operate from within the tradition's own standards of rational enquiry.

annotations.content: ies why you need people like me
annotations.anchor_text: _mistake of much nineteenth- and early twentieth-century Thomism_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is making a self-referential, possibly sardonic note positioning himself as the kind of interpreter who understands this third-type methodology and can apply it correctly — unlike the nineteenth-century Thomists who made the strategic error MacIntyre describes. This suggests Tom sees RC as a project that consciously corrects this historical mistake by engaging adversaries on tradition-constituted rather than neutral rationalist grounds.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is a direct statement of Tom's self-understanding of RC's mission: to be the kind of practitioner who knows which 'type' of rational engagement is required when challenging contemporary liberal or postmodern frameworks on civility and moral community.

annotations.content: (people who think like me)
annotations.anchor_text: _mistake of much nineteenth- and early twentieth-century Thomism_
annotations.interpretation: A clarifying gloss on the note above — Tom is specifying that 'people like me' means a community of like-minded interpreters, not just himself individually. This could reflect awareness that RC requires a community of enquiry, not a lone scholar, consistent with MacIntyre's own emphasis on tradition as communal rather than individual.
annotations.rc_relevance: Connects to RC's theme of civic friendship and subsidiarity: the recovery of Thomistic enquiry requires forming communities of practice, not just producing academic arguments.

annotations.content: "let me just explain to you do a deeper dive take narrating"
annotations.anchor_text: _Yet against Nietzschean opponents it would not be enough to recognize this error_
annotations.interpretation: Tom appears to be drafting a pedagogical or rhetorical move for RC presentations — noting that simply identifying the mistake is insufficient; one must narrate the deeper structural account of why the error occurred. The quoted format suggests this is either a phrase he uses when presenting or a reminder to himself to shift rhetorical register from assertion to explanation when engaging skeptical audiences.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's communication strategy: MacIntyre's point that recognizing error is insufficient against Nietzschean critics maps onto RC's challenge of engaging contemporary audiences who suspect all tradition-talk is power-masking.

annotations.content: Yet against Nietzschean opponents it would not be enough to recognize this error.
annotations.anchor_text: _Yet against Nietzschean opponents it would not be enough_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as a pivot sentence — MacIntyre escalates the difficulty of the task when facing genealogical (Nietzschean) opponents versus rationalist ones. The underlining marks a structural turn in the argument that Tom wants to be able to locate quickly. It also signals that Tom sees the Nietzschean challenge as the hardest and most relevant for RC's contemporary context.
annotations.rc_relevance: The genealogist challenge is one of RC's three rival frameworks; Tom's mark here shows he is tracking precisely where MacIntyre distinguishes the Nietzschean problem as categorically harder than the Kantian one — essential for RC's engagement with postmodern critiques of virtue ethics.

annotations.content: a philosophical or theological position may be so organized, both in its intellectual structures and in its institutionalized modes of presentation and enquiry that conversation with an opposing position may reveal that its adherents are systematically unable to recognize in it even those errors, defects, and limitations which ought to be recognized as such in the light of their own standards.
annotations.anchor_text: _philosophical or theological position may be so organized_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines Nietzsche's crucial contribution to MacIntyre's argument: the insight that institutional forms of enquiry can produce systematic blindness even by the tradition's own standards. This is the most epistemically disturbing claim on the page — a tradition can be self-undermining without knowing it. Tom likely flags this as both a warning for RC and as the key diagnostic tool for analyzing liberal modernity's failures.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the heart of RC's diagnostic power: modern liberal institutions may be systematically organized to prevent recognition of the civic and moral failures that Thomistic analysis can identify. RC's project is partly about naming this institutional blindness.

annotations.content: aka being (about their own version is not enough & they will)
annotations.anchor_text: _systematically unable to recognize in it even those errors_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is paraphrasing MacIntyre's point in his own words: being 'about their own version' (i.e., internal self-consistency) is not sufficient to avoid systematic error. The trailing 'they will' is ambiguous but likely anticipates a consequence — perhaps that they will nonetheless claim rational authority, or that they will be shown up by tradition-constituted critique. This is a working note for how to translate MacIntyre's technical point into accessible RC language.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's engagement with contemporary secularism and liberal civic discourse requires exactly this move: showing that these frameworks cannot self-correct on the question of social fragmentation because their institutional forms prevent them from recognizing it as a failure by their own standards.

pages.summary: MacIntyre on page 148 (visible header) is outlining what is at stake in the confrontation between the three rival traditions — encyclopaedist, genealogist, and Thomistic — arguing that the intellectual issues dividing Nietzschean genealogy from the Thomistic tradition cannot be resolved by simply bracketing questions about ideological distortions and errors. The page appears to be setting up the conditions under which genuine rational enquiry across traditions is possible, specifically what rhetorical and argumentative resources would need to be deployed. MacIntyre is pushing toward the claim that the Thomistic tradition has a structural advantage because it can account for the failures of rival traditions from within its own framework.

annotations.rc_relevance: Central to RC's engagement with MacIntyre: RC needs MacIntyre's framework but also needs to identify where it underdetermines practical solutions to fragmentation, particularly around rhetoric and persuasion.

annotations.rc_relevance: The '5th level' concept could become an RC theoretical contribution: the rhetorical-persuasive dimension of tradition-to-tradition encounter that MacIntyre undertheorizes.

't-plural' likely means 'tradition-plural' — arriving at consensus across multiple traditions. 'Run the experiment' is a striking pragmatist injunction, suggesting Tom thinks MacIntyre's framework should be tested empirically or enacted institutionally, not just theorized. annotations.rc_relevance: This is the heart of RC's ambition: not just diagnosing fragmentation (MacIntyre's strength) but running the experiment of actually achieving cross-tradition consensus — which is what RC as a project is attempting.

annotations.anchor_text: _but this 5th level is the contention point_
annotations.interpretation: The second pink highlight marks Tom's own positive thesis statement — the idea that unified enquiry is the goal. He is highlighting his own marginal writing, which is unusual and signals that this phrase has become a candidate for RC's mission statement or a key organizing concept.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly maps to RC's goal of recovering a shared framework for moral enquiry across traditions — subsidiarity, civic friendship, and Thomistic rationality all converge here.

annotations.content: Exactly Plato's task in the Republic (not enough to have an explanation of blindness; you need a cure, or you don't have the track record [spec?] of the philosophical storytella[r] in Rep X.
annotations.anchor_text: _arrive at a stable consensus across t-plural exploration_
annotations.interpretation: Tom draws a parallel between MacIntyre's project and Plato's in the Republic: both must do more than diagnose moral blindness, they must effect a cure. 'Philosophical storyteller in Republic X' likely refers to the myth of Er or the function of the philosopher-king as one who has lived the full arc of moral education. 'Track record' is a pragmatic criterion — the tradition must demonstrate results, not just coherence. This is Tom applying a performative or phronetic standard to theoretical philosophy.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery of civility must be more than diagnostic — it must be therapeutic and formative. This connects to RC's theme of institutions forming character: the tradition must produce virtuous citizens, not just correct arguments.

annotations.content: • ?154 will['s] independence from intell[ect] only in failure/sin
annotations.anchor_text: _ideological distortions which enter into moral enquiry_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is cross-referencing page 154, anticipating or recalling MacIntyre's discussion of the will's independence from intellect — a Thomistic-Augustinian point that the will becomes disordered and autonomous from reason only through sin or moral failure. This is likely a note about akrasia or the fall's epistemological consequences. Tom flags this as a question mark, suggesting he is uncertain whether MacIntyre makes this argument or whether Tom is supplying it himself.
annotations.rc_relevance: Connects directly to RC's engagement with Thomistic moral psychology: the fragmentation of modern moral discourse may be traced not just to philosophical error but to the disordering of the will — making cultural renewal, not just argument, necessary.

annotations.content: 2 differences w/ [unclear: tradition?] rhetoric
annotations.anchor_text: _intellectual issues which divide Nietzschean genealogy_
annotations.interpretation: A brief note in the top-right margin signaling that Tom is tracking two specific differences — likely between MacIntyre's account and Tom's own view, or between two traditions' uses of rhetoric. The annotation is partially obscured and abbreviated, making full transcription uncertain, but it reinforces Tom's running concern with the rhetorical dimension.
annotations.rc_relevance: Tracking distinctions in how traditions use rhetoric is essential for RC's practical program of cross-tradition dialogue and the recovery of civic friendship.

chapters.title: In the Aftermath of Defeated Tradition

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that with Aquinas, the unified tradition of systematic philosophical enquiry reaching from Plato through the Islamic commentators achieves its highest point and then largely disappears from view — Thomistic commentary becomes a marginal, eclectic activity rather than a sustained cooperative project. He anticipates the objection that he is imposing a retrospective unity on what was in fact a heterogeneous, fragmented history of enquiry, and responds that all successful enquiry is necessarily written retrospectively — the history of physics provides the analogy. A tradition of enquiry characteristically bears within itself an always-open-to-revision history of itself.

annotations.content: lets make commentary central - how else can you show systematic mastery?
annotations.anchor_text: _plicit that the concept of progress in enquiry_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is pushing back on — or extending — MacIntyre's lament about commentary becoming marginal. He seems to be arguing that commentary is not a symptom of decline but is in fact the proper vehicle for demonstrating systematic mastery of a tradition. This may be a pedagogical or institutional observation: if commentary is central, then the Thomistic decline MacIntyre describes is really a failure of institutional form, not of intellectual content.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to the RC theme of tradition vs. reform and the role of institutions in forming character — if commentary is the mechanism by which a tradition transmits and tests itself, then its marginalization signals an institutional failure that RC must address.

annotations.content: go with Natural Sciences
annotations.anchor_text: _So it is in the history of the modern natural sciences_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging MacIntyre's analogy to natural science as a move worth following or exploiting. The bracketed orange arrow suggests Tom sees this as a structural pivot in the argument — MacIntyre is about to legitimate retrospective history-writing by appeal to how physics writes its own history, and Tom wants to track that move carefully.
annotations.rc_relevance: Relevant to the encyclopaedist strand of MacIntyre's tripartite structure — the encyclopaedist privileges natural science as the model of rational enquiry, so MacIntyre's co-opting of that analogy is a dialectically significant moment for RC's engagement with Enlightenment rationalism.

annotations.content: So it is in the history of the modern natural sciences
annotations.anchor_text: _So it is in the history of the modern natural sciences_
annotations.interpretation: The orange highlight marks the opening of MacIntyre's key analogy claim. Tom treats this sentence as a thesis-level statement rather than mere transition — he wants to be able to return to the exact moment MacIntyre stakes the claim that tradition-based enquiry mirrors natural-scientific self-understanding.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the hinge on which MacIntyre defends tradition-constituted rationality against the encyclopaedist charge of arbitrariness — central to RC's engagement with the question of whether Thomistic moral enquiry can claim rational authority in a pluralist public.

annotations.content: astonishing is that with Aquinas those histories terminate
annotations.anchor_text: _astonishing is that with Aquinas those histories terminate_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's strong, almost polemical word 'astonishing' — this is a claim designed to shock the reader into recognizing the magnitude of what was lost. The highlighting suggests Tom agrees with the diagnosis or at minimum finds it rhetorically and argumentatively decisive for what follows.
annotations.rc_relevance: The termination of unified systematic enquiry with Aquinas is the historical wound that RC implicitly seeks to address — the recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry is impossible without first acknowledging this rupture.

annotations.content: and tradition in philosophy, at its point of highest achievement, largely disappears from view
annotations.anchor_text: _tradition in philosophy at its point of highest achievement largely disappears_
annotations.interpretation: This is one of MacIntyre's most striking claims on the page and Tom marks the whole clause. The irony — that a tradition disappears precisely at its peak — is philosophically important: it is not failure that kills the tradition but perhaps its very success, which left no further obvious cooperative project. Tom may be interrogating whether this is a structural feature of traditions generally.
annotations.rc_relevance: The disappearance of tradition at its apex is a central RC puzzle — how do you revive what was not defeated but simply abandoned? This bears directly on RC's question of whether Thomistic civic and moral frameworks can be institutionally re-embedded.

annotations.content: death of Thomistic enquiry
annotations.anchor_text: _became a marginal activity in an increasingly fragmented_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is naming the phenomenon MacIntyre describes: this is the 'death of Thomistic enquiry' as a living tradition. The label is a shorthand for what he sees as the central historical claim of this section, and it signals he will use this phrase as a reference point — possibly in RC writing or in conversation.
annotations.rc_relevance: Names the historical problem RC must respond to — if Thomistic enquiry died institutionally, the RC project is implicitly a project of resurrecting it, which raises the question of whether revival is possible or whether a new tradition must be constructed.

annotations.content: of enquiry as long-term cooperative activity in the construction of a systematic overall understanding of theory and practice no longer dominated
annotations.anchor_text: _of enquiry as long-term cooperative activity in the construction_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets the definition of genuine enquiry: long-term, cooperative, systematically oriented toward theory and practice together. This is a key definitional passage for MacIntyre and Tom is isolating it — likely because the RC project itself needs a working definition of what counts as a tradition of enquiry worth reviving.
annotations.rc_relevance: This definition of enquiry as cooperative and long-term is foundational for RC's conception of civic friendship and subsidiarity — genuine moral community requires exactly this kind of sustained, shared intellectual project, not merely individual virtue.

annotations.content: it will be retorted
annotations.anchor_text: _To this it will be retorted and in part rightly_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the adversarial structure of the argument — MacIntyre is staging a dialectical exchange. The underline signals Tom is tracking the logical architecture: thesis, objection, rebuttal. This is important for anyone reading MacIntyre's argument as a model of how tradition-based enquiry defends itself.
annotations.rc_relevance: The willingness to entertain and partially concede the objection ('in part rightly') is a model of the kind of intellectually honest engagement RC needs to practice when confronting critiques of its Thomistic commitments.

annotations.content: I am imposing retrospectively
annotations.anchor_text: _I am imposing retrospectively on what was in fact_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the self-critical moment where MacIntyre concedes the force of the objection. The orange highlight marks this as a hinge — MacIntyre admits the charge has prima facie plausibility before deflecting it. Tom may be noting either the intellectual honesty of the move or the vulnerability it exposes in MacIntyre's historical narrative.
annotations.rc_relevance: The charge of retrospective imposition is one RC will face — any project that claims to recover a tradition risks being accused of constructing rather than recovering it. MacIntyre's response here is a template RC can use.

annotations.content: es is to know to itself
annotations.anchor_text: _wie es eigentlich gewesen_
annotations.interpretation: This appears to be Tom's loose rendering or gloss of the German 'wie es eigentlich gewesen' (how it actually was — Ranke's famous historiographical phrase). The note seems to be Tom working out the translation or significance: history 'knows itself' through retrospective narration. This is a philosophically loaded moment — MacIntyre is invoking and then subverting the Rankean ideal of objective history.
annotations.rc_relevance: The tension between objectivist historiography (Ranke) and tradition-constituted retrospective history is central to RC's epistemological commitments — RC cannot appeal to a 'view from nowhere' but must defend its retrospective framing of classical tradition.

annotations.content: history of all successful enquiry is and cannot but be written retrospectively
annotations.anchor_text: _history of all successful enquiry is and cannot but be written retrospectively_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's direct answer to the objection — the retrospective character of history-writing is not a distortion but a necessity of all successful enquiry. This is the epistemological core of the page. Tom likely sees this as a key quotable claim for RC writing.
annotations.rc_relevance: This claim licenses RC's own retrospective recovery of classical and Thomistic traditions — it is not anachronism but the proper method of any living tradition of enquiry. This directly addresses potential critics of RC's historical methodology.

annotations.content: necessarily retrospective histo[ry] of successful inquiry
annotations.anchor_text: _history of all successful enquiry is and cannot but be written retrospectively_
annotations.interpretation: Tom restates MacIntyre's thesis in his own words at the bottom of the page, compressing it into a formulaic phrase. This is a note-to-self that doubles as a conceptual label he can deploy in writing or lecture. The phrasing 'necessarily retrospective' is stronger than MacIntyre's formulation and shows Tom sharpening the claim.
annotations.rc_relevance: This formulation could become a RC methodological principle — the project's recovery of tradition is not nostalgic but epistemologically required by the nature of successful enquiry itself. This is a powerful rebuttal to critics who dismiss RC as backward-looking.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that there is no 'single neutral nonpartisan history' of philosophy — the very shape of philosophical history depends on which tradition's standpoint one occupies, making rival histories genuinely incommensurable. He illustrates this with Aquinas: his account of Aquinas as the culmination of Augustinian and Aristotelian traditions is not how Aquinas was understood by his contemporaries, who found him eccentric and at odds with thirteenth-century orthodoxy. The remarkable historical fact, MacIntyre insists, is not Aquinas's condemnation in 1277 but his repeated revival and eventual canonization despite institutional resistance.

annotations.content: how the history of philosophy written will depend in key part upon what are taken to be its achievements, what its frustrations and failures
annotations.anchor_text: _What follows from this is that_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is marking MacIntyre's core epistemological claim that historiography is always already shaped by normative commitments about success and failure. This is not a minor methodological note — it is the anti-encyclopaedist thesis in miniature. Tom likely sees this as the hinge between Part I of the book and the constructive argument that follows, flagging it as a claim he will need to engage directly in RC.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly bears on the RC project's concern with how traditions legitimate themselves: which 'achievements' count as achievements is itself a tradition-internal judgment, which means civility and civic friendship cannot be reconstructed from a neutral standpoint.

annotations.content: there will be rival, incompatible, and sometimes incommensurable histories of philosophy
annotations.anchor_text: _merely different, but incompatible ways, as they do_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the triple intensifier — rival, incompatible, incommensurable — suggesting he is tracking the precise logical gradient MacIntyre is building. 'Incommensurable' is the strongest claim and the most contested; Tom may be noting where MacIntyre crosses from pluralism into a stronger Kuhnian/Gadamerian position that has significant consequences for the RC project's appeal to shared civic norms.
annotations.rc_relevance: If traditions are genuinely incommensurable, the RC project's aspiration to recover civility as a cross-traditional resource faces a fundamental challenge — how do you appeal across traditions without assuming the neutral standpoint MacIntyre has just demolished?

annotations.content: The notion of a single neutral nonpartisan history is one more illusion engendered by the academic standpoint of the encyclopaedist
annotations.anchor_text: _histories of philosophy. The notion of a single_
annotations.interpretation: This is one of MacIntyre's most polemical formulations and Tom has singled it out for emphasis. The word 'illusion' is doing heavy philosophical work — MacIntyre is not just saying neutral history is difficult, but that it is a structural fantasy produced by a specific institutional and intellectual posture (the encyclopaedist). Tom is likely tracking MacIntyre's use of 'encyclopaedist' as a term of art throughout the book.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's MacIntyre strand depends on this critique: Enlightenment-style civic education projects that claim neutrality are, on this account, covertly partisan — a point with direct implications for how RC frames its own normative commitments.

annotations.content: the illusion that there is the past waiting to be discovered, wie es eigentlich gewesen, independent of characterization from some particular standpoint
annotations.anchor_text: _it is the illusion that there is_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has underlined MacIntyre's invocation of Ranke's famous phrase ('wie es eigentlich gewesen' — 'as it actually was'), which MacIntyre weaponizes against positivist historiography. The German phrase is left in the original, signaling MacIntyre's awareness that this is the motto of a whole tradition of supposedly objective historical method. Tom is likely noting this as a key polemical move against historicism-without-tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery project is vulnerable to the same Rankean objection — if one claims to be recovering 'authentic' classical civility, one must account for the standpoint-dependence MacIntyre identifies here. This annotation suggests Tom is alert to this self-referential risk.

annotations.content: a particular kind of progress in enquiry terminates, not entirely but to a surprising degree, with Aquinas, can only be made from the standpoint of one such partisan history. But to adopt that standpoint is not to obscure the fact that my account of Aquinas's work as the culmination and integration of the Augustinian and Aristotelian traditions is not at all how Aquinas was understood by much the greater part of both his contemporaries and his immediate successors.
annotations.anchor_text: _asserting that a particular kind of progress_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets this entire passage, which is MacIntyre's most self-aware moment on the page — the acknowledgment that his own account of Aquinas is itself a partisan reading, not the view of Aquinas's contemporaries. The bracket likely signals Tom recognizing a structurally important concession: MacIntyre is not claiming God's-eye objectivity even for his own preferred tradition. This is the key move that distinguishes tradition-constituted rationality from relativism.
annotations.rc_relevance: This passage is directly relevant to RC's navigation between the Scylla of relativism and the Charybdis of false universalism: MacIntyre is modeling how to make strong tradition-internal claims without pretending to neutrality — exactly the posture RC needs to articulate.

annotations.content: msuctte angelic doctor
annotations.anchor_text: _not at all how Aquinas was understood by much_
annotations.interpretation: Tom's handwritten gloss 'angelic doctor' is Aquinas's honorary ecclesiastical title (Doctor Angelicus), and 'msuctte' is likely an abbreviation or misspelling of 'misunderstood' or possibly 'misconstrued.' He is flagging the irony MacIntyre identifies: the Angelic Doctor — the Church's eventually canonized champion — was at the time of his writing regarded as heterodox by the mainstream. Tom is noting the gap between posthumous reputation and contemporary reception.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to the RC theme of how institutions form and deform character: the very institution that later canonized Aquinas initially resisted him. This has direct implications for RC's thinking about institutional reform versus tradition preservation.

annotations.content: not at all how Aquinas was understood by much the greater part of both his contemporaries and his immediate successors
annotations.anchor_text: _work as the culmination and integration_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this to flag the historical revisionism at the heart of MacIntyre's project: the Aquinas we inherit is a retrospective construction. This is not incidental — it is MacIntyre's evidence that even within a tradition, the 'canonical' reading is the product of later selective recovery, not transparent transmission. Tom is likely tracking this as a methodological point about how traditions work.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this raises the question of which Aquinas is being recovered — the thirteenth-century heterodox thinker or the post-canonization scholastic synthesis. MacIntyre's answer (both, understood in their tension) is the model RC would want to emulate for recovering civility traditions.

annotations.content: Aquinas was eccentric to, and in variety of ways at odds with, the dominant and orthodox mainstream of institutionalized thirteenth-century enquiries and even more with their fourteenth-century continuations
annotations.anchor_text: _Aquinas was eccentric to, and in variety_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's strong claim about Aquinas's marginality within his own institutional context. The word 'eccentric' (off-center) and the phrase 'even more' with fourteenth-century continuations suggests MacIntyre sees Aquinas's influence as operating against the grain of the dominant scholastic establishment. Tom is likely noting this as evidence for MacIntyre's broader thesis that the most generative intellectual moves happen at the margins of institutional orthodoxy.
annotations.rc_relevance: This speaks directly to the RC theme of subsidiarity and the tension between institutional formation and genuine intellectual/moral innovation — institutions can suppress the very tradition they are meant to carry.

annotations.content: c. Hoebl 7 1274
annotations.anchor_text: _condemnation of 1277, nor the rejection by_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes what appears to be a citation or cross-reference: possibly 'c. Hoebl 7 1274,' suggesting a secondary source (perhaps a scholar named Hoebl or a chapter/page reference) and the date 1274 (the year of Aquinas's death). The proximity to the mention of the 1277 condemnation suggests Tom is tracking the chronology: Aquinas died in 1274, the condemnation came three years later. He may be noting that the condemnation was partly posthumous and therefore particularly revealing about institutional dynamics.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 1277 condemnation is a case study in how institutions resist the thinkers who will later become their defining authorities — directly relevant to RC's analysis of how moral traditions survive institutional opposition.

annotations.content: canonization
annotations.anchor_text: _led to his canonization_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the single word 'canonization' — Aquinas was canonized in 1323. The orange highlight (distinct from the blue underlining used elsewhere) suggests this is a key data point Tom wants to return to, possibly as the culminating historical fact of the paragraph's argument. Canonization is both a theological and institutional act, and MacIntyre is using it to show how a tradition retrospectively claims a figure it initially marginalized.
annotations.rc_relevance: Canonization as an institutional act of retrospective recovery is a powerful analogy for RC's own project: recovering traditions of civility involves a similar selective elevation of figures and texts that were marginal in their own time.

annotations.anchor_text: _led to his canonization_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has written and highlighted (in orange) the specific date of Aquinas's canonization — July 18, 1323. This is a researcher's precision move: pinning the abstract claim ('canonization') to a verifiable historical date. The bright orange highlight distinguishes this as factual reference material rather than an interpretive note, suggesting Tom plans to cite this date in his own writing.
annotations.rc_relevance: The specificity of this date suggests Tom is building a historical timeline for RC, likely to situate the Thomistic revival within the broader narrative of institutional recognition and tradition formation.

annotations.content: Doctor 1567
annotations.anchor_text: _led to his canonization_
annotations.interpretation: Tom notes '1567' alongside 'Doctor,' which almost certainly refers to Aquinas being declared a Doctor of the Church by Pope Pius V in 1567 — a further stage of institutional elevation beyond the 1323 canonization. Tom is tracking the layered process by which Aquinas moved from condemned eccentric to supreme ecclesiastical authority, which is precisely the arc MacIntyre is describing. The two dates together (1323, 1567) give Tom the full temporal span of this institutional reversal.
annotations.rc_relevance: The two-stage rehabilitation (canonization then Doctor of the Church) illustrates how tradition-formation is not a single event but a cumulative institutional process — relevant to RC's analysis of how civic traditions gain or lose authority over time.

annotations.content: do his ability to accomm[odate] / [harmo]ny th[e] / [two?] (his) / hist tradition / to this ch[urch?]
annotations.anchor_text: _Neither theology nor the subordinate artes liberales_
annotations.interpretation: Tom's partially legible note at the bottom right appears to reflect on Aquinas's capacity to harmonize or accommodate competing intellectual traditions — likely the Augustinian and Aristotelian streams MacIntyre has been discussing. The word 'tradition' and possible reference to 'church' suggest Tom is connecting Aquinas's synthetic genius to his institutional context. The fragmented syntax indicates this is a quick thinking-note rather than a polished gloss.
annotations.rc_relevance: Aquinas's capacity for synthesis across traditions is a model RC explicitly draws on: if civility is to be recovered, it requires the same kind of integrative intelligence that can hold Aristotelian civic friendship and Christian caritas in productive tension.

annotations.content: o. see Battonica: Chenu
annotations.anchor_text: _find room for the Aristotelian system_
annotations.interpretation: Tom's note at the bottom references 'Battonica' (possibly 'Battenhouse' or more likely a misspelling of 'Battonca' — though the most probable referent is M.-D. Chenu, the Dominican historian of medieval theology whose work on Aquinas and the thirteenth century is standard). 'Chenu' is clearly legible and is almost certainly Marie-Dominique Chenu OP, whose 'Toward Understanding Saint Thomas' (1964) is a key secondary source on the intellectual milieu MacIntyre is describing. Tom is flagging a secondary source to consult.
annotations.rc_relevance: Chenu's work on how Aquinas engaged the intellectual resources of his time is a natural complement to MacIntyre's argument — both emphasize that Thomistic synthesis was historically situated, not timeless, which RC must account for in its recovery project.

pages.summary: MacIntyre examines how the medieval reception of Aristotelianism was piecemeal and unsystematic, producing ad hoc revisions rather than coherent synthesis, with Duns Scotus emerging as the most distinguished anti-Thomistic figure. The central contrast developed is between Aquinas and Scotus on the telos of human beings: Aquinas argues that perfect happiness cannot be had in this life and that rational beings naturally seek it elsewhere, while Scotus contends that natural reason can know nothing of any life but this one, and that the imperfections Aquinas identifies as pointing beyond this life are merely the imperfections of embodied, mortal existence. This sets up a deeper theological disagreement about whether an essentially embodied mind — whose natural self-understanding is as mortal — can grasp the soul's immortality or the resurrection without revelation.

annotations.content: already: the disquieting suggestion becomes evident... (An Intro)
annotations.anchor_text: _portions of theory detached from the whole_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is cross-referencing the book's Introduction, where MacIntyre introduces the 'disquieting suggestion' that Enlightenment moral discourse is in a state of disorder precisely because fragments of older traditions have been torn from the wholes that gave them meaning. The note 'already' signals Tom tracking the moment where that thesis first becomes demonstrably visible in the historical argument — the unsystematic, ad hoc reception of Aristotelian theses is the concrete evidence for what was only asserted in the Introduction. The parenthetical '(An Intro)' is a structural cross-reference, not a summary.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly central to the RC project's MacIntyrean foundation: the fragmentation of tradition is not accidental but structural, and the RC project's goal of 'recovering' civility must reckon with the fact that the pieces available to us are already deracinated fragments. Tom is marking the moment the argument becomes actionable.

annotations.content: particular Aristotelian theses, and portions of theory detached from the whole of which they were parts, and this systematically unsystematic reception
annotations.anchor_text: _What then made an impact were particular_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the oxymoron 'systematically unsystematic reception' — MacIntyre's precise formulation for how Aristotelianism entered medieval thought not as a living whole but as a pattern of selective, reactive borrowing. The underlining suggests Tom finds this phrase analytically loaded: it is not mere eclecticism but a structured disorder, which is worse than random fragmentation because it has the appearance of method.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project's concern with tradition vs. reform turns on exactly this distinction: reform that proceeds by extracting useful parts from a tradition without its teleological framework reproduces the same pathology MacIntyre diagnoses here. Tom is flagging the mechanism of tradition-decay.

annotations.content: ad hoc revisions of Augustinian positions
annotations.anchor_text: _in a series of equally ad hoc revisions_
annotations.interpretation: The underlining of 'ad hoc revisions of Augustinian positions' pairs with the previous underline to show the double fragmentation: both the Aristotelian and the Augustinian inheritances are being treated as reservoirs for reactive, situational borrowing. Tom appears to be tracking the symmetry of the decay — it is not just Aristotle being misread but Augustine too, which compounds the crisis.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this matters because the Augustinian tradition is one of the primary sources for civility and moral community; if that tradition was itself already being revised ad hoc by the 14th century, the recovery project faces a deeper archaeological problem than simply returning to Augustine.

annotations.content: happiness is to be had in this life and not in another (Sent. 176). Aquinas had argued against this very proposition, contending that all rational beings seek perfect happiness and that it can be shown rationally that perfect happiness is not to be had in this life but only in some other.
annotations.anchor_text: _happiness is to be had in this life and not in another_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets the Averroist thesis and Aquinas's direct counter-argument together as a unit, treating them as a paired dialectical structure. This is a structural annotation: he is isolating the exact proposition at stake (happiness is only in this life) and Aquinas's two-part response (all rational beings seek perfect happiness; it cannot be found here). The bracketing suggests Tom sees this as a self-contained argumentative node worth isolating for later use or citation.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project's concern with civic friendship and the common good depends on what telos is operative — if happiness is purely immanent, civic life becomes its own end; if it is transcendent, civic institutions are ordered to something beyond themselves. Tom is marking the exact hinge-point of that question.

annotations.content: convinced, as anyone should be without faith in a revelation, of his or her mortality.
annotations.anchor_text: _convinced as anyone should be without faith in a revelation_
annotations.interpretation: This second bracket isolates Scotus's key epistemological claim: that the naturally rational person, without revelation, is rightly convinced only of their mortality — not their immortality. Tom brackets this separately from the larger Scotus passage, suggesting he sees it as the crux of Scotus's position rather than merely part of it. The phrase 'as anyone should be' is Scotus making a normative claim about what natural reason properly yields, and Tom appears to be flagging that this is philosophically provocative.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this connects to the MacIntyrean critique of Enlightenment rationalism: if natural reason, properly conducted, yields only mortality, then any project of civic renewal that relies on natural reason alone is working within Scotus's disenchanted framework. The recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry requires contesting precisely this Scotist limitation on reason.

annotations.content: So Scotus is Given unmasked → I-IIq2a4 precisely
annotations.anchor_text: _Scotus by contrast argues that so far as natural reason_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes 'Given unmasked' — likely shorthand for 'Givenness unmasked' or the Scotist position being exposed as what it truly is: a restriction of reason to the immanent. The Summa reference 'I-IIq2a4' is Aquinas, Summa Theologiae, Prima Secundae, Question 2, Article 4 — which asks whether happiness consists in worldly goods, and argues it does not. Tom is doing live cross-referencing, verifying that the Aquinas position MacIntyre describes is precisely documented at that locus. 'Precisely' confirms the cross-reference checks out.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is Tom building the scholarly apparatus of the RC project in real time — verifying MacIntyre's reading of Aquinas against the primary text. The specific locus I-IIq2a4 would be a key citation for any RC argument about why civic goods cannot be the ultimate human end.

annotations.content: Duns Scotus
annotations.anchor_text: _dieval anti-Thomistic mode was Duns Scotus_
annotations.interpretation: Circling the name at its introduction signals Tom marking Scotus as a named protagonist entering the argument — a common reading practice for tracking when key figures are formally introduced. Given the other annotations, Tom is not merely noting the name but marking this as the point where Scotus's role as the principal anti-Thomistic alternative becomes explicit in MacIntyre's narrative.
annotations.rc_relevance: Scotus represents for MacIntyre (and for RC) the moment when the Thomistic synthesis begins to fracture from within the tradition — his voluntarism and restriction of natural reason are precursors to the Enlightenment rationalism the RC project opposes.

annotations.content: the relationship between the telos of human beings as considered from the standpoint of philosophy and as considered from the standpoint of theology
annotations.anchor_text: _Consider in this light the great differences between Aquinas and Scotus_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the double standpoint formulation — philosophy vs. theology on the telos question — as the structural organizing principle of the Aquinas/Scotus contrast. This is a key MacIntyrean methodological point: the same question (what is the human telos?) receives different answers depending on which rational framework is operative, and Scotus's move is to restrict what philosophy can say, leaving more to theology alone.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry depends on maintaining the integration of philosophy and theology on the question of human ends — precisely what Scotus begins to sunder. Tom is marking the architectonic of the problem.

annotations.content: an essentially embodied mind could not but understand itself as mortal. It is only the soul independent of the body that can be thought of as immortal
annotations.anchor_text: _to show that an essentially embodied mind could not_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the Scotist anthropological claim that shapes his entire epistemology: the mind, qua embodied, knows itself as mortal; only an abstracted, disembodied soul-concept can be thought immortal. This is MacIntyre showing how Scotus's endorsement of the Averroist account of the human being creates a systematic wedge between natural self-knowledge and theological claims about the soul. Tom underlines this as the payoff of the whole page's argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this Scotist anthropology is the root of the problem with modernity's self-understanding: if the naturally known self is essentially mortal and bounded, then civic life and its institutions cannot be ordered to transcendent goods. The RC project's insistence on subsidiarity and virtue-forming institutions implicitly requires contesting this anthropology.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that Aquinas's deep integration of Aristotelian hylomorphism into his account of soul-body unity represents a more radical departure from Augustinianism than Scotus could accept. For Aquinas, a human being is not a soul that merely uses a body but a body that has a soul, making human experience essentially bodily and the mind 'radically incomplete' without sensory encounter with singulars. This Thomistic position provoked institutional Franciscan backlash — de la Mare's 1279 Correctorium and the 1282 prohibition on copying the Summa — setting the stage for Scotus's rival assertion of the soul's independence from bodily experience.

annotations.content: To have recognized that this is what is at stake
annotations.anchor_text: _To have recognized that this is what_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging MacIntyre's meta-argumentative signal — the phrase 'what is at stake' marks the hinge where MacIntyre crystallizes the real philosophical issue beneath the historical dispute. Tom likely notes this as a moment where MacIntyre steps back to name the deeper structure of the disagreement, not just its surface content. This is the kind of framing move Tom would track carefully because it shows MacIntyre doing tradition-archaeology: identifying what the participants themselves could not fully articulate.
annotations.rc_relevance: In the RC project, recognizing 'what is at stake' in tradition conflicts is central to MacIntyre's method of tradition-constituted enquiry. Tom is likely marking this as a model for how RC should frame contemporary civility disputes — what is really at stake beneath surface disagreements about norms.

annotations.content: to recognize the depth of Scotus's disagreement with Aquinas, not only on particular issues, but above all in overall perspective
annotations.anchor_text: _depth of Scotus's disagreement with Aquinas_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this to mark MacIntyre's distinction between local disagreements and deep perspectival disagreements — a distinction central to his entire three-rival-versions framework. This is not a disagreement about a particular conclusion but about the whole framework within which conclusions are reached. Tom is almost certainly tracking this because it exemplifies MacIntyre's claim that tradition-rivals cannot be adjudicated by neutral criteria.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly maps onto the RC theme of the 'encyclopaedist / genealogist / tradition' tripartite structure: the disagreement between Aquinas and Scotus is precisely a clash of overall perspectives, not mere policy disputes. RC must account for this depth of disagreement in contemporary moral fragmentation.

annotations.content: relationship of soul to body, indeed the existence of body, had been something of an embarrassment to later Augustinians
annotations.anchor_text: _existence of body, had been something of an embarrassment_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the striking phrase 'embarrassment' — MacIntyre is making a pointed historical-philosophical claim that Augustinian theology had an unresolved tension with embodiment that it never adequately confronted. Tom likely finds this significant because it shows how a tradition can carry an internal incoherence for centuries. The word 'embarrassment' is doing real philosophical work here, not just rhetorical.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this speaks to the theme of tradition vs. reform: traditions can persist while carrying unresolved internal tensions that only become visible when a rival tradition (Aristotelianism via Aquinas) forces the issue. RC asks whether modern civility traditions carry analogous embarrassments about embodied, communal life.

annotations.content: on this specific point
annotations.anchor_text: _And on this specific point Aquinas's integration_
annotations.interpretation: The underline on 'on this specific point' combined with what appears to be a small 'ZRB' or similar notation inline suggests Tom is pinpointing the precise locus of Franciscan offense — not Aquinas's Aristotelianism generally, but specifically his account of soul-body unity and its epistemological consequences. Tom may be distinguishing this from other Thomistic provocations to track exactly what the institutional rejection was targeting.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to the RC theme of institutions forming (or deforming) character: the Franciscan order's hostility was institutionally enacted (the 1282 prohibition) over a very specific philosophical point about embodied knowing, showing how institutions police the boundaries of acceptable tradition.

annotations.content: For Aquinas a human being is not a soul plus a body but a body which has a soul.
annotations.anchor_text: _a human being is not a soul plus a body_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets this as a definitional crux — this is MacIntyre's most compressed formulation of the Thomistic anthropology that drove the entire controversy. The bracket signals Tom wants to be able to locate this exact formulation again, likely for citation or direct engagement. The inversion ('not soul plus body but body which has soul') is rhetorically and philosophically precise and Tom recognizes it as load-bearing.
annotations.rc_relevance: This formulation is directly relevant to RC's concern with subsidiarity and embodied community: if human experience is essentially bodily and relational, then any account of civil life that treats persons as essentially disembodied rational agents (the Enlightenment encyclopaedist position) is operating with a false anthropology. RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry depends on this very claim.

annotations.content: Human experience is bodily experience
annotations.anchor_text: _Human experience is bodily experience, and the soul_
annotations.interpretation: A separate underline from the bracket above, suggesting Tom treats this as a standalone thesis worthy of independent emphasis — not just as part of the Aquinas-vs-Scotus dispute, but as a positive philosophical claim about the nature of experience. Tom may be flagging this for use outside the immediate historical context, as a foundation for RC's own anthropological commitments.
annotations.rc_relevance: The claim that human experience is essentially bodily is foundational for RC's critique of disembodied liberal individualism and for its positive account of civic friendship as requiring face-to-face, embodied community. MacIntyre via Aquinas here provides RC with philosophical grounding.

annotations.content: incomplete without that encounter with the objects of sense from which it moves to the actuality of knowledge
annotations.anchor_text: _incomplete without that encounter with the objects of sense_
annotations.interpretation: Tom continues to underline the epistemological consequences of Aquinas's hylomorphism — the mind is not merely aided by sense experience but constituted through it. This extends the 'radically incomplete' citation from Boler and Tom wants to capture the full logical chain: incompleteness → encounter with sensibles → actuality of knowledge. He is tracking the argument's structure, not just isolated claims.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this epistemological point has direct civic implications: moral knowledge itself requires encounter with particular persons and communities, not just abstract principles. This supports RC's critique of procedural liberalism and its preference for tradition-embedded practical wisdom.

annotations.content: the mind essentially, and not only accidentally, requires the body for its operations
annotations.anchor_text: _mind essentially, and not only accidentally, requires the body_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the essential/accidental distinction as a precise Aristotelian-Thomistic technical claim. The force of 'essentially' versus 'accidentally' is that this is not a contingent feature of human cognition that might be transcended but a necessary feature of what human minds are. Tom is tracking this as the sharpest edge of the Aquinas-Scotus divide — Scotus would deny the 'essentially' while possibly granting the 'accidentally'.
annotations.rc_relevance: This distinction maps directly onto RC's MacIntyrean theme: the Enlightenment project treats reason's dependence on tradition and community as accidental (correctable by method) while MacIntyre-Aquinas treats it as essential. RC's entire argument for tradition-constituted enquiry rests on this move from accidental to essential dependence.

annotations.content: In 1282 the Franciscan order prohibited the copying of the Summa except as accompanied by the Correctorium
annotations.anchor_text: _Franciscan order prohibited the copying of the Summa_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this institutional fact — the 1282 prohibition — as a vivid example of institutional power being mobilized against a tradition-rival's most important text. This is not just a historical curiosity but a case study in how institutions enforce tradition-boundaries. Tom likely sees this as MacIntyre's concrete evidence that the Aquinas-Scotus dispute was not merely academic but institutionally consequential.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly engages the RC theme of the role of institutions in forming (or suppressing) character and enquiry. The Franciscan prohibition is an instance of institutional subsidiarity working against rather than for the flourishing of intellectual tradition — a cautionary example for RC's account of how institutions should relate to moral enquiry.

annotations.content: Arrow or bracket mark pointing toward the Correctorium/Corruptorium passage at the bottom of the page
annotations.anchor_text: _some younger Dominicans as the Corruptorium_
annotations.interpretation: The right-margin symbol (appears to be a curved bracket or arrow) beside the final paragraph marks the institutional drama of the Correctorium being renamed 'Corruptorium' by Dominicans — a polemical renaming that itself enacts the tradition-rivalry. Tom flags this as rhetorically significant: the naming dispute is a microcosm of the larger tradition conflict, where even vocabulary becomes contested territory.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this is an instance of how tradition-rivals contest the very language used to describe their dispute — relevant to MacIntyre's argument that rival traditions cannot share a neutral meta-vocabulary. RC faces an analogous problem in trying to recover the vocabulary of 'civility' from its contested modern usages.

annotations.content: ZRB
annotations.anchor_text: _on this specific point Aquinas's integration_
annotations.interpretation: These initials appearing inline near 'on this specific point' are ambiguous — they could be a cross-reference abbreviation, a source abbreviation, or a personal notation system Tom uses. 'ZRB' does not obviously correspond to a standard text abbreviation. It may refer to a specific interlocutor, a text, or a section in Tom's own RC notes. The placement near the 'specific point' of Franciscan offense suggests Tom is cross-referencing this moment to another location in his research.
annotations.rc_relevance: If 'ZRB' is a cross-reference, it suggests Tom is building a web of connections across RC materials at precisely the point where institutional rejection of Thomistic embodied anthropology is named — a structurally important moment for RC's argument about tradition and institution.

pages.summary: MacIntyre contrasts Aquinas's Aristotelian account of practical reason — where the will operates dependently on the intellect's apprehension of the genuine good — with the Augustinian tradition's unconditional primacy of will over intellect, a move Scotus inherits and radicalizes. The core argument is that once Scotus rejects Aristotle's psychology of will-intellect integration, the only remaining framework for moral obligation becomes divine command, and the 'ought' of 'one ought to obey God' collapses into the same 'ought' as practical reasoning generally — a conceptual flattening with enormous consequences. MacIntyre is showing how Scotus's voluntarism, born from a specific historical rupture, generates the distinctively modern problem of grounding moral obligation independently of rational apprehension of the good.
