annotations.content: P1 has not locked down Aris's acct out his pivotal pages leaves open why not
annotations.anchor_text: _importance for future history_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is noting that MacIntyre has not yet fully secured his Aristotelian account — the 'pivotal pages' leave the argument partially open, perhaps deliberately, and Tom wants to know why MacIntyre withholds full closure here. This suggests Tom is tracking the rhetorical structure of MacIntyre's exposition: where does he commit versus where does he leave interpretive space? The abbreviation 'Aris's acct' signals Tom is monitoring whether MacIntyre's Aristotle is being fairly or strategically rendered.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this matters because if MacIntyre's Aristotelian account is incompletely defended at a pivot point, the tradition-vs.-reform argument may rest on a foundation Tom needs to either shore up or contest when applying MacIntyre to contemporary civility debates.

annotations.content: Scotus
annotations.anchor_text: _will operates independently of intellect_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is labeling the section's protagonist — Scotus — as a kind of running header or anchor for what follows. This is a structural annotation, ensuring he can quickly relocate where Scotus's position enters the argument. It may also signal that Tom sees this as 'Scotus's move' being diagnosed, not endorsed.
annotations.rc_relevance: Scotus's voluntarism is the hinge between Thomistic integrated practical reason and the later Enlightenment fragmentation MacIntyre critiques; identifying this entry point is essential for RC's account of how civic virtue traditions were disrupted.

annotations.content: will operates independently only when and insofar as someone is not pursuing his or her genuine good
annotations.anchor_text: _will operates independently only when_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this as the Thomistic thesis that will's independence from intellect is a marker of defect, not freedom. This is the crux of Aquinas's anti-voluntarist psychology: autonomy of will signals disorder. Tom likely flags this because it is the exact claim Scotus will invert, and the contrast is architecturally central to MacIntyre's chapter.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly bears on RC's concern with character formation and institutions: if the will properly ordered is intellect-guided, then institutions that cultivate practical wisdom are doing the work of integrating will and intellect — the Thomistic alternative to mere rule-following.

annotations.content: what the will is returned to is an order in which the primacy belongs to the practical intelligence
annotations.anchor_text: _primacy belongs to the practical intelligence_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this as MacIntyre's positive formulation of the Augustinian-Thomistic synthesis: right order returns primacy to practical intellect, not will. This is a definitional claim Tom clearly wants to retain as a touchstone. The double-highlighting of both this and the previous passage suggests Tom is marking the full Thomistic position before watching it be dismantled by Scotus.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, the primacy of practical intelligence over will is the foundation for MacIntyre's claim that virtue traditions — not arbitrary commands or procedures — are the proper locus of moral formation and civic life.

annotations.content: The Augustinian tradition however had assigned an unconditional primacy to will and Scotus's rejection of Aristotle's psychology deprived him of the only available alternative way of conceiving the relationship of will to intellect. This had a consequence of the first importance for future history.
annotations.anchor_text: _consequence of the first importance for future history_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets this entire passage as a hinge-claim — MacIntyre's explicit signal that Scotus's move is historically catastrophic, not merely philosophically wrong. The phrase 'first importance for future history' is MacIntyre at his most dramatic, and Tom's bracket marks this as a claim he wants to either deploy or scrutinize. It also brackets a compressed causal argument: Augustinian voluntarism + Scotus's anti-Aristotelianism = no available integrated psychology of will-intellect.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the genealogical rupture RC must account for: the moment when the tradition loses its internal resources for integrating will and reason, opening the door to the Enlightenment 'encyclopaedist' alternatives MacIntyre opposes throughout the book.

annotations.content: simplicity w/ Hartians nihilatio primacy in the decay
annotations.anchor_text: _primacy belongs to the practical intelligence_
annotations.interpretation: Tom appears to be cross-referencing H.L.A. Hart's positivism (or a Hartian position) and noting that a similar 'nihilation' or erasure of the primacy of practical intelligence operates there too. 'Decay' may refer to the decay of Thomistic moral order under both Scotist voluntarism and later legal positivism. This is a sophisticated comparative note linking medieval voluntarism to modern jurisprudential debates.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with the recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry is directly implicated: if Hartian legal theory repeats a Scotist structure (will/command over reason), then RC's critique of proceduralist civility norms has deep roots in exactly this historical rupture.

annotations.content: A: whatever the X mean changes in law is by defn the good, so B = ?
annotations.anchor_text: _deprived him of the only available alternative_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is constructing a formal schema: A is a position (possibly Scotus's or a Hartian analog) where whatever the relevant authority (X) determines as law just is the good by definition, and B is the unknown — presumably what grounds or limits X's authority, or what 'ought' means independently of X's decree. The '?' signals Tom finds this question unanswered or deliberately suppressed in the text. This is Tom doing philosophy in the margin, not just reading.
annotations.rc_relevance: This schema maps directly onto RC's concern with the collapse of civic virtue into procedural legitimacy: if 'good' is simply what authority defines, then civic friendship and subsidiarity — which require goods independent of legislative fiat — become impossible to articulate.

annotations.content: living virtually confronted with the good precepts in practice actively yet not reserved, distinctions + limits
annotations.anchor_text: _acquired the necessary education in the intellectual and moral virtues_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is glossing Aristotle's account of the practically educated person: one who lives virtuously, confronts genuine goods through practice, and actively exercises the virtues without needing further external motivation. The phrase 'not reserved' likely means such a person is not held back or skeptical — they act. 'Distinctions + limits' suggests Tom is also noting that this ideal has qualifications MacIntyre is about to complicate.
annotations.rc_relevance: This annotation tracks the Aristotelian model of character formation that RC aims to recover — the idea that civic virtue is lived, habituated, and practically intelligent, not procedurally commanded, is foundational to the RC project.

annotations.content: no motivl skeptism
annotations.anchor_text: _can find no room for any question as to why_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is labeling MacIntyre's point that for Aquinas/Aristotle, there is no 'motivational skepticism' — no gap between recognizing the good and being moved to pursue it. The integrated will-intellect means that genuine apprehension of one's good already motivates action. Tom abbreviates this as a named position ('no motivl skeptism') suggesting he is tracking it as a technical term across his reading.
annotations.rc_relevance: Motivational skepticism — the worry that even knowing the good, one may not be moved — is precisely the modern problem RC must address; MacIntyre's claim that Thomistic practical reason dissolves this problem is central to RC's argument for virtue-based over rule-based civic ethics.

annotations.content: recognizes that something is one's true good, one should act so as to achieve it. Neither further reason, nor—for the morally educated, virtue-informed person whose will is rightly ordered by the intellect—further motive, are either necessary or possible
annotations.anchor_text: _recognizes that something is one's true good_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as the decisive Thomistic-Aristotelian claim: for the virtuous person, recognition of the good is both sufficient reason and sufficient motive — nothing further is needed or even available. This is MacIntyre's strongest statement against both Kantian duty-ethics (which requires a separate motivational structure) and Humean sentimentalism (which requires desire to bridge is-ought). Tom likely underlines this as the thesis he will test against Scotus's challenge.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the core Thomistic psychology RC depends on: civic virtue formation works precisely because it produces persons for whom seeing the good and being moved by it are unified — which is why institutions of character formation (family, guild, university) are indispensable to RC's argument.

annotations.content: see wjur? p.140
annotations.anchor_text: _further motive, are either necessary or possible_
annotations.interpretation: Tom cross-references p.140, likely an earlier passage in the book (possibly about will, judgment, or practical reason) that illuminates or complicates the claim here. 'wjur' is likely an abbreviation — possibly 'w/ jurisprudence' or a shorthand for a thinker or concept Tom has been tracking. This is a structural reading annotation connecting distant parts of the argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: Cross-referencing within MacIntyre suggests Tom is building a synthetic reading of the book's architecture — essential for RC's use of MacIntyre as a unified framework rather than a collection of isolated arguments.

annotations.content: Hence to know that God commands those precepts of the natural law, in obedience to which one's good is to be realized, gives one no further, additional reason for obedience to those precepts
annotations.anchor_text: _God commands those precepts of the natural law_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as MacIntyre's startling Thomistic conclusion: divine command adds nothing to the rational obligation already present in the natural law's orientation to genuine good. This is anti-voluntarist to the core — God's commanding does not create the 'ought,' it merely confirms what practical reason already discloses. Tom underlines it likely because it directly contradicts the Scotist/Ockhamist position about to be introduced.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this claim is critical: civility and civic virtue grounded in natural law do not require theological voluntarism to be binding — they are rationally accessible, which means RC's argument for recovered civic virtue can be made in pluralist public discourse without collapsing into sectarianism.

annotations.content: The 'ought' of 'One ought to obey God' is the same 'ought' as the 'ought' of 'To do so and so is the good of such a one; so such a one ought to do so and so'—the same 'ought', that is, as the 'ought' of practical reasoning.
annotations.anchor_text: _ought of practical reasoning_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets this as MacIntyre's reductio or synthesis: divine command 'ought' and practical reason 'ought' are identical in structure. This is either MacIntyre's triumphant integration (Aquinas absorbs divine command into practical reason) or, read differently, a warning that Scotus will split them apart — and that split will generate the modern 'is-ought' problem. Tom's bracket suggests he sees this as a hinge conclusion demanding sustained attention.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the Thomistic unity that RC wants to recover: the 'ought' of civic virtue, natural law, and divine ordinance converging in practical reason, against the Enlightenment fragmentation that produces rival and incommensurable moral vocabularies.

annotations.content: ID Act of Good implied (even if rejected)?
annotations.anchor_text: _ought of practical reasoning_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes 'ID Act of Good implied (even if rejected)?' — this is philosophically dense. 'ID Act' likely means 'identity of the act of the good' or possibly references the identity theory of practical reasoning. Tom is asking whether MacIntyre's account implies that every genuine act of practical reason is an act toward the good — even when (as in sin or error) the good is rejected, the structure of the 'ought' remains implicit. The question mark signals genuine uncertainty or a productive puzzle Tom wants to pursue.
annotations.rc_relevance: This question maps onto RC's concern with how civic virtue can be addressed even to those who have rejected it: if the 'ought' structure is implied even in rejection, then RC's recovery project is not merely sectarian — it can speak to persons who have implicitly already oriented themselves toward goods they may not acknowledge.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that Scotus's reconceptualization of the will as free and the intellect as practically inert generates a distinctively new 'ought' of moral obligation — one unknown to Aristotle — grounded not in the pursuit of the good but in the command of another. This move creates the problems that will define modern moral philosophy (Occam through Kant): why obey the command, and whether we can judge God's goodness by the same standard that grounds the obligation. In parallel, Scotus's doctrine of the soul's immediate intuitive knowledge of singulars transforms the metaphysical relationship between particular and universal, generating a new problematic for emerging academic metaphysics.

annotations.content: Aa: Scotus:: Mac=Finnis (on 2:w G F)
annotations.anchor_text: _For Scotus, however, an Augustinian understanding_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is establishing a cross-reference matrix: 'Aa' likely abbreviates 'Alasdair' (MacIntyre), and he is aligning MacIntyre's reading of Scotus with John Finnis's account, possibly on the topic of the will ('w') and the good ('G') and perhaps freedom or first principles ('F'). The notation '2:w' may refer to a chapter, section, or page in Finnis. This is a comparative scholarly tracking move — Tom is reading MacIntyre and Finnis simultaneously on Scotus's voluntarism.
annotations.rc_relevance: The MacIntyre-Finnis comparison on Scotus is directly relevant to RC's engagement with natural law theory and the Thomistic tradition; both thinkers are key interlocutors for recovering a pre-Enlightenment moral framework that RC depends upon.

annotations.content: on 2: w G F
annotations.anchor_text: _Aa: Scotus:: Mac=Finnis_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has circled this sub-notation, likely to flag it as a specific cross-reference he wants to return to — perhaps a page or section number in Finnis or a second source. The circling distinguishes this locator from the general comparative note, treating it as an actionable reference.
annotations.rc_relevance: Flagging precise cross-references between MacIntyre and natural law theorists like Finnis is part of RC's effort to triangulate between rival but overlapping traditions on the question of moral obligation's grounding.

annotations.content: the intellect is inert, the will is free
annotations.anchor_text: _Augustinian understanding of the primacy of the will_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the core Scotist thesis that MacIntyre is diagnosing as the hinge point of the argument. By isolating 'intellect is inert / will is free,' Tom is marking the precise anthropological claim that breaks with Aristotelian-Thomistic psychology, where intellect directs the will toward the good. This is the crux from which all subsequent problems flow.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's critique of Enlightenment rationalism traces back precisely to this split — the decoupling of intellect and will produces the command-based moral obligation that MacIntyre sees as generative of modern moral disorder. Identifying this moment is foundational for RC's genealogical argument.

annotations.anchor_text: _the intellect is inert, the will is free_
annotations.interpretation: The circled '1' in the right margin marks this as the first in a numbered sequence of claims or structural moves Tom is tracking in MacIntyre's argument about Scotus. It suggests Tom is organizing the passage analytically, expecting a '2' to follow — possibly at the parallel point about singular knowledge lower on the page.
annotations.rc_relevance: Structural numbering indicates Tom is building an argumentative map of how Scotus's innovations cascade; this is directly useful for RC's genealogical narrative about how tradition becomes defeated and replaced.

annotations.content: the will therefore can only exhibit its obedience to God by not only obeying the natural law qua directive of our good but also qua divine commandment
annotations.anchor_text: _The will therefore can only exhibit its obedience_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the doubled structure of obligation in Scotus: the will obeys natural law both as directive of good AND as divine command. This is MacIntyre's key diagnostic point — the 'qua commandment' layer is the new addition that Scotus introduces, and Tom recognizes this as the hinge. The underlining captures the exact sentence where the two-level structure of obligation is stated.
annotations.rc_relevance: This doubled obligation structure — good-directed vs. command-directed — is precisely what RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry seeks to undo; Thomism collapses these back into a unified teleological account, which RC holds as the more coherent civic and moral framework.

annotations.content: 2 ought's intended
annotations.anchor_text: _alongside the 'ought' of practical reasoning_
annotations.interpretation: Tom glosses MacIntyre's argument: Scotus introduces a second 'ought' — the 'ought' of moral obligation grounded in command — alongside the Aristotelian 'ought' of practical reasoning directed at the good. 'Intended' likely means Tom is noting that MacIntyre is making a deliberate analytical distinction, or possibly that Scotus himself intended this bifurcation. This note shows Tom tracking the argumentative structure at the level of metaethics.
annotations.rc_relevance: The distinction between two 'oughts' is central to RC's diagnosis of modernity: once obligation is severed from teleology, civic friendship and virtue-based community lose their rational grounding, producing the fragmentation RC seeks to address.

annotations.content: unknown to Aristotle and to the ancient world generally, the one unknown 'ought' of moral obligation
annotations.anchor_text: _unknown to Aristotle and to the ancient world_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's historically specific claim that moral obligation as a concept is a medieval novelty, not a recovery of antiquity. This is a historiographically charged claim — MacIntyre is arguing that the 'ought' of obligation (as distinct from prudential or virtue-based 'ought') has no ancient precedent. Tom likely underlines this because it anchors the genealogical argument and is contestable.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery project depends on this genealogical claim: if obligation-language is a deviation rather than a refinement, then returning to Thomistic virtue ethics is not reactionary but restorative — a key rhetorical and philosophical move for RC.

annotations.content: a moral obligation as an obligation not because of what it enjoins in the doing or achieving of something good, but in virtue of the command of another
annotations.anchor_text: _once one has identified a moral obligation_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's definition of the distinctively modern concept of moral obligation — grounded in command rather than in the good. This is the pivotal definitional moment in the passage, and Tom's underlining suggests he sees it as a quotable, load-bearing claim. The contrast between 'achieving something good' and 'command of another' encapsulates the Thomist vs. voluntarist divide.
annotations.rc_relevance: This definition of command-based obligation is what RC identifies as the source of contemporary moral incoherence — when obligation floats free of the good, there is no shared rational basis for civic life or moral community.

annotations.content: questions arise as to why we should obey this command
annotations.anchor_text: _in virtue of the command of another, questions arise_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the regress problem that command-based obligation immediately generates. Once obligation is grounded in command rather than good, the question 'why obey?' becomes unanswerable within the framework — it demands either a further command or a goodness-based answer, which collapses the voluntarist position. Tom is tracking the internal instability MacIntyre diagnoses.
annotations.rc_relevance: This regress is precisely the problem RC argues civic institutions cannot solve through procedural or legalistic means alone — they require a substantive account of the good, which only a tradition-based community can supply.

annotations.content: if we are in a position to judge that God is good, then we are not also in a position to judge by the same standard by which we judged the goodness of God whether or not we have an obligation
annotations.anchor_text: _to judge that God is good, then we are not_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's formulation of the Euthyphro-type dilemma embedded in Scotist voluntarism: if we can assess God's goodness, our standard of goodness is independent of God's command, which means obligation cannot simply rest on command. This is the logical trap MacIntyre is exposing. Tom appears to be following the argument carefully, recognizing this as a decisive objection.
annotations.rc_relevance: This dilemma is foundational for RC's argument that purely procedural or authority-based accounts of social obligation (whether divine command or democratic mandate) are philosophically unstable without a substantive teleological grounding.

annotations.content: dedcset thower respect opens the way for alternative accounts of new oblig standard
annotations.anchor_text: _Scotus thus not only made possible but provoked a good deal of later moral philosophy_
annotations.interpretation: Tom's note — likely reading 'dedcset' as 'defeats' or 'discredits' — appears to gloss MacIntyre's point that Scotus's move, by generating insoluble problems, 'shows' (thower = 'throws'?) into relief the need for alternative accounts of obligation and its standard. Tom is reading this as MacIntyre arguing that Scotus's framework is self-undermining, which 'opens the way' for successor accounts (Occam, Kant). The note tracks the dialectical consequence of the Scotist position.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's narrative of tradition defeat is directly illustrated here: Scotus's innovation within Augustinian theology inadvertently generates the very secular and rationalist moral philosophies that MacIntyre sees as fragmenting modern civic life. Tom is marking the mechanism of tradition's self-defeat.

annotations.content: from Occam all the way to Kant
annotations.anchor_text: _provoked a good deal of later moral philosophy, directly and indirectly, from Occam_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's sweeping genealogical claim connecting Scotus to the entire trajectory of voluntarist and deontological ethics through Occam to Kant. This is a signature MacIntyrean move — tracing a long causal chain from a medieval philosophical decision to the dominant frameworks of modern moral philosophy. Tom likely underlines it as a key thesis statement worth citing.
annotations.rc_relevance: This genealogical line is the backbone of RC's critique of Enlightenment ethics — the MacIntyrean argument that Kantian deontology is not a neutral starting point but the terminus of a particular tradition's self-defeat, which RC uses to justify returning to pre-Scotist Thomism.

annotations.content: In a parallel way Scotus's doctrine of the soul's immediate intuitive knowledge of singulars, so that the singular is intelligible — even if only somewhat so — in independence of the universal, both transforms the conception of intelligibility and places constraints upon how the relationship of particular to universal can be understood, in such a way as to generate a new problematic for the about to emerge academic discipline of metaphysics or first philosophy.
annotations.anchor_text: _In a parallel way Scotus's doctrine of the soul's_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets the entire second major movement of MacIntyre's argument — the metaphysical parallel to the ethical argument. Just as Scotus's voluntarism generates modern moral philosophy's problems, his epistemology of singulars generates modern metaphysics' problems. Tom's bracketing signals he sees this as a structurally parallel but distinct argument deserving unified treatment, possibly to compare with or distinguish from the moral argument above.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with subsidiarity and the dignity of particular communities resonates with the metaphysical question of singular vs. universal — the Scotist privileging of the singular has implications for how we think about individual vs. communal identity, a live tension in RC's account of civic friendship.

annotations.content: knowledge of singulars
annotations.anchor_text: _Scotus's doctrine of the soul's immediate intuitive knowledge_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'knowledge of singulars' within the bracketed passage, isolating the specific technical term that anchors the metaphysical argument. This suggests Tom wants to hold this concept separately from the broader passage — possibly to cross-reference it with another text on Scotist epistemology or to track it as a key term across the book.
annotations.rc_relevance: The question of how we know particulars — individuals, particular communities, singular historical traditions — is directly relevant to RC's account of how tradition-based moral enquiry proceeds, which cannot be reduced to universal abstract principles.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the Aristotelian-Thomistic synthesis of enquiry could not be accommodated within the existing curricular categories of either theology or the arts faculty at the University of Paris, because its scope and method made theological and philosophical enquiry inseparable. The outcome was an enlargement of the Arts curriculum to include moral, natural, and metaphysical philosophy, making philosophy the dominant academic discipline — but at the cost of theology's eventual isolation and unimportance. MacIntyre implies this institutional victory for philosophy carried the seeds of its own later crisis, a pattern academic philosophy would re-enact in the nineteenth and twentieth centuries.

annotations.content: emergence of Phil as autonomous discipline
annotations.anchor_text: _set the scene instead for the emergence_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging the precise historical moment MacIntyre identifies as the origin point of philosophy's institutional autonomy. The abbreviation 'Phil' and the phrase 'autonomous discipline' track a key term MacIntyre uses technically — philosophy redefines itself by institutional boundaries rather than by its own tradition of enquiry. Tom likely sees this as a diagnostic moment: the very act of becoming 'autonomous' severs philosophy from its formative tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly engages the RC theme of how institutions shape (or deform) intellectual traditions — the moment philosophy gains institutional autonomy is also the moment it begins losing its substantive moral and teleological grounding, a central MacIntyrean concern for the RC project.

annotations.content: creation of this irreversible (even fatal?) first step
annotations.anchor_text: _ceases to be itself a tradition of enquiry_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is reading MacIntyre's argument as a tragedy-in-miniature: the institutionalization of philosophy is not merely a neutral organizational fact but an irreversible inflection point, possibly fatal to the tradition's integrity. The parenthetical question mark on 'fatal' suggests Tom is testing the strength of MacIntyre's claim — is MacIntyre being too deterministic? Or is Tom agreeing and amplifying the diagnosis?
annotations.rc_relevance: This annotation speaks directly to the RC theme of tradition vs. reform: Tom is asking whether institutional capture of a living tradition of enquiry constitutes an irreversible rupture, which would have strong implications for whether civility and virtue traditions can be 'resurrected' or are permanently foreclosed.

annotations.content: philosophy is redefined as an autonomous academic discipline, whose boundaries are institutional boundaries, and ceases to be itself a tradition of enquiry
annotations.anchor_text: _philosophy is redefined as an autonomous academic discipline_
annotations.interpretation: This is the most heavily marked sentence on the page and is the argumentative crux MacIntyre is building toward. Tom underlines the full tripartite claim: redefinition, institutional boundaries, loss of tradition-status. He is tracking the structural move by which a tradition becomes a discipline — a transformation MacIntyre treats as catastrophic. The underlining of all three clauses suggests Tom sees them as logically inseparable steps in the same fatal process.
annotations.rc_relevance: This sentence is arguably the single most important line for the RC project on this page: it defines the pathology MacIntyre diagnoses — the conversion of living enquiry into academic discipline — which RC seeks to reverse by recovering tradition-embedded moral reasoning.

annotations.content: the works of Aristotle should be taught was the fact that the Aristotelian scheme and synthesis of enquiry
annotations.anchor_text: _significant factor in the dispute as to in which faculties_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the institutional conflict over Aristotle's texts as evidence that the Thomistic synthesis was genuinely disruptive to existing academic categories. This is not just a historical footnote — it shows that the tradition of enquiry MacIntyre valorizes was itself at one point institutionally homeless, which supports the RC argument that tradition-based moral enquiry can exist in tension with dominant institutional arrangements.
annotations.rc_relevance: Relevant to RC's interest in subsidiarity and institutional formation: the dispute over where Aristotle belongs in the curriculum prefigures modern debates about where moral enquiry belongs in secular institutions.

annotations.content: simply could not be fitted into the earlier curricular categories either of the faculty of theology or of the faculty of arts
annotations.anchor_text: _could not be fitted into the earlier curricular categories_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this to mark the moment of genuine incommensurability — the Aristotelian-Thomistic synthesis was not merely difficult to classify but structurally incompatible with the existing institutional grid. This is a precise instance of MacIntyre's broader argument that different traditions of enquiry operate with different standards of rationality that cannot simply be mapped onto one another.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to the RC theme of the 'encyclopaedist / genealogist / tradition' tripartite structure: the Thomistic tradition could not be absorbed into encyclopaedist categories without distortion, a structural point with direct implications for how RC frames the recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry in contemporary secular academia.

annotations.content: theological and philosophical enquiry are at key points made inseparable
annotations.anchor_text: _to the Summa Theologiae theological and philosophical enquiry_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks Aquinas's insistence on the inseparability of theology and philosophy as a positive feature of the tradition, contrasting it with the later institutional separation. The underlining here likely functions as a counter-point to the earlier marks about autonomous discipline — this is what was lost when philosophy became 'autonomous.'
annotations.rc_relevance: Central to RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry: the inseparability of theological and philosophical enquiry in Aquinas is precisely what RC needs to argue against the fragmented disciplinary silos of contemporary academic ethics.

annotations.content: of the Arts curriculum. To the seven liberal arts were added the three philosophies: moral philosophy, natural philosophy, and metaphysics
annotations.anchor_text: _outcome both at Paris and elsewhere was the enlargement_
annotations.interpretation: Tom tracks the moment of curricular expansion as a decisive institutional event. The addition of the three philosophies to the liberal arts is the structural outcome MacIntyre has been building toward — it marks philosophy's institutional triumph, but MacIntyre's framing suggests Tom reads this as a pyrrhic victory.
annotations.rc_relevance: Relevant to RC's concern with how institutions form (or deform) character and enquiry: the liberal arts tradition is often invoked in civility discourse, and Tom is noting the historical moment when moral philosophy entered that tradition — with all the ambiguities MacIntyre attaches to it.

annotations.content: theology as a discipline was to preserve its autonomy only at the price of its eventual isolation and unimportance, a history which in the late nineteenth and twentieth centuries academic philosophy in turn was doomed to reenact.
annotations.anchor_text: _theology as a discipline was to preserve its autonomy_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets the concluding irony of MacIntyre's argument — that theology's strategy of self-preservation through disciplinary autonomy became the very model academic philosophy would disastrously repeat. The bracket (rather than underlining) suggests Tom is flagging this as a structurally significant conclusion, perhaps to return to or cite. The parallel between theology's fate and philosophy's fate is MacIntyre's sharpest diagnostic point on this page.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the most directly RC-relevant passage on the page: MacIntyre's claim that both theology and philosophy were 'doomed' by their pursuit of institutional autonomy at the cost of tradition-embedded enquiry is the negative case RC is implicitly arguing against — the RC project being, in part, an attempt to break this cycle.

annotations.content: vertical bracket with arrow pointing left, adjacent to the final bracketed passage
annotations.anchor_text: _price of its eventual isolation and unimportance_
annotations.interpretation: The leftward-pointing arrow on the bracket likely signals that Tom wants to cross-reference this passage with something earlier in the book or in his own notes — possibly the earlier discussion of encyclopaedism or the genealogist's critique. It may also be a 'pull quote' marker, indicating he intends to cite this passage directly in RC writing.
annotations.rc_relevance: The arrow suggests Tom is actively integrating this passage into the RC argumentative architecture, likely as a key piece of evidence for why both Enlightenment encyclopaedism and its academic successors failed on MacIntyre's account.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that while fourteenth- and fifteenth-century physics and astronomy were 'completed in being defeated' — absorbed into new frameworks via retrospective rational narrative — medieval moral philosophy and metaphysics dissolved into nothing: variety and heterogeneity rather than culminating achievement. This dissolution gave rise to academic philosophy as an institutionalized, professionalized university discipline, a 'scholasticism' with roots in 18th-19th century Scotland, Germany, and France, fully embodied in contemporary American academic culture. The marks of this institutionalized philosophy include high levels of professional skill, logical and conceptual technique, and admission only of those who master these techniques — a formation that structurally excludes the kinds of moral enquiry MacIntyre wants to recover.

annotations.content: earth,
annotations.anchor_text: _fourteenth and fifteenth century physics and astronomy_
annotations.interpretation: Tom appears to be glossing the natural sciences (physics, astronomy) as 'earthly' or empirical disciplines — distinguishing them from moral philosophy and metaphysics discussed in contrast below. This may be a quick shorthand marking the ontological register of the disciplines being compared, noting that physics concerns the material world and so its 'defeat' is more tractable than the defeat of moral traditions.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's concern with the difference between technical-scientific progress and moral tradition: the former can be 'completed in defeat,' but moral traditions require living communities to survive — a core MacIntyrean point foundational to RC.

annotations.content: fourteenth and fifteenth century physics and astronomy were completed in being defeated
annotations.anchor_text: _Hence fourteenth and fifteenth century physics_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's paradoxical formulation that a tradition can be 'completed in being defeated' — i.e., superseded in a way that retrospectively validates its internal logic. This is a crucial structural distinction MacIntyre is drawing: sciences can be rationally defeated and absorbed; moral traditions apparently cannot.
annotations.rc_relevance: This distinction is central to RC's argument about why moral tradition is not simply replaceable by modern rationalism — the Enlightenment 'defeat' of Thomistic moral enquiry was not a completion but a dissolution, which is what RC seeks to reverse.

annotations.content: We can write of them just that kind of retrospective history which reveals the continuous directedness characteristic of genuine tradition of enquiry
annotations.anchor_text: _reveals the continuous directedness characteristic of genuine tradition_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights the concept of 'retrospective history' showing 'continuous directedness' as the hallmark of a genuine tradition of enquiry. This is MacIntyre's criterion for what makes a tradition rationally coherent — it can be narrated as moving toward an internal telos even when superseded. Tom is likely flagging this as a definitional crux for the whole book's argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC depends on exactly this claim: that Thomistic moral enquiry constitutes a 'genuine tradition of enquiry' with continuous directedness, and that recovering it is therefore rationally defensible rather than merely nostalgic.

annotations.content: physics
annotations.anchor_text: _fourteenth and fifteenth century physics and astronomy_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is diagramming the logic of MacIntyre's argument about the natural sciences — sketching a visual schema to capture the relationship between physics, astronomy, and their 'defeat.' The bracket and arrows likely represent the idea that these disciplines were subsumed into a new framework, visualizing the narrative structure of rational supersession. This kind of structural diagram suggests Tom is tracking argument architecture, not just content.
annotations.rc_relevance: By diagramming this separately from his notes on moral philosophy below, Tom is building a contrast structure that maps directly onto RC's distinction between domains where Enlightenment rationalism 'worked' (natural science) and domains where it catastrophically failed (ethics and politics).

annotations.content: By contrast, in what did fourteenth- and fifteenth-century medieval moral philosophy and metaphysics issue? The answer is: as a whole, nothing.
annotations.anchor_text: _medieval moral philosophy and metaphysics issue_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's stark verdict — 'as a whole, nothing' — on medieval moral philosophy's outcome. The orange highlighting and the circling of 'whole/Nothing' in the margin suggest this is a thesis statement Tom considers pivotal. The bluntness of 'nothing' is rhetorically striking and Tom seems to want to remember it as the negative foil against which the book's recovery project is constructed.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the diagnosis RC is responding to: the dissolution of unified moral enquiry into variety and heterogeneity. RC's entire project of resurrecting civility implicitly answers the question of whether 'nothing' must remain the verdict, or whether retrieval is possible.

annotations.content: moral
annotations.anchor_text: _medieval moral philosophy and metaphysics issue_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes 'moral / natural / philosophy' as a two-part contrast corresponding to the text's distinction between natural philosophy (physics/astronomy, which were 'completed') and moral philosophy (which dissolved into nothing). This is a structural annotation organizing the parallel structure of MacIntyre's argument — Tom is making sure he tracks the asymmetry between the two types of tradition-defeat.
annotations.rc_relevance: This asymmetry is foundational for RC: the claim is not that all pre-modern thought was defeated in the same way, but that moral and political traditions require a different kind of recovery than scientific ones — they cannot simply be superseded and absorbed.

annotations.content: φ =
annotations.anchor_text: _academic philosophy as an organized and professionalized university discipline_
annotations.interpretation: Tom equates φ (philosophy) with 'academic philosophy' as institutionalized by the APA (American Philosophical Association), and adds the tag 'pluralist?' — likely questioning whether the APA's institutional pluralism is itself a symptom of the dissolution MacIntyre diagnoses. The question mark signals genuine uncertainty or suspicion: is the APA's 'big tent' approach not a solution to heterogeneity but its bureaucratic expression? This is a sharp and original critical gloss.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with institutions forming or deforming character is directly implicated here: Tom is asking whether the APA as an institution is constitutively unable to host the kind of unified moral enquiry RC wants to recover, because its very pluralism is a product of the dissolution MacIntyre describes.

annotations.content: discipline
annotations.anchor_text: _academic philosophy as an organized and professionalized university discipline_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'discipline' — a key term that carries double meaning: an academic discipline (institutional category) and disciplinary formation (character formation through practice). MacIntyre's point is that the institutionalized form hollows out the second meaning. Tom is likely flagging this terminological tension, possibly intending to explore it in RC writing.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC theme of subsidiarity and institutional formation of character hinges on this ambiguity: a genuine 'discipline' in the classical sense forms virtuous practitioners; an academic 'discipline' in the modern sense merely credentializes technical skill.

annotations.content: most of the history of philosophy has occurred outside the history of that particular professionalized institution
annotations.anchor_text: _history of philosophy has occurred outside the history_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's claim that most philosophy happened outside the university — a historically grounding move that relativizes the current institutional form. This is likely being tracked because it supports RC's recovery project: if philosophy has mostly been non-institutional, then retrieval of pre-institutional moral enquiry is historically legitimate rather than antiquarian.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly supports RC's legitimacy: recovering pre-academic traditions of civility and virtue is not a retreat from philosophy but a return to its predominant historical mode. It challenges the assumption that academic philosophy is the only serious venue for moral enquiry.

annotations.content: institutionalized
annotations.anchor_text: _conception of philosophy as almost exclusively restricted to institutionalized activities within the university_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes 'institutionalized philosophy' as a summary label for the phenomenon MacIntyre is critiquing — likely a term Tom wants to use in RC writing as a named target. The placement beside MacIntyre's key claim about the university's social roots suggests Tom is abstracting a usable critical concept from this passage.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's critique of contemporary moral fragmentation will need a name for what it is opposing; 'institutionalized philosophy' as Tom labels it here points to the APA-dominated, technically specialized, historically amnesiac mode of academic ethics that RC aims to challenge.

annotations.content: The conception of philosophy as almost exclusively restricted to institutionalized activities within the university is in the modern world a social phenomenon with roots in eighteenth- and nineteenth-century Scotland, Germany, and France which has achieved its fullest embodiment in the contemporary culture of the United States.
annotations.anchor_text: _roots in eighteenth and nineteenth century Scotland Germany and France_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's historical genealogy of academic philosophy — tracing it to Scottish, German, and French Enlightenment institutions before its American apotheosis. This is a genealogical move in MacIntyre's own sense: showing that what presents itself as universal rational enquiry is actually a historically contingent social formation. Tom is likely flagging this as the sociological underpinning of MacIntyre's critique.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's engagement with MacIntyre's 'genealogist' rival: MacIntyre here uses genealogical method against institutionalized philosophy itself, showing it to be a power-formation rather than pure reason. RC needs to appropriate this move carefully without collapsing into Nietzschean nihilism.

annotations.content: But it had a distinguished and very similar predecessor in the philosophy of the later Middle Ages.
annotations.anchor_text: _distinguished and very similar predecessor in the philosophy of the later Middle Ages_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's claim that modern academic institutionalized philosophy has a medieval predecessor — i.e., late medieval scholasticism. This is a structurally important point: it means the problem of institutionalized philosophy is not uniquely modern, and that the dissolution MacIntyre diagnosed in medieval moral philosophy partly resulted from its own institutionalization. Tom is likely noting this as a complication for any simple 'recovery' narrative.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is a crucial complication for RC: if late medieval scholasticism was already a problematic institutionalization that contributed to moral dissolution, then RC cannot simply advocate returning to 'medieval' modes — it must distinguish between the living Thomistic tradition and its scholastic institutionalization.

annotations.content: 'scholasticism.'
annotations.anchor_text: _name that would suggest itself is scholasticism_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'scholasticism' — the term MacIntyre proposes to name what institutionalized academic philosophy in both medieval and modern periods shares. The circle marks it as a defined term being introduced, and given Tom's other annotations about APA pluralism, he is likely thinking about whether 'scholasticism' is a term RC can deploy critically against contemporary academic moral philosophy.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's project of recovering genuine moral enquiry must distinguish itself from scholasticism in MacIntyre's critical sense — a technically proficient but morally empty institutional practice. Naming this distinction is essential for RC's self-presentation.

annotations.content: What are the marks of this type of philosophy?
annotations.anchor_text: _What are the marks of this type of philosophy_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights MacIntyre's transition question introducing the criteria of scholastic/institutionalized philosophy. The highlighting signals that Tom recognizes this as a structural pivot in the argument — MacIntyre is about to give a diagnostic list that Tom will want to apply critically. This is the beginning of MacIntyre's 'marks of scholasticism' passage.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC will need to apply these marks diagnostically to contemporary academic ethics and political philosophy — showing that what passes for moral enquiry in the university today is scholasticism in MacIntyre's pejorative sense, and that RC offers an alternative mode.

annotations.content: achievement and sustaining of high levels of professional skill in the elaboration and use of logical and conceptual techniques.
annotations.anchor_text: _high levels of professional skill in the elaboration_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the first 'mark' of scholastic philosophy: technical professional skill in logic and conceptual elaboration. This is the criterion MacIntyre is about to use to indict institutionalized philosophy as morally hollow despite intellectual sophistication. Tom is tracking MacIntyre's criteria carefully, likely to deploy them in RC as a critique of contemporary academic moral philosophy.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery of civility and virtue requires distinguishing technical philosophical competence from substantive moral formation — exactly the distinction MacIntyre is building here. This annotation flags the core RC claim that professional philosophy is insufficient for civic and moral renewal.

annotations.content: by admitting to the discussion only those who attain
annotations.anchor_text: _part sustained by admitting to the discussion only_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles MacIntyre's phrase about restricting philosophical discussion to those who attain the professional marks — highlighting the exclusionary gatekeeping function of institutionalized philosophy. This is likely being flagged as evidence that academic philosophy is constitutively incapable of serving as a vehicle for civic moral enquiry, because its admission criteria exclude the kinds of participants genuine moral deliberation requires.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's concern with civic friendship and subsidiarity: a philosophy that admits only credentialed professionals cannot sustain the kind of broad moral community RC envisions. The exclusionary structure of academic philosophy is itself a symptom of the fragmentation RC seeks to address.

pages.summary: MacIntyre characterizes the type of philosophy associated with defeated or continuing traditions by four features: (1) bureaucratically sanctioned standards and licensed professional skills, (2) problems that persist because they are never definitively solved but only reformulated, (3) recurrent and ineliminable basic disagreements due to lack of shared evaluative standards, and (4) practitioners who bring extra-philosophical commitments (ideological weltanschauungen) to the enquiry that the philosophy itself cannot adjudicate. The fourth characteristic explains why disagreement is ineliminable: these external standpoints (Tolstoyan morality, Bloomsbury aestheticism, scientific materialism, medieval theology) cannot be validated by the philosophical tradition itself, yet they shape what problems are acknowledged. This sets up MacIntyre's deeper point that tradition-constituted enquiry cannot be understood from a tradition-neutral standpoint.

annotations.content: bureaucratically sanctioned standard in the use of such tech-
annotations.anchor_text: _bureaucratically sanctioned standard in the use_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging the institutional mechanism by which professional philosophy reproduces itself — licensure and curriculum control. This is not merely a sociological observation for Tom; it signals MacIntyre's critique that what counts as legitimate philosophical work is policed by institutional gatekeepers rather than by rational adjudication. Tom likely sees this as a structural diagnosis of how tradition-defeat gets institutionalized rather than overcome.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's concern with how institutions form (or deform) character and inquiry — the bureaucratic sanctioning of standards mirrors the way civic institutions can either transmit virtue or merely enforce compliance without genuine moral formation.

annotations.content: ascribed achievement is through the exercise of acknowledged and licensed types of professional skill on par-ticular problems, treated piecemeal
annotations.anchor_text: _ascribed achievement is through the exercise of acknowledged_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines 'piecemeal' as a diagnostic term — the fragmentation of enquiry into isolated professional problems is precisely what prevents the tradition from confronting its foundational aporia. This connects to MacIntyre's broader argument that encyclopaedist philosophy mistakes technical competence for genuine moral understanding. Tom may be noting that piecemeal treatment is both symptom and cause of tradition-defeat.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project's concern with social fragmentation finds a philosophical analog here: piecemeal treatment of problems reflects the same atomism that fractures civic life, preventing the kind of integrated moral reasoning that subsidiarity and civic friendship require.

annotations.content: 2) aka journal articles
annotations.anchor_text: _licensed types of professional skill on par-ticular problems_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is translating MacIntyre's abstract description of 'licensed professional skill on particular problems treated piecemeal' into its contemporary institutional form: the academic journal article. This is a sharp, somewhat sardonic gloss — Tom recognizes that the journal article format structurally enforces piecemeal enquiry and bureaucratic credentialing. He is applying MacIntyre's historical-philosophical diagnosis to the present academic apparatus.
annotations.rc_relevance: This annotation cuts to the heart of RC's methodological self-awareness: if the journal article format is itself a symptom of tradition-defeat, then the RC project must think carefully about what form its own scholarly output should take to avoid reproducing the very fragmentation it critiques.

annotations.content: persevered problems
annotations.anchor_text: _problems persist because they are never, or almost never, definitively solved_
annotations.interpretation: Tom appears to be glossing or correcting a partially legible word in the text — possibly 'presented' — substituting 'persevered' as his interpretive rendering of what MacIntyre means. Alternatively, 'persevered' may be Tom's own conceptual label for this feature: problems that endure not because they resist solution but because the tradition perseveres them as its living content. This suggests Tom is thinking about the tradition's relationship to its unsolved problems as constitutive rather than incidental.
annotations.rc_relevance: For the RC project, this is crucial: the problems of civility, virtue, and moral community are precisely 'persevered problems' in this sense — they cannot be finally solved, only inhabited and reformulated across generations, which is why tradition-constituted enquiry is indispensable.

annotations.anchor_text: _Thirdly and correlatively, certain types of basic disagreement are recurrent and incliminable_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is numbering the structural features of this type of philosophy as MacIntyre enumerates them — this is the third characteristic. The circled '3' functions as an organizational tag, helping Tom track MacIntyre's four-part taxonomy. The fact that Tom circles rather than just writes the number suggests he wants this structural marker to be visually prominent, likely because the four-characteristic structure is the architectonic frame he will need to reference repeatedly.
annotations.rc_relevance: The ineliminability of disagreement under this third feature is the philosophical problem RC must confront directly: if civic friendship requires some shared evaluative standard, and this type of philosophy cannot generate one, then the recovery of tradition-constituted enquiry is not merely academic but politically urgent.

annotations.content: But there is no or insufficient shared agreement as to how these commitments and consequences are to be evaluated
annotations.anchor_text: _But there is no or insufficient shared agreement as to how_
annotations.interpretation: This is the crux of the third characteristic and Tom marks it as such. The absence of a shared evaluative standard is MacIntyre's explanation for why disagreement is ineliminable — not irrationality or bad faith, but structural incomparability of standards. Tom likely sees this as the diagnostic center of MacIntyre's critique of liberal moral philosophy, where procedural agreement masks the absence of substantive shared standards.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the philosophical nerve of the RC project: contemporary social fragmentation is not merely political or cultural but reflects this deeper absence of shared evaluative standards. RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry is precisely an attempt to reconstruct such standards from within a living tradition.

annotations.content: without such a shared standard a large measure of disagreement is bound to be ineliminable
annotations.anchor_text: _without such a shared standard a large measure of disagreement_
annotations.interpretation: Tom double-marks this conclusion, reinforcing the underline on the earlier passage. The repetition signals that Tom considers this the thesis statement of the third characteristic — the logical consequence of the absence of shared standards. He may also be noting the carefully qualified 'a large measure of' rather than 'all' disagreement, which is a philosophically significant modulation.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this sentence is almost a motto: the project begins from the acknowledgment that disagreement is currently ineliminable and asks what tradition-constituted resources might reconstruct the shared standards that make genuine moral discourse possible.

annotations.content: When does this lack arise?
annotations.anchor_text: _ineliminable. When does this lack arise?_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the rhetorical transition question that pivots from the third to the fourth characteristic. By underlining it, he is flagging MacIntyre's dialectical move — the question performs a diagnostic deepening, asking not just that disagreement exists but why. Tom recognizes this as the hinge of the argument and wants to flag the explanatory structure MacIntyre is building.
annotations.rc_relevance: The question 'when does this lack arise?' is also RC's foundational question: at what historical or structural moment did Western moral traditions lose the shared standards that made civic friendship and virtue-formation possible? MacIntyre's answer (the fourth characteristic) directly informs RC's genealogical analysis.

annotations.content: fourth
annotations.anchor_text: _derives in key part from a fourth characteristic of this type_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'fourth' to complete his structural numbering of MacIntyre's four-part taxonomy (having previously circled '3'). This organizational annotation reveals that Tom is reading this section as a formal argument with enumerable parts, not just as continuous prose. The circling of the ordinal rather than a separate margin number suggests Tom wanted to mark MacIntyre's own word rather than impose his own label.
annotations.rc_relevance: The fourth characteristic — that practitioners bring extra-philosophical ideological commitments the tradition cannot validate — is perhaps the most directly relevant to RC's diagnosis of contemporary moral discourse, where liberal, libertarian, and progressive frameworks function as unacknowledged weltanschauungen shaping what moral questions get asked.

annotations.anchor_text: _fourth characteristic of this type of philosophy_
annotations.interpretation: This is Tom's own margin label for the fourth characteristic, paralleling his circled '3' earlier. The two circled numbers together constitute a running structural index Tom is building as he reads, likely for ease of reference when he returns to this page. The placement on the right margin makes it a navigational marker.
annotations.rc_relevance: Tom's careful structural indexing suggests he intends to use MacIntyre's four-characteristic taxonomy as an analytical framework within RC — possibly to diagnose contemporary institutions or philosophical traditions against this typology.

annotations.content: Those who engage in it characteristically although not always come to it bringing with them commitments to some extra-philosophical standpoint
annotations.anchor_text: _Those who engage in it characteristically although not always_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks MacIntyre's careful qualification ('although not always') alongside the central claim about extra-philosophical commitments. The qualification matters philosophically — it prevents the claim from being a deterministic sociological law and preserves the possibility of genuine philosophical enquiry even within tradition-defeated contexts. Tom may be noting this as a point of tension or as a hedge that could be exploited by critics of MacIntyre.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this passage raises the question of whether RC's own practitioners bring extra-philosophical commitments (Catholic social teaching? republican civic humanism?) and whether that disqualifies or situates the project. MacIntyre's 'although not always' is important breathing room.

annotations.content: These ideological weltanschauungen cannot be provided with support by this type of philosophy and they are permitted to enter into it only insofar as theses abstracted from them can be brought to bear in a piecemeal way on the acknowledged problems of philosophy
annotations.anchor_text: _These ideological weltanschauungen cannot be provided with support_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the central paradox MacIntyre identifies: the ideological commitments that motivate philosophical enquiry cannot be vindicated by that enquiry, yet they shape its direction through piecemeal abstraction. Tom likely sees this as MacIntyre's explanation for why tradition-defeated philosophy is both driven by and unable to evaluate its own animating commitments — a structural self-concealment.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's critique of liberal political philosophy: liberalism's animating weltanschauung (individual autonomy, neutral proceduralism) enters philosophical discourse only piecemeal, concealing its tradition-specific origins and making it appear as tradition-neutral rationality.

annotations.content: But it is they which furnish, to their adherents, what the philosophy itself cannot fur-
annotations.anchor_text: _But it is they which furnish to their adherents_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the climactic reversal: the extra-philosophical weltanschauungen furnish what the philosophy cannot — presumably the substantive moral orientation, the sense of what matters, the telos that organizes enquiry. This is MacIntyre's implicit argument that tradition-defeated philosophy is parasitic on pre-philosophical moral commitments it cannot justify. Tom recognizes this as the setup for whatever follows on the next page.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the philosophical vindication of RC's entire project: if tradition-defeated philosophy cannot furnish what its adherents actually need from moral enquiry, then the recovery of tradition-constituted enquiry (Thomistic or otherwise) is not nostalgic but practically necessary.

annotations.content: what problems are not acknowledged? Eg, S's defining problems: how to make G or Alcibiades (s) a better man, or Athens (Rio) a better city.
annotations.anchor_text: _acknowledged problems of philosophy. But it is they which_
annotations.interpretation: This is Tom's most intellectually rich annotation on the page. He asks what problems fall outside the 'acknowledged problems of philosophy' in tradition-defeated enquiry, and answers with Socratic examples: how to make a particular person (Alcibiades) or city (Athens) better. The 'S' abbreviations are almost certainly 'Socrates' and 'Socratic.' The parenthetical '(Rio)' is ambiguous but possibly references a contemporary city or context Tom is thinking about alongside Athens, suggesting he is applying the Socratic problematic to a present case. The annotation contests MacIntyre's (or the tradition-defeated philosophy's) narrowing of acknowledged problems by pointing to the richer, person-and-polis-oriented questions that Socratic enquiry foregrounded.
annotations.rc_relevance: This annotation is at the core of RC's entire motivation: the project's central question — how do we recover civility and virtue in contemporary communities — is precisely one of those 'unacknowledged problems' that tradition-defeated academic philosophy cannot pose. Tom is explicitly linking the RC problematic to the Socratic tradition of civic moral enquiry that MacIntyre's analysis shows has been displaced.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that academic philosophy — whether medieval or modern — makes no genuine progress, because rival philosophical positions merely replace and displace one another without any overall directedness or teleological development emerging. He then turns to twentieth-century analytic philosophy, arguing it has condemned itself to repeat the history of late medieval Scholastic philosophy, each illuminating the other's structural failures. As evidence, he examines the Cambridge History of Later Medieval Philosophy, arguing that the encyclopaedic format itself enacts the Encyclopaedist error: assigning sovereignty over the past by measuring it against present standards of judgment.
