annotations.content: extra- & commitments furnish study
annotations.anchor_text: _standard of evaluation and of preference whereby_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is glossing MacIntyre's point that rival traditions can only be assessed by appeal to commitments and standards external to the competing positions themselves. The abbreviated note suggests Tom sees this as a key epistemological claim: that evaluative frameworks are never neutral but are furnished by prior substantive commitments. He may be flagging the circularity problem — that 'furnishing' a standard of study is itself a tradition-laden act.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly engages RC's concern with the MacIntyrean critique of Enlightenment rationalism: the idea that there is no tradition-independent standpoint from which to adjudicate rival moral enquiries is foundational to the RC project's argument against liberal proceduralism.

annotations.content: There is consequently no progress in this type of academic philosophy, whether medieval or modern, except in skill, method, and technique in the formulation of problems.
annotations.anchor_text: _There is consequently no progress in this type_
annotations.interpretation: This is the thesis statement of the passage and Tom has marked it as the central claim. The highlighting signals this as a load-bearing assertion Tom wants to return to. The qualification 'except in skill, method, and technique' is important — MacIntyre is not denying intellectual sophistication, but denying directedness toward truth. Tom may be assessing whether this is an overly sweeping claim or a precise diagnostic.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the core MacIntyrean diagnosis that RC must either accept, qualify, or answer: if academic philosophy as such cannot make progress, then the recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry must operate outside the academic-encyclopaedist mode — a tension central to the RC project's institutional strategy.

annotations.content: so 'scholastic' (p.159) is not AT + has no telos; not does modern acad. (p.160)
annotations.anchor_text: _no progress in this type of academic philosophy whether medieval or modern_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is cross-referencing p.159 where MacIntyre presumably defined or characterized 'Scholastic' philosophy, and is now synthesizing: both Scholastic and modern academic philosophy share the same structural defect — the absence of a telos. The note 'not AT' likely means 'not Aquinas's Tradition' or 'not Aristotelian-Thomistic,' distinguishing the living Thomistic tradition from the institutionalized scholasticism that merely mimics it. Tom is tracking MacIntyre's crucial distinction between a tradition alive with teleological direction and its academic simulacrum.
annotations.rc_relevance: This annotation marks the RC project's key distinction: Thomistic moral enquiry as a living tradition is not the same as 'scholasticism' as an academic style. RC's recovery project depends on this distinction — it is recovering a teleologically directed enquiry, not an institutional relic.

annotations.content: cal positions merely replace and displace one another without overall directedness appearing
annotations.anchor_text: _positions merely replace and displace one another_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is marking the precise criterion by which MacIntyre distinguishes mere change from progress: the absence of 'overall directedness.' This is a technical term in MacIntyre's philosophy of tradition — a living tradition has a directedness toward its telos, whereas academic philosophy cycles through positions without accumulation toward truth. The underlining suggests Tom is tracking this as a key evaluative criterion.
annotations.rc_relevance: The concept of 'directedness' maps directly onto RC's interest in teleological community formation — the difference between institutions that form character toward an end and those that merely process individuals through competing frameworks.

annotations.content: possible to give a teleological account of its history, for no overall directedness emerges
annotations.anchor_text: _possible to give a teleological account of its history_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is underlining the negative claim: there is no teleological account available for this kind of philosophy. This connects to MacIntyre's broader argument that a tradition can only be narrated teleologically if it has a genuine telos. The inability to write such a history is diagnostic of a pseudo-tradition. Tom may be noting this as a methodological criterion for distinguishing genuine from spurious traditions.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this raises the question of whether contemporary civic institutions can be given a teleological narrative — and if not, what that implies for the possibility of recovering civic friendship and moral community within them.

annotations.content: this philosophy are of the highest relevance to a number of types of tradition of rational enquiry, it is not itself such a tradition
annotations.anchor_text: _of the highest relevance to a number of types_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is marking MacIntyre's paradox: academic philosophy is relevant to genuine traditions of enquiry but is not itself one. This is a subtle and important distinction — academic philosophy can serve as a resource or foil for real traditions without constituting one. Tom may be flagging the implication for how RC should relate to academic philosophical discourse: instrumentally, not constitutively.
annotations.rc_relevance: This has direct institutional implications for RC: the project may need to position itself as drawing on academic philosophy without being reducible to it, situating itself within a living tradition of enquiry rather than within the academic encyclopaedist mode.

annotations.content: indeed institutionalized in such a way as to exclude the type of dialectical discourse and argument characteristic of such traditions from its professionalized discourse
annotations.anchor_text: _institutionalized in such a way as to exclude_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is marking the institutional-structural claim: the professionalization of academic philosophy actively excludes the dialectical argument proper to living traditions. This is not merely a cultural observation but a structural critique — the institution itself is the problem. Tom likely sees this as the mechanism behind the 'no progress' thesis.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is central to RC's concern with institutions forming or deforming character: if academic institutions structurally exclude the dialectical reasoning proper to virtue traditions, then character formation must occur in alternative institutional spaces — a key RC practical concern.

annotations.content: hence G lecture series sourcelessness
annotations.anchor_text: _Gifford lecturers to make discernible progress_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is connecting MacIntyre's claim about the inability of Gifford lecturers to make progress to his diagnosis of 'sourcelessness' — presumably a term from earlier in the book or from Tom's own RC framework. The Gifford Lectures are institutionally prestigious but, on this analysis, structurally unable to produce genuine enquiry because they operate within the encyclopaedist academic mode. Tom sees this as MacIntyre using the Gifford Lectures as a concrete institutional exhibit of his abstract thesis.
annotations.rc_relevance: The concept of 'sourcelessness' — the inability to trace one's enquiry back to a living tradition with substantive moral sources — is a core RC diagnostic of contemporary social fragmentation. Tom is tracking how MacIntyre grounds this abstract problem in a concrete institutional case.

annotations.content: sourcelessness
annotations.anchor_text: _sourcelessness of this type of academic philosophy_
annotations.interpretation: The circling of 'sourcelessness' as a standalone term suggests Tom regards it as a technical or key term requiring special attention — either it is MacIntyre's own coinage being introduced here, or Tom is flagging it as a concept that names something important for his own RC framework. The circle distinguishes it from mere underlining: Tom wants to return to this word specifically.
annotations.rc_relevance: Sourcelessness is likely a direct RC concept-in-development: the condition of moral and civic institutions that have lost connection to the traditions that gave them meaning. This is the negative counterpart to RC's positive project of recovering those sources.

annotations.anchor_text: _marked in the first lecture upon how the inability of twentieth-century_
annotations.interpretation: The circled '1' labels the first of two respects in which MacIntyre says he is concerned with twentieth-century embodiments of this type of philosophy. Tom is tracking the structural organization of MacIntyre's argument — the two respects are being numbered for easy retrieval. This is organizational annotation, but it signals Tom is following the logical skeleton of the argument carefully.
annotations.rc_relevance: Tracking the two-part structure of MacIntyre's engagement with twentieth-century academic philosophy is important for RC because each respect likely yields a different kind of critique — one perhaps diagnostic, one perhaps prescriptive — both of which RC needs to appropriate.

annotations.anchor_text: _condemned itself to repeat the history of late medieval Scholastic philosophy_
annotations.interpretation: The circled '2' marks the second respect: that twentieth-century analytic philosophy has condemned itself to repeat late medieval Scholastic philosophy's trajectory. Tom is continuing his structural numbering. The juxtaposition of (1) sourcelessness and (2) repetition of Scholastic decline suggests Tom is building a two-pronged critique of academic philosophy for RC purposes.
annotations.rc_relevance: The parallel between analytic philosophy's decline and Scholasticism's decline is directly relevant to RC's argument that the current moment mirrors the late medieval crisis MacIntyre diagnosed in After Virtue — and that a similar retrieval of Thomistic resources is therefore now required.

annotations.content: hence broken medieval histories & med. & scholasticism
annotations.anchor_text: _philosophy is written, when written from this modern twentieth-century philosophical point of view_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is glossing MacIntyre's point about the Cambridge History of Later Medieval Philosophy: because it is written from a modern analytic standpoint, it produces a 'broken' or distorted history of medieval philosophy — one that cannot see medieval thought on its own terms. The note 'med. & scholasticism' suggests Tom is distinguishing medieval philosophy from scholasticism as an academic style, reinforcing the earlier margin note about 'AT' vs. scholasticism.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's historiographical concerns: if histories of medieval thought written from encyclopaedist assumptions are structurally distorted, then RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry requires an alternative historiographical approach — one internal to the tradition itself.

annotations.content: It is the mark of encyclopaedia that the present stands in judgment upon the past, assigning to itself a sovereignty which allows it to prove that in the past which can be represented as a precursor of its own standards of judgment
annotations.anchor_text: _It is the mark of encyclopaedia that the present stands in judgment_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is underlining what is perhaps the sharpest formulation of MacIntyre's critique of the Encyclopaedist version of moral enquiry: it is defined by its assumption of present sovereignty over the past. This is both an epistemological and a political claim — the encyclopaedia does not enquire into the past but judges it. Tom likely sees this as the most quotable and deployable formulation of the anti-encyclopaedist position.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the Encyclopaedist error that RC must name and resist: the assumption that contemporary liberal frameworks are the proper measure of past traditions. RC's recovery project requires the inverse — allowing the tradition to stand in judgment over the present, which is the Thomistic and Augustinian move RC seeks to rehabilitate.

pages.summary: MacIntyre critiques the Cambridge History of Later Medieval Philosophy for organizing its editorial perspective around 'recent analytic philosophy' as the standard of value, thereby distorting medieval thought by severing individual theses from the wholes they belong to. He argues this editorial stance makes it impossible to understand Aquinas's unified enquiry — the very unity that Aquinas, his Franciscan contemporaries, and his Averroist contemporaries shared but which Occam explicitly rejected, fracturing enquiry into irreducibly multiple and heterogeneous parts. The page thus traces the historical moment when the unity of moral-metaphysical enquiry collapsed, with Occam as the pivotal figure.

annotations.content: the work was done to the greater glory of contemporary analytic philosophy, which is equated with "recent philosophy"
annotations.anchor_text: _the work was done to the greater glory_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is marking MacIntyre's central polemical claim: that the Cambridge History's editors have subordinated medieval thought to the presuppositions of analytic philosophy, using 'recent philosophy' as a covert evaluative standard. The underline signals Tom's recognition that this is not merely an editorial complaint but a structural diagnosis — the encyclopaedist project (in MacIntyre's tripartite schema) colonizing history of philosophy. Tom likely wants to return to this as evidence of how dominant traditions erase rival ones.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly maps onto RC's concern with MacIntyre's encyclopaedist / tradition / genealogist tripartite: the Cambridge History exemplifies encyclopaedism imposing its own standards on a defeated tradition, precisely the mechanism RC investigates when asking why classical traditions of civility become illegible.

annotations.content: by those topics in which recent philosophy has made the greatest progress" in order "to end the era in which medieval philosophy has been studied in a philosophical ghetto"
annotations.anchor_text: _topics in which recent philosophy has made_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the 'ghetto' metaphor and the editors' stated intention to 'end' that era. MacIntyre is exposing how the rhetoric of liberation (ending the ghetto) actually enforces assimilation — medieval philosophy is freed from its ghetto only by being made to speak analytic philosophy's language. Tom seems to be tracking the irony: the editors' progressive framing conceals a regressive epistemological move.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with tradition vs. reform is sharpened here: institutional reform framed as liberation can destroy the internal standards of a tradition. This mirrors debates in RC about whether civic institutions can be reformed without losing their formative character.

annotations.content: presumably that ghetto in which the attempt was being made to understand medieval philosophy in the light of its own standards and presuppositions
annotations.anchor_text: _attempt was being made to understand medieval_
annotations.interpretation: This is arguably the most philosophically loaded underline on the page. Tom marks MacIntyre's parenthetical redefinition of the 'ghetto' — what the editors call a ghetto is actually the methodologically sound practice of reading a tradition on its own terms. Tom is flagging MacIntyre's reversal: the 'ghetto' is not a failure but a virtue. This is central to MacIntyre's epistemological argument about tradition-constituted rationality.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is core to RC's recovery project: the claim that Thomistic moral enquiry must be understood from within its own standards and presuppositions before it can be brought into dialogue with contemporary fragmentation. Tom is likely building toward this methodological point.

annotations.content: the achievements of medieval logicians are historically more distinctive and philosophically more valuable than anything else in medieval thought
annotations.anchor_text: _achievements of medieval logicians are historically more distinctive_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the editors' hierarchy of value within medieval thought — logic above theology, above ethics, above metaphysics. MacIntyre is showing that this ranking is itself a projection of analytic philosophy's preferences onto medieval material. Tom's underline suggests he is tracking this as an example of how evaluative frameworks are smuggled into ostensibly historical accounts.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this connects to the question of which parts of classical tradition get 'recovered' in reform projects. If logic is privileged over moral theology, the civic and ethical dimensions of Thomistic enquiry — precisely what RC wants to recover — are marginalized.

annotations.content: the difference between the editorial perspective and that of so many medieval thinkers
annotations.anchor_text: _the difference between the editorial perspective_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks MacIntyre's structural observation that the high quality of individual essays paradoxically accentuates the distortion by making the editorial perspective more persuasive. Tom is likely noting that this is MacIntyre's dialectical move: acknowledging the book's merits while showing they compound the problem.
annotations.rc_relevance: This speaks to RC's concern with institutions forming character — a well-executed but misdirected institutional project (like the Cambridge History) can entrench false standards more deeply than a poor one would.

annotations.content: No one's thought is treated as a whole and therefore the relationship of individual theses and arguments as parts to wholes never appears
annotations.anchor_text: _thought is treated as a whole and therefore_
annotations.interpretation: Tom heavily underlines this as MacIntyre's methodological indictment: the Cambridge History's essay format structurally prevents the reader from encountering medieval thinkers as unified enquirers. This is not merely a format complaint — it is an epistemological claim about how parts divorced from wholes become unintelligible. Tom is tracking MacIntyre's argument that understanding requires seeing the teleological structure of an enquiry.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's Thomistic recovery: subsidiarity and civic friendship in Aquinas cannot be understood as isolated doctrines but only as parts of a unified enquiry about the good. Tom is marking the methodological precondition for RC's entire project.

annotations.content: this, even more than the cursory treatment of, for example, moral enquiry, which would make it impossible for anyone relying solely on this history to begin to understand the scale, nature, and structure of Aquinas's enquiries
annotations.anchor_text: _cursory treatment of, for example, moral enquiry_
annotations.interpretation: The specific mention of 'moral enquiry' being treated cursorily is almost certainly why Tom underlined this passage. MacIntyre is identifying that ethics/moral enquiry is the most damaged domain in the Cambridge History's treatment — the very domain most central to MacIntyre's project (and RC's). The word 'impossible' is emphatic: this is not a gap but a structural exclusion.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's entire project depends on recovering Thomistic moral enquiry as a living tradition capable of addressing contemporary fragmentation. MacIntyre is saying the dominant scholarly apparatus actively prevents this recovery — a direct RC concern.

annotations.content: to engage in dialogue with Aquinas
annotations.anchor_text: _therefore to engage in dialogue with Aquinas_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the phrase 'engage in dialogue with Aquinas' — MacIntyre's language of dialogue rather than mere scholarly study signals his view that Aquinas's tradition is still alive and contestable, not merely historical. Tom is likely flagging this as MacIntyre's implicit claim about the status of tradition: it demands interlocutors, not archivists.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery project is fundamentally about dialogue with classical tradition, not antiquarianism. MacIntyre's framing here validates RC's approach: the tradition must be engaged as a living interlocutor if its resources for addressing social fragmentation are to be activated.

annotations.content: a method and a perspective which in relation to the twelfth and thirteenth century are in significant ways distorting and obscurantist become a great deal less so in the fourteenth
annotations.anchor_text: _method and a perspective which in relation to the twelfth_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks MacIntyre's historically specific claim: the analytic method is less distorting when applied to the 14th century (Occam, late scholasticism) than to the 12th-13th (Aquinas, Bonaventure). This is a subtle but important point — the method fits its object better when the object has already fragmented. Tom may be noting MacIntyre's implied chronological argument about when and why the unified tradition broke.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this establishes a historical periodization: the 13th century represents the height of unified moral-civic enquiry, and the 14th century its fragmentation. This periodization underlies RC's claim that something was genuinely lost and needs recovering.

annotations.content: The Middle Ages gradually moved towards becoming what the editors of the Cambridge History clearly wished that they had always been
annotations.anchor_text: _Middle Ages gradually moved towards becoming_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks what is perhaps MacIntyre's sharpest irony in the passage: the editors' preferred portrait of medieval philosophy — fragmented, topic-driven, analytically tractable — is actually accurate for the late Middle Ages, but only because the tradition had already collapsed into that form. The Cambridge History inadvertently tells a story of decline while celebrating it as the norm. Tom seems to recognize this as MacIntyre's most polemically effective sentence.
annotations.rc_relevance: This encapsulates RC's core historical-diagnostic claim: modernity's fragmentary moral culture is not the baseline but the aftermath of a defeated tradition. The editors mistake the wreckage for the building.

annotations.content: the unity of enquiry, so crucial not only to Aquinas but also to both his Franciscan and his Averroist contemporaries, gradually becomes lost to view
annotations.anchor_text: _unity of enquiry so crucial not only to Aquinas_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the claim that the unity of enquiry was shared across rival 13th-century schools — Thomist, Franciscan, Averroist — before Occam broke it. This is a crucial structural point: the unity was not unique to Thomism but was the common presupposition of the tradition as a whole. Tom is likely flagging this to distinguish the unity of enquiry from any particular doctrinal position.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this matters enormously: the civic and moral resources Tom seeks to recover are not sectarian (Thomist only) but belong to a broader pre-Occamist consensus. This broadens RC's genealogical claim and its potential audience.

annotations.content: So Occam explicitly rejected any notion of a unified science with a specifically unified subject-matter as the goal of enquiry
annotations.anchor_text: _Occam explicitly rejected any notion of a unified science_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the pivot point of the historical argument: Occam's explicit rejection of unified science as the terminus of the preceding tradition's fragmentation. MacIntyre is identifying Occam not merely as a different thinker but as the agent of a structural rupture. Tom likely sees this as MacIntyre's diagnosis of the origin of the modern condition — the philosophical 'Fall' in the story RC is trying to address.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's account of social fragmentation needs a historical origin point. MacIntyre's identification of Occam as the explicit rejector of unified enquiry provides RC with a precise intellectual genealogy for the disintegration of civic and moral community.

annotations.content: Enquiry itself became irreducibly multiple and hetero-
annotations.anchor_text: _Enquiry itself became irreducibly multiple_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the sentence that closes the page — dramatically cut off mid-word — as MacIntyre's summative characterization of the post-Occamist condition. 'Irreducibly multiple' is the key phrase: not merely diverse but incapable of being unified, structurally resistant to the kind of common enquiry that Aquinas practiced. Tom is tracking this as MacIntyre's definition of the modern predicament.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the diagnosis RC must answer: if enquiry is now irreducibly multiple, can civility, civic friendship, or moral community be recovered at all? Tom is marking the depth of the problem RC is trying to address.

annotations.content: It is this, even more than the cursory treatment of, for example, moral enquiry, which would make it impossible for anyone relying solely on this history to begin to understand the scale, nature, and structure of Aquinas's enquiries and therefore to engage in dialogue with Aquinas
annotations.anchor_text: _make it impossible for anyone relying solely_
annotations.interpretation: The curly bracket on the right margin groups this entire passage about the impossibility of understanding Aquinas through the Cambridge History. Tom is isolating this as a self-contained argumentative unit — MacIntyre's strongest single indictment. The bracket suggests Tom plans to cite or quote this block directly, or return to it as a key piece of evidence.
annotations.rc_relevance: This bracketed passage is likely a quotation candidate for RC's methodological sections, where Tom will need to argue why dominant scholarly frameworks must be set aside before Thomistic moral resources can be recovered for contemporary use.

annotations.content: B+Dt
annotations.anchor_text: _gradually moved towards becoming what the editors_
annotations.interpretation: This is Tom's most compressed and therefore most important annotation. 'B+D' likely refers to 'B' and 'D' as structural labels Tom is tracking — possibly 'B' = the beginning of the unified tradition, 'D' = its defeat or decline; or they map onto two of MacIntyre's three rival versions (possibly Encyclopaedia and the defeated Tradition). '14thC↓' almost certainly means '14th century = the point of decline/collapse of the tradition.' 'trad' = tradition (in MacIntyre's sense). 'MtD' is likely Tom's abbreviation for 'MacIntyre's Tradition Diagnosis' or possibly 'Moved to Decline' — flagging that this passage is where MacIntyre locates the historical moment the tradition deteriorated. Together the note reads as a structural map: two forces (B+D) converge in the 14th century to push tradition downward.
annotations.rc_relevance: This margin note is Tom building his own analytical schema on top of MacIntyre's text, likely cross-referencing the tripartite structure (encyclopaedist / genealogist / tradition) and the historical periodization. For RC, identifying when and how the tradition declined is prerequisite to any recovery project.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the later Middle Ages, shaped by its institutional structures and modes of thought, was fundamentally unable to engage with Aquinas's Summa as a unified whole — a condition Aquinas himself had tried to remedy by writing the Summa to place moral enquiry within a systematic theological context. After Aquinas's death, the Dominican order reaffirmed his teaching but the tradition of commentary upon him became increasingly independent of late medieval philosophical mainstream, with parts of the Summa (especially the Secunda Secundae) being copied and studied in isolation from the whole — ironically inverting Aquinas's own integrative intention. This fragmentation of the Summa's reception becomes MacIntyre's evidence that institutional and curricular structures can defeat even the most architectonically unified moral project.

annotations.content: CHreads mAc finds w/ LMp
annotations.anchor_text: _Cambridge History to some degree and perhaps_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is logging a reading note indicating that he ('CH' likely being a collaborator or student, or possibly 'CH' as shorthand for 'Cambridge History') reads MacIntyre and finds convergence with 'LMp' — almost certainly Alasdair MacIntyre's later work or possibly 'Late Medieval Philosophy.' The shorthand 'finds w/ LMp' suggests Tom is tracking a point of contact or agreement between the Cambridge History's implicit methodology and late medieval philosophy as MacIntyre diagnoses it. This is a cross-referencing note connecting the historiographical source to MacIntyre's diagnostic argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: This maps directly onto RC's concern with how traditions are received and distorted by later institutional contexts — Tom is flagging that even scholarly historiography (the Cambridge History) inadvertently reproduces the fragmentation MacIntyre critiques, which is central to the encyclopaedist/tradition tension in RC.

annotations.content: inadvertently shows us high medieval thought from the standpoint of late medieval thought
annotations.anchor_text: _inadvertently shows us high medieval thought_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this because it is MacIntyre's sharpest methodological critique of the Cambridge History: the very act of historiography here is shaped by the epistemological standpoint of a defeated tradition. The word 'inadvertently' is crucial — it signals that the distortion is not malicious but structural, a product of the tradition's own limitations. Tom likely sees this as a model for how contemporary moral philosophy similarly reads the past through a distorting lens without knowing it.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is a foundational RC insight: that Enlightenment and post-Enlightenment scholarship reads virtue ethics and Thomistic moral enquiry through the lens of the very tradition MacIntyre critiques — the encyclopaedist standpoint presenting itself as neutral. RC's recovery project depends on exposing this distortion.

annotations.content: strong similarity, if not quite identity, of standpoint of modern analytic philosophy and late medieval philosophy
annotations.anchor_text: _strong similarity if not quite identity of standpoint_
annotations.interpretation: This is one of MacIntyre's most provocative structural claims on the page — that analytic philosophy and late medieval philosophy share a standpoint. Tom underlines it to mark it as a thesis-level claim worth holding onto. The qualification 'if not quite identity' suggests MacIntyre is being careful not to overstate, and Tom may be noting this precision. The claim sets up MacIntyre's tripartite structure by showing that the 'encyclopaedist' rival is not simply modern but has deep medieval roots in the fragmentation of Thomism.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this is significant because it suggests that the fragmentation of moral enquiry is not simply an Enlightenment rupture but has longer institutional roots — the Thomistic tradition was already being hollowed out from within. RC's recovery project must therefore go deeper than simply critiquing modernity.

annotations.content: able to appropriate or even to enter into dialogue with Aquinas's thought as a whole and more particularly with the Summa as a whole
annotations.anchor_text: _able to appropriate or even to enter into dialogue_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the double emphasis on 'as a whole' — MacIntyre is arguing that the institutional and intellectual conditions of the later Middle Ages made holistic engagement with Aquinas architectonically impossible. The escalating phrase 'appropriate or even to enter into dialogue' suggests a spectrum of engagement, with appropriation being harder than mere dialogue. Tom is likely tracking MacIntyre's argument that fragmentation is not just a reading problem but a structural incapacity built into institutions.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to RC's theme of subsidiarity and institutional formation of character — the inability to engage with a tradition 'as a whole' is produced by institutions that parse and fragment moral enquiry. RC asks how institutions can be reformed to enable the kind of holistic moral engagement MacIntyre describes here.

annotations.content: to be treated in the light of Aquinas's own intentions
annotations.anchor_text: _Consider how the Summa came to be treated_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks this phrase because it introduces the intentionalist criterion MacIntyre is using: the Summa should be evaluated by what Aquinas intended it to do, not by how it was received. This is a methodological move Tom wants to flag — MacIntyre is claiming that reception history distorted Aquinas's intentions, and the scholarly task is to recover those intentions. This may also connect to Tom's own RC methodology of reading texts against their institutional reception.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry depends precisely on this move: distinguishing what Aquinas intended from how the tradition received him. The fragmentation of the Summa into separately studied parts is paradigmatic of how institutions defeat tradition even while claiming to transmit it.

annotations.content: hand in organizing the form of teaching for his students
annotations.anchor_text: _Aquinas had an entirely free hand in organizing_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the pedagogical dimension here — the Summa's origin in Aquinas's freedom to organize teaching is significant for MacIntyre's argument that the text's structure is inseparable from its pedagogical intention. The freedom Aquinas had is contrasted with the curricular constraints that later fragmented his work. Tom may be thinking about the RC project's own pedagogical dimension — how moral formation requires institutional freedom to present tradition whole.
annotations.rc_relevance: This speaks directly to RC's concern with the role of institutions in forming character: Aquinas had the institutional latitude to create a unified curriculum; his successors did not, and this institutional constraint produced the fragmentation MacIntyre diagnoses. RC must ask what institutional conditions enable rather than defeat moral formation.

annotations.content: Underlying this, so Boyle argues, was a dissatisfaction with the current Dominican manuals and teaching on moral questions
annotations.anchor_text: _Underlying this so Boyle argues was a dissatisfaction_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this to capture the reform motive behind the Summa — Aquinas wrote it not as a purely speculative exercise but as a corrective to inadequate institutional teaching. This 'dissatisfaction with manuals' is structurally analogous to MacIntyre's own project (and RC's) of writing against the fragmented moral curriculum of modernity. Tom likely sees this as a historical precedent for the kind of institutional critique RC undertakes.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is a strong RC parallel: just as Aquinas's dissatisfaction with Dominican manuals prompted a systematic recovery of integrated moral theology, RC's dissatisfaction with contemporary moral discourse prompts a similar recovery. The 'manual problem' — moral teaching severed from its systematic theological and philosophical context — is precisely what RC seeks to address.

annotations.content: an intention to remedy this by setting moral enquiry in its appropriate systematic theological context so that the parts should not misleadingly be studied in abstraction from the whole
annotations.anchor_text: _intention to remedy this by setting moral enquiry_
annotations.interpretation: This is the architectonic thesis of the page and Tom marks it as such. MacIntyre is identifying Aquinas's core intention: moral enquiry cannot be properly conducted in abstraction from its systematic theological whole. The double negative 'should not misleadingly be studied in abstraction' emphasizes that studying parts in isolation is not just incomplete but actively misleading. Tom is flagging this as a structural claim about the relationship between parts and wholes in moral enquiry — a claim RC must engage directly.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is perhaps the single most important passage for RC on this page: the fragmentation of moral enquiry from its systematic context is identified as the root pathology MacIntyre diagnoses in both medieval and modern philosophy. RC's recovery project is essentially an attempt to re-embed civic and moral discourse within a systematic tradition — to reverse the abstraction MacIntyre identifies here.

annotations.content: an intention to remedy this by setting moral enquiry in its appropriate systematic theological context so that the parts should not misleadingly be studied in abstraction from the whole
annotations.anchor_text: _intention to remedy this by setting moral enquiry_
annotations.interpretation: The bracket in the left margin (the diamond-like shape visible in the image) marks this passage as structurally significant beyond the underline — Tom is flagging it for extraction or quotation. The use of both underline and bracket suggests this is a passage Tom intends to cite directly in RC work. The bracket may also indicate he wants to discuss this specific claim in a seminar or writing context.
annotations.rc_relevance: The bracketed passage encapsulates the RC argument for why tradition cannot be received piecemeal — moral enquiry requires systematic context. This is the Thomistic answer to the encyclopaedist fragmentation RC critiques.

annotations.content: Tof commentary for AA margin [use?] from instructor Consider entity (3AV as rec for full belong or leadership Revised TT.)
annotations.anchor_text: _tradition of commentary upon Aquinas had its first notable figures_
annotations.interpretation: This is a complex and partially illegible note in orange/dark ink, heavily abbreviated. 'Tof commentary for AA' likely means 'Tradition of commentary for After Aquinas' — Tom is noting the pedagogical or curricular use of this passage. 'margin use from instructor' suggests he is thinking about how to deploy this in a teaching context. 'Consider entity (3AV as rec for full belong or leadership' may refer to a three-part structure ('3AV' possibly '3 rival versions') recommended for a course on belonging or institutional leadership. 'Revised TT' likely means 'Revised Tutorial/Teaching Text' or a revised course outline. This is a planning note, not a philosophical annotation.
annotations.rc_relevance: This planning note suggests Tom is actively thinking about how to translate MacIntyre's argument about the fragmentation of commentary tradition into RC's pedagogical and institutional reform agenda — specifically how the 'three rival versions' structure can be used in leadership or civic formation contexts.

annotations.content: But the subsequent history of such commentary, no matter how distinguished, is quite independent of the mainstream of late medieval philosophy
annotations.anchor_text: _subsequent history of such commentary no matter how distinguished_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks this because it captures MacIntyre's paradox: the Thomistic commentary tradition, however excellent, became a parallel track isolated from the mainstream — it could not influence or be influenced by the dominant philosophical culture. This is the institutional dimension of tradition's defeat: not erasure, but marginalization and quarantine. Tom is tracking the mechanism by which a tradition is simultaneously preserved and rendered impotent.
annotations.rc_relevance: This speaks to RC's concern about the fate of virtue traditions in contemporary culture — they may survive in isolated communities (religious orders, universities, civic associations) while remaining unable to engage the mainstream. RC must ask not just how to preserve tradition but how to re-connect it with the center of public moral discourse.

annotations.content: the Summa never became part of the curriculum
annotations.anchor_text: _Dominican priory schools the Summa never became_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks this striking claim — even within the Dominican order itself, the Summa was not used as a curriculum text. This is MacIntyre's sharpest evidence that institutional allegiance to Aquinas and genuine intellectual engagement with his project are completely different things. The irony is acute: the order that produced Aquinas and claimed his authority never actually taught his central work as a whole.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is a cautionary note for RC: institutional allegiance to a tradition (claiming Aquinas, claiming civility, claiming virtue) does not guarantee genuine engagement with that tradition's architectonic logic. RC must distinguish between nominal and substantive recovery of tradition.

annotations.content: were studied in isolation from the context in which it had been one of his central intentions to place them
annotations.anchor_text: _were studied in isolation from the context_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks this as the culminating irony of the page's argument: the parts of the Summa most frequently copied and studied (the Secunda Secundae on moral virtues) were studied in precisely the mode of isolation Aquinas had written the whole Summa to overcome. The reception of his work perfectly inverted his intention. Tom likely sees this as the paradigm case of how tradition can be actively defeated by the very act of its transmission.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is MacIntyre's most pointed example of how institutional and curricular fragmentation defeats the integrative intention of a moral tradition — directly relevant to RC's argument that contemporary moral discourse has inherited this fragmentation and must recover the integrative context Aquinas intended.

pages.summary: MacIntyre traces the fragmentation of Thomistic moral enquiry in the later Middle Ages: the Summa was dismembered by copyists and read piecemeal, Buridan's commentary on the Nicomachean Ethics exemplified a growing divorce between theoretical and practical philosophy, and Occam's voluntarist theory of obligation (evil as nothing other than acting against a divine command) displaced the Aristotelian-Thomistic synthesis. The result was the multiplication of moral standpoints independent of and hostile to Aristotle and Aquinas, including entirely new theories of natural rights that have no room within either an Aristotelian or Thomistic framework.

annotations.content: like Chem Studies
annotations.anchor_text: _Aquinas had been an eccentric thinker_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is drawing an analogy between the piecemeal, consumer-driven dismemberment of Aquinas's Summa by medieval copyists and the way a complex scientific discipline (chemistry?) gets fragmented or selectively excerpted in survey courses or popular use. He is flagging MacIntyre's point that institutional and market pressures, not intellectual merit, drove what got transmitted — a structural observation about how traditions decay not through refutation but through atomization.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's concern with how living traditions fragment: just as the Summa was chopped up to meet 'consumer demand,' contemporary civic and moral traditions are excerpted and decontextualized, losing the architectonic unity MacIntyre insists is essential to coherent moral enquiry.

annotations.content: Thus was the Summa dismembered by medieval copyists, responding to consumer demand in the academic marketplace, anticipated the editors of the Cambridge History.
annotations.anchor_text: _Thus was the Summa dismembered by medieval copyists_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the full opening claim, which is MacIntyre's sharpest polemical move on this page — equating medieval scholastic fragmentation with modern academic editorial practice (the Cambridge History). The underline suggests Tom sees this as a key argumentative hinge: MacIntyre is collapsing the distance between medieval and modern failures of tradition, implicating contemporary scholarship in the same dismemberment.
annotations.rc_relevance: Central to RC's critique of Enlightenment and post-Enlightenment institutions: scholarly apparatuses (encyclopaedias, Cambridge Histories) reproduce the same fragmenting logic as medieval copyists, substituting survey for synthesis and market demand for intellectual integrity.

annotations.content: It is, in the light of all this, surprising that during this period his theological primacy was continually reendorsed by papal authority, something not at all explicable by the intellectual Zeitgeist, but only by what is either pure historical contingency or divine providence.
annotations.anchor_text: _surprising that during this period his theological primacy_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks MacIntyre's pointed irony: that Aquinas's papal endorsement cannot be explained by the intellectual climate, leaving only contingency or providence as explanations. This is MacIntyre being deliberately provocative — he refuses to naturalize the survival of the Thomistic tradition, suggesting it requires a theological or at minimum non-rationalist explanation. Tom likely flags this because it is a moment where MacIntyre's own tradition-constituted reasoning becomes self-referentially visible.
annotations.rc_relevance: Touches RC's tension between tradition and reform: if the survival of a tradition cannot be explained on purely intellectual-historical grounds, what does that say about the basis on which RC claims to 'recover' or 'resurrect' civic traditions? The question of what sustains a tradition across hostile periods is live for the project.

annotations.content: a growing divorce between philosophy as concerned with practice and philosophy as theoretical enquiry.
annotations.anchor_text: _commentary does exemplify, however, is a growing divorce_
annotations.interpretation: Tom isolates this phrase as a diagnostic term for the pathology MacIntyre is describing. The 'divorce' between practical and theoretical philosophy is not merely a pedagogical inconvenience — for MacIntyre it represents the dissolution of the Aristotelian unity of ethics, since practical wisdom (phronesis) requires their integration. Tom may be tracking this as a structural concept he wants to deploy in RC.
annotations.rc_relevance: Core RC theme: the separation of moral theory from civic practice is precisely the condition RC seeks to address. This phrase may serve Tom as a historical origin point for the contemporary fragmentation of civility from philosophical grounding.

annotations.content: TusP
annotations.anchor_text: _commentary does exemplify, however, is a growing divorce_
annotations.interpretation: Likely an abbreviation — possibly 'T us P' meaning 'This Us Present' (i.e., this applies to us/now), or a cross-reference to another text or section (perhaps 'Towards a Unity of Singular Practice' or a personal shorthand). It appears adjacent to the 'divorce between practice and theory' passage, suggesting Tom is flagging this as directly applicable to a current problem or to another source he is reading alongside MacIntyre. The capitalization pattern suggests it may be a proper title or acronym.
annotations.rc_relevance: If 'TusP' refers to a contemporary parallel or another text in Tom's RC reading list, this is a cross-referencing move — linking MacIntyre's historical diagnosis to a contemporary manifestation of the theory/practice divorce that RC is designed to address.

annotations.content: so that the Aristotelian connection between the intellectual virtues and the moral virtues disappeared from view.
annotations.anchor_text: _Aristotelian connection between the intellectual virtues and the moral virtues_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as a precise statement of what was lost: not just a philosophical thesis but the structural unity of virtue theory itself. The disappearance of the connection between intellectual and moral virtues means that prudence (practical wisdom as an intellectual virtue governing moral life) becomes unintelligible — ethics collapses into either rules or sentiment. Tom is marking the exact mechanism of the tradition's internal collapse.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's recovery of virtue ethics: if civility is to be reconstructed as a virtue rather than a norm or convention, the re-integration of intellectual and moral virtues is a prerequisite. This passage identifies what specifically needs to be 'resurrected.'

annotations.content: Asterisk or starburst drawn in margin
annotations.anchor_text: _the Aristotelian connection between the intellectual virtues and the moral virtues disappeared from view_
annotations.interpretation: A bold symbol (appears as a drawn star or asterisk) placed next to the 'disappeared from view' passage. This is Tom's strongest emphasis mark on the page — he is flagging this sentence as a key claim to remember, possibly for direct quotation or as a structural pivot in his own argument. The symbol's boldness relative to other marks suggests it is more than a reading aid; it may mark a passage Tom intends to cite in RC writing.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is likely the single most RC-relevant sentence on the page: the loss of the Aristotelian virtue-unity is the historical event MacIntyre is narrating, and RC's project is precisely the attempt to make that connection visible again in contemporary civic life.

annotations.content: quite new theories of natural rights, for which there is no room within either an Aristotelian or a Thomistic framework.
annotations.anchor_text: _quite new theories of natural rights for which there is no room_
annotations.interpretation: Tom highlights this in orange — his most emphatic annotation type — indicating this is a passage of high RC priority. MacIntyre's claim that nominalist natural rights theories are incommensurable with Aristotelian-Thomistic frameworks is one of his most contested and important moves, anticipating his argument in 'After Virtue.' Tom highlights it likely because it frames a core RC tension: contemporary rights-discourse is not merely an extension of classical moral theory but a rival and incompatible tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: Crucial for RC: if natural rights theories (the language of contemporary liberal civility) are genuinely incommensurable with the Thomistic framework RC seeks to recover, then RC cannot simply translate between them. This forces RC to take a position on whether it is reconstructing civility within a tradition or attempting cross-tradition dialogue.

annotations.content: Nominalism rights
annotations.anchor_text: _quite new theories of natural rights for which there is no room_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes 'Nominalism rights' as a keyword tag adjacent to the highlighted passage, condensing MacIntyre's argument into a diagnostic label. He is noting the genealogical connection MacIntyre draws: rights-language emerges from nominalist theology (Occam's voluntarism), not from classical natural law. This is a term Tom may use as a chapter heading, index entry, or cross-reference in RC.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's engagement with MacIntyre's 'encyclopaedist/genealogist/tradition' tripartite structure: nominalist natural rights represent the genealogical critique's origin point. Understanding rights as a nominalist artifact rather than a classical inheritance is essential to RC's historical argument about why contemporary civility discourse feels groundless.

annotations.content: the multiplication and growing diversity of standpoints within moral philosophy are partially rooted in the changing form of the conflicts of the political and social world.
annotations.anchor_text: _multiplication and growing diversity of standpoints within moral philosophy_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's sociological-historical claim: moral pluralism is not simply a product of intellectual progress but reflects real political and social conflicts. This is MacIntyre resisting any purely idealist history of philosophy — moral standpoints are embedded in, and partially constituted by, material social conditions. Tom marks this likely because it complicates any purely intellectual 'recovery' of tradition; RC must also address the social conditions that produce moral fragmentation.
annotations.rc_relevance: Key for RC's institutional dimension: if moral standpoints are 'partially rooted' in political and social conflicts, then resurrecting civility requires not just philosophical recovery but institutional and social reform. This supports RC's emphasis on subsidiarity and character-forming institutions.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that Aquinas and Dante, as Augustinians and Aristotelians, tested legitimate government by the degree to which it secures justice, but by the early fourteenth century this appeal to justice was displaced by appeals to individual rights (ius), understood as a faculty entitling every creature to exert its abilities — rights now claimable without invoking shared conceptions of the common good. This shift coincides with the rise of classical rhetoric (via Petrarch and Cicero) as a rival to moral philosophy, and with the practice of casuistry expanding especially in economic life, eroding unconditional prohibitions like usury. The net effect is that the Thomistic framework, in which rights were embedded in a thick account of common good and virtue, becomes displaced by a thinner, more individualist and rhetorical moral culture.

annotations.content: D, Aq: / as / Fndin
annotations.anchor_text: _nas and Dante, as Augustinians and Aristotelians_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is abbreviating 'Dante, Aquinas: as Foundation' — likely shorthand tracking the pairing of Dante and Aquinas as the Augustinian-Aristotelian baseline MacIntyre is establishing before showing its displacement. The 'Fndin' probably means 'foundation,' marking this moment as the structural grounding of the tradition-based account of justice and government against which the subsequent rights-shift will be measured. Tom may be flagging this for cross-reference to earlier chapters where MacIntyre establishes the Thomistic synthesis.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly maps to RC's interest in the Thomistic tradition as a coherent moral framework — Tom is marking the 'before' state of a tradition that RC seeks to recover, specifically the Aquinas-Dante axis as a foundation for legitimate authority grounded in justice rather than rights-claims.

annotations.content: is bound to obey, and where the laws enjoin what is vicious, one is indeed bound not to obey.
annotations.anchor_text: _as a ruler makes positive laws not in accordance_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is underlining MacIntyre's summary of the Thomistic position on civil disobedience — that unjust law (especially vicious law) does not bind conscience. This is a strong normative claim about the limits of positive law, and Tom likely marks it because it represents the tradition-based alternative to both liberal rights-language and mere legal positivism. The double emphasis ('bound to obey' / 'bound not to obey') captures the paradox MacIntyre is drawing out.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly relevant to RC's concern with the formation of conscience and the limits of institutional authority — the Thomistic tradition here provides a non-liberal, non-positivist basis for resistance to corrupt law, which RC needs as an alternative to both statist and libertarian frameworks.

annotations.content: good and legitimate government the degree to which it secures justice
annotations.anchor_text: _Aquinas and Dante, as Augustinians and Aristotelians, made the test_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks the core Thomistic criterion of legitimate government — justice, not consent or rights. This is the standard MacIntyre says is later displaced. Tom is likely tracking the precise moment MacIntyre names this criterion so he can contrast it with the rights-language that emerges in the following paragraph.
annotations.rc_relevance: Central to RC's subsidiarity and civic friendship themes — legitimate institutions are those oriented toward justice as a substantive good, not merely procedural fairness or rights-protection. This is the normative benchmark RC wants to recover.

annotations.content: this earlier appeal to justice is displaced by the primacy given to appeals to rights
annotations.anchor_text: _own theoretical apologetics, this earlier appeal to justice_
annotations.interpretation: This is arguably the most important underline on the page — MacIntyre's thesis statement about the historical shift from justice-based to rights-based moral language. Tom underlines it because it names the rupture that MacIntyre's whole genealogy is tracing. The word 'displaced' is key: it's not that justice disappears, but that rights become primary, inverting the order of justification.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the hinge point for RC's critique of modernity: the displacement of justice (a common good concept) by rights (an individualist concept) is precisely the fragmentation RC diagnoses. Tom marking this signals it as a foundational text for RC's argument.

annotations.content: whereby every creature is entitled to exert its abilities in certain ways
annotations.anchor_text: _By the very early fourteenth century ius has become understood_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the new definition of ius as individual faculty/entitlement — a semantic shift MacIntyre treats as philosophically momentous. The phrase 'every creature' is striking: this is a universalist, pre-political claim that bypasses community and teleology. Tom likely marks it to show how rights-language abstracts away from the particular practices and virtues that give abilities their meaning in the Thomistic framework.
annotations.rc_relevance: Relevant to RC's engagement with MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism — the 'encyclopaedist' mode of moral reasoning, which treats rights as self-evident and universal, has its origin in precisely this semantic shift that Tom is tracking.

annotations.content: Human rights thus understood are characteristically claimed against someone else.
annotations.anchor_text: _Human rights thus understood are characteristically claimed_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks MacIntyre's structural observation that rights, as now conceived, are adversarial — they are claims against others, not expressions of shared goods. This contrasts sharply with the Thomistic framework where justice is ordered toward a common end. The word 'against' carries the entire weight of MacIntyre's critique of rights-language as inherently conflictual and atomizing.
annotations.rc_relevance: Directly illuminates RC's concern with social fragmentation — if rights are structurally adversarial, they cannot serve as the basis for civic friendship or community. RC's recovery project needs an alternative moral vocabulary, which is exactly what the Thomistic tradition offers.

annotations.content: conceptions of a common good had become sufficiently indirect for them to be deployable without invoking such conceptions in a theoretically substantial rather than a merely rhetorical way.
annotations.anchor_text: _The relationship envisaged between them and shared conceptions_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks MacIntyre's precise description of how rights become 'unmoored' from the common good — the connection still exists but has become so indirect as to be practically inoperative. The contrast between 'theoretically substantial' and 'merely rhetorical' is crucial: rights-talk retains the surface grammar of moral argument but loses its metaphysical depth. Tom likely sees this as MacIntyre's sharpest diagnostic claim.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the core of what RC diagnoses as contemporary moral discourse's failure — institutions and public argument invoke community and common good rhetorically but without substantive philosophical grounding. RC's project is precisely to re-establish the 'theoretically substantial' connection MacIntyre says was lost.

annotations.content: merely
annotations.anchor_text: _rather than a merely rhetorical way_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'merely' to flag MacIntyre's own qualification — MacIntyre is distinguishing between genuinely substantial and merely rhetorical uses of common good language. The circle may also signal mild skepticism: is the distinction between 'substantial' and 'rhetorical' itself rhetorically substantial? Tom may be noting a tension in MacIntyre's language, or simply marking a key analytical distinction for later reference.
annotations.rc_relevance: Relevant to RC's methodological concern: if common good language has become merely rhetorical, what would make RC's recovery of it 'theoretically substantial' rather than nostalgic? This annotation marks a self-reflexive challenge RC must answer.

annotations.content: I based / v.1 / R-based / Bndatns / fergrt—
annotations.anchor_text: _this earlier appeal to justice is displaced by the primacy given_
annotations.interpretation: Tom appears to be constructing a structural contrast: 'I based' likely means 'justice-based' (or 'institution-based'), 'v.1' means 'version 1,' and 'R-based' means 'rights-based,' with 'Bndatns' as 'Foundations' and 'fergrt' possibly 'forgotten' or a person's name (Fergrt?). This looks like Tom is labeling two rival foundations — a justice/institution-based one versus a rights-based one — and noting that the foundations of the first have been forgotten. This is a structural annotation organizing MacIntyre's historical argument into a comparative schema.
annotations.rc_relevance: This annotation is RC's diagnostic framework in miniature — the project must articulate what was lost when justice-based foundations were replaced by rights-based ones. The 'forgotten foundations' framing is central to RC's recovery argument.

annotations.content: Can rights / be claimed / in ways [only?] / invoking a / conception / of the common / good?
annotations.anchor_text: _them to be deployable without invoking such conceptions_
annotations.interpretation: Tom poses a direct philosophical question in response to MacIntyre's claim: can rights-claims be reformulated so they require invoking a conception of the common good? This is not passive note-taking — it is Tom pushing back on or extending MacIntyre's argument. He may be gesturing toward a reform of rights-language rather than its wholesale rejection, asking whether rights can be rehabilitated within a tradition-based framework. This tension between rejection and reform of rights-language is live in contemporary Thomism.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is perhaps the most RC-relevant annotation on the page. RC must decide whether to reject rights-language as irredeemably individualist or to reconstruct it within a common good framework. Tom's question here is the RC project's central practical-theoretical challenge.

annotations.content: moral philosophy acquired a new rival in the form of the revived classical rhetoric of the fourteenth century, for which as a discipline a political relevance lacking in philosophy was claimed from Petrarch onwards.
annotations.anchor_text: _Yet this kind of use of 'merely' is itself perhaps inappropriate_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's identification of classical rhetoric as the disciplinary rival to moral philosophy — a rival that claimed political relevance philosophy lacked. This is a significant structural move in MacIntyre's argument: the shift isn't just philosophical but institutional, a competition between disciplines for authority over political life. Tom likely marks it to track how the 'encyclopaedist' mode of enquiry gains its foothold through rhetoric rather than argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: Relevant to RC's concern with institutions forming character — when rhetoric displaces moral philosophy as the master discipline for political life, the formation of civic virtue loses its philosophical grounding. RC's recovery project requires reclaiming that ground.

annotations.content: the practice of casuistry took on new dimensions, perhaps especially in the economic order
annotations.anchor_text: _At the same time the practice of casuistry took on new_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks MacIntyre's example of casuistry's expansion in economic life as symptomatic of the broader moral fragmentation. Casuistry here functions as a case-by-case erosion of principle — the prohibition on usury being the concrete example. Tom likely sees this as evidence of how tradition-based moral prohibitions are worn away not by frontal assault but by incremental case-by-case accommodation.
annotations.rc_relevance: Relevant to RC's interest in how institutions (including economic ones) either form or deform character — casuistry's expansion represents institutions adapting moral norms to practice rather than practice being reformed by norms, which is the inversion RC wants to correct.

annotations.content: What largely disappeared from view during this period in which
annotations.anchor_text: _its being discarded. What largely disappeared from view_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the opening of what appears to be a key summarizing sentence (continuing on the next page) — MacIntyre is about to name what was lost in this historical transition. The double underline or strong underline suggests Tom marks this as a structural pivot in the chapter's argument, the moment MacIntyre names the loss that his whole project is trying to diagnose and reverse.
annotations.rc_relevance: This sentence likely names exactly what RC is trying to recover. Tom marking it here suggests he intends to return to it as a definitive statement of the project's motivation — what disappeared is what RC seeks to resurrect.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that the thirteenth-century confrontation between Averroist Aristotelianism and Augustinianism produced two distinct outcomes: first, Aquinas's constructive dialectical synthesis, which had the capacity to direct enquiry further; and second, the subsequent university curriculum's marginalization of Aquinas's systematic thought, which excluded serious engagement with it and instead encountered it only as discrete theses. A third mode of repudiation is then introduced through Meister Eckhart, a Dominican thinker whose influence derived precisely from his antithetical, non-Thomistic use of materials drawn from Aquinas, representing the conceptual and moral pluralism of late medieval philosophy. This sets up MacIntyre's broader claim that the synthetic mode of Thomistic enquiry largely disappeared as a recognized tradition, surviving only in fragmentary or distorted form until the sixteenth-century Scholastic revival.
