annotations.content: synthetic mode of T.P. largely disappeared to be / coins no longer recognized as such
annotations.anchor_text: _modes of argument and problems of disparate kinds_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is distilling MacIntyre's core claim for this section: that the Thomistic Practice (T.P.) as a synthetic, integrated mode of enquiry effectively ceased to be recognized as a living tradition. The phrase 'coins no longer recognized as such' is a striking metaphor Tom is either borrowing from nearby MacIntyre text or generating himself — suggesting that Thomistic concepts circulated in later thought but were no longer identified as belonging to a coherent tradition, like currency stripped of its mint-mark. This is Tom tracking a key structural claim about tradition-death versus tradition-continuity.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly engages the RC theme of tradition vs. reform — specifically, how a tradition can persist in fragmented, unrecognized form, which is precisely the diagnostic problem RC must address when attempting to 'resurrect' civility from classical sources.

annotations.content: possibility of any overall synthetic and systematic mode of thought and practice
annotations.anchor_text: _multiplied was the possibility of any overall_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this phrase because it names the precise thing that was lost — not just Thomism's conclusions, but its synthetic and systematic character as a mode of thought and practice. This is MacIntyre's argument that what was defeated was not a set of doctrines but an entire rational practice. Tom is flagging the specific quality of the loss, distinguishing it from mere intellectual disagreement.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project is itself attempting to recover a synthetic mode — connecting virtue, civic life, and institutional formation — so Tom is marking the very target of RC's recovery effort as what MacIntyre identifies as historically extinguished.

annotations.content: embodied within the continuities of a tradition, being recognized
annotations.anchor_text: _embodied within the continuities of a tradition_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines 'embodied' and 'being recognized' together, suggesting he is tracking MacIntyre's insistence that a tradition must be socially recognized to function — it is not enough to exist latently. The emphasis on embodiment points to MacIntyre's anti-abstractionist view that traditions live in practices and institutions, not texts alone. Tom may be noting the sociological condition of tradition-survival that RC must meet.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's concern with institutions forming character is directly implicated here — MacIntyre is saying that without institutional embodiment and communal recognition, even a philosophically superior tradition cannot sustain itself, a warning RC must heed.

annotations.content: acceptance of the standpoint of the Summa Theologiae would have required so radical a repudiation of the presuppositions and terms of later fourteenth-century and fifteenth-century debate
annotations.anchor_text: _acceptance of the standpoint of the Summa Theologiae_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this extended passage because it is MacIntyre's explanation for why Thomism failed to win adherents — not because it was refuted, but because accepting it would have required too radical a break with prevailing intellectual frameworks. This is a sociological and rhetorical explanation for tradition-defeat, not an epistemological one. Tom is likely noting that tradition-loss is often a function of institutional momentum rather than argumentative failure.
annotations.rc_relevance: This speaks to the RC theme of MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism — the implication being that dominant intellectual frameworks self-perpetuate not by winning arguments but by setting the terms such that alternatives seem unintelligible, a dynamic RC must navigate in its own context.

annotations.content: sterility of late medieval thought had become widely recognized by the beginning of the sixteenth century
annotations.anchor_text: _Only when the sterility of late medieval thought_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the claim that it took the visible failure — the 'sterility' — of the dominant tradition before alternatives could re-emerge. This is a historically significant claim: Thomism was not revived by argument alone but by the exhaustion of its rivals. Tom may be reading this as both historical analysis and a normative lesson about the conditions under which tradition-recovery becomes possible.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this is a directly applicable diagnostic: contemporary social fragmentation may itself be the condition of sterility that makes the recovery of classical civility traditions not just possible but necessary — the collapse of rival frameworks opens space for the synthetic alternative.

annotations.content: 6th C revival + waxing of Aq. systematic inquiry
annotations.anchor_text: _nascence of Scholasticism from Vitoria to Suarez_
annotations.interpretation: Tom labels this moment as '6th C revival' (sixteenth-century revival) and marks it as item ① in what appears to be a numbered sequence he is constructing to track the two distinct outcomes of the thirteenth-century confrontation that MacIntyre introduces. The circled '1' suggests Tom is organizing MacIntyre's argument into a structured list, with this being the first outcome — the eventual Scholastic recovery of Aquinas via Vitoria and Suarez. 'Waxing of Aq. systematic inquiry' confirms Tom reads this revival as the partial restoration of the synthetic mode he noted was lost.
annotations.rc_relevance: The Scholastic revival represents a historical precedent for what RC is attempting — recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry after a period of marginalization — and Tom marking it as item ① suggests he sees it as a structurally analogous moment to the present.

annotations.content: drew so largely upon materials provided by Aquinas and yet made such an un-Thomistic use of them
annotations.anchor_text: _drew so largely upon materials provided by Aquinas_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this paradox — that the Scholastic revival used Aquinas's materials but in a non-Thomistic way — because it is crucial to MacIntyre's argument about how a tradition can be simultaneously cited and betrayed. The revival did not restore the synthetic mode; it used Thomistic content without Thomistic method or spirit. Tom is tracking this as a warning about pseudo-recovery.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is a direct warning for the RC project: recovering the language and even the conclusions of a tradition without recovering its mode of enquiry and its living embodiment is not genuine tradition-recovery. RC must attend to method and practice, not just doctrine.

annotations.content: thirteenth-century university confrontation between Averroist Aristotelianism and Augustinianism had two distinct and contrasting outcomes
annotations.anchor_text: _thirteenth-century university confrontation between Averroist_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this structuring sentence because it announces the two-outcome framework MacIntyre is about to elaborate. This is an architectural mark — Tom is identifying the hinge of the argument where MacIntyre pivots from historical narrative to analytical claim. The word 'distinct' is important: MacIntyre is resisting any simple story of Thomism's defeat and insisting on a differentiated account.
annotations.rc_relevance: MacIntyre's tripartite structure (encyclopaedist / genealogist / tradition) is being instantiated historically here — Tom is tracking how MacIntyre's analytical framework maps onto actual historical confrontations, which is essential for RC's use of MacIntyre as a diagnostic tool.

annotations.content: Aquinas's own constructive correction, reinterpretation, and integration of the contending traditions into a new dialectical synthesis
annotations.anchor_text: _Aquinas's own constructive correction reinterpretation_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines 'constructive correction, reinterpretation, and integration' and specifically 'new dialectical synthesis' — this is MacIntyre's positive characterization of what Aquinas achieved. The circle on 'new dialectical synthesis' (see circle annotation) confirms Tom sees this phrase as definitionally important. Tom is marking Aquinas's method as a model, not just a historical fact — correction, reinterpretation, and integration are the verbs of living tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the methodological heart of what RC aims to do — not antiquarian revival but constructive correction and reintegration of classical traditions into a new synthesis adequate to contemporary fragmentation. Tom is marking his own project's method.

annotations.content: new dialectical synthesis
annotations.anchor_text: _integration of the contending traditions into a_
annotations.interpretation: Tom circles 'new dialectical synthesis' specifically, elevating it above the surrounding underlined text. This suggests the phrase is doing special conceptual work for him — it is not just a description of Aquinas but a term of art that defines what a successful tradition-response to intellectual conflict looks like. The circle functions as a definition-marker or key-term marker.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's core aspiration is precisely to produce something analogous — a dialectical synthesis of classical civility traditions that can respond constructively to contemporary rival versions of social life. Tom may be naming his own project's goal in MacIntyre's terms.

annotations.content: ensuing development both of the university curriculum and of the dominant tendencies in intellectual and moral debate, a development which excluded for the most part engagement with Aquinas's thought understood systematically and not just as a set of discrete theses
annotations.anchor_text: _ensuing development both of the university curriculum_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as the second outcome — the institutional exclusion of Aquinas understood systematically. The phrase 'not just as a set of discrete theses' is crucial: MacIntyre is distinguishing between token-use of Aquinas and genuine engagement with his systematic thought. Tom is tracking how institutions can formally include a thinker while substantively excluding their mode of enquiry.
annotations.rc_relevance: This speaks directly to RC's concern with institutions forming character — the university as an institution that shapes what counts as legitimate enquiry can exclude a tradition even while nominally engaging it, a dynamic RC must recognize in contemporary academic and civic institutions.

annotations.content: ②
annotations.anchor_text: _ensuing development both of the university curriculum_
annotations.interpretation: Tom marks this passage as ② in his running list, confirming he is tracking MacIntyre's two distinct outcomes of the thirteenth-century confrontation. Outcome ② is the institutional exclusion of systematic Thomism through university curriculum development. Paired with ① (the 16th-century Scholastic revival noted above), Tom is building a structured reading of MacIntyre's historical argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: The numbering suggests Tom is building an analytical scaffold — identifying the historical mechanisms by which tradition-defeat operates — which RC must understand in order to avoid repeating them in its own recovery project.

annotations.content: yet another way in which Aquinas's distinctive positions were repudiated, one as pregnant with future possibility as the conceptual, theoretical, and moral pluralism of late medieval philosophy, but antithetical in its mode
annotations.anchor_text: _yet another way in which Aquinas's distinctive positions_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as MacIntyre introduces a third mode of repudiation — distinct from both the Scholastic misappropriation (①) and the university's exclusion (②). The phrase 'pregnant with future possibility' catches Tom's attention because MacIntyre is not dismissing this third mode as mere error but acknowledging its generative power. 'Antithetical in its mode' is the key qualifier — this is repudiation through inversion, not neglect.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project must contend with traditions that are antithetically derived from the very sources RC seeks to recover — distortions that carry the vocabulary of virtue while inverting its substance. This is the genealogist mode in MacIntyre's tripartite structure.

annotations.content: 2b
annotations.anchor_text: _Dominican origins in the thought of another Dominican regent_
annotations.interpretation: Tom labels Eckhart's tradition as '2b' — a sub-category of outcome ②, suggesting Tom reads Eckhart's antithetical repudiation as a variant or subspecies of the institutional/university failure to engage Aquinas systematically, rather than as a fully independent third category. This is a subtle interpretive move: Tom may be arguing that Eckhart's antithetical use of Aquinas is enabled by the same institutional conditions that produced outcome ②.
annotations.rc_relevance: The '2b' label suggests Tom sees the Eckhartian antithetical tradition and the university's discrete-thesis approach as structurally related failures — both are forms of tradition-disembodiment that RC must diagnose and resist.

annotations.content: When in 1325 he was accused of heresy, he claimed to be a Thomist. But it is precisely because and insofar as he was not
annotations.anchor_text: _When in 1325 he was accused of heresy_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this passage because it is MacIntyre's sharpest formulation of the Eckhart paradox: influence through non-identity. Eckhart claimed Thomist identity but exerted influence precisely through his deviation from it. The emphasis on 'was not' (which appears to be double-underlined or stressed in the text itself) is MacIntyre making a pointed logical claim. Tom is flagging this as a key example of how tradition-claims can be simultaneously sincere and structurally false.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this is a warning about false tradition-recovery — claiming the mantle of a tradition (civility, virtue, Thomism) while actually being its antithesis. RC must develop criteria for distinguishing genuine recovery from antithetical appropriation.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that Heidegger's rejection of Aquinas was not a misunderstanding but rather a rejection of the entire Platonic-Aristotelian tradition's conceptualization of being — yet ironically, what drove that rejection was precisely what made Heidegger most akin to Meister Eckhart. MacIntyre then pivots to Eckhart's sermons (not his philosophical works) as the relevant texts, arguing that in them we find an anticipation of Heidegger's thesis that being speaks to us through language rather than us using language to speak of being, and that conceptualization, systematic enquiry, and genre conventions become barriers to openness to being. This sets up a contrast between the Thomistic tradition's analogical predication — which acknowledges what lies beyond concepts while still requiring conceptualization — and the Eckhartian-Heideggerian move to non-argumentative, non-conceptual modes of speech.

annotations.content: (10 line sentence)
annotations.anchor_text: _Heidegger's rejection of Aquinas is of course_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging the extraordinary syntactic length of the sentence beginning with Heidegger's rejection of Aquinas. This is not mere stylistic observation — Tom is likely noting that MacIntyre's own prose mirrors the complexity of the tradition he is defending, a kind of enactment of the discursive reasoning Heidegger rejects. It may also be a reading aid, marking a sentence that requires multiple passes to parse, or a pedagogical note if Tom is teaching this text.
annotations.rc_relevance: MacIntyre's dense, tradition-embedded prose style is itself an implicit argument against the Heideggerian preference for non-argumentative speech — Tom flagging this sentence length may signal awareness of how form and content interact in the 'tradition' version of moral enquiry, a core RC structural concern.

annotations.content: Heidegger's rejection of Aquinas is of course at the most fundamental level
annotations.anchor_text: _Heidegger's rejection of Aquinas is of course_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the claim that the rejection operates at the most fundamental level, not merely a technical or methodological disagreement. This marks the stakes: this is not a family dispute within a shared framework but a root-level divergence about what being is and how it can be approached. Tom is likely tracking this as the key diagnostic claim before MacIntyre's pivot to Eckhart.
annotations.rc_relevance: The 'most fundamental level' framing is essential to MacIntyre's tripartite structure — the encyclopaedist, genealogist, and tradition-bearer do not merely disagree about conclusions but about the very nature of rational enquiry itself. This speaks directly to RC's concern with why Enlightenment critique cannot simply be answered on its own terms.

annotations.content: in moving from sense experience to true judgment that the mind first perfects itself
annotations.anchor_text: _moving from sense experience to true judgment_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the Thomistic epistemological claim about the mind's perfection through judgment. This is a key structural moment: MacIntyre is articulating what Aquinas actually holds before showing how Heidegger rejects it. Tom may be marking this as the positive Thomistic position to contrast with what follows — the Heideggerian inversion where being speaks rather than mind judges.
annotations.rc_relevance: The Thomistic account of judgment as the locus of the mind's perfection is central to the tradition-based model of moral enquiry RC seeks to recover — it grounds the possibility of genuine rational progress within a tradition, against both encyclopaedist foundationalism and genealogist nihilism.

annotations.content: there is indeed that which lies beyond concepts, beyond saying, which our analogical ordering of concepts nonetheless points us towards in our speaking of God as truth and goodness
annotations.anchor_text: _beyond concepts, beyond saying, which our analogical ordering_
annotations.interpretation: This is one of the most heavily marked passages on the page, and rightly so — Tom is flagging MacIntyre's articulation of the Thomistic via negativa combined with analogical predication. The key move is that Aquinas acknowledges the beyondness of God while insisting that conceptual ordering still points us there. Tom is likely distinguishing this from the Eckhartian position, which treats conceptualization itself as a barrier rather than a pointer.
annotations.rc_relevance: This passage captures the tension between apophatic mysticism and analogical reasoning that RC must navigate — the tradition MacIntyre defends does not deny transcendence but insists that civic, moral, and theological discourse requires conceptual structure, which has direct implications for RC's recovery of Thomistic moral community.

annotations.content: there is indeed also a mode of speaking about God at the level of philosophy and theology—as contrasted for example with the prophetic images of Scripture—which is conceptually ordered through such analogical predications
annotations.anchor_text: _there is indeed also a mode of speaking about God_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's careful distinction between the prophetic-imagistic mode of Scripture and the philosophically ordered analogical mode. This is a crucial structural move: MacIntyre is not dismissing non-conceptual speech but insisting that philosophy and theology require conceptual order. Tom appears to be tracking how MacIntyre carves out space for Thomistic discourse against both scriptural literalism and Heideggerian mysticism.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's project of recovering Thomistic moral enquiry depends precisely on this middle path — neither collapsing into non-rational tradition nor adopting Enlightenment foundationalism. This passage is a locus classicus for MacIntyre's defense of a rationally ordered tradition.

annotations.content: Heidegger did not misunderstand Aquinas in rejecting him
annotations.anchor_text: _Heidegger did not misunderstand Aquinas in rejecting him_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as a pivotal concession by MacIntyre — he is granting Heidegger hermeneutical seriousness. The rejection was not based on a failure to read Aquinas carefully; it was a deliberate, eyes-open rejection. This is intellectually honest and raises the stakes considerably: if Heidegger understood and still rejected, the disagreement is genuinely fundamental, not resolvable by better reading. Tom may be flagging this as a moment where MacIntyre respects the genealogist tradition even while opposing it.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's engagement with MacIntyre's genealogist interlocutors — the project cannot simply dismiss critics of the Thomistic tradition as uninformed. The RC project must engage the genuine force of the Heideggerian critique, which this sentence acknowledges.

annotations.content: his sermons
annotations.anchor_text: _relevant parts of Eckhart's writings are not his philosophical cal works, but his sermons_
annotations.interpretation: Tom isolates 'his sermons' with an underline, flagging MacIntyre's methodological move to shift the textual focus from Eckhart's philosophical to his homiletical works. This is significant because sermons represent precisely the non-argumentative, non-systematic mode of speech that MacIntyre is about to analyze — the form itself embodies the content. Tom may be noting that this is where MacIntyre locates the Eckhart-Heidegger kinship.
annotations.rc_relevance: The distinction between philosophical treatise and sermon maps onto RC's concern with different institutional settings for moral formation — the pulpit versus the academy. MacIntyre's argument suggests that the mode of speech appropriate to forming character may be non-systematic, which has implications for how RC thinks about civility's transmission.

annotations.content: it is in the sermons that we find Eckhart's anticipation of Heidegger's theses that it is not true that we use language in speaking of being, but that instead being speaks to us in language, being which is to be named, or rather which is to deliver itself, only through nonargumentative modes of speech, in which the categorizations, conceptualizations, and genres of systematic enquiry are repudiated as barriers to openness to being
annotations.anchor_text: _it is in the sermons that we find Eckhart's anticipation_
annotations.interpretation: This is the longest and most structurally important underline on the page, marking MacIntyre's full statement of the Eckhartian-Heideggerian thesis. Tom is carefully marking the exact claim that will be contested — that conceptualization is a barrier rather than a path. The underline suggests Tom sees this as the central proposition MacIntyre is setting up to critique or at least to distinguish from Thomistic analogical reasoning. The phrase 'repudiated as barriers to openness to being' is the sharpest formulation of what Aquinas would deny.
annotations.rc_relevance: This passage is directly relevant to RC's core tension between tradition and reform — if systematic enquiry and genre conventions are barriers to truth, then institutional forms of moral reasoning (including those RC seeks to recover) are themselves suspect. MacIntyre's argument here is the key diagnostic for why the genealogist tradition poses a genuine threat to civic and moral institutions.

annotations.content: nonargumentative modes of speech, in which the categorizations, conceptualizations, and genres of systematic enquiry are repudiated as barriers to openness to being
annotations.anchor_text: _nonargumentative modes of speech, in which the categorizations_
annotations.interpretation: In addition to the underline, Tom adds a bracket on the left margin around this specific clause, elevating it above the surrounding underlined text. The bracket signals that this is not just important within the argument but may be a key term or concept Tom wants to extract for use elsewhere — perhaps in RC writing, teaching, or cross-referencing with MacIntyre's treatment of the genealogist elsewhere in the book. The bracket may also mark a point of personal agreement or contestation.
annotations.rc_relevance: The repudiation of genre and systematic enquiry as barriers is precisely what RC must resist in order to defend institutional moral formation — if genres and conceptualizations are barriers, then the recovery of Thomistic civility is itself a barrier. Tom bracketing this suggests he recognizes it as the sharpest challenge RC must answer.

annotations.content: Vertical line in left margin alongside the Eckhart sermons paragraph
annotations.anchor_text: _The relevant parts of Eckhart's writings are not his philosophical_
annotations.interpretation: A vertical line alongside a full paragraph typically signals that Tom considers the entire paragraph — not just individual phrases — to be a unit of argument worth preserving whole. This paragraph introduces the Eckhart-Heidegger comparison through the sermonic mode, and Tom's line suggests he sees it as a discrete and important argumentative step rather than mere background. It may also flag this as a passage to quote directly in RC writing.
annotations.rc_relevance: The paragraph as a whole introduces the claim that non-institutional, non-systematic speech is where Heidegger's genealogical impulse finds its medieval precedent — this is directly relevant to RC's question of what institutions and genres of speech are necessary for civic moral formation.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that Meister Eckhart's preaching represented a deliberate disintegration of the Thomist idiom of rational discourse, replacing it with a mode of speech unconstrained by logic, analogy, or scientia — one that claimed access to a 'noble power of the soul' beyond reason. This move was not merely heterodox theology but a structural separation of preaching from rational theology as the Dominicans had understood it, effectively creating preaching as a self-sufficient cultural artifact and social practice. The page sets up Eckhart as an early exemplar of the 'genealogist' type — someone who abandons tradition's rational constraints in favor of a discourse that claims its own sovereign warrant.

annotations.content: For what Eckhart resorted to in his preaching was a mode of speech unconstrained by logic
annotations.anchor_text: _For what Eckhart resorted to in his preaching_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has underlined the definitional core of MacIntyre's characterization of Eckhart's move — the abandonment of logical constraint as the operative feature of his discourse. This is not merely a historical note; Tom is likely flagging this as the structural hinge of the argument: the moment when a tradition-embedded thinker breaks from the rational grammar of that tradition. The underline suggests Tom sees this as a key diagnostic criterion for distinguishing tradition-constituted from rival modes of enquiry.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly maps onto the RC concern with MacIntyre's tripartite structure — Eckhart's 'unconstrained' speech is a precursor to the genealogist's rejection of rational tradition, and Tom is likely tracking how the breakdown of shared rational grammar produces the kind of social fragmentation RC seeks to address.

annotations.content: a noble
annotations.anchor_text: _a noble power of the soul_
annotations.interpretation: The isolated underline of 'a noble' within the quotation about the soul's power suggests Tom is flagging the rhetorical or quasi-Aristotelian vocabulary Eckhart employs even while departing from Thomistic rationality — using the language of virtue ('noble') to authorize a trans-rational faculty. Tom may be noting the irony or tension that Eckhart appeals to a recognizable moral vocabulary while undermining its rational framework.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this tension is important: civic friendship and virtue formation depend on shared moral vocabulary, but Eckhart's move shows how that vocabulary can be hollowed out and redirected to authorize non-rational discourse — a warning about the fragility of tradition's linguistic resources.

annotations.content: I am deeply indebted to Caputo's discussion
annotations.anchor_text: _I am deeply indebted to Caputo's discussion_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has underlined MacIntyre's acknowledgment of John D. Caputo, likely to track the scholarly genealogy of the argument and perhaps to note a tension: Caputo reads Eckhart through Heidegger, while MacIntyre is reading him through the lens of tradition and rational enquiry. Tom may be flagging this to contest or interrogate whether MacIntyre's use of Caputo is fully consistent with Caputo's own Heideggerian framing.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project is sensitive to the difference between Heideggerian and Thomistic accounts of tradition and being — flagging Caputo here signals Tom is watching for where MacIntyre's argument is vulnerable to the genealogist's counter-reading from within.

annotations.content: For what Eckhart resorted to in his preaching was a mode of speech unconstrained by logic, by the structures of rational theory and practice, and by analogical ordering in his predications, a mode which he took to express a power higher than those exhibited in sense experience and in rationality, "a noble power of the soul, which is so high and noble that it grasps God in His own naked being," as he calls it in one place, "a power of the soul which touches neither time nor flesh" (quoted by Caputo, pp. 110–11). It was in the light afforded by this power that Eckhart denied that creatures have being but are rather "a pure nothing," one of the claims rightly condemned by Pope John XXII in 1329.
annotations.anchor_text: _It was in the light afforded by this power_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has bracketed the entire passage describing Eckhart's trans-rational epistemology and its consequences — the denial of creaturely being. The bracket with an arrow likely signals that this whole block is structurally significant, not just a detail. Tom is marking this as the full articulation of what 'unconstrained by logic' means in practice, including its ontological consequences (creatures as 'pure nothing'). The arrow may indicate a connection to a passage elsewhere in the book or his notes.
annotations.rc_relevance: This passage is crucial for RC's engagement with MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment rationalism: Eckhart's move anticipates the genealogist's dissolution of tradition-constituted ontology, replacing a shared metaphysical grammar with a sovereign inner authority — precisely the move RC argues leads to civic and moral fragmentation.

annotations.content: What Eckhart did, in fact, was to disintegrate the Thomist idiom
annotations.anchor_text: _What Eckhart did, in fact, was to disintegrate_
annotations.interpretation: This is one of MacIntyre's strongest declarative claims on the page and Tom has underlined it as such. The word 'disintegrate' is doing heavy philosophical work — it is not reform or revision but structural dissolution. Tom is almost certainly marking this as a thesis sentence, the argumentative payoff of the preceding description, and may be comparing it to how MacIntyre elsewhere describes the Enlightenment's treatment of Aristotelian tradition.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project's concern with tradition vs. reform finds its sharpest edge here: MacIntyre distinguishes internal reform (which preserves rational grammar) from disintegration (which abandons it). Tom is tracking this distinction as foundational to RC's argument about what genuine renewal of civic tradition requires versus what merely fragments it further.

annotations.content: in defiance of reason
annotations.anchor_text: _claims allegiance independently of and, if necessary, in defiance of reason_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has underlined the escalating phrase 'in defiance of reason' — MacIntyre's sharpest characterization of Eckhart's discourse. The qualifier 'if necessary' is absorbed into the underline, suggesting Tom is noting that this is not accidental irrationalism but a principled, structural defiance. This may be flagged to compare with Nietzsche's genealogical stance elsewhere in the book.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this is the moment where the genealogist type is embryonically present in medieval theology — the claim to a warrant that can override reason is structurally identical to what MacIntyre later attributes to Nietzsche. Tom is likely building an argument that social fragmentation has theological as well as philosophical roots.

annotations.content: this setting free of preaching from scientia, so opposed not only to the whole Dominican ideal as Albertus Magnus and Aquinas had understood it but also to the Augustinian tradition, resulted in a new cultural artifact, a new form of social practice, preaching as a self-sufficient form of activity
annotations.anchor_text: _this setting free of preaching from scientia_
annotations.interpretation: Tom has underlined this long clause in full, suggesting he sees it as MacIntyre's sociological and institutional conclusion — not just a theological point but an account of how the break from rational tradition produces a new autonomous social practice. The phrase 'self-sufficient form of activity' is key: it describes an institution that has severed its accountability to a larger rational community. Tom is likely connecting this to MacIntyre's broader argument about practices and institutions in 'After Virtue'.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's concern with subsidiarity and the role of institutions in forming character: when a social practice (preaching, or by analogy, any civic institution) becomes 'self-sufficient' and severs its connection to rational tradition, it loses its capacity to form virtue and becomes an instrument of fragmentation rather than community.

annotations.content: preaching as a self-sufficient form of activity
annotations.anchor_text: _resulted in a new cultural artifact, a new form of social practice_
annotations.interpretation: The circle around 'preaching as a self-sufficient form of activity' isolates this phrase as a term of art or a structurally pivotal concept within the underlined passage. Tom is distinguishing it from the surrounding underline, suggesting he sees 'self-sufficient practice' as carrying specific theoretical weight — possibly connecting it to MacIntyre's vocabulary of practices in 'After Virtue' or flagging it as the sociological category that bridges Eckhart's theology and modern institutional fragmentation.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is centrally concerned with how institutions either sustain or undermine civic friendship and virtue formation. The circle signals Tom's recognition that 'self-sufficient' practices — those that sever accountability to rational tradition — are structurally incapable of performing the formative social role RC seeks to recover.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that philosophers stand in two distinct relationships to society: as active participants in public rational debate (type a), or as professionalized academic elites whose technical vocabulary excludes the broader public (type b). When philosophy becomes professionalized, those excluded from the elite discourse respond not with counter-arguments but with rhetorical effectiveness and manipulation. This dynamic explains why doctrines promoted by rhetorically effective popular preachers function as untouchable talismans — their purpose is not rational persuasion but inoculation against rational accountability.

annotations.content: trast between the irrationalism of this type of preacher and the hyper rationalism of the academic philosopher should not be allowed to obscure the fact that these figures are cultural counterparts, playing complementary social roles.
annotations.anchor_text: _trast between the irrationalism of this type of preacher_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets this passage to mark MacIntyre's core structural claim: that the popular irrational preacher and the hyperrational academic philosopher are not opposites but complements within a single cultural system. This is a crucial anti-dualist move — Tom is likely flagging it because it disrupts the easy assumption that rationalism is always the corrective to irrationalism. The bracket suggests Tom sees this as architecturally important to the chapter's argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly connects to the RC theme of tradition vs. Enlightenment rationalism — MacIntyre is showing that the Encyclopaedist's hyperrationalism and populist irrationalism are two faces of the same crisis, which is precisely the diagnostic RC needs to understand contemporary fragmentation.

annotations.content: a
annotations.anchor_text: _A philosopher can stand in two very different_
annotations.interpretation: Tom labels the first of MacIntyre's two types of philosopher-society relationships as 'a' — the philosopher as active participant in public debate, appealing to shared rational standards accessible to a generally educated public. This is a structural label tracking MacIntyre's binary taxonomy. Tom is organizing the argument's architecture for later retrieval or citation.
annotations.rc_relevance: Type 'a' maps onto the RC ideal of civic friendship and public rational discourse — the philosopher who remains accountable to the broader community and its shared standards, which is what MacIntyre's 'tradition' model aspires to recover.

annotations.content: b
annotations.anchor_text: _But when professionalized academic philosophy makes the rational_
annotations.interpretation: Tom labels the second philosopher-type as 'b' — the professionalized academic who monopolizes rational discourse through certified technical skills and inaccessible vocabulary. The 'b' label paired with 'a' shows Tom is systematically mapping MacIntyre's structural argument. He likely intends to use this taxonomy as an analytical tool in RC to classify contemporary intellectual figures or institutions.
annotations.rc_relevance: Type 'b' is the Encyclopaedist mode of enquiry in institutional form — professionalized, exclusionary, and ultimately self-undermining because it generates the very rhetorical irrationalism it cannot contain. RC is explicitly concerned with this failure of Enlightenment-style academic philosophy.

annotations.content: radical critic, as Plato did
annotations.anchor_text: _this can be the case even when the philosopher_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this phrase to mark MacIntyre's acknowledgment that even a radical critic like Plato remained within type 'a' — publicly accountable and appealing to shared standards. This is a significant qualification: radicalism alone doesn't push a philosopher into the professionalized exclusionary mode. Tom may be noting this to distinguish productive from destructive forms of philosophical challenge.
annotations.rc_relevance: This connects to the RC question of whether tradition-based critique (e.g., Thomistic or Augustinian) can function as type 'a' radical criticism — challenging modernity while remaining civically engaged rather than retreating into academic enclosure.

annotations.content: But when professionalized academic philosophy makes the rational discussion of questions of fundamental import the prerogative of an academic elite with certified technical skills, using a vocabulary and writing in genres which are unavailable to those outside that elite, the excluded are apt to respond by repudiating the rationality of the philosophers.
annotations.anchor_text: _import the prerogative of an academic elite with certified_
annotations.interpretation: This is the longest and most heavily underlined passage on the page, suggesting Tom identifies it as the thesis statement of the section. The word 'prerogative' is key — Tom is flagging MacIntyre's claim that professionalization is an act of intellectual enclosure, not just specialization. The consequence — the excluded repudiating rationality altogether — is the mechanism by which rhetorical irrationalism is produced by academic elitism.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the precise diagnostic RC needs: the contemporary culture wars and anti-intellectualism are not spontaneous but are structurally produced by academic philosophy's self-enclosure. Subsidiarity and civic friendship as RC themes require breaking this enclosure.

annotations.content: effectiveness in persuasion above rationality of arguments is from this point of view less important than their function. That function is to prevent any challenge to the effective rhetorical performer which might make him or her, or seem to make him or her, rationally accountable by appeal to some public standard.
annotations.anchor_text: _to prevent any challenge to the effective rhetorical performer_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this to capture MacIntyre's functional analysis of populist doctrine: it is not primarily about content but about shielding the performer from rational accountability. The phrase 'rationally accountable by appeal to some public standard' is doing heavy lifting — Tom is tracking the collapse of the very public standard that type 'a' philosophy requires. This connects to his margin note about Trump.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project's concern with civic friendship and institutional character formation depends on shared public standards of rational accountability — MacIntyre here identifies exactly what destroys those standards, which is the core problem RC is trying to address.

annotations.content: Trump
annotations.anchor_text: _to prevent any challenge to the effective rhetorical performer_
annotations.interpretation: Tom explicitly applies MacIntyre's analysis of the rhetorically effective popular performer to Donald Trump, noting that Trump uses 'the news' to boost attraction — i.e., media presence functions as the rhetorical forum MacIntyre describes, where persuasion and manipulation prevail over rational argument. The asterisk links this margin note to the underlined passage about preventing rational accountability. Tom is not merely illustrating the text; he is testing MacIntyre's framework as a diagnostic tool for contemporary politics.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is a direct RC application moment: the Trump annotation shows Tom reading MacIntyre's medieval/early modern analysis as live political theory. The RC project's civility theme is here confronted with its most challenging contemporary test case — a figure who epitomizes the type 'b' cultural dynamic MacIntyre describes.

annotations.content: asterisk with vertical line and checkmark or tick
annotations.anchor_text: _to prevent any challenge to the effective rhetorical performer_
annotations.interpretation: The asterisk appears to function as a cross-reference marker linking the margin note (Trump) to the underlined passage about preventing rational accountability. The vertical line and tick suggest Tom is confirming a connection — this is a 'confirmed instance' mark rather than a question mark. He is validating MacIntyre's framework against a contemporary case.
annotations.rc_relevance: The symbol's cross-referencing function suggests Tom is building an evidential structure for RC — connecting classical analysis to contemporary phenomenon, which is the project's core methodological move.

annotations.content: Modern evangelical fundamentalist doctrines
annotations.anchor_text: _Modern evangelical fundamentalist doctrines_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the opening phrase of what MacIntyre is about to argue — that modern evangelical fundamentalism exemplifies the talisman/fetish doctrine pattern that exempts performers from rational accountability. The underline at the very bottom of the page, paired with the bottom margin note, suggests Tom is flagging a second contemporary instantiation of MacIntyre's framework alongside Trump. He may be noting the convergence of political and religious forms of this phenomenon.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC's engagement with MacIntyre's tradition tripartite structure must reckon with how 'tradition' can itself be weaponized as a talisman against rational enquiry — this underline marks that danger from within the tradition MacIntyre is trying to recover.

annotations.content: why there is such hostility
annotations.anchor_text: _Modern evangelical fundamentalist doctrines_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes 'why there is such hostility' as a gloss on the evangelical fundamentalist passage — he is reading MacIntyre as explaining the structural origin of evangelical hostility to rational critique (because the doctrines function as talismans shielding performers from accountability). 'Ev y T' likely abbreviates 'Evangelicals and Trump' or possibly 'Every T[radition?]', connecting this observation back to his Trump margin note. This is Tom synthesizing across annotations on the page.
annotations.rc_relevance: This bottom note is one of the most RC-relevant annotations on the page: Tom is identifying MacIntyre's framework as an explanation for why civility projects face such fierce resistance — the hostility is not incidental but structurally produced by the talisman-doctrine dynamic. RC must account for this if it hopes to 'resurrect' civic rational discourse.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that Eckhart's 'emphatic' rhetorical style represents a divorce between rhetorical effectiveness and rational argumentation, which violates the thirteenth-century Dominican ideal—exemplified by Aquinas—that the homily should be the end-product of philosophical and theological education. He then broadens this into a cultural-epistemological claim: cultures are defined not only by their achievements but by what they structurally cannot see or acknowledge, and the failure to understand Aquinas in the late Middle Ages is a paradigm case of such culturally-induced blindness—one that may persist into the present.

annotations.content: Late medieval mysticism and pietism
annotations.anchor_text: _one example of such fetishism. Late medieval_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is flagging the specific historical phenomena MacIntyre identifies as symptomatic of the fetishism of experience over rational discourse. The underline suggests he wants to track the category of 'mysticism and pietism' as a contrasting tradition to Thomistic rational enquiry — possibly to ask whether these movements represent a genuine rival tradition or merely a degenerate form of the encyclopaedist mode.
annotations.rc_relevance: This bears directly on RC's concern with how traditions degrade: mysticism and pietism here represent a retreat from the civic, rationally-formed moral community into privatized experience, mirroring contemporary fragmentation.

annotations.content: or external pietism
annotations.anchor_text: _Late medieval mysticism and pietism provide_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is glossing or extending MacIntyre's category — noting that the fetishism of experience can manifest as an externalized pietism (public, performative religiosity) as well as interior mysticism. This distinction matters because external pietism would have different civic and institutional implications than inward mysticism, and Tom seems to be thinking about the range of forms this tradition-failure can take.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this note probes how the collapse of Thomistic rational community produces not just private irrationalism but also performative public religion — a phenomenon highly relevant to contemporary debates about civil religion and civic virtue.

annotations.content: Just his style
annotations.anchor_text: _Caputo has emphasized Eckhart's own defense_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is registering skepticism or irony about Caputo's defense of Eckhart — the note 'Just his style' reads as a dismissive gloss, suggesting Tom agrees with MacIntyre that appealing to rhetorical effectiveness as a defense is question-begging or insufficient. It may also flag a methodological point: reducing theological content to stylistic performance is itself a symptom of the very rationality-deficit MacIntyre is diagnosing.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's concern with the substantive vs. performative dimensions of civic and moral discourse — a tradition reduced to stylistic effectiveness has lost the rational architecture needed to form genuine moral community.

annotations.content: this divorce between rhetorical effectiveness and rational argumentation is deeply at odds with the thirteenth-century Dominican ideal, especially as articulated by Aquinas, in which the homily was to be the end-product of an education in philosophy and theology
annotations.anchor_text: _this divorce between rhetorical effectiveness and rational argumentation_
annotations.interpretation: This is the most heavily marked passage on the page, suggesting Tom identifies it as the argumentative core of this section. The underline captures MacIntyre's normative claim about what preaching should be — not mere rhetoric but the culminating public expression of a formed rational intellect. Tom is likely tracking this as the positive standard against which both Eckhart's mysticism and contemporary discourse fail.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is foundational for RC: it articulates the classical model in which public speech (the homily as civic-institutional address) is downstream of philosophical formation — precisely the vision of civic friendship and virtue-formation through institutions that RC seeks to recover.

annotations.content: It was thus not only on account of its overall structure and the understanding of enquiry implicit in that structure that the Summa Theologiae as a whole was so alien to the mainstream of intellectual development. Its educational presuppositions as a manual of instruction were generally shared neither by the academic nor by the popular mind.
annotations.anchor_text: _It was thus not only on account of its overall structure_
annotations.interpretation: Tom is marking MacIntyre's two-part explanation for the Summa's alienation from its cultural context: (a) its structural form of enquiry, and (b) its educational presuppositions. The 'a)' and 'b)' labels in the margin confirm Tom is explicitly parsing this as a two-pronged argument, probably to use this distinction analytically elsewhere — distinguishing failure of form from failure of institutional precondition.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this distinction is crucial: it means the recovery of Thomistic enquiry requires not just re-presenting the Summa's arguments but rebuilding the institutional and educational conditions that make its mode of enquiry intelligible — a deeply subsidiarity-relevant point.

annotations.content: Letter 'a)' in blue ink

annotations.interpretation: Tom is labeling the first of two reasons MacIntyre gives for the Summa's cultural alienation: its structural form and the understanding of enquiry implicit in that structure. The explicit lettering signals Tom is reading this passage analytically and may be building a schematic of MacIntyre's argument for use in RC writing or teaching.
annotations.rc_relevance: Structuring MacIntyre's argument this way suggests Tom is preparing to deploy it as a diagnostic framework — RC needs to distinguish which kind of failure (structural or presuppositional) is operative in contemporary moral discourse.

annotations.content: Letter 'b)' in blue ink

Paired with 'a)', Tom is building a binary analytical structure. The 'b)' point — about shared presuppositions — may be the one Tom finds more generative for RC, since it points to the problem of common moral formation across social strata.
annotations.rc_relevance: The failure of shared educational presuppositions is precisely the RC problem: without common institutions of moral formation, neither elites nor ordinary citizens can sustain the civic friendship and rational moral community that RC aims to recover.

annotations.content: Culture is present also in failure, and it can in crucial respects blind the educated to what needs to be seen. So it was with the failure to understand the work of Aquinas in the late Middle Ages. So perhaps it may be with us.

This is MacIntyre's explicit warning that the contemporary reader may be in the same position as the late medieval culture that failed to understand Aquinas. Tom's underlining suggests he finds this either the most important or most usable passage on the page — it is MacIntyre's direct address to the present.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is perhaps the most RC-relevant passage on the page: it directly frames the RC project's urgency — that our own cultural habits of mind may be systematically blinding us to the resources Thomistic tradition offers for addressing social fragmentation, and that intellectual humility before tradition is a civic as well as epistemic virtue.

chapters.title: Tradition against Encyclopaedia: Enlightened Morality as the Superstition of Modernity

pages.summary: MacIntyre opens Chapter VIII by identifying the deepest point of conflict among the three rival traditions: not merely how moral enquiry should be conducted, but whether rational persons can even agree on the standards and criteria for evaluating competing claims. The Ninth Edition encyclopaedists presupposed that all rational persons can resolve disagreements on questions of standards and method, while Nietzschean genealogy denies this entirely. MacIntyre then poses the question of why the Ninth Edition standpoint deserves continued serious engagement, given that nobody now explicitly holds it, and promises three reasons why dismissing it would be a mistake.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that even after the collapse of the Encyclopaedist project, three residual legacies persist: (1) academic institutions still operate as if Enlightenment coherence were intact, (2) the 'ghosts of the Ninth Edition' haunt the contemporary academy and must be exorcized, and (3) most critically, the encyclopaedic belief in the universal commensurability of rational standpoints continues to dominate curriculum design — the assumption that any text from any culture can be translated and made intelligible to any student. He notes that while some philosophers defend this via Donald Davidson's arguments, the dominant academic culture holds this belief not because of philosophical argument but as an unexamined presupposition.

annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this connects to the question of how tradition is transmitted: if the curriculum assumes universal translatability, it cannot genuinely engage with the particularity of any tradition, including the Thomistic one RC seeks to recover. This is a polemical point — it disarms the Davidson-based defense by showing it is post-hoc rationalization of a pre-philosophical commitment. Tom likely sees this as one of MacIntyre's strongest rhetorical moves on the page.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is directly relevant to RC's argument about the genealogy of contemporary moral assumptions — the Encyclopaedist framework persists not because it has won philosophical arguments but because it has been institutionalized as common sense. RC's recovery project must therefore be cultural and institutional, not merely argumentative.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues that contemporary academic practice differs crucially from its Enlightenment predecessors by accepting only a 'weak' conception of rationality — one that tolerates the coexistence of widely divergent views without requiring conclusive resolution. Whereas Enlightenment rationality (epitomized by the Ninth Edition of the Encyclopaedia Britannica) held that rational debate could always, if properly conducted, yield definitive truth, contemporary academia has abandoned this aspiration. The deeper reason for this abandonment is that any standard strong enough to adjudicate between rival frameworks would itself have to be embedded in some particular theoretical and conceptual scheme — there is no view from nowhere.

pages.summary: MacIntyre argues on this page that when two large-scale theoretical standpoints are systematically at odds, it may appear there is no rational way to adjudicate between them — a conclusion Nietzschean genealogists embrace. But he counters that Aquinas demonstrably refuted this conclusion in the thirteenth century by engaging Augustinianism and Aristotelianism on their own internal terms, showing each tradition's inadequacies from within. The Thomist strategy — raising critical questions for a rival tradition in that tradition's own terms, not one's own — is thus the model for how enquiry across rival frameworks can proceed rationally, and MacIntyre extends this as a challenge to both encyclopaedists and genealogists.

annotations.rc_relevance: This annotation connects to RC's concern with how institutions and systems of moral enquiry can calcify into self-referential frameworks that exclude tradition-based reasoning — a core tension in the RC project's critique of procedural liberalism.

annotations.content: Red drawn diagram or circled annotation with lines — appears to be a circled label with radiating lines, possibly organizing competing positions (encyclopaedist / genealogist / tradition)
annotations.anchor_text: _no theoretically neutral pre-theoretical ground from which_
annotations.interpretation: This looks like Tom visually mapping the tripartite structure MacIntyre is deploying — the circle with lines could be a hub-and-spoke diagram indicating that all three rival versions (encyclopaedia, genealogy, tradition) converge on this problem of adjudication. The red color signals it as a structural or organizational mark rather than a simple emphasis mark. It may also be flagging that this is the crux of the chapter's argument.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project is explicitly built around MacIntyre's tripartite structure; this visual map signals Tom identifying the exact argumentative node where all three versions of moral enquiry are shown to face the same problem, which is the entry point for the RC project's constructive proposal.

annotations.content: since Augustinianism and the Aristotelianism of the Islamic commentators were systematically at odds in just this way, each having internal to itself its own standards of rational evaluation, no rational way could be found to settle the differences between them.
annotations.anchor_text: _since Augustinianism and the Aristotelianism of the Islamic_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines MacIntyre's historical example — that in the thirteenth century the same problem of incommensurable frameworks existed between Augustinians and Islamic Aristotelians. This is a key move: MacIntyre is showing the problem is not uniquely modern, which undermines the genealogist's claim that it is a product of the Enlightenment's collapse. Tom tracks this because it grounds the Thomist solution historically.
annotations.rc_relevance: For RC, this annotation is pivotal: it shows that the crisis of moral fragmentation is not unprecedented, and that the Thomist tradition has resources to address it — directly supporting RC's recovery of Thomistic moral enquiry as a live option.

annotations.content: since Aquinas decisively showed this conclusion to be false, those able to learn from him have every reason to resist it in the present instance.
annotations.anchor_text: _Aquinas decisively showed this conclusion to be false_
annotations.interpretation: Tom double-underlines (in blue/dark ink) this sentence, which is the argumentative pivot of the page: MacIntyre's affirmative claim that Aquinas actually refuted the incommensurability thesis. This is the crux Tom is building toward — not merely a critique of rivals but a positive Thomist counter-demonstration. The emphasis suggests Tom sees this as the most important sentence on the page for his own constructive project.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the intellectual heart of RC's recovery project: if Aquinas already solved the problem of rational adjudication across rival traditions, then the RC project can invoke this precedent to argue that civil and moral community can be rationally reconstructed even across deep ideological fragmentation.

annotations.content: yet this
annotations.anchor_text: _every reason to resist it in the present instance_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes 'yet this' — likely a promissory note to himself flagging a concession or complication he wants to track. It may mean: 'yet this [Thomist solution] still faces the problem of...' or 'yet this [refutation] is contested by...' It reads as a gesture toward a reservation Tom is holding in tension with MacIntyre's confident assertion.
annotations.rc_relevance: This annotation reflects RC's honest engagement with the limits of the Thomist recovery — Tom is not simply endorsing MacIntyre but stress-testing whether the Aquinas solution can be transplanted into contemporary civil discourse without further qualification.

annotations.content: Thomistic strategy: assess rival frameworks in T's
annotations.anchor_text: _Aquinas's strategy, if I have understood it correctly_
annotations.interpretation: Tom condenses MacIntyre's description of Aquinas's method into a formula: 'assess rival frameworks in T's [their own] terms.' The abbreviation 'T's' likely means 'their own terms' — capturing MacIntyre's point that Aquinas engaged Augustinians and Aristotelians by their own internal standards. This is Tom's working summary of the Thomist dialectical method, which he will likely use as an operative concept in RC.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is the methodological core of RC's approach: rather than imposing an external standard (encyclopaedist move) or dissolving all standards (genealogist move), the Thomist strategy engages rival traditions on their own terms — a model for RC's engagement with secular liberal and postmodern interlocutors.

annotations.content: So we also need to proceed by raising critical questions for encyclopaedists and genealogists, not in our terms, but in theirs.
annotations.anchor_text: _So we also need to proceed by raising critical_
annotations.interpretation: Tom brackets this sentence because it is MacIntyre's explicit methodological prescription — the practical upshot of the Thomist strategy applied to the present enquiry. The bracket (rather than underline) suggests Tom sees this as a passage he will quote or deploy directly, marking it for extraction. It is a 'to-do' as much as an analytical mark.
annotations.rc_relevance: This sentence is potentially a programmatic statement for the RC project itself: RC's engagement with civic and institutional interlocutors should proceed by entering their own frameworks and demonstrating inadequacies from within, rather than simply asserting the superiority of the Thomist tradition.

annotations.content: Just such a problem is posed for the genealogist, so I shall suggest in the ninth lecture, by his or her conception of personal identity.
annotations.anchor_text: _Just such a problem is posed for the genealogist_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines this as a forward reference — MacIntyre is announcing that the genealogist's concept of personal identity will be the site where the Thomist immanent critique is applied. Tom tracks this because it connects epistemology to selfhood, a crucial bridge for the RC project's concern with character formation and moral identity.
annotations.rc_relevance: Personal identity is a live issue for RC's concern with civic friendship and character: if the genealogist's self is incoherent on its own terms, this opens space for RC to argue that a tradition-constituted account of selfhood is necessary for genuine civic community.

annotations.content: EsQ: personal identity
annotations.anchor_text: _by his or her conception of personal identity_
annotations.interpretation: Tom writes 'EsQ' — likely 'Essay Question' or 'Essential Question' — flagging 'personal identity' as a topic for a research essay or a key question for the RC project. This is a meta-annotation: Tom is not just reading but actively converting MacIntyre's argument into a research agenda. The green ink distinguishes it as a planning note.
annotations.rc_relevance: This directly maps onto RC's theme of how institutions form character and sustain moral identity: the question of personal identity under rival versions of moral enquiry is the anthropological foundation of RC's civic and political argument.

annotations.content: in the encyclopaedist's idiom no expression invites such questions more obviously and more insistently than 'mo—'
annotations.anchor_text: _in the encyclopaedist's idiom no expression invites_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the truncated sentence ending in 'mo—' (presumably 'morality' continued on the next page), marking MacIntyre's claim that the encyclopaedist's use of 'morality' is itself the most internally problematic term in their own idiom. Tom is tracking the terminological critique — the word 'morality' becomes a site of internal incoherence for the encyclopaedist.
annotations.rc_relevance: RC is deeply concerned with how moral language has been hollowed out in contemporary public discourse — this annotation flags MacIntyre's claim that the very word 'morality' in Enlightenment usage is internally unstable, supporting RC's argument for recovering richer moral vocabulary from the classical tradition.

annotations.content: EsO on morality
annotations.anchor_text: _no expression invites such questions more obviously_
annotations.interpretation: Another 'EsO' (Essay/Essential Question) note — Tom flags 'morality' as a second research question, distinct from personal identity. This pairs with the previous EsQ annotation, suggesting Tom is building a list of RC research questions directly from this page. The green ink connects these two planning notes as a set.
annotations.rc_relevance: The RC project's engagement with 'contemporary moral debate' (noted at the page bottom) is here given its analytical anchor: the instability of the term 'morality' in encyclopaedist discourse is the opening for RC's constructive Thomist alternative.

annotations.content: Contemporary moral debate is notoriously inconclusive in its outcomes, perhaps in part because of the extent to which it makes
annotations.anchor_text: _Contemporary moral debate is notoriously inconclusive_
annotations.interpretation: Tom underlines the opening of what is likely a new section or argumentative turn at the page bottom. The green underline (matching the EsO notes) suggests Tom is connecting this passage to his research questions — this is MacIntyre's empirical claim about contemporary moral discourse that will ground his critique. The downward red arrow reinforces that the argument continues and Tom wants to follow it to the next page.
annotations.rc_relevance: This is perhaps the most directly RC-relevant sentence on the page: 'contemporary moral debate is notoriously inconclusive' is precisely the social phenomenon the RC project is responding to. MacIntyre's diagnosis here is the problem-statement that RC's recovery project aims to address.

annotations.content: Red downward arrow below the last underlined sentence
annotations.anchor_text: _because of the extent to which it makes_
annotations.interpretation: A red arrow pointing down at the page bottom, indicating the argument continues onto the next page and Tom does not want to lose the thread. This is a navigation mark — Tom is signaling to himself that this sentence is incomplete and must be read with its continuation. It may also mean the argument is escalating or pointing toward a conclusion Tom considers pivotal.
annotations.rc_relevance: The arrow signals Tom's awareness that MacIntyre's diagnosis of contemporary moral inconclusiveness is building toward a structural argument — likely about the encyclopaedist's role in producing that inconclusiveness — which is foundational to RC's problem-statement.

annotations.content: Red drawn box or rectangle around 'LOF' or similar abbreviation near bottom left
annotations.anchor_text: _outcomes, perhaps in part because of the extent_
annotations.interpretation: The letters 'LOF' (or similar) in a red box near the bottom are ambiguous but may stand for 'Lack of Foundation' or 'Loss of Framework' — Tom abbreviating MacIntyre's diagnosis of why contemporary moral debate fails. The box signals it is a term or concept Tom is coining or tracking across multiple pages as a shorthand.
annotations.rc_relevance: If 'LOF' means 'Lack of Foundation,' this is a compressed version of RC's core diagnostic: that moral fragmentation results from the loss of the tradition-constituted frameworks that alone make moral reasoning coherent — a claim RC seeks to address constructively.
